### [1] [DraDDP: A Multimodal Multi-Party Dialogue Discourse Parsing Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00012)
*Shannan Liu,Peifeng Li,Yaxin Fan,Qiaoming Zhu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They introduce DraDDP, the first public English multimodal (text+video) dataset for multi-party dialogue discourse parsing, and show multimodal cues help identify dialogue structures and relation types.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing discourse parsing work focuses on text-only or two-party dialogues, which does not reflect realistic, complex, multi-party, and multimodal conversations such as TV dramas or real-world interactions. There is a need for a public benchmark with aligned video and text to study how multimodal signals contribute to understanding discourse structure in multi-party dialogues.

Method: They build DraDDP from American TV dramas, segmenting episodes into dialogue segments and annotating utterance-level discourse dependency structures and relation types, aligned with 9.1 hours of video. They then define the multi-party multimodal discourse parsing task on this data and create benchmarks by training/evaluating models that use different modality settings (e.g., text-only vs. multimodal), followed by systematic ablations and analyses on modality impact.

Result: DraDDP contains 495 dialogue segments, 6,374 utterances, and 9.1 hours of aligned video, providing diverse multi-party interaction scenarios. Benchmark experiments show that including multimodal information (beyond text) improves performance in capturing dialogue dependency structures and classifying relation types compared with text-only baselines.

Conclusion: DraDDP fills a gap by offering the first public English multimodal dataset for multi-party dialogue discourse parsing and demonstrates that multimodal cues are beneficial for understanding dialogue structure. The authors will release the dataset, annotation guidelines, and code to support further work in multimodal dialogue understanding and benchmarking on this task.

Abstract: Multi-party dialogue discourse parsing aims to identify dependency structures and relation types between utterances in conversations. Previous studies are mostly limited to textual modality or two-party dialogue, failing to meet the multimodal and multi-party settings. In this paper, we construct the first publicly available English multimodal dataset DraDDP for multi-party dialogue discourse parsing, based on American TV dramas. DraDDP contains 495 dialogue segments with 6,374 utterances and 9.1 hours of parallel video content, covering rich multi-party interaction scenarios. Moreover, we establish comprehensive benchmarks by evaluating this task on DraDDP and conducting in-depth analysis on the impact of different modalities. Experimental results demonstrate the value of multimodal information in capturing dialogue structures and relation types. We will publicly release the dataset, annotation guidelines, and code to promote future research in multimodal dialogue understanding.

</details>


### [2] [Toward Robust In-Context Learning: Leveraging Out-of-distribution Proxies for Target Inaccessible Demonstration Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00014)
*Hao Xu,Rite Bo,Fausto Giunchiglia,Yingji Li,Rui Song*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: DOPA is a framework that selects in-context demonstrations for LLMs using an out-of-distribution (OOD) proxy and a diversity constraint to improve robustness on unseen target domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs perform well on some OOD tasks, but their performance degrades as the distribution shift between source and target data grows. Existing methods try to retrieve demonstrations similar to the (unknown) target domain from a source domain to improve in-context learning. However, in real situations, the target domain is inaccessible, making it hard to estimate its distribution and thus to select truly relevant demonstrations. There is a need for a principled way to approximate the target distribution and guide demonstration retrieval under such OOD conditions.

Method: The paper proposes DOPA, a demonstration search framework that uses an OOD proxy to approximate the inaccessible target domain. This proxy-based evaluation provides a surrogate signal for how similar source examples are to the hypothetical target distribution, guiding the selection of demonstrations. Additionally, DOPA imposes a global diversity constraint based on Mahalanobis distance to ensure that the chosen demonstrations are not only proxy-relevant but also sufficiently diverse in feature space, avoiding redundancy and improving coverage of the data manifold.

Result: Across multiple LLMs and tasks, experiments show that DOPA leads to better robustness and performance in OOD settings compared to baselines that do not use a proxy or do not enforce diversity. The method consistently improves inference quality when there is a substantial distribution shift between source and target domains.

Conclusion: By introducing an OOD proxy to approximate the inaccessible target distribution and combining it with a Mahalanobis-distance-based diversity constraint, DOPA provides a more effective demonstration retrieval strategy for in-context learning with LLMs. This approach improves robustness and performance in OOD scenarios and demonstrates that carefully designed proxy signals and diversity-aware selection can mitigate the challenges posed by unknown target domains.

Abstract: Although studies have demonstrated that Large Language Models (LLMs) can perform well on Out-of-Distribution (OOD) tasks, their advantage tends to diminish as the distribution shift becomes more severe. Consequently, researchers aim to retrieve distributionally similar and informative demonstrations from the available source domain to boost the inference capabilities of LLMs. However, in practical scenarios where the target domain is inaccessible, evaluating the unknown distribution is challenging, which indirectly impacts the quality of the selected demonstrations. To address this problem, we propose \textbf{DOPA}, a demonstration search framework that incorporates an OOD proxy to approximate the inaccessible target domain and guide the retrieval process. Building on proxy-based evaluation, DOPA further introduces a Mahalanobis distance-based global diversity constraint to ensure sufficient diversity among the retrieved demonstrations. Experimental results on multiple LLMs and tasks demonstrate that DOPA effectively enhances robustness in OOD settings\footnote{https://github.com/bort64/ood\_code}.

</details>


### [3] [AEyeDE: An Attention-Based Attribution Framework for AI-Generated Text Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00016)
*Aria Nourbakhsh,Adelaide Danilov,Christoph Schommer,Salima Lamsiyah*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces AEyeDE, a method that detects AI-generated text using attention-based attribution maps and a CNN classifier, outperforming text-only baselines and providing interpretable signals across multiple settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As language models become more fluent and can bypass detectors based on surface statistics or likelihood, it is increasingly difficult to distinguish AI-generated text from human-written text. There is a need for more robust, model-agnostic, and interpretable detection methods that go beyond text-level cues.

Method: The authors propose AEyeDE, which uses a proxy Transformer model with white-box access to extract attention-based attribution matrices from both human and AI-generated text. These matrices, essentially attention maps, are treated as structured inputs to a lightweight Convolutional Neural Network that learns discriminative representations. The approach is evaluated in both encoder-decoder and decoder-only settings, and under various robustness tests such as cross-dataset transfer and spelling perturbations. The authors also perform structural analysis of the attention maps to identify recurring local patterns and frequency differences between human and AI texts.

Result: In encoder-decoder translation scenarios, AEyeDE consistently outperforms a text-only detection baseline. In decoder-only scenarios, it achieves strong performance for generator-specific detection, remains competitive on widely used benchmarks, and is robust under cross-dataset transfer and alternative-spelling perturbations. Analysis reveals stable differences in the local structures and relative frequencies of patterns in attention maps between human and AI-generated text, across datasets and proxy models.

Conclusion: Attention-based attribution maps encode useful, discriminative patterns that differ systematically between human and AI-generated text. These maps provide a complementary and interpretable signal that improves AI-text detection over text-only baselines, and the proposed AEyeDE framework demonstrates robust performance across multiple architectures and datasets. The authors plan to release their code to encourage further work in attention-based detection methods.

Abstract: Detecting AI-generated text is becoming increasingly challenging as modern language models approach human-level fluency and can evade detectors that rely on surface statistics or likelihood-based signals. We propose \textsc{AEyeDE}, an attribution-driven approach to human-AI authorship detection that leverages model attention as a discriminative signal. Specifically, we extract attention-based attribution matrices for both human- and AI-generated text using a \emph{proxy} Transformer model with white-box access and train a lightweight Convolutional Neural Network to learn representations from these attribution maps. Across encoder-decoder translation settings, our method consistently outperforms a text-only baseline. In decoder-only settings, it performs strongly in generator-specific detection, remains competitive on standard benchmarks, and shows robustness under cross-dataset transfer and alternative-spelling perturbations. We further show that attention maps exhibit recurring local structures whose relative frequencies differ consistently between human- and AI-generated text across datasets and proxy models. These findings suggest that attention-based attribution maps provide a complementary and interpretable signal for AI-generated text detection. We will make the code publicly available to support future research.

</details>


### [4] [CSRP: Chain-of-Thought Reasoning for Chinese Text Correction via Reinforcement Learning with Efficiency-Aware Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00020)
*Wei Tian,Yuhao Zhou,Man Lan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes CSRP, a three-stage framework for Chinese Grammatical Error Correction (CGEC) using LLMs, addressing lack of linguistic priors and over-correction issues, and achieves state-of-the-art results via continual pre-training, CoT SFT, and RL with an efficiency-aware reward.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based CGEC systems struggle with subtle Chinese grammatical distinctions because general models lack specific linguistic priors. Moreover, standard supervised fine-tuning with maximum likelihood tends to optimize for recall rather than precision-focused metrics used in CGEC, causing over-correction where models introduce unnecessary edits. There is a need for a method that both internalizes domain-specific grammar knowledge and explicitly optimizes for edit precision and efficiency.

Method: CSRP is a three-stage framework: (1) Continual Pre-training (CPT) on 5.9M balanced CGEC-related samples to inject domain-specific grammatical and error-distribution knowledge into the base LLM. (2) Chain-of-Thought Supervised Fine-Tuning (CoT SFT), where the model is trained to explicitly reason about and explain detected errors before producing corrections, improving diagnostic transparency and structured correction behavior. (3) Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), a reinforcement learning alignment stage with a novel Efficiency-Aware Reward that rewards correct edits while explicitly penalizing unnecessary or excessive changes, thus pushing the model toward high precision and minimal-edit corrections aligned with CGEC metrics like F0.5.

Result: On the NACGEC benchmark, CSRP achieves state-of-the-art performance, with an F0.5 score of 50.99 and precision of 57.17, clearly surpassing previous models and mitigating over-correction typically seen with MLE-trained systems. On the CSCD Chinese spelling correction benchmark, CSRP attains 59.61 F1, outperforming GPT-4 by 5.20 points. Ablation studies show that the RL alignment stage contributes an 8% relative improvement over the SFT-only baseline and that this improvement is complementary to gains from large-scale continual pre-training, confirming the distinct value of explicit edit-efficiency optimization.

Conclusion: Explicitly combining domain-specific continual pre-training, structured error reasoning via Chain-of-Thought SFT, and RL alignment with an efficiency-aware reward yields substantial gains in Chinese grammatical and spelling error correction. The approach not only achieves state-of-the-art performance on key benchmarks but also directly addresses over-correction by optimizing for precision and minimal edits, demonstrating that edit-efficiency-aware optimization is crucial for high-quality CGEC with LLMs.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) based Chinese Grammatical Error Correction (CGEC) systems face two critical challenges: general-purpose models lack specialized linguistic priors for subtle grammatical distinctions, and Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with Maximum Likelihood Estimation fails to optimize for precision-focused metrics, leading to systematic over-correction. We propose CSRP, a three-stage framework that progressively builds correction capability through Continual Pre-training (CPT) on 5.9M balanced samples to internalize domain knowledge, Chain-of-Thought SFT with explicit error reasoning for diagnostic transparency, and Group Relative Policy Optimization with a novel Efficiency-Aware Reward that explicitly penalizes unnecessary edits. On the NACGEC benchmark, CSRP achieves state-of-the-art performance with 50.99 $F_{0.5}$ and 57.17 precision, substantially outperforming previous best results while effectively mitigating the over-correction bias inherent in MLE-trained models. Our method also advances CSCD spelling correction to 59.61 F1, surpassing GPT-4 by 5.20 points. Comprehensive ablation studies demonstrate that the RL alignment stage contributes a 8\% relative gain over the SFT baseline, and that this gain is orthogonal to the contribution of large-scale CPT, validating that explicit optimization for edit efficiency is essential for high-quality grammatical error correction. Our code is available at https://github.com/TW-NLP/ChineseErrorCorrector.

</details>


### [5] [SENSE: Semantic Embedding Navigation with Soft-gated Evaluation for Retrieval-based Speculative Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00021)
*Shaowen Chen,Zhicheng Liao,Hongwei Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SENSE is a new speculative decoding method that uses target-model hidden states and semantic-level verification to make retrieval-based speculative decoding more robust and faster, achieving higher acceptance lengths and speedups without hurting quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Retrieval-based Speculative Decoding (RSD) methods are attractive because they are plug-and-play and speed up LLM inference, but they rely on rigid lexical (surface-form) matches. This makes both retrieval and verification brittle to small wording changes, limiting performance and robustness. The authors want a speculative decoding approach that is semantically robust while retaining the efficiency benefits.

Method: They propose SENSE (Semantic Embedding Navigation with Soft-gated Evaluation). Instead of retrieving candidate continuations based on surface-form tokens, SENSE anchors retrieval on the hidden states (semantic embeddings) of the target LLM, ensuring semantic alignment between draft and target. A Soft-gated Evaluation module then verifies candidates by checking semantic equivalence rather than exact lexical overlap. Additionally, they define a unified framework that decomposes existing speculative decoding methods into atomic primitives, enabling controlled, component-wise comparisons and ablations.

Result: Across multiple domains and on both LLaMA and Qwen model families, SENSE achieves longer mean acceptance lengths (up to 4.09 tokens accepted per verification step) and up to 3.26x speedup in inference, while maintaining generation quality compared to several baseline speculative decoding and retrieval-based methods.

Conclusion: Semantic-aware retrieval and verification, grounded in target-model hidden states and soft-gated evaluation, can substantially improve the effectiveness of retrieval-based speculative decoding. SENSE delivers higher speedups and acceptance rates without degrading output quality, and the unified primitive-based framework provides a rigorous way to benchmark different speculative decoding designs.

Abstract: Speculative Decoding (SD) accelerates Large Language Model (LLM) inference by employing a lightweight draft model to propose candidate tokens, which are verified in parallel by the target model, without compromising generation quality. While Retrieval-based Speculative Decoding (RSD) is favored for its plug-and-play versatility, its potential is impeded by rigid lexical dependencies, rendering both retrieval and verification brittle to surface-level variations. To address this, we propose SENSE (Semantic Embedding Navigation with Soft-gated Evaluation). By anchoring retrieval on the hidden states of the target model, SENSE establishes robust semantic alignment, which empowers the Soft-gated Evaluation module to validate semantic equivalence rather than surface forms. To ensure rigorous benchmarking, we deconstruct existing methods into atomic primitives within a unified framework, facilitating granular, component-level comparison. Extensive experiments across diverse domains demonstrate that SENSE outperforms multiple baselines on the LLaMA and Qwen families, attaining up to 4.09 mean acceptance length and 3.26x speedup, while preserving generation quality. Our code will be released upon publication.

</details>


### [6] [lmfaoooo at SemEval-2026 Task 1: Humor Is an Audience. Preference Modeling for Constrained Humor Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00022)
*Alexey Tikhonov,Alexey Ivanov*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents a winning system for a humor-generation shared task that uses a generate-many-then-select pipeline with a learned preference model trained on pairwise human judgments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humor generation is difficult because funniness depends on audience, context, and culture, and existing supervision is noisy with low annotator agreement. The SemEval MWAHAHA task evaluates humor systems via human preferences, motivating methods that can both generate diverse humorous candidates and select those most likely to be preferred by humans.

Method: The authors use a two-stage pipeline. Stage 1: generate a diverse set of humorous candidates per prompt using multi-step prompting, an ensemble of models, and diversity-oriented decoding strategies. Stage 2: learn a preference model from pairwise human comparisons (instead of scalar funniness scores) collected in a Humor Arena prototype, then use this model to score and rank candidates. They design an interpretable procedure to convert comparison labels into a preference model, train and evaluate it on three datasets, and finally deploy it to rank candidates for the MWAHAHA task.

Result: Their preference models outperform baseline methods on three different preference datasets and exhibit better cross-domain transfer, indicating robustness. In the MWAHAHA SemEval-2026 shared task, their full system ranks 1st in English and Chinese subtasks and 2nd in the Spanish subtask. They also release 2.5K human pairwise comparison judgments, candidate pools, and rankings as resources for future research.

Conclusion: A generate-many-then-select approach, combined with a preference model trained on pairwise human judgments, is effective for constrained humor generation and transfers well across domains. The released data and artifacts can support further work on humor modeling and subjective, preference-based language generation tasks.

Abstract: Humor generation remains difficult not only because producing fluent, novel jokes is hard, but because "funny" is audience-dependent and supervision is noisy -- preferences vary with audience, context, and culture, and annotator agreement is often low. In this paper, we describe our system for the SemEval-2026 Task-1 (MWAHAHA), which focuses on humor generation under explicit constraints. The task evaluates submitted systems via human preference judgments in 1-on-1 arena-style comparisons.
  We adopt a "generate-many -> select-best" strategy. First, we generate a diverse pool of candidates per instance using multi-step prompting, model ensembling, and diversity-oriented decoding. Second, we select outputs using a preference model that approximates a "reader" by learning from human comparisons rather than absolute funniness scores. To support this approach, we release 2.5K human pairwise judgments collected through the Humor Arena prototype. We further propose an interpretable pipeline that converts labeled comparisons into a preference model. Across three preference datasets, our models consistently outperform baselines and show stronger cross-domain transfer. Finally, we apply the learned preference model to rank candidates for the MWAHAHA setting and release intermediate artifacts (candidate pools and rankings) to facilitate follow-up work.
  Our system ranked 1st in the English and Chinese subtasks of MWAHAHA and 2nd in the Spanish subtask.

</details>


### [7] [TrustLDM: Benchmarking Trustworthiness in Language Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00023)
*Yichuan Mo,Yukun Jiang,Yanbo Shi,Mingjie Li,Michael Backes,Yang Zhang,Yisen Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces TrustLDM, a benchmark and automated framework to evaluate and expose trustworthiness weaknesses (safety, privacy, fairness) in Language Diffusion Models under various decoding configurations and contextual settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Language Diffusion Models (LDMs) are emerging as strong competitors to auto-regressive language models, particularly due to their flexible, any-order decoding and fast generation. However, this flexibility may introduce new and poorly understood trustworthiness risks, including safety, privacy, and fairness issues that can depend on decoding order, context configuration, and generation length. There is a lack of systematic benchmarks and tools specifically designed to reveal these vulnerabilities in LDMs.

Method: The authors build TrustLDM, a benchmark focused on LDMs, which assesses trustworthiness along three dimensions: safety, privacy, and fairness. They construct multiple categories of static post contexts (including benign and malicious ones) that are attached to masked responses in LDM pipelines. They then run empirical evaluations across different LDM architectures and under varying decoding configurations (e.g., decoding order, context length, and generation length). Additionally, they design TrustLDM-Auto, an automatic evaluation framework that exploits LDMs’ flexible decoding to search over and systematically probe vulnerable configurations.

Result: Empirical experiments show that LDMs behave relatively well in terms of trustworthiness when responding only to user prompts, but their alignment quality significantly deteriorates when malicious post contexts are appended to the masked responses. The study finds that longer contexts do not always have stronger effects, and that both decoding order and generation length substantially influence trustworthiness metrics. Using TrustLDM-Auto, the authors automatically uncover many vulnerable decoding configurations, revealing prominent weaknesses across all evaluated models and across safety, privacy, and fairness dimensions.

Conclusion: TrustLDM and the automated TrustLDM-Auto framework reveal that current Language Diffusion Models, despite appearing trustworthy under standard prompting, can exhibit serious vulnerabilities when exposed to particular contextual and decoding setups. The work underscores that trustworthiness in LDMs is configuration-dependent and that systematic, LDM-specific evaluation is necessary. The benchmark and tools aim to guide the community in diagnosing, understanding, and ultimately building more robust and trustworthy LDMs.

Abstract: The rapid development of Language Diffusion Models (LDMs) challenges the dominant position of auto-regressive competitors in language processing. However, their flexible, any-order decoding strategies not only enable fast decoding speed but also potentially bring new trustworthiness challenges. To better understand the risks behind their pipelines, we introduce a comprehensive trustworthiness benchmark tailored to LDMs (TrustLDM), evaluating safety, privacy, and fairness across different LDM architectures with multiple categories of static post contexts. Our empirical results show that although LDMs generally exhibit strong trustworthiness with only the user prompts, their alignment behavior degrades noticeably when the malicious post contexts are attached to the masked responses. We further observe that longer contexts do not necessarily induce stronger effects, and both decoding order and generation length affect the evaluation outcomes. Finally, we propose TrustLDM-Auto, an automatic evaluation framework that leverages LDM decoding flexibility to systematically identify vulnerable configurations, revealing substantial trustworthiness weaknesses across all evaluated models and dimensions. Our work may potentially help the community build more trustworthy LDMs. Our code is available at https://github.com/PKU-ML/TrustLDM.

</details>


### [8] [ART: Attention Run-time Termination for Efficient Large Language Model Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00024)
*Chen Qiu,Guozhong Li,Panos Kalnis*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces ART, a run-time mechanism that speeds up long-context decoding in LLMs by terminating insignificant attention computations early, achieving higher throughput with similar accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context decoding in LLMs is bottlenecked by the memory bandwidth needed to fetch large key-value caches. Existing methods mostly prune keys only, ignoring that attention depends on both keys and values because handling values is considered too costly. There is a need for a method that reduces KV cache access cost during decoding without adding heavy overhead and that works alongside current KV management strategies.

Method: The authors propose Attention Run-time Termination (ART), a lightweight mechanism embedded into the attention kernel. ART tracks the running sum of attention outputs as KV blocks are processed. During kernel execution, it monitors how much additional contribution each new KV block makes to the accumulated attention output. Once those additional contributions fall below a negligible threshold, ART stops accessing further KV blocks, effectively terminating attention computation early. This is done at run time, does not require changing the model or pre-decoding pruning, and is orthogonal so it can combine with existing key-based KV cache pruning methods.

Result: On LongBench benchmarks, ART improves generation throughput by about 20% compared to the state-of-the-art KV management baselines, especially at large batch sizes, while delivering accuracy that is comparable to these baselines. This demonstrates that early termination based on accumulated attention outputs can substantially reduce KV memory access without sacrificing model quality.

Conclusion: ART provides an efficient, run-time attention early-termination mechanism that reduces KV cache access for long-context decoding in LLMs. By monitoring and cutting off negligible contributions during attention computation, it improves throughput significantly while preserving accuracy, and can be seamlessly integrated with existing key-based KV cache management techniques due to its orthogonal design.

Abstract: Long-context decoding in Large Language Models (LLMs) is severely constrained by the memory bandwidth required to fetch the extensive Key-Value (KV) cache. Most existing KV management methods rely on key-only pruning before decoding, despite the evidence that attention outputs depend jointly on keys and values, as incorporating values in their methods incurs prohibitive additional overhead. In this paper, we propose Attention Run-time Termination (ART), a lightweight run-time mechanism that tracks accumulated attention outputs during kernel execution and terminates subsequent KV block accesses once further contributions become negligible. This design makes ART orthogonal to existing key-based KV cache management methods, enabling seamless integration with them. Experiments on LongBench benchmarks show that ART achieves 20% higher generation throughput in large batch size than state-of-the-art baseline while maintaining comparable accuracy.

</details>


### [9] [Cognitive-Linguistic Indicators of Depression in Online Communities: Analysed by DistilBERT and Holographic Reduced Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00026)
*Brian Van Steen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper tests whether adding cognitively grounded features to transformer embeddings improves automated depression detection in Reddit text, and finds that a DistilBERT+HRR hybrid model substantially outperforms a TF-IDF+Naive Bayes baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated depression detection from social media can help with early identification and intervention, but current models often rely solely on surface-level text statistics or black-box neural embeddings, lacking explicit psychological grounding. The authors want to see if incorporating features inspired by Beck's Cognitive Theory of Depression—measurable cognitive distortions in language—can improve performance and interpretability beyond standard text representations.

Method: They use a subset of the Kaggle Reddit Suicide and Depression Detection dataset, drawing posts from depression-related and control subreddits. From each post, they extract cognitively motivated features: first-person pronoun density, absolutist words, and negative emotion indicators. These features are encoded via Holographic Reduced Representations (HRR) and concatenated with DistilBERT sentence embeddings. Two classifiers are compared: (1) a TF-IDF representation with a Naive Bayes classifier as a baseline; (2) the proposed hybrid model (DistilBERT embeddings + HRR-encoded cognitive-linguistic features) with Logistic Regression. Performance is evaluated using macro F1, 5-fold cross-validated F1, and AUC.

Result: The hybrid DistilBERT+HRR model substantially outperforms the TF-IDF+Naive Bayes baseline. It achieves a macro F1 score of 0.94 compared to 0.80 for the baseline. Under 5-fold cross-validation, F1 improves from 0.83 (baseline) to 0.92 (hybrid), and AUC increases from 0.958 to 0.981, demonstrating gains in both overall performance and robustness.

Conclusion: Combining cognitively grounded linguistic features, derived from Beck's Cognitive Theory of Depression, with transformer-based embeddings improves automated depression detection in Reddit text relative to a strong TF-IDF baseline. The results suggest that explicit modeling of cognitive distortions can enhance both the accuracy and potential interpretability of depression detection models, supporting further integration of psychological theory with modern NLP architectures.

Abstract: This paper investigates whether combining cognitively grounded linguistic features with transformer-based embeddings improves automated detection of depression in online text. Using Beck's Cognitive Theory of Depression, the study extracts cognitive distortions as measurable features, including first-person pronoun density, absolutist words, and negative emotion in Reddit posts from depression-related and control communities. Using a subset of the Kaggle Reddit Suicide and Depression Detection dataset, two classification pipelines are compared, a TF-IDF embedding with Naive Bayes as a baseline, and a hybrid model that concatenates DistilBERT sentence embeddings with Holographic Reduced Representation (HRR) vectors encoding the cognitive-linguistic features, followed by Logistic Regression. The hybrid DistilBERT HRR model achieves a macro F1 score of 0.94 versus 0.80 for the TD-IDF baseline, with 5-fold cross validation F1 improving from 0.83 to 0.92, and AUC from 0.958 to 0.981.

</details>


### [10] [A Multi-Domain Red Teaming Framework for Safety, Robustness, and Fairness Evaluation of Medical Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00027)
*Andrei Marian Feier,Veysel Kocaman,Yigit Gul,Ahmet Korkmaz,Alexander Thomas,Aleksei Zakharov,Jay Gil,Mehmet Butgul,David Talby*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a comprehensive clinical red-teaming framework showing that even top-performing LLMs in healthcare can catastrophically fail in specific, safety-critical scenarios, so mean accuracy is an insufficient safety metric.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used in healthcare, existing benchmarks mostly measure average performance on standard tasks and do not capture how models behave under adversarial prompts or ethically complex, real-world situations. There is a need to understand worst-case, domain-specific, and equity-related failures to ensure clinical safety and reliability.

Method: The authors designed a multi-domain red-teaming framework with 690 clinically grounded scenarios covering nine domains and over 150 subcategories. These scenarios included adversarial transformations to stress-test model behavior. Eleven contemporary LLMs were evaluated. Model responses were scored using a seven-dimensional rubric, with LLM-assisted automatic scoring and human-in-the-loop validation by clinicians or domain experts.

Result: Performance varied widely across the eleven LLMs, with mean scores from 0.791 to 0.984. Even highly rated models exhibited complete failures in specific safety-critical scenarios that would be masked by looking only at averages. The best systems (X-BAI, GPT-5, Claude Opus 4.1) exceeded 0.97 mean scores with relatively low variance but still showed domain-dependent weaknesses. Equity-focused tasks showed 10–20% higher error rates when scenarios were modified to include different demographic characteristics. Human reviewers caught important clinical failures that automated rubric-based scoring did not flag.

Conclusion: Clinically meaningful reliability of LLMs cannot be inferred from mean accuracy alone; understanding performance variance, worst-case failures, and domain-specific weaknesses is crucial. Equity concerns are amplified under demographic variations. Robust evaluation in healthcare requires hybrid approaches that combine automated assessments with clinician oversight to identify subtle and high-stakes failures and to build credible safety cases for deployment.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed across healthcare, yet existing benchmarks fail to capture model behavior under adversarial or ethically complex conditions common in clinical practice. We developed a multi-domain red teaming framework evaluating eleven contemporary LLMs across 690 clinically grounded scenarios spanning nine domains and over 150 subcategories. Scenarios incorporated adversarial transformations, and responses were assessed using a seven-dimension rubric with LLM-assisted scoring and human-in-the-loop validation. Results revealed substantial performance variance, with mean scores ranging from 0.791 to 0.984. Critically, several high-performing systems produced complete failures in individual safety-critical scenarios, demonstrating that aggregate accuracy masks clinically meaningful risk. The highest-performing systems (X-BAI, GPT-5, Claude Opus 4.1) achieved scores above 0.97 with low variance, while performance varied significantly across domains. Equity-related tasks showed 10-20% error amplification with demographic modifications, and human reviewers identified clinically relevant failures missed by automated evaluation. Our findings demonstrate that performance variance and worst-case failures provide more clinically meaningful reliability indicators than mean accuracy alone, and that hybrid evaluation approaches combining automation with clinician oversight are essential for credible safety assessment.

</details>


### [11] [TCAR-Gen: Temporal Graph Retrieval with Evidence Fusion for Knowledge-Grounded Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00029)
*Sidra Nasir,Muhammad Noman Zahid,Rizwan Ahmed Khan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces TCAR-Gen, a temporal and graph-based retrieval-augmented generation framework that improves reasoning-intensive QA over historical crime narratives by explicitly modeling time and fusing multiple evidence sources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Retrieval-augmented generation systems have difficulty performing temporal reasoning, multi-hop inference, and coherent evidence fusion, especially for complex questions over historical criminal case narratives. Existing methods either ignore query semantics in retrieval or cannot effectively integrate multiple evidence pieces, leading to poor grounding and reasoning quality. The paper aims to address these limitations and improve faithful QA in temporally rich, knowledge-grounded corpora.

Method: The authors propose Temporal Context Augmented Retrieval Generation (TCAR-Gen). It uses query-conditioned graph neural networks to build and traverse a context graph, incorporates temporal evidence fusion with a temporal penalty mechanism to prioritize time-consistent information, and applies chain-of-trees reasoning to structure multi-branch evidence paths. This combined framework conditions retrieval on the query, explicitly models temporal relations, and fuses multiple retrieved evidence branches before passing them to a language model for answer generation.

Result: On the Victorian Crime Diaries benchmark, TCAR-Gen achieves 0.3738 Recall@5 and outperforms several strong baselines, including Vanilla RAG, Temporal RAG, GraphRAG-C, and GraphRAG-T, across seven query types such as multi-hop and counterfactual questions. Ablation studies show that removing the context graph, temporal penalty mechanism, or query conditioning significantly reduces performance. Cross-model tests from GPT-OSS 20B down to TinyLlama 1.1B indicate that TCAR-Gen maintains strong retrieval coverage even with smaller models, though the generated answers themselves degrade as model size shrinks.

Conclusion: Explicit temporal modeling and structured multi-branch evidence fusion are crucial for accurate, reasoning-heavy question answering on historical, knowledge-grounded corpora. TCAR-Gen demonstrates that combining query-conditioned graph retrieval, temporal constraints, and chain-of-trees reasoning substantially improves retrieval quality and grounding, providing a more faithful basis for answer generation, though final generation quality still depends heavily on language model capacity.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation systems struggle with temporal reasoning and evidence fusion when answering complex questions over historical criminal case narratives. Existing approaches either retrieve independently of query semantics or fail to integrate multiple evidence sources coherently. We propose Temporal Context Augmented Retrieval Generation (TCAR-Gen), a framework that combines query-conditioned graph neural networks, temporal evidence fusion, and chain-of-trees reasoning to ground answer generation in retrieved evidence. On the Victorian Crime Diaries benchmark, TCAR-Gen achieves 0.3738 Recall@5, outperforming Vanilla RAG, Temporal RAG, GraphRAG-C, and GraphRAG-T across seven query types including multi-hop reasoning and counterfactual questions. Ablation studies reveal that the context graph, temporal penalty mechanism, and query conditioning are critical components. Cross-model evaluation across five language model (GPT-OSS 20B to TinyLlama 1.1B) demonstrates that TCAR-Gen maintains robust retrieval coverage at smaller model scales, though generation quality degrades substantially with reduced model capacity. Our work shows that explicit temporal modelling and multi-branch evidence fusion are essential for faithful, reasoning-intensive question answering over knowledge-grounded corpora.

</details>


### [12] [LLMs for Cardiovascular Risk Prediction from Structured Clinical Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00031)
*Jeba Maliha,Md Rafiul Kabir*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a hybrid framework that uses large language models (LLMs) to turn structured coronary artery disease (CAD) data into clinical narratives, validates their fidelity, and compares classic ML models with LLM-based CAD prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Coronary artery disease is a leading cause of death, and effective early prediction is crucial. Traditional ML handles structured data well but cannot directly interpret rich natural-language clinical descriptions, whereas LLMs excel at language but are underexplored for structured-to-text and text-based prediction in CAD. There is a gap in integrating structured clinical variables with natural-language-based models in a privacy-preserving way.

Method: Using a public CAD dataset of 1,190 patients with 11 attributes, the authors (1) convert structured variables into interpretable feature descriptions and synthetic clinical narratives using LLMs; (2) design a validation pipeline that re-extracts variables from the generated narratives and compares them to the original data, yielding a consistency (fidelity) score; and (3) benchmark four conventional ML models against LLM-based zero-shot and few-shot CAD classification using two LLMs (GPT and Gemini).

Result: The reverse-extraction validation pipeline attains an average fidelity of 94.61% between original structured data and the LLM-generated narratives. In predictive performance, Random Forest outperforms other models and surpasses LLM-based zero-shot and few-shot classification. LLMs are nevertheless able to perform CAD risk classification from purely textual patient descriptions.

Conclusion: Although Random Forest remains superior in predictive accuracy on structured CAD data, LLM-based approaches offer advantages for real-world clinical use by operating directly on natural-language descriptions without exposing sensitive numeric values. The study demonstrates that structured clinical data can be reliably transformed into high-fidelity narratives and that hybrid systems combining classic ML with LLM-generated text open promising directions for privacy-aware, language-centric clinical prediction frameworks.

Abstract: Coronary artery disease (CAD) remains one of the leading causes of death globally, highlighting the need for reliable predictive systems to support early diagnosis and risk assessment. While traditional machine learning models perform well on structured clinical data, large language models (LLMs) present new possibilities to interpret medical information expressed in natural language. In this work, we develop a hybrid framework that bridges structured clinical data and natural-language representations for CAD prediction. Using a publicly available dataset of 1,190 patient records with 11 clinical attributes, structured variables are converted into interpretable feature representations and synthetic clinical narratives using LLMs. A validation pipeline performs reverse extraction of clinical variables and computes a consistency score with the original records, achieving an average fidelity of 94.61%. We then evaluate four conventional machine learning models and compare their performance with LLM-based classification under zero-shot and few-shot prompting settings. We use two LLMs here, GPT and Gemini. Experimental results show that Random Forest achieves the highest accuracy. Despite this advantage, LLM-based classification remains beneficial in real-world clinical settings. This is because LLMs operate directly on natural language patient descriptions, meaning that sensitive numerical patient data such as exact lab values, blood pressure readings, and diagnostic codes are kept private. Findings suggest that combining structured clinical data with LLM-generated narratives can enable new directions for hybrid clinical prediction systems.

</details>


### [13] [Graph-Augmented Retrieval for Cross-Entity Financial Sentiment Analysis: A Comparative Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00062)
*Rajan Bastakoti,Sagar Bhetwal,Nirajan Acharya,Gaurav Kumar Gupta*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper compares a novel two-hop Graph-RAG architecture with a standard vector-only RAG baseline for cross-entity financial sentiment analysis, showing that graph-augmented retrieval improves entity recall and multi-entity answer relevance without harming answer quality, at the cost of modestly higher but more stable latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard vector-based RAG struggles to capture structured, multi-entity relationships that are crucial in financial market analysis, where understanding how entities influence each other is essential. The authors want to determine whether augmenting RAG with a sentiment-weighted knowledge graph and graph traversal can better support complex, relational financial queries and what trade-offs this introduces for practitioners.

Method: They build a sentiment-weighted knowledge graph from 255 news articles, covering 59 equity entities tied to 10 major technology stocks. The graph encodes INFLUENCES edges between entities with sentiment intensities. They implement a two-hop Graph-RAG that combines dense vector retrieval with intensity-filtered graph traversal to surface relational evidence. They compare this against a vector-only RAG baseline on 100 grounded queries (30 direct, 70 relational) using metrics like semantic similarity, entity recall, RAGAS-based answer quality, latency measures, and ablations over the graph traversal intensity threshold τ to study precision-coverage trade-offs.

Result: Graph-RAG significantly improves entity recall by 6.4% (p < 0.001) and increases answer relevancy by 11.7% overall, with the largest benefits (+16.1%) on relational, multi-entity questions. These improvements occur without degrading answer quality (semantic similarity change is negligible, Cohen's d = 0.078). Latency increases modestly by 22.6% on average, but latency variance drops by 80%, making response times more predictable. The ablation on the traversal intensity threshold τ shows an inverted-U relationship with answer quality, and identifies τ = 0.5 as better than the original τ = 0.7 setting.

Conclusion: Graph-augmented RAG, through sentiment-weighted knowledge graphs and controlled graph traversal, meaningfully improves retrieval of relevant entities and relational evidence in financial sentiment analysis without sacrificing answer quality, while slightly increasing but stabilizing latency. The identified optimal traversal intensity threshold (τ = 0.5) highlights a practical precision-versus-coverage trade-off and offers concrete guidance for deploying RAG systems in multi-entity financial contexts.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has become foundational for grounding large language models in domain-specific corpora, yet conventional vector-based RAG systems are fundamentally limited in their ability to capture the structured, multi-entity relationships that underpin financial market analysis. This paper presents a comprehensive comparative study of a novel two-hop Graph-RAG architecture versus a standard vector-only baseline for cross-entity financial sentiment analysis. Our system constructs a sentiment-weighted knowledge graph of 59 equity entities from 255 news articles covering 10 major technology stocks, then augments dense retrieval with intensity-filtered graph traversal over INFLUENCES edges to surface relational evidence inaccessible to vector search alone.
  We evaluate both architectures on 100 grounded queries (30 Direct, 70 Relational) using semantic similarity, entity recall, RAGAS metrics, latency benchmarks, and ablation studies. Graph-RAG achieves a statistically significant improvement in entity recall (+6.4%, p < 0.001, Wilcoxon signed-rank) and delivers substantially more relevant answers for complex multi-entity queries (+11.7% Answer Relevancy), with gains concentrating in relational question types (+16.1%). Critically, these improvements come at no measurable cost to answer quality (delta = +0.001 semantic similarity, Cohen's d = 0.078), with a modest 22.6% increase in mean latency offset by an 80% reduction in latency variance.
  An ablation study on the graph traversal intensity threshold reveals an inverted-U relationship with answer quality, identifying tau = 0.5 as optimal over the production default of tau = 0.7. These findings characterize a precision-for-coverage trade-off inherent to graph-augmented retrieval and provide actionable architectural guidance for practitioners building RAG systems for multi-entity financial analysis.

</details>


### [14] [DLLM-JEPA: Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures for Masked Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00091)
*Sangdae Nam*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: DLLM-JEPA combines Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures with masked-diffusion language models to get the benefits of JEPA-style self-supervision while cutting compute and removing the need for paired data, improving reasoning and code/text tasks over diffusion-only tuning and standard fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: JEPA has been powerful for self-supervised vision and has recently been adapted to language (LLM-JEPA), but the causal-attention setup makes it expensive: it needs explicit multi-view data pairs and two gradient-carrying passes per step. The authors want a JEPA-style objective for language that is cheaper to train, does not rely on paired modalities, and ideally improves downstream performance and forgetting behavior compared to ordinary fine-tuning.

Method: They replace the autoregressive JEPA substrate with a masked-diffusion language model (DLLM). Using bidirectional attention and different masking rates on the same text, the model naturally obtains two semantically different "views" without explicit text-code or other pairs. The architecture is trained with a JEPA objective using only a single gradient-carrying forward pass, leveraging the diffusion process as the prediction mechanism. They evaluate this DLLM-JEPA training on several backbones (e.g., LLaDA-8B, Dream-7B) and tasks (GSM8K, Spider, NL-RX-SYNTH, Django, MMLU, Wikitext) and probe internal representations layer-wise.

Result: DLLM-JEPA reduces training FLOPs by about 33% versus LLM-JEPA while removing the requirement for explicit multi-view data. Empirically, it outperforms diffusion-only fine-tuning on all tested (model, task) pairs, with gains up to +18.7 percentage points on GSM8K for LLaDA-8B and +11.4 points for Dream-7B, and consistent improvements on Spider, NL-RX-SYNTH, and Django. On LLaDA-8B (Wide-t), DLLM-JEPA simultaneously improves GSM8K accuracy, lowers held-out Wikitext loss below the base model, and maintains MMLU at base level, while a standard L2-anchored fine-tuning cannot achieve these combined benefits. Probing shows that DLLM-JEPA induces larger weight movement from the base (especially in middle layers) yet less forgetting on language modeling.

Conclusion: Pairing JEPA with masked-diffusion language models yields a more compute-efficient, data-flexible self-supervised objective for language models than LLM-JEPA and standard diffusion fine-tuning. DLLM-JEPA delivers better task performance and a favorable trade-off between specialization and forgetting, characterized by a "dual-win" where reasoning capabilities improve without harming general language modeling and knowledge benchmarks, supported by a distinctive pattern of geometric-functional drift across transformer layers.

Abstract: Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) have reshaped self-supervised representation learning in vision. The recent LLM-JEPA ported JEPA to autoregressive language models but inherited two steep costs from the causal-attention substrate: it demands explicit multi-view data (e.g., text-code pairs), and it requires two gradient-carrying forward passes per step. We introduce DLLM-JEPA, which pairs JEPA with masked-diffusion language models to eliminate both costs at once. The bidirectional attention of diffusion models yields two semantically distinct views of the same input via different masking rates -- no explicit pairs needed -- and supports a single gradient-carrying forward pass, cutting training FLOPs by 33% relative to LLM-JEPA. DLLM-JEPA improves over diffusion-only fine-tuning in every (task, architecture) combination we evaluate: up to +18.7 pp on LLaDA-8B GSM8K and +11.4 pp on Dream-7B GSM8K, with consistent positive gains on Spider, NL-RX-SYNTH, and Django. Beyond accuracy, DLLM-JEPA exhibits a dual-win property: on LLaDA-8B with the Wide-t configuration, it simultaneously raises GSM8K accuracy (67.1 vs. 65.2, +1.8 pp), drives held-out Wikitext loss below the pre-trained base, and preserves MMLU accuracy at base level across three fine-tuning seeds -- whereas an L2-to-base parameter anchor matches baseline accuracy with no task gain. Layer-wise probing reveals the mechanism: a geometric-functional drift dissociation in which the fine-tuned backbone moves further from the pre-trained weights than the baseline yet forgets less on held-out Wikitext, with the amplification concentrated in middle transformer layers. The pattern appears on Dream-7B as well, indicating the phenomenon is not specific to a single backbone.

</details>


### [15] [Agreement Metrics for LLM-as-Judge Evaluation: What to Report and Why](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00093)
*Delip Rao,Chris Callison-Burch*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper critically examines how agreement metrics are used to validate LLM judges against human annotations, showing that many commonly reported statistics are redundant in binary settings and offering guidance on what to report instead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-as-judge is increasingly used to evaluate model outputs, but validation practices are inconsistent and often poorly documented. Researchers report many different agreement metrics without clarifying scales, tie/abstention handling, or the actual meaning of each metric. This can give a misleading impression of robust validation while hiding important details such as label imbalance and abstentions. The authors aim to clarify the relationships among popular agreement measures and to standardize more informative, transparent reporting.

Method: The authors survey 24 recent LLM-as-judge papers to catalog which agreement metrics and evaluation choices they use (judgment scales, handling of ties, invalid outputs, and abstentions). They then analytically derive relationships among common agreement metrics (e.g., Pearson’s r, Spearman’s ρ, Kendall’s τb, φ, MCC, Cohen’s κ) under binary MET/UNMET criteria. They extend this analysis to settings where the judge can abstain (CANNOT_ASSESS) and to multi-judge ensembles using Fleiss’ κ and Krippendorff’s α, quantifying when different preprocessing and aggregation choices are equivalent or meaningfully different. Finally, they propose a concrete checklist for reporting LLM-judge validation results.

Result: For non-degenerate binary judgment data, the paper shows that Pearson’s r, Spearman’s ρ, Kendall’s τb, φ, and MCC collapse to a single underlying number; reporting multiple such metrics does not add evidence but only multiplies cosmetic statistics. Cohen’s κ is distinct: while it shares the same numerator as φ, its different normalization makes it sensitive to differences in positive-label rates between LLM and human annotators, so the gap between φ and κ is informative about prevalence drift. When allowing abstentions, the authors show that the three prevalent strategies (e.g., dropping abstentions, treating them as a separate category, or imputing them) answer different inferential questions and break the equivalence of binary metrics. In multi-judge settings, similar equivalence patterns reappear for Fleiss’ κ and Krippendorff’s α, up to small finite-sample corrections. The authors also derive and highlight the importance of reporting coverage and the full confusion matrix.

Conclusion: Many current LLM-as-judge validation practices use an overabundance of redundant agreement statistics without clearly specifying key design decisions, which can misrepresent the robustness and meaning of the validation. In binary, rubric-based evaluations, most rank and correlation metrics are mathematically equivalent; the focus should instead be on one representative coefficient plus Cohen’s κ, along with explicit reporting of label distributions. Abstention handling is not a neutral preprocessing choice but encodes distinct evaluation questions, and must be clearly specified. The paper recommends a concise reporting checklist: always state the judgment scale, abstention and tie handling, coverage, the confusion matrix, and the aggregation level, and then report any scalar agreement coefficients in that context.

Abstract: Validating an LLM judge against human annotations usually means reporting several agreement statistics: accuracy, precision, recall, $F_1$, Cohen's $κ$, and one or more rank correlations. A survey of 24 recent LLM-as-judge papers finds metric choice entangled with the judgment scale, tie handling, invalid outputs, and abstention handling, and those choices rarely stated. For binary criteria -- the common case in rubric-based evaluation, where each criterion is graded MET or UNMET -- most of the reported numbers are redundant: Pearson's $r$, Spearman's $ρ$, Kendall's $τ_b$, the phi coefficient $φ$, and the Matthews Correlation Coefficient all reduce to a single number on non-degenerate binary data, so reporting several of them only creates an illusion of corroborating evidence. Cohen's $κ$ is the one agreement coefficient that adds information: it shares $φ$'s numerator but normalizes differently, and the gap between them measures how far the judge's positive-label rate has drifted from the human's. We then trace what changes when a judge may abstain with a CANNOT_ASSESS verdict: the three common ways of handling abstentions are not interchangeable preprocessing choices but answer different questions, and they break the binary equivalences. The same equivalences reappear, up to a negligible finite-sample correction, for multi-judge ensembles scored with Fleiss' $κ$ or Krippendorff's $α$. We close with a reporting checklist that names the judgment scale, the abstention and tie handling mode, coverage, the confusion matrix, and the aggregation level alongside any scalar agreement coefficient.

</details>


### [16] [Enhancing BiGRU with a KAN Block for Legal Document Classification and Summarization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00116)
*Ahmed Faizul Haque Dhrubo,Souvik Pramanik,Most. Aysha Siddika Sumona,Shahnewaz Siddique,Mohammad Ashrafuzzaman Khan,Mohammad Abdul Qayum,Mohsin Sajjad*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a KAN-augmented BiGRU architecture for multilingual, low-resource legal document classification and summarization, demonstrating improved performance over baselines and showing that KAN notably boosts classification accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Legal document processing in low-resource, multilingual environments (Bengali, English, transliterated Bengali) suffers from domain-specific language, code-switching, long-range dependencies, and class imbalance. Existing classical ML and standard neural or pretrained language models often underperform under such constraints, especially when training data is limited. The authors aim to design an architecture that can handle these linguistic and data challenges while remaining effective in both classification and summarization tasks for Bangladeshi legal documents.

Method: The authors construct a dataset of Bangladeshi legal documents from Manupatra, containing Bengali, English, and transliterated Bengali. For classification, they design a BiGRU-based model augmented with a Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) module to better capture complex feature interactions. For summarization, they employ an attention-based GRU encoder-decoder architecture with a KAN module as the prediction head. They train and evaluate the models on this multilingual dataset, perform an ablation study isolating the contribution of KAN, and compare against classical ML methods and pretrained language models as baselines.

Result: The KAN-based BiGRU classification model achieves 67.96% accuracy and 0.65 F1-score. The summarization model reaches ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, and ROUGE-L F1 scores of 0.38, 0.23, and 0.31, respectively. The ablation study indicates that adding the KAN module improves classification accuracy from 57.34% to 67.96%, highlighting its contribution. The proposed approach outperforms several classical machine learning algorithms and pretrained language model baselines under the evaluated conditions.

Conclusion: KAN-augmented recurrent architectures (BiGRU for classification and attention-based GRU for summarization) are effective for low-resource, multilingual legal document processing, particularly in the Bangladeshi context with Bengali, English, and transliterated Bengali. The KAN module significantly enhances classification performance and yields competitive summarization quality, suggesting that such hybrid architectures are promising alternatives or complements to standard neural and pretrained language model approaches in specialized, low-resource domains.

Abstract: This study introduces a novel architecture of KAN-based BiGRU model for the task of classification and summarization of legal documents in a low-resource multilingual setup. In order to tackle problems associated with domain language, the usage of different languages, long dependencies within context, and class imbalance, we employ the dataset composed of legal documents from Bangladesh and taken from Manupatra, which include Bengali, English, and transliterated Bengali languages. Our classification task involves BiGRU model, along with Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) module, while the summarization part utilizes attention-based GRU, combined with a KAN model head. Classification model yields 67.96% of accuracy and 0.65 F1 score; while ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, and ROUGE-L measures for summarization yield 0.38, 0.23, and 0.31 F1 scores, correspondingly. Ablation study shows that the use of KAN increases classification accuracy from 57.34% to 67.96%. Moreover, our proposed technique is compared to several baselines, including classical ML algorithms and pretrained language models.

</details>


### [17] [RealityTest: How People Probe AI Identity and Whether Models Disclose It](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00168)
*Anna Gausen,Sarenne Wallbridge,Bessie O'Dell,Christopher Summerfield,Hannah Rose Kirk*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: RealityTest is a benchmark and dataset to evaluate whether AI systems properly disclose that they are AI across languages, modalities, and realistic conversation contexts, revealing large gaps between current evaluations and real-world behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI is widely used in conversations, but users often cannot tell if they are talking to a human or an AI. Regulators increasingly demand explicit AI disclosure, yet current tests are English-only, text-only, and rely on synthetic, machine-generated questions that may not reflect how real users ask about identity. This creates a safety and compliance blind spot.

Method: The authors build RealityTest, a large-scale benchmark and dataset of 3,152 identity-probing queries collected from about 750 human participants in 49 countries and five languages, spanning text and speech. Participants interact in ambiguous, realistic scenarios and ask questions about who/what they are talking to. The authors then evaluate 17 text models and 6 speech models on this benchmark, including conditions where a single suppression instruction discourages disclosure, and systematically analyze how phrasing, language, modality, and conversation context affect disclosure rates.

Result: Empirically, only about 31% of people ask directly about the system’s identity, and their questions are substantially more varied than synthetic, machine-generated prompts. Across 17 text and 6 speech models, disclosure performance varies widely, but all models show a marked drop in disclosure when given a single suppression instruction, with rates falling below 30% even for the best systems. Analyses show that the way a question is phrased and the surrounding conversational context have a stronger impact on whether models disclose than the choice of model itself.

Conclusion: Human-grounded, multilingual, and multimodal evaluation reveals that AI disclosure in realistic settings is fragile and highly sensitive to prompt phrasing and context, especially under simple suppression instructions. Benchmarks based on narrow or synthetic query sets can significantly misrepresent actual disclosure behavior in deployment, so safety and compliance evaluations must be built on diverse, real-user data to accurately measure and improve AI transparency.

Abstract: AI systems are increasingly deployed in conversational settings where users may be uncertain whether they are speaking with a human or an AI. Despite mounting regulatory attention to this known safety risk, existing evaluations of AI disclosure are typically English-only, based on machine-generated questions, and restricted to text. We present RealityTest to comprehensively test whether AI systems disclose their identity when asked. The benchmark is the first large-scale multimodal and multilingual evaluation, grounded in human data on how people actually encounter and question AI identity in the real-world. Alongside the benchmark, we release the underlying dataset of 3,152 identity-probing queries collected from ~750 participants across 49 countries and five languages, in text and speech scenarios. We find that only 31% of people ask about identity directly in ambiguous scenarios, and that the questions people ask are far more diverse than machine-generated queries. We test 17 text and 6 speech models, and find substantial variation in disclosure behaviour. However, a single suppression instruction reduces disclosure rates to below 30%, even in the best-performing models. Validating our investment in diverse, human-grounded evaluation data, we find that how the question is phrased and the context of the conversation matter more for disclosure than which model is being tested. Safety evaluations built on narrow or synthetic query sets risk mischaracterising how models behave in realistic deployment settings.

</details>


### [18] [BOUTEF: A Multilingual Corpus for FakeNews in North Africa -- Language as a Weapon](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00193)
*Kamel Smaili,Yassine Toughrai,Amina Laggoun,David Langlois*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents BOUTEF, a large multilingual corpus (fake/genuine news plus comments and debunking) for studying fake news in Algeria and Tunisia, and uses it to empirically analyze how misinformation spreads and differs from debunking content.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fake news is spreading rapidly on social media, and this problem is especially acute in multilingual, low-resource regions like North Africa where tools and datasets are scarce. Existing misinformation resources largely focus on high-resource languages and do not capture local dialects, code-switching, or region-specific dynamics. The authors aim to fill this gap by building a large, annotated dataset tailored to the linguistic and sociocultural realities of Algeria and Tunisia, enabling more accurate study and detection of misinformation there.

Method: The authors construct BOUTEF, a large-scale multilingual corpus that integrates three components: fake narratives, genuine narratives, and user-generated comments, each linked to verified debunking information. The dataset covers multiple language varieties (MSA, Algerian and Tunisian dialects, Arabizi, French, English, and code-switched content). Using this corpus, they run a comprehensive empirical study that mixes quantitative statistical analysis with qualitative examination. They analyze thematic categories, linguistic and rhetorical strategies, sentiment, and social engagement metrics; they also perform comparative analyses between Algeria and Tunisia.

Result: Statistical analyses show strong associations between topic categories and whether messages are fake or genuine, and they find strong correlations between user engagement (e.g., visibility, interaction) and the spread of fake content. Fake news is characterized by emotionally charged narratives, sensational framing, and mixed/hybrid language practices, which boost virality and engagement. Debunking content, by contrast, uses more factual, verification-oriented language. The cross-country comparison reveals both common patterns and distinct characteristics shaped by each country’s sociopolitical context. The released dataset is rich, annotated, and publicly accessible.

Conclusion: Informal and hybrid language practices play a key role in how misinformation spreads and is received in Algeria and Tunisia. The BOUTEF corpus offers an important new resource for studying these phenomena, improving fake news detection, and advancing NLP for low-resource, multilingual, and code-switched settings. This work underscores the need to consider local linguistic and sociocultural factors when designing systems to combat fake news and understand information disorders in complex environments.

Abstract: The rapid spread of fake news on social media has become a major challenge, particularly in multilingual and under-resourced contexts such as North Africa. In this paper, we introduce BOUTEF, a large-scale multilingual corpus designed to study the propagation, characteristics, and impact of fake news in Algeria and Tunisia. The corpus integrates three complementary components: fake narratives, genuine narratives, and associated user-generated comments, along with verified debunking information. It covers a wide range of languages and linguistic varieties, including MSA, Algerian and Tunisian dialects, Arabizi, French, English, and code-switched language. Building on this resource, we conduct a comprehensive empirical analysis combining quantitative and qualitative approaches. We examine thematic distributions, linguistic and rhetorical strategies, sentiment patterns, and social engagement dynamics. Statistical analyses reveal significant associations between thematic categories and message veracity, as well as strong correlations between user engagement and the visibility of fake content. Our findings show that fake news relies heavily on emotionally charged narratives, sensational framing, and hybrid linguistic practices that enhance virality and audience engagement. In contrast, debunking content adopts a more factual and verification-oriented style. Furthermore, a comparative analysis between Algeria and Tunisia highlights both shared dynamics and country-specific characteristics shaped by sociopolitical contexts. The results emphasize the role of informal language practices in the diffusion and reception of misinformation. By providing a rich, annotated, and publicly available dataset, this work contributes to advancing research on fake news detection, low-resource language processing, and the understanding of information disorders in complex linguistic environments.

</details>


### [19] [DeSQ: Decomposition-based SPARQL Query Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00203)
*Papa Abdou Karim Karou Diallo,Aditya Sharma,Neshat Elhami Fard,Amal Zouaq*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes DeSQ, a framework that answers questions over knowledge bases by decomposing them into atomic constraints, mapping each to SPARQL fragments, then assembling a full SPARQL query, achieving better accuracy and robustness than prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing KBQA methods either generate full formal queries (which are brittle, hard to debug, and not very explainable) or directly retrieve answers by exploring the KB (which is computationally expensive and can hallucinate answers). There is a need for a method that is both efficient and accurate while remaining interpretable and easier to evaluate.

Method: DeSQ is a KB-agnostic, three-stage framework: (1) It decomposes complex natural language questions into Atomic Constraints (ACs) that reflect the relational structure of the KB. (2) It produces a structured output consisting of SPARQL Fragments for each AC (with standardized variables and URI placeholders) and a URIs Grounding block that specifies concrete URIs for the placeholders. (3) It assembles the fragments and grounded URIs into a complete executable SPARQL query.

Result: On five major KBQA benchmarks, DeSQ outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods on four of them and exhibits better robustness to lexical variation in questions. Additionally, because it outputs structured intermediate representations, it simplifies evaluation (no need for a live KB endpoint) and enables detailed error analysis.

Conclusion: DeSQ effectively combines the advantages of query-generation and retrieval-based KBQA, providing a more accurate, robust, and interpretable framework. Its decomposition into atomic constraints, fragment-based SPARQL generation, and explicit grounding not only improves performance but also facilitates offline evaluation and fine-grained debugging and model improvement.

Abstract: Dominant approaches to Knowledge Base Question Answering (KBQA) fall into two categories. First is the generation of a formal query that suffers from brittleness and limited explainability, and the second is direct answer retrieval through KB exploration that is computationally costly and prone to hallucination. To combine the strengths of both paradigms while mitigating their respective weaknesses, we introduce DeSQ (Decomposition-based SPARQL Query Generation), a KB-agnostic framework that operates in three stages. First, it decomposes complex questions into Atomic Constraints (ACs) that mirror the relational structure of the underlying KB. Second, it generates a two-part structured output: (a) Mapping of each AC to its corresponding SPARQL Fragment, using standardized variable and URIs placeholders, and (b) URIs Grounding block describing each placeholder. Third, it assembles these fragments into a complete SPARQL query. DeSQ surpasses state-of-the-art approaches on four out of five major benchmarks and demonstrates superior robustness to lexical variation. Beyond performance gains, our framework greatly simplifies evaluation by eliminating the need for a live KB endpoint, and its structured output enables fine-grained error analysis, allowing more targeted interventions for improvement.

</details>


### [20] [Effects of Varying LLM Access on Essay Writing Behavior](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00250)
*Julia Christenson,Karin de Langis,Shirley Anugrah Hayati,Dongyeop Kang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Pilot study on how different levels of LLM access (none, limited, unlimited) affect university students’ essay quality, writing behavior, and sense of authorship.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs become common in higher education, educators need evidence on how different ways of giving students access to LLMs influence learning, writing performance, and academic integrity. Instead of simply banning or fully allowing LLMs, it is important to know whether specific forms of constrained access can support learning while preserving students’ sense of ownership over their work.

Method: Twenty‑four college students were randomly assigned to one of three conditions while writing a short essay: (1) no access to an LLM, (2) limited LLM access (at most three prompts, with each LLM response capped at 100 words), or (3) unlimited LLM access. The researchers compared overall essay quality across groups, and examined writing behaviors, prompting strategies, students’ perceived authorship/ownership of the essays, and self‑reported effects on organization and creativity.

Result: Essay quality did not differ significantly between groups. However, students’ experiences and behaviors did. In the limited‑access condition, more students felt ownership of their work (62.5% said they would submit it as independent work vs. 25% in the unlimited group), reported stronger gains in organization, and used prompts more strategically for revision. Students with unlimited access spent more time writing, produced essays that more closely resembled typical LLM output, and reported feeling less creative. These differences highlight that access level shapes how students interact with LLMs and how they view their own authorship.

Conclusion: The study concludes that simply banning or fully allowing LLMs may be suboptimal. Instead, constraining LLM access—e.g., limiting number and length of prompts—can preserve students’ sense of authorship and creativity while still providing useful AI scaffolding for organizing and revising their writing. Thoughtfully designed limits might better align LLM use with educational goals than unrestricted use or outright prohibition.

Abstract: Investigating the degree to which large language models (LLMs) affect teaching and learning in universities can help identify strategies for integrating LLMs in a way that supports, rather than undermines, student learning outcomes. This study examined how varying levels of LLM assistance affect writing performance, engagement, and perceived authorship. We report a pilot study in which 24 college students were randomly assigned to write a short essay with no LLM access, limited access (<=3 prompts, responses capped at 100 words), or unlimited access. Overall essay quality was statistically indistinguishable across groups. Yet writing behavior and perceived authorship diverged sharply: students with limited access reported higher ownership (62.5% would submit the essay as independent work, vs. 25% in the unlimited group), stronger organizational gains, and more strategic, revision-focused prompting.
