The unlimited group spent more time writing, produced essays more similar to LLM output, and reported reduced creative expression. Our findings suggest that constraining, rather than banning, LLM access may preserve authorship confidence while retaining the scaffolding benefits of AI assistance.

</details>


### [21] [Parameter Alignment Mitigates Catastrophic Forgetting in Multilingual Expert Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00284)
*Sanchit Ahuja,Terra Blevins*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how to continually pretrain multilingual language models on new language families without catastrophic forgetting, and proposes layer-aware parameter alignment strategies to balance new language acquisition and preservation of prior abilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continual pretraining on new languages is necessary to adapt large language models to more languages, but naïvely doing so leads to catastrophic forgetting of existing knowledge and capabilities, even if training is organized by language families. There is a need to understand and control parameter changes that cause forgetting, and to find practical training strategies that enable new language acquisition while preserving previous competencies across many tasks and languages.

Method: The authors connect catastrophic forgetting in multilingual continual pretraining to parameter drift across layers of the model. They then design five layer-aware parameter alignment strategies: hard layer freezing (locking certain layers during CPT), soft regularization (penalizing deviation from original parameters), post-hoc weight reversion (reverting selected layers or parameters after training), and model merging (combining parameters from the original and CPT models). These strategies are systematically evaluated against two unregularized CPT baselines. Experiments cover 32 training languages from five language families, plus held-out languages, and evaluate four axes: perplexity, reading comprehension, physical reasoning, and translation.

Result: Parameter alignment methods substantially mitigate catastrophic forgetting relative to unregularized CPT while still enabling effective acquisition of new languages. Different strategies favor different task types: layer freezing and soft regularization best preserve reading comprehension performance and broader reasoning abilities, whereas post-hoc weight reversion yields the largest gains in translation while still limiting forgetting. Overall, the methods trace a trade-off frontier between learning new family-expert capabilities and preserving prior knowledge.

Conclusion: Layer-aware parameter alignment is an effective way to manage the trade-off between new-language acquisition and preservation of prior abilities in multilingual continual pretraining. By understanding forgetting as parameter drift and constraining or correcting that drift in structured ways, practitioners can choose specific strategies—such as freezing, regularization, or post-hoc reversion—depending on whether their priority is general comprehension/reasoning or translation. The work provides a practical map of the acquisition–forgetting frontier and concrete deployment guidelines for family-expert continual pretraining of multilingual language models.

Abstract: While continual pretraining~(CPT) is a practical way to extend large language models to new languages, naïve finetuning on targeted data erodes existing capabilities through catastrophic forgetting. Organizing training around language families reduces cross-language interference but cannot alone prevent forgetting of the general knowledge needed for downstream tasks. We link this forgetting to parameter drift in multilingual CPT and present a suite of five layer-aware parameter alignment strategies: hard layer freezing, soft regularization, post-hoc weight reversion, and model merging. We systematically compare our alignment strategies against two unregularized CPT baselines on benchmarks spanning 32 training languages from five language families, plus held-out languages, across four evaluation axes: perplexity, reading comprehension, physical reasoning, and translation. Parameter alignment substantially reduces forgetting at minimal cost to language acquisition: layer freezing and regularization best preserve comprehension, whereas post-hoc reversion yields the strongest translation gains. Together, these results map the acquisition--forgetting frontier for family-expert CPT and offer practical deployment guidelines pairing each strategy to the tasks it best serves.

</details>


### [22] [Model-Based Quality Assessment for Massively Multilingual Parallel Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00285)
*Abdelaziz M. A. Ibrahim,Zihao Li,Jörg Tiedemann,Shaoxiong Ji*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how to automatically assess large multilingual parallel corpora by separating two tasks: checking whether sentence pairs are actually parallel, and estimating translation quality without references, and finds that no single model works reliably for all language directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large multilingual bitext corpora are noisy: many sentence pairs are not true translations, and others are poor-quality translations. Training strong multilingual models requires reliably filtering and scoring such data, but existing assessment tools may not generalize consistently across thousands of language pairs. The authors aim to understand and benchmark how well current embedding-based parallelism detectors and reference-free quality estimation models work across a very large set of language directions.

Method: The authors decompose the assessment problem into (1) parallelism detection using multilingual sentence embeddings, and (2) reference-free quality estimation (QE). For parallelism, they benchmark four multilingual embedding models on FLORES-200 and BOUQuET retrieval tasks, covering 6,654 source–target language directions. For QE, they benchmark nine reference-free evaluators on professionally translated FLORES-200 data across 41,412 ordered source–target directions. They then analyze reliability per direction, study ensembling strategies, and correlate performance with language coverage in model documentation.

Result: The experiments show that no single embedding model or QE model is consistently reliable across all language directions. Some directions are well handled while others perform poorly. Naive ensembles of QE models fail to improve robustness and instead tend to wash out strong signals from the best-performing models. They also find a strong correlation between the documented target-language coverage of a model and the QE scores it produces, indicating that models work better on languages they explicitly support.

Conclusion: Multilingual parallel-data assessment cannot rely on a single universal metric or model across all language directions. Instead, it should be treated as a direction-aware routing and calibration problem: different language pairs may require different models or configurations, and systems should dynamically choose and calibrate metrics depending on the specific source–target direction to obtain reliable parallelism and quality judgments.

Abstract: Large-scale multilingual bitext often contains two distinct problems: non-parallel sentence pairs and low-quality translations. We decompose model-based assessment for such data into two independent components: parallelism assessment with multilingual embeddings and reference-free quality estimation (QE). For parallelism, we benchmark four embedding models on FLORES-200 and BOUQuET retrieval tasks, covering 6,654 source--target directions in our target language-pair inventory. For QE, we evaluate nine reference-free evaluators on professional FLORES-200 translations across 41,412 ordered source--target directions. Results show that no model is universally reliable across translation directions. Naive QE ensembles dilute strong model signals, while documented target-language coverage is strongly associated with higher QE scores. Overall, these findings suggest that multilingual parallel-data assessment is best approached as a direction-aware routing and calibration problem, where no single universal metric is expected to suffice across all languages.

</details>


### [23] [Uncovering Temporal Framing in the News](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00294)
*Tarek Mahmoud,Veronika Solopova,Premtim Sahitaj,Ariana Sahitaj,Max Upravitelev,Mervat Abassy,Hana Fatima Shaikh,Neda Foroutan,Vera Schmitt,Preslav Nakov*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces and studies “temporal framing” in news, showing that persuasive uses of time-related language (past, present, future) form identifiable patterns that can be detected automatically from text.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing work on time in language mostly treats temporal expressions as factual markers placing events on a timeline. However, in news discourse, references to time are also used rhetorically to frame events (e.g., invoking the past to justify present actions or projecting futures to persuade readers). There was no systematic taxonomy, dataset, or computational study of these persuasive temporal uses, limiting our understanding of how time is used as a framing device in media.

Method: The authors (1) define “temporal framing” as persuasive use of temporal language beyond mere chronology; (2) develop a taxonomy of eight temporal frames based on prior work on temporality and framing; (3) construct and expert-annotate a multilingual corpus (English and German news) with sentence-level temporal frame labels, yielding over 2K framed sentences and ~3K annotations from a base of 20K+ sentences; (4) analyze statistics such as frame prevalence, co-occurrence patterns, and lexical cues; and (5) run computational experiments using supervised fine-tuning and zero-shot classification models to detect temporal framing automatically.

Result: They obtain a labeled corpus of 458 news articles with detailed temporal framing annotations. Analysis reveals which temporal frames are most common, how different frames co-occur within articles, and what lexical markers correlate with specific frames. In machine learning experiments, supervised models trained on the annotated data can reliably identify temporal framing at the sentence level and substantially outperform zero-shot classification approaches, indicating that temporal framing is a learnable phenomenon for current NLP models.

Conclusion: Temporal references in news should be understood not only as neutral temporal markers but also as structured rhetorical devices—“temporal frames”—that influence interpretation. The authors’ eight-frame taxonomy, multilingual annotated corpus, and baseline detection models demonstrate that temporal framing is both conceptually tractable and computationally detectable. By releasing the corpus, they enable further research on how time-related language shapes media narratives and persuasion.

Abstract: Temporal language does more than place events on a timeline. In news discourse, references to the past, present, and future can function as rhetorical devices that shape interpretation and persuasion. Here, we study temporal framing, defined as the persuasive use of time-related language to structure meaning rather than to report chronology. We propose a taxonomy of eight temporal frames grounded in prior work on temporality and framing, and we realize it through expert annotation of a multilingual news corpus. The resulting dataset includes 458 English and German news articles, with over 2K temporally framed sentences and approximately 3K temporal framing annotations identified from a corpus of more than 20K sentences. We analyze frame prevalence, co-occurrence patterns, and lexical cues, and evaluate temporal framing detection using supervised fine-tuning and zero-shot classification. Our experiments show that temporal framing is learnable at the sentence level, with supervised models substantially outperforming zero-shot approaches. We publicly release the corpus to support future research on temporal framing: https://mbzuai-nlp.github.io/temporal-framing/.

</details>


### [24] [Bridging Reasoning Trajectories in On-Policy Distillation via Near-Future Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00305)
*Yuxuan Jiang,Francis Ferraro*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper identifies a limitation of standard On-Policy Distillation (OPD) for reasoning LLMs—its supervision is token-level despite using trajectory samples—and proposes Trajectory-aware OPD (TOPD), which uses near-future trajectory information to better detect and repair true reasoning divergences, yielding notable accuracy gains on math benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard OPD samples full reasoning trajectories from the student model but applies supervision at the token level via reverse KL on high-loss tokens. This mismatch means OPD often reacts to surface-form differences rather than genuine reasoning errors, and it struggles to fix short-horizon distributional drift where small early deviations compound into incorrect reasoning. The authors are motivated to design a distillation method that is sensitive to trajectory-level divergences and can more effectively align the student’s reasoning process with the teacher’s.

Method: The authors first empirically analyze standard OPD, showing that many high-loss tokens occur in low-divergence states (surface-form mismatches) and that genuine reasoning failures involve short-horizon drift not well handled by isolated token corrections. They then propose Trajectory-aware OPD (TOPD), which (1) uses near-future segments of the trajectory to detect whether a state is truly divergent from the teacher, and (2) spreads the supervision signal across multiple future tokens in the trajectory rather than focusing only on individual high-loss tokens. They also evaluate a simpler variant that only suppresses non-divergent high-loss tokens within vanilla OPD.

Result: Empirical results show that filtering out non-divergent high-loss tokens already boosts standard OPD’s average accuracy from 47.8% to 48.2%. The full TOPD method further improves average accuracy to 52.2%. On specific math reasoning benchmarks, AIME24 accuracy increases from 60.0% to 63.3%, and AIME25 from 46.7% to 53.3%, demonstrating that trajectory-aware supervision provides substantially better reasoning alignment than token-only OPD.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that conventional OPD’s token-level supervision, despite using trajectory samples, is fundamentally limited for aligning reasoning trajectories between teacher and student models. Many high-loss tokens are not indicative of real reasoning divergence, and true failures arise from short-horizon drift that token-local corrections cannot reliably fix. By incorporating near-future trajectory information to identify genuinely divergent states and distributing guidance over multiple tokens, TOPD yields significantly better reasoning performance, suggesting that effective distillation for reasoning LLMs must be trajectory-aware rather than purely token-based.

Abstract: On-Policy Distillation (OPD) improves large language model reasoning by training a student model on trajectories sampled from its own policy under teacher supervision. Although OPD operates on trajectories, its learning signal remains token-level: it identifies deviations through high-loss tokens and repairs them through local reverse-KL correction. We show that this "trajectory-sampled but token-learned" mechanism cannot reliably bridge student trajectories toward teacher trajectories. About 30% of high-loss tokens fall into the low-divergence regime, indicating that many are surface-form mismatches rather than real reasoning forks. Moreover, even truly divergent tokens are difficult to repair with isolated token-level supervision, since reasoning failures often unfold as short-horizon distributional drift. We propose Trajectory-aware OPD (TOPD), which uses near-future trajectory information to identify real divergent states and distribute guidance across multiple future tokens. Experiments show that suppressing non-divergent high-loss tokens improves standard OPD from 47.8% to 48.2% average accuracy, while TOPD further improves performance to 52.2%, with gains on AIME24 from 60.0% to 63.3% and AIME25 from 46.7% to 53.3%.

</details>


### [25] [Which Institutional Frameworks Do Chatbots Assume? Auditing Jurisdictional Defaults in Multilingual LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00333)
*Zhizhi Wang,Harini Suresh*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how large language models implicitly choose a legal or administrative jurisdiction (e.g., U.S. vs. China) based only on the language of the user’s question, and shows that this causes systematic mismatches between user intent and the institutional framework used in the answer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are increasingly used to answer practical questions about laws, regulations, and administrative procedures. For multilingual users, the language they type in may not match the jurisdiction whose rules apply to them. Prior work showed that prompt language shifts cultural or political content, but it is unclear what legal-administrative frameworks LLMs assume when the jurisdiction is not specified. Understanding this behavior is important because incorrect jurisdictional assumptions can mislead users seeking institutional guidance.

Method: The authors audit seven widely deployed LLMs (developed in the U.S. or China) using 60 legal-administrative prompts posed in both English and Mandarin Chinese, without specifying jurisdiction. They test three different system-prompt conditions and collect a total of 2,520 responses. Human annotators then label which jurisdictional or institutional framework each answer appears to use (e.g., U.S.-specific, China-specific, comparative, or generic). They compare patterns across models, languages, and prompting conditions.

Result: Across models and system-prompt conditions, prompts in Chinese are more likely to elicit China-specific answers, while prompts in English are more likely to yield U.S.-specific, comparative, or generic answers. When prompts are structured to demand a single concrete answer, this language-jurisdiction linkage becomes stronger: on average, 74.5% of English-language responses use a U.S. framework, whereas 53.3% of Chinese-language responses use a China framework. All seven models show this same directional bias linking input language to jurisdictional framework.

Conclusion: The authors define this behavior as an “institutional-framework misselection risk”: LLMs frequently and systematically treat input language as a proxy for legal-administrative jurisdiction, which can cause fluent but misleading answers when the user’s preferred language does not match the applicable jurisdiction. They argue that LLM interfaces should not infer jurisdiction from language alone. Instead, when a user’s location or legal context is missing, systems should explicitly ask for it or clearly indicate the jurisdictional scope and limits of their answers.

Abstract: LLMs increasingly answer questions about taxes, labor protections, healthcare, education, pensions, and administrative procedures, where usefulness often depends on the applicable jurisdiction. Multilingual users may write in their most comfortable language rather than one associated with the country or region whose rules apply. We ask whether deployed LLMs use input language as a default jurisdictional signal when prompts omit any country or region. Prior multilingual audits show that prompt language can shift cultural, political, or normative outputs; we examine which legal-administrative framework models supply when jurisdiction is underspecified. We evaluate seven LLMs developed in the United States or China on 60 underspecified legal-administrative prompts in English and Mandarin Chinese under three system-prompt conditions, yielding 2,520 manually annotated responses. Across models and conditions, Chinese input more often produces China-specific answers, while English input more often produces U.S.-specific, comparative, or generic answers. Prompts requiring a single answer further increase jurisdiction selection: pooled across models, 74.5% of English-input responses adopt a U.S. framework, while 53.3% of Chinese-input responses adopt a China framework. This directional pattern appears in all seven models. We describe this deployment-level pattern as institutional-framework misselection risk: a fluent answer may rely on a legal-administrative context the user did not intend, especially when their preferred language differs from the relevant jurisdiction. LLM interfaces should not route institutional advice by input language alone; when location is absent, they should request it or state the jurisdictional scope of the answer.

</details>


### [26] [Isolating LLM Lexical Bias: A Curation-Free Triangulated Metric for Preference-Stage Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00334)
*Xiaoyang Ming,Jose Hernandez,Thomas Stephan Juzek*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces an automatic metric, Triangulated Preference Shift (TPS), to measure how preference-learning methods like RLHF change a language model’s lexical behavior, showing that such training can introduce systematic lexical biases and potentially push models toward a prestige-like language register.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large Language Models often exhibit lexical quirks after preference learning (e.g., RLHF), such as overusing certain words or formats that are not present in the base models. These shifts can misalign model outputs from natural language usage and may affect trust and usability. Existing studies of such lexical misalignment rely heavily on manual analysis and curation, which is not scalable and can be subjective. There is a need for an automated, principled way to isolate and quantify the lexical changes specifically caused by preference learning, distinct from the base model’s behavior or human norms.

Method: The authors propose the Triangulated Preference Shift (TPS) score, which compares lexical behavior across three sources: (1) human gold-standard language data, (2) base (preference-untuned) models, and (3) instruction-tuned or preference-tuned variants of those models. By triangulating across these three, TPS isolates which lexical shifts are specifically induced by preference learning. They compute this metric for six different model families and analyze how preference training affects word choices and stylistic patterns, checking whether these shifts move models toward a more prestige-like language variety as discussed in sociolinguistic literature.

Result: Across six model families, TPS reveals systematic lexical shifts that emerge only after preference learning, including increased usage of particular words and formats that are not present to the same extent in human data or base models. The analysis suggests that preference training can bias models toward a prestige-like register—more formal, standardized, or elite-coded language—rather than toward average natural language use. The metric successfully surfaces these biases without manual curation, demonstrating its utility as an automated diagnostic tool.

Conclusion: Triangulated Preference Shift is a practical automated metric for quantifying how preference-learning stages like RLHF alter a language model’s lexical behavior. The study shows that preference learning can introduce nontrivial lexical biases and push models toward a prestige-like register, which raises important considerations for alignment and fairness. TPS can help researchers and developers diagnose and potentially mitigate such unintended shifts, contributing to more transparent and trustworthy AI systems.

Abstract: Various language domains have undergone remarkable changes in recent years; these shifts are largely attributed to the advent of Large Language Models and their misalignment with natural language usage. These misalignments are thought to partly originate in the preference-learning stage, e.g. Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback, which generally makes models more useful but simultaneously may introduce systematic lexical bias. In terms of lexical behavior, this is visible in a model's preference for certain formats or the overuse of words (delve, furthermore), even when such patterns are not present in base model outputs. Research on lexical misalignment induced during preference training is constrained by reliance on manual curation. We address this, by introducing the Triangulated Preference Shift score, a metric that triangulates between human gold standards, base models, and instruct variants to isolate shifts induced specifically by preference learning, without manual curation. We provide data across six model families, anchor the results in the literature, and illustrate the general approach's utility by analyzing whether preference learning shifts models toward what could be interpreted as a "language of prestige". The metric provides an initial automated method to quantify behavioral shifts attributable to preference tuning, and thus, may help inform model alignment and development of trustworthy AI.

</details>


### [27] [How Far Do Auto-Interpretation Labels Generalize: A Controlled Study Across Languages, Scripts, and Rewordings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00356)
*Sripad Karne*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates whether natural-language labels for sparse autoencoder (SAE) features truly reflect underlying concepts across languages and scripts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Auto-generated descriptions of SAE features are widely used to interpret what internal features of language models represent, but it is unclear if these labels generalize beyond the high-resource language (usually English) on which they are produced. There is a need to test whether labeled features that appear semantic in one language still capture the same concept across different languages and scripts.

Method: The authors use Serbian digraphia—Serbian written in both Latin and Cyrillic with deterministic transliteration—as a controlled setting. They compare which SAE features are activated by semantically equivalent content across different languages, scripts, and paraphrases, measuring overlap via Jaccard similarity to quantify cross-lingual semantic consistency. They then evaluate automatic natural-language labels for these features, checking whether a feature whose label indicates a given concept actually activates for that concept in Serbian Latin and Cyrillic, and how this varies with model depth and data representation in training.

Result: They show that SAE feature sets activated by equivalent content across languages and scripts share substantial overlap, with peak Jaccard similarity of 0.57 compared to a random baseline of 0.13, indicating real cross-lingual semantic features in the model. However, the auto-interpretation labels frequently fail to track the concept across forms: features labeled with semantic meanings miss the corresponding meaning in Serbian up to four times more often than in English, and miss it more in Serbian Cyrillic than in Serbian Latin despite the two being deterministically transliterable. The discrepancy increases with network depth, and the labels themselves provide no signal that they are failing in these cases.

Conclusion: SAE features can genuinely encode cross-lingual semantic concepts, but the natural-language labels automatically assigned to them do not reliably generalize across languages and scripts. These labels tend to describe how features behave on well-represented inputs (like English) rather than encoding the underlying concept in a language-agnostic way. Therefore, users should be cautious when interpreting such labels as universal explanations of model features, especially for underrepresented languages and scripts and at deeper network layers.

Abstract: Sparse autoencoder (SAE) features are increasingly used to interpret language models, with auto-generated natural-language labels serving as the primary interface for understanding what each feature represents. We ask whether these labels generalize: does a feature labeled for a concept actually track that concept across languages and scripts? Using Serbian digraphia as a controlled testbed -- the same language written in both Latin and Cyrillic via deterministic transliteration -- we first find that SAE feature sets activated by the same content in different languages, scripts, and wordings share substantial overlap (peak Jaccard similarity 0.57 vs.\ 0.13 random baseline), suggesting genuine cross-lingual semantic features. We then test whether auto-interpretation labels keep pace. They often do not: features whose labels describe semantic content miss the same meaning in Serbian up to $4\times$ more often than within English, and miss Serbian Cyrillic more than Serbian Latin -- two scripts that are deterministic transliterations of each other -- suggesting the failures track how well each form is represented in training. The gap grows with network depth, yet the labels give no indication that they fail. These results suggest that auto-interpretation labels may reflect a feature's behavior on well-represented inputs rather than the concept itself.

</details>


### [28] [Masking Stale Observations Helps Search Agents -- Until It Doesn't: A Regime Map and Its Mechanism](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00408)
*Haoxiang Zhang,Qixin Xu,Zhuofeng Li,Lei Zhang,Pengcheng Jiang,Yu Zhang,Julian McAuley*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies when and why masking older observations in long-horizon search agents helps, finding an asymmetric inverted-U relation between masking benefits and base model accuracy, driven by the interaction between retriever strength and model capacity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon search agents make many tool calls and thus accumulate large contexts, straining limited context windows. A simple fix is to mask (hide) older, likely-irrelevant observations, but it is not clear under what conditions this improves performance, how it interacts with retrievers and model size, and what mechanisms underlie its effects. The authors aim to systematically characterize this behavior to guide context management in agentic search systems.

Method: They perform a systematic empirical study across multiple agent backbones ranging from 4B to 284B parameters and three different retrievers, evaluating both on offline and live-web agentic search benchmarks. They apply an observation-masking strategy that hides stale retrieved content as trajectories progress, then measure accuracy and behavior changes. They analyze how the impact of masking varies with model accuracy, retriever recall, and the model’s implicit filtering capacity, and they inspect attention patterns and page-reopening statistics to understand the mechanism.

Result: They observe that the accuracy gains from masking follow an asymmetric inverted-U curve as a function of the model’s baseline accuracy without masking: there is little gain with weak retrievers, maximal gains when a strong retriever is paired with a mid-sized model, and a sharp drop in performance when models are already saturated (very strong). The findings indicate that masking’s effectiveness depends critically on the interaction between retriever recall and the model’s own ability to filter relevant information, rather than either dimension alone. They further identify that masking effectively trades tokens allocated to old observations for additional interaction turns, by removing content that the model had largely stopped attending to and pages the agent rarely re-opens.

Conclusion: Observation masking is not a universally beneficial context-management strategy but a regime-dependent intervention. It is most useful when a strong retriever feeds large amounts of content to a mid-capacity model that needs help managing context, and it can hurt when applied to already-saturated high-capacity models. The work reframes context management as navigating a token-for-turn trade-off and provides a holistic lens for understanding context use in agentic deep search. The authors release their scaffold and trajectories to facilitate future research on context management and long-horizon agent behavior.

Abstract: Long-horizon search agents accumulate large amounts of retrieved content across many tool calls, making context-budget efficiency increasingly important. A minimal intervention is to mask stale observations from the context as the trajectory progresses, but it remains unclear when this form of context management helps and why. We study observation masking through a systematic sweep over various agent backbones (4B to 284B parameters) and three retrievers on offline and live-web agentic search benchmarks. We find that the accuracy gain from masking follows an asymmetric inverted-U shape when plotted against the model's accuracy without context management: a plateau under weak retrievers, a peak when a strong retriever meets a mid-capacity model, and a sharp collapse when the model is saturated. This pattern reflects the interaction between retriever recall and the model's implicit filtering capacity, rather than either factor in isolation. Mechanistically, masking implements a token-for-turn trade-off: it removes observations the model has largely stopped attending to and pages the agent rarely re-opens. The added turns help when they convert failures into successes, but they fail when masking removes evidence the model would otherwise have used. We therefore reframe context management as a regime-dependent intervention and provide a holistic perspective for analyzing context use in agentic deep search. We release our scaffold and trajectories here (https://github.com/i-DeepSearch/observation-masking) to support future research.

</details>


### [29] [ProtStructQA: A Denotation Threshold in Protein Structural Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00451)
*Aravind Mandiga,Guoming Li,Jin Lu,Ismailcem Budak Arpinar,Khaled Rasheed,Samuel E. Aggrey*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ProtStructQA is a large executable benchmark that turns natural-language questions about protein 3D structures into typed DSL programs run on AlphaFold models, revealing when LLMs can map language into precise structural measurements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing protein-language evaluations mostly judge whether generated text sounds biologically plausible, which is fuzzy. Structural questions, however, have precise semantics: numeric measurements in 3D coordinates. The authors want a sharper, executable way to test whether language models truly understand protein structure by seeing if they can translate natural-language queries into correct structural operations and measurements.

Method: They construct ProtStructQA, where each question is backed by a hidden typed DSL program whose execution on an AlphaFold-predicted structure yields the ground-truth answer. They generate 382.2K questions across multiple structural aspects (confidence, distances, PAE, solvent exposure, secondary structure, topology, contacts) with both active benchmark questions and a hard-negative robustness pool. They then evaluate multiple sizes of Qwen3 (0.6B–8B) and Gemma-3 models, using several prompting and reasoning strategies: direct prompting, chain-of-thought, grammar-constrained executable voting, executable voting with chain-of-thought, and multi-turn ReAct-style tool use. Analyses include capability thresholds, parse failures, and family-level behavior across question types.

Result: They observe a clear capability-dependent threshold between Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen3-4B. Smaller models frequently fail to produce executable denotations, making tool-mediated ReAct strategies superior. Larger models cross a transition where they reliably produce parseable, executable structural expressions, and in this regime chain-of-thought becomes highly beneficial and outperforms other strategies on most question splits. Grammar constraints and execution-based strategies remain particularly useful for PAE and secondary-structure questions. Similar qualitative findings hold for Gemma-3 models.

Conclusion: ProtStructQA reframes scientific QA as compilation from natural language to executable structural measurements, offering a precise, programmatic test of when LLMs can ground words in 3D protein geometry. The benchmark reveals a structural understanding threshold in model scale where language becomes reliably compilable to executable denotations, and it shows that the utility of chain-of-thought and tool use is strongly capability-dependent. This resource provides a diagnostic framework for studying and improving LLMs’ ability to perform accurate, grounded protein-structure reasoning.

Abstract: Protein-language systems are often evaluated by whether they generate plausible biological text, but a structural question has a sharper semantics: it denotes a measurement in a 3D coordinate system. We introduce ProtStructQA, an executable benchmark for protein structural question answering in which each natural-language question is generated from a hidden typed domain-specific language (DSL) program and the answer is obtained by executing that program on an AlphaFold-predicted structure. ProtStructQA releases 382.2K questions covering confidence, distances, predicted aligned error (PAE), solvent exposure, secondary structure, topology and contacts, and held-out compositions: a 330K active benchmark over 10K proteins from four species, plus a 52.2K hard-negative robustness pool. Without fine-tuning, we evaluate Qwen3 models from 0.6B to 8B under direct prompting, chain-of-thought, grammar-constrained executable voting, executable voting with chain-of-thought, and multi-turn ReAct-style tool use, and replicate the headline finding on Gemma-3-1B and Gemma-3-12B. We find a capability-dependent denotation threshold between Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen3-4B: below it, tool-mediated ReAct dominates because models often fail to produce executable denotations; above it, chain-of-thought flips from mostly harmful to strongly beneficial and becomes the strongest strategy on most splits. Parse-failure and family-level analyses show that the threshold is a transition from unparseable language to executable structural denotation, while grammar and execution remain selectively valuable for PAE and secondary-structure queries. ProtStructQA reframes scientific QA as compilation from language to measurement and provides a diagnostic testbed for when language models can map words to executable 3D structural measurements.

</details>


### [30] [SALSA: Speech Aware LLM Adaptation via Learned Steering Activation Vectors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00460)
*Yekaterina Yegorova,Argyrios Gerogiannis,Haolong Zheng,Julia Hockenmaier,Chang D. Yoo,Mark A. Hasegawa-Johnson*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SALSA is a lightweight method to adapt speech-aware LLMs to new domains by learning layer-wise steering vectors, leading to large gains in ASR tasks, especially by steering later encoder layers rather than the LLM decoder.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Speech-aware LLMs struggle to generalize to out-of-domain scenarios such as children’s speech, multilingual speech, and code-switching. Existing steering methods typically rely on contrastive activation differences and may not optimally adapt internal representations for new domains. There is a need for an efficient, supervised way to steer internal model activations so that higher-level acoustic and phonetic features align better with the pretrained language model’s representation space, improving ASR without full model retraining.

Method: SALSA (Speech-Aware LLM Adaptation via Learned Steering Activations) introduces learnable, layer-wise steering vectors that are added to model activations. Instead of constructing these vectors from contrastive examples, SALSA directly optimizes them using a supervised loss on downstream ASR tasks. The method explores steering at different parts of the speech-aware model, including the encoder and the LLM backbone, with a focus on which layers benefit most from adaptation.

Result: On benchmarks covering children’s speech, multilingual speech, and Mandarin-English code-switching, SALSA significantly improves over zero-shot inference and speech in-context learning baselines, with up to 46.8% relative performance gain compared to zero-shot. Empirical analysis indicates that steering the encoder, particularly its later layers, produces larger improvements than steering the LLM backbone/decoder.

Conclusion: Learned steering activations provide an efficient and effective way to adapt speech-aware LLMs to challenging, out-of-domain ASR settings. The key gains arise from adjusting higher-level acoustic and phonetic encoder representations so they better match the pretrained language model’s representation space, rather than altering the decoder itself. This suggests future adaptation methods should focus on encoder-side representation alignment for robust speech-aware LLM performance across diverse domains.

Abstract: Speech-aware large language models often generalize poorly to out-of-domain settings. We propose SALSA (Speech-Aware LLM Adaptation via Learned Steering Activations), a lightweight adaptation method that learns layer-wise steering vectors. Unlike commonly used steering approaches that rely on contrastive activation differences, SALSA directly optimizes steering vectors using a supervised objective. Across children's speech, multilingual speech, and Mandarin-English code-switching benchmarks, SALSA substantially improves performance over zero-shot inference and speech in-context learning baselines, achieving up to 46.8% relative improvements over zero-shot. Analysis further demonstrates that steering the encoder, particularly the later layers, is more effective than steering the LLM backbone. These findings suggest that steering improves downstream ASR performance by adapting higher-level acoustic and phonetic representations to better align with the pretrained language model representation space, rather than by modifying the decoder itself.

</details>


### [31] [Short-form Text Rewriting with Phi Silica](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00462)
*Divya Tadimeti,Shawn Pan,Sameera Lanka,Chenghui Zhou,Sadid Hasan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how to adapt a small language model for accurate, low‑hallucination short-form text rewriting using specialized data, prompts, and efficient fine-tuning, showing it can rival larger cloud models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Short-form text rewriting (like rewriting slide bullets) is challenging because the text is short, dense in meaning, and leaves little room for variation. Large models handle paraphrasing relatively well, but small language models often lose meaning or hallucinate, which is problematic in precision-critical applications. The authors want to see how to systematically adapt a small model so it can perform these rewrites reliably, reducing dependence on larger, remote models.

Method: They start from a specific small language model, Phi Silica. They (1) curate a dataset of short, presentation-style texts from public slide decks; (2) use a stronger model, GPT-5-chat, to generate supervised rewrite examples and to act as an automatic judge; (3) perform prompt distillation, transferring effective prompting patterns from the large model to the small one; and (4) apply parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods to adapt Phi Silica to the rewrite task. They then evaluate the adapted model using LLM-as-a-judge comparisons against GPT-5-chat rewrites, focusing on semantic fidelity, hallucination rate, and preference win rate.

Result: After fine-tuning with their curated data and methods, the adapted Phi Silica achieves higher semantic fidelity to the source texts, shows fewer hallucinations, and wins more pairwise preferences when its rewrites are compared to those of GPT-5-chat under LLM-as-a-judge evaluation. Quantitatively, these improvements narrow the performance gap between the small on-prem model and the stronger cloud model for this specific rewrite task.

Conclusion: Task-targeted adaptation—via curated short-form data, distilled prompts, and parameter-efficient fine-tuning—can make small language models much more reliable for precision-critical short-form rewriting, significantly closing the gap to larger cloud models. This provides a practical recipe for organizations that need controllable, efficient SLMs for high-fidelity rewriting instead of relying solely on large, external LLMs.

Abstract: Short-form text rewriting is a constrained variant of paraphrasing in which limited context and high semantic density leave little room for variation. While large language models perform well on general paraphrasing, small language models (SLMs) often struggle with semantic fidelity and hallucination robustness in short-form settings. In this work, we present an empirical study of adapting an SLM, Phi Silica, for short-form rewrite through dataset curation, prompt distillation, parameter-efficient fine-tuning, and evaluation. We curate a dataset of short presentation-style text from public slide decks and use GPT-5-chat both to generate rewrite supervision and to conduct LLM-as-a-judge evaluation. Our results show that finetuning improves semantic fidelity, reduces hallucinations, and increases preference win rate against GPT-5-chat rewrites. The findings suggest that targeted adaptation for SLMs can substantially narrow the gap to cloud models and provide practical guidance for adapting SLMs to precision-critical rewrite tasks.

</details>


### [32] [On the Limits of LLM Adaptability: Impact of Model-Internalized Priors on Annotation Task Performance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00467)
*Etienne Casanova,Rafal Kocielnik,R. Michael Alvarez*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how well large language models can be steered by prompts when used as zero-shot annotators or judges, focusing on toxicity detection. It finds that many zero-shot errors cannot be fixed by adding more information to prompts, and introduces a new metric, Definition-Specific Familiarity (DSF), which better explains performance than memorization measures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used for zero-shot annotation and for judging content, it is critical to understand when their internal notions of a concept (e.g., toxicity) match the definitions users provide, and whether prompting can reliably correct mistakes. Existing work often assumes that better prompts or more task familiarity can fix errors, but it is unclear how much internal priors and memorization vs. alignment with task definitions really drive performance.

Method: The authors conduct experiments on toxicity detection across multiple domains (social media, gaming, news, and forums) using both dense and mixture-of-experts LLMs. They examine three aspects: (1) the effect of model familiarity with data and task definitions on performance; (2) how often additional prompt information can correct initial zero-shot errors, quantified as a rescue rate and characterized via “decision stickiness”; and (3) how models behave when given misaligned task definitions. They introduce Definition-Specific Familiarity (DSF) to quantify how closely the model’s internal concept aligns with the provided task definition, and compare DSF’s predictive power against various text-level memorization metrics (ROUGE-L, BERTScore, and embedding cosine similarity).

Result: Experiments show that about two-thirds of zero-shot errors cannot be corrected by adding more information to prompts, yielding an overall rescue rate of only 34.8%; high-confidence errors are especially resistant. When supplied with misaligned task definitions, LLMs comply with those definitions while keeping their confidence levels similar to the aligned setting. DSF is positively correlated with performance (partial r = +0.41) after controlling for dataset-level factors, whereas multiple memorization metrics fail to exhibit a positive correlation with performance.

Conclusion: Prompting has limited power to fix LLM zero-shot annotation errors, particularly those made with high confidence. The key factor for reliable LLM-as-a-judge behavior is not text-level memorization but how well the model’s internal concept matches the intended task definition, as captured by DSF. Thus, improving definition alignment is more important than relying on prompt engineering or data memorization to enhance annotation reliability.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used for zero-shot annotation and LLM-as-a-judge tasks, yet their reliability hinges on how model-internalized priors interact with user-provided instructions. We investigate three dimensions of this interaction: (1) how an LLM's familiarity with data and task definitions affects performance, (2) the extent to which additional information in prompts can correct zero-shot errors ("decision stickiness"), and (3) model susceptibility to misaligned task definitions. Through experiments on toxicity detection across diverse datasets (spanning social media, gaming, news, and forums) using both dense and mixture-of-experts models, we find that nearly two-thirds of zero-shot errors are resistant to correction, with an overall rescue rate (fraction of initial errors corrected by prompting) of only 34.8%. High-confidence errors prove especially resistant to correction. When given misaligned definitions, LLMs follow them while maintaining confidence levels unchanged from the aligned condition. Crucially, we introduce Definition-Specific Familiarity (DSF), which measures alignment between a model's internal concept and the task definition. After controlling for dataset-level confounds, DSF shows a positive association with model performance (partial r = +0.41), while three distinct memorization metrics (ROUGE-L, BERTScore, and embedding cosine similarity) all fail to show a positive association. These findings show the limitations of prompt-based correction in annotation tasks, highlighting the importance of definition alignment over text-level memorization.

</details>


### [33] [Do Text Edits Generalize to Visual Generation? Benchmarking Cross-Modal Knowledge Editing in UMMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00477)
*Xin Gao,Cheng Yang,Chufan Shi,Taylor Berg-Kirkpatrick*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces UniKE, the first benchmark and analysis of how well knowledge edits in unified multimodal models transfer from text to image generation, revealing a large modality gap and proposing a reasoning-augmented editing method to partially close it.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unified multimodal models are increasingly used in real-world applications and thus need mechanisms to update or correct their internal knowledge. While knowledge editing is fairly mature for text-only models, it is unknown whether such edits work consistently across modalities in unified multimodal systems, especially for image generation. This raises the need to systematically evaluate cross-modality knowledge editing and understand why textual edits may fail to affect visual outputs.

Method: The authors build UniKE, a benchmark with 2,971 editing cases (covering attribute and relational facts) for unified multimodal models. They test existing knowledge editing methods on text outputs and then evaluate whether the edited knowledge is reflected in generated images using VQA-based automatic visual verification. They analyze the performance gap between modalities and introduce Reasoning-augmented Parameter Editing, which explicitly activates or reasons over the edited knowledge before image generation. Mechanistic probing is used to examine alignment between edited textual representations and the model’s visual conditioning pathways.

Result: Experiments show that knowledge edits are highly effective for text (around 92% success rate) but transfer poorly to image generation, where the best VQA-verified accuracy under direct image generation is only 18.5%. The proposed Reasoning-augmented Parameter Editing improves image-side VQA accuracy for all tested model-editor combinations, with gains of up to 18.6 percentage points. Mechanistic analysis indicates that the gap is due to only partial alignment between the edited text representations and the internal circuits that condition image synthesis, causing edits that are sufficient for text to be too weak or misaligned for vision.

Conclusion: Textual knowledge editing in unified multimodal models does not reliably propagate to image generation, revealing a substantial modality gap. Effective cross-modality knowledge editing requires modality-aware designs that better align edited textual knowledge with visual conditioning pathways. The UniKE benchmark and the proposed Reasoning-augmented Parameter Editing method provide a first systematic framework and baseline for studying and improving such multimodal knowledge updates.

Abstract: Unified multimodal models (UMMs) have emerged as a promising paradigm for general-purpose multimodal intelligence. As they are deployed in real-world applications, effectively updating internal knowledge becomes critical. While knowledge editing has matured for text-only models, it remains unclear whether edits that successfully modify textual outputs also transfer to image generation in UMMs. To study this question, we introduce UniKE, the first benchmark for cross-modality knowledge editing in UMMs, comprising 2,971 edit subjects spanning attribute and relation edits. Using VQA-based visual verification, we reveal a striking modality gap: text-side efficacy can reach approximately 92%, whereas the best overall VQA accuracy under direct image generation is only 18.5%. We further propose Reasoning-augmented Parameter Editing, which explicitly activates edited knowledge before generation and improves overall VQA accuracy for all evaluated model-editor pairs, with gains up to 18.6 percentage points. Mechanistic analysis shows that this gap is associated with partial alignment between edited textual representations and the conditioning pathways for visual generation, where edits sufficient for text outputs may remain too weak or misaligned to steer image synthesis. These findings show that textual knowledge edits do not guarantee reliable cross-modality transfer and motivate modality-aware editing methods. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/gxx27/UniKE.

</details>


### [34] [LaSR: Context-Aware Speech Recognition via Latent Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00507)
*Heyang Liu,Ziyang Cheng,Jiayi Huang,Wenyang Xiao,Ronghua Wu,Qunshan Gu,Yanfeng Wang,Yu Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces LaSR, a latent reasoning paradigm for Speech LLMs that improves context-aware terminology recognition without extra latency, and releases a new academic speech corpus.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Speech LLMs have improved in spoken language understanding and reasoning, but they still struggle with contextual awareness in speech recognition, especially for reflecting speaker intent and accurately handling topical or specialized vocabulary. Existing approaches typically rely on explicit chain-of-thought tokens or lack mechanisms to ground recognition in broader context while remaining efficient.

Method: Propose LaSR (Latent Speech Reasoning), a training paradigm that uses context-aware, latent reasoning instead of explicit intermediate tokens. The model is supervised using chain-of-thought (CoT) signals aligned to the acoustic feature region of targeted words, and it introduces latent reasoning periods dedicated to grounding context information and enabling smoother transcriptional transitions. Additionally, they construct Spoken Darwin-Science, a large-scale spoken corpus rich in academic and specialized terminology, to benchmark contextual recognition performance.

Result: On the Fun-Audio-Chat benchmark, LaSR markedly improves recognition of specialized terminology while maintaining the same decoding latency, and it consistently outperforms conventional supervised fine-tuning baselines on contextual recognition tasks.

Conclusion: Latent, implicitly modeled reasoning trajectories can significantly boost context-aware speech recognition, particularly for specialized terminology, without incurring additional latency. LaSR demonstrates that aligning CoT supervision to acoustic regions and using latent reasoning periods is an effective way to build more efficient, context-sensitive speech assistants, and the new Spoken Darwin-Science corpus supports rigorous evaluation in academic domains.

Abstract: Recent advances in Speech Large Language Models (Speech LLMs) have significantly enhanced spoken language understanding and reasoning. However, their contextual awareness is limited, struggling to perform speech recognition that effectively reflects the speaker's intent and topical context. In this paper, we propose LaSR (Latent Speech Reasoning), a novel training paradigm featuring a context-aware reasoning trajectory that leverages the latent reasoning process. Instead of generating explicit intermediate tokens, LaSR aligns chain-of-thought (CoT) supervision around the acoustic feature region of the targeted word, and introduces latent reasoning periods for context information grounding and transcriptional transition. Furthermore, to effectively benchmark contextual recognition on specialized vocabulary, we propose Spoken Darwin-Science, a large-scale corpus focusing on academic terminologies. Preliminary experiments on Fun-Audio-Chat demonstrate that LaSR significantly improves terminology recognition without introducing additional latency and consistently outperforms standard supervised fine-tuning baselines. Our findings highlight the potential of latent reasoning in building efficient, context-aware speech assistants.

</details>


### [35] [Skill or Skip? Learning Selective Skill Invocation in Agentic Tasks via Dual-Granularity Preference Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00510)
*Chishui Chen,Jiaye Lin,Te Sun,Junxi Wang,Yi Yang,Cong Qin,Yangen Hu,Lu Pan,Ke Zeng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SelSkill is a preference-learning framework that decides when an AI agent should invoke a skill versus proceed without it, improving task success and precision while transferring to new domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing agent systems focus on how to select or improve skills but neglect the decision of whether a relevant skill should actually be invoked at a given step. Unnecessary or harmful skill calls can inject irrelevant context and derail successful execution, so there is a need to learn selective invocation policies.

Method: SelSkill treats each potential skill call as a binary skill-or-skip decision. It uses predictive uncertainty to identify high-value decision points and builds invoke-vs-skip preference pairs from trajectories sharing the same prefixes, thus controlling for context. It then performs preference learning at two levels: episode-level outcome preferences (overall success) and step-level invocation preferences (local effectiveness of a skill call), combining them into a unified invocation policy.

Result: On ALFWorld with Qwen3-8B, SelSkill increases task success by 10.9 percentage points and execution precision by 29.1 percentage points. On BFCL, it boosts task success by 5.7 percentage points and execution precision by 29.5 percentage points. Moreover, zero-shot tests on Tau-bench and PopQA with unseen skills show that the learned invocation policy generalizes across domains.

Conclusion: Selective skill invocation, learned via dual-granularity preference signals over invoke-or-skip decisions, significantly improves both success and precision of agentic execution and can transfer to new environments and novel skills.

Abstract: Agent skills are callable procedural modules that provide reusable knowledge and execution policies for complex agentic tasks. However, existing methods mainly focus on selecting relevant skills or improving the skills themselves, while overlooking whether a relevant skill should actually be invoked at the current decision point. Unhelpful invocations may introduce irrelevant context and disrupt an otherwise correct execution process. To address this issue, we propose SelSkill, a dual-granularity preference-learning framework for selective skill invocation. SelSkill formulates skill use as a skill-or-skip decision, uses predictive uncertainty to prioritize candidate decision points, and constructs controlled invoke-skip preference pairs from shared trajectory prefixes. It further combines episode-level outcome preferences with step-level invocation preferences to capture both overall trajectory quality and the local effectiveness of skill invocation. On ALFWorld with Qwen3-8B, SelSkill improves task success by 10.9 percentage points and execution precision by 29.1 percentage points. On BFCL, it improves task success by 5.7 percentage points and execution precision by 29.5 percentage points. Zero-shot results on Tau-bench and PopQA further suggest that the learned invocation policy transfers to new domains with previously unseen skills.

</details>


### [36] [ProactiveLLM: Learning Active Interaction for Streaming Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00523)
*Junlong Tong,Yao Zhang,Anhao Zhao,Yingqi Fan,Yunpu Ma,Xiaoyu Shen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ProactiveLLM is a streaming large language model that reduces latency and compute by learning when it has seen enough input to act, using only its own internal signals rather than external timing labels or teacher models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard LLMs must read the full input before generating, which causes latency and extra computation, especially in streaming scenarios like live speech or text feeds. Existing streaming methods generate while reading but cannot effectively decide when to interact with the stream without hand-crafted rules or expensive external supervision. A method is needed that lets an LLM autonomously infer from partial input when it has sufficient semantic information to respond, without relying on external labels or stronger models.

Method: The authors introduce ProactiveLLM, which makes interaction decisions based on internal model states. Training uses two mechanisms: (1) mask-based streaming modeling, where monotonic random masking simulates incrementally arriving input so the model learns to handle partial contexts and local dependencies; and (2) synchronized privileged self-distillation (SPSD), where a partial-context student is trained to match a full-context teacher produced by the same evolving model, so full-context information guides learning under incomplete observations. These induce endogenous cues of semantic sufficiency that can drive various decision heads in a plug-and-play fashion.

Result: Across multiple text and speech streaming benchmarks, ProactiveLLM achieves significantly lower interaction latency compared with baselines while maintaining task performance, demonstrating that the model can dynamically and effectively decide when to act during streaming without external alignment signals.

Conclusion: ProactiveLLM shows that an LLM can learn internal sufficiency cues from partial inputs, via masking and self-distillation, enabling proactive and dynamic interaction in streaming settings. This approach removes the need for external teachers or timing annotations and provides a general foundation for integrating different decision mechanisms to balance latency and quality in real-time applications.

Abstract: Standard Large Language Models (LLMs) follow a read-then-generate paradigm, causing unnecessary latency and computation. Streaming LLMs alleviate this issue by generating while receiving inputs, but still struggle to decide when to interact with the stream. Existing methods either hard-code interaction timing or rely on costly external alignment signals, such as timing labels, reasoning trajectories, or stronger teachers. In this paper, we propose ProactiveLLM, which achieves active interaction by leveraging the model's endogenous states to guide interaction decisions. The model first learns to perceive semantic sufficiency from partial inputs through two complementary training mechanisms: mask-based streaming modeling and synchronized privileged self-distillation (SPSD). The former applies monotonic random masking to the input during training, simulating progressively revealed streaming inputs and enabling the model to learn local semantic dependencies from partial-input views. The latter aligns the partial-context student view with a full-context teacher view generated by the same evolving model, allowing privileged full-context evidence to guide the student's understanding under incomplete observations. Together, these mechanisms induce endogenous sufficiency cues without requiring external teachers or annotations, providing a versatile foundation for the plug-and-play integration of diverse decision heads. Extensive evaluation across text and speech streaming tasks confirms that ProactiveLLM significantly reduces interaction latency while maintaining quality, validating its capacity for dynamic and active interaction. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/EIT-NLP/StreamingLLM/tree/main/ProactiveLLM.

</details>


### [37] [Learning to Retrieve: Dual-Level Long-Term Memory for Text-to-SQL Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00547)
*Yibo Wang,Nikki Lijing Kuang,Philip S. Yu,Zhewei Yao,Yuxiong He*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces MERIT, a multi-horizon, reinforcement-learned memory retrieval framework that improves performance and efficiency of interactive text-to-SQL agents by using both episode-level and turn-level memories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interactive text-to-SQL agents must handle complex, multi-turn interactions that involve exploring database schemas, executing queries, interpreting feedback, and revising decisions. While long-term memory can help reuse past experiences, existing retrieval methods are inadequate: static retrieval uses fixed similarity heuristics that are not optimized for task success, and dynamic retrieval typically learns only from sparse final outcomes and retrieves at a single decision point. This fails to capture that different stages of interaction (early planning vs. later execution and revision) require different kinds of memory, motivating a framework that can select and use memories differently across multiple decision horizons.

Method: The authors propose MERIT, a dynamic multi-horizon memory retrieval framework for interactive text-to-SQL agents. MERIT maintains two levels of memory: (1) episode-level memory that stores entire past interactions to guide global strategy and planning, and (2) turn-level memory that stores fine-grained information for local, state-conditioned decisions. Each level is controlled by its own learned retrieval policy trained with reinforcement learning to optimize the downstream performance of the agent. To address the challenge of sparse supervision at the turn level, MERIT introduces a lightweight Process Reward Model that provides dense proxy rewards for individual local memory selections, enabling effective RL training of the turn-level retrieval policy.

Result: On the BIRD-Interact benchmark, MERIT achieves higher task success rates than agents with no memory, agents using static retrieval, and agents using standard dynamic retrieval. Additionally, MERIT reduces the average number of interaction turns required to solve tasks, indicating improved efficiency. Transfer experiments on the Spider2-Snow benchmark show that the retrieval policies learned by MERIT transfer positively across benchmarks without needing benchmark-specific tuning, suggesting robustness and generality of the approach.

Conclusion: MERIT demonstrates that explicitly modeling memory retrieval at multiple horizons—episode-level for global strategy and turn-level for local decisions—substantially improves experience reuse in interactive text-to-SQL agents. By training retrieval policies with reinforcement learning and using a Process Reward Model to provide dense feedback at the turn level, MERIT overcomes limitations of both static and conventional dynamic retrieval methods. The observed gains in success rate, efficiency, and cross-benchmark transfer indicate that multi-horizon, utility-optimized memory retrieval is a promising direction for building more capable interactive text-to-SQL systems.

Abstract: Interactive text-to-SQL agents solve database tasks through multi-turn interactions involving schema exploration, query execution, feedback interpretation, and decision revision. Long-term memory helps agents reuse past experiences, but existing retrieval methods remain limited. Static methods rely on fixed similarity heuristics that do not optimize downstream utility, while dynamic methods often learn from sparse final outcomes and retrieve memories at a single decision horizon. This is insufficient when memory usefulness changes across interaction stages, since memories useful for initial planning may differ from those needed for local, state-conditioned execution. We propose MERIT, a dynamic multi-horizon memory retrieval framework. MERIT maintains episode-level memory for global strategic guidance and turn-level memory for local decision support. Both levels use learned retrieval policies optimized with reinforcement learning. To train turn-level retrieval despite limited intermediate supervision, MERIT uses a lightweight Process Reward Model to provide dense proxy rewards for local memory selection. Experiments on BIRD-Interact show that MERIT outperforms no-memory, static-retrieval, and dynamic-retrieval baselines in success rate while reducing average interaction turns. Transfer results on Spider2-Snow further show positive cross-benchmark transfer without benchmark-specific tuning. These results suggest that multi-horizon retrieval improves experience reuse in interactive text-to-SQL agents.

</details>


### [38] [Revisiting Parameter-Based Knowledge Editing in Large Language Models: Theoretical Limits and Empirical Evidence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00570)
*Wanying Ren,Xin Song,Futing Wang,Guoxiu He,Aixin Sun*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies the limitations of parameter-based knowledge editing in LLMs and shows, both theoretically and empirically, that such edits often damage core model capabilities, while a simple retrieval-based approach works better and safer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Parameter-based knowledge editing is popular for updating LLM knowledge without full retraining, but current methods lack a solid theoretical understanding of their limits and are rarely tested under realistic, multi-edit, complex-knowledge scenarios. There is a need to understand why such methods can cause unexpected side effects and whether they are genuinely preferable to simpler alternatives.

Method: The authors first develop a theoretical analysis grounded in the Dimensional Collapse Hypothesis, arguing that localized weight changes can propagate along fragile directions in representation space and cause widespread interference and reasoning failures. They then run a broad empirical study that varies knowledge complexity, the number of edits, evaluation axes, and compares multiple parameter-editing techniques to a retrieval-based baseline.

Result: They find that parameter-based knowledge editing methods systematically and significantly harm core capabilities of LLMs, especially as knowledge complexity and number of edits increase. In all tested configurations, a simple retrieval-based approach outperforms every parameter-editing method in maintaining performance and avoiding degradation.

Conclusion: The work concludes that current parameter-based knowledge editing is fundamentally risky because of structural limitations linked to representation collapse, and that future approaches must prioritize preserving base model capabilities. Retrieval-based methods emerge as a more reliable alternative under practical conditions, and safety-preserving evaluation protocols should become standard in knowledge editing research.

Abstract: Parameter-based knowledge editing updates the internal knowledge of large language models (LLMs) via localized weight modifications and has attracted significant attention. However, most existing methods overlook fundamental theoretical limitations and are rarely evaluated under realistic, practice-oriented settings. In this paper, we first present a theoretical analysis based on the dimensional Collapse Hypothesis, explaining how localized parameter edits can propagate along fragile directions in the representation space, inducing global interference and ultimately causing reasoning collapse. Building on this insight, we conduct a comprehensive empirical evaluation by systematically varying knowledge complexity, number of edits, evaluation dimensions, and baseline methods. Our results show that parameter-based editing methods consistently damage core LLM capabilities. In contrast, a simple retrieval-based baseline achieves consistently stronger performance than all parameter-editing methods across all evaluated conditions. These findings highlight that preserving the fundamental capabilities of LLMs after knowledge editing should be a central concern for future research.

</details>


### [39] [Sandboxed Coding Agents are Competitive Omni-modal Task Solvers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00579)
*Dongping Chen,Xuanao Huang,Zhihan Hu,Qingyuan Shi,Dianqi Li,Tianyi Zhou*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper shows that text+image coding agents using tool calls can rival or beat native multimodal LLMs on audio-video tasks by converting them into retrieval and information-processing problems, introduces a taxonomy of failures and skill injection methods, and releases a training recipe, dataset, and benchmark for many-modality processing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most multimodal LLM research assumes that handling audio and video requires native omnimodal models that directly ingest raw media streams. The authors question this assumption and investigate whether a text+image model, augmented with coding and tool-use capabilities, can effectively solve omnimodal tasks by working over derived signals (transcripts, frames, features) instead of raw streams. They also want to understand the strengths, weaknesses, and scaling path of such coding agents, and provide open-source resources to study and train them.

Method: They build coding agents with only text+image input plus a sandboxed tool-use interface, enabling the agents to write and execute code that calls tools to process transcripts, extracted frames, and other modality-specific signals for audio-video tasks. They evaluate these agents against state-of-the-art native omnimodal models and multimodal agent scaffolds on multiple audio-video benchmarks. They perform trajectory and process-level analyses to understand how the agents solve tasks, identify a taxonomy of failure modes, and test “skill injection” strategies using both human-authored and self-distilled skills. They then propose an open-source training recipe, Code-X, based on a new OmniCoding trajectory dataset and verifiable reward signals, implemented on Qwen-3.5-9B and Qwen-3.6-27B baselines. Finally, they introduce TerminalBench-O, a process-level benchmark for realistic many-modality processing tasks.

Result: Text+image coding agents with tool-use match or surpass state-of-the-art native omnimodal models and predefined multimodal agents on several audio-video benchmarks, despite not directly consuming raw audio/video streams. Trajectory analysis indicates that the agents succeed by decomposing omnimodal tasks into retrieval and information-processing subtasks via code and tools. The authors identify systematic limitations via a failure taxonomy and show that simple skill injection, including human-written and self-distilled skills, significantly boosts performance. They also provide working baselines for the Code-X training recipe on Qwen-3.5-9B and Qwen-3.6-27B, and release TerminalBench-O as a challenging benchmark for many-modality processing.

Conclusion: Native omnimodal LLMs are not strictly necessary to achieve strong performance on many audio-video tasks; instead, coding agents that operate over derived multimodal signals with tool-use can be competitive or superior. The key power lies in programmatic orchestration of tools to transform rich media tasks into structured retrieval and reasoning problems. However, such agents still exhibit characteristic failure modes, which can be mitigated by injecting targeted skills. The paper positions many-modality processing—handling diverse, heterogeneous modalities via tools and code—as the next frontier, and provides Code-X, OmniCoding data, and TerminalBench-O to support open, reproducible research in this direction.

Abstract: As multimodal LLMs increasingly target video and audio, it is often assumed that such tasks require native omnimodal models. We show that this is not always the case: coding agents with only text+image access and a sandboxed tool-use interface can match, and in several settings outperform, SOTA native omnimodal models and predefined multimodal agent scaffolds across multiple audio-video benchmarks. Our trajectory analysis suggests that their strength comes from writing code and orchestrating tools to extract relevant evidence from transcripts, frames, and other modality signals, thereby converting omnimodal tasks into retrieval and information-processing problems rather than ingesting entire media streams. We further characterize their limitations through a failure taxonomy and process-level trace analysis, and show that simple skill injection, including human-written and self-distilled skills, substantially improves performance. To explore open-source elicitation, we introduce Code-X, a training recipe with the OmniCoding trajectory dataset and verifiable reward, and provide baselines on Qwen-3.5-9B and Qwen-3.6-27B. Finally, we argue that the next frontier is many-modality processing, and introduce TerminalBench-O, a process-level benchmark for real-world omnimodal processing tasks. Code will be available at https://github.com/Dongping-Chen/OmniCoding.

</details>


### [40] [SPADER: Step-wise Peer Advantage with Diversity-Aware Exploration Rewards for Multi-Answer Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00593)
*Qiming Shi,Zhaolu Kang,Yunfan Zhou,Di Weng,Yingcai Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces SPADER, an RL framework that improves large language model agents’ ability to use tools for multi-answer question answering by better step-level credit assignment and diversity-aware exploration rewards.


<details>
