  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most tool-using LLM agents are trained and evaluated on tasks with a single correct answer, but many real-world queries require discovering a comprehensive set of answers (Multi-Answer QA). This setting is challenging because it requires assigning credit over long reasoning and tool-use trajectories and encouraging exploration beyond easy, frequent answers to uncover long-tail entities. Existing methods are not well-suited to these challenges, leading to incomplete answer sets and suboptimal exploration.

Method: The authors propose SPADER, an RL framework tailored to long-horizon tool use in Multi-Answer QA. SPADER introduces: (1) Step-wise Peer Advantage (SPA), a critic-free step-level credit assignment mechanism that aligns multiple parallel trajectories by decision step and estimates the advantage of each action from the returns of its peers instead of using a learned value critic; and (2) a diversity-aware exploration reward that increases rewards for discovering rare, previously unseen entities and decreases rewards for repeatedly finding common or already-found entities, thereby promoting coverage of the long-tail answer space.

Result: On four Multi-Answer QA benchmarks—QAMPARI, Mintaka, WebQSP, and QUEST—SPADER-trained agents outperform prompting-based tool agents, outcome-supervised RL baselines, and recent step-level supervision methods. The improvements are reported mainly in recall and overall F1, evidencing better coverage of valid answers and improved quality of the final answer sets.

Conclusion: SPADER effectively addresses two key challenges in Multi-Answer QA with tool-using LLM agents: fine-grained credit assignment over long-horizon trajectories and encouraging sustained, diversity-oriented exploration. By using SPA for critic-free step-level credit and a diversity-aware exploration reward, the framework increases answer coverage and performance on several benchmarks, suggesting it is a promising approach for training more capable multi-answer tool-augmented LLM agents.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly deployed as tool-augmented agents to acquire information beyond parametric knowledge. While recent work has improved long-horizon tool-use reasoning, most approaches focus on tasks with a single correct answer. In contrast, many real-world queries require discovering a comprehensive set of valid answers, a setting known as Multi-Answer QA. This setting raises two challenges: fine-grained credit assignment over long search trajectories and reward alignment for sustained exploration beyond easy high-frequency entities. We propose SPADER, a reinforcement learning framework for long-horizon tool use in Multi-Answer QA. SPADER includes Step-wise Peer Advantage (SPA), a critic-free step-level credit assignment mechanism that aligns parallel trajectories by decision step and estimates advantages from peer returns. It also includes a diversity-aware exploration reward that promotes long-tail entity discovery by upweighting rare findings and downweighting redundant ones. Experiments on QAMPARI, Mintaka, WebQSP, and QUEST show that SPADER generally improves recall and overall F1 over prompting-based agents, outcome-supervised RL methods, and recent step-level supervision approaches. Our code and model weights are available at https://github.com/KhanCold/spader.

</details>


### [41] [Toward Responsible and Epistemically Grounded Multilingual LLMs for Computational Social Science and Humanities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00596)
*Wajdi Zaghouani*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new framework for evaluating multilingual reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) specifically for Social Sciences and Humanities (SSH) research, treating them as tools that shape interpretation across cultures and languages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of multilingual LLMs focus on task performance in NLP benchmarks and overlook issues crucial for SSH research, such as interpretive validity, cultural and linguistic situatedness, and how LLMs mediate knowledge and meaning. There is a need for a rigorous, theory-informed way to assess multilingual reasoning when these models are used as research instruments in interpretive disciplines.

Method: The authors synthesize theory from hermeneutics, philosophy of technology, science and technology studies, multilingual NLP, and computational social science to reconceptualize LLMs as hermeneutic instruments. On this basis, they design an evaluation framework and experimental protocol, defining operational metrics for cultural alignment, cross-lingual stability, and reasoning faithfulness, and specify transparency requirements for interpretive use cases. They then demonstrate the framework via a case study in multilingual political discourse analysis.

Result: The paper presents: (1) a conceptual reframing of multilingual reasoning LLMs as active participants in meaning construction; (2) a structured evaluation protocol tailored to SSH needs; (3) concrete, operational metrics for assessing cultural alignment, cross-lingual stability, and reasoning faithfulness; and (4) a worked example showing how the framework can be applied in a multilingual political discourse analysis scenario.

Conclusion: Multilingual reasoning LLMs should be evaluated not just as generic NLP systems but as hermeneutic instruments that shape interpretive outcomes in SSH research. The proposed framework and protocol offer a conceptual and methodological basis for more responsible, transparent, and culturally aware integration of such models into computational social science infrastructures, addressing gaps in current benchmark-driven evaluation practices.

Abstract: Large language models have rapidly evolved in multilingual competence and reasoning capacity, enabling their integration into Social Sciences and Humanities research workflows. Yet existing evaluation paradigms remain anchored in task-based NLP benchmarks and fail to address interpretive validity, cultural situatedness, and epistemic mediation. This paper reconceptualizes multilingual reasoning LLMs as hermeneutic instruments that actively structure meaning production across linguistic and cultural contexts. Drawing on hermeneutics, philosophy of technology, science and technology studies, multilingual NLP research, and computational social science methodology, we develop a theoretically grounded framework for evaluating multilingual reasoning in Social Sciences and Humanities (SSH) research. We articulate a rigorous experimental protocol with operationalized metrics for cultural alignment, cross-lingual stability, and reasoning faithfulness, along with transparency requirements tailored to interpretive research tasks. We illustrate the framework through a concrete application scenario involving multilingual political discourse analysis. The paper contributes a conceptual and methodological foundation for responsible integration of multilingual reasoning LLMs into computational social science infrastructures.

</details>


### [42] [Linguistics-Aware Non-Distortionary LLM Watermarking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00613)
*Shinwoo Park,Hyejin Park,Hyeseon An,Yo-Sub Han*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes LUNA, a linguistically adaptive, non‑distorting watermarking method for detecting language‑model outputs, especially in multilingual settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing watermarking methods for language models either hurt text quality, depend on the model provider for verification, or perform inconsistently across languages with different morphology, segmentation, and scripts. There is a need for a watermark that works robustly in multilingual contexts, preserves text quality, and supports model‑independent detection.

Method: LUNA uses part‑of‑speech (POS) contexts from an external corpus to estimate normalized next‑tag entropy for each context. This entropy guides the depth of a binary tournament sampler that injects a watermark without changing single‑token distributions (non‑distortionary, single‑token). Under a standard random‑key model, the detector, given only the text, tokenizer, tagger, and secret key, reconstructs the same sampling schedule and tests for watermark presence using a model‑free statistical detector.

Result: Across six typologically diverse languages and two domains (12 settings total), LUNA is compared against eight baselines. It achieves an AUROC of 0.9959 and the lowest mean absolute median perplexity shift of 0.045, with a 95% bootstrap confidence interval [0.022, 0.073] that is smaller and lower than all baselines. It also yields the smallest shifts in Self‑BLEU, Distinct‑1, surprisal, and entropy, indicating minimal impact on text diversity and fluency. It is the only method that consistently satisfies AUROC > 0.99 and absolute median perplexity shift < 0.1 in most settings (9/12), while no baseline reaches this regime in more than 2 settings.

Conclusion: LUNA provides a practical, linguistically adaptive watermarking strategy that maintains text quality while enabling strong, model‑independent detection of language‑model generated text across multiple languages. Its empirical performance suggests it is more robust and less distortive than existing watermarking methods, particularly in challenging multilingual deployments.

Abstract: Watermarking should identify language-model output without degrading quality or limiting verification to the model provider. Multilingual deployment makes this harder because morphology, segmentation, and script change where watermark evidence can enter naturally. We introduce LUNA, a linguistically adaptive watermark that combines model-free detection with single-token non-distortion under the standard random-key model. LUNA estimates normalized next-tag entropy from part-of-speech contexts in an external corpus and uses it to set the depth of a non-distortionary binary tournament sampler; the detector reconstructs the same schedule from text, a tokenizer, a tagger, and a secret key. We evaluate six typologically diverse languages and two domains against eight primary baselines. LUNA attains an AUROC of 0.9959 and the lowest mean absolute median perplexity shift of 0.045 across the twelve settings; its 95% bootstrap interval [0.022, 0.073] lies below all baseline intervals. LUNA also records the lowest mean Self-BLEU, Distinct-1, surprisal, and entropy shifts. It is the only method that simultaneously achieves AUROC > 0.99 and an absolute median perplexity shift below 0.1 in a majority of settings, reaching this regime in 9 of the 12 settings while no baseline reaches it in more than 2. Our code is available at: https://github.com/Shinwoo-Park/luna_watermark

</details>


### [43] [MemPro: Agentic Memory Systems as Evolvable Programs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00619)
*Qingshan Liu,Guoqing Wang,Wen Wu,Jingqi Huang,Xinqi Tao,Dejia Song,Jie Zhou,Liang He*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: MemPro is a framework that evolves the entire memory pipeline for long-horizon autonomous agents, not just the memory bank, leading to better and more cost-effective performance across multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon agents need robust memory to handle long histories and reuse knowledge beyond short context windows. Existing systems mostly tweak the memory bank while keeping the larger construction-retrieval pipeline fixed, which fails to adapt to diverse task-specific failure modes and becomes misaligned as memory scales and changes. A more holistic, evolvable approach is needed at the system level.

Method: MemPro models the whole memory construction-retrieval (MCR) pipeline as an evolvable program. It maintains a version tree of full memory-system implementations. An Evolving Agent iteratively: (1) selects promising pipeline versions, (2) diagnoses recurring failures on tasks, and (3) generates improved child versions through failure-mode-guided editing and debugging of the pipeline code/configs. This results in progressive, system-level refinement rather than isolated memory-bank updates.

Result: On benchmarks like LongMemEval, LoCoMo, HotpotQA, and NarrativeQA, MemPro outperforms strong static systems and prompt-level evolution baselines after only a few evolution iterations. Performance continues to improve with additional evolution steps, and MemPro achieves better accuracy-cost trade-offs than comparison methods.

Conclusion: Treating the entire memory pipeline as an evolvable program enables long-horizon agents to adapt their memory behavior over time, overcoming limitations of fixed-pipeline designs. MemPro’s evolutionary framework yields consistently better and more cost-efficient memory performance across diverse benchmarks, demonstrating the value of system-level, failure-driven evolution for agent memory architectures.

Abstract: Long-horizon autonomous agents require memory systems to retain historical information, track evolving states, and reuse relevant knowledge beyond finite context windows. Existing agentic memory systems typically follow a memory construction-retrieval (MCR) pipeline, but often adapt mainly the memory bank while keeping the surrounding pipeline fixed after deployment. This fixed-pipeline design struggles to handle heterogeneous task-specific failure modes and can become misaligned with memory banks that evolve in scale and structure over time. To address these limitations, we propose MemPro, a system-level evolution framework that treats the entire MCR pipeline as an evolvable program rather than adapting only the memory bank or prompt text. MemPro maintains a version tree of runnable memory-system implementations, where an Evolving Agent iteratively selects promising versions, diagnoses recurring failures, and creates improved child versions through failure-mode-guided edit-debug refinement. Experiments on LongMemEval, LoCoMo, HotpotQA, and NarrativeQA show that MemPro consistently outperforms strong static and prompt-level evolving baselines within a few iterations, continues to improve with evolution, and achieves a favorable performance-cost trade-off. Code is available at https://github.com/wanghai673/MemPro.

</details>


### [44] [Robust Reasoning via Dynamic Token Selection for Distribution-Aligned Self-Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00628)
*Ruiqi Zhang,Lingxiang Wang,Hainan Zhang Zhiming Zheng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Distribution-Aligned Self-Distillation (DASD), a method that selectively keeps knowledge-rich corrections while filtering style noise during self-distillation, leading to better reasoning performance and fewer harmful high-perplexity tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard self-distillation rewrites reference answers to match the model’s distribution, but the references impose strong stylistic biases. High-perplexity tokens in the rewritten data come from both good (logical improvements) and bad (style drift) sources. Treating them uniformly can distort the base model’s distribution and hurt performance, especially on hard reasoning tasks. The motivation is to keep the beneficial corrections while avoiding harmful stylistic imitation.

Method: DASD employs an answer-aware reference model to propose candidate rewrite tokens. It then uses the base model’s own confidence to dynamically filter these candidates: tokens aligned with the base model’s distribution and adding logical knowledge are preserved, while distributionally misaligned tokens—largely style artifacts—are suppressed. This yields training data whose distribution better matches the base model while retaining useful reasoning improvements.

Result: Across math, code, and commonsense reasoning benchmarks, DASD consistently surpasses strong self-distillation baselines. It decreases the number of high-perplexity tokens in the training data and yields models that are more robust across tasks of varying difficulty.

Conclusion: By aligning self-distillation data with the base model’s distribution and filtering out stylistic noise, DASD allows models to absorb logical knowledge without overfitting to reference style. This targeted handling of high-perplexity tokens improves efficiency, robustness, and performance on diverse reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Self-distillation improves learning efficiency by rewriting reference answers as training data that better matches the model's own distribution. However, reference answers also introduce strong stylistic biases, causing the generative model to imitate surface forms rather than learn useful reasoning patterns. We observe that the rewriting data contains a large number of high-perplexity (PPL) tokens, coming from two distinct sources: beneficial knowledge-enhancing logical corrections, and harmful stylistic drift induced by reference imitation. Treating all such tokens equally can disrupt the base model's original distribution and degrade performance, especially on difficult reasoning tasks. To address this, we propose Distribution-Aligned Self-Distillation (DASD), which uses an answer-aware reference model to generate candidate tokens and dynamically filters them according to the base model's confidence. DASD preserves tokens that encode useful logical knowledge while suppressing distributionally misaligned style noise. Experiments on math, code, and commonsense reasoning benchmarks show that DASD consistently outperforms competitive baselines, reduces high-PPL tokens, and improves robustness across tasks of varying difficulty.

</details>


### [45] [French parsing enhanced with a word clustering method based on a syntactic lexicon](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00634)
*Anthony Sigogne,Matthieu Constant,Eric Laporte*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper integrates information from a French syntactic lexicon (Lexicon-Grammar) into a probabilistic parser and shows that verb clustering on the French Treebank improves a PCFG-based parser’s performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: French syntactic parsing quality can benefit from rich lexical-syntactic information, but existing probabilistic parsers often underuse external lexicons like the Lexicon-Grammar. The authors aim to see whether leveraging such a lexicon, via clustering verbs in a treebank, can improve parsing accuracy for French.

Method: They extract data from the French Lexicon-Grammar and apply clustering methods to verbs occurring in the French Treebank. These clusters are then integrated into a probabilistic parser based on a Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (following Petrov et al., 2006), and parsing performance is evaluated on French.

Result: Using verb clusters derived from the Lexicon-Grammar and the French Treebank leads to accurate parsing performance for French with the PCFG-based parser, outperforming a baseline without this integration (implicit from the abstract).

Conclusion: Lexical-syntactic information from the Lexicon-Grammar, when transformed into verb clusters and incorporated into a PCFG parser, effectively improves French parsing performance, indicating that external syntactic lexicons can usefully complement probabilistic parsing models.

Abstract: This article evaluates the integration of data extracted from a French syntactic lexicon, the Lexicon-Grammar (Gross, 1994), into a probabilistic parser. We show that by applying clustering methods on verbs of the French Treebank (Abeillé et al., 2003), we obtain accurate performances on French with a parser based on a Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (Petrov et al., 2006).

</details>


### [46] [LinguIUTics at PsyDefDetect: Iterative Imbalance-Aware Fine-tuning of Qwen3-8B for Psychological Defense Mechanism Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00647)
*Shefayat E Shams Adib,Ahmed Alfey Sani,Md Hasibur Rahman Alif,Ajwad Abrar*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents a system for detecting nine types of psychological defense mechanisms in text, achieving strong performance in a shared task through fine-tuning a Qwen3-8B model and targeted techniques for imbalanced data and post-processing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Psychological defense mechanisms are clinically important but difficult to detect automatically in conversational text, especially when some defense types are rare. Existing BERT-style models and zero-shot LLMs perform poorly on these minority classes, limiting the clinical utility of NLP systems. The shared task (PsyDefDetect 2026) provides a benchmark, and the authors aim to build a competitive system that especially improves performance on rare and ambiguous categories like the "Unclear" class.

Method: The authors fine-tune a Qwen3-8B language model via QLoRA for nine-class utterance classification. To address data leakage and class imbalance, they use grouped stratified cross-validation, ensuring that related data are kept in the same fold and class proportions are preserved. For minority classes, they apply a round-robin lexical data augmentation scheme to synthetically increase sample diversity. Finally, they implement a post-processing pipeline that includes logit-bias tuning (adjusting model logits to correct systematic biases toward majority classes) and ensemble blending of multiple models or checkpoints to stabilize and boost performance, especially on rare classes.

Result: Their system, LinguIUTics, achieves a macro F1-score of 0.3917 on the official leaderboard, placing 4th of 21 teams and beating the Ministral-8B baseline by 7.7 absolute macro-F1 points (24.4% relative improvement). The methods substantially improve minority-class recall, with the most striking gain in the "Unclear" class (Level 8), where performance increases from nearly zero to an F1 of 0.797. The gap between validation and leaderboard performance is also reduced, indicating better generalization.

Conclusion: Combining QLoRA fine-tuning of a strong base LLM (Qwen3-8B) with careful handling of grouped data splits, targeted lexical augmentation for minority classes, and tailored post-processing (logit bias and ensembling) yields significant gains for highly imbalanced clinical text classification. The approach is particularly effective in rescuing performance on previously underperforming classes, such as the ambiguous "Unclear" category, and demonstrates a practical recipe for similar imbalanced multi-class NLP tasks.

Abstract: Detecting psychological defense mechanisms in conversational text remains a challenging clinical NLP problem. For the PsyDefDetect 2026 shared task (nine-class utterance classification evaluated via macro F1), our team LinguIUTics achieves a macro F1-score of 0.3917 on the official positive-class leaderboard, ranking 4th out of 21 registered teams and improving over the Ministral-8B task baseline (31.48 macro F1) by 7.7 absolute points (24.4 percent relative). BERT-family encoders and zero-shot LLMs proved ineffective on rare classes due to severe class imbalance, leading us to QLoRA fine-tuning of Qwen3-8B. We leverage three key strategies: grouped stratified cross-validation (preventing leakage), minority-class round-robin lexical augmentation, and a post-processing pipeline with logit bias tuning and ensemble blending. Together, these components close much of the validation-to-leaderboard gap and substantially improve minority-class recall, driving the critical "Unclear" class (Level 8) from near-zero performance to an F1 score of 0.797.

</details>


### [47] [FineVerify: Scaling Test-Time Compute with Fine-Grained Self-Verification for Agentic Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00660)
*James Xu Zhao,Hui Chen,Bryan Hooi,See-Kiong Ng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: FineVerify is a self-verification framework for agentic search that decomposes questions into sub-questions, verifies candidate answers locally, and aggregates these checks to robustly select the best answer, outperforming standard test-time scaling approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agentic search agents must answer complex queries by exploring many sources, but simply scaling test-time compute by sampling more trajectories and using global score-based selection is unreliable. Correct answers are sparse and selection depends heavily on model calibration, causing good candidates to be overlooked. A more reliable, interpretable way to evaluate and select among multiple candidate answers is needed.

Method: FineVerify decomposes each complex question into fine-grained, checkable sub-questions. For each sampled candidate trajectory/answer, the system performs explicit verification checks against each sub-question, scoring how well the candidate satisfies each check. These local verification scores are then aggregated into an overall score per candidate, and the candidate with the highest aggregated score is selected as the final answer. Verification uses consistent, explicit criteria to reduce dependence on global heuristic scores, and it also produces a structured verification trace.

Result: Across four agentic search benchmarks and two language models (GPT-5-mini and Gemini-3-flash), FineVerify consistently outperforms standard scaling baselines. With only four sampled trajectories, it improves GPT-5-mini by 8.2 accuracy points and Gemini-3-flash by 5.6% on average. With 12 samples, FineVerify allows GPT-5-mini to surpass a stronger frontier model (GPT-5) on the BrowseComp-Plus benchmark.

Conclusion: FineVerify shows that structuring self-verification as fine-grained, per-sub-question checking yields more accurate and robust agentic search than traditional score-based test-time scaling. It not only boosts answer accuracy but also generates interpretable verification traces that can be used to audit benchmark errors and inspect agent behavior, indicating potential for broader applications in evaluating and debugging agentic search systems.

Abstract: Agentic search requires language model agents to explore many sources and answer complex information-seeking questions. Scaling test-time compute is a promising way to improve these agents, but current approaches can fail, because correct answers are often sparse and score-based selection depends on model calibration. We propose FineVerify, a fine-grained self-verification framework that decomposes each question into checkable sub-questions, verifies sampled candidates against each sub-question, and selects the candidate with the highest aggregated score. This per-check structure turns selection into simpler local judgments and produces scores under the same explicit criteria. Across four agentic search benchmarks and two models, FineVerify consistently outperforms standard scaling baselines. With only four sampled trajectories, it improves GPT-5-mini by 8.2 accuracy points and Gemini-3-flash by 5.6% on average. With 12 samples, FineVerify enables GPT-5-mini to surpass frontier GPT-5 on BrowseComp-Plus. Beyond accuracy, FineVerify produces interpretable verification traces that help audit benchmark errors, suggesting broader applications for inspecting agentic search systems. Code and data are available at https://github.com/XuZhao0/fineverify

</details>


### [48] [OCC-RAG: Optimal Cognitive Core for Faithful Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00683)
*Maksim Savkin,Mikhail Goncharov,Alexander Gambashidze,Alla Chepurova,Dmitrii Tarasov,Nikita Andriianov,Daria Pugacheva,Vasily Konovalov,Andrey Galichin,Ivan Oseledets*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Optimal Cognitive Core (OCC), a family of small, task-specialized language models, and presents OCC-RAG for faithful, context-grounded question answering. OCC-RAG uses a large synthetic dataset for multi-hop reasoning and achieves performance comparable to or better than much larger general-purpose models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling up large language models has focused on absorbing more parametric knowledge, but many real-world applications need strong reasoning over provided context rather than memorized facts. The authors aim to design compact models that excel at multi-hop reasoning, faithfulness to context, and calibrated refusal, offering a more efficient and controllable alternative to large general-purpose models.

Method: They propose the Optimal Cognitive Core (OCC) framework for small, task-specialized language models and instantiate it as OCC-RAG for retrieval-augmented QA. They build a novel synthetic data generation pipeline to create over three million multi-context, multi-hop QA examples emphasizing reasoning over supplied passages, strict grounding to context, and correct abstention when needed. OCC-RAG models are mid-trained on this corpus to produce structured reasoning traces with explicit source citations tied to literal context quotes.

Result: They release two OCC-RAG models (0.6B and 1.7B parameters) and evaluate them on multi-hop reasoning, faithfulness, and refusal benchmarks such as HotpotQA, MuSiQue, TAT-QA, ConFiQA, and MuSiQue-Un. The compact OCC-RAG models match or surpass the performance of general-purpose language models that are 2–6 times larger, especially on multi-hop reasoning and context-faithful QA tasks.

Conclusion: Task-specialized small language models, trained on carefully designed synthetic datasets and optimized for reasoning over provided context, can rival or outperform substantially larger general-purpose LLMs. This supports OCC as a promising design paradigm for building efficient, faithful, and controllable reasoning systems, particularly in retrieval-augmented QA settings.

Abstract: Recent progress in the development of language models has been defined by scale, with each generation absorbing more of the world's knowledge into its weights. However, many practical applications benefit more from robust reasoning than from extensive parametric knowledge. In this setting, task-specialized small language models (SLMs) offer a principled design choice. We introduce Optimal Cognitive Core (OCC), a family of SLMs built around this premise. As a variant of OCC, we present OCC-RAG, optimized for faithful question answering (QA) grounded in the provided context. This task directly aligns with the OCC design approach, requiring multi-hop reasoning over supplied passages while ignoring memorized knowledge. To train OCC-RAG, we implement a novel pipeline for synthesizing multi-context, multi-hop QA data at scale, producing a corpus of over three million examples targeting multi-hop reasoning, strict context faithfulness, and calibrated abstention. We release OCC-RAG-0.6B and OCC-RAG-1.7B, both mid-trained on this corpus. The models produce structured reasoning traces with source citations grounded in literal quotes from the context. Through OCC-RAG, we demonstrate that compact, task-specialized SLMs can match or exceed general-purpose models 2 -- 6x their size across multi-hop reasoning (HotpotQA, MuSiQue, TAT-QA), faithfulness (ConFiQA), and refusal (MuSiQue-Un) benchmarks.

</details>


### [49] [EPIC: Efficient and Parallel Inference under CFG Constraints for Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00722)
*Hyundong Jin,Yo-Sub Han*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: EPIC is an efficient CFG-constrained decoding framework for diffusion language models that preserves parallelism while drastically cutting the latency overhead from grammar enforcement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Controlling language model outputs with structural constraints like CFGs is crucial for validity and usability, but current CFG-constrained decoding for diffusion language models is very slow and undermines one of their main benefits: parallel decoding. There is a need for a method that enforces CFG constraints without large runtime penalties.

Method: The authors introduce EPIC, a CFG-constrained decoding framework for diffusion language models that combines three key ideas: (1) lexing memoization to avoid repeatedly tokenizing the same prefixes or candidates; (2) Earley-style parsing for validity checking instead of deterministic automata, which better supports efficient incremental validation; and (3) relaxed compatible subset selection that permits committing multiple tokens in parallel when they are jointly consistent with the CFG, thus preserving parallelism. Together these reduce repeated lexing and validation overhead while enabling larger parallel decoding steps.

Result: Across three benchmarks and four diffusion language models, EPIC reduces overall inference time by up to 67.5% and cuts the additional overhead introduced by CFG constraints by up to 90.5% when compared to prior CFG-constrained decoding methods for diffusion LMs.

Conclusion: EPIC shows that CFG constraints can be enforced in diffusion language models with far less overhead than previous approaches, largely restoring their advantage in parallel decoding while maintaining structural validity. This makes CFG-constrained diffusion decoding more practical for applications needing strict output formats.

Abstract: Controlling language model outputs is essential for ensuring structural validity, reliability, and downstream usability, and diffusion language models are no exception. Recent advances in diffusion language model decoding have extended output control beyond regular constraints to context-free grammar (CFG) constraints. Existing methods, however, can be up to four times slower than unconstrained decoding. More importantly, they substantially diminish one of the key advantages of diffusion language models over autoregressive models, namely parallel decoding. This slowdown arises because sequential validity checking introduces significant overhead during parallel generation. We propose an efficient CFG-constrained decoding framework, EPIC, that addresses this limitation. Our method improves decoding efficiency by combining lexing memoization, validation using Earley-style parsing instead of deterministic automata, and relaxed compatible subset selection for parallel commit. It reduces repeated lexing and validation overhead while allowing multiple compatible tokens to be committed together. Experiments on three benchmarks using four models show that our method reduces inference time by up to 67.5% and decreases the additional overhead by up to 90.5% compared with existing CFG-constrained decoding methods. Our implementation is available at https://github.com/hyundong98/EPIC-Decoding.git .

</details>


### [50] [WaveFilter: Enhancing the Long-Context Capability of Diffusion LLMs via Wavelet-Guided KV Cache Filtering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00724)
*Jinnan Yang,Yan Wang,Zhen Bi,Kehao Wu,Xiaojie Li,Jungang Lou,Zechao Li,Jing Liu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: WaveFilter is a training-free, plug-and-play KV-cache framework for diffusion LLMs that uses wavelet transforms to select key tokens in ultra-long contexts, enabling sparse caching that speeds up multi-step inference while preserving generation quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion Large Language Models require multi-step iterative inference, which makes them computationally expensive and slow, especially on long-context tasks. Existing KV caching methods struggle in this regime: blindly caching everything is costly, while aggressive pruning harms generation quality because they cannot accurately and efficiently identify which tokens are truly important in very long sequences. There is a need for a principled, lightweight mechanism to filter critical tokens so that long-context processing becomes both faster and accurate, without retraining models.

Method: The paper introduces WaveFilter, a universal and training-free caching framework that uses wavelet transforms to analyze long input sequences. It decomposes sequences into different frequency components, leveraging the multi-resolution nature of wavelets to detect salient or information-dense regions across varying scales. Based on this wavelet-based importance signal, WaveFilter selects a subset of key tokens and constructs a sparse KV cache that stores keys and values only for these tokens. During diffusion LLM inference, this sparse cache is used to compute contextual representations, effectively approximating full-context attention with far fewer cached tokens. WaveFilter is designed as a plug-and-play component that can wrap around existing KV caching strategies.

Result: Experiments on complex long-context tasks show that integrating WaveFilter consistently boosts the performance of mainstream KV cache methods. The framework maintains or improves generation quality while significantly reducing computational overhead and inference latency in diffusion LLMs. Across benchmarks with ultra-long inputs, WaveFilter enables sparse caching schemes to approach the effectiveness of full-context caching with much lower cost, demonstrating clear advantages in both efficiency and accuracy trade-offs.

Conclusion: WaveFilter demonstrates that wavelet-based sequence decomposition is an effective tool for long-context token selection in diffusion LLMs. By providing a training-free, model-agnostic, and plug-and-play KV cache framework, it alleviates the core bottlenecks of computation and latency in long-context diffusion inference. The approach improves existing KV caching methods on challenging tasks and offers a practical path toward scalable deployment of diffusion LLMs in scenarios requiring ultra-long context handling.

Abstract: Diffusion Large Language Models (DLMs) have demonstrated significant advantages across various tasks. However, constrained by their multi-step iterative inference mechanism, their computational overhead and inference latency in long-context tasks have become core bottlenecks restricting their large-scale deployment. When processing long sequences, existing Key-Value (KV) caching mechanisms often face a dilemma where generation quality degrades drastically, where the core challenge lies in precisely and efficiently filtering critical tokens within ultra-long contexts. Inspired by the human reading process, we propose \textbf{WaveFilter}, a universal and training-free caching framework. This framework innovatively introduces the wavelet transform for decomposition of long sequences to achieve precise identification of key tokens, based on which a sparse KV Cache is constructed to compute the final contextual representation. Experimental results demonstrate that WaveFilter, as a plug-and-play generic framework, significantly enhances the performance of existing mainstream KV Cache methods in complex long-context tasks.

</details>


### [51] [From Empathy to Personalized Empathy: Adapting Empathetic Strategies to Individual Users](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00728)
*Wuqiang Zheng,Chengbing Wang,Yilin Yang,Junyi Cheng,Jianfei Xiao,Hu Sun,Yi Xie,Yangyang Li,Wenjie Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces personalized empathy for LLMs, creates a dataset (PersonaEmp) from long-term user–AI interactions, and proposes a reward modeling framework (PereGRM) that improves LLMs’ personalized empathetic responses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs are used in long-term interactions, they need not only generic empathy but also responses tailored to users’ stable traits (personality, histories). Existing work largely ignores how different personalities require different empathetic strategies over time, creating a gap between current empathetic LLMs and real-world needs.

Method: 1) Define the task of personalized empathy, where models adapt empathetic strategies to user-specific characteristics inferred from interaction history. 2) Build PersonaEmp, a dataset from long-term user–AI dialogues that includes user histories, persona profiles, and explicit empathy-seeking user turns. 3) Propose PereGRM, a reward modeling framework that aligns with empathy evaluation structures and uses dynamically generated evaluation criteria to provide fine-grained rewards for training LLMs on personalized empathy.

Result: Across multiple experimental settings and using several LLM judge models, PereGRM-based training consistently yields the largest performance gains on personalized empathy tasks, outperforming baseline methods on standard and personalized empathy metrics.

Conclusion: Modeling user personality and histories is crucial for effective empathetic LLMs in long-term use. The PersonaEmp dataset enables research on this problem, and the PereGRM reward framework substantially enhances LLMs’ personalized empathetic capabilities, as validated by multi-judge evaluations.

Abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in long-term interactions with users, empathy has become an increasingly important capability. However, existing research overlooks the influence of users' personality traits on empathetic strategies during long-term interactions. To address this gap, we introduce the task of personalized empathy, which focuses on adapting empathetic strategies according to users' personalized characteristics derived from history. To study and enhance this capability, we construct PersonaEmp, a personalized empathy dataset built from long-term user-AI interactions, featuring rich user histories, persona information, and empathy-seeking queries. We further propose PereGRM, a reward modeling framework that combines the empathy evaluation structure with dynamic evaluation criteria generation for fine-grained reward modeling. Experimental results across different settings and multiple judge models show that PereGRM consistently achieves the strongest performance improvements, indicating its effectiveness for enhancing personalized empathetic capabilities.

</details>


### [52] [I-WebGenBench : Evaluating Interactivity in LLM-Generated Scientific Web Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00750)
*Dasen Dai,Biao Wu,Meng Fang,Shuoqi Li,Wenhao Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They build an AI agent that turns research papers into working interactive web systems instead of static summaries, using a structured generation framework and a new benchmark to evaluate it.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing document agents mostly produce static outputs (summaries, slides, webpages) that cannot capture the dynamic mechanisms, models, and state transitions often described in technical papers. This limits users’ ability to explore and understand how systems in the paper actually behave under different inputs. There is a need for agents that can automatically convert such papers into executable, interactive systems so users can manipulate parameters and observe resulting behaviors.

Method: They define a new task: converting a PDF research paper into an executable interactive web system end-to-end, without human intervention. The agent pipeline covers three stages: (1) paper understanding (parsing and extracting key concepts, mechanisms, and system components), (2) system modeling (formalizing mechanisms and interaction logic into an executable model), and (3) interactive webpage synthesis (generating a web interface that allows users to control inputs and visualize system behavior). They introduce PaperVoyager, a structured generation framework that explicitly models mechanisms and interaction logic during synthesis, likely using stepwise or modular generation to translate paper content into code and UI components. They create a benchmark of 19 research papers paired with expert-crafted interactive systems as ground truth for evaluation.

Result: On their benchmark of 19 papers and corresponding expert-made interactive systems, PaperVoyager produces significantly higher-quality interactive systems compared to baselines or simpler approaches. The experiments demonstrate that explicitly modeling mechanisms and interaction logic in the generation framework leads to more faithful, usable, and functionally correct interactive web systems derived from papers.

Conclusion: Automatically converting research papers into interactive web systems is feasible and beneficial for scientific document understanding. Their Paper-to-Interactive-System Agent and the PaperVoyager framework provide a new paradigm where users can explore dynamic mechanisms described in papers by directly manipulating inputs and observing behaviors in generated interfaces. The proposed benchmark and results indicate that structured, mechanism-aware generation is key to producing high-quality interactive systems from textual scientific descriptions.

Abstract: Recent advances in visual language models have enabled autonomous agents for complex reasoning, tool use, and document understanding. However, existing document agents mainly transform papers into static artifacts such as summaries, webpages, or slides, which are insufficient for technical papers involving dynamic mechanisms and state transitions. In this work, we propose a Paper-to-Interactive-System Agent that converts research papers into executable interactive web systems. Given a PDF paper, the agent performs end-to-end processing without human intervention, including paper understanding, system modeling, and interactive webpage synthesis, enabling users to manipulate inputs and observe dynamic behaviors. To evaluate this task, we introduce a benchmark of 19 research papers paired with expert-built interactive systems as ground truth. We further propose PaperVoyager, a structured generation framework that explicitly models mechanisms and interaction logic during synthesis. Experiments show that PaperVoyager significantly improves the quality of generated interactive systems, offering a new paradigm for interactive scientific paper understanding.

</details>


### [53] [Internalize the Temperature: On-Policy Self-Distillation as Policy Reheater for Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00755)
*Xuewei Yang,Jiachen Yu,Jie Wu,Shaoning Sun,Junjie Wang,Yujiu Yang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes TS-OPSD, a method that “reheats” reinforcement-learned language models after entropy collapse by distilling a high-temperature version of the model back into itself, restoring entropy while preserving reasoning ability and improving subsequent RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards boosts LLM reasoning but typically causes entropy collapse: the policy becomes overly confident, rollouts lose diversity, and the learning signal degrades. Existing fixes (entropy regularization, rollout temperature tricks) operate externally to the model and don’t change its internal parameters to permanently encode exploratory behavior. The authors want a simple, internal, post-collapse intervention that restores entropy without extra teachers, data, or inference cost, and that yields a better starting point for continued RL.

Method: They introduce Temperature-Scaled On-Policy Self-Distillation (TS-OPSD). Starting from an RL checkpoint that has undergone entropy collapse, they create a self-teacher by applying a high temperature to the model’s logits to obtain a smoother output distribution. They then perform on-policy self-distillation, training the model (student) to match this high-temperature teacher distribution, thereby embedding higher-entropy behavior directly into the parameters. No external model or data is needed, and inference-time cost remains unchanged because temperature scaling is only used during this distillation stage, not at deployment.

Result: On Qwen3-4B-Base and Qwen3-8B-Base, applying TS-OPSD to entropy-collapsed RL checkpoints produces policies that serve as better initializations for further RL than (1) simply continuing RL from the collapsed checkpoint and (2) reheating only at rollout time via higher sampling temperature. Analyses show that TS-OPSD substantially reduces output distribution sharpness while largely preserving internal representations, the set of top candidate tokens, and the model’s reasoning competence.

Conclusion: TS-OPSD is an effective, lightweight policy-reheating procedure that restores entropy after RL-induced collapse by internalizing temperature effects through self-distillation. It improves the quality of subsequent RL training while keeping reasoning ability intact, suggesting that such entropy restoration is a practical post-collapse tool for extending the useful RL phase of reasoning-focused language models.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards improves the reasoning ability of large language models, but often suffers from entropy collapse, in which increasingly concentrated policies reduce rollout diversity and useful learning signals. Existing remedies either constrain the RL objective (e.g., entropy regularization) or adjust sampling temperature during rollout collection, but these interventions remain external to the model parameters. We propose Temperature-Scaled On-Policy Self-Distillation (TS-OPSD), a lightweight policy reheating method that internalizes the exploratory effect of temperature into model parameters. Starting from an entropy-collapsed RL checkpoint, TS-OPSD constructs a self-teacher by applying high-temperature scaling to the model's own logits, then distills the resulting smoother distribution back into the student. This policy reheating requires no external teacher, privileged data, or additional inference cost. Experiments on Qwen3-4B-Base and Qwen3-8B-Base show that policy reheating yields a stronger initialization for continued RL than both standard continued RL and rollout-level temperature reheating. Further analyses show that TS-OPSD mainly reduces output sharpness while preserving intermediate representations, top candidate sets, and reasoning capability. These results suggest that entropy restoration can serve as a simple post-collapse intervention for extending reasoning-oriented RL.

</details>


### [54] [Not All Flips Are Conformity: Decomposing Stance Convergence in Multi-Agent LLM Debate](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00820)
*Xiqi Hao,Zengqing Wu,Yu-Xuan Qiu,Chuan Xiao,Ruiqi Xu,Shuyuan Zheng,Jianbin Qin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper dissects why multi-agent debates between language models lead to answer changes, separating genuine reasoning from instability and social conformity, and shows that much of the observed convergence is harmful conformity rather than improved reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-agent debate (MAD) methods for large language models assume that when multiple agents converge on an answer, the process reflects useful deliberation and leads to better reasoning and accuracy. However, convergence could also arise from undesirable mechanisms such as social compliance, instability, or persuasion by flawed arguments. There is a need to rigorously decompose these mechanisms to understand when MAD genuinely helps reasoning versus when it merely spreads errors.

Method: The authors introduce a three-source decomposition framework that separates answer changes into (1) spontaneous instability, (2) stance-induced conformity, and (3) reasoning-induced persuasion. They design controlled counterfactual conditions to isolate each mechanism. Experiments are conducted primarily on MMLU-Pro, with robustness checks on GPQA-Diamond and across three LLM families. They also run a controlled information-gradient experiment where agents are exposed to argumentation of varying informativeness, including vacuous reasoning, to measure its persuasive effect. Using features from the initial debate round (Round 0), they train predictive models to identify cases at high risk of harmful conformity and evaluate a targeted intervention strategy aimed at reducing such risk.

Result: In the main MMLU-Pro experiments, 37% of agent-question responses change under self-reflection alone, evidencing considerable spontaneous instability in single-agent reasoning. Strict conformity accounts for 29% of changes in the main setting and is largely harmful, with 57–77% of such conformist shifts going from correct to incorrect answers across multiple model replications. The information-gradient experiment shows that even vacuous reasoning leads to 20–39% adoption of errors among previously resistant agents, indicating that reasoning-like presentation itself has strong persuasive power irrespective of content quality. Harmful conformity is predictable using Round 0 features, achieving an AUC of 0.79, and a risk-targeted intervention can reduce harmful conformity by 13.6 percentage points (p < 0.001). However, when correctness labels and self-reflection controls are unavailable, merely suppressing peer adoption does not yield accuracy gains because it indiscriminately limits both harmful and beneficial influences.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that much of the observed convergence in multi-agent LLM debates arises from instability and social conformity rather than genuine reasoning improvements. Conformity is often harmful, systematically propagating errors, and even content-poor but reasoning-like arguments exert notable persuasive effects. While harmful conformity can be predicted and partially mitigated with targeted interventions, blindly reducing agent-to-agent influence without additional signals (like correctness labels or self-reflection baselines) does not reliably improve accuracy. The work implies that future MAD designs must explicitly control for and manage these distinct mechanisms if they aim to harness collaboration for better reasoning instead of error amplification.

Abstract: Multi-agent debate (MAD) is a promising strategy for improving LLM reasoning, but when agents converge on a shared answer, it is unclear whether that convergence reflects genuine deliberation or social compliance. We show that the conventional answer flip rate conflates three distinct mechanisms: spontaneous instability, stance-induced conformity, and reasoning-induced persuasion. Our three-source decomposition framework isolates each through controlled counterfactual conditions. In the primary MMLU-Pro setting, 37% of agent-question observations change under self-reflection alone, while robustness tests show substantial model-dependent instability across GPQA-Diamond and three model families; strict conformity is 29% in the primary setting and remains predominantly harmful across model replications (57-77% correct-to-wrong). A controlled information-gradient experiment reveals that even vacuous reasoning is associated with 20-39% error adoption among resistant agents, with reasoning-like presentation carrying substantial persuasive weight. Harmful conformity can be predicted from Round 0 features (AUC = 0.79), and risk-targeted intervention reduces it by 13.6 percentage points (p < 0.001). However, without correctness labels or self-reflection controls, reducing peer adoption does not improve accuracy, because harmful and beneficial influence cannot be distinguished.

</details>


### [55] [Momento: Evaluating Persistent Memory and Reasoning with Multi-Session Agentic Conversations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00832)
*Adril Putra Merin,David Anugraha,Ayu Purwarianti,Genta Indra Winata*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents Momento, a benchmark for evaluating AI agents in multi-session, persistent task environments that require handling evolving user states and goals over time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing agentic AI benchmarks typically evaluate performance within a single, isolated session. They fail to capture realistic long-term interactions where agents must remember and correctly interpret past actions, preferences, and decisions. The authors want to address this gap to better assess whether agents can support persistent, personalized user goals in service-like scenarios.

Method: The authors design Momento, a benchmark consisting of multi-session service environments where agents must use tools, plan over multiple steps, and handle temporal dependencies across sessions. Tasks are structured so that agents must infer and update user state, revisit and re-validate past information, and adapt to evolving user goals. They then evaluate current state-of-the-art agents on this benchmark.

Result: Experiments show that current agents perform poorly on Momento. The main failure mode is misestimating the user’s current state: agents over-trust prior session history as if it were still valid, instead of recognizing it may be outdated and needs confirmation or re-validation. This leads to incorrect or suboptimal actions in later sessions.

Conclusion: The study concludes that there is a significant gap between current agent capabilities and the requirements of realistic, long-horizon human-agent interactions. To bridge this gap, future agentic AI systems must better model, monitor, and update user state over time, treating historical context as potentially stale information that requires active validation in new sessions.

Abstract: Recent advances in agentic AI have enabled agents to complete complex tasks through tool use, reasoning, and multi-step planning. Yet existing benchmarks evaluate agents within a single session, ignoring past actions, stated preferences, and prior decisions that agents must integrate to fulfill personalized user goals. We introduce Momento, a benchmark for persistent agentic task completion in multi-session service environments, requiring agents to take consequential, tool-mediated actions while resolving temporal dependencies and evolving user goals across sessions. Experimental results reveal that current agents fail primarily through misestimation of user state, treating prior session history as a reliable proxy for current context rather than stale information requiring re-validation, highlighting a substantial gap between current agent capabilities and realistic long-horizon human-agent interaction.

</details>


### [56] [IDEAFix: Evaluation Framework for Creative Defixation Prompting in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00875)
*F. Carichon,S. Sharma,M. Girard,R. Rampa,G. Farnadi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: IDEAFix is a controlled evaluation framework to study how LLMs generate creative, divergent ideas and how prompting and task design affect originality and diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is disagreement about how creative LLMs truly are: some work shows they can outperform humans, while others show limitations like fixation and homogenized outputs. Current evaluations either use overly narrow tasks that miss goal-oriented creativity or broad settings that mix many factors, making it hard to isolate what drives performance. In particular, the effect of structured prompting strategies on idea generation is not well understood.

Method: The authors design IDEAFix, a framework that evaluates divergent thinking in open-ended idea generation. LLMs are prompted to generate multiple original solutions to short design scenarios. These scenarios are systematically varied along controlled task attributes and combined with different defixation (structured) prompting strategies. This allows the authors to compare how changes in task formulation, attribute selection, and prompting shape the ideas that models produce.

Result: Experiments using IDEAFix show that both how a task is described (formulation) and which attributes are highlighted significantly impact LLM performance. Simple structured prompting strategies measurably increase the originality of generated ideas. Despite these gains, models consistently exhibit output homogenization—different models often converge on similar kinds of solutions—indicating persistent limits in their ability to produce diverse ideas.

Conclusion: IDEAFix offers a controlled and extensible way to analyze LLM creativity, especially divergent thinking in idea generation. It demonstrates that prompt and task design can meaningfully improve originality but also reveals inherent constraints in LLMs’ capacity to generate truly diverse solutions. The framework can be used to systematically study and compare mechanisms and interventions aimed at enhancing LLM creativity.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for tasks involving creative problem solving and idea generation. However, there is a lack of consensus concerning their creative capabilities: some studies report superior performances compared to humans, while others highlight structural limitations such as fixation and the homogenization of outputs. Existing evaluation approaches either rely on narrow, decontextualized tasks that do not capture goal-oriented generation or on broader settings that confound multiple aspects of the creative process, making it difficult to isolate the effects of task formulation, prompting, and evaluation design. Significantly, the role of structured prompting strategies in shaping idea generation remains underexplored. Therefore, we introduce IDEAFix, an evaluation framework for analyzing divergent thinking in open-ended idea generation tasks. We prompt models to generate multiple original solutions to controlled variations of short design scenarios, task attributes, and defixation prompting strategies. This design enables systematic analysis of how structured guidance influences LLMs' idea generation. Our results show that both task formulation and attribute selection significantly affect models' performance, and that simple prompting strategies can boost the originality of solutions. However, we also observe persistent output homogenization across models, confirming inherent limits in their ability to generate diverse solutions. Overall, IDEAFix provides a controlled, extensible framework for studying the mechanisms underlying LLMs' creativity.

</details>


### [57] [Chunking Methods on Retrieval-Augmented Generation - Effectiveness Evaluation Against Computational Cost and Limitations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00881)
*Mateusz Śmigielski,Michał Rajkowski,Mateusz Zbrocki,Michał Bernacki-Janson,Karol Kunicki,Julianna Godziszewska,Maciej Piasecki,Konrad Wojtasik*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper systematically investigates how different chunking strategies affect Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, revealing that chunking, often seen as trivial preprocessing, in fact raises important, underexplored challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although RAG improves LLMs by combining retrieval with generation, the chunking step—how documents are split before indexing—is usually handled with simple fixed-size or semantic methods. Recently, many new chunking strategies have been proposed, each often tuned to specific data and claiming superiority, but without broad, systematic comparison. This fragmentation makes it hard to know which methods work best in general and what trade-offs they entail. The authors are motivated to fill this gap by rigorously and comprehensively evaluating a wide range of chunking techniques and clarifying the real challenges chunking creates in RAG pipelines.

Method: The authors conduct a systematic evaluation of numerous chunking methods used in RAG, including traditional fixed-size and semantic chunking as well as more recent, specialized strategies. They compare these approaches across diverse use cases and data types, examining their impact on RAG performance. Through controlled experiments, they analyze how chunking decisions affect retrieval and generation quality and identify common issues and failure modes introduced at this preprocessing step.

Result: The study finds that many proposed chunking strategies do not consistently outperform basic fixed-size or semantic chunking across varied tasks and datasets. Performance gains tend to be narrow and context-dependent. The experiments reveal that chunking can significantly influence the effectiveness of RAG, introducing subtle but impactful issues—such as information fragmentation, context loss, and retrieval inconsistencies—that are often ignored when chunking is treated as a trivial preprocessing step.

Conclusion: Chunking is not a simple, one-size-fits-all preprocessing operation in RAG systems; instead, it is a critical design choice with complex, scenario-dependent effects. The paper concludes that existing chunking methods lack robust, generalizable advantages and that their evaluation has been insufficiently systematic. The authors highlight the need for more principled, standardized evaluation frameworks and more thoughtful chunking strategies that explicitly account for the challenges they identify, urging practitioners to treat chunking as a core component of RAG system design rather than an afterthought.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has demonstrated significant capabilities in enhancing the performance of Large Language Models (LLMs). One of the key tasks in RAG systems is the chunking process. Traditionally, fixed-size chunking and semantic chunking have been the standard approaches. However, interest in chunking strategies has been increasing, leading to a growing number of proposed methods that often claim improved performance over these conventional techniques. Many of these approaches are tailored to specific use cases and data types, with limited evidence of their effectiveness across diverse scenarios. As a result, it remains challenging to directly compare different techniques and assess their relative strengths. To the best of our knowledge, this study is the first to systematically evaluate the effectiveness of a wide range of chunking methods and emphasize the underlying challenges of chunking strategies in RAG systems. While chunking is commonly treated as a simple preprocessing step, we show that it introduces a range of impactful and often overlooked issues.

</details>


### [58] [Citation Grounding: Detecting and Reducing LLM Citation Hallucinations via Legal Citation Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00898)
*Volodymyr Ovcharov*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a metric and a training method to automatically detect and reduce hallucinated legal citations by large language models, using a massive citation graph built from Ukrainian court decisions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models frequently fabricate or misuse legal citations, which is risky in real legal practice. There is no scalable, automated way to measure how often this happens or to train models to avoid it, especially without relying on costly human annotation. The authors want a principled, fine-grained way to evaluate citation correctness and a method to improve models using only existing court data.

Method: 1) Build a large citation graph from 100.8M Ukrainian court decisions, encoding statute nodes and their citation edges with temporal information. 2) Define Citation Grounding (CG) as a metric with three components: precision (does the cited law exist), relevance (is it contextually appropriate), and temporality (was it valid at the relevant time). 3) Evaluate CG on answers to 100 Ukrainian legal queries across four commercial LLMs via AWS Bedrock plus a RAG-based production system. 4) Propose Citation Grounding DPO (CG-DPO): automatically generate preference pairs by corrupting true citations from court decisions via four targeted corruption strategies, and then fine-tune a Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct model with LoRA using DPO-style training to prefer correct over corrupted citations.

Result: Measured CG scores for the tested systems range between 0.791 and 0.873, showing that 13–21% of generated citations are hallucinated despite reasonably high overall performance. The CG-DPO training yields a model that can distinguish valid from corrupted citations with 98.5% mean validation accuracy, and a large positive reward margin (+14.9) that is stable across random seeds (std < 0.3 percentage points). The authors also release the large-scale citation graph, benchmark framework, and CG-DPO dataset to the community.

Conclusion: Citation hallucinations in legal LLMs are both common and measurable. The proposed Citation Grounding metric provides a structured way to dissect hallucination types, while CG-DPO offers an automated, annotation-free training strategy that substantially improves a model’s ability to recognize incorrect citations. The released resources can support future research on safer, more reliable legal AI systems.

Abstract: Large language models systematically hallucinate legal citations -- fabricating statute references, citing repealed provisions, and confusing jurisdictions -- yet no automated method exists to measure or reduce this behavior at scale. We propose citation grounding (CG), a metric that verifies LLM-generated legal citations against a ground-truth citation graph extracted from 100.8 million Ukrainian court decisions (502 million edges, 21,736 unique statute nodes). CG decomposes into three components -- citation precision (does the cited provision exist?), citation relevance (is it contextually appropriate?), and citation temporality (was it valid at the relevant date?) -- enabling differential diagnosis of hallucination types. Empirical evaluation on 100 Ukrainian legal queries across five systems -- four commercial LLMs via AWS Bedrock (Claude Haiku 4.5, Mistral Pixtral Large, Amazon Nova Pro/Lite) and one RAG-augmented production system -- reveals CG ranging from 0.791 to 0.873, with 13-21% of citations hallucinated. To reduce hallucinations without human annotation, we introduce Citation Grounding DPO (CG-DPO): a method that constructs preference pairs algorithmically by corrupting verified citations from real court decisions via four targeted strategies. On a dataset of 2,244 court decisions, a Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct model fine-tuned with LoRA achieves 98.5% mean validation accuracy in distinguishing correct from corrupted citations (rewards margin +14.9, std < 0.3 pp across 3 seeds). The citation graph, evaluation framework, and CG-DPO dataset are released as open resources.

</details>


### [59] [MLLM-Microscope: Unlocking Hidden Structure Within Multimodal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00909)
*Ravil Mussabayev,Rustam Mussabayev*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces MLLM-Microscope, a system to probe and quantify geometric properties (linearity, intrinsic dimension, anisotropy) of token embeddings in multimodal large language models across layers, and uses it to compare LLaVA-NeXT and OmniFusion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While MLLMs achieve strong performance, their internal multimodal representations and how different fusion strategies affect them are poorly understood. There is a need for systematic tools to inspect hidden states of multimodal models to guide architecture and training choices.

Method: Design a probing system (MLLM-Microscope) that measures linearity, intrinsic dimensionality, and anisotropy of both main and residual token streams across transformer layers, separately for text and image tokens. Apply this analysis pipeline to two SOTA MLLMs, LLaVA-NeXT and OmniFusion, using ScienceQA as the multimodal benchmark dataset.

Result: Both models show highly linear behavior in main and residual streams for text and image tokens across layers. LLaVA-NeXT’s image token linearity slightly declines with depth, whereas OmniFusion’s stays stable. OmniFusion’s image token intrinsic dimension remains higher than LLaVA-NeXT’s across layers, and OmniFusion maintains consistently low anisotropy throughout the network.

Conclusion: The geometric properties of token embeddings (linearity, dimension, anisotropy) differ systematically between LLaVA-NeXT and OmniFusion and appear closely tied to the models’ modality fusion strategies. MLLM-Microscope can surface such differences, suggesting that fusion design critically shapes internal representations, and that these diagnostics can inform future MLLM architecture and optimization choices.

Abstract: This work presents MLLM-Microscope, a novel system designed for analyzing the hidden representations within Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). Our system evaluates the linearity, intrinsic dimension, and anisotropy of multimodal token embeddings across transformer layers. Utilizing the ScienceQA dataset, we evaluate two state-of-the-art MLLMs, LLaVA-NeXT and OmniFusion. We find that both the main and residual streams for tokens of both modalities exhibit highly linear behaviors across transformer layers. However, LLaVA-NeXT's image tokens reveal a slight decline in linearity, whereas OmniFusion's remain consistent. Image token dimensions in OmniFusion remain consistently higher across layers compared to LLaVA-NeXT. Also, the OmniFusion's anisotropy is observed to stay consistently low throughout the layers. These findings suggest that the inner workings of MLLMs highly depend on the nature of modality fusion performed before passing the token sequence into LLM. This and other new potential insights obtainable from our system are surely capable of enhancing our understanding of the inner workings of MLLMs, informing future model design and optimization.

</details>


### [60] [Towards Lightweight Reliability: Using Soft Prompts for Hallucination Mitigation in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00919)
*S M Tahmid Siddiqui,Akib Jawad Ononto,Anoop Singhal,Latifur Khan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a parameter-efficient soft-prompting method (RCSP) that reduces hallucinations and encourages abstention in LLM-based QA while maintaining factual recall.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models are widely used but prone to hallucinations, which is especially risky in high-stakes domains. Existing mitigation often requires expensive fine-tuning or harms recall. The paper aims to provide a more reliable and efficient way to curb hallucinations and promote responsible abstention without retraining the entire model.

Method: The authors introduce Responsible Contrastive Soft Prompting (RCSP), which learns soft prompts via a composite loss that: (1) penalizes hallucinatory answers, (2) rewards abstention when the model is uncertain, and (3) preserves or improves factual recall. They incorporate contrastive loss to distinguish good vs. bad behaviors, curriculum learning to gradually introduce harder examples, and KL regularization to keep the prompted model close to the base model. The approach is evaluated using an LLM-as-a-Judge framework on five QA datasets with Gemma 3 (12B) and Llama 3.1 (8B) backbones.

Result: Across five generative QA benchmarks, RCSP reduces hallucinations and increases appropriate abstentions while maintaining or improving factual recall, leading to better F-scores than standard reasoning and instruction prompts. It achieves these gains by training only a small fraction of parameters compared to full fine-tuning or heavier adaptation methods.

Conclusion: Soft-prompt-based RCSP offers a modular, computationally efficient way to make LLM QA systems more reliable. By jointly optimizing for low hallucination, sensible abstention, and high recall, the method achieves a better trade-off than typical prompting baselines, suggesting soft prompts are a practical tool for reliability-focused LLM deployment.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have seen widespread adoption across various domains, yet their reliability is frequently undermined by hallucinations - responses that are plausible-sounding but factually incorrect. In high-stakes domains, these errors can reduce trust and introduce real-world risk. To address this challenge, we present a parameter-efficient approach that uses soft prompts to mitigate hallucinated content and promote responsible abstention in generative question-answering (QA) tasks.
  Our method, called Responsible Contrastive Soft Prompting (RCSP), uses a composite loss to train soft prompts that balance three goals: suppressing hallucinatory content, encouraging abstention under uncertainty, and preserving or improving factual recall. To achieve these goals, we incorporate contrastive loss, curriculum learning, and KL regularization into our training mechanism. We evaluate our approach on five diverse generative QA datasets using an LLM-as-a-Judge framework. Experimental results on the Gemma 3 (12B) and Llama 3.1 (8B) backbones demonstrate that RCSP effectively balances factual recall with hallucination suppression and abstention, yielding a generally superior F-score over standard reasoning and instruction-based prompting baselines. Notably, these improvements are achieved by training only a fraction of the parameters required by other tuning techniques. Our results demonstrate that soft prompts provide a modular and computationally efficient path toward improving LLM reliability.

</details>


### [61] [Detection vs. Execution: Single-Bucket Probes Miss Half the Mamba-2 State Sink](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00930)
*Yuhang Jiang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper shows that in Mamba-2, standard probing methods can detect a representational pattern (the “state sink”) without actually finding the main circuit that executes the relevant computation, revealing a systematic gap between representation and causation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mechanistic interpretability often assumes that if a probe finds a feature (like a BOS/newline signature) in some neurons or heads, those components are the ones doing the corresponding computation. The authors want to test this assumption in Mamba-2 and understand how its “state sink” structure actually implements behavior over long contexts.

Method: They analyze the state sink phenomenon (strong Delta-gate activation on boundary tokens) in Mamba-2 using probing and causal interventions. They train probes (single-bucket and multi-class) on BOS/newline signatures, perform ablations of specific head sets, compare representational similarity versus causal impact, and run controlled channel-bucketing experiments to separate execution from detection circuits.

Result: Single-bucket probes mainly identify a small set of BOS-specialist heads that genuinely drive BOS-context and newline-target predictions, but miss a larger set of “dual” heads that share the same representational signature yet have much weaker causal effects. Multi-class aggregation of the same probe recovers these dual heads, which are more representationally similar to BOS/newline but are functionally less important. Ablating BOS-specialist heads destroys RULER NIAH retrieval performance at long context length, while ablating size-matched complementary heads does not, and random channel-bucketing shows this is not just about granularity but about the shared Delta projection in Mamba-2.

Conclusion: Probes that detect a representational signature do not necessarily identify the core execution circuit; in Mamba-2, the state sink splits into execution (BOS-specialist) and detection (dual) circuits with similar representations but very different causal roles. Reliable circuit identification requires class-conditional ablation rather than relying solely on representational similarity (e.g., cosine), and mechanistic interpretability must distinguish detection from execution circuits, especially at coarse substrate granularities.

Abstract: Mechanistic interpretability often assumes that probes identifying a representational signature also identify the circuit executing the corresponding computation.
