We show that this assumption can fail systematically in Mamba-2. Studying the state sink (disproportionate Delta-gate activation on boundary tokens, analogous to the attention sink), we find that single-bucket probes recover only a small execution layer while missing a much larger detection layer with the same representational signature.
  In Mamba-2, the state sink decomposes into two functional head sets. Single-bucket BOS-specialist heads (about 5% of heads at 2.7B) causally support both BOS-context and newline-target predictions across model scales and corpora. Dual heads (27-35% of heads, recovered by multi-class aggregation of the same probe) show stronger BOS-newline representational similarity but substantially weaker causal effects under ablation. Representational similarity does not imply functional equivalence.
  This distinction matters for downstream behaviour: ablating BOS-specialist heads collapses RULER NIAH retrieval accuracy from 1.00 to 0.00 at 1024 context length in both Mamba-1 2.8B and Mamba-2 2.7B, while size-matched complements preserve baseline performance. A random channel-bucketing control rules out substrate granularity alone, implicating Mamba-2's head-shared Delta projection. Probe-derived specialty can identify execution circuits; at coarse granularity the same probe also recovers detection circuits, and separating them requires class-conditional ablation rather than class-conditional cosine.

</details>


### [62] [HypothesisMed: Inference-Time Answer Fusion and Structured Hypothesis-Space Reporting for Biomedical Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00971)
*Md Motaleb Hossen Manik,Ge Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces HypothesisMed, an inference-time reliability pipeline that augments biomedical multiple-choice QA with structured reliability signals (SPACE labels, confidence, parseability) and answer fusion, showing these reliability dimensions are separable from raw answer accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing biomedical QA evaluations focus mainly on answer accuracy, which ignores whether model outputs are parseable, follow reliability instructions, diagnose weak answer spaces, or avoid overconfident errors. In safety‑critical biomedical settings, these limitations make it hard to treat LLMs as trustworthy workflow components. The authors are motivated to build an evaluation and inference framework that explicitly measures and improves structured reliability aspects beyond simple correctness.

Method: They design HypothesisMed, an inference-time pipeline that: (1) runs multiple prompting strategies (direct, chain-of-thought, HypothesisMed‑v3 style prompting), (2) fuses their answers to select a final prediction, and (3) uses HypothesisMed‑v3 to assign SPACE labels to each question’s answer space—VALID, INCOMPLETE, or CONTRADICTED—along with confidence scores. They then benchmark several LLMs (Qwen2.5‑7B, Phi‑4‑mini, DeepSeek‑R1‑32B, BioMistral‑7B) on three biomedical multiple-choice datasets (MedQA, MedMCQA, PubMedQA) using 1,000‑example subsets and larger 10k+ evaluations for two models. They further conduct a 12k‑example SPACE stress test to assess how well models diagnose answer-space reliability.

Result: Across models and datasets, the HypothesisMed pipeline increases weighted accuracy relative to each model’s best direct or chain-of-thought baseline, while also improving the fraction of examples with correctly parsed outputs and SPACE annotations. For large‑scale runs, answer fusion substantially boosts Phi‑4‑mini’s accuracy (0.4296→0.5192), while for Qwen2.5‑7B, pure chain-of-thought slightly outperforms fusion on bare accuracy but fusion yields perfect parse and SPACE coverage and reduces false confident commitments. The SPACE stress test reveals that answer-space diagnosis is still challenging: SPACE accuracy is only 0.3074 for Qwen2.5‑7B and 0.4168 for Phi‑4‑mini.

Conclusion: The study concludes that answer accuracy, parseability, structured reliability reporting (SPACE labels), calibration, and false‑commitment behavior are distinct capabilities that should be evaluated separately. HypothesisMed offers a reproducible inference-time framework to treat biomedical QA models as auditable components, emphasizing structured reliability constraints instead of solely pursuing state-of-the-art accuracy. The work positions itself as a practical reliability and evaluation toolkit rather than a claim of universal SOTA performance.

Abstract: Biomedical question answering with large language models is commonly evaluated using answer accuracy, but answer accuracy alone does not indicate whether a model can produce parseable outputs, follow structured reliability instructions, recognize weak answer spaces, or avoid confident incorrect commitments. This paper presents HypothesisMed, an inference-time reliability pipeline for biomedical multiple-choice question answering. It combines direct, chain-of-thought, HypothesisMed-v3 prompting, and answer fusion. The final answer is selected by fusion, while HypothesisMed-v3 supplies SPACE labels and confidence information. SPACE labels mark the answer space as VALID, INCOMPLETE, or CONTRADICTED. We evaluate Qwen2.5-7B, Phi-4-mini, DeepSeek-R1-32B, and BioMistral-7B on MedQA, MedMCQA, and PubMedQA using 1,000 examples per dataset. The pipeline improves weighted accuracy over each model's best direct or chain-of-thought baseline while increasing parse and SPACE coverage. We also scale evaluation to Qwen2.5-7B and Phi-4-mini using 10,183 examples per model. Fusion improves Phi-4-mini accuracy from 0.4296 to 0.5192, while Qwen2.5-7B chain-of-thought remains slightly higher in answer accuracy. However, Qwen2.5-7B fusion achieves complete parse and SPACE coverage with much lower false commitment. A 12,000-example SPACE stress test shows answer-space diagnosis remains difficult, with SPACE accuracy of 0.3074 for Qwen2.5-7B and 0.4168 for Phi-4-mini. These results show that answer accuracy, parseability, structured reliability reporting, calibration behavior, and false-commitment behavior are separable capabilities. The main contribution is not a universal state-of-the-art claim, but a reproducible inference-time framework for evaluating biomedical question answering models as auditable workflow components under structured reliability constraints.

</details>


### [63] [Lost in Delusion: Examining LLM Safety Under User Delusions and Distress](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00975)
*Andrew Aquilina,Chetna Nihalani,Vasudha Varadarajan,Nathan S. Fishbein,Yu-Ru Lin,Maarten Sap*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how large language model (LLM) chatbots handle users in psychological distress when that distress is entangled with delusional beliefs, revealing that models often recognize distress but fail to provide appropriate safety interventions in delusional contexts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are increasingly used as first-line support for people in psychological distress, including those with delusional beliefs. Existing safety research mainly looks at overall therapeutic quality or single-turn crisis detection, which does not reflect how models behave in realistic, multi-turn conversations where distress and delusion are intertwined. There is a need to understand whether and how delusional framing changes models’ safety behavior over time.

Method: The authors run matched, multi-turn conversation simulations using clinically grounded personas across six different LLMs. For each conversation where distress is embedded in delusional content, they construct a paired “distress-only” control conversation without delusional framing, to isolate the effect of delusions. They then compare how often models recognize distress, offer safety interventions, and how their behaviors evolve over turns. They further test different prompting strategies, including asking models to explicitly assess distress and using delusion-aware prompts with explicit response guidance, and they assess reliance on a delusion classifier to trigger such prompts.

Result: The study finds a recognition–intervention gap: LLMs detect user distress at similar rates in both delusional and non-delusional framings, but they are much less likely—up to 4.5 times less—to provide safety interventions when distress is embedded in delusions. This failure correlates with the model increasingly accepting the user’s delusional premises as the conversation progresses, not merely with providing emotional validation. Attempts to fix this by prompting models to explicitly assess distress actually worsen performance in delusional contexts, whereas delusion-aware prompting with explicit guidance can close the gap, but only when a preceding delusion classifier correctly flags the conversation—and that classifier performs worst on the models most in need of help.

Conclusion: Delusional framing systematically undermines the safety behavior of LLM mental-health chatbots: they recognize distress but fail to act on it once it is embedded in delusional content. Effective mitigation requires treating delusional framing itself as a distinct risk signal that should override usual conversational accommodation, and relying on robust delusion detection. Simple prompts that just ask models to evaluate distress are insufficient and can backfire, highlighting the need for specialized, delusion-aware safety mechanisms before such systems are safely deployed in mental health contexts.

Abstract: LLM chatbots increasingly serve as a first source of support for people in psychological distress, including those whose distress is entangled with delusional beliefs. Prior work on LLM mental-health safety largely evaluates general therapeutic quality or single-turn crisis detection, leaving unclear how models behave when distress is intertwined with delusion over sustained conversations. We address this gap with matched multi-turn simulations, across clinically grounded personas and six LLMs, that pair each delusional conversation with a distress-only control to isolate the effect of delusional framing. This reveals a recognition-intervention gap: models detect distress at comparable rates regardless of framing, yet sharply fail to act on it once distress is embedded in delusion, with safety interventions suppressed by up to 4.5x. The failure tracks accumulated acceptance of the user's premises rather than emotional validation. Worse, the intuitive fix of prompting models to assess user distress backfires under delusional framing; only delusion-aware prompting with explicit response guidance closes the gap, and even this depends on a delusion classifier that is itself unreliable on the most vulnerable models. Safe deployment therefore requires treating delusional framing as a distinct risk signal that overrides conversational accommodation.

</details>


### [64] [Robust Asynchronous Planning via Auto-Formalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00981)
*Jiayi Zhang,Jianing Yin,Ben Zhou,Li Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how large language models (LLMs) perform on complex asynchronous planning tasks and finds that using a constraint-programming formalization (CP-SAT) scales far better than direct plan generation or PDDL-style planning languages, especially under execution-time updates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world planning problems often involve asynchronous events: actions with different durations, concurrency, and constraints that can change during execution. Existing LLM planning benchmarks mostly consider simpler, synchronous settings, so they don’t reveal how well LLMs handle realistic, large-scale asynchronous planning. The authors want to understand whether LLMs should act as direct planners or as translators into formal planning representations, and which representations make planning scalable and robust.

Method: The authors first unify various asynchronous planning challenges (non-uniform durations, concurrency, execution-time constraints and updates) into a single formal framework. They then build three large-scale benchmarks that exercise these challenges. They compare three paradigms: (1) Planner, where the LLM directly outputs action sequences; (2) PDDL2.1 Formalizer, where the LLM converts tasks into PDDL2.1 for a planner; and (3) CP-SAT Formalizer, where the LLM encodes tasks as constraint satisfaction problems for a CP-SAT solver. They evaluate plan accuracy as dependency graph size grows (5–100 actions) and under dynamic execution-time updates, and perform diagnostics on representation faithfulness (how consistently LLMs maintain predicates, effects, and goals).

Result: As problem size increases, direct LLM planning (Planner) degrades from 96% to 5% accuracy, and PDDL2.1 Formalizer from 13% to 0%, whereas CP-SAT Formalizer maintains high performance, averaging 94% and still achieving 83% accuracy at 100 actions. Faithfulness analyses show that the predicate-centric PDDL2.1 representation is fragile for LLMs, which struggle to keep predicates, effects, and goals aligned, while the more general constraint-based CP-SAT representation is much more robust. When planning constraints must be updated at execution time, performance drops sharply across the board (Planner 23.9%, PDDL2.1 0.7%, CP-SAT 46.1%), but introducing a state-aware repair strategy that modifies only event-induced constraints restores CP-SAT Formalizer performance to 84.5%.

Conclusion: LLMs alone are poor at scaling to large, asynchronous planning problems when directly generating plans or when forced to work with brittle predicate-based languages like PDDL2.1. The critical factor for scalability is the choice of formal representation: encoding planning problems as general constraint satisfaction (CP-SAT) enables LLMs to remain effective even as problem size and asynchrony grow. Moreover, dynamic execution-time changes are very challenging, but a targeted, state-aware repair mechanism can largely recover performance for the CP-SAT-based approach. Overall, LLMs should be used primarily as formalizers to robust constraint solvers rather than as standalone planners, especially in realistic asynchronous domains.

Abstract: LLMs can plan by either generating action sequences directly as a Planner or translating tasks into domain specific language for an external solver as a Formalizer. While most real-world tasks are asynchronous with non-uniform durations, concurrency, and execution-time constraints, existing benchmarks hardly cover them. We unify these asynchronous planning challenges under a single formulation and introduce the first three benchmarks that address each at scale. We conclude that the choice of formal representation primarily determines whether planning scales: as dependency graphs grow from 5 to 100 actions, Planner collapses from 96% to 5% plan accuracy and PDDL2.1 Formalizer from 13% to 0%, while CP-SAT Formalizer averages 94% and still achieves 83% at 100 actions. Faithfulness diagnostics show that PDDL2.1's predicate-based planning representation becomes brittle compared to general constraint satisfaction programs, when LLMs must keep predicates, effects, and goals consistent. Execution-time updates of planning constraints further degrade performance sharply (Planner 23.9%, PDDL2.1 0.7%, CP-SAT 46.1%), but a state-aware repair strategy that updates only event-induced constraints recovers CP-SAT Formalizer to 84.5%.

</details>


### [65] [A Registry-Bound LLM Pipeline for Evidence-Grounded Trait Extraction across Tropical Plants, Aquatic Species, and Exotic Pets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00994)
*Jeff Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A pipeline uses large language models to extract structured, evidence-linked trait data for hundreds of thousands of tropical, aquatic, and pet species, with strong auditability mechanisms but no claim of full correctness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for scalable, structured, and auditable extraction of biological trait data from text for large numbers of species. Existing methods either do not scale, lack structure, or are not easily auditable or verifiable. The authors aim to show that LLMs can be harnessed to generate large trait databases if their outputs are constrained, linked to evidence, and versioned to support scientific curation.

Method: They build a registry-bound extraction pipeline around an LLM. The pipeline uses: (1) a versioned trait registry with 39 keys and a closed vocabulary, imposing a typed schema on every trait value; (2) a verbatim evidence quote for each row that must be drawn from the source text and supports the value; (3) a per-row confidence label, keeping only medium and high confidence rows; and (4) full multi-version preservation of outputs for reproducibility. This is run at scale on the Tropical Species Encyclopedia, targeting 409,880 species. They run the pipeline over 700k times and store the resulting trait records with associated evidence and metadata. They then apply three validation layers: automatic checking that quotes are literal substrings of sources; a manual quote-supports-value audit on a stratified sample excluding especially tricky (non-red-zone) traits; and a manual face-validity audit on a smaller sample of red-zone (hard or risky) traits.

Result: The pipeline produced 5,489,881 trait records across 409,820 of 409,880 target species (99.985% coverage), with 81.57% of rows labeled high confidence. Automated validation shows that 90.12% of the 5,427,588 evidence-bearing rows have evidence quotes that exactly appear in the source text, rising to 93.49% when excluding a specific compliance meta-trait. In manual validation, a 100-row stratified sample of non-red-zone records yielded 100/100 correct quote-supports-value judgments (binomial lower bound about 96.30%), and a 50-row red-zone sample yielded 50/50 face-valid Accept judgments (lower bound about 92.86%).

Conclusion: The work demonstrates a scalable LLM-based extraction pipeline for species traits, centered on a four-part auditability framework: registry-constrained schema, verbatim evidence quotes, confidence labeling, and version preservation. While the authors explicitly do not claim that all individual trait records are correct and emphasize the need for human curation, they show that high volumes of evidence-grounded, structured records can be produced with quantifiable support and audit trails. The primary contribution is thus methodological and infrastructural, offering a generalizable approach for building auditable LLM-derived scientific databases.

Abstract: We describe a registry-bound large-language-model extraction pipeline producing evidence-grounded structured trait records at scale, on cultivated tropical plant, aquatic, and pet species. Four mechanisms render LLM-derived rows auditable: a versioned 39-key closed-vocabulary trait registry constraining every admitted value to a typed schema; a per-row verbatim evidence quote tying each value to source text; a per-row confidence label (high or medium; low dropped pre-persist); and multi-version preservation. Applied to 409,880 publishable species from the Tropical Species Encyclopedia, the pipeline executed 706,220 runs and persisted 5,489,881 trait records across 409,820 species (99.985%), 81.57% at high confidence. We report three validation layers in descending evidentiary strength: at full population, 90.12% of 5,427,588 evidence-bearing rows have their quote as a verbatim source substring (93.49% excluding one compliance meta-trait); a quote-supports-value audit on n=100 stratified non-red-zone rows yielded 100/100 (lower bound 96.30%); face-validity on n=50 red-zone rows yielded 50/50 Accept (lower bound 92.86%). Per-record correctness is not claimed; 100% pending human curation. The contribution is the four-mechanism framework.

</details>


### [66] [Decoding in Order-Agnostic Language Models: Chain-Rule Deviation and Uniform Spreading](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00997)
*Lin Yao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper studies order-agnostic language models (OALMs) and shows that their learned conditional distributions depend on the token reveal order, proposes a diagnostic based on the variance of per-step log-confidence, and recommends reporting confidence mean and variance when evaluating decoding paths.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Order-agnostic language models, such as discrete diffusion language models, can generate sequences under arbitrary token reveal orders, but it is unclear whether their conditionals are consistent with a single joint distribution and how to meaningfully compare different decoding strategies. The paper aims to (1) test whether learned conditionals define a coherent joint, (2) understand the behavior of confidence-first decoding, and (3) develop better diagnostics for evaluating and comparing decoding paths beyond total likelihood.

Method: The authors analyze the log-likelihood of target sequences under different reveal orders for OALMs to test joint coherence, empirically study the reveal orders induced by confidence-first decoding, and introduce a theoretical uniform-spreading theorem relating target recoverability to how per-step confidence (log probabilities) is distributed across steps. They then propose using the variance of per-step log confidence, Var(log q_t), as a diagnostic and evaluate it on C4 and four downstream benchmarks, comparing structured paths vs. random token orders and relating variance to task correctness.

Result: They find that changing only the reveal order can change the target log-likelihood by up to 0.49 nats per token, demonstrating that OALM conditionals do not correspond to a single coherent joint distribution and that likelihood conflates difficulty with path artifacts. They show that confidence-first decoding, though formally order-agnostic, produces reveal orders that closely resemble left-to-right ordering on content tokens. Empirically, they validate the uniform-spreading perspective by showing that the variance of per-step log confidence separates structured decoding paths from random orders and is consistently correlated with downstream correctness across C4 and multiple benchmarks.

Conclusion: Order-agnostic language models exhibit path-dependent likelihoods, so likelihood alone is an unreliable metric for comparing decoding paths. Confidence-first decoding de facto behaves similarly to left-to-right on content tokens. A more informative characterization of decoding behavior is given by the mean and variance of per-step confidence: low Var(log q_t) corresponds to more structured, effective decoding paths and correlates with better downstream performance. The authors recommend jointly reporting mean confidence and confidence variance when evaluating and comparing OALM decoding strategies.

Abstract: Order-agnostic language models (OALMs), including discrete diffusion language models (dLLMs), are trained to predict masked tokens under arbitrary conditioning sets, allowing sequences to be generated or scored under arbitrary reveal orders at inference time. In LLaDA-2.1, we report three findings. First, the learned conditionals are not exact factorizations of a coherent joint distribution: changing only the reveal order shifts target log-likelihood by up to 0.49 nats/token, so likelihood alone mixes content difficulty with path-dependent artifacts. Second, although confidence-first (CF) decoding is order-agnostic, its reveal orders are close to left-to-right (L2R) on content tokens. Third, we propose a complementary diagnostic based on the shape of the confidence trace. A uniform-spreading theorem shows that, at fixed total likelihood, target recoverability is maximized when per-step confidence is spread uniformly; the resulting deviation motivates $\mathrm{Var}(\log q_t)$ as a diagnostic for comparing decoding paths. Across C4 and four downstream benchmarks, low variance separates structured paths from random ordering, and variance is consistently associated with downstream correctness. These results support reporting mean confidence and confidence variance jointly when comparing OALM decoding paths.

</details>


### [67] [PolySpeech-100: A Large-Scale Benchmark for Speech Understanding Across 100+ Languages and Dialects](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01016)
*Sicheng Yang,Shulan Ruan,Shiwei Wu,Yu Liu,Lu Fan,Zhi Li,You He*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: PolySpeech-100 is a large multilingual and multi-dialect speech benchmark to evaluate native-level semantic comprehension of Speech-LLMs across 110 linguistic variants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Speech-LLM benchmarks mostly test simple ASR, are biased toward high-resource languages, and ignore dialectal variation. As E2E Speech-LLMs become capable of rich semantic reasoning, there is a need for an evaluation suite that covers many languages and dialects, tests higher-level understanding, and reflects real-world inclusivity.

Method: The authors construct PolySpeech-100, a benchmark spanning 110 linguistic variants, via a hybrid pipeline that combines gold-standard human recordings with instruction-driven synthetic speech. The dataset covers 19 Chinese dialects and 80+ low-resource languages and is designed to probe native-level semantic understanding. They then evaluate 22 state-of-the-art systems—including commercial and open-source E2E Speech-LLMs and cascade ASR+LLM setups—under standardized settings, including zero-shot and Chain-of-Thought prompting.

Result: Empirical results show: (1) open-source end-to-end Speech-LLMs outperform cascade ASR+LLM systems on heavily dialectal speech, suggesting direct audio modeling better captures paralinguistic and prosodic cues; (2) there is a large performance gap between commercial and open-source models, with open-source systems failing badly on low-resource languages; and (3) Chain-of-Thought prompting, under typical zero-shot usage, often harms speech understanding performance, indicating challenges in multimodal alignment for current models.

Conclusion: PolySpeech-100 provides a rigorous, inclusive benchmark for evaluating semantic-level speech understanding across many languages and dialects. It exposes key weaknesses of current Speech-LLMs—especially for low-resource languages and under CoT prompting—and is intended as a standard resource (with open data, demo, and code) to drive the development of more robust, omni-capable Speech-LLMs.

Abstract: While End-to-End (E2E) Speech-Large Language Models (Speech-LLMs) are rapidly evolving, their evaluation methodologies remain limited to the era of simple transcription. Existing benchmarks suffer from three critical limitations: a pronounced bias towards high-resource languages, a focus on low-level recognition (ASR) rather than semantic reasoning, and a neglect of regional dialects. To bridge this gap, we introduce PolySpeech-100, a massive-scale benchmark designed to assess `native-level' speech comprehension across 110 linguistic variants. We employ a novel hybrid construction pipeline that augments gold-standard human recordings with instruction-driven synthetic speech, allowing us to cover 19 distinct Chinese dialects and over 80 low-resource languages. Extensive evaluation of 22 state-of-the-art models (including Gemini-3, GPT-Audio, and Qwen2.5-Omni) yields pivotal insights. First, we demonstrate that open-source E2E models outperform Cascade (ASR+LLM) systems on heavy dialects, proving that direct audio processing preserves critical paralinguistic cues and prosodic features (e.g., intonation, stress) that are often lost in standard transcription. Second, we reveal a significant performance gap: while commercial models maintain robustness, open-source models suffer catastrophic degradation on low-resource languages. Finally, counter-intuitively, we observe that under standard zero-shot settings, Chain-of-Thought prompting frequently degrades speech understanding performance for most evaluated models, revealing a potential modality alignment gap in current architectures. PolySpeech-100 establishes a rigorous standard for the next generation of inclusive, omni-capable Speech-LLMs. The data, demo, and code are publicly available at https://github.com/YoungSeng/PolySpeech-100.

</details>


### [68] [Hybrid Verified Decoding: Learning to Allocate Verification in Speculative Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01019)
*Xin Su,Dawid Majchrowski,Fangyuan Yu,Vanshil Atul Shah,Sebastian Rogawski,Pawel Morkisz,Anahita Bhiwandiwalla,Phillip Howard*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes Hybrid Verified Decoding, a speculative decoding strategy for LLMs that dynamically chooses between cache-based and model-based draft sources by predicting how many drafted tokens will be accepted, achieving significant speedups, especially in agentic workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive decoding for LLMs is expensive because the model is invoked once per generated token. Speculative decoding can reduce this cost by drafting multiple tokens at once, but its actual speedup heavily depends on how many drafted tokens are eventually accepted by the target model. Parameter-free, cache-based drafting can sometimes propose long, cheap continuations, especially in structured or agentic settings, but drafts that appear promising at one step may lead to poor payoff at subsequent steps. There is a need for a method that can better anticipate the payoff of different draft sources and select among them at runtime to maximize speed and efficiency.

Method: The authors introduce Hybrid Verified Decoding, a decoding framework that uses both cache-based drafts and a model-based drafter. The key idea is to predict the eventual accepted length (payoff) of each cache draft before running the expensive verification step. The system then compares the predicted payoff of verifying a cache draft to the expected payoff of using a model-based drafter and chooses the option with higher estimated benefit. This introduces a payoff-guided runtime selection mechanism over draft sources during speculative decoding. The method is evaluated across three different LLMs and sixteen datasets, with particular attention to agentic and structured workloads where cache opportunities are more prevalent.

Result: Hybrid Verified Decoding achieves substantial speedups, particularly on agentic workflows. Across three LLMs and sixteen datasets, it consistently outperforms a strong baseline method, EAGLE3, in every reported setting, with an average speedup of 2.73x. The analysis shows that certain prompt structures create rich opportunities for cache reuse, and that the most beneficial (high-payoff) cache drafts are concentrated in a relatively small region of the draft space. The payoff prediction enables the system to focus verification effort on these high-yield drafts.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that runtime, payoff-guided draft selection is a powerful and under-explored dimension for improving speculative decoding. By accurately predicting the accepted length of cache drafts and using this to choose between cache verification and model-based drafting, Hybrid Verified Decoding reduces the amount of sequential decoding work and delivers robust speedups. The findings suggest that combining multiple draft sources with intelligent selection policies can substantially improve the efficiency of LLM generation, especially in agentic and structured prompting scenarios.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) generation remains expensive because autoregressive decoding calls the model once for each new token. Speculative decoding reduces this cost by drafting multiple tokens and verifying them with the target model in one step, but its speedup depends on how many drafted tokens are accepted. Parameter-free draft sources can propose long continuations at low cost in structured and agentic workloads, yet a cache match that looks promising at one generation step may have low payoff at the next. We propose Hybrid Verified Decoding, which predicts the accepted length of a cache draft before verification and uses this payoff estimate to choose between cache verification and a model-based drafter. Across three LLMs and sixteen datasets, Hybrid Verified Decoding is especially effective on agentic workflows, where it outperforms EAGLE3 in every setting with a 2.73x average speedup. Our analysis shows how prompt structure creates cache opportunities, how high-payoff cache drafts concentrate in a small part of the draft space, and how payoff-guided selection reduces sequential decoding work, pointing to runtime draft selection as a promising direction for speculative decoding.

</details>


### [69] [DSL-LLaDA: Scaling Continuous Denoising to 8B Masked Diffusion LMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01024)
*Longxuan Yu,Yunshu Wu,Yu Fu,Siheng Xiong,Rob Brekelmans,Hui Liu,Yue Dong,Greg Ver Steeg*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They adapt a discrete masked diffusion language model to perform continuous embedding-space denoising, enabling high‑quality, non‑repetitive long text generation within very few decoding steps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discrete masked diffusion language models (DLMs) decode in a small number of parallel steps, but under a fixed step budget they face a hard tradeoff: either generate short, high‑quality text or longer outputs that become repetitive or terminate poorly. Continuous denoising models could avoid this by evolving all token positions jointly in a continuous space, but training such models from scratch at scale is expensive and practically challenging. The authors want a scalable, efficient way to get the benefits of continuous denoising while reusing powerful pretrained masked DLMs.

Method: They start from a pretrained masked diffusion language model, LLaDA‑8B‑Instruct. Instead of retraining from scratch, they lightly adapt it via 1,000 steps of continued pretraining using Discrete Stochastic Localization (DSL). DSL replaces hard binary token masking with continuous Gaussian noise added to token embeddings, acting as a soft mask. The model is trained to denoise these noisy embeddings. At inference, they perform continuous denoising dynamics in embedding space (all positions updated jointly) and only map back to discrete tokens at the final step, thereby avoiding early hard token commitments. They compare against standard discrete masked diffusion training under the same compute budget as control baselines.

Result: The DSL‑adapted model, DSL‑LLaDA‑SDE, supports continuous inference over embeddings and outperforms baselines on zero‑shot summarization under very low step budgets (≤16 forward passes). It achieves the best ROUGE‑1 scores on four summarization benchmarks and largely avoids the tradeoff seen in standard iterative unmasking between premature termination and repetitive long outputs. Additionally, the same adaptation gives the model selective noisy‑state robustness: it can fix corrupted tokens while leaving already correct tokens unchanged. Control models trained with standard masked diffusion under the same compute do not show these advantages.

Conclusion: A large masked diffusion language model can be efficiently transformed into a continuous denoising model by a short continued pretraining phase with DSL noise instead of hard masks. This yields practical continuous embedding‑space inference that updates all positions jointly and delays discrete token decisions to the last step, resolving much of the length‑versus‑quality tradeoff at low step budgets. The DSL adaptation also confers desirable robustness properties—correcting noisy tokens while preserving clean ones—that do not emerge from standard masked diffusion training with comparable compute.

Abstract: Discrete Masked diffusion language models generate text by iterative parallel decoding, but few-step decoding suffers from a tradeoff between length and quality: with a fixed step budget, standard methods can generate a short, high-quality output, or they can produce long but repetitive text. Continuous denoising can sidestep this tradeoff by evolving all positions jointly in embedding space, but building such a model from scratch at scale remains an open problem. We show that a pretrained masked DLM can instead be lightly adapted to support continuous embedding-space denoising. Starting from LLaDA-8B-Instruct, we continue-pretrain for only 1,000 steps with Discrete Stochastic Localization (DSL), replacing binary masking with continuous per-token Gaussian noise as a soft mask. The adapted model supports continuous inference that evolves all positions jointly in embedding space and defers hard token commitment to the final step. On zero-shot summarization at low step budgets (<=16 forward passes), DSL-LLaDA-SDE achieves the best ROUGE-1 on all four benchmarks and largely avoids the premature-termination / repetition tradeoff of iterative unmasking. The same adaptation also yields selective noisy-state robustness: the model corrects corrupted tokens while preserving clean ones. Control experiments using standard masked diffusion training with the same compute demonstrate neither behavior.

</details>


### [70] [Revise, Don't Freeze: Sampler-Matched Training for Self-Correcting Masked Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01026)
*Longxuan Yu,Shaorong Zhang,Yu Fu,Hui Liu,Yue Dong,Greg Ver Steeg*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes D3IM, a parameter-free sampling method for masked diffusion language models that allows revising already visible tokens, and SCOPE, a post-training scheme to reduce a newly identified preservation bias, achieving large gains on reasoning and code benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Masked diffusion language models conceptually can revise every token at each denoising step, but standard sampling strategies commit tokens irreversibly once revealed, wasting the model’s inherent revision capability. Existing fixes rely on heuristics, auxiliary modules, or remasking to [MASK], and there is no principled sampler that directly updates visible tokens. Moreover, the behavior of such a sampler and the model limitations it exposes (like a tendency to preserve its own mistakes) have not been thoroughly analyzed.

Method: The authors derive D3IM as a corrector-style reverse update for masked diffusion language models. Unlike standard unmasking samplers, D3IM repeatedly updates all token positions, including already visible ones, in a parameter-free way without additional modules or extra denoising passes. Through this they uncover a model-side issue called preservation bias, where the model is inclined to repeat previously committed (possibly erroneous) tokens instead of changing them. To mitigate this, they introduce SCOPE (Self-Conditioned On Prediction Errors), a lightweight post-training method that conditions the model on its own prediction errors and simulates the D3IM sampling process during training so the model learns to correct earlier mistakes.

Result: Using LLaDA-8B with 64 denoising steps, the combination SCOPE+D3IM substantially outperforms the baseline LLaDA-8B with standard unmasking on several benchmarks: +13.0 absolute improvement on GSM8K (achieving 68.3%), +4.8 on MATH-500 (23.6%), +15.3 on HumanEval (29.3%), and +10.4 on MBPP (30.8%). The improvements become larger as the number of denoising steps increases for math and HumanEval tasks, indicating the method better leverages longer diffusion trajectories.

Conclusion: A principled, parameter-free sampler (D3IM) that directly revises visible tokens unlocks the latent revision capacity of masked diffusion language models but reveals an inherent preservation bias. By addressing this bias with the SCOPE post-training scheme, the authors significantly improve performance on mathematical reasoning and code generation benchmarks, especially with more denoising steps, demonstrating the importance of enabling and training models for iterative self-correction during the diffusion process.

Abstract: Masked diffusion language models (MDLMs) re-predict every position at each denoising step, but standard samplers commit tokens once revealed, leaving this revision capability unused. Existing approaches either add heuristic or learned mechanisms to revise committed tokens, or remask them back to [MASK] before re-predicting; a principled sampler that directly revises visible tokens without auxiliary modules remains underexplored. We introduce D3IM, a parameter-free sampler derived as a corrector-style reverse update that permits direct visible-to-visible revision without additional modules or auxiliary passes. D3IM also reveals a model-side obstacle we term preservation bias: the model tends to reproduce its own wrong committed tokens rather than correct them. We address this with SCOPE (Self-Conditioned On Prediction Errors), a lightweight post-training procedure that simulates D3IM's sampling process. On LLaDA-8B at 64 denoising steps, SCOPE+D3IM improves over the original LLaDA-8B with standard unmasking by +13.0 on GSM8K (68.3%), +4.8 on MATH-500 (23.6%), +15.3 on HumanEval (29.3%), and +10.4 on MBPP (30.8%), with gains that increase as more denoising steps are used on math and HumanEval.

</details>


### [71] [A Finite-Calibration Regime Map for LLM Judge Panels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01034)
*Bin Zhu,Yanghui Rao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper analyzes how to best calibrate panels of LLM judges under limited human-label budgets, comparing low-dimensional (scalar/reliability) aggregation to higher-dimensional joint output tables, and finds that simple scalar aggregation usually wins unless strong, estimable interactions exist between judges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM judge panels are widely used to evaluate model outputs, but their raw scores are biased and need calibration using human labels. There is a practical tradeoff: simple low-dimensional calibration models are easy to estimate with few labels but cannot capture complex interactions between judges, while rich joint models can capture interactions but require many labels and suffer from sparse data and unseen judge-output combinations. Practitioners lack guidance on when to use which type of calibrator under finite human-label budgets.

Method: The authors formalize the choice between low-dimensional stackers and joint output tables as a finite-calibration regime map. They introduce Finite-Calibration Panel Selection, a validation-based selector that, given a panel of LLM judges, chooses among judge paths, prefix lengths, and aggregator families (scalar/reliability models vs joint tables), using diagnostics on table sparsity and parametric estimation. They then empirically test this framework on multiple benchmark datasets (RewardBench, LLMBar, SummEval, Arena100K) using a pool of seven LLM judges including DeepSeek V4 Flash, and run controlled experiments where they synthetically vary interaction structure and the amount of calibration data to study performance across regimes.

Result: Across 20 combinations of real datasets and label budgets, scalar/reliability aggregation wins in 16 cases, suggesting that current LLM judge outputs are largely additive or redundant and that complex interaction modeling rarely pays off under realistic label constraints. In synthetic settings with controlled calibration data growth, they show the complementary regime: when labels are generated additively, scalar aggregation continues to dominate, but when labels depend on a strong six-way interaction among judges and enough labeled data is provided so that table sparsity and unseen combinations disappear, a large joint table becomes preferable, with its test MSE improving dramatically (from 0.224 to 0.061).

Conclusion: The work concludes that the key design question for LLM judge panels is not simply how many judges to include, but whether the incremental information from additional judges is estimable given the available human labels. In current practical regimes, simple scalar/reliability calibration is typically optimal because judge signals are mostly additive or redundant and label budgets are finite. However, in settings where strong interactions between judges truly exist and sufficient calibration data can be collected to cover the joint output space, higher-dimensional joint-table calibrators can yield substantial gains, and tools like Finite-Calibration Panel Selection can help identify this regime.

Abstract: We study when LLM judge panels should be calibrated with low-dimensional stackers versus joint output tables under finite human-label budgets. Low-dimensional stackers have small estimation cost but miss interactions, whereas joint-table calibrators can represent interactions but pay for cell counts and unseen patterns. We cast this tradeoff as a finite-calibration regime map and instantiate it as Finite-Calibration Panel Selection, a deployable validation selector over judge path, prefix size, and aggregator family with table and parametric estimation diagnostics. On RewardBench, LLMBar, SummEval, and Arena100K with a seven-judge pool including DeepSeek V4 Flash, scalar/reliability aggregation wins 16 of 20 real dataset--budget cells, indicating that current judge outputs are often additive or redundant. Controlled calibration-growth data show the complementary regime: additive labels remain scalar-favored, whereas a six-way interaction selects a larger joint table and its test MSE drops from 0.224 to 0.061 once unseen mass vanishes. Thus the practical question is not ``how many judges?'' but whether the next judge's information is estimable under the available human labels.

</details>


### [72] [ExpWeaver: LLM Agents Learn from Experience via Latent RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01041)
*Tao Feng,Tianyang Luo,Jingjun Xu,Zhigang Hua,Yan Xie,Shuang Yang,Ge Liu,Jiaxuan You*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ExpWeaver is an experience-learning framework that lets LLM agents retrieve and use past interactions directly in latent space, improving performance and token efficiency without a separate text-based RAG module.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing experience-learning and RAG-style methods for LLM agents operate purely in text space: they store past interactions as text, retrieve them via semantic similarity, and prepend them to the prompt. This leads to large token overhead, constrained context windows, and an architecture where retrieval and generation are decoupled and optimized separately. There is a need for a more integrated, token-efficient way for LLMs to learn from and reuse past experiences across tasks and domains.

Method: ExpWeaver encodes previous experiences using the LLM’s own hidden states rather than text embeddings. During decoding, at each step the model retrieves relevant experiences directly in latent space. These latent experiences are fused into the current computation through cross-attention aggregation and gated residual connections, allowing the model to dynamically condition on past experience as it generates. The entire retrieval-and-generation pipeline is trained end-to-end via reinforcement learning, and is applied to both generative tasks (e.g., QA, reasoning, coding) and ranking tasks (e.g., recommendation).

Result: On 13 tasks across QA, reasoning, coding, scientific prediction, and recommendation, ExpWeaver obtains state-of-the-art performance on 12 tasks. It beats the strongest baseline by more than 6.8% on average, matches or nearly matches the token usage of non-retrieval baselines, while text-based retrieval systems require 1.5–2× more tokens, and shows significantly better cross-domain generalization, with +16.32% under zero-shot transfer and +15.21% under few-shot transfer versus the best baseline.

Conclusion: Latent retrieval-augmented generation with experience representations in the model’s own hidden space allows LLM agents to learn from and reuse past interactions more effectively and efficiently than text-based RAG. By integrating retrieval directly into the decoding process and training end-to-end with reinforcement learning, ExpWeaver reduces token overhead, removes the need for a separate RAG module, achieves SOTA accuracy on a wide range of tasks, and improves cross-domain transfer, indicating a promising direction for scalable experience learning in LLM agents.

Abstract: Experience learning has achieved promising results in enhancing LLM agent planning and reasoning by integrating past interactions as reusable knowledge. However, existing methods remain confined to explicit text space, retrieving experiences via semantic similarity and concatenating them into the context window, leading to substantial token overhead and a decoupled architecture that separates retrieval from generation. To address these limitations, we propose ExpWeaver, a framework that enables LLM agents to learn from experience via latent retrieval-augmented generation, without requiring a separate RAG module. ExpWeaver encodes experiences using the LLM's own hidden states, retrieves relevant experiences directly in latent space at each decoding step, and integrates them through cross-attention aggregation and gated residual mechanisms. The entire pipeline is optimized end-to-end with reinforcement learning, supporting both generative and ranking tasks. We evaluate ExpWeaver on 13 diverse tasks spanning question answering, reasoning, coding, scientific prediction, and recommendation. Results demonstrate that ExpWeaver achieves state-of-the-art performance on 12 out of 13 tasks, outperforming the strongest baseline by over 6.8%; maintains token efficiency comparable to non-retrieval baselines while text-based retrieval methods require 1.5 to 2 times more tokens; and exhibits superior cross-domain generalization, outperforming the strongest baseline by 16.32% under zero-shot transfer and 15.21% under few-shot transfer. Our code for ExpWeaver is released at https://github.com/ulab-uiuc/ExpWeaver.

</details>


### [73] [Child-directed speech facilitates production, not comprehension, in BabyLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01045)
*Bastian Bunzeck,Sina Zarrieß*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates how child-directed speech (CDS) vs other corpora affect both comprehension and production in small language models, introducing a new frame-completion task to better capture production abilities relevant to language acquisition theories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work suggests CDS is not helpful for BabyLMs, but this has been judged mainly on comprehension benchmarks. Usage-based theories stress production and the role of constructional frames in early language use. There is a need for evaluation methods that reflect these production-oriented aspects and can reveal potential advantages of CDS that comprehension tasks miss.

Method: The authors train Llama-based BabyLMs on three types of data: child-directed speech (CDS), the BabyLM corpus, and web-crawl educational data (FineWeb-edu). They then compare these models on standard comprehension benchmarks (e.g., minimal pair judgments) and introduce a new generation-based evaluation: a frame-completion task where models must complete constructional frames with appropriate slot-fillers. They analyze grammaticality of completions and how probability mass is distributed over candidate fillers.

Result: Models trained on FineWeb-edu perform best on comprehension benchmarks like minimal pair discrimination. In contrast, models trained on CDS produce grammatical completions at earlier training stages and better focus probability mass on suitable slot-fillers in the frame-completion task. This reveals a dissociation: web-trained models excel at comprehension metrics, while CDS-trained models show stronger early production abilities aligned with constructional frames.

Conclusion: Standard comprehension-focused benchmarks underestimate the benefits of child-directed speech for BabyLMs. CDS appears particularly valuable for developing production-oriented capabilities, such as generating grammatical and semantically appropriate completions in constructional frames. Evaluations of BabyLM training data should therefore include generation-based, frame-sensitive tasks to capture the full impact of CDS on language learning in small language models.

Abstract: Recent studies suggest that child-directed speech is not conducive to language learning in BabyLMs. However, current evaluations focus predominantly on comprehension and not production, which is central to usage-based theories of language acquisition which argue how CDS facilitates early language use through constructional ''frames'' (frequent lexical patterns with open slots). We introduce a novel generation-based evaluation inspired by such theories in form of a frame-completion task, and compare Llama models trained with CDS, the BabyLM corpus, and web-crawl data (FineWeb-edu) on comprehension benchmarks and our novel framework. Our results reveal a clear dissociation between models' comprehension and production capabilities: while FineWeb-trained models excel at minimal pairs, CDS-trained models produce grammatical completions substantially earlier in training and concentrate probability mass on appropriate slot-fillers. These findings show that comprehension benchmarks underestimate what CDS affords to BabyLMs.

</details>


### [74] [PMC-InterCPT: Rethinking Biomedical Interleaved Data for Multimodal Continued Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01049)
*Guanghao Zhu,Zeyu Liu,Zhitian Hou,Pengkai Wang,Zhijie Sang,Minheng Ni,Wenjun Wang,Yanggan Gu,Shuo Cai,Congkai Xie,Jianmin Wu,Hongxia Yang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper builds a cleaned, context-rich, interleaved biomedical image–text corpus (PMC-InterCPT) from scientific literature to improve medical multimodal continued pretraining, showing better performance with fewer tokens than using the raw data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing large-scale biomedical image–text datasets from literature rely mainly on figure captions, which are short, context-dependent, and incomplete without surrounding text. Automatic large-scale extraction also introduces structural noise like missing captions, markup, duplicate or fragmented context. These issues limit the effectiveness of multimodal continued pretraining, especially in medicine where precise, context-grounded understanding is critical. The authors aim to rebuild the data pipeline so that pretraining corpora are cleaner, more context-rich, and better balanced across modalities.

Method: The authors construct PMC-InterCPT, an interleaved image–text corpus that incorporates both captions and the figure-referencing body text. Their pipeline: (1) recovers missing captions and cleans both caption and context text (removing markup, deduping, fixing incoherent segments); (2) reconstructs coherent interleaved image–text samples that reflect how figures and text appear in the article; (3) applies LLM-supervised classifiers to filter records by medical relevance and textual quality; and (4) analyzes modality imbalance and proposes a four-bucket evidence taxonomy to enable modality-aware resampling for training. They then perform medical multimodal continued pretraining on Qwen3.5-4B-Base followed by supervised fine-tuning to evaluate the corpus.

Result: Continued pretraining on PMC-InterCPT, followed by supervised fine-tuning, leads to improved performance on both medical and general multimodal benchmarks compared with training on the raw, unprocessed source pool. These gains are achieved despite using fewer CPT tokens, indicating more efficient learning. The experiments also show that both data quality (cleaning, filtering) and modality composition (balancing different evidence types) contribute complementary benefits to medical multimodal pretraining.

Conclusion: Context-grounded, cleaned, and modality-aware biomedical image–text corpora like PMC-InterCPT are more effective for medical multimodal continued pretraining than naïvely extracted image–caption datasets. Incorporating surrounding figure-referencing text, filtering for medical relevance and quality, and resampling according to a modality-aware taxonomy collectively yield better model performance with fewer tokens. The study highlights that careful data construction—both in terms of textual context and modality balance—is crucial for efficient and robust medical multimodal model training.

Abstract: Large-scale biomedical image-text datasets extracted from scientific literature provide valuable resources for medical multimodal model training. These datasets are commonly organized as image-caption pairs; however, figure captions are often short, context-dependent, and only partially informative without the surrounding article text. At the same time, large-scale automatic extraction introduces structural noise such as missing captions, residual markup, duplicated context, and incoherent multi-paragraph figure descriptions. We revisit data construction for medical multimodal continued pretraining (CPT) and present PMC-InterCPT, a context-grounded biomedical interleaved corpus that incorporates figure-referencing body text in addition to captions. Our pipeline recovers missing captions, cleans caption and context text, reconstructs coherent interleaved image-text samples, and applies LLM-supervised medical relevance and quality classifiers to filter noisy records. We further reveal strong modality imbalance in the resulting corpus and introduce a four-bucket evidence taxonomy for modality-aware resampling. Through CPT followed by supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on Qwen3.5-4B-Base, PMC-InterCPT effectively improves medical and general multimodal performance while using fewer CPT tokens than the raw source pool. The experimental results also illustrate the complementarity between the data quality and modality for medical multimodal CPT.

</details>


### [75] [MENTIS: What Belief Changes Under Alignment? Measuring Multi-Scale Latent Torsion in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01060)
*Partha Pratim Saha,Samarth Raina,Mayur Parvatikar,Amit Dhanda,Vinija Jain,Aman Chadha,Amitava Das*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how preference alignment changes the internal geometry of large language models, going beyond behavioral evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although preference alignment improves observable behavior of LLMs, aligned models still fail under adversarial settings like jailbreaks and prompt injection. This indicates that behavior-level evaluation is insufficient, motivating a study of how alignment alters internal representations and computation.

Method: The authors introduce MENTIS, a geometry-first analytical framework. It compares instruction-tuned (IT) and preference-aligned (PA) model checkpoints with: (1) a layerwise covariance-based torsion norm (T1) to quantify geometric change; (2) a spectral torsion diagnostic (T2) for deeper spectral insights; and (3) an Energy-Radiance-Activation (ERA) measure to localize these changes across layers. They run analyses across four 7–8B model pairs on the LITMUS benchmark, examining word-, prompt-, and model-level structure.

Result: They find that alignment-induced changes are selective, not uniform. Normative concepts (involving values and preferences) show larger torsion shifts than factual concepts. Torsion is negatively correlated with contextual entropy, and the strongest effects are localized to model-specific mid-to-late layers. These patterns consistently appear at word, prompt, and model scales across different architectures.

Conclusion: Preference alignment leaves structured, depth-localized geometric signatures in LLM internal computation. These internal changes correlate with normative content and uncertainty, demonstrating that alignment reshapes representational geometry in specific ways that behavior-level metrics alone cannot fully capture.

Abstract: Preference alignment has substantially improved the observable behavior of large language models, yet it remains unclear what alignment changes internally. Aligned systems still fail under jailbreaks, prompt injection, and retrieval-time corruption, suggesting behavior-level evaluation alone is incomplete. Post-training should leave measurable traces in internal computation. We ask: when an instruction-tuned (IT) model becomes a preference-aligned (PA) model, what geometric structure changes, where do those changes concentrate, and how selectively do they vary across concepts, prompts, and model families?
  We introduce MENTIS, a geometry-first framework for measuring alignment-induced internal reorganization in paired checkpoints. MENTIS compares IT and PA models using a primary layerwise covariance-based torsion norm (T1), a secondary spectral torsion diagnostic (T2), and an Energy-Radiance-Activation measure (ERA) for depth localization. Across four 7-8B model pairs on LITMUS, our study reveals that alignment-induced change is selective rather than uniform: normative concepts exhibit larger torsion shifts than factual concepts on average; torsion is negatively correlated with contextual entropy; and peak effects localize to architecture-specific mid-to-late layers. The same pattern appears across word-level, prompt-level, and model-level analyses. These results suggest preference alignment leaves structured, depth-localized geometric signatures in internal computation beyond what behavior-level evaluation alone can reveal.

</details>


### [76] [When Is 0.1% Enough? Analyzing the Combined Effects of Dimensionality Reduction and Quantization on Text Embedding Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01074)
*Riku Kisako,Hayato Tsukagoshi,Ryohei Sasano*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how to best compress text embeddings by jointly using dimensionality reduction and quantization, finding that their combination yields far stronger compression with minimal performance loss compared to using either alone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: State-of-the-art text embedding models produce very high-dimensional, real-valued vectors that are expensive to store and compute with. Existing compression approaches either reduce dimensionality or quantize values, but it is unclear how effective their combination is and what trade-offs it offers across different tasks and models.

Method: The authors conduct a systematic empirical study on compressing text embeddings by first applying dimensionality reduction and then quantization (or vice versa / in combination). They evaluate various compression configurations over four families of MTEB tasks and four pretrained embedding models, comparing performance and compression ratios.

Result: Experiments show that combining dimensionality reduction and quantization can achieve much stronger compression than either approach alone. In some configurations, embeddings can be compressed down to about 0.1% of their original storage size while preserving almost the same task performance. The study also reveals that the best combination strategy varies by task type.

Conclusion: Joint use of dimensionality reduction and quantization is a highly effective strategy for compressing text embeddings, offering extreme compression with minimal loss of performance. However, there is no single optimal setting; the preferred compression scheme should be selected based on the target task family.

Abstract: Recent high-performing text embedding models often output high-dimensional real-valued vectors, resulting in substantial storage and computational costs. To address this issue, compression methods based on dimensionality reduction or quantization have been proposed; however, the effects of combining dimensionality reduction and quantization have not been sufficiently investigated. In this paper, we systematically examine the effectiveness of compressing text embeddings by combining dimensionality reduction and quantization, using four MTEB task families and four pretrained embedding models. The experimental results demonstrate that combining dimensionality reduction and quantization enables substantially stronger compression than using either method alone, that in some settings embeddings can be reduced to as little as 0.1% of their original size with almost no performance degradation, and that the optimal compression strategy depends on the task.

</details>


### [77] [On the Generalization Gap in Self-Evolving Language Model Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01075)
*Zhenting Qi,Susanna Maria Baby,Stefanie Anna Baby,Kan Yuan,Andrew Tomkins,Tu Vu,Da-Cheng Juan,Cyrus Rashtchian*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how well large language models can improve themselves using only self-generated supervision, without external labels, under a strictly closed-loop setting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether and when self-evolution of LLMs—training them using feedback and signals they generate themselves—can approach the effectiveness of traditional training with ground-truth (oracle) labels, especially when only unlabeled prompts and a base model are available.

Method: They define a unified offline self-evolution framework under a strict closed-loop constraint (only unlabeled prompts plus a base model, no external labels). Within this framework, they compare four strategies: (1) single-round verification, where the model checks and filters its own answers; (2) multi-turn revision with feedback, where a critic model provides feedback and the model revises its answers; (3) iterative training, repeatedly training the model on its self-generated data; and (4) curriculum learning, where training progresses from easier to harder automatically generated examples. They run controlled experiments mainly on Knights and Knaves logical reasoning tasks with deterministic solutions and tunable difficulty, and then test on real-world reasoning benchmarks. They measure performance gains versus the base model and compare to an oracle-supervised upper bound.

Result: Across strategies, self-evolution reliably improves performance over the base model, but gains saturate after enough training compute is used and still fall short of oracle-supervised training. Among the strategies, multi-turn critic–revision with larger models performs best; for example, Gemma 12B with this approach nearly matches the oracle-supervised upper bound on Knights and Knaves tasks. However, when extending to real-world reasoning benchmarks, improvements from closed-loop self-evolution remain modest and the gap to oracle supervision is more apparent.

Conclusion: Closed-loop self-evolution of LLMs, using only internally generated supervision from unlabeled prompts, can yield meaningful but limited improvements. The approach can substantially boost performance in structured logical tasks, especially with strong critic–revision loops and sufficiently large models, yet it still typically underperforms oracle-supervised training and exhibits early performance plateaus. These findings indicate that while self-evolution is a useful tool, purely internal supervision is not enough to replace high-quality external labels under this minimal closed-loop formulation.

Abstract: Recent work suggests that large language models (LLMs) can improve through self-evolution (SE), using supervision signals generated by the model itself. In this work, we ask: under a strict closed-loop setup, where the self-evolution algorithm has access only to an unlabeled prompt set and a base model, how close can internally generated supervision come to oracle-supervised training? We analyze four representative strategies in a unified offline self-evolution framework: single-round verification, multi-turn revision with feedback, iterative training, and curriculum learning. Our primary experiments use Knights and Knaves (KK) logical reasoning tasks, which provide deterministic solutions, controlled difficulty levels, and a clean testbed for easy-to-hard generalization. We first show that self-evolution consistently improves over the base model, but plateaus after excessive training compute is invested, and eventually still leaves a non-trivial gap to oracle supervision. We find that multi-turn critic-revision with large models can reach strong self-evolution performance, with Gemma 12B nearly matching oracle-supervised training. Beyond Knights and Knaves, we also evaluate self-evolution on real-world reasoning benchmarks, where gains are also modest. Overall, our results characterize when closed-loop self-evolution can help and show how internally generated supervision remains insufficient under this minimal formulation.

</details>


### [78] [Deep Research as Rubric for Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01091)
*Wangyi Mei,Zhouhong Gu,Zhenhan Bai,Yin Cai,Lefan Zhang,Zhenxin Ding,Bo Chen,Yan Gao,Yi Wu,Yao Hu,Jiaqing Liang,Deqing Yang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces DR-rubric, a framework that turns rubric construction for open-ended reasoning tasks into a deep research process to create better reward signals for training models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-ended reasoning and long-form generation tasks lack reliable, automatic verification signals, making reward-based policy optimization difficult. Existing rubrics are static, often hand-crafted or quickly prompt-generated, and fail to capture nuanced, task-specific, knowledge-intensive criteria. This leads to distorted reward signals and suboptimal model training. The authors are motivated to systematically discover what actually constitutes correctness and insight for these tasks, treating rubric design itself as a research challenge.

Method: The authors propose DR-rubric, a two-stage framework. In Stage I, the system performs iterative, multi-turn, agentic search to elicit domain facts, structural constraints, and common failure modes relevant to a task. In Stage II, it distills this collected evidence into a set of atomic, independently verifiable constraints that can act as rubric items. These rubrics are then used to provide fine-grained rewards within a GRPO-based policy optimization framework. The same model being trained (an 8B model) can be used to generate its own rubrics via bootstrapping, and the approach can also leverage rubrics generated by stronger external models like GPT-5 and Gemini.

Result: On six benchmarks covering both agentic research and expert reasoning tasks, DR-rubric yields competitive or strong performance using only 1K–3K training examples. Rubrics generated by GPT-5 provide broad coverage on agentic tasks; Gemini-based rubrics offer balanced performance across both agentic and expert reasoning tasks. Bootstrapped rubrics, generated by the 8B model itself over several iterations, show an evolution pattern from specialization towards a more balanced rubric, achieving the best overall performance by the third iteration.

Conclusion: Treating rubric construction as an evidence-driven research process rather than as a static template leads to more scalable and fine-grained reward signals for open-ended reasoning and long-form generation. DR-rubric demonstrates that deeply researched, constraint-based rubrics can substantially improve reward-based policy optimization, even with limited training data, and that models can effectively bootstrap their own rubrics over successive refinement cycles.

Abstract: Open-ended reasoning and long-form generation tasks lack reliable automatic verification signals for reward-based policy optimization. Rubrics offer a promising alternative, but existing approaches treat them as given artifacts -- either hand-crafted or prompt-generated -- and often miss the task-specific, knowledge-intensive dimensions that matter most, distorting the reward signal. Our key observation is that rubric construction is itself a research problem: identifying what makes a response correct or insightful requires discovering and synthesizing external knowledge. We propose Deep Research as Rubric (DR-rubric), a two-stage framework for constructing such rubrics. Stage I elicits domain facts, structural constraints, and failure modes through iterative multi-turn agentic search; Stage II distills this evidence into atomic, independently verifiable constraints for GRPO-based policy optimization. Because the model under training can serve as its own rubric generator, DR-rubric-8B supports bootstrap rubric generation without frontier-model assistance. We evaluate on 6 benchmarks spanning agentic research and expert reasoning. Experiments show that DR-Rubric achieves strong competitive performance with only 1K -- 3K training instances, where GPT-5-generated rubrics particularly benefit breadth coverage on agentic tasks, Gemini-generated rubrics yield the most balanced performance across agentic and expert reasoning tasks, and bootstrap rubrics exhibit a specialization-to-rebalancing evolution achieving the best overall performance at the third iteration. Results demonstrate that reframing rubric construction from static evaluation templates into an evidence-driven research process yields more scalable, fine-grained reward signals for open-ended tasks.

</details>


### [79] [MiCU: End-to-End Smart Home Command Understanding with Large Language Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01099)
*Haowei Han,Kexin Hu,Weiwei Cai,Debiao Zhang,Bin Qin,Yuxiang Wang,Jiawei Jiang,Xiao Yan,Bo Du*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes MiCU, a domain-specific large language model for smart-home command understanding, trained with synthesized data, curriculum learning, RL with domain rules, and a token compression technique, achieving large accuracy gains and successful real-world deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing smart home command understanding systems work well on precise, literal commands but fail on ambiguous, high-level, or misaligned user intents. While general LLMs can handle ambiguity better, in practice they are limited by lack of domain-specific data, insufficient task adaptation, and high computational and latency costs for real deployment. The authors aim to build a smart-home–specialized LLM that is both accurate on complex commands and efficient enough for large-scale production use, without requiring massive amounts of manually labeled data.

Method: 1) Automatically synthesize training data from real user logs using LLMs, reducing manual annotation needs. 2) Start from a base LLM and apply curriculum learning: progressively inject smart-home domain knowledge and increasingly difficult command understanding tasks. 3) Enhance reasoning with a cold-start training phase followed by reinforcement learning guided by handcrafted, domain-specific thinking rules that shape how the model parses and resolves user requests. 4) Compress long device descriptions into single special tokens via a token compression technique, enabling a lightweight variant, MiCU-fast, that is optimized for long-context input with much lower inference overhead.

Result: In offline experiments, MiCU achieves an average accuracy improvement of about 20.01% compared to baselines across all device categories in the smart-home ecosystem. The token compression and MiCU-fast variant allow efficient inference on long inputs. In real-world deployment within the Xiaomi Home app, MiCU serves around 1.7 million page views daily, reduces user correction rate by 1.57%, and improves human-audited accuracy by 32.05%, indicating both better understanding and improved user experience.

Conclusion: Automated log-based data synthesis combined with domain-aware curriculum learning, RL with thinking rules, and token compression can produce an effective, efficient domain-specific LLM for smart-home command understanding. MiCU substantially outperforms traditional and generic LLM baselines in accuracy, scales to production traffic, and measurably improves user experience. The approach and released resources (data and code) provide a practical recipe for building similar domain-specialized LLMs in other IoT or application settings.

Abstract: Command understanding systems in smart home ecosystems can automate device control and substantially improve user experience. However, while they perform well on precise utterances (e.g., "turn on the bedroom light"), they struggle with ambiguous or misaligned commands (e.g., "make the bedroom cozy"). Large language models (LLMs) generalize well across various domains and can outperform traditional rule-based systems on such tasks, but their effectiveness is often constrained by scarce domain-specific data, insufficient task-specific adaptation, and high computational costs. In this paper, we propose an automated training data synthesis workflow using user logs and LLMs; then we build MiCU, a domain-specific LLM that excels at command understanding. Specifically, we employ curriculum learning to inject domain knowledge into the base LLM, then we enhance its reasoning ability via cold-start training combined with reinforcement learning (RL) guided by domain-specific thinking rules. Additionally, we introduce a token compression technique that condenses device description into a single special token, substantially reducing inference overhead and enabling \model-fast, an efficient variant optimized for long inputs. Extensive experiments show that MiCU significantly outperforms baselines, with an average accuracy gain of 20.01% across all device categories. We have deployed MiCU in the Xiaomi Home app, receiving approximately 1.7 million page views per day. Production evaluations show that MiCU reduces user correction rate by 1.57% and increases human audited accuracy by 32.05%. Our data and code are available at https://github.com/xiaomi-research/iot_spec_llm

</details>


### [80] [From Outliers to Errors: Auditing Pali-to-English LLM Translations with Multi-Reference Adjudication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01136)
*Máté Metzger,Nadnapang Phophichit,Hansa Dhammahaso*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes an audit framework for classical-language-to-English translations by large language models, using multiple human translations as a reference envelope and embedding-based drift to triage outputs for human/LLM adjudication instead of relying on a single-score metric.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional single-score translation metrics treat divergence from a single reference as error, which fails badly for classical languages like Pali where many different English renderings can all be valid. This risks penalizing legitimate variation and obscuring the specific severe errors that actually mislead readers, especially in doctrinal or scriptural texts. The authors want a method that respects permissible variation while still efficiently surfacing genuinely problematic translations and comparing model behavior.

Method: They translate 1,700 Pali Canon passages with four LLMs (GPT-5.5, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Grok 4.3). For each passage, they take three established human translations as a local reference envelope (Sujato, Thanissaro, Bodhi) rather than a single gold standard.
