Motivation: Handling very long contexts (100k+ tokens) is computationally expensive because prefill cost scales with sequence length, yet many real-world tasks like long code debugging or multi-document reasoning need such contexts. Existing training-free attention-based compression methods reduce cost but significantly hurt accuracy on challenging long-context tasks, particularly code reasoning. The work aims to design a trainable, generalizable compression mechanism that preserves or improves accuracy across diverse long-context benchmarks while being lightweight and transferable across models.

Method: The paper proposes LongAttnComp, an extension of AttnComp tailored for long contexts. It (1) fine-tunes a lightweight cross-attention scoring layer that scores and selects important tokens; (2) introduces token-level chunking to handle very long sequences; (3) uses a token-budget top-p algorithm to select tokens under a fixed budget; (4) applies positional reordering to maintain useful positional structure after compression; and (5) adds a format-agnostic query parser to better understand and represent the user query for conditioning the compressor. Training uses a two-stage fine-tuning recipe: Stage 1 trains on NIAH (Needle-in-a-Haystack-style) retrieval data to build strong general retrieval capabilities, and Stage 2 augments with multi-hop and reasoning data to adapt the compressor to complex long-context reasoning tasks. The resulting compressor can be plugged into different LLM backbones with minimal modifications.

Result: On the InfiniteBench Code-Debug benchmark, LongAttnComp achieves accuracy matching or exceeding that of using the full uncompressed context while substantially reducing prefill cost, and it significantly outperforms training-free attention-based compression baselines. The method transfers effectively to four different target models from three distinct model families, showing good cross-model generalization. On LongBench v2, especially on multi-document reasoning tasks, the two-stage training closes most of the performance gap observed after Stage 1 while preserving the strong performance on Code-Debug, indicating that the approach scales well to varied long-context reasoning scenarios.

Conclusion: LongAttnComp demonstrates that a small, trainable attention-based compression layer, combined with carefully designed token selection and ordering mechanisms plus a staged fine-tuning strategy, can make long-context inference substantially more efficient without sacrificing and sometimes even improving accuracy. The approach generalizes across multiple LLM families and task types, particularly excelling on challenging code debugging and multi-document reasoning benchmarks. This suggests that learned context compression is a practical path to enabling efficient 100k+ token LLM applications.

Abstract: As real-world applications increasingly require processing inputs of 100k+ tokens, the gap between context length and inference efficiency has become a critical bottleneck. Context compression offers a way to reduce prefill costs while preserving task accuracy. However, existing training-free attention-based methods leave substantial gaps in demanding long-context tasks such as code reasoning. We present LongAttnComp, a long-context adaptation of AttnComp that fine-tunes a lightweight cross-attention scoring layer and introduces tokenlevel chunking, a token-budget top-p algorithm, positional reordering, and a formatagnostic query parser. We further design a two-stage fine-tuning recipe for the compressor: Stage 1 builds a general retrieval foundation from NIAH-style data, and Stage 2 extends it with multi-hop and reasoning data for broader long-context task coverage. On InfiniteBench Code-Debug, LongAttnComp matches or exceeds full-context accuracy, substantially outperforms training-free baselines, and transfers across four target models from three families. On LongBench v2, the two-stage recipe largely closes the Stage 1 gap on multi-document reasoning while preserving Code-Debug performance.

</details>


### [101] [Benchmarking Local LLMs for Natural-Language-to-SQL Querying in Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing: An Empirical Benchmark on Consumer-Grade Hardware](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01338)
*Sagar Bhetwal,Rajan Bastakoti,Nirajan Acharya,Gaurav Kumar Gupta*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper benchmarks locally deployed open-source LLMs for converting natural-language questions into SQL over a synthetic pharmaceutical manufacturing database, showing that general code-tuned LLMs can feasibly support GxP-aligned, on-premises NLQ systems but still need human oversight.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Biopharmaceutical manufacturers face strict regulatory constraints (FDA, EU GMP, EU AI Act) that limit the use of cloud-based AI for sensitive GxP data, yet they need intelligent query interfaces to complex manufacturing databases. Locally deployed LLMs promise privacy and regulatory alignment, but it is unclear whether they are accurate and reliable enough for practical use in pharma manufacturing analytics, especially for natural-language-to-SQL tasks central to data access and investigations.

Method: The authors created PharmaBatchDB AI, a FastAPI-based evaluation platform backed by a synthetic Microsoft SQL Server database (~63k records) modeling Batch, MES, and CIP modules. They locally deployed four open-source LLMs via Ollama (Qwen 2.5 Coder 7B, Llama 3.1 8B, Mistral 7B, Meditron 7B) and tested them on 60 pharmaceutical manufacturing natural-language questions. They evaluated each model’s outputs using metrics such as SQL extraction rate, SQL compliance (syntactic/semantic validity), factual consistency with ground truth, ROUGE-L similarity, hallucination rate, throughput, and latency, then compared model performance statistically.

Result: Qwen 2.5 Coder 7B, Llama 3.1 8B, and Mistral 7B were able to generate SQL for all tasks, while Meditron 7B largely failed due to limited context handling and weak SQL skills. Among successful models, Llama 3.1 8B produced the most compliant SQL, whereas Qwen 2.5 Coder 7B delivered the best text similarity to reference queries and highest factual consistency. However, the performance gap between Qwen and Llama was not statistically significant. Overall, code-tuned, general-purpose LLMs clearly outperformed the domain-specific biomedical model for structured query generation.

Conclusion: Local, on-premises LLM-based natural-language querying of pharmaceutical manufacturing data is technically feasible on consumer-grade hardware and can comply with GxP-aligned regulatory expectations regarding data locality and privacy. Code-specialized general LLMs are better suited than current domain-specific biomedical models for SQL generation tasks in this setting. Nonetheless, the accuracy and reliability of these systems are not yet sufficient for fully autonomous use in regulated environments; human review and validation of generated queries and results remain necessary.

Abstract: Biopharmaceutical manufacturing organizations operate under regulatory frameworks such as FDA guidance, EU Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP), and the EU AI Act, which can restrict the use of cloud-based artificial intelligence systems. Locally deployed large language models (LLMs) offer a privacy-preserving alternative, but their suitability for pharmaceutical manufacturing tasks remains underexplored. This study evaluates four open-source LLMs (Qwen 2.5 Coder 7B, Llama 3.1 8B, Mistral 7B, and Meditron 7B) deployed locally via Ollama for natural-language-to-SQL generation over a pharmaceutical manufacturing database.
  A FastAPI-based evaluation platform, PharmaBatchDB AI, was developed using a synthetic Microsoft SQL Server database containing approximately 63,000 records across Batch, Manufacturing Execution System (MES), and Clean-In-Place (CIP) modules. Models were benchmarked on 60 domain-specific natural-language questions using metrics including SQL extraction rate, SQL compliance, factual consistency, ROUGE-L, hallucination rate, throughput, and latency.
  Qwen 2.5 Coder 7B, Llama 3.1 8B, and Mistral 7B generated SQL for all evaluation tasks, while Meditron 7B failed on nearly all tasks due to context-window limitations and poor SQL generation capability. Llama 3.1 8B achieved the highest SQL compliance, whereas Qwen 2.5 Coder 7B achieved the strongest overall text similarity and factual consistency. Performance differences between the two leading models were not statistically significant.
  The results show that code-tuned general-purpose LLMs outperform a domain-specific biomedical model on structured query generation for pharmaceutical manufacturing data. Although fully local, GxP-aligned NLQ systems are feasible on consumer hardware, current performance levels still require human oversight and downstream validation for regulated use.

</details>


### [102] [Dr. DocBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Expert-Level and Difficult Document Parsing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01393)
*Minglai Yang,Xinyan Velocity Yu,Pengyuan Li,Xinyu Guo,Zhenting Qi,Konwoo Kim,Longtian Ye,Xiaolong Luo,Jinhe Bi,Henry Zhang,Haris Riaz,Xuan Zhang,Yunze Xiao,Bangya Liu,Tom Tang,Yunfei Zhao,Qunshu Lin,Zihan Wang,Minghao Liu,Michael Lingzhi Li,Yilun Du,Jesse Thomason,Rogerio Feris,Alex Pentland,Zexue He*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces Dr. DocBench, a difficulty-aware benchmark focused on expert-level document parsing where existing systems fail.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing OCR and document parsing benchmarks mostly cover common, relatively easy document genres, and do not adequately evaluate expert-domain structures (e.g., chemical formulas, music notation, complex tables, cross-page layouts) where modern parsers still struggle.

Method: Construct a difficulty-aware benchmark from a large-scale multilingual book corpus spanning 52 BISAC domains. Use parser-failure-based sampling to select challenging pages from long documents (~100 pages each). Annotate 4,514 pages with 65k page- and block-level labels covering layout, reading order, hierarchy, and domain-specific visual content.

Result: The resulting Dr. DocBench dataset exposes that both pipeline-based document parsers and general-purpose VLMs, which perform well on existing benchmarks, experience substantial performance drops on expert-level documents and complex structures in this benchmark.

Conclusion: Dr. DocBench serves as a comprehensive, challenging testbed for diagnosing weaknesses and driving progress in document intelligence, especially for expert-domain and structurally complex documents.

Abstract: Document parsing and recognition are fundamental capabilities for vision-language models (VLMs) and document processing systems. However, existing Optical Character Recognition (OCR) and document parsing benchmarks are increasingly limited in coverage and difficulty: many focus on common document genres or uniformly sampled pages where modern parsers already perform strongly, while offering limited annotation for expert-domain structures such as chemical formula, music notation, complex tables, and cross-page layouts. We introduce Dr. DocBench, a difficulty-aware benchmark for expert-level document parsing. Built from a large-scale multilingual book corpus, Dr. DocBench spans 52 BISAC subject domains and selects challenging documents through parser-failure-based sampling, targeting cases where multiple state-of-the-art systems struggle. It contains 4,514 annotated pages from long documents averaging around 100 pages, with 65k high-quality page- and block-level annotations for layout, reading order, hierarchical relations, and domain-specific visual contents. Evaluations of pipeline-based parsers and general-purpose VLMs show that strong performance on existing benchmarks does not transfer to our expert-level document parsing. Our analysis reveals substantial failures across subjects, content types, and structural attributes, highlighting Dr. DocBench as a comprehensive testbed for diagnosing and advancing document intelligence.

</details>


### [103] [UniD$^3$: A Knowledge Graph-Enhanced RAG Framework for Drug-Disease Discovery and Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01394)
*Qing Wang,Tianshi Liu,Minghao Zhou,Jialu Liang,Sen Guo,Guangyu Wang,Jing Su,Qianqian Song*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents UniD^3, a unified framework that combines large language models with knowledge graph–enhanced retrieval-augmented generation (KG-RAG) to systematically extract, structure, and validate drug–disease knowledge from biomedical literature at scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Drug discovery and repurposing require a systematic understanding of drug–disease relationships, but the biomedical literature is large, heterogeneous, and fast-growing. Existing manually curated datasets are incomplete and costly to maintain, while pure LLM-based methods suffer from hallucinations and lack strong evidence grounding. There is a need for a scalable, reliable way to convert unstructured literature into structured, verifiable drug–disease knowledge.

Method: The authors propose UniD^3, which processes 157,849 PubMed articles using Llama 3.3-70B and a knowledge-graph–enhanced retrieval-augmented generation (KG-RAG) pipeline. They use a dual-stage strategy: (1) paper-level extraction, where LLMs extract drug, disease, and target information from individual articles; and (2) KG-level consolidation, where extracted facts are merged into knowledge graphs centered on drug and disease entities. These knowledge graphs then drive KG-RAG-based generation of structured datasets for three tasks: Drug–Disease Matching (DDM), Drug Effectiveness Assessment (DEA), and Drug–Target Analysis (DTA). They build six knowledge graphs and construct large-scale QA-style datasets. Performance is evaluated via external benchmarks, fuzzy matching to curated resources, and clinician review.

Result: UniD^3 creates six knowledge graphs and large datasets, including 28,915 DDM, 15,042 DEA, and over 4,000 DTA QA pairs. External validation indicates strong performance, with F1 scores of 0.85–0.87 for DDM and DEA and 0.82 for DTA. Clinician review yields an AUROC of 0.90, suggesting high reliability. KG-RAG–augmented models outperform standalone LLMs. A UniD^3 chatbot is also implemented to provide interpretable, citation-supported exploration of drug–disease relations.

Conclusion: UniD^3 demonstrates that integrating LLMs with knowledge graph–enhanced RAG enables scalable, accurate transformation of unstructured biomedical literature into structured drug–disease knowledge. The resulting knowledge graphs and datasets support AI-driven drug discovery, repurposing, and precision medicine, and the framework is presented as extensible to future data and use cases.

Abstract: Systematic characterization of drug-disease relationships is essential for drug discovery and repurposing, yet is hindered by the heterogeneity and rapid growth of biomedical literature. Existing datasets rely on labor-intensive curation and are often incomplete, while LLM-only approaches suffer from hallucination and weak evidence grounding. We introduce UniD$^3$, a unified framework that integrates Large Language Models with Knowledge Graph-enhanced Retrieval-Augmented Generation (KG-RAG) to extract, organize, and validate drug-disease knowledge across Drug-Disease Matching (DDM), Drug Effectiveness Assessment (DEA), and Drug-Target Analysis (DTA). UniD$^3$ processes 157,849 PubMed articles with Llama 3.3-70B and constructs knowledge graphs via a dual-stage strategy combining paper-level extraction with KG-level consolidation centered on drug and disease entities. These graphs support KG-RAG-based generation of structured datasets, evaluated through external benchmarks, fuzzy matching with curated resources, and clinician review. UniD$^3$ produces six knowledge graphs and large-scale datasets, including 28,915 DDM, 15,042 DEA, and over 4,000 DTA QA pairs. External validation shows strong performance (F1: 0.85-0.87 for DDM/DEA; 0.82 for DTA), with clinician review confirming high reliability (AUROC = 0.90). KG-RAG-augmented models outperform standalone LLMs, and the UniD$^3$ chatbot enables interpretable, citation-supported exploration of drug-disease relationships. UniD$^3$ provides a scalable, extensible framework for transforming unstructured biomedical literature into high-quality, structured drug-disease knowledge, supporting AI-driven discovery, repurposing, and precision medicine.

</details>


### [104] [Consistent and Distinctive: LLM Benchmark Efficiency via Maximum Independent Set Prompt Selection on Similarity Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01400)
*Denica Kjorvezir,Marko Djukanović,Ana Gjorgjevikj,Gjorgjina Cenikj,Tome Eftimov*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a graph-based method to select a diverse subset of prompts from large evaluation benchmarks for LLMs, reducing evaluation cost while largely preserving model rankings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating LLMs on full, large-scale benchmarks is computationally expensive and slow, especially as the number of models and tasks grows. Many prompts in these benchmarks are semantically redundant, so there is a need for principled methods to select smaller, diverse subsets of prompts that still yield reliable model rankings.

Method: The authors represent each benchmark as a similarity graph where each node is a prompt and edges connect prompts whose embedding-based similarity exceeds a configurable percentile threshold. They then run Maximum Independent Set (MIS) algorithms to choose a subset of nodes (prompts) that are not connected to each other, ensuring diversity and low redundancy. They systematically compare four MIS solvers (CPLEX, GREEDY, Online-MIS, ReduMIS) under combinations of six embedding models, three distance metrics, six similarity thresholds, and four benchmarks, and evaluate the impact on ranking stability of 66 LLMs using Kendall’s W (ranking consistency across random seeds) and correlation with full-benchmark rankings.

Result: Across nearly all stochastic configurations, repeated MIS-based subset selection yields highly consistent LLM rankings, with Kendall’s W ≥ 0.90 in 99.2% of cases and an average W of 0.997 ± 0.008. At higher similarity percentile thresholds, the selected subsets reduce the number of prompts by 25–48% on average. Only 15.95% of configurations show noticeable divergence in rankings from the full benchmark (ρ < 0.95), and these discrepancies are mainly observed at low thresholds (p10–p20) and in specific benchmarks (GPQA, IFEval), indicating that overly dense similarity graphs degrade the method’s reliability.

Conclusion: A graph-based MIS framework can substantially shrink LLM evaluation benchmarks while maintaining stable and accurate model rankings. The method is robust across different solvers, embeddings, and distance metrics, but care must be taken to avoid low similarity thresholds that lead to overly dense graphs, which in turn can cause ranking divergence from full-benchmark evaluations.

Abstract: Evaluating large language models (LLMs) across comprehensive benchmarks is expensive and time-consuming. We propose a graph-based prompt selection framework that models each benchmark as a similarity graph -- nodes are prompts connected if their embedding-space distance falls above a configurable threshold -- and applies Maximum Independent Set (MIS) algorithms to select a maximally diverse, non-redundant subset. We evaluate four MIS solvers (CPLEX, GREEDY, Online-MIS, ReduMIS) across six embedding models, three distance measures, six percentile thresholds, and four benchmarks (GPQA, IFEval, MMLU-Pro, Omni-MATH) covering 66 LLMs. Our central hypothesis -- that repeated selection under different random seeds yields consistent LLM rankings that may also differ from the full-benchmark baseline -- is strongly confirmed: Kendall's $W \geq 0.90$ in 99.2\% of stochastic configurations (mean $W = 0.997 \pm 0.008$), while at higher percentile thresholds selected subsets achieve 25--48\% prompt reduction on average. Ranking divergence from the full benchmark ($ρ< 0.95$) occurs in only 15.95\% of configurations, concentrated at low thresholds ($p_{10}$--$p_{20}$) and benchmarks (GPQA, IFEval), identifying overly dense graphs as the primary failure mode.

</details>


### [105] [DrugClaw and DrugAudit: A Primary-Source-Grounded Agent and Authority-Aware Benchmark for Drug-Information Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01434)
*Qing Wang,Bo Li,Jialu Liang,Daling Shi,Bob Zhang,Qianqian Song*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces DrugClaw, a multi-agent retrieval-augmented QA system designed for high-stakes drug information, and DrugAudit, an authority-aware benchmark to evaluate evidence-grounded, citation-faithful answers, showing DrugClaw outperforms baselines on multiple datasets and metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Drug information QA for clinicians is high-stakes: hallucinated or unsupported facts can harm clinical decision-making, and it is not enough to be correct—the answer must be traceable to authoritative primary sources (regulatory documents, peer-reviewed studies). Existing QA systems and benchmarks focus mostly on answer correctness, not on whether citations truly match and support each claim. The authors aim to build a system and an evaluation framework that explicitly model source authority, evidence provenance, and citation faithfulness in drug-related QA.

Method: They design DrugClaw, a multi-agent retrieval-augmented generation system. It uses a registry of specialized drug and pharmacovigilance skills that can query primary regulatory and peer-reviewed records. A reflection-driven state-machine workflow orchestrates these agents: the system iteratively plans, retrieves documents, reflects on retrieved evidence, and refines its answer and citations. For evaluation, they build DrugAudit, a 3,772-item benchmark with authority-aware labels and an LLM-as-judge evaluation panel. The panel uses a dual-judge protocol to assess (1) whether sources match or are even more primary than a gold reference, (2) token-level semantic overlap between cited snippets and gold evidence, and (3) citation faithfulness—whether the answer is truly supported by the cited text. Inter-judge agreement is high (kappa = 0.88). They also test on drug-related subsets of MedQA and PubMedQA.

Result: DrugClaw achieves top performance across all main metrics compared to baselines on DrugAudit and on drug-related subsets of MedQA (751 items) and PubMedQA (512 items). It has the highest composite Evidence Index under both judges, the best judge-mediated answer correctness, and significantly higher primary-source usage rate (0.918, 10.1 percentage points above the next-best system) and citation faithfulness (0.887, 5.9 percentage points higher). On the QA tasks, it reaches 0.920 accuracy on the MedQA subset and 0.693 on the PubMedQA subset, outperforming competing methods.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates that a multi-agent, reflection-driven RAG architecture targeted at authoritative drug sources can materially improve both correctness and evidence quality in drug-information QA. DrugClaw not only answers more accurately but also grounds its answers in more primary and faithful sources than existing systems. DrugAudit provides a rigorous, authority-aware benchmark and evaluation framework—using dual LLM judges and fine-grained evidence metrics—that can be used to assess future systems where evidence provenance and citation reliability are critical. Together, they move drug QA closer to clinically trustworthy, auditable decision support.

Abstract: Drug-information question answering is a high-stakes setting where hallucinated facts can mislead clinical decision-making and the provenance of each cited fact matters as much as the fact itself. We present DrugClaw, a multi-agent retrieval-augmented system that queries a registry of drug and pharmacovigilance skills via a reflection-driven state-machine workflow and returns answers grounded in primary regulatory or peer-reviewed records. We also contribute DrugAudit, a 3,772-item authority-aware benchmark with an evaluation panel that scores upstream-of-gold source match, token-level semantic snippet overlap, and citation faithfulness under a dual-judge LLM-as-judge protocol with inter-judge kappa = 0.88 (almost-perfect). Across DrugAudit plus drug-related subsets of MedQA (751) and PubMedQA (512), DrugClaw is top-1 on every column of the headline table: composite Evidence Index under both judges, judge-mediated answer correctness, primary-source rate (0.918, +10.1 pp over next-best), faithfulness (0.887, +5.9 pp), MedQA (0.920), and PubMedQA (0.693).

</details>


### [106] [Learning from Saturated Data: Signals Beyond Correctness for LLM Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01436)
*Hanno Hiss,Jasper Dekoninck,Martin Vechev*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They study whether already-solved (saturated) questions can still help train LLMs by using finer-grained quality signals instead of just right/wrong, and test this on arithmetic and GSM8K with mixed results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many existing benchmarks and training datasets for LLMs are saturated—models already achieve near-perfect accuracy—reducing their usefulness for further improvement. The authors want to know if these "solved" questions still contain useful training signal when we look beyond binary correctness and capture more nuanced information about solution quality.

Method: They replace binary correctness labels with two more detailed quality signals: (1) pairwise LLM self-judgments, where the model compares two of its own answers and decides which is better, and (2) token-level entropy, where the model’s uncertainty at each token is used as a proxy for overall solution quality. These signals are integrated into several training algorithms and applied to training Qwen3-1.7B-Base, first on a simple arithmetic task and then on GSM8K, comparing performance against standard supervised fine-tuning (SFT).

Result: On a simple arithmetic task, using quality-based signals yields up to 18.6% improvement over the base model and clearly outperforms standard SFT when training only on that task. On GSM8K, the improvements are smaller and highly sensitive to which quality signal is used. In particular, self-judgments often disagree with evaluations from a stronger external judge and can even harm performance, pushing it below the base model.

Conclusion: Fine-grained quality signals like self-judgments and entropy can extract additional learning signal from saturated questions and meaningfully improve a base model on relatively simple tasks. However, for more complex tasks, the reliability and calibration of these quality signals become critical; naively applying them can lead to modest gains at best and sometimes even degrade performance, indicating that more careful design and study are necessary.

Abstract: The growing capabilities of large language models (LLMs) have led to the saturation of many benchmarks and training datasets used to improve them. Motivated by this, we investigate whether questions solved with perfect empirical accuracy can nevertheless be used to improve downstream performance. To do so, we replace binary correctness with two sources of more fine-grained quality signals: (1) pairwise LLM self-judgments, in which the model evaluates the relative quality of its own solutions, and (2) token-level entropy, where token-level uncertainty is used as a proxy for solution quality. We incorporate these signals into several training algorithms and evaluate them on Qwen3-1.7B-Base. When training exclusively on a simple arithmetic task, quality-based signals improve performance by up to $18.6\%$ over the base model, substantially outperforming SFT. On GSM8K, however, gains are more modest and depend strongly on the quality signal. For instance, self-judgments show poor agreement with a stronger external judge and can even degrade performance below the base model. Overall, our results suggest that quality-based training can extract useful signal from saturated questions for base models, but that applying such signals to more complex tasks requires careful calibration and further study.

</details>


### [107] [Before and After Temperature: A Distributional View of Creative LLM Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01451)
*V. S. Raghu Parupudi,Harsha Ponnada,Aditi Kaushal,S. Shria Parupudi,Saiteja Dasari,Sahiti Bulusu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new reference-free metric of LLM creativity based on how temperature reshapes token distributions, achieving much higher correlation with human and LLM-judge creativity rankings than existing perplexity/entropy-style measures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing reference-free metrics for evaluating LLM creativity (self-perplexity, entropy, top-1 margin, compression ratio) correlate only moderately with human or strong-LLM judgments. The authors suspect that richer information lies not in the static token distribution but in how it changes under temperature scaling, especially across coherent vs. incoherent regimes.

Method: They generate continuations from Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct to 500 open-ended creative prompts under three temperatures (0.3, 0.8, 1.5). For each token, they examine the model’s predicted token distribution before and after temperature scaling and derive a single feature capturing the “reshaping” effect, focusing on cumulative-mass width (e.g., n_95) and how much probability mass moves outside the pre-temperature top-90% plausible set. They compute per-token aggregates and compare them to four standard reference-free baselines. Creativity ground truth is obtained from averaged GPT-4o/Gemini-2.5-Pro rankings for 500 prompts and from majority vote among three human raters for 150 prompts. They then compute Spearman rank correlations between metrics and these ground truths.

Result: Their reshaping-based per-token feature predicts within-prompt creativity rank with Spearman ρ=0.918 vs. the averaged GPT-4o/Gemini-2.5-Pro judge and ρ=0.870 vs. three-rater human-majority rankings, outperforming baselines whose |ρ|≈0.76 at best. The two ground-truth panels correlate at ρ=0.83, higher than the inter-human ceiling ρ=0.77, indicating low judge noise. Mechanistically, they show that at T=1.5 the model enters an incoherent regime where n_95(q) inflates from ≈1 to ≈131 tokens and about 13 percentage points of probability mass shift outside the pre-temperature top-90% set, giving a clear signature of incoherence. However, these per-token signals cannot distinguish T=0.8 from T=0.3, so separating coherent regimes requires sequence-level indicators.

Conclusion: Temperature-induced reshaping of token distributions yields a highly predictive, reference-free signal of LLM creativity that substantially outperforms perplexity and related baselines, especially for detecting the transition into incoherent high-temperature behavior. While very effective at separating coherent from incoherent regimes, the approach still needs complementary sequence-level features to differentiate degrees of creativity within the coherent range.

Abstract: Reference-free evaluation of large language model (LLM) creativity relies on perplexity, entropy, and top-1 margin. We show that a much stronger signal lives one step earlier in the pipeline: in how sampling temperature \emph{reshapes} the model's token distribution before the next token is drawn. On Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct generations of 500 open-ended creative prompts at $T \in \{0.3, 0.8, 1.5\}$, a single per-token feature derived from this reshaping predicts the within-prompt creativity rank at Spearman $ρ{=}0.918$ against an averaged gpt-4o\,/\,gemini-2.5-pro judge ($n{=}500$) and $ρ{=}0.870$ against a three-rater human-majority ranking ($n{=}150$). Each of four standard reference-free baselines (self-perplexity, mean predictive entropy, top-1 margin, gzip compression ratio) tops out at $|ρ|\!\approx\!0.76$ on both ground truths: a gap of $+0.165$ on averaged-LLM and $+0.110$ on human-majority, both far larger than the spread among the baselines themselves. The two ground-truth panels agree with each other at $ρ{=}0.83$, above the inter-human ceiling of $ρ{=}0.77$, so the comparison is not bottlenecked by judge noise. Mechanistically, the win comes from a sharp distributional signature of the incoherence regime: at $T{=}1.5$ the cumulative-mass width $n_{95}(q)$ inflates from $\sim\!1$ to ${\sim}\!131$ tokens and post-temperature mass leaks off the pre-temperature top-$90\%$ plausible set by about $13$ percentage points. The per-token aggregates do not separate $T{=}0.8$ from $T{=}0.3$; discriminating the two coherent regimes is left to sequence-level features.

</details>


### [108] [Cross-lingual Self-Consistency for Multilingual Reasoning with Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01464)
*Ahmed Elhady,Eneko Agirre,Mikel Artetxe*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes an unsupervised RL method that improves multilingual reasoning in LLMs by enforcing cross-lingual self-consistency, yielding large gains on MGSM and good generalization to unseen and out-of-distribution languages without using labeled or parallel data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Advanced reasoning abilities of LLMs are mainly available in high-resource languages such as English, while performance in many other languages lags behind. Existing approaches to multilingual reasoning rely on scarce multilingual reasoning data, parallel corpora, or supervised signals, and they generalize poorly to unseen languages. The authors want a scalable way to transfer and align reasoning capabilities across languages without needing gold answers or parallel data.

Method: They introduce an unsupervised reinforcement learning framework that enforces cross-lingual self-consistency: given semantically equivalent problems in different languages, the model is trained so that its final answers match across languages. The method does not rely on gold labels for correctness or explicit parallel data; instead, it uses the model’s own cross-lingual agreement as a training signal and optimizes the policy (the LLM) via RL to increase consistency of answers across languages, thereby enhancing multilingual reasoning ability.

Result: On the MGSM benchmark across 10 languages, their method yields average improvements of up to 21.7%. It also generalizes well to languages not seen during training, achieving an 18.2% mean gain on MGSM for unseen languages. Additionally, it improves performance by up to 6.2% on three out-of-distribution reasoning benchmarks, indicating that the learned consistency signal transfers beyond the training setup.

Conclusion: Enforcing cross-lingual self-consistency through an unsupervised RL objective can significantly enhance the multilingual reasoning ability of LLMs without needing supervised labels or parallel corpora. This consistency-based approach scales to many languages, generalizes to unseen and out-of-distribution settings, and offers a promising direction for closing the reasoning gap between high-resource and low-resource languages.

Abstract: Despite expanding their multilingual coverage, the advanced reasoning capabilities of LLMs remain largely confined to a few high-resource languages like English. To address this, we propose an unsupervised Reinforcement Learning (RL) approach to enhance multilingual reasoning by enforcing cross-lingual self-consistency: the principle that a model should produce the same final answer for equivalent problems in different languages. Existing methods are limited by the scarcity of multilingual reasoning data and show weak generalization to unseen languages. Our approach requires neither gold answers nor parallel data, and it achieves average gains of up to 21.7% on MGSM across 10 languages. In addition, our method demonstrates strong generalization, with an 18.2% mean improvement on MGSM languages unseen during training, and up to 6.2% gain on 3 out-of-distribution benchmarks. These results show the potential of consistency-based methods to improve the multilingual capabilities of LLMs without requiring supervised data.

</details>


### [109] [Peacemaker at ATE-IT: Automatic term extraction from Italian text for waste management data using encoder model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01469)
*Mahdi Bakhtiyarzadeh,Hadi Bayrami Asl Tekanlou,Jafar Razmara*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a low-cost, interpretable method for automatic term extraction tailored to a shared task, achieving consistent, balanced performance with limited resources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automatic term extraction underpins modern search engines and text processing, but faces challenges: scarcity of annotated training data, difficulty in accurately extracting multi-word terms, and domain shifts. There is a need for approaches that are both resource-efficient and interpretable, especially in low-resource settings and shared-task scenarios.

Method: The authors design an automatic term extraction system specifically for Task A of the ATE Shared Task. Their approach focuses on fine-tuning extraction strategies that can run on modest computational resources and remain interpretable. They evaluate the system using both type-level and micro-level precision, recall, and F1-score to capture complementary aspects of performance.

Result: Experiments show that the proposed method achieves consistent and balanced precision, recall, and F1-scores, performing competitively with other teams in the shared task despite its relative simplicity and low computational cost.

Conclusion: The method offers a simple, low-cost, and interpretable baseline for automatic term extraction in low-resource conditions. The results suggest substantial room for future model expansion, where more advanced, higher-performing systems might still preserve interpretability while improving performance further.

Abstract: The development of automatic term extraction has become increasingly important in modern technology. Automatic term extraction can be found in virtually every search engine that is currently available to users. Recent advancements have provided promising results for the extraction of automatic terms; however, accurate labeling is difficult because of several factors, such as the limited number of annotated documents available for training and the complexity of extracting multi-word expressions due to shifts in the domain. In this paper, we will present a low-cost and interpretable method of automatic term extraction, developed specifically for Task A of the ATE Shared Task. This new method utilizes fine-tuning extraction strategies that can run on a small amount of computational resources. We evaluated our automated system using both type-level and micro-level measures of precision, recall, and F1-score to measure both complementary aspects of the extraction performance. According to the experimental results, our proposed approach achieves consistent and balanced performance compared to other teams. Even though the technique itself is relatively straightforward, it serves as a good starting point for low-resource models. Overall, the findings point toward the possibility of significant future advancements (in model expansion) with higher-level performance still able to retain their ability to be interpreted.

</details>


### [110] [Sparse Autoencoders for Interpretable Emotion Control in Text-to-Speech](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01479)
*Hongfei Du,Jiacheng Shi,Sidi Lu,Gang Zhou,Ye Gao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They use sparse autoencoders to uncover and steer interpretable latent features in LLM-based TTS, enabling controllable emotional induction and suppression without changing model weights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based text-to-speech models can produce expressive speech but lack transparent, fine-grained, and interpretable emotional control. Existing methods mainly use external conditioning or coarse global steering, which do not reveal how emotions are internally represented and limit precise, bidirectional control of emotional expression.

Method: They analyze the semantic hidden states of an LLM-based TTS model with sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to discover sparse latent features related to emotion. They inspect how emotion-related variation distributes across these features and then design a feature-level intervention strategy that selectively amplifies or suppresses specific latent features during generation, without changing the backbone parameters. They also relate individual features to acoustic attributes such as pitch to understand their functional roles.

Result: They find that emotional variation is spread over multiple sparse latent features, but manipulating a small subset can effectively control emotions. Distinct features correlate with different acoustic properties like pitch. Empirically, steering these sparse features reaches or surpasses the performance of global steering and standard TTS baselines for emotion induction and suppression tasks.

Conclusion: Emotion in LLM-based TTS is encoded via coordinated contributions of multiple sparse latent features rather than a single global factor. By identifying and intervening on these features with SAEs, one can achieve interpretable, bidirectional emotional control without retraining or modifying backbone parameters, offering a more transparent and effective approach to controllable emotional TTS.

Abstract: Integrating large language models (LLMs) into text-to-speech (TTS) systems has improved speech expressiveness, yet interpretable emotional control remains challenging. Existing approaches primarily rely on external conditioning or global activation steering, offering limited insight into the internal representations underlying emotional control. In this work, we analyze emotion-related variation in the semantic hidden states of LLM-based TTS models using sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to identify sparse latent features. Our analysis shows that emotional variation is distributed across multiple sparse latent features, while intervening on a small subset enables interpretable emotion control. Building on this observation, we introduce a feature-level intervention framework for bidirectional emotion induction and suppression without modifying backbone parameters. We further show that distinct latent features are associated with specific acoustic attributes (e.g., pitch), suggesting that emotional expression arises from coordinated latent contributions rather than a single global shift. Empirically, steering these sparse latent features achieves comparable or superior emotion induction and suppression performance relative to global steering and existing TTS baselines.

</details>


### [111] [Beyond Topical Similarity: Contrastive Evidence Retrieval with Interpretable Attention Alignment in RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01482)
*Francielle Vargas,João Robiatti,Diego Alves,Lucas Pascotti Valem,Maximilian Seeth,Sebastián Ferrada,Ameeta Agrawal,Daniel Pedronette,André Freitas*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes CERA, a retrieval framework that improves factuality and interpretability in RAG by combining contrastive learning with attention alignment to human rationales, leading to better retrieval effectiveness and more faithful, evidence-focused explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems often suffer from factuality issues and low interpretability, especially in high-stakes domains like clinical trials. Existing dense retrievers typically focus on topical similarity and standard hard negative mining, which may not capture the specific evidential tokens that justify an answer, and model attention patterns are often unfaithful as explanations. The authors aim to build a retriever that not only retrieves more factually grounded documents but can also highlight token-level evidence in a way that aligns with human rationales.

Method: The authors introduce Contrastive Evidence Rationale Attention (CERA), a dense retrieval framework trained with two coupled objectives. First, they use triplet-based contrastive learning with a novel subjectivity-based hard negative selection strategy that chooses challenging non-relevant passages based on their subjective content, improving discriminative power. Second, they add an auxiliary interpretable attention alignment loss that supervises CLS-to-token attention. This loss uses human-annotated factual rationales and a part-of-speech–weighted masking distribution to encourage attention to focus on tokens that serve as evidence. By injecting this evidential inductive bias into the retriever’s attention mechanism during training, CERA learns to associate queries with specific evidence tokens rather than only overall topical similarity.

Result: On a large corpus of clinical trial reports, CERA outperforms standard baselines, including Contriever and other hard negative selection schemes, in retrieval effectiveness, particularly due to the subjectivity-based hard negative mining. Additionally, the attention alignment objective improves the faithfulness of the model’s attention as an explanation of its behavior, without materially sacrificing retrieval quality. The retriever becomes better at highlighting tokens that align with human-annotated factual rationales.

Conclusion: CERA demonstrates that combining subjectivity-aware hard negative selection with rationale-guided attention alignment can yield a dense retriever that is both more effective and more interpretable. Attention, when explicitly guided by human evidence annotations and POS-aware masking, can function as a more faithful explanation of retrieval decisions. This moves RAG systems beyond coarse topical similarity toward token-level evidence identification, enabling more transparent and evidence-centric retrieval in applications such as clinical trial report analysis.

Abstract: Ensuring factuality and interpretability in RAG remains an open and urgent problem. We introduce Contrastive Evidence Rationale Attention (CERA), the first retrieval framework to employ subjectivity-based hard negative selection and inject an evidential inductive bias into contrastive learning through an auxiliary attention alignment loss. CERA fine-tunes a dense retriever using two training objectives: triplet-based contrastive learning and interpretable attention alignment, which supervises CLS-to-token attention using a part-of-speech-weighted masking distribution over human-annotated factual rationales as evidence signals. Experiments on a large corpus of clinical trial reports demonstrate that the subjectivity-based hard negative selection substantially improves retrieval effectiveness compared to both Contriever and hard negative selection baselines. Furthermore, rationale alignment improves faithfulness while maintaining competitive retrieval performance, supporting the hypothesis that attention can serve as a more faithful explanation of model behavior when guided by human rationales. Moving beyond topical similarity, CERA enables the retriever to identify the specific tokens that constitute supporting evidence, promoting more interpretable evidence selection in RAG systems.

</details>


### [112] [TimeSage-MT: A Multi-Turn Benchmark for Evaluating Agentic Time Series Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01498)
*Yaxuan Kong,Qingren Yao,Yuqi Nie,Yichen Li,Yilei Shao,Stefan Zohren,Anna Vettoruzzo,Joaquin Vanschoren,Ming Jin,Qingsong Wen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces TimeSage-MT, a multi-turn benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on time series reasoning across realistic, evolving conversational workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing time series benchmarks mainly test single-step tasks like forecasting or anomaly detection, and do not reflect real conversational workflows where user goals evolve, analyses are iterative, and conclusions are drawn from cumulative evidence. It is unclear how well LLM agents can handle such multi-turn, agentic time series reasoning, including memory and decision-making over time.

Method: They build TimeSage-MT, a reproducible pipeline that transforms real-world time series from 8 domains into 240 multi-turn tasks containing 2,680 dialogue turns. Each task forms a conversation with verifiable answers, covering stages from basic exploration to complex, decision-oriented analysis. They also define a unified evaluation protocol and create a public leaderboard. Using this benchmark, they evaluate several frontier LLMs and TimeSage, a newly proposed structured agent with a dedicated time series skill library.

Result: Benchmarking shows that all evaluated systems experience significant performance drops on decision-oriented, multi-step tasks compared to simpler tasks. The analysis attributes this to failures in long-horizon memory, handling of uncertainty, and incorporation of domain knowledge into decisions, even for strong frontier LLMs and the proposed TimeSage agent.

Conclusion: TimeSage-MT reveals important limitations of current LLM-based time series agents in realistic, multi-turn decision workflows. The benchmark provides a rigorous, reproducible testbed and common protocol that can guide and measure future advances in agentic time series reasoning and decision-making systems.

Abstract: Time series data inform critical decisions across many real-world domains. While large language model (LLM) agents can analyze data through natural language and tools, it remains unclear whether they can conduct reliable time series analysis across multi-turn conversations. Existing benchmarks focus on single-step tasks such as forecasting and anomaly detection, overlooking practical workflows where user goals evolve, agents must build on prior analyses, and conclusions emerge from accumulated evidence. In this work, we introduce TimeSage-MT, a multi-turn benchmark for agentic time series reasoning with 240 tasks and 2,680 dialogue turns across 8 real-world domains, spanning basic exploration to decision-oriented analysis. TimeSage-MT is built through a reproducible pipeline that converts real-world time series data into multi-turn conversations with verifiable answers. It provides a unified evaluation protocol and public leaderboard for comparing time series agentic systems. To demonstrate the benchmark's utility, we evaluate frontier LLMs alongside TimeSage, a novel structured agent equipped with a comprehensive time series skill library. The results show sharp performance drops on decision-oriented tasks, driven by failures in memory, uncertainty handling, and domain-based decision making. TimeSage-MT exposes critical gaps in current agentic reasoning and provides a rigorous foundation for future development.

</details>


### [113] [Identifying High-Confidence Social Biases in LLMs for Trustworthy Conversational Tutoring Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01584)
*Aitor Arronte Alvarez,Naiyi Xie Fincham*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates how large language models used as conversational tutors handle social biases, finding that detecting bias in natural tutoring dialogues is harder than in benchmarks and that models are often wrongly overconfident, which can distort their feedback to students.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based tutoring systems are increasingly used for scalable, personalized education, but these models can perpetuate or amplify social stereotypes. Existing bias benchmarks typically use decontextualized sentences, not the rich, multi-turn instructional settings where these systems are deployed. There is a need to understand whether and how LLMs fail to recognize bias, especially when they are highly confident, because such failures directly affect students through distorted explanations and feedback.

Method: The authors create a new dataset tailored to conversational tutoring by regenerating realistic student–AI tutor dialogues and then injecting controlled, stereotypically biased turns derived from an existing bias benchmark. They then prompt multiple LLMs to judge these turns, measuring whether the models detect bias and how confident they are in their judgments. Both computational analyses and human evaluations are used to study model accuracy, confidence calibration, and how confidence shapes the models’ reasoning and feedback within the tutoring conversation.

Result: Across models, detecting stereotypical bias in the rich context of tutoring dialogues is significantly harder than in standard benchmark formats. State-of-the-art LLMs frequently fail to flag biased statements and remain highly confident in these incorrect judgments. The analyses show a systematic overconfidence: models often assign strong confidence to answers that overlook or mischaracterize social bias. This miscalibrated confidence also affects the tone and content of the feedback and reasoning they provide to the student.

Conclusion: The study concludes that current LLMs, when deployed as conversational tutors, pose specific risks related to undetected and overconfident social bias. Bias detection degrades in realistic instructional settings, and inflated model confidence can propagate or legitimize biased feedback to learners. The authors argue that mitigation must focus not only on improving bias recognition but also on calibrating confidence and reasoning in context, and they outline implications and directions for designing safer, more reliable LLM-based tutoring agents.

Abstract: Conversational tutoring agents have been shown to improve learning engagement and student outcomes, and large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used in these systems to provide scalable, personalized feedback. However, LLMs may perpetuate or amplify stereotypical social biases, posing particular risks in educational settings.
  In this study, we evaluate LLMs in conversational tutoring scenarios to identify high-confidence social biases, instances where models are unable to identify biased judgments in tutoring conversations while maintaining strong confidence in their assessments, potentially affecting their reasoning and the feedback they provide to learners. We present a new dataset generation method that enables bias evaluation under naturalistic instructional conditions by regenerating student-AI tutor interactions and introducing turns with controlled bias derived from a benchmark dataset.
  Using this data, we assess multiple LLMs' ability to detect stereotypical biases and analyze the confidence and reasoning underlying their responses through computational and human evaluations. We find that bias detection is substantially more challenging in conversational tutoring contexts than in benchmark-based evaluations, and that state-of-the-art LLMs are overconfident in their incorrect assessments of stereotypical bias statements. Moreover, model confidence strongly influences reasoning and feedback, highlighting the risks of overconfident, biased behavior in LLM-based tutoring agents. We conclude by discussing implications, mitigation considerations, and directions for future research.

</details>


### [114] [EvoPool: Evolutionary Programmatic Annotation for Label-Efficient Specialized Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01617)
*Tianyi Xu,Yaolun Zhang,Xuan Ouyang,Huazheng Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: EvoPool is an evolutionary multi-agent framework that generates cheap, high-quality annotators and uses a specialized aggregator to produce soft labels, enabling better supervised models than LLM-based annotation on specialized, label-scarce tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models are strong generalists but often worse than smaller supervised models in specialized, high-stakes domains (e.g., biomedical, legal) where manually labeled data is scarce and expensive. Existing practice of using LLMs as annotators is slow and costly at scale and still not optimal in performance, especially on tasks where LLMs are weak. The paper aims to create a scalable, low-cost way to generate high-quality training labels without intensive human labeling or expensive LLM calls.

Method: The authors propose EvoPool, an evolutionary multi-agent framework where three specialized agents iteratively generate executable annotator code. A small labeled validation set offers a fitness signal. A deterministic gate enforces viability (minimum performance), diversity (annotators are meaningfully different), and marginal contribution (each annotator adds value to the pool). Over generations, this process yields a pool of weak but complementary annotators that can label large datasets cheaply. To convert pool votes into training labels, they introduce EvoAgg, a text-aware aggregation method that combines semantic representations of the input text with annotator-vote patterns to output soft labels.

Result: The EvoPool-generated annotator pool achieves near-zero per-example labeling cost and is reported to be 4500–31000x faster than LLM-based annotation on a 100K-example dataset. On 7 of 8 tasks where LLMs perform relatively poorly—covering biomedical relation extraction, legal clause classification, complex reasoning, and dense multi-label biomedical problems—models trained on EvoPool/EvoAgg labels outperform the strongest LLM-annotation baseline by an average macro-F1 gain of +0.141, with maximum gains of +0.301 on the ChemProt dataset and +0.265 on a PubMed task.

Conclusion: EvoPool offers an efficient alternative to direct LLM annotation for specialized, label-scarce tasks, using evolutionary search over annotator programs plus a text-aware label aggregator to generate high-quality soft labels at very low marginal cost. This approach significantly improves downstream supervised performance in several complex, domain-specific benchmarks and demonstrates that carefully evolved annotators can outperform expensive general-purpose LLM annotators in LLM-weak regimes.

Abstract: Large language models excel at general tasks but underperform smaller supervised models in specialized, high-stakes domains where training labels are costly. We address this regime with EvoPool, an evolutionary multi-agent framework inspired by Darwinian evolution. Three specialized agents iteratively propose executable annotator code, a small validation set provides a fitness signal, and a deterministic gate keeps only annotators that pass viability, diversity, and marginal-contribution checks across generations. Pool votes are mapped to soft training labels by EvoAgg, a text-aware aggregator combining semantic features with annotator-vote features. The authored pool runs at near-zero per-example cost and is 4500 to 31000x faster than LLM annotation on 100K examples. Across 7 of 8 LLM-weak specialized and complex tasks spanning biomedical relation extraction, legal-clause classification, complex reasoning, and dense multi-label biomedical classification, EvoPool beats the strongest LLM annotation baseline by an average +0.141 macro-F1, peaking at +0.301 on ChemProt and +0.265 on PubMed. Code is available at: https://github.com/tianyi0216/EvoPool

</details>


### [115] [Benchmarking LLM-as-a-Judge for Long-Form Output Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01629)
*Junjie Chen,Yuxi Dong,Haitao Li,Weihang Su,Yujia Zhou,Min Zhang,Yiqun Liu,Qinyao Ai*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces LongJudgeBench, a benchmark to test how reliably large language models can act as judges for long-form text, revealing significant reliability gaps compared with human evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used for generating long-form text, there is a need to evaluate their outputs at scale. LLM-as-a-judge is promising but prior benchmarks mainly address short-form tasks, leaving open whether these judge models are reliable for more complex, document-level, long-form evaluation scenarios.

Method: The authors build LongJudgeBench, a comprehensive benchmark covering diverse real-world long-form scenarios and judging protocols. They then systematically test many LLM judges, spanning different base models and judging configurations (with/without rubrics, with/without reference answers), to measure their reliability across tasks.

Result: Experiments show a large reliability gap: existing LLM judges behave inconsistently across different long-form scenarios, and while providing evaluation rubrics or reference texts can improve performance, these aids do not fully solve the instability and misalignment issues.

Conclusion: LongJudgeBench exposes the limitations of current LLM-as-a-judge approaches for long-form evaluation and is intended as a foundation for developing more robust, context-aware, and human-aligned LLM judges; the authors release their code to facilitate further research.

Abstract: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for long-form generation, reliably evaluating long-form outputs has become a critical challenge. LLM-as-a-judge offers a scalable alternative to human evaluation, yet its reliability in long-form output evaluation remains underexamined: existing meta-evaluation benchmarks focus mainly on short-form outputs. Compared with short-form evaluation, long-form evaluation is not merely a matter of output length; it often requires judges to handle more complex document-level demands. In this work, we introduce LongJudgeBench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating LLM judges on long-form outputs across diverse real-world scenarios and judging protocols. We systematically evaluate a broad range of LLM judges, covering multiple base models and judging settings. Our results reveal a substantial reliability gap: current LLM judges remain unstable across scenarios, and rubrics or references are helpful but not always sufficient. We hope LongJudgeBench will support future research on more robust, context-aware, and human-aligned LLM-as-a-judge methods. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/LongJudgeBench-F782.

</details>


### [116] [AlphaToken: Decoupling Adaptation and Stability for Path-Aware Response Token Valuation in LLM Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01635)
*Liu Qing,Ou Wu,Yi Du*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: AlphaToken is a framework for valuing individual response tokens during LLM post-training, so training focuses on the most useful tokens while avoiding catastrophic forgetting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM post-training methods usually use simple, local heuristics to decide which tokens to train on (or weigh), and they rarely treat token selection as a rigorous valuation problem. This can lead to suboptimal learning on the target tasks and to catastrophic forgetting of pre-trained abilities. The authors want a principled, token-level way to decide where training signal should be concentrated.

Method: They define a token valuation framework called AlphaToken that decomposes value into two components: (1) adaptation—how much a token helps learning the target task, and (2) stability—how much it helps preserve the model’s pre-trained capabilities. Both objectives are made path-aware by combining (a) local, direct-path information from token gradients and (b) downstream, causal-path information from the autoregressive generation process. Because original retention data are not available, stability is approximated by a Fisher-drift proxy computed around a frozen pre-trained reference model. For scalable implementation, they generalize the Ghost Dot-Product technique to compute token-wise scores efficiently. During fine-tuning and preference optimization, they mask or downweight low-value tokens so that optimization focuses on high-value positions.

Result: When applied to LLM post-training, AlphaToken’s token selection improves performance on target tasks compared to standard training and heuristic-based token filtering. At the same time, it reduces catastrophic forgetting, preserving more of the model’s original capabilities, as shown by better retention metrics on pre-trained tasks.

Conclusion: Principled, path-aware valuation of individual response tokens can significantly improve LLM post-training. By separating adaptation from stability and approximating stability via a Fisher-drift proxy, AlphaToken provides an efficient, general mechanism to select informative tokens. Masking low-value tokens during optimization yields better task performance and mitigates forgetting, suggesting that fine-grained token valuation is an effective lever for controlling the trade-off between learning new tasks and retaining old skills.

Abstract: Token selection is pivotal for effective LLM post-training. However, existing methods mostly rely on local heuristics and rarely formulate token selection as a principled valuation of individual response tokens. We introduce $\textbf{AlphaToken}$, a response token valuation framework that decouples valuation into $\textbf{adaptation}$ (promoting target-task learning) and $\textbf{stability}$ (preserving pre-trained capabilities), and makes each objective $\textbf{path-aware}$ by combining the direct-path signal from local token gradients with the downstream causal-path signal in autoregressive generation. Since retention data are typically unavailable, AlphaToken approximates stability via a $\textbf{Fisher-drift proxy}$ anchored at the pre-trained reference model. For efficient computation, we extend Ghost Dot-Product to token-level valuation. AlphaToken masks low-value response tokens during fine-tuning and preference optimization, concentrating training signals on more valuable positions. Experiments show that AlphaToken improves post-training performance and mitigates catastrophic forgetting.

</details>


### [117] [Easier to Mislead Than to Correct: Harmful and Beneficial Revision in LLM Conformity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01637)
*Jiaming Qu,Lucheng fu,Yibo Hu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how large language models change their answers when exposed to other agents’ responses, showing that social influence often harms accuracy more than it helps, especially under consensus and authority cues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs are increasingly deployed in multi-agent settings where they see and react to each other’s outputs, there is a risk that they conform to group answers or authoritative peers even when those peers are wrong. Prior work showed that LLMs tend to move toward majority opinions, but it was unclear whether such conformity is on balance beneficial (correcting errors) or harmful (introducing new errors). Understanding this tradeoff is crucial for designing safe and reliable multi-agent LLM systems.

Method: The authors run a controlled experiment where an LLM first answers a question alone, then is shown simulated peer answers, and finally makes a revised decision. They systematically vary (1) the pattern of peer consensus (e.g., majority vs minority support for different answers) and (2) the presence of authority labels on peers, and measure how these social cues affect whether the model changes its answer. They evaluate four open-weight LLMs across seven question-answering datasets, and distinguish between beneficial revisions (changing from wrong to right) and harmful revisions (changing from right to wrong). They also test reasoning-style interventions like chain-of-thought and reflection to see if these reduce harmful conformity.

Result: Across models and datasets, peer agreement is much more effective at causing correct initial answers to be changed to incorrect ones than at correcting initial mistakes. Authority labels further increase the likelihood that models adopt the labeled answer, irrespective of its correctness. Attempts to mitigate this using generic reasoning prompts, such as chain-of-thought or reflection, do not consistently decrease harmful revisions while preserving beneficial ones. The net effect is that social influence in these settings tends to be systematically skewed toward harmful conformity rather than robust error correction.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that naïvely aggregating or exposing LLMs to peer answers in multi-agent systems is risky, as social cues like consensus and authority can easily mislead otherwise-correct models. Because generic reasoning interventions fail to reliably protect against harmful revisions, system designers should focus on mechanisms that explicitly verify the correctness of peer answers instead of relying on social agreement or authority to improve performance. Robust verification and evaluation of peer outputs is necessary for safe multi-agent LLM deployments.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly used in multi-agent systems, where they see and respond to other agents' answers. A key risk is conformity: a model may abandon its own answer simply because others agree on a different one. Prior studies show that LLMs often revise toward a majority answer, but it remains unclear whether these revisions help correct mistakes as often as they introduce new errors. In this paper, we conduct a controlled study in which an LLM first answers a question, then sees simulated peer responses before making a final decision. We manipulate two social cues: consensus structure and authority labels assigned to peers, and measure how they influence beneficial and harmful revisions. Across four open-weight LLMs and seven QA datasets, we find that peer agreement makes it much easier to mislead initially correct models than to correct initially wrong ones. Authority labels make models more likely to choose the endorsed answer, regardless of whether it is correct. More concerningly, generic reasoning interventions such as chain-of-thought and reflection do not reliably reduce harmful revision while preserving beneficial revision. These findings suggest that multi-agent LLM systems should verify peer answers rather than simply aggregate them.

</details>


### [118] [When Meaning Travels: A Granular Lens on Hybrid-MoE's Role in Idiomatic Understanding for Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01671)
*Sarmistha Das,Vaibhav Vishal,Shreyas Guha,Amaan Ali,Kitsuchart Pasupa,Sriparna Saha*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A paper introducing Varnika, a multimodal multilingual idiom corpus and a Hybrid Mixture-of-Experts framework to better represent and evaluate figurative language in low-resource Southeast Asian languages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Idioms in low-resource Southeast Asian languages carry rich figurative and cultural meaning that are hard for current computational models and cross-lingual transfer methods to capture, limiting creative, culturally informed multilingual education and NLP applications.

Method: The authors build Varnika, a multimodal corpus of 3,533 idioms with seven labeled idiomatic tones and aligned text–image representations. They then propose HybridMoE, a hybrid mixture-of-experts architecture that combines outputs from both selected and unselected experts to address expert sparsity and inject Idiomatic Property Signals using masked multimodal embeddings. They also design a three-stage evaluation framework (IDIO-TONE and Idiomatic Validation Score) to assess literal translation fidelity, visual-semantic alignment, and idiomatic meaning retention.

Result: HybridMoE, when applied on top of strong vision–language models, delivers 5–6% performance improvements on idiom-related tasks compared to baselines, especially in capturing figurative and culturally grounded meanings in Hindi, Bengali, and Thai idioms.

Conclusion: Curating a dedicated multimodal idiom corpus and using a hybrid mixture-of-experts architecture with idiom-aware signals significantly improves computational modeling of figurative, culturally embedded expressions in low-resource multilingual settings, and the proposed evaluation pipeline provides a more holistic way to assess such models.

Abstract: In the contemporary epoch of multilingual education, learning idioms provides a fascinating gateway towards creativity, cultural values, historical context, and diverse perspectives inherent to various linguistic traditions. This paper showcases the navigation of retaining figurative and cultural semantics in low-resource Southeast Asian languages such as Hindi, Bengali, and Thai, where culturally rich idioms pose significant obstacles for computational modeling and cross-linguistic transfer due to their deep metaphorical complexity. To tackle such complexity, we present Varnika, a reconstructed multimodal idiom corpus comprising 3,533 multilingual idioms, enriched with seven idiomatic tones aligned with both textual and visual representations. Additionally, to infer informative idiomatic understanding, we introduce a Hybrid Mixture-of-Experts (HybridMoE) framework that embeds multiple idiomatic expert opinions while mitigating expert sparsity by integrating outputs from both selected and unselected experts through controlled hybridization, further augmented with Idiomatic Property Signals via masked multimodal embeddings. To analyze the performance across multiple dimensions, we propose the IDIO-TONE and Idiomatic Validation Score, a three-stage evaluation pipeline measuring (i) literal translation fidelity, (ii) visual-semantic alignment, and (iii) idiomatic meaning retention. Empirical evaluations highlight that HybridMoE achieves 5--6\% performance gains across advanced vision language models, demonstrating improved representation of figurative language and culturally embedded meaning in multilingual multimodal settings

</details>


### [119] [Why Do Self-Harm Prediction Models Struggle to Generalise? Lexical and Semantic Variations in Emergency Department Triage Notes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01678)
*Liuliu Chen,Mike Conway,Jo Robinson,Vlada Rozova*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates why NLP models for detecting self-harm from ED triage notes often fail to generalize across hospitals by comparing lexical patterns, predictive features, and topics in notes from two institutions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-harm visits to EDs are key markers for elevated suicide risk, and NLP systems can help identify such cases automatically. However, models trained in one hospital often perform poorly in others, limiting clinical usefulness. Understanding the sources of this cross-site degradation is necessary to build more generalisable and reliable tools for self-harm detection.

Method: The authors compare emergency department triage notes from two hospitals. They analyze and contrast (1) lexical characteristics (word usage and expression patterns), (2) features that are most predictive of self-harm in each site’s models, and (3) salient topics in the notes, likely via topic modeling or similar techniques. They then relate these textual and feature differences to observed drops in cross-site model performance.

Result: They find substantial differences between hospitals in how self-harm is documented: variation in lexical expression and in which features the models treat as most predictive, even though central clinical themes like self-poisoning and self-injury are present in both datasets. These documentation differences correlate with reduced model performance when applied across sites.

Conclusion: Institution-specific documentation practices significantly affect NLP-based identification of self-harm in clinical notes and contribute to poor cross-hospital generalisation. To improve model robustness, methods that account for or adapt to institutional variation—such as domain adaptation, harmonised documentation standards, or feature normalisation—are needed.

Abstract: Self-harm presentations to emergency departments (EDs) are strongly associated with higher suicide risk. NLP models have shown robust performance in detecting self-harm from triage notes within single hospitals, yet performance often declines across institutions. To examine potential causes, we compare ED triage notes from two hospitals by analyzing lexical characteristics, highly associated predictive features, and salient topics. Our results reveal variation in lexical expression and feature importance related to self-harm across hospitals, despite consistent core themes such as self-poisoning and self-injury. These documentation differences are associated with reduced cross-site performance. Our findings provide insight into how institutional variation affects the identification of self-harm in clinical text and highlight potential methods to improve model generalisability.

</details>


### [120] [Encoded but Not Routed: Explaining the Table-Chart Gap in Scientific Claim Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01679)
*Sunisth Kumar,Xanh Ho,Tim Schopf,Andre Greiner-Petter,Florian Boudin,Akiko Aizawa*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates why multimodal LLMs perform worse on charts than on tables for the same data when checking scientific claims. It finds that models do encode chart information internally, but this information is not properly routed to the final prediction layer, unlike with tables.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal LLMs are increasingly used to help with scientific peer review, where they must judge whether textual claims are supported by evidence such as tables or charts. Prior work found a performance gap: models are much better with tables than with charts, even when both contain the same data. It is unclear whether this gap arises because models cannot extract information from charts at all (encoding failure) or because they fail to use the extracted information when making predictions (routing/usage failure). Understanding the root cause is important for improving VLMs for scientific and other high-stakes applications.

Method: The authors compare model behavior on paired table and chart evidence that represent the same underlying data. They use layer-wise linear probing to test how well each layer’s internal representations encode relevant chart versus table information, and they perform attention analysis to see how information flows from visual inputs to the prediction token. They run these analyses on three open-weight vision-language models (VLMs), examining how chart and table information is encoded and routed through the network at inference time.

Result: They find that chart information is robustly present in intermediate representations across layers—indicating that models can and do encode chart contents. However, for charts, this encoded information is not effectively propagated to the prediction position (the token responsible for the final answer), whereas for tables the information flows through successfully. This “routing gap” between encoding and prediction is consistent across models, data conditions, and experimental settings. Attention patterns further show that the way this disconnect appears differs by model architecture, suggesting architecturally distinct failure modes.
