Extensive experiments across multiple LLMs and multimodal models show that DocFormFlow consistently improves formatting accuracy while reducing token consumption compared to representative baselines. Further analysis reveals that precise target localization is the primary factor influencing formatting performance. We hope DocFormBench and DocFormFlow will facilitate future research toward more intelligent and reliable document formatting.

</details>


### [141] [Eyettention II: A Dual-Sequence Architecture for Modeling Fixation Location, Within-Word Landing Position, and Fixation Duration in Reading](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01964)
*Shuwen Deng,Cui Ding,David R. Reich,Paul Prasse,Lena A. Jäger*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Eyettention II is a lightweight deep-learning model that generates realistic, human-like eye-movement scanpaths during reading, addressing data scarcity for eye-tracking-based NLP and cognitive research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Eye movements during reading reveal cognitive processes and text properties and are useful for applications like improving language models and inferring reader traits. These applications require large eye-tracking datasets, but collecting such data is expensive and time-consuming, leading to data scarcity that limits model development and deployment.

Method: The authors design Eyettention II, an end-to-end deep-learning model that predicts complete scanpaths while reading. It outputs sequences of fixations in chronological order, including fixation locations, within-word landing positions, and fixation durations. The model is lightweight, trainable with limited GPU resources, and structured to align with established cognitive theories of eye-movement control in reading.

Result: Eyettention II achieves state-of-the-art performance in scanpath prediction, outperforming prior models. It reproduces key psycholinguistic phenomena observed in human eye-movement data, showing that its generated scanpaths are realistic and human-like.

Conclusion: Eyettention II effectively addresses eye-tracking data scarcity by generating high-quality synthetic scanpaths that reflect human-like gaze behavior. This enables progress in NLP tasks that use eye-movement data, supports piloting and design of psycholinguistic experiments, and offers a tool for exploring cognitive mechanisms beyond what is explicitly described in existing theoretical models.

Abstract: The way our eyes move while reading provides valuable insights into both the reader's cognitive processes and the properties of the text. In particular, eye-tracking-while-reading data has shown to be highly beneficial in various technological applications, such as enhancing and interpreting language models and inferring a reader's characteristics. However, these applications often rely on large-scale, data-driven models, which demand extensive eye-tracking datasets that are challenging to obtain due to the resource-intensive nature of data collection. To address the challenge of data scarcity, we develop Eyettention II, an end-to-end trained deep-learning model capable of generating realistic scanpaths consisting of a complete set of fixation attributes in chronological order, including fixation location, within-word landing position, and fixation duration. Our model is lightweight, efficiently trainable on limited GPU resources, and closely aligned with cognitive theories. We demonstrate that Eyettention II surpasses state-of-the-art models in scanpath prediction and mirrors human-like gaze behavior by capturing key psycholinguistic phenomena. With its robust performance, Eyettention II holds the potential to drive advancements in natural language processing, facilitate piloting the materials of psycholinguistic experiments, and uncover new insights beyond what is explicitly encoded in theoretical cognitive models.

</details>


### [142] [Training Prompt Matters: State-Adaptive Optimization for Robust Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01967)
*Wenhang Shi,Yiren Chen,Shuqing Bian,Zhe Zhao,Jinhao Dong,Pengfei Hu,Wei Lu,Xiaoyong Du*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper shows that seemingly similar training prompts can lead to very different forgetting and generalization behaviors in fine-tuned LLMs, and proposes a method (SAPO) that dynamically optimizes prompts during training to reduce forgetting and improve generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most work in prompt engineering focuses on inference-time prompts, assuming that during fine-tuning, different paraphrases of a task instruction are equivalent as long as they are semantically similar. However, practitioners observe catastrophic forgetting and unstable cross-task behavior in fine-tuned LLMs, and the effect of training prompt wording on these phenomena is poorly understood. The paper is motivated by the need to understand and exploit how training prompts influence learning dynamics, forgetting, and generalization across tasks.

Method: The authors empirically study how paraphrased training prompts affect both in-task performance and cross-task impacts such as catastrophic forgetting and generalization. They measure performance across multiple tasks and identify correlations between prompts’ cross-task effects. They then show that superior prompts can be predicted using pre-training task loss before actual learning. Building on these findings, they propose State-Adaptive Prompt Optimization (SAPO), which treats the training prompt as a dynamic variable that adapts based on the model’s state. SAPO uses task loss signals to select or adjust prompts during fine-tuning rather than keeping a fixed instruction, and is implemented as a lightweight add-on to standard fine-tuning procedures.

Result: Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that paraphrased prompts, while often similar in in-task accuracy, can differ substantially in how much they cause forgetting or support generalization to other tasks. The cross-task impacts of prompts are positively correlated across tasks, confirming that some prompts are consistently superior. The authors demonstrate that such superior prompts can be identified reliably using pre-learning task loss. Using SAPO, they achieve marked reductions in catastrophic forgetting and improvements in generalization, with significant performance gains over state-of-the-art fine-tuning baselines.

Conclusion: Training prompts are not interchangeable surface forms: their wording has systematic and substantial effects on forgetting and generalization in fine-tuned LLMs. Because some prompts consistently yield better cross-task behavior and can be predicted via pre-learning loss, they can be optimized and adapted during training. The proposed SAPO framework operationalizes this idea by making prompts state-adaptive, leading to more robust, generalizable fine-tuning. This shifts the view of prompts during training from static instructions to key levers for controlling learning dynamics.

Abstract: While prompt engineering is instrumental in maximizing the capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) during inference, the role of prompts during training remains critically underexplored. Prevailing fine-tuning paradigms typically treat training prompts as mere surface forms, assuming that semantically equivalent instructions yield identical learning outcomes. However, we reveal that this equivalence is deceptive: while paraphrased prompts often lead to comparable in-task performance, they induce drastically different cross-task impacts regarding catastrophic forgetting and generalization. Crucially, these impacts are positively correlated across tasks, indicating the existence of superior prompts that consistently yield better performance. Furthermore, we discover that these superior prompts can be robustly identified by task loss prior to learning. Leveraging these insights, we introduce State-Adaptive Prompt Optimization (SAPO), a lightweight yet effective training strategy that shifts task formulation from a static input to a dynamic, state-adaptive variable. Comprehensive experiments on diverse benchmarks confirm its effectiveness, which significantly mitigates forgetting while improving generalization, achieving substantial performance gains over state-of-the-art methods. These results provide insights into how training prompts shape learning dynamics and offer a practical recipe for robust fine-tuning. Our code is available at https://github.com/Eric8932/SAPO.

</details>


### [143] [MMG2Skill: Can Agents Distill In-the-Wild Guides into Self-Evolving Skills?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01993)
*Xinyu Che,Junqi Xiong,Yunfei Ge,Xinping Lei,Shihao Li,Hang Yan,Han Li,Yuanxing Zhang,Zhiqi Bai,Jinhua Hao,Ming Sun,Han Li,Jiaheng Liu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces guide-to-skill learning, turning noisy, human-oriented multimodal web guides into agent-executable skills, and presents a benchmark and framework (MMG2Skill) that significantly improves VLM agent performance across multiple domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Web guides contain rich procedural knowledge that could help autonomous agents solve long-horizon tasks, but these guides are multimodal, unstructured, noisy, and assume human actors, so existing agents cannot directly use them as skills. There is a need to systematically convert such real-world guides into reliable, editable, and improvable skills for agents and to evaluate how well agents can leverage them.

Method: 1) Formalize the problem of converting in-the-wild, multimodal guides into executable skills and improving them from observed trajectories, termed guide-to-skill learning. 2) Build MMG2Skill-Bench, a benchmark spanning GUI control, open-ended gameplay, and strategic card play, to evaluate agents on this task. 3) Propose MMG2Skill, a closed-loop framework that: (a) compiles web guides into structured, editable skills; (b) conditions a fixed VLM agent on these skills during task execution; and (c) revises the skills based on trajectory-level, root-cause feedback without using benchmark scores. 4) Add an analyzer-based early stopping mechanism for tasks where success can be inferred, to halt attempts once success is detected and avoid late-stage regressions.

Result: Across three domains and six different vision-language model backbones, MMG2Skill consistently beats vanilla baseline agents in every model-domain combination, with macro-average performance gains of +12.8 to +25.3 percentage points. Ablations show that naively prompting agents with raw guides can hurt performance, while both structured skill construction and trajectory-driven revision are critical to the improvements. The early-stopping analyzer reduces 25%-53% of attempts on tasks with reliable success signals, while preventing performance regressions that occur in late stages of execution.

Conclusion: Converting noisy, multimodal web guides into structured, revisable skills and integrating them in a closed-loop with a fixed VLM agent substantially enhances agent performance on long-horizon tasks. Raw guides alone are not sufficient and can be harmful; explicit skill structuring plus trajectory-based refinement are key. The proposed MMG2Skill framework and MMG2Skill-Bench establish a concrete setting and tools for studying guide-to-skill learning, and techniques like analyzer-based early stopping can further improve efficiency and stability when task success is inferable.

Abstract: Abundant procedural knowledge on the Web holds great potential for helping agents solve long-horizon tasks. However, such knowledge is often multimodal, heterogeneous, noisy, and implicitly assumes human executors, making it difficult to use directly as the skills required by agents. To bridge the gap between human-oriented guides and agent-executable skills, we formalize this problem as guide-to-skill learning: converting in-the-wild guides into executable skills and continuously improving them from trajectories observable to the agent. To evaluate the capability of existing agents on this task, we introduce MMG2Skill-Bench, the first benchmark designed for this problem. We further propose MMG2Skill, a closed-loop framework that compiles guides into editable skills, conditions a fixed vision-language model (VLM) agent on these skills during execution, and revises the skills from trajectory-level root-cause feedback without using benchmark scores. Across GUI control, open-ended gameplay, and strategic card play with six VLM backbones, MMG2Skill consistently outperforms vanilla baseline agents in every model-domain setting, achieving macro-average gains of +12.8 to +25.3 percentage points across backbones. Ablation studies show that directly prompting agents with raw guides can degrade performance, while both structured skill construction and trajectory-driven revision are necessary for the observed improvements. On success-inferable tasks, analyzer-based early stopping further prevents late-stage performance regressions and saves 25%-53% of attempts when the success signal is properly calibrated.

</details>


### [144] [CARTE: A Benchmark for Mapping Language Model Knowledge Across France](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01995)
*Sarah Almeida Carneiro,Christos Xypolopoulos,Xiao Fei,Yang Zhang,Michalis Vazirgiannis*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: CARTE is a multiple-choice benchmark to test how well LLMs handle fine-grained, region-specific knowledge within France, especially linguistic variation, showing notable performance gaps across regions and model sizes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM cultural benchmarks mostly test national-level knowledge and ignore how culture, language, and other characteristics can differ substantially within a single country. The authors want to measure whether LLMs can distinguish closely related regional contexts inside France instead of treating the country as culturally homogeneous.

Method: They construct CARTE, a 2,431-question multiple-choice benchmark covering the 13 metropolitan regions of France and 14 thematic domains (culture, language, demographics, economy, environment, mobility, etc.). They also build a focused subset, CARTE-LV, to test linguistic variation between regions. Then they evaluate 27 LLMs (1B–12B parameters) in few-shot settings to measure regional and domain-wise performance.

Result: Across 27 evaluated models, accuracy varies significantly by region and by model scale. Smaller and even some mid-sized models struggle with fine-grained intra-national distinctions, and performance disparities suggest that some regions are underrepresented or less well captured in pretraining data.

Conclusion: LLMs that appear competent at national-level cultural tasks can still fail at finer regional distinctions. The observed regional performance gaps and scale effects indicate systematic biases or coverage issues in pretraining corpora and highlight the need for better data and evaluation methods for intra-national cultural and linguistic variation.

Abstract: We introduce CARTE 1 (Culturally Anchored Regional-Territorial Evaluation), a multiplechoice benchmark for evaluating the ability of large language models (LLMs) to perform fine-grained reasoning over geographically grounded and regionally differentiated knowledge within France. While prior benchmarks focus on national-level cultural understanding, they largely overlook intra-country variation and the need to distinguish between closely related regional contexts. CARTE addresses this gap by introducing 2,431 questions spanning the 13 metropolitan regions of France and covering 14 thematic domains, including culture, language, demographics, economy, environment, and mobility. We further introduce CARTE-LV, a subset targeting Linguistic Variation across French regions, enabling focused evaluation of language-related differences. We evaluate 27 LLMs ranging from 1B to 12B parameters under few-shot settings. Our experiments reveal performance disparities across regions and model scales, suggesting systematic gaps in pretraining coverage and limited robustness to intra-national variation.

</details>


### [145] [Scaling Agentic Capabilities via Grounded Interaction Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02001)
*Wenhang Shi,Jinhao Dong,Yiren Chen,Zhe Zhao,Shuqing Bian,Wei Lu,Xiaoyong Du*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: GAIS is a framework that automatically synthesizes diverse, difficult, long-horizon agentic interaction data by grounding task generation in real-world tool protocols and structure-guided planning, yielding better and more data-efficient training data for tool-using LLM agents than prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches to building datasets for tool-using LLM agents rely heavily on unconstrained LLM generation, which tends to sample from the models' own priors rather than reflect the diversity, difficulty, and long-horizon structure of real-world tool interactions. Human annotation is too costly for large-scale coverage of realistic tools and tasks. The authors aim to systematically and automatically construct high-quality, grounded interaction data so that base LLMs can be trained into strong agentic systems without expensive human labeling and without overfitting to synthetic artifacts.

Method: GAIS works in two grounded phases. First, it constructs protocol-anchored environments by connecting to real-world Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers, ensuring that the available tools and interactions reflect realistic functional diversity and difficulty. Second, it performs structure-guided planning within these environments to synthesize tasks and interaction trajectories. This planning explicitly enforces logical dependencies between steps and adopts adversarial policies to push for higher task complexity and challenge. The result is a large set of long-horizon, high-fidelity trajectories of agent-tool interactions, which are then used to train base LLMs for agentic behavior.

Result: When evaluated on benchmarks for tool-using and agentic capabilities (BFCL, τ^2-Bench, ACEBench), models trained on GAIS-synthesized data substantially outperform state-of-the-art synthetic data baselines. Remarkably, base LLMs trained on GAIS data can match or surpass their own official instruction-tuned versions. GAIS also demonstrates superior data efficiency and scalability: it achieves strong performance with significantly fewer training examples and continues to yield performance gains as more data are added, whereas competing methods plateau.

Conclusion: Grounding synthetic data generation in real-world tool protocols and structure-aware planning is an effective way to train strong, scalable agentic LLMs. GAIS overcomes the limitations of unconstrained LLM-based synthesis by creating diverse, difficult, long-horizon tasks that better mirror real-world tool use. This leads to better-performing and more data-efficient agents, suggesting that grounded interaction synthesis is a promising direction for advancing general agentic intelligence.

Abstract: General agentic intelligence hinges on the ability to interact with diverse real-world tools to complete complex tasks, a capability fundamentally tied to the quality of interaction data. To bypass the prohibitive costs of human annotation, prevailing paradigms depend entirely on Large Language Models (LLMs) to scale the synthesis of agentic environments and tasks. However, such unconstrained generation often degenerates into biased random sampling of LLMs' internal priors, failing to capture the diversity and difficulty of real-world domains or construct high-fidelity, long-horizon tasks. In this work, we introduce Grounded Agentic Interaction Synthesis (GAIS), a framework that automates the scalable construction of diverse environments and complex tasks via a two-phase grounding mechanism. Specifically, we construct protocol-anchored environments derived from real-world Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers to ensure functional diversity and difficulty. Subsequently, we employ structure-guided planning to navigate these environments, actively enforcing logical dependencies and adversarial policies to generate complex tasks. Experiments on BFCL, $τ^2$-Bench, and ACEBench demonstrate that GAIS-synthesized data significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, enabling base models to match or even surpass their official instruction-tuned counterparts. Furthermore, GAIS exhibits superior data efficiency and scalability, achieving exceptional capabilities with significantly less data while maintaining continuous growth where baselines stagnate. Our code and dataset are publicly available at https://github.com/Eric8932/GAIS.

</details>


### [146] [Machine Learning for Coding Retail Product Names to Consumer-Price Categories: A Rule-plus-Bag-of-Words Pipeline with Reliability-Weighted Human-in-the-Loop Labeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02004)
*Vladimir Beskorovainyi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They build and evaluate a practical, reproducible pipeline to map messy product text from alternative data (scanner, web, receipts) into standard consumption categories, finding that simple bag-of-words models with few labels work extremely well and that common advanced labeling-weighting tricks add little.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Statistical offices increasingly use alternative price data, but product descriptions are noisy, short, and lack standard codes. To calculate official price indices, each product must be reliably mapped into a consumption classification like COICOP. Existing approaches are often ad-hoc, not easily reproducible, and may assume more structure (clean text, long descriptions) than these sources provide. There is a need to understand what simple, robust methods can achieve on this task and how to design efficient human-in-the-loop labeling protocols that scale.

Method: They propose a three-stage classification pipeline: (i) normalize and tokenize noisy product names; (ii) apply a rule-based pre-classifier using a trie (prefix tree) of category-specific key phrases and stop phrases to tentatively assign categories; (iii) for each candidate category, use a per-category binary confirmation model to decide final membership. For large-scale labeling, they employ a human-in-the-loop scheme where annotators only give binary valid/reject judgments; these are aggregated using dynamically updated reliability weights per annotator. The same rule-based logic used in the model is applied during labeling so that both improve over time. They conduct an empirical evaluation on a controlled setup (one category, real positives vs. hard negatives, multiple random seeds) comparing bag-of-words linear models, MLPs, and n-gram features. They also run a Monte Carlo simulation to compare different label aggregation schemes (reliability-weighted voting vs. majority vs. Dawid–Skene) and discuss implications for price-level quality control and system design for statistical offices.

Result: In their main classification experiment, simple bag-of-words models nearly solve the task, reaching F1 scores around 0.99; a linear classifier performs as well as a multilayer perceptron, and including word-order features (n-grams) brings no measurable benefit. They find that roughly 67 labeled examples are already enough to achieve this high performance. In the Monte Carlo study of annotator-aggregation schemes, the reliability-weighted voting rule offers only marginal gains over plain majority voting because the additive reliability weights saturate. In contrast, the Dawid–Skene EM-based method recovers true labels substantially better. They also identify practical checks for price-level data quality and pitfalls in designing classification systems for transaction data, though these are only discussed qualitatively since no confidential data or code is shared.

Conclusion: For mapping noisy transaction-style product descriptions to standard consumption categories, relatively simple and transparent approaches are sufficient: bag-of-words with linear models and modest amounts of labeled data can effectively saturate performance, and more complex architectures or word-order modeling bring negligible gains in their setting. Human-in-the-loop labeling can be run efficiently with basic aggregation rules, but when higher label quality is needed, methods like Dawid–Skene clearly outperform simple reliability-weighted or majority voting. The work yields practical design recommendations for statistical offices, emphasizing reproducibility, simplicity, and robust quality control for using alternative data in official consumer-price measurement, while making clear that the presented figures are illustrative and not based on confidential sources.

Abstract: Consumer-price measurement increasingly draws on alternative data sources -- scanner, web-scraped, and transaction/receipt data. A recurring obstacle is that product descriptions in such sources are short, noisy, and abbreviated, with no standard product code, so each item must first be mapped to a consumption classification (e.g., the UN COICOP scheme) before prices can be compared. This paper studies that mapping as a general, reproducible method. The pipeline is: (i) text normalization and tokenization of noisy item names; (ii) a prefix-tree (trie) rule-based pre-classifier driven by per-category key-phrases and stop-phrases; and (iii) a per-category binary confirmation model deciding whether an item belongs to a tentatively assigned category. For labels at scale we use a human-in-the-loop protocol in which annotators give a binary valid/reject judgment, aggregated by a dynamically updated reliability weight; the model joins the same rule, enabling continual fine-tuning. Our empirical finding is deflationary: in a controlled, leakage-free study (one category, real positives vs. hard negatives, five seeds), bag-of-words models essentially saturate the task (F1 about 0.99) -- a linear classifier matches a multilayer perceptron, explicit word-order (n-gram) features add nothing, and about 67 labeled examples already suffice. A Monte-Carlo study of the labeling protocol shows the reliability-weighted vote barely beats plain majority (its additive weights saturate) while Dawid-Skene recovers labels markedly better. We also discuss price-level quality control and design lessons for statistical offices considering transaction data. All figures are illustrative; no confidential data, code, or documentation is reproduced.

</details>


### [147] [Automated Essay Scoring and Language Certification: Assessing Generalizability, Agreement and Validity for French](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02009)
*Rodrigo Wilkens,Rémi Cardon,Vincent Folny,Thomas François*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes and applies an enhanced, practical validation framework for evaluating Automated Essay Scoring (AES) systems, demonstrating its usefulness on French exam essays and improving the state of the art.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AES benchmarks often focus on narrow, minimalist metrics (e.g., a single correlation or agreement score), which conflicts with broader evaluation recommendations like the argument-based validation framework (ABV), especially for high-stakes language tests where rich, multidimensional evidence is required to justify system use.

Method: The authors extend the ABV framework by making it more operational and practical: they incorporate fairness analysis, study correlations between model scores and linguistic features, analyze prediction errors, and evaluate model agreement with human raters. They then apply this framework to French AES, systematically comparing 8 different model architectures on a large corpus of 27k exam essays (each rated by 2 human raters) and a separate generalization corpus of 961 essays (each rated by at least 9 raters).

Result: Using the enhanced ABV framework, the authors obtain a nuanced picture of how different French AES models perform, including where they succeed, where they fail, and how fairly and consistently they operate relative to human raters. Their experiments show both the methodological value of the framework and empirical performance gains: they advance the state-of-the-art performance for French AES on the studied datasets.

Conclusion: The enhanced ABV-based evaluation approach provides a richer, more trustworthy assessment of AES systems than standard minimalist benchmarks. Applying it to French AES both reveals important model strengths and weaknesses and pushes performance forward, making a case that high-stakes AES deployments should rely on such multidimensional validation rather than on single-metric evaluations.

Abstract: In Automated Essay Scoring (AES), benchmarking practices have fostered minimalist evaluation practices, in contrast with the broader-view recommendations of evaluation frameworks, such as the argument-based validation framework (ABV), which argued in favor of a multidimensional assessment of systems, especially in the context of high-stakes language tests. In this paper, we introduce an enhanced and more practical version of the ABV framework, incorporating fairness analysis, correlations with linguistic features, prediction error evaluation, and model agreement compared with human raters. Applying this framework to French AES, we compare 8 model architectures on a corpus of 27k exam essays (2 raters each) and a generalization corpus of 961 essays (at least nine raters each). Our analyses illustrate the benefits of applying the ABV framework to better understand the capabilities and pitfalls of AES models, while also advancing the state-of-the-art for French AES.

</details>


### [148] [PlanarBench: Evaluating LLM Spatial Reasoning via Planar Graph Drawing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02010)
*Oleksandr Nikitin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces PlanarBench, a benchmark testing LLMs’ ability to draw planar graphs as ASCII art from edge lists, showing that graph edge count is the main factor driving difficulty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM graph benchmarks typically treat node count as the primary measure of graph difficulty and often focus on tasks that can be partially solved via memorization or pattern recall. The authors aim to create a benchmark that directly tests spatial and combinatorial reasoning about planar graphs, where superficial memorization is ineffective because many graph representations are equivalent under permutations of edges, orientations, and node labels.

Method: The authors design PlanarBench, a dataset and evaluation protocol where models are given only an edge list of a connected planar graph and must output a correct planar embedding as ASCII art. They select the 199 simplest non-isomorphic connected planar graphs with 2–7 vertices. Ninety-one language models are evaluated on this task. Models’ performance is then analyzed against structural properties of the graphs, particularly edge count and node count, to see which better predicts difficulty.

Result: Across 91 evaluated models, the primary empirical finding is that edge count strongly predicts task difficulty (correlation r = -0.85 between edge count and model performance), whereas node count alone is less explanatory. This pattern had not been identified by prior LLM graph benchmarks, which mainly used node count as the difficulty axis.

Conclusion: PlanarBench reveals that, for LLMs solving planar graph drawing from edge lists, the number of edges is a key determinant of problem difficulty, highlighting the importance of graph density and spatial complexity rather than just size in nodes. This challenges existing benchmark design choices and suggests future graph reasoning benchmarks should account for edge-related complexity, not only node count.

Abstract: PlanarBench tests whether LLMs can draw planar graphs as ASCII art given only an edge list -- a spatial reasoning task that resists memorization because edge order, edge orientation, and node labels are all permutable.
  We evaluate 91 models on the 199 simplest non-isomorphic connected planar graphs (2 - 7 vertices).
  Edge count is the dominant difficulty predictor ($r = -0.85$) -- a finding not reported in prior LLM graph benchmarks, which use only node count as the difficulty axis.

</details>


### [149] [Unveiling the Entropy Dynamics of Chain-of-Thought Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02020)
*Ting Xu,Xu He,Yupu Lu,Jiankai Sun,Dong Li,Wai Lam,Jianye Hao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how the uncertainty (entropy) of Chain-of-Thought reasoning evolves over time and uses this structure to make inference more efficient and reliable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Chain-of-Thought prompting improves reasoning but is often inefficient: models keep generating many tokens after they have effectively converged to the correct answer. There is also a need for safe early-exit mechanisms and better test-time scaling that can reliably identify when a reasoning trajectory has stabilized without extra training or supervision. The authors aim to understand the dynamics of CoT uncertainty and exploit them to design principled, training-free inference control methods.

Method: The authors analyze the entropy dynamics of CoT token sequences and identify a two-phase pattern: an initial Uncertainty Region (exploration) followed by a Confidence Region (convergence). They formalize detection of the transition point as a sequential change-point detection problem. They then apply the Cumulative Sum (CUSUM) algorithm, a classical statistically optimal change-point detector, to monitor CoT entropy in real time and detect entry into the Confidence Region. On top of this, they build a training-free framework for two tasks: (1) early exit—stopping generation once the Confidence Region is detected; and (2) test-time scaling—using Confidence Region signals to weight and prioritize converged trajectories in voting schemes.

Result: Empirically, their CUSUM-based framework gives a better accuracy–efficiency trade-off (Pareto frontier) for early exit than prior methods such as DEER and Dynasor. Specifically, CUSUM attains 63.06% accuracy with 11.1% token reduction, improving accuracy over DEER and Dynasor by 3.28% and 4.36%, respectively, at comparable efficiency. For test-time scaling, CUSUM-weighted voting consistently outperforms standard self-consistency, indicating that trajectories detected as being in the Confidence Region are more informative and reliable.

Conclusion: CoT reasoning exhibits a robust two-phase entropy structure, with a high-uncertainty exploration phase followed by a high-confidence convergence phase. The Confidence Region is both highly reliable (answers there are stable and accurate) and highly redundant (many extra tokens add little value). By casting Confidence Region detection as a change-point detection problem and using CUSUM, the authors provide a training-free, statistically principled way to control inference in real time. This yields safer early exit and stronger test-time scaling than existing baselines, demonstrating that monitoring entropy dynamics is a powerful lever for improving CoT efficiency and reliability.

Abstract: This paper investigates the entropy dynamics of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and uncovers a consistent two-phase structure: an Uncertainty Region of exploration transitioning sharply to a Confidence Region of convergence. We demonstrate that the Confidence Region possesses two critical properties: 1) High Reliability -- answers in the confidence region become highly accurate and stable, and 2) High Redundancy -- models generate unnecessary tokens long after reaching the correct answer. These properties unlock more efficient and reliable inference strategies: 1) Early Exit leverages reliability and redundancy to terminate computation safely when returns diminish, and 2)Test-Time Scaling uses the Confidence Region signal to prioritize converged trajectories. To operationalize these insights, we formulate Confidence Region detection as a sequential change-point detection problem, being the first to apply classical change-point methods to monitor CoT reasoning. Using the Cumulative Sum (CUSUM) algorithm, a statistically optimal change-point detector, we develop a training-free framework for real-time inference control. Experiments show our approach establishes a superior Pareto-frontier for early exit. CUSUM achieves 63.06% accuracy with 11.1% token reduction, outperforming DEER and Dynasor by 3.28% and 4.36% in accuracy respectively. For test-time scaling, CUSUM-weighted voting consistently outperforms self-consistency.

</details>


### [150] [SentGuard: Sentence-Level Streaming Guardrails for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02041)
*Jiaqi Yu,Xin Wang,Yixu Wang,Jie Li,Yan Teng,Xingjun Ma,Yingchun Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces SentGuard, a sentence-level streaming safety guardrail for LLMs that moderates outputs in near real time, balancing timely intervention with decision stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing moderation approaches for LLMs are either response-level, which intervene only after the full output is generated (too late for streaming), or token-level, which act on incomplete semantics and thus cause unstable, noisy, and over-frequent safety triggers. With LLMs increasingly used in streaming, long, reasoning-heavy settings, there is a need for a guardrail that can make reliable, early safety decisions as the output unfolds, without excessive latency or false alarms.

Method: Propose SentGuard, a sentence-level streaming moderator that runs in parallel with the main LLM. A small waiting buffer aggregates generated tokens into sentence chunks. Only chunks that pass safety checks are released to the user, creating a short delay that lets SentGuard analyze already-complete sentences while the LLM continues decoding later content. To train and evaluate this, the authors construct StreamSafe, a benchmark with per-sentence annotations over 8 harm categories, marking how safety risks appear and evolve across reasoning and answer segments. SentGuard is trained with a coarse-to-fine objective designed to detect unsafe intent as soon as it appears at sentence boundaries.

Result: On 5 safety benchmarks, SentGuard detects 90.5% of unsafe cases within two sentences. It keeps the streaming false-positive rate low at 7.41%, and outperforms existing token-level or response-level baselines in timely detection and overall safety performance.

Conclusion: Sentence-level streaming moderation offers a more effective tradeoff between timeliness and stability than current token- or response-level guardrails. With the StreamSafe benchmark and the coarse-to-fine training objective, SentGuard can reliably catch unsafe content very early in the generation while avoiding excessive false alarms, making it well-suited for real-time, reasoning-intensive LLM applications.

Abstract: Large language models increasingly stream long, reasoning-intensive responses in real time, making when to moderate as critical as whether to moderate. Existing guardrails fall into two unsatisfactory extremes: response-level methods delay intervention until the full output is generated, whereas token-level methods act on incomplete semantics, often producing unstable decisions and excessive guard invocations. To address this challenge, we propose SentGuard, a sentence-level streaming guardrail that operates in parallel with generation. A lightweight waiting buffer groups streamed tokens into sentence chunks and releases only verified chunks to the user, introducing a small offset that enables SentGuard to assess the current prefix while the target LLM decodes subsequent content. To support this, we construct StreamSafe, a benchmark with structured per-sentence annotations across 8 harm categories, capturing the evolution of safety risks across both reasoning and response segments. We further train SentGuard with a coarse-to-fine objective to detect unsafe intent as soon as it emerges at sentence boundaries. Experiments on 5 safety benchmarks show that SentGuard outperforms existing baselines, detecting 90.5% of unsafe cases within two sentences while maintaining a low streaming false-positive rate of 7.41%.

</details>


### [151] [DFlare: Scaling Up Draft Capacity for Block Diffusion Speculative Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02091)
*Jiebin Zhang,Zhenghan Yu,Song Liu,Eugene J. Yu,Zheng Li,Dawei Zhu,Jiangshan Duo,Weimin Xiong,Yifan Song,Guanghua Yu,Jianchen Zhu,Sujian Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new speculative decoding method, \


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain the problem addressed by the paper and why it is important or interesting.

Method: Summarize the approach, model, algorithm, or experimental setup used to solve the problem.

Result: Describe the main experimental findings and quantitative improvements reported in the paper.

Conclusion: Summarize the overall takeaway, implications, or future directions suggested by the paper.

Abstract: Block diffusion speculative decoding accelerates LLM inference by predicting all tokens within a block simultaneously for the target model to verify in parallel. Predicting an entire block at once requires a sufficiently capable draft model and effective utilization of the target model's internal knowledge. However, the state-of-the-art method DFlash constrains all draft layers to share a single fused representation derived from only a few target layers, limiting per-layer expressiveness and hindering further scaling of draft capacity. In this paper, we present \modelname, which flares out the narrow conditioning bottleneck of DFlash through a lightweight layer-wise fusion mechanism: each draft layer attends to its own learnable combination of a broad set of target layers at negligible overhead, simultaneously injecting richer target knowledge and providing every draft layer with a distinct input. This enhanced per-layer expressiveness enables scaling the draft model to deeper architectures with consistent gains. We further scale training data from 800K to 2.4M samples to fully exploit the enlarged capacity. On six benchmarks spanning mathematical reasoning, code generation, and conversation, \modelname attains average wall-clock speedups of 5.52x on Qwen3-4B, 5.46x on Qwen3-8B, and 3.91x on GPT-OSS-20B, improving over DFlash by roughly 11\%, 8\%, and 5\% respectively. Our code is available at https://github.com/Tencent/AngelSlim.

</details>


### [152] [The Role of Ambiguity in Error Prediction via Uncertainty Quantification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02093)
*Ieva Raminta Staliūnaitė,James Bishop,Andreas Vlachos*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper improves error prediction for LLMs by separating input ambiguity from standard uncertainty signals, leading to significantly better detection of wrong answers in QA tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard uncertainty quantification (UQ) is widely used to predict whether model outputs are correct, but UQ conflates two sources of uncertainty: (1) model ignorance/limitations and (2) aleatoric uncertainty inherent to ambiguous inputs. This mixture makes error prediction less reliable, especially when questions have multiple plausible answers. The authors aim to explicitly model and factor out input ambiguity so that UQ focuses more on model errors rather than on unavoidable ambiguity.

Method: They experiment on QA with six existing UQ metrics applied to LLMs, first empirically showing that these metrics predict errors better on unambiguous questions than on ambiguous ones. Then they introduce an ambiguity-aware error prediction framework: (a) obtain ambiguity labels (both gold and model-predicted) for QA instances, (b) use Gated Experts to route instances based on ambiguity, and (c) apply Selective Prediction to decide when to abstain or flag potential errors, integrating the ambiguity signal with UQ metrics.

Result: Empirically, ambiguity labels significantly enhance the predictive power of UQ metrics. The method yields more accurate error prediction across different LLM families, training and evaluation setups, and multiple QA datasets, including those designed to be unambiguous. Incorporating ambiguity leads to gains of over 10 PRR points for individual UQ metrics on standard benchmarks, showing that separating aleatoric uncertainty from model uncertainty measurably improves error detection.

Conclusion: Error prediction for LLMs benefits from explicitly modeling input ambiguity in addition to standard UQ. By disentangling aleatoric uncertainty from the UQ signal and routing instances via ambiguity-aware mechanisms, the framework improves the reliability of identifying when a model answer is likely wrong. This suggests future UQ and reliability work for LLMs should systematically account for and exploit ambiguity, even in datasets believed to be unambiguous.

Abstract: The task of Error Prediction, namely predicting whether a model output is correct, is commonly tackled with Uncertainty Quantification (UQ). However, while uncertainty metrics capture when models lack knowledge or capacity to make a prediction, they also reflect aleatoric uncertainty, which is inherent in the model input and context. This paper presents a method for improving error prediction for Large Language Models (LLMs), by disentangling input ambiguity from UQ signal. We conduct experiments on the task of Question Answering (QA) with six UQ metrics and show that UQ metrics are more predictive of errors on unambiguous instances than on questions with multiple plausible answers. We use Gated Experts and Selective Prediction to incorporate gold and predicted ambiguity labels into the error prediction pipeline. We find that ambiguity information improves error prediction scores across model families, training and evaluation paradigms, datasets (including allegedly unambiguous ones), and sources of aleatoric uncertainty, yielding improvements of over 10 points of PRR for individual UQ metrics on standard datasets.

</details>


### [153] [PortBERT: Navigating the Depths of Portuguese Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02100)
*Raphael Scheible-Schmitt,Henry He,Armando B. Mendes*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces PortBERT, an efficient RoBERTa-based family of Portuguese language models that balance performance and compute efficiency, outperforming or matching existing models while reporting detailed efficiency metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a lack of efficient, language-specific transformer models for Portuguese; existing work prioritizes model size or accuracy and largely ignores training and deployment efficiency and compute-performance tradeoffs.

Method: Train two RoBERTa-style Portuguese models (base and large) from scratch using byte-level BPE on 450+ GB of cleaned, deduplicated mC4 and OSCAR23 (CulturaX) data with fairseq, ensuring stable pre-training across GPU and TPU. Evaluate them on ExtraGLUE (translated GLUE and SuperGLUE tasks) and measure not only accuracy but also training and inference times and fine-tuning throughput.

Result: PortBERT base and large achieve competitive or superior performance compared to existing monolingual and multilingual models on ExtraGLUE, while providing detailed empirical data on training and inference efficiency and fine-tuning throughput.

Conclusion: PortBERT offers an effective and efficient family of Portuguese language models that fill a gap in Portuguese NLP by explicitly addressing compute-performance tradeoffs. The models, along with Hugging Face releases and fairseq checkpoints, enable further research and practical, resource-aware applications.

Abstract: Transformer models dominate modern NLP, but efficient, language-specific models remain scarce. In Portuguese, most focus on scale or accuracy, often neglecting training and deployment efficiency. In the present work, we introduce PortBERT, a family of RoBERTa-based language models for Portuguese, designed to balance performance and efficiency. Trained from scratch on over 450 GB of deduplicated and filtered mC4 and OSCAR23 from CulturaX using fairseq, PortBERT leverages byte-level BPE tokenization and stable pre-training routines across both GPU and TPU processors. We release two variants, PortBERT base and PortBERT large, and evaluate them on ExtraGLUE, a suite of translated GLUE and SuperGLUE tasks. Both models perform competitively, matching or surpassing existing monolingual and multilingual models. Beyond accuracy, we report training and inference times as well as fine-tuning throughput, providing practical insights into model efficiency. PortBERT thus complements prior work by addressing the underexplored dimension of compute-performance tradeoffs in Portuguese NLP. We release all models on Huggingface and provide fairseq checkpoints to support further research and applications.

</details>


### [154] [A Primer in Post-Training Reasoning Data: What We Know About How It Works](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02113)
*Yaoming Li,Guangxiang Zhao,Qilong Shi,Lin Sun,Xiangzheng Zhang,Tong Yang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A primer that synthesizes and systematizes over 150 studies on post-training reasoning data for large reasoning models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Research on post-training reasoning data is rapidly expanding but highly fragmented across many types of publications and system reports. There is no unified framework to understand what reasoning data are used for post-training, what makes them effective, how they are built, and how they scale, making it hard for researchers and practitioners to design, evaluate, and attribute progress in large reasoning models.

Method: The authors conduct a broad literature synthesis of 150+ public studies and system reports related to post-training reasoning data. They organize this body of work around four central questions—data objects, usefulness, construction, and scaling—creating a structured framework that integrates dataset papers, RL recipes, reward-model work, benchmarks, and system reports.

Result: They produce a taxonomy and organizational framework that categorizes existing post-training reasoning data and methods, clarifies their roles and properties, and maps out relationships across diverse lines of work. The synthesis highlights common patterns, differences, and open areas in how reasoning data is collected and used to train large reasoning models.

Conclusion: A unified, question-driven framework can organize the scattered landscape of post-training reasoning data, serving as a foundation for clearer attribution of progress and more systematic design of future reasoning datasets and post-training recipes. This primer is intended as a reference point to guide future research and practical deployments involving reasoning data.

Abstract: Post-training has become a primary driver of recent progress in large reasoning models, and reasoning data are often the key variable determining whether this stage succeeds. Work on post-training reasoning data has grown rapidly, yet this literature remains scattered across dataset papers, reinforcement-learning recipes, reward-model studies, benchmarks, and frontier system reports. This paper is the first primer to synthesize over 150 key public studies and system reports on post-training reasoning data. We organize the field around four questions: what data objects exist, what makes them useful, how they are constructed, and how they scale. Together, this organization provides an attribution framework for future reasoning-data releases and post-training recipes.

</details>


### [155] [Multilingual Idioms in Sentences and Conversations Across High-, Medium-, and Low-Resource Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02147)
*Saeed Almheiri,Bilal Elbouardi,Salsabila Zahirah Pranida,Irina Nikishina,Ashwath Rao B,Parameswari Krishnamurthy,Muhammad Cendekia Airlangga,Rifo Ahmad Genadi,Nguyen Phan Gia Bao,Amir Hossein Yari,Hawau Olamide Toyin,Nurdaulet Mukhituly,Mena Attia,Besher Hassan,Ahmad Fathan Hidayatullah,Tatsuki Kuribayashi,Haonan Li,Suma Bhat,Fajri Koto*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: MIDI is a multilingual idiom dataset with sentence and conversational contexts across 18 languages, revealing that models struggle more with idioms in low-resource languages and with literal readings, even when given richer context.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Idiomatic expressions are hard for multilingual NLP systems because their meanings depend on context and differ between figurative and literal usages. Existing work centers on high-resource languages and tests idioms in isolation, which does not reflect realistic discourse and provides limited insight into true idiom understanding and cross-lingual generalization.

Method: The authors construct MIDI, a multilingual idiom dataset covering 3 high-, 3 medium-, and 12 low-resource languages, curated by native speakers. The dataset includes idioms in both sentence-level and conversational contexts, each annotated for literal and figurative readings. They then benchmark state-of-the-art language models on idiom interpretation tasks, analyze performance by language resource level and reading type, and run controlled tests plus interventions on hidden representations to disentangle memorization effects from genuine reasoning.

Result: Benchmarking shows that model performance on idiom understanding drops significantly in low-resource languages, and that literal interpretations are consistently more difficult than figurative ones across all resource tiers. Adding conversational context improves accuracy but does not close the gap across languages or between literal and figurative readings. Representation-level analyses indicate that much of current model success stems from memorization rather than robust reasoning about context and meaning.

Conclusion: MIDI exposes systematic weaknesses in current multilingual models: they do not generalize idiom understanding well to low-resource languages and are particularly poor at recognizing literal readings, even with richer conversational context. The findings suggest that improved training strategies and architectures are needed to go beyond memorization and achieve deeper, context-sensitive idiom comprehension across diverse languages.

Abstract: Idiomatic expressions pose a major challenge for multilingual NLP because their meanings shift between figurative and literal usage, often requiring context for accurate interpretation. Prior work has focused on high-resource languages typically evaluates isolated idiom-meaning questions, overlooking realistic discourse. We introduce MIDI, a multilingual idiom dataset spanning 3 high-, 3 medium-, and 12 low-resource languages, curated by native speakers. Unlike previous datasets, MIDI provides idioms embedded in both sentence-level and conversational contexts, capturing both literal and figurative readings. Benchmarking state-of-the-art models shows that idiom comprehension degrades in low-resource languages and that, in all resource tiers, literal interpretations are substantially harder than figurative ones. Conversational context improves performance but does not eliminate these disparities. Through controlled tests and interventions on hidden representations, we further separate memorization from reasoning, exposing core limitations of current models.

</details>


### [156] [On the Salience of Low-Probability Tokens for AI-Generated Text Detection: A Multiscale Uncertainty Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02158)
*Yikai Guo,Bin Wang,Xilai Fan,Wenjun Ke,Haoran Luo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes an uncertainty-based, multiscale method to detect AI-generated text by focusing on low-probability tokens rather than overall likelihood, improving robustness and generalization over prior detectors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing statistical detectors of AI-generated text are attractive for being efficient and model-agnostic, but they fail in realistic settings. Two central issues are: (1) boilerplate dominance, where common tokens that both humans and LLMs use mask informative signals, and (2) brittleness from relying on a single probability score, which is easily perturbed or adversarially manipulated. The authors aim to build a detector that is less affected by boilerplate and more robust and stable, while generalizing across models and datasets.

Method: The authors introduce Uncertainty, a detector that quantifies multiscale uncertainty concentrated on low-probability tokens. Locally, it selects low-probability tokens in a text and averages their log-probabilities to dampen the influence of high-frequency boilerplate tokens that are uninformative. Globally, it characterizes the full distribution of these low-probability token probabilities using Rényi entropy to capture distributional shape instead of a single point estimate. They then propose Uncertainty++, which further stabilizes the uncertainty estimates via conditional independent sampling from the underlying language model, giving a more robust statistical characterization across samples.

Result: Across seven datasets and sixteen different LLMs, the proposed Uncertainty and Uncertainty++ methods achieve strong performance in detecting AI-generated text. The experiments show high detection accuracy, better generalization to unseen models and datasets, and improved robustness to attacks and manipulations compared with existing statistical detectors that rely on point likelihood or boilerplate-sensitive features.

Conclusion: Focusing on low-probability tokens and modeling their uncertainty at both local and global scales provides a more reliable signal for distinguishing AI-generated from human-written text than traditional point-likelihood methods. By mitigating boilerplate dominance and brittleness, Uncertainty and its enhanced variant Uncertainty++ offer an effective, generalizable, and robust framework for AI-generated text detection, validated across diverse datasets and language models.

Abstract: AI-generated text increasingly blends with human writing, raising practical risks such as misinformation, academic misuse, and corpora contamination. While statistical detectors are appealing for efficiency and generalization, they suffer from two key limitations. (i) Boilerplate dominance, boilerplate tokens shared across human and LLM writing can overwhelm discriminative signals. (ii) Brittle point estimates, relying on a single probability score yields unstable decisions under adversarial manipulations. To address these issues, we propose Uncertainty, a multiscale uncertainty estimator that focuses on informative low-probability tokens, which more clearly expose distributional discrepancies. Locally, it alleviates boilerplate dominance by averaging the log-probabilities of low-probability tokens; globally, it reduces brittleness by capturing the distributional shape of this low-probability region via Rényi entropy. We further extend the detector to Uncertainty++ via conditional independent sampling, yielding a more stable uncertainty estimation. Experiments across seven datasets and sixteen LLMs demonstrate high effectiveness, generalization, and robustness. Our code is available at https://github.com/guoyikai2000/Uncertainty-AIGT.

</details>


### [157] [CRAFTQA: A Code-Driven Adaptive Framework for Complex Structured Data Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02170)
*Chengtao Gan,Zhiqiang Liu,Long Jin,Yushan Zhu,Lei Liang,Wen Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces CRAFTQA, a unified framework for question answering over heterogeneous structured data that uses adaptive Python code generation to support complex reasoning beyond predefined functions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing unified structured data QA systems operate over multiple data formats (tables, knowledge graphs, etc.) but depend on a fixed library of predefined functions. This limits their reasoning expressiveness and adaptability, especially for complex, compositional, or previously unseen operations. There is a need for a more flexible approach that can dynamically create and execute new reasoning procedures over heterogeneous structured data.

Method: The authors propose CRAFTQA, which consists of two main modules. (1) CodeSTEP: a paradigm that generates an end-to-end executable Python code sequence representing stepwise reasoning operations conditioned on the natural language question. This replaces rigid function pipelines with general-purpose code generation. (2) CRAFT: a complementary module that, when the required operation is not covered by existing functions, dynamically synthesizes custom Python functions and integrates them into the CodeSTEP program. This combination yields an adaptive, code-driven reasoning process that can operate over multiple types of structured data in a unified way.

Result: Through experiments on several structured QA benchmarks that include different data forms, CRAFTQA is shown to substantially outperform prior unified QA methods, particularly on tasks requiring complex and flexible reasoning beyond predefined operators. The empirical evidence highlights gains in accuracy on complex reasoning subsets and demonstrates the effectiveness of the adaptive code generation strategy.

Conclusion: CRAFTQA overcomes the inherent limitations of predefined-function-based unified structured QA by using adaptive Python program generation and dynamic function synthesis. This design enables more expressive and flexible reasoning over heterogeneous structured data, leading to strong performance improvements, especially on complex reasoning questions, and suggests that code-driven approaches are a promising direction for future unified structured QA systems.

Abstract: Real-world scenarios involve massive heterogeneous structured data (e.g., tables, knowledge graphs), making effective reasoning over such diverse data increasingly important. Unified structured data question answering has emerged as a prominent research trend, aiming to answer natural language questions across different structured data types within a single framework. However, existing unified methods share a common limitation: they rely on a set of predefined functions, which restricts their ability to perform complex reasoning beyond these predefined operations. To overcome this fundamental limitation, we propose CRAFTQA, a novel adaptive code-driven framework comprising two core modules, CodeSTEP and CRAFT. The CodeSTEP module is a paradigm that generates a complete executable Python code sequence, which contains step-by-step code-based reasoning operations based on the question. The CRAFT module dynamically generates custom code functions for operations beyond the predefined function set, and seamlessly integrates with CodeSTEP to significantly enhance flexibility in handling complex reasoning. Comprehensive experiments on multiple structured datasets demonstrate that CRAFTQA achieves remarkable improvements in complex reasoning scenarios compared to existing unified methods.

</details>


### [158] [Cross-Environment Neural Reranking for Sample-Efficient Action Selection in Text-Based Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02204)
*Kan Shao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They study whether a single compact reranker model can choose actions for LLM agents across several diverse environments, showing that carefully rebalanced joint training over three benchmarks approaches single-environment performance and yields efficient cross-domain transfer, though advanced adapter routing remains unstable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based agents perform well in interactive text environments but are too expensive to run at scale, so people use smaller neural rerankers per environment; however, maintaining separate models for every environment is costly and inflexible, motivating a single lightweight model that can generalize and be shared across tasks and domains.

Method: They jointly train DeBERTa-v3 models (184M–434M parameters) as action-selection rerankers across ALFWorld, WebShop, and ScienceWorld, using minority-class upsampling and clean train splits; they compare single- vs multi-environment training, analyze scaling of model size, study sample-efficient cross-environment adaptation by fine-tuning on limited target-domain data, and explore environment-aware routing via LoRA adapters with PCGrad for multi-task optimization; they evaluate across multiple random seeds and report combined net gains and per-environment performance.

Result: Two-environment joint training significantly improves ALFWorld performance while keeping WebShop competitive compared to single-environment baselines; three-environment joint training achieves a mean combined net gain of +0.551 ± 0.024, with per-environment results close to specialized models and evidence of positive transfer; cross-domain fine-tuning is very sample-efficient—using only 9.2% of target-domain data recovers 93% of full-data performance; increasing model size gives limited additional benefit, suggesting data diversity matters more; environment-aware LoRA routing with PCGrad can reach a best-seed combined gain of +0.611 but has high variance across seeds.

Conclusion: A single compact DeBERTa-based reranker can effectively support action selection for LLM agents across multiple diverse environments when trained with clean splits and data rebalancing, delivering near-specialized performance, strong and sample-efficient cross-domain transfer, and only modest gains from scaling model size; more sophisticated multi-task techniques like environment-aware LoRA routing are promising but currently unstable, and they release a new three-environment benchmark and model checkpoints to support further research.

Abstract: Large language model agents achieve strong performance on text-based benchmarks but incur prohibitive inference costs, motivating the use of compact neural rerankers for action selection. We investigate whether a single lightweight model can perform action selection across multiple diverse environments, a capability that would eliminate per-environment model maintenance. Training DeBERTa-v3 (184M-434M parameters) jointly on ALFWorld, WebShop, and ScienceWorld with minority-class upsampling, we find that rebalanced two-environment joint training substantially improves over single-environment ALFWorld performance (net gain +0.412) while maintaining competitive WebShop performance (+0.214 vs. +0.249 single-environment). Three-environment training yields a mean combined net gain of +0.551 +/- 0.024 across 4 seeds, with per-environment results approaching specialized single-environment models while providing positive cross-domain transfer. Cross-environment adaptation is highly sample-efficient: fine-tuning on only 9.2% of target-domain data recovers 93% of full-data performance, and scaling model capacity yields limited benefits, indicating data diversity is the primary driver. Environment-aware LoRA adapter routing with PCGrad achieves a best-seed result of +0.611 (seed 42), with seeds 456 and 789 at +0.554 and +0.559, but exhibits high variance due to seed 123 collapsing to +0.263 (4-seed mean +0.497 +/- 0.158), representing a promising but currently unstable direction. Joint training with clean splits and data rebalancing is a key ingredient. We will release our three-environment benchmark of 51,580 training instances (41,740 raw unique states with minority-class upsampling) and all model checkpoints upon acceptance.

</details>


### [159] [Consistency Training while Mitigating Obfuscation via Rate Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02211)
*Sohaib Imran,Prakhar Gupta,Jannes Elstner,David Demitri Africa*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Rate Matching Consistency Training (RMCT), a method that reduces large language models’ reliance on extraneous bias cues while preserving their explicit mention, thus improving robustness without sacrificing monitorability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing consistency-training methods for large language models try to make model outputs similar across inputs with and without extraneous features (like user-preferred-answer cues). However, these methods operate on whole responses or internal activations, which unintentionally teaches models to hide or stop verbalizing those cues while still being influenced by them. This leads to “obfuscation,” undermining our ability to monitor and audit models’ biases. The authors want a training method that reduces harmful behavioral influence of such cues while maintaining transparency about when they are present and being referenced.

Method: The authors propose Rate Matching Consistency Training (RMCT). Instead of enforcing similarity of complete responses or representations across perturbed inputs, RMCT focuses on specific behavioral properties. It trains the model so that the *rate* (frequency) of a target behavior—such as following a bias cue—is matched across different input conditions (e.g., original vs. perturbed prompts). RMCT does not require perfectly paired inputs with and without the extraneous feature and does not constrain the exact wording or expression of the behavior. This allows applying consistency training even when extraneous features cannot be easily removed. The method is evaluated on sycophancy reduction in open-weight language models.

Result: On sycophancy reduction tasks with two open-weight language models, RMCT reduces bias-following to a degree comparable with a standard consistency-training baseline on unseen (held-out) bias types. Importantly, it does this while largely preserving the model’s tendency to explicitly mention or verbalize the bias cues, thereby avoiding the obfuscation seen with standard methods. The authors also find that RMCT is more data-efficient—needing fewer training examples—but less compute-efficient than the baseline under their experimental setup.

Conclusion: RMCT demonstrates that consistency training can be targeted at specific behavioral properties to improve robustness to extraneous cues without forcing models to hide or suppress explicit mentions of those cues. This reduces obfuscation and supports better monitorability and auditing of model behavior. The approach extends consistency training to scenarios where extraneous features are not easily removable and offers a practical trade-off: improved data efficiency at some cost in compute efficiency.

Abstract: Large language models are often influenced by extraneous input features, such as cues revealing a user's preferred answer. Consistency training reduces this influence by training models to behave similarly across inputs with and without the extraneous feature. However, existing methods train for consistency over entire responses or internal activations, which also constrains whether the model verbalises said extraneous features. We show this leads to obfuscation, where the model learns not to mention a cue while remaining influenced by it, which may undermine monitorability. To address this, we introduce Rate Matching Consistency Training (RMCT), which trains for consistency over selected behavioural properties without constraining how this behaviour is expressed. RMCT matches the rate at which the model exhibits a target behaviour (e.g., following a bias cue) across input perturbations, rather than requiring paired inputs with and without the extraneous feature, extending consistency training to settings where the extraneous features cannot be removed. We evaluate RMCT on sycophancy reduction in two open-weight language models, achieving reductions in bias-following comparable to a standard consistency-training baseline on held-out bias types, while largely preserving the model's tendency to verbalise the bias cue. Further, we find that RMCT is more data-efficient at the expense of being less compute-efficient in our experiments. Overall, RMCT shows that consistency training can improve behavioural robustness without directly trading off against monitorability.

</details>


### [160] [Do Gender Cues Affect LLM Value Trade-offs? Evidence from a Controlled Decision Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02214)
*Yangyang Liu,Dong Yu,Pengyuan Liu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a benchmark to test whether large language models change their value-based decisions when only the genders of people in the scenario are altered.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used in contexts where decisions should not depend on demographic attributes, it is important to understand whether explicit gender cues improperly influence their value judgments and whether models can accurately self-report such influence.

Method: The authors build the Realistic Value Decision Benchmark (RVDB), a set of scenarios where only role-gender assignments are varied while all other elements (scenario text, value pairs, candidate decisions, Value Distance, and Decision Severity) are held constant. They evaluate seven LLMs using a position-balanced design, measure how often gender perturbations cause decision changes, and use self-attribution prompts to ask models whether gender influenced their choices. They also analyze cross-gender role swaps and examine where in the value space gender effects are concentrated.

Result: Across seven models, explicit gender cues cause limited but systematic decision flips when genders are perturbed. A notable asymmetry appears when the proposed decision comes from a female role. Models’ self-attributions frequently deny or misattribute gender influence even when behavior changes. Gender effects are concentrated near less determinate value boundaries and in more severe decision contexts. Overall value rankings stay mostly stable, but specific value trade-offs shift across different role-gender configurations.

Conclusion: Gender information can systematically affect LLM value-based decisions in subtle, context-dependent ways, even when models claim gender had no influence. Because such effects emerge behaviorally but are obscured in self-explanations, the authors argue that controlled behavioral audits like RVDB are necessary complements to explanation-based evaluations when assessing fairness and robustness of LLM value reasoning.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly used in value-sensitive decision settings, where irrelevant demographic cues should not alter judgments. We construct the Realistic Value Decision Benchmark (RVDB), a controlled benchmark that varies only the role-gender configuration while holding the scenario, ordered value pair, roles, candidate decisions, Value Distance, and Decision Severity fixed. Using a position-balanced evaluation across seven models, we test whether models preserve decision invariance under gender perturbations and whether their self-attributions reflect observed behavioral changes. We find that explicit gender cues induce bounded but systematic decision flips, including under an explicit gender-attribution prompt that asks models to report whether gender influenced their choice. Cross-gender role swaps reveal a consistent female-proposed-decision asymmetry, while models often attribute flipped decisions to No Influence or other non-gender factors. Further analysis shows that gender effects concentrate near less determinate value boundaries and under more severe decision contexts, suggesting that gender cues act as local boundary-shifting factors rather than global overrides of value reasoning. Value rankings remain largely stable, but ordered value-pair trade-offs shift unevenly across role-gender configurations. These results show that gender can enter LLM value trade-offs behaviorally while remaining obscured in self-attribution, motivating controlled behavioral audits beyond explanation-based evaluation.

</details>


### [161] [Better with Experience: Self-Evolving LLM Agents for Evidence-Grounded Health Community Notes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02215)
*Zihang Fu,Fanxiao Li,Jianyang Gu,Haonan Wang,Preslav Nakov,Bryan Hooi,Min-Yen Kan,Jiaying Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents EvoNote, an LLM-based, self-improving system for writing health Community Notes that outperforms human-written notes and works much faster.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-augmented Community Notes handle each misinformation post independently and do not reuse past correction experience, leading to inefficiency and potentially inconsistent quality. Health misinformation on social platforms requires scalable, timely, and evidence-grounded corrections, motivating a system that can accumulate and reuse correction knowledge over time.

Method: The authors propose EvoNote, an agentic framework with an evolving experience memory. EvoNote decomposes the note-generation process into steps—claim analysis, evidence acquisition, and note writing—and performs fine-grained credit assignment from trajectory-level feedback (e.g., note helpfulness) down to action-level behaviors. This feedback is distilled into an editable memory that guides future notes. They construct MM-HealthCN, a 1.2K-instance multimodal dataset of user-flagged health posts, associated human Community Notes, and crowd helpfulness labels, and use a human-validated hierarchical LLM judge to compare EvoNote’s notes against human ones.

Result: On MM-HealthCN, EvoNote’s generated notes are preferred over human-written Community Notes in 89.6% of evaluated cases. On a separate subset of posts that lacked enough ratings to determine crowd helpfulness, EvoNote generated notes that were judged helpful in 82.0% of cases. EvoNote also reduces the median time to produce a candidate correction from more than 13 hours in the human pipeline to under 2 minutes, demonstrating strong gains in both quality and efficiency.

Conclusion: EvoNote’s self-evolving, memory-based approach leads to higher-quality, faster health Community Notes than the existing human pipeline. The key driver is improved evidence use and reusable correction strategies captured in its experience memory. The framework suggests that agentic, self-improving note generation can be an effective component of health misinformation governance on social platforms.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM)-augmented Community Notes offer a scalable path for timely, evidence-grounded correction of health misinformation on social platforms. However, they still reset at every post, leaving useful correction experience from prior cases unused. We introduce EvoNote, an agentic framework that enables health Community Notes generation to self-evolve through an evolving experience memory of prior misinformation correction episodes. Its core is fine-grained credit assignment: EvoNote grounds trajectory-level feedback in health-specific note qualities and distills it into action-level memory for claim analysis, evidence acquisition, and note writing. We evaluate EvoNote on MM-HealthCN, a 1.2K-instance multimodal benchmark of user-flagged health posts with human-written Community Notes and crowd-derived helpfulness labels.
