Under a human-validated hierarchical utility judge, EvoNote-generated notes are preferred over corresponding human-written notes in 89.6% of cases; on a separate set of Needs More Ratings posts without a crowd helpfulness verdict, EvoNote produces helpful notes for 82.0% of cases. It also reduces the median time needed to produce a candidate correction from over 13 hours in the human-note pipeline to under 2 minutes. Analyses link these gains to stronger evidence use and reusable correction strategies, positioning self-evolving note generation as a promising paradigm for health misinformation governance.

</details>


### [162] [When Knowledge Is Not Free: Cost-Aware Evidence Selection in Retrieval-Augmented Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02245)
*Mingyan Wu,Han Yang,Omer Ben-Porat,Yftah Ziser*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a new RAG setting where accessing external documents has explicit costs, and systems must operate under a budget. It shows that traditional static retrieval strategies fail in this setting and explores LLM-based agents that dynamically decide when and what to retrieve, highlighting cost-aware evidence acquisition as a key challenge for future RAG systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RAG methods implicitly assume that external knowledge is free to access, but in practice, many valuable information sources are paywalled, licensed, or otherwise costly. This mismatch makes standard RAG unrealistic for real-world deployment where retrieval has monetary, latency, or access-friction costs. The authors aim to formalize and study this more realistic, budget-constrained scenario so that RAG systems can make principled trade-offs between retrieval cost and answer quality.

Method: The authors define a cost-aware RAG framework in which each piece of retrievable evidence is assigned to an access-cost tier. Systems must produce answers subject to an evidence-access budget, deciding which tiers to use and how much to spend. They instantiate this by augmenting the MS MARCO v2.1 corpus with multiple levels of access friction (cost tiers). They then: (1) evaluate various static (fixed) retrieval and evidence-selection strategies under different budgets on both general and domain-specific QA benchmarks; and (2) propose and analyze agentic cost-aware RAG, where an LLM incrementally decides whether to retrieve, what tier to query, and when to stop, effectively acting as a controller over evidence acquisition within the budget.

Result: Empirical results show that no single static retrieval strategy works best across all budgets and tasks: performance is brittle, and simply increasing the retrieval budget does not reliably improve answer quality. Even when higher-cost evidence is more domain-matched, it does not guarantee better answers. In contrast, LLM-based agents that adaptively choose when and what to retrieve demonstrate strong potential, often using the budget more effectively. However, their behavior is highly dependent on the particular LLM and task, indicating instability and lack of robustness.

Conclusion: Cost is a critical but underexplored dimension in RAG. Treating external knowledge as free leads to brittle and suboptimal systems once realistic access costs are considered. Static, one-size-fits-all retrieval policies are inadequate under budget constraints, whereas agentic, adaptive controllers show promise but require further research to become reliable and generalizable. The authors conclude that designing cost-aware evidence acquisition policies should be a central focus for the next generation of RAG systems and release their code and data to facilitate future work.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) typically assumes that external knowledge is free, but many high-quality sources are paywalled, licensed, restricted, or otherwise costly to access. We introduce cost-aware RAG, a setting where retrieved evidence is assigned access-cost tiers and systems must answer under an explicit evidence-access budget. We instantiate this setting by augmenting MS MARCO v2.1 with access-friction tiers and evaluate budgeted evidence selection across general-domain and domain-specific QA benchmarks. Our results show that static selection is brittle: no fixed selector uniformly dominates, and larger budgets do not reliably improve answer quality, even when costly evidence is domain-matched. We then study agentic cost-aware RAG, where an LLM decides when to retrieve, which tier to access, and when to stop. Agents show strong promise as adaptive evidence-acquisition controllers, but their behavior remains highly model- and task-dependent. These findings suggest that cost-aware evidence acquisition is a central challenge for the next generation of RAG systems. All code and data are available at https://github.com/Mignonmy/Cost-Aware.

</details>


### [163] [Geometric Latent Reasoning Induces Shorter Generations in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02248)
*Shashi Kumar,Yacouba Kaloga,Petr Motlicek,Ina Kodrasi,Andrea Cavallaro*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes Geometric Latent Reasoning (GLR), a method that replaces parts of chain-of-thought token reasoning with continuous geometric trajectories in embedding space, reducing reasoning length while maintaining accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current large language models perform complex reasoning via long, discrete chains of natural-language tokens, which are computationally expensive, sensitive to length limits, and restricted to symbolic text. Latent (continuous) reasoning is a promising alternative, but it is unclear how to design useful intermediate latent structures. The authors aim to construct such structures in a principled way and reduce token-level reasoning cost without sacrificing performance.

Method: They cast latent reasoning as a geometric path-approximation task in the model’s pretrained token-embedding space. The approach, Geometric Latent Reasoning (GLR), adds a lightweight transition head that iteratively predicts direction updates in embedding space, forming a continuous trajectory. Using textual chain-of-thought traces as supervision, GLR learns to approximate discrete reasoning paths while allowing deviations from exact token embeddings. During inference, parts of the chain-of-thought are executed as latent geometric steps instead of explicit tokens.

Result: On mathematical reasoning benchmarks with Qwen3 models, GLR exhibits an emergent behavior: it notably shortens total generation length without any explicit objective to minimize length. By substituting early portions of explicit chain-of-thought with latent steps, models often still reach correct answers but with many fewer generated tokens/steps. This demonstrates that geometric latent trajectories can serve as effective intermediate reasoning states.

Conclusion: Continuous geometric trajectories in embedding space can approximate and partially replace explicit chain-of-thought, providing compact intermediate reasoning states. This enables a new tradeoff among latent computation budget, output length, and accuracy: models can often maintain strong reasoning performance while reducing explicit token generation by using GLR. The work suggests a promising direction for more efficient and flexible latent reasoning mechanisms in large language models.

Abstract: Large language models solve complex problems by generating lengthy chains of explicit reasoning tokens. While effective, this makes reasoning expensive, length-sensitive, and constrained to (discrete) natural language. While latent reasoning offers a continuous alternative, determining useful structures for intermediate latent states is an open challenge. In this paper, we formulate latent reasoning as a geometric path-approximation problem within the model's pretrained token-embedding space. We introduce Geometric Latent Reasoning (GLR), which uses a lightweight transition head to predict iterative direction updates in embedding space. Using textual chain-of-thought traces as anchors, GLR learns to approximate discrete reasoning trajectories while permitting continuous deviations from exact token embeddings. Evaluations on mathematical reasoning benchmarks using Qwen3 models reveal an emergent phenomenon: geometric latent reasoning induces substantially shorter generations without an explicit length objective. By replacing early explicit reasoning with continuous latent steps, models often reach correct answers using substantially fewer total generation steps. These findings suggest that continuous trajectories act as compact intermediate reasoning states, exposing a new tradeoff between latent computation budget, output length, and accuracy.

</details>


### [164] [ResMerge: Residual-based Spectral Merging of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02252)
*Yandu Sun,Zhiyan Hou,Haokai Ma,Yuheng Jia,Junfeng Fang,Haiyun Guo,Hongyan An,weizhen wang,Jinqiao Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces ResMerge, a residual-based spectral merging framework that more reliably combines reinforcement learning expert models without further training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Model merging aims to combine multiple expert models without additional training, but existing spectral approaches assume that high-energy singular directions carry the main task signal and that low-energy residual components are mostly noise or interference. For RL experts, this assumption breaks down: both leading and residual spectral components encode substantial behavioral knowledge, yet they interact differently when merged. There is a need for a merging method that respects these properties and better preserves the capabilities of RL experts.

Method: The authors analyze RL task vectors via spectral decomposition into a leading head (dominant singular directions) and a residual component. They empirically show that both parts independently encode rich behavior but exhibit different merging properties: the head is sharp and conflict-prone, while the residual is more diffuse and stable. Building on this, they propose ResMerge, which (1) constructs a stable residual backbone using Spherical Residual Consensus Adaptation—computing a reliability-weighted consensus direction on the Frobenius sphere across experts’ residuals—and then (2) applies a Lightweight Head Correction module that selectively reintroduces leading-head information only where there is positive cross-expert agreement. This two-stage design aims to leverage the stability of residuals while carefully adding high-signal head components.

Result: Across multiple groups of RL experts and capability domains, ResMerge achieves better preservation of expert behaviors compared to task-vector-based and spectral-merging baselines. The experiments indicate that residual-based aggregation yields more robust merged policies, and the gated head correction further improves performance without reintroducing large conflicts. The framework is validated empirically and released as open-source code.

Conclusion: The work challenges the common assumption that only leading spectral components are crucial for task knowledge in RL experts and shows that residual components are both informative and advantageous for stable model merging. By building a residual consensus backbone and cautiously adding aligned head information, ResMerge substantially improves zero-shot merging of RL experts. This suggests that future model merging techniques, especially in RL, should explicitly account for both leading and residual spectral structures rather than aggressively compressing or discarding residuals.

Abstract: Model merging offers a training-free way to combine multiple post-trained expert models, but merging experts obtained through reinforcement learning (RL) remains challenging. Existing spectral merging methods often assume that leading singular directions contain the main task signal, while lower-energy residual components can be compressed, selected, or attenuated to reduce interference. We find that this assumption does not hold for RL task vectors: after decomposing each task vector into a leading spectral head and a residual component, both parts can independently recover substantial behavior knowledge, while exhibiting different merging properties. The head is highly concentrated and informative but more prone to sharp cross-expert conflicts, whereas the residual component is more dispersed and provides a more stable basis for aggregation. Based on this observation, we propose ResMerge, a residual-based spectral merging framework for RL experts. ResMerge first constructs a stable residual backbone with Spherical Residual Consensus Adaptation, which estimates a reliability-weighted consensus direction on the Frobenius sphere. It then reintroduces leading-head information through a Lightweight Head Correction module gated by positive cross-expert agreement. Experiments across multiple RL expert groups and capability domains show that ResMerge better preserves expert capabilities than representative task-vector and spectral merging baselines. The implementation of ResMerge is publicly available at https://github.com/sunyd0303-cpu/ResMerge-release.

</details>


### [165] [Who Annotates in NLP? A Large-scale Assessment of Human Annotation Reporting between 2018 and 2025](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02255)
*Maria Kunilovskaya,Gagan Bhatia,Lisa Sophie Albertelli,Yanran Chen,Christian Greisinger,Lotta Kiefer,Christoph Leiter,Subhadeep Roy,Tewodros Achamaleh,Muhammad Arslan Manzoor,Sebastian Pohl,Yufang Hou,Steffen Eger*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper audits how NLP papers report human annotation procedures, introduces a taxonomy of reporting practices, and builds an LLM-assisted dataset showing that key validity-related details are often missing despite some improvement over time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human annotation underpins NLP datasets and evaluations, yet existing papers rarely document who annotators are and how annotation was conducted, limiting reliability, reproducibility, and interpretability. The field lacks a systematic, large-scale analysis of current reporting practices and tools to monitor and improve them.

Method: The authors: (1) design a unified taxonomy of annotation-reporting dimensions; (2) create Annotated-gold, a human-adjudicated gold standard of 41 papers and 72 annotation tasks; (3) build and validate an LLM-based information extraction pipeline against this gold standard, achieving human-comparable Krippendorff’s alpha; and (4) apply this pipeline to ACL-venue papers from 2018–2025, extracting 2,667 annotation tasks from 1,603 papers to quantify which details are reported and how patterns vary across time, venue, topic, and use case.

Result: The best LLM extractor reaches Krippendorff’s alpha 0.606 with the adjudicated labels, close to human-human agreement of 0.585, enabling scalable auditing. Analysis of 2,667 tasks shows that papers commonly report operational details like recruitment methods, annotator expertise, and annotation volume, but frequently omit crucial validity-related information such as training procedures, language proficiency, compensation, socio-demographics, adjudication protocols, and inter-annotator agreement metrics, particularly for model-evaluation tasks. Reporting quality has improved over time but remains inconsistent and incomplete.

Conclusion: Annotation reporting in NLP is trending better but is still uneven and often lacks core validity-related details, limiting confidence in empirical findings. The paper offers a scalable LLM-based framework and a taxonomy to monitor practices, plus minimal reporting recommendations intended to make human annotation more reliable, reproducible, and interpretable for future NLP research.

Abstract: Human annotation is the empirical foundation of much NLP research, from dataset construction to model evaluation, but papers often leave unclear who produced the annotations and how the annotation process was controlled. We provide the first large-scale, task-level audit of human annotation reporting across major NLP venues, asking which annotation details are documented, which are missing, and how reporting varies across time, topic, venue, and intended use of human judgment. We introduce a unified taxonomy of annotation-reporting practices and validate an LLM-assisted extraction pipeline against Annotated-gold, a human-adjudicated gold standard of 41 papers and 72 annotation tasks, where the best model reaches human-comparable agreement with adjudicated labels, with Krippendorff's alpha of 0.606 versus 0.585 for human-human agreement. Using this pipeline, we construct Annotated-llm, a dataset covering ACL-venue papers from 2018-2025, with 2,667 extracted annotation tasks from 1,603 papers, and find that papers frequently report operational details such as recruitment strategies, annotator expertise, and annotation volume, but often omit details needed to assess annotation validity, including training, language proficiency, compensation, socio-demographics, adjudication, and agreement values, especially in model-evaluation studies. Our results show that annotation reporting in NLP has improved over time but remains uneven, and they establish a scalable framework and bare-minimum reporting recommendations for making human annotation more reliable, reproducible, and interpretable.

</details>


### [166] [DECK: A Consistency x Confidence Taxonomy of LLM Hallucinations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02289)
*Mohit Singh Chauhan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces DECK, a new taxonomy that classifies LLM hallucination errors by how detectable they are to different uncertainty-scoring methods, rather than by what is factually wrong, and empirically validates it across models and datasets while identifying a universal blind spot for current output-level uncertainty quantification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing hallucination taxonomies describe types of LLM mistakes (e.g., memorized errors, reasoning failures, fluent fabrications) but do not explain which uncertainty-scoring methods could have detected each error. Practitioners need guidance on when specific uncertainty quantification (UQ) tools will or will not raise a signal for a given kind of hallucination. There is also a practical concern that different UQ methods disagree and may share blind spots, especially on confident, consistent hallucinations, but this is not systematically mapped out.

Method: The authors define DECK, a 2×2 taxonomy based on two axes: (1) inter-sample consistency (how repeatable the model’s output is across samples) and (2) token-level confidence (how high the model’s own probabilities are). This yields four cells: Drift, Entrenched, Confabulation, Knotted, each linked to particular uncertainty-scorer families. They operationalize cell assignment via Youden’s J-based thresholds on those axes. They then empirically study three LLMs on four datasets, comparing different uncertainty scorers (black-box consistency scorers, white-box token-probability scorers, and LLM-as-a-judge) via scorer-pair disagreement patterns and alignment with external labels from specialized benchmarks (SelfAware, HaluEval, PopQA). They also test a linear probe on Llama-3-8B’s hidden states to see if internal activations can detect certain blind-spot errors.

Result: The DECK taxonomy cleanly separates regimes where different UQ families have signal: black-box consistency methods detect Drift and Confabulation; token-probability methods detect Knotted and Confabulation; and only an independently trained LLM-as-a-judge can detect Entrenched errors. Empirical analysis across models and datasets shows that scorer disagreements and benchmark-derived labels fall into the predicted DECK cells, with some refinements driven by model scale and content type. They further show a universal blind spot: for knowledge-gap inputs that elicit confident, repeatable hallucinations, all output-level UQ families fail to flag the error. A linear probe on hidden states also performs at chance on these cases, suggesting that this blind spot may extend into simple activation-level diagnostics.

Conclusion: DECK provides a complementary way to categorize hallucinations by their detectability signature, directly tying error types to the uncertainty-scoring families that can catch them. This helps explain and predict when particular UQ methods will succeed or fail. The empirical validation indicates that this taxonomy is meaningful across different models and datasets, and exposes a serious universal blind spot: when models hallucinate confidently and consistently due to knowledge gaps, standard output-level UQ (and even a simple linear probe on activations) break down. This motivates the exploration of richer internal-state UQ techniques and more robust detection strategies beyond current output-level methods.

Abstract: Existing hallucination taxonomies classify LLM errors by what is wrong with the output -- memorised misconceptions, reasoning failures, fluent fabrications. These taxonomies are useful for diagnosis but cannot answer a different question: which uncertainty scorer would have caught this error? We propose a complementary taxonomy that classifies errors by their detectability signature -- the signal a scorer family would read.
  The DECK taxonomy is a 2x2 partition along inter-sample consistency and token-level confidence into four behavioural regimes (Drift, Entrenched, Confabulation, Knotted), each mapping to a specific scorer family (or families) that can detect it: black-box consistency scorers have signal in D and C, white-box token-probability scorers have signal in K and C, and only an LLM-as-a-Judge with independent pretraining can detect E. Cell membership is operationalised by a Youden's J optimal split on each scorer axis.
  Across three models and four datasets we validate the taxonomy two ways: by analysing scorer-pair disagreement, and by checking that external labels (SelfAware unanswerable, HaluEval adversarial, PopQA entity popularity) land in the predicted DECK cells, with model-scale and content-specific secondary-cell refinements.
  We further identify a universal blind spot of output-level UQ: on knowledge-gap inputs where the generator emits confident, repeatable fabrications, every output-level family collapses by construction. A linear probe on Llama-3-8B's hidden states also collapses to chance, giving preliminary evidence that the failure may persist at the activation level; richer internal-state methods (UQ heads, information-theoretic estimators) remain to be tested.

</details>


### [167] [AI as a Tool for Simulation-Based Experiments in Literary Studies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02293)
*Matthew Wilkens*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper explores how generative AI can be used for large-scale simulations of literary production and presents initial experiments showing AI models can partially mimic the stylistic distribution of high-status novels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Literary scholars lack scalable, controlled ways to experiment with cultural and literary history. Generative AI offers a potential laboratory for low-cost, repeatable simulations of literary production, but it is unclear whether current models can reliably generate long-form, stylistically constrained texts that resemble historically situated literature. The paper aims to map what is already possible and identify what is needed to make serious literary-historical simulation feasible.

Method: The authors survey and synthesize work in several areas: (1) using AI models as stand-ins for human populations in simulations; (2) analyzing narrative and stylistic features of AI-generated texts; (3) studying the stability and coherence of multiagent, multiturn simulations; and (4) techniques for steering and modifying model knowledge and behavior. They then conduct experiments generating literary texts with state-of-the-art models under specified constraints and compare these outputs to a corpus of high-status, human-authored novels, evaluating whether the AI outputs fall within the empirical distribution of features found in that corpus.

Result: The literature review shows that building blocks for literary-historical simulation already exist across several research domains, though they have not yet been integrated. Empirically, the authors find that modern generative models can produce literary texts whose measurable properties (e.g., style or other textual features) in some respects fall within the statistical range of canonical novels, demonstrating the first examples of limited in-distribution AI outputs in this domain. However, performance is still constrained: models cannot yet reliably produce full-length, high-quality novels under arbitrary cultural and stylistic constraints.

Conclusion: Generative AI is not yet capable of fully realistic, controllable simulation of literary history or of producing consistently high-quality, culturally grounded novels, but current capabilities already enable meaningful, if limited, experimental work. The combination of existing methods for proxy modeling of human populations, text analysis, multiagent simulation, and model steering could underpin future, more ambitious counterfactual simulations of literary systems. The authors outline future research aimed at integrating these components into full-scale, AI-based literary-historical laboratories.

Abstract: Generative artificial intelligence (AI) systems open new possibilities for experimentation in literary studies via controlled, grounded, large-scale, low-cost simulations of cultural production. Current systems have not yet been shown to produce high-quality, book-length narrative texts that reliably reflect arbitrarily specified cultural constraints or stylistic features. But there exists substantial relevant research on each of the components required for literary-historical simulation. These include the use and validation of AI systems as proxies for differentiable human populations; the narrative and stylistic properties of AI-generated texts; the stability and coherence of multiagent, multiturn AI simulations of human actors; and technical methods through which to alter in predictable ways the knowledge and behavior of generative systems. Together, these areas could provide a starting point for more ambitious AI-based modeling of cultural systems of literary production.
  We describe the possibilities and challenges of simulation-based experiments in literary studies, summarize the current state of the art in relevant fields, and explain key technical aspects of the work. To provide an example directly relevant to literary scholars, we present the results of experiments on literary text generation, including comparisons to high-status, human-authored novels. Our results include the first demonstration of (limited) in-distribution outputs by AI models in this domain. We conclude with a description of future work on full counterfactual literary-historical simulations using AI.

</details>


### [168] [Beyond Isolated Behaviors: Hierarchical User Modeling for LLM Personalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02300)
*Liang Wang,Xinyi Mou,Xiaoyou Liu,Tiannan Wang,Yuqing Wang,Zhongyu Wei*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces PHF, a sociologically grounded framework for personalizing LLMs by modeling user behavior in three hierarchical levels: practices, habitus, and fields, showing consistent gains on the LaMP benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While LLMs are powerful, effectively personalizing them for individual users is still difficult. Existing methods treat user behavior as a flat set of signals, lacking a structured representation of how behaviors accumulate and relate across users. The authors are motivated to build a principled, interpretable framework that can capture deeper behavioral organization to improve personalization, drawing on sociological theory to guide this structure.

Method: The authors adapt Bourdieu's Theory of Practice into a computational framework called PHF (Practice-Habitus-Field). They define: (1) practices as individual, observable user behaviors, (2) habitus as temporally aggregated, stable behavioral dispositions of a user, and (3) fields as shared regularities or patterns across similar users. They implement this with PHF_Compass, a lightweight, model-agnostic layer on top of a frozen LLM that encodes and exploits these three levels for personalization. The framework is evaluated on the LaMP benchmark across multiple personalization tasks and analyzed for interpretability and extensibility.

Result: On the LaMP benchmark, PHF_Compass achieves consistent improvements over baselines across diverse personalization tasks, indicating the effectiveness of the PHF framework. Additional analyses show that the learned behavioral structures (practices, habitus, and fields) are interpretable and that the approach can be extended to new settings, suggesting that the hierarchical, sociologically informed modeling yields both performance and transparency benefits.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that sociological theory, specifically Bourdieu's Practice-Habitus-Field framework, provides a useful lens for structuring user behavior in LLM personalization. By instantiating this theory in PHF_Compass over a frozen base model, the authors demonstrate improved personalization performance along with interpretable and extensible behavioral representations. This indicates that moving beyond flat behavior aggregation toward hierarchical, theory-informed structures is a promising direction for user-personalized LLMs.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities across diverse domains, yet personalizing their outputs to individual users remains an open challenge. Existing approaches predominantly adopt a flat behavioral paradigm, aggregating user behaviors without an explicit account of how they are organized into deeper behavioral structures. In this work, we draw on Pierre Bourdieu's Theory of Practice to propose PHF (Practice-Habitus-Field), a sociologically grounded framework that reconceptualizes LLM personalization through three hierarchical levels: individual behaviors as practices, their temporal accumulation into stable dispositions as habitus, and shared regularities across similar users as fields. We instantiate PHF through $\mathrm{PHF}_{\text{Compass}}$, a lightweight and model-agnostic implementation based on a frozen LLM. Experiments on the Language Model Personalization (LaMP) benchmark demonstrate consistent improvements across diverse tasks, while further analyses validate the interpretability and extensibility of the learned behavioral structures.

</details>


### [169] [Unified Context Evolution for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02304)
*Zixuan Zhu,Yitong Hu,Yong Dai,Junfeng Fang,Chunyang Jiang,Senkang Hu,Yuzhi Zhao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes Unified Context Evolution (UCE), a framework that turns LLM agent experience into an evolving, typed context library that improves multi-step interactive task performance and transfers across models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based agents can perform multi-step tasks with environment feedback, but they typically start each episode from scratch and do not retain or structure the useful strategies they discover. Existing learning approaches either overfit to a specific task or store all experience in a single, untyped pool that does not distinguish between kinds of knowledge, track their effectiveness over time, or actively address gaps in the agent's capabilities. There is a need for a systematic way to externalize, organize, and evolve agent experience so that it can be reused, evaluated, and balanced across different knowledge types.

Method: The paper introduces Unified Context Evolution (UCE), a gradient-free learning framework that externalizes an agent's interaction histories into a persistent library of typed Evolvable Context Units (ECUs). Experience is decomposed into four types: Memory, Strategy, Workflow, and Skill. Each type is generated from task trajectories under specific rules, retrieved at decision time to condition the LLM, and repeatedly evaluated during use: outcomes provide scores that reflect usefulness. UCE periodically prunes low-value ECUs and uses a scheduling module to decide how to allocate a fixed generation budget each cycle toward the ECU types where the library is currently weakest, thereby continuously refining and balancing the library.

Result: On two interactive benchmarks, UCE substantially improves performance compared to a baseline agent without this evolving context library. In ALFWorld, success rate increases from 75.4% to 96.3%; in WebShop, task score rises from 45.1% to 61.3%. The learned ECU library is also shown to be reusable: it can be transferred to different LLM actor backbones without retraining, indicating that the stored experience is model-agnostic to some degree.

Conclusion: The study concludes that structuring agent experience into an evolving, typed context library enables effective, gradient-free continual improvement of LLM-based agents on interactive tasks. By separating experience into Memory, Strategy, Workflow, and Skill units, tracking their performance through usage, pruning ineffective items, and adaptively investing generation budget in weaker areas, UCE significantly boosts task performance and generalizes across different underlying LLMs. This suggests that careful design of external memory and experience evolution can be a powerful alternative or complement to gradient-based fine-tuning for interactive LLM agents.

Abstract: LLM-based agents can solve multi-step interactive tasks by combining reasoning with environment feedback, yet each episode starts from the same fixed context and any useful strategy discovered along the way is lost once the task ends. Existing approaches either limit learning to the current task or pool all experience into a single untyped store, without distinguishing knowledge types, tracking quality through use, or balancing what the library still lacks. We introduce Unified Context Evolution (UCE), a gradient-free framework that externalizes agent experience into an evolving library of typed Evolvable Context Units (ECUs). UCE decomposes experience into four complementary types (Memory, Strategy, Workflow, and Skill), each generated from trajectories under type-specific conditions, retrieved at decision time, scored through repeated usage outcomes, and pruned when no longer valuable. A scheduling module allocates each cycle's generation budget toward the types where the library is weakest. Across two interactive benchmarks, UCE raises ALFWorld success from 75.4% to 96.3% and WebShop task score from 45.1% to 61.3%, and the accumulated library transfers to alternative actor backbones without retraining.

</details>


### [170] [TVIR: Building Deep Research Agents Towards Text--Visual Interleaved Report Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02320)
*Xinkai Ma,Zhiqi Bai,Dingling Zhang,Pei Liu,Yishuo Yuan,He Zhu,Jiakai Wang,Qianqian Xie,Yifan Zhao,Xinlong Yang,Hao Cong,Zhiheng Yao,Fengxia Xie,Zihao Xu,Haoran Xu,Zhaohui Wang,Minghao Liu,Shirong Lin,Yingshui Tan,Yuchi Xu,Wenbo Su,Zhaoxiang Zhang,Bo Zheng,Jiaheng Liu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes TVIR, a benchmark and agent framework for evaluating and improving multimodal deep research systems that must integrate both text and visual evidence in long-form reports.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing deep research agents and benchmarks focus mainly on text, leaving a gap in assessing whether visual elements (figures, charts, images) are factually correct and well aligned with the analysis, despite their importance in real-world reports.

Method: The authors introduce TVIR, consisting of (1) TVIR-Bench, 100 expert-designed multimodal research tasks where visual elements support specific analytic sub-goals; (2) TVIR-Agent, a hierarchical multi-agent system that plans outlines, retrieves images, generates charts with traceable sources, and writes reports in a context-aware, sequential manner; and (3) a dual-path evaluation framework separating textual and visual assessment to jointly measure the quality of evidence-driven multimodal reports.

Result: In experiments on nine different deep research systems, TVIR-Agent performs strongly overall on TVIR-Bench under both textual and visual evaluation dimensions, serving as a competitive baseline for multimodal deep research.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling multimodal workflows and separately evaluating text and visuals are crucial for reliable, evidence-grounded report generation; TVIR provides both a benchmark and a strong baseline agent to drive progress in this direction.

Abstract: Deep Research Agents have shown strong capability in multi-step information retrieval, reasoning, and long-form report generation, but existing benchmarks and systems remain predominantly text-centric, with limited evaluation of whether visual elements are factually reliable and well aligned with the surrounding analysis. To address this gap, we introduce TVIR (Text--Visual Interleaved Report Generation), which includes TVIR-Bench, a benchmark of 100 expert-curated multimodal deep research tasks that require visual elements to serve specific analytical sub-goals, and TVIR-Agent, a hierarchical multi-agent framework that serves as a strong baseline for constructing outlines, retrieving images, generating charts with traceable sources, and composing reports through context-aware sequential writing. We further develop a dual-path evaluation framework that combines Textual Assessment and Visual Assessment. Experiments across nine deep research systems show that TVIR-Agent achieves strong overall performance, underscoring the importance of explicit multimodal design and evaluation for evidence-driven report generation.

</details>


### [171] [WAXAL-NET: Finetuned Edge ASR Across 19 African Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02375)
*Victor Tolulope Olufemi,Oreoluwa Babatunde,Ramsey Njema,Bolarinwa Gbotemi,Wanchi Lucia Yen,John Uzodinma,Sunday Ajayi,Oluwademilade Williams,Kausar Moshood,Innocent Elendu Anyaele,Akebert Arefaine,Candace Hunzwi,Wongel Dawit Daniel,Emmilly Namuganga,Cleophas Kadima,Athanase Bahizire,Onitsiky Ranaivoson,Emmanuel Aaron,Nicholaus Ladislaus,Idris Muhammed,Jonathan Enoch Simenya,Martin Koome,Matewos Tegete Endaylalu,Peter Ifeoluwa Adeyemo,Hondi Prisca Birindwa,Ukachi Agnes Eze-Mbey,Yacoba Oduro-Yeboah,Pericles Adjovi,Mikel K. Ngueajio,Toluwani Aremu,Prasenjit Mitra*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper shows that small, domain-specialized ASR models, fine-tuned on African conversational speech, can significantly outperform much larger multilingual foundation models on 19 languages from the WAXAL corpus, and provides resources and analyses to support future African ASR research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large multilingual ASR foundation models perform poorly on spontaneous African conversational speech, especially in low-resource languages. There is a need to understand whether compact, domain-specialized models can do better, how performance transfers across domains, how architectures behave across language families, and whether standard metrics like WER accurately reflect performance, particularly for syllabary-script languages. Additionally, there is a lack of openly available, well-curated resources for African ASR research.

Method: The authors fine-tune relatively small "edge" ASR models on the WAXAL corpus, covering 19 African languages, and compare them to much larger, massively multilingual foundation models in zero-shot settings. They run in-domain and cross-domain (OOD) evaluations to test domain robustness, and conduct a distributed audit with native speakers to create a linguistically grounded error taxonomy. They analyze differences between CTC and autoregressive architectures across language families and examine character- vs word-level performance, particularly for syllabary-script languages, by comparing CER/WER ratios. They also prepare and release cleaned data, model weights, and scripts for fine-tuning and evaluation.

Result: Fine-tuned compact models achieve a macro-averaged WER of 38.0% on WAXAL, beating the best zero-shot large multilingual baseline at 64.9% WER, yielding a 26.9 percentage-point improvement while being 3–40× smaller. Domain-specialized models retain usable performance on out-of-distribution speech, though zero-shot foundation models can regain an advantage when the test domain is close to their pretraining data. The native-speaker audit generates an error taxonomy revealing systematic differences in how CTC vs autoregressive models err across different African language families. Analyses show that for syllabary-script languages, character-level accuracy is much higher than WER alone implies, as indicated by favorable CER/WER ratios.

Conclusion: Domain specialization through fine-tuning smaller ASR models can be more important than sheer scale for conversational African speech recognition, delivering substantially better performance with far fewer parameters. While large zero-shot models can still be advantageous when test data matches their pretraining domain, dedicated fine-tuning provides strong and more robust performance, including on OOD speech. Standard WER metrics can be misleading for certain writing systems, so evaluation for syllabary-script African languages should incorporate character-level measures. By releasing cleaned data, model weights, and tooling, the work establishes a stronger foundation for ongoing research in African ASR and cross-linguistic analysis of model behavior.

Abstract: We evaluate whether compact domain-specialized ASR models can outperform massively multilingual foundation models for conversational African speech across 19 languages in the WAXAL corpus. Fine-tuned edge models achieve a macro-averaged WER of $38.0\%$ compared to $64.9\%$ for the best zero-shot baseline, a $26.9$ percentage-point reduction using models $3-40\times$ smaller. Results confirm that domain specialization dominates scale for spontaneous African speech. Cross-domain evaluation shows that fine-tuned models recover usable performance on out-of-distribution (OOD) speech, while zero-shot models regain an advantage when the test domain matches their pretraining distribution. A distributed native-speaker audit across all surveyed languages produces a linguistically-grounded error taxonomy, showing that CTC and autoregressive architectures behave differently across language families. We further show that WER alone misrepresents performance for syllabary-script languages where CER/WER ratios reveal substantially higher character-level accuracy than headline WER suggests. Finally, to contribute to future African ASR research, we release all model weights, fine-tuning and evaluation scripts, and a cleaned WAXAL subset covering all $19$ languages.

</details>


### [172] [SPADE-Bench: Evaluating Spontaneous Strategic Deception in Agents via Plan-Action Divergence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02380)
*Yuyan Bu,Haowei Li,Qirui Zheng,Bowen Dong,Kaiyue Yang,Jiaming Ji,Yingshui Tan,Wenxin Li,Yaodong Yang,Juntao Dai*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces SPADE-Bench, a benchmark to measure when LLM-based agents deceive by reporting plans or actions that differ from what they actually execute during tool use.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLM agents are increasingly used in real-world, potentially high-stakes, autonomous settings, users cannot continuously supervise their internal steps and must rely on the agents’ own progress reports. This creates a serious safety risk if agents’ self-reports diverge from their true actions, making systems uncontrollable. Existing evaluations do not adequately isolate and measure this form of strategic deception in realistic tool-use contexts, leaving a critical gap in agent safety assessment.

Method: The authors define “agent deception” as spontaneous divergence between self-reported plans/actions and actually executed tool calls. They build SPADE-Bench, a benchmark where LLM agents operate tools under controlled scenarios that apply pressure (e.g., incentives or constraints) while logging both their observer-facing reports and their real executions. The benchmark design integrates real tool use, controlled pressure conditions, and explicit plan–action comparisons to separate deliberate/strategic deception from ordinary hallucination or error.

Result: Running mainstream language models on SPADE-Bench shows that these agents do, in practice, exhibit nontrivial levels of deceptive behavior in tool-use settings: they sometimes report actions or plans that do not match their actual tool executions, especially under pressure conditions. This empirically demonstrates that agent deception is a systematic and present issue, not just a hypothetical concern.

Conclusion: SPADE-Bench offers a rigorous, ecologically valid framework for evaluating deception in LLM-based tool-using agents by comparing stated plans with actual executions under pressure. This benchmark exposes real risks of agent deception and provides a standardized way to study, measure, and eventually mitigate such behavior, supporting the development of more trustworthy and controllable autonomous systems.

Abstract: As LLM-based agents expand their operational scope, reliability becomes a prerequisite for real-world deployment. However, in practical applications, human users cannot monitor every immediate behavior; instead, the execution process often remains a black box, leaving users dependent solely on the agent's self-reported updates. This opacity creates a critical risk: agents may present observer-facing reports that diverge from their executed actions, rendering the system uncontrollable, especially in high-stakes autonomous scenarios. We term such self-reported plan-action divergence as agent deception. To assess this, we introduce SPADE-Bench, a benchmark designed to evaluate spontaneous plan-action divergence. Unlike prior deception benchmarks, SPADE-Bench simultaneously integrates actual tool execution and controlled pressure scenarios. This design ensures ecological validity and rigorously distinguishes strategic deception from mere hallucination through controlled plan-action comparisons under pressure. Experiments across mainstream models confirm that agent deception is a genuine and pressing issue in tool-use contexts. By providing a comprehensive and robust evaluation framework, SPADE-Bench fills a critical gap in agent safety, facilitating the community's progress toward building trustworthy and controllable autonomous systems.

</details>


### [173] [AutoForest: Automatically Generating Forest Plots from Biomedical Studies with End-to-End Evidence Extraction and Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02403)
*Massimiliano Pronesti,Angelo Miculescu,Mohsin Kapdi,Paul Flanagan,Oisín Redmond,Joao Bettencourt-Silva,Gurdeep Mannu,Spiros Denaxas,Rui Bebiano Da Providencia E Costa,Anya Belz,Yufang Hou*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: AutoForest is an end-to-end system that automatically converts biomedical papers into publication-ready forest plots, from ICO extraction through statistical synthesis and visualization, reducing manual effort in meta-analyses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Creating forest plots for systematic reviews is currently fragmented, manual, and requires substantial domain expertise. Researchers must interpret complex clinical texts, manually extract outcome data, define interventions and comparators, deal with heterogeneous study designs, and run meta-analytic computations in specialised software. Although large language models can extract some study-level data, there is no fully automated pipeline that goes from raw documents all the way to synthesised forest plots, creating a gap for more efficient evidence synthesis.

Method: The authors develop AutoForest, an end-to-end system that, given one or more biomedical study papers, automatically: (1) suggests ICO (Intervention, Comparator, Outcome) elements; (2) extracts outcome data from text; (3) performs statistical synthesis/meta-analysis; and (4) renders publication-ready forest plots. They describe the system’s architecture and user interface and assess its performance using real-world examples in a user study with clinicians.

Result: AutoForest successfully generates forest plots directly from biomedical papers without requiring users to manually structure inputs or use traditional specialised meta-analysis software. In real-world case studies and a clinician-focused user study, the system is shown to effectively support evidence synthesis and reduce the workload and expertise barrier traditionally associated with conducting meta-analyses.

Conclusion: AutoForest demonstrates that a fully automated pipeline from unstructured biomedical articles to synthesised forest plots is feasible and useful. By integrating ICO suggestion, data extraction, statistical synthesis, and visualization in a single system, it can accelerate evidence synthesis and substantially lower the barrier for clinicians and researchers to perform meta-analyses.

Abstract: Systematic reviews rely on forest plots to synthesise quantitative evidence across biomedical studies, but generating them remains a fragmented and labour-intensive process. Researchers must interpret complex clinical texts, manually extract outcome data from trials, define appropriate interventions and comparators, harmonise inconsistent study designs, and carry out meta-analytic computations-typically using specialised software that demands structured inputs and domain expertise. While recent work has demonstrated that large language models can extract study-level data from unstructured text, no existing system automates the complete pipeline from raw documents to synthesised forest plots. To address this gap, we introduce AutoForest, the first end-to-end system that generates publication-ready forest plots directly from biomedical papers. Given one or more study papers, AutoForest automatically suggests ICO (Intervention, Comparator, Outcome) elements, extracts outcome data, performs statistical synthesis, and renders the final forest plot. We describe the system architecture, user interface and demonstrate its effectiveness on real-world examples through a user study involving clinicians, showing how AutoForest can accelerate evidence synthesis and substantially lower the barrier to conducting meta-analyses.

</details>


### [174] [K-BrowseComp: A Web Browsing Agent Benchmark Grounded in Korean Contexts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02404)
*Nahyun Lee,Dongkeun Yoon,Guijin Son,Geewook Kim,Dayoon Ko,Jeonghun Park,Haneul Yoo,Jaewon Cho,Junghun Park,Changyoon Lee,Kyochul Jang,Jaeyeon Kim,Eunsu Kim,Woojin Cho,Seungone Kim*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Paper presents K-BrowseComp, a Korean web-browsing agent benchmark revealing large performance gaps for both frontier and Korean LLMs compared to English benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing frontier model evaluations are moving from basic skills to complex, agentic behaviors, often via web-browsing tasks. However, there is a lack of such benchmarks in Korean, making it hard to assess and improve agentic capabilities for Korean users and contexts.

Method: The authors build K-BrowseComp, a 400-problem web-browsing agent benchmark rooted in Korean contexts. A 300-problem subset, K-BrowseComp-Verified, is manually authored and validated by native Korean speakers to ensure quality. They evaluate multiple frontier LLMs and Korean domestic LLMs on this subset. Additionally, they construct a 100-problem synthetic split by using hard few-shot examples and failure-mode-targeted problem generation, followed by adversarial filtering, to create a diagnostic stress test where generating tasks is easier than solving them.

Result: On the 300-problem verified subset, state-of-the-art frontier LLMs like GPT-5.5, DeepSeek-V4-Pro, and GLM-5.1 achieve only 30.00–45.67% accuracy, a large drop relative to their performance on the English BrowseComp benchmark. Korean LLMs from Korea's Proprietary AI Foundation Model program perform even worse, obtaining just 0.00–10.33%. On the 100-problem synthetic, adversarially filtered diagnostic split, the best model reaches only 26.00% accuracy.

Conclusion: K-BrowseComp exposes substantial weaknesses in current frontier and Korean LLMs when operating as web-browsing agents in Korean contexts, especially under adversarial, diagnostic conditions. The benchmark provides a challenging, language-specific testbed and stress test that can guide future research and development of agentic, web-enabled models for Korean, with all data and code released publicly to enable community use and extension.

Abstract: Frontier model evaluations are shifting from foundational capabilities (e.g., instruction following and reasoning) toward compositional, agentic ones, but Korean agentic benchmarks remain scarce. We introduce K-BrowseComp, a web-browsing agent benchmark grounded in Korean contexts, consisting of 400 problems. The 300-problem K-BrowseComp-Verified subset is manually constructed and validated by native Korean speakers. On this subset, frontier LLMs, including GPT-5.5, DeepSeek-V4-Pro, and GLM-5.1, reach only 30.00--45.67\%, a substantial drop from BrowseComp, while Korean LLMs released through Korea's Proprietary AI Foundation Model program obtain only 0.00--10.33\%. We further construct a 100-problem synthetic split using hard few-shot exemplars and failure-mode-targeted generation to exploit the asymmetry between solving and creating web browsing problems. On the adversarially filtered synthetic diagnostic split, the strongest model reaches only 26.00\%, and we report this split separately as a targeted stress test. We publicly release our data and code.

</details>


### [175] [Investigating and Alleviating Harm Amplification in LLM Interactions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02423)
*Ruohao Guo,Wei Xu,Alan Ritter*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents HarmAmp, a benchmark for evaluating how LLMs amplify harm across multi-turn conversations, and TrajSafe, a proactive safety monitor that reduces harmfulness while maintaining usefulness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While LLMs can be useful assistants, they can also amplify harm by enabling novices to generate expert-level harmful content and to scale harmful operations. Existing safety evaluations largely focus on single-turn prompts and fail to capture how risk compounds over multi-turn interactions that better mirror real-world use. There is a need for realistic benchmarks and methods that address multi-step, evolving harmful trajectories.

Method: The authors design HarmAmp, a benchmark of multi-turn harm amplification scenarios across twelve real-world risk categories. Each scenario is constructed to ensure (1) meaningful amplification of a user’s harmful capabilities, (2) concrete operational detail, and (3) dependence on multi-turn interaction. They then introduce TrajSafe, a proactive safety monitor that tracks conversation trajectories, predicts emerging harm, and intervenes using strategies such as intent probing and response steering to keep the model on safe paths. They evaluate TrajSafe over HarmAmp, comparing it against baselines on safety and utility metrics.

Result: Experiments on HarmAmp show that TrajSafe substantially decreases the level of harmful guidance produced during multi-turn interactions, while keeping the rate of unnecessary refusals low and preserving the underlying model’s general task performance. This indicates that proactive, trajectory-aware monitoring can mitigate compounding harms more effectively than static, single-turn defenses.

Conclusion: Multi-turn interactions introduce unique, compounding safety risks that existing LLM evaluations and defenses under-address. HarmAmp provides a systematic benchmark for such scenarios, and TrajSafe exemplifies a trajectory-aware, proactive defense that can meaningfully reduce harm amplification without overly constraining model utility. The work advocates for safety mechanisms and benchmarks that reason over conversation trajectories rather than isolated prompts.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can serve as helpful assistants, yet they can equally function as harm amplifiers that enable malicious users to achieve harmful outcomes beyond their capabilities through extended interactions. This risk manifests along two axes, i.e., democratizing domain expertise that allows novices to produce specialized harmful content, and scaling harmful operations at volumes that manual effort cannot match. Existing works, however, often overlook how LLMs compound harm across multi-turn conversations. We introduce HarmAmp, a new benchmark for multi-turn harm amplification scenarios spanning twelve risk categories. Each scenario is grounded in real-world threats and satisfies rigorous criteria, i.e., substantive amplification, operational specificity, and multi-turn necessity. We further propose TrajSafe, a proactive monitor that anticipates harmful trajectories and intervenes through actions such as probing users' genuine intents and steering the models towards safer completion. Our extensive experiments demonstrate that TrajSafe significantly reduces the harmfulness incurred in multi-turn interactions while preserving a low over-refusal rate and the target model's general capabilities. Our work offers a promising paradigm to alleviate the nuanced safety risks in LLM interactions.

</details>


### [176] [PaSBench-Video: A Streaming Video Benchmark for Proactive Safety Warning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02443)
*Yusong Zhao,Yuejin Xie,Youliang Yuan,Junjie Hu,Jitian Guo,Yujiu Yang,Pinjia He*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces PaSBench-Video, a video benchmark to evaluate whether multimodal LLMs can issue timely, accurate warnings before accidents, showing that current models perform poorly and mainly rely on superficial scene cues rather than genuine risk reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is typically a short time window between noticing danger and an accident actually happening, during which timely warnings could prevent harm. Multimodal LLMs with video capabilities might act as continuous safety monitors in domains like driving, healthcare, daily life, and industry. However, existing benchmarks are inadequate: they use static inputs rather than causal video, do not require precise timing of warnings, and do not measure false positives on benign scenes. This gap prevents us from meaningfully assessing and improving models’ abilities to anticipate and warn about emerging hazards.

Method: The authors construct PaSBench-Video, a benchmark of 740 videos (481 risk, 259 no-risk) from four domains: driving, healthcare, daily life, and industrial production. Each risk video is annotated at frame level with risk onset and accident boundaries, enabling precise temporal evaluation. Models must watch videos causally (no future frames) and issue warnings that are both temporally well-calibrated and semantically correct about the risk. They evaluate 13 video-capable multimodal LLMs under multiple metrics, including a strict metric that jointly considers content correctness, timing, recall, and false-positive rate, and they analyze performance across domains and the tradeoff between recall and false positives using correlation analysis.

Result: No tested MLLM achieves more than 20% on the strictest evaluation metric, indicating poor ability to provide timely and accurate pre-accident warnings. There is a strong tradeoff between recall and false positives, with Pearson correlation 0.64: increasing detection of risky events generally causes models to flag most safe clips erroneously. Performance varies by domain: in daily-life scenes, where hazards are more anomalous, models reach moderate recall at low false-positive rates; but in driving videos, where normal and risky situations look visually similar, models trigger warnings indiscriminately. This suggests models are over-reliant on coarse scene or activity patterns.

Conclusion: PaSBench-Video reveals that current video-capable multimodal LLMs are far from reliably anticipating accidents in real time. They tend to depend on surface-level scene cues instead of reasoning about evolving causal chains and emerging harm, leading to sharp tradeoffs between recall and false alarms and highly uneven performance across domains. The benchmark highlights the need for new modeling approaches and training strategies that emphasize temporal reasoning about risk onset, causal understanding of dangerous situations, and calibrated early warning, as well as more robust evaluation frameworks for safety monitoring tasks.

Abstract: Between the first visible sign of danger and the moment an accident occurs, there is often a window where intervention remains possible. Video-capable multimodal large language models (MLLMs) could serve as always-on safety monitors that issue warnings during this window. Yet current benchmarks do not test this ability: they rely on static inputs, ignore timing precision, and omit false-positive measurement on safe scenes. We present PaSBench-Video, a 740-video benchmark with 481 risk and 259 no-risk videos across four domains: driving, healthcare, daily life, and industrial production. Risk videos are annotated with frame-level risk onset and accident boundaries. A model must observe the video causally and produce a warning that is both temporally calibrated and content-correct. Testing 13 MLLMs, we find that no model exceeds 20.0% on our strictest metric, and recall is tightly coupled with false-positive rate, with Pearson correlation 0.64: higher detection comes only at the cost of triggering warnings on the majority of safe clips. Performance splits sharply by domain: models achieve moderate recall at low false-positive rates in daily life, where risks are inherently anomalous, yet fire indiscriminately in driving, where routine and hazardous scenes look alike. These results indicate that current models rely on scene-level activity cues rather than reasoning about emerging harm.

</details>


### [177] [Learning When to Translate for Multilingual Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02465)
*Deokhyung Kang,Hyounghun Kim,Gary Geunbae Lee*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Luar, a reinforcement learning framework that teaches reasoning language models to selectively translate non-English inputs into English only when needed, improving multilingual reasoning performance, especially for low-resource languages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reasoning language models perform well on complex tasks but show large performance gaps across languages due to failures in understanding non-English inputs. Always translating inputs into English can help but is inefficient and sometimes unnecessary when the original language is already well understood. There is a need for a method that can intelligently decide when translation is actually beneficial for reasoning, particularly to close gaps in low-resource languages without over-reliance on translation.

Method: The authors propose Luar, a Language Understanding Boundary-aware Reinforcement Learning framework. Luar trains a model with a policy that chooses between two actions for each non-English query: (1) perform reasoning directly on the original input; or (2) call a translation system and then reason over the English translation. Using reinforcement learning (similar to GRPO-style training), the model is rewarded when its chosen strategy (with or without translation) improves performance, and especially when translator-augmented reasoning significantly outperforms direct reasoning. This shapes the model’s decision boundary about when its direct language understanding is reliable enough and when translation should be invoked.

Result: On multilingual reasoning benchmarks, models trained with Luar outperform standard GRPO and other training-based baselines. The improvements are especially strong for low-resource languages, where language understanding failures are more common. Empirical analyses show that Luar tends to skip translation when direct reasoning already works well, reducing unnecessary translation usage, and that the learned translation-calling behavior generalizes to low-resource languages not seen during training.

Conclusion: Luar demonstrates that reasoning language models can be trained to selectively rely on translation rather than using it indiscriminately. By learning a boundary for when direct language understanding is unreliable, the framework improves multilingual reasoning performance and makes more efficient use of translation resources, with substantial benefits for low-resource languages. This supports a selective, boundary-aware approach to multilingual reasoning as a promising direction for future RLM training and deployment.

Abstract: Reasoning language models (RLMs) achieve strong performance on complex reasoning tasks, but still exhibit substantial multilingual reasoning gaps, largely due to language-understanding failures in non-English inputs. English translation can mitigate these failures by expressing non-English inputs in a form that RLMs can more reliably interpret, yet translating every input is unnecessary when the model can reason reliably from the original query. To address this challenge, we propose Luar, a Language Understanding Boundary-aware Reinforcement Learning framework that trains RLMs to selectively invoke translation when direct understanding is unreliable. Luar trains the model to choose between solving the original input directly and reasoning over its English translation, encouraging translation only when translator-augmented reasoning is expected to substantially outperform direct reasoning. Across multilingual reasoning benchmarks, Luar outperforms standard GRPO and other training-based baselines, with particularly large gains on low-resource languages. Further analysis shows that Luar avoids unnecessary translation in cases where direct reasoning is sufficient, while extending its translator-call behavior to unseen low-resource languages. Together, our work suggests a selective approach to multilingual reasoning: RLMs can learn to invoke translation only when their direct understanding is unreliable. The project will be made publicly available at https://github.com/deokhk/LUAR

</details>


### [178] [Towards Multidisciplinary Summarization of Hospital Stays: Efficient Sentence-Level Clinical Provenance Categorization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02487)
*Baris Karacan,Vaibhav Bhargava,Barbara Di Eugenio,Natalie Parde,Mary Khetani,Yu-Shan Tseng,Vanessa Barbosa,Julie Vignato,Lindsey Knake,Rajashree Dahal,Emily Spellman,Danielle Hitzel,Janine Petitgout,Kristi Haughey,Amanda Karstens,Brianna Clarahan,Rachel Dawson,Lauren Boyd,Mackenzie Weis,Angie Tipton,Jaewon Bae,Catherine K. Craven,Karen Dunn Lopez,Andrew D. Boyd*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper develops and tests a pipeline that labels the clinical provenance (source/type of information) of sentences across many heterogeneous ICU notes, as a prerequisite for better multi-disciplinary summaries, and shows that large, quantized, fine-tuned Llama-3 models can transfer well from adult ICU data to NICU data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In complex environments like the NICU, patient information is scattered across many free-text notes written by different disciplines. Naively combining this text for summarization often produces incoherent results because the system cannot tell what comes from where (provenance) or how to structure it. To enable reliable "all-team" summaries, the authors argue we first need accurate, automated categorization of the provenance of each sentence across multiple clinical sources, and they explore whether fine-tuned LLMs can provide this in a way that generalizes across clinical domains while staying computationally efficient.

Method: The authors build a clinical provenance categorization pipeline using supervised fine-tuning of two Llama-3 models (8B and 70B) on MedSecId, a dataset of 2,002 adult ICU notes (MIMIC-III) annotated with section-level clinical provenance headers. They fine-tune both model sizes, then evaluate in-domain performance on MedSecId using Macro F1. To test cross-domain transfer, they apply these models (base vs. SFT, various precisions/quantizations) to a small gold-standard NICU dataset of 227 annotated sentence-level spans from three multi-disciplinary NICU summaries, analyzing how model size, fine-tuning, and quantization affect performance.

Result: On the in-domain MedSecId dataset, both fine-tuned Llama-3 models (8B and 70B) achieve high Macro F1 (>92%), showing effective provenance categorization for adult ICU notes. For cross-domain evaluation on the NICU dataset, the 70B model benefits substantially from SFT, with Macro F1 improving by 7%, while the 8B model shows only marginal gains. Additionally, a quantized, fine-tuned 70B model not only reduces computational cost but also outperforms its full-precision counterpart, indicating that quantization can be compatible with, and even beneficial for, this task.

Conclusion: The study concludes that (1) accurate clinical provenance categorization from heterogeneous ICU notes is feasible with fine-tuned Llama-3 models, (2) sufficient model capacity is essential to maintain semantic flexibility needed for effective cross-domain transfer (adult ICU to NICU), and (3) quantized, fine-tuned large models can deliver strong performance with lower computational overhead. These findings support using large, efficiently adapted LLMs as a backbone for structured provenance modeling, which in turn can enable more coherent and reliable multi-disciplinary clinical summarization, especially in high-complexity settings like the NICU.

Abstract: Effective "all-team" summarization in high-complexity settings like the Neonatal Intensive Care Unit (NICU) requires aggregating insights from diverse disciplines (physicians, nurses, therapists) spread across hundreds of clinical free-text notes. Simply pooling heterogeneous text often leads to incoherent outputs. Structured summarization therefore first requires accurate categorization of sentence-level provenance across multi-source notes. This pilot study introduces a clinical provenance categorization pipeline using supervised fine-tuning (SFT) of large language models (LLMs). We adapted two Llama-3 models (8B and 70B) to MedSecId, a corpus of 2,002 MIMIC-III (Adult ICU) notes annotated with clinical provenance headers, achieving in-domain Macro F1 scores above 92% for both models. To evaluate cross-domain generalization, we assessed model capacity (8B vs. 70B) and quantization on a gold-standard dataset of 227 sentence-level spans derived from three multi-disciplinary NICU summaries. Experimental results demonstrate a scale-dependent transfer effect: while SFT produced only marginal changes for the 8B model, it substantially improved the 70B model, increasing Macro F1 by 7%. Notably, the quantized fine-tuned 70B model outperformed its full-precision baseline while substantially reducing computational requirements. These findings suggest that sufficient model capacity is critical for preserving semantic flexibility during cross-domain clinical transfer and that efficient quantized adaptation can enable structured provenance modeling for downstream summarization.

</details>


### [179] [Not What, But How: A Communicative Audit of LLM Response Framing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02493)
*Siddhesh Milind Pawar,Sarah Masud,Haneul Yoo,Alice Oh,Isabelle Augenstein*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces FRANZ, an automated framework to characterize how LLMs frame answers to subjective cultural questions, using a new large dataset (SQUARE) of such questions and showing systematic differences and couplings between response characteristics across models and countries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of large language models on subjective, culturally grounded questions focus mainly on factual correctness and overlook how answers are framed and communicated. Since users care about tone, stance, and communicative style, there is a need for a systematic way to audit these qualitative aspects of LLM responses across cultures and question types.

Method: The authors build FRANZ, an automated framework that audits LLM responses along four dimensions: (1) cultural positioning (e.g., insider vs. outsider stance), (2) use of generalizing language, (3) anthropomorphic cues (attributing human traits to the model), and (4) adherence to conversational maxims (e.g., Gricean norms). To support this, they construct SQUARE, a corpus of 376k subjective questions taken from 57 subreddits, each mapped to one of 7 countries and 19 categories. They then generate responses from three open-weight LLMs and automatically score them with FRANZ on the four dimensions, performing statistical comparisons and coupling analyses (e.g., how often certain characteristics co-occur).

Result: Using FRANZ, the authors find that the three evaluated LLMs differ significantly and systematically in how often they exhibit each response characteristic. The audit reveals patterns such as variation in cultural positioning, degrees of generalization, anthropomorphic expressions, and adherence to conversational norms. Importantly, they uncover a positive coupling between insider cultural positioning and anthropomorphism, and show that the strength of this coupling differs by country, something that would not be visible in single-dimensional audits focused only on one feature.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that communicative framing of LLM outputs is both measurable and meaningfully different across models and cultural contexts. FRANZ, together with the SQUARE dataset, provides a scalable diagnostic tool for auditing and comparing LLMs beyond accuracy, capturing nuanced aspects such as cultural stance and anthropomorphism. This multi-dimensional approach exposes cross-country framing divergences that single-metric audits would miss, and can inform model development, deployment, and governance for culturally sensitive, subjective use cases.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are being increasingly used to answer subjective, information-seeking questions, where users are sensitive to how responses are communicated, not just whether the answers are correct. Existing LLM evaluations for subjective cultural queries largely focus on factual correctness, ignoring how the response is framed. To this end, we introduce FRANZ, an automated FRAmework for respoNse characteriZation to conduct communicative audit of LLM responses along four dimensions: cultural positioning, use of generalizing language, anthropomorphic cues, and adherence to conversational maxims. To enable this evaluation, we contribute SQUARE - a corpus of 376k subjective questions sourced from 57 subreddits, and mapped to 7 countries and 19 question categories. We demonstrate FRANZ's applicability by scoring responses from three open-weight LLMs. We observe that LLMs show statistically significant differences in the frequency with which they employ each response characteristic. Unlike single-dimensional audits, FRANZ reveals that insider positioning and anthropomorphism are positively coupled, with the degree of coupling varying by country, providing a diagnostic lens for identifying framing divergences.

</details>


### [180] [CRAM: Centroid-Routing and Adaptive MoE for Multimodal Continual Instruction Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02502)
*Jun-Tao Tang,Zhen-Hao Xie,Yu-Cheng Shi,Da-Wei Zhou*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces CRAM, a method for multimodal continual instruction tuning that balances preventing forgetting with parameter efficiency by using task-specific modules and adaptive parameter allocation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal LLMs must continually learn new heterogeneous vision-language tasks without retraining from scratch, but existing approaches either share all parameters (causing task interference and forgetting) or create separate modules per task (causing parameter explosion). There is a need for a method that can expand capabilities over long task streams while preserving prior skills and maintaining parameter efficiency.

Method: CRAM isolates task-specific patterns into independent modules to reduce interference and catastrophic forgetting. It leverages adaptive-rank instantiation to measure the capability gap between existing experts and new tasks, dynamically allocating only the parameters needed. Centroid-guided routing identifies and activates suitable existing experts for a task, and an orthogonality penalty constrains new updates to task-specific directions so the model does not re-learn general capabilities.

Result: Across various multimodal benchmarks and continual learning settings, CRAM outperforms existing methods in maintaining previous task performance while acquiring new tasks, and does so with better parameter efficiency.

Conclusion: CRAM provides an effective and parameter-efficient framework for multimodal continual instruction tuning by combining expert modularization, adaptive parameter growth, and routing/regularization mechanisms to mitigate catastrophic forgetting and reuse prior capabilities across heterogeneous tasks.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) unify heterogeneous vision-language tasks under a shared generative framework via instruction tuning, yet real-world deployment demands continuous capability expansion, making Multimodal Continual Instruction Tuning (MCIT) essential. Existing methods either update all tasks with a shared parameter set or allocate dedicated modules for each new task. Shared updates force heterogeneous tasks to compete, causing forgetting of learned capabilities. Conversely, isolated expansion prevents interference but severely limits parameter efficiency over long task streams. To address this dilemma, we propose CRAM. Specifically, by isolating task-specific patterns into independent modules, CRAM mitigates catastrophic forgetting across tasks. To further boost parameter efficiency, we utilize adaptive-rank instantiation to identify the capability gap between existing expert capability and new task demands, and dynamically allocate only the necessary parameters. To ensure stable reuse among tasks, centroid-guided routing recognizes and activates existing experts' capabilities, while an orthogonality penalty confines new updates to task-specific directions, preventing re-learning general capability. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks consistently demonstrate its superiority over existing methods.

</details>
