### [219] [TAPS: Target-Aware Prefix Tree Selection for Diffusion-Drafted Speculative Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00487)
*Zhuoyu Wang,Junnan Huang,Xinyu Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes TAPS, a target-aware prefix selection strategy that improves speculative decoding with diffusion-based parallel drafting by selecting better prefixes to verify, yielding substantial end-to-end speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Speculative decoding with diffusion models enables parallel drafting of multiple future tokens, greatly reducing drafting latency, but verification of these drafts becomes the new bottleneck. Existing diffusion-based draft-tree methods rank nodes only by marginal probability and ignore that verification is conditioned on accepted prefixes, leading to wasted verification on descendants of unlikely or rejected prefixes and thus poor acceptance-cost tradeoffs.

Method: The authors analyze the mismatch between diffusion-based draft trees and prefix-conditioned verification, then introduce TAPS (Target-Aware Prefix Selection). TAPS converts diffusion-marginal probabilities into path-conditioned estimates of whether a draft token sequence will be accepted by the target model. Using these estimates and a fixed verification budget, TAPS constructs a compact prefix-closed subtree that prioritizes paths with higher expected acceptance, instead of simply growing a large tree by marginal scores. This yields a more efficient verification schedule within a given latency constraint.

Result: Across multiple datasets and language-model families, TAPS provides up to 7.9× lossless end-to-end speedup compared to standard autoregressive decoding, and also surpasses prior diffusion-based speculative decoding methods DFlash and DDTree, achieving 1.36× and 1.74× higher speedup respectively under comparable settings.

Conclusion: By making diffusion-based drafting explicitly aware of prefix-conditioned verification through target-aware prefix selection, TAPS substantially improves the acceptance–cost tradeoff in speculative decoding. This shifts speculative diffusion methods from naively expanding draft trees toward more principled, verification-efficient prefix selection, delivering significant practical decoding speedups without loss in model quality.

Abstract: Using a diffusion model for parallel drafting is a promising approach for speculative decoding. By predicting tokens at multiple future positions in a single forward pass, diffusion drafters substantially reduce drafting latency. However, this shifts the bottleneck to verification: verifying a single sequence limits acceptance length, while verifying large draft trees incurs excessive target-model latency. We identify a key mismatch in existing draft-tree methods: existing diffusion-tree methods rank nodes by the marginal probability, ignoring that verification is prefix-conditioned. As a result, they may verify unreachable descendants of rejected prefixes, increasing latency with limited acceptance gains. To address this, we propose TAPS, a target-aware prefix selection method that turns diffusion marginals into path-conditioned acceptance estimates. TAPS then selects a compact prefix-closed subtree under a fixed verification budget, improving the acceptance-cost tradeoff rather than simply expanding the draft tree. Experiments across diverse datasets and model families demonstrate that TAPS achieves up to 7.9x lossless end-to-end speedup over vanilla autoregressive decoding, outperforming state-of-the-art DFlash and DDTree by 1.36x and 1.74x respectively. Our work is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/TAPS-EMNLP2026-53DD

</details>


### [220] [EnergyMamba: An Uncertainty-Aware Graph-Enhanced Selective State Space Model for Energy Consumption Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00506)
*Dahai Yu,Rongchao Xu,Lin Jiang,Guang Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes EnergyMamba, a spatiotemporal, uncertainty-aware model that improves both accuracy and calibrated uncertainty for regional energy consumption forecasting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing energy consumption prediction models mainly treat the task as a pure time-series problem, ignoring spatial relationships between regions in a power grid, and they rarely provide reliable uncertainty estimates, especially under abnormal conditions like extreme weather or other distribution shifts. This limits their usefulness for grid management, demand response, and planning, where both accuracy and trustworthy uncertainty are required.

Method: The authors design EnergyMamba, which has two main components: (1) GE-Mamba, a graph-enhanced selective state space model that injects spatial information derived from the grid topology into the temporal modeling process, allowing joint spatiotemporal learning; and (2) AS-CQR, an adaptive sequential conformalized quantile regression module that uses locally adaptive normalization and an online feedback mechanism to continuously adjust prediction intervals in the presence of distribution shifts, enabling better calibrated uncertainty estimates over time.

Result: On four large-scale real-world datasets from Florida, New York, and California, EnergyMamba outperforms 15 state-of-the-art baseline methods, delivering about 5% better point-forecast accuracy and 6% better uncertainty quantification quality (e.g., more accurate and well-calibrated prediction intervals).

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling grid-based spatial dependencies together with temporal dynamics, and combining this with adaptive conformalized uncertainty estimation, yields more accurate and reliable energy consumption forecasts. EnergyMamba demonstrates that graph-enhanced state-space modeling plus adaptive interval calibration is an effective approach for practical, robust power demand forecasting under both normal and abnormal conditions.

Abstract: Energy consumption prediction is essential for efficient grid management, demand-side optimization, and sustainable energy planning. Although advanced machine learning methods have been employed for better prediction performance, existing works have two key limitations: (1) they usually formulate this task as a purely time-series prediction problem without explicitly modeling the spatial dependencies among different regions, and (2) they fail to provide reliable predictions with uncertainty estimates under abnormal situations such as extreme weather events. To advance existing research, we propose EnergyMamba, an uncertainty-aware spatiotemporal learning framework for accurate and reliable energy consumption prediction, which comprises two key components: (i) a novel Graph-Enhanced Selective State Space Model (GE-Mamba) that injects spatial context learned from the grid topology into the temporal dynamics, enabling coupled spatiotemporal modeling, and (ii) an Adaptive Sequential Conformalized Quantile Regression (AS-CQR) module, which includes locally adaptive normalization and an online feedback mechanism to dynamically calibrate prediction intervals under potential distribution shifts. We evaluate EnergyMamba on four large-scale real-world datasets from Florida, New York, and California. Results show EnergyMamba achieves around 5% improvement in prediction accuracy and 6% improvement in uncertainty quantification over 15 state-of-the-art baselines.

</details>


### [221] [Threshold-Based Exclusive Batching for LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00516)
*Weifang Zhang,Yuzhou Nie,Bowen Pang,Guangrui Ma,Shining Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies when mixed batching (interleaving prefill and decode) is actually optimal for LLM inference, finds that on bandwidth-limited GPUs it is often suboptimal, and proposes conditions and a hybrid scheduler to switch between mixed and exclusive batching for higher throughput.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mixed batching is widely used to maximize utilization in LLM inference by interleaving prefill and decode in one batch, but practitioners have observed unexplained slowdowns and it is unclear how GPU memory bandwidth, model size, and workload composition affect its true efficiency compared with exclusive batching of prefill and decode. There is a need for a principled understanding and practical scheduling rules to choose the best strategy per hardware and workload.

Method: The authors run controlled experiments across GPUs with different memory bandwidth (e.g., H200 vs RTX PRO 6000), model sizes, and prefill/decode mixes to measure "prefill-decode interference" and its impact on per-step marginal cost. They analytically model throughput, derive a closed-form condition for when exclusive batching (EB) outperforms mixed batching (MB), and compute asymptotically optimal thresholds for switching phases and safe batch sizes under EB. They then implement a hybrid online scheduler (EB+) that monitors workload composition and dynamically switches between EB and MB using the derived condition.

Result: They find that prefill-decode interference makes MB more expensive per token than pure decode once decode dominates the batch, with the crossover heavily dependent on memory bandwidth: on an H200 (4.8 TB/s) MB becomes worse only when decode tokens exceed about 80% of the batch, whereas on an RTX PRO 6000 (1.792 TB/s) MB becomes worse already at about 20% decode share. With their optimized EB strategy, throughput improves by up to 41.9% on bandwidth-constrained GPUs, while MB remains superior for large models on high-bandwidth GPUs. Their hybrid EB+ scheduler consistently matches or exceeds the best static strategy and outperforms MB alone by up to 36.4% under non-stationary workloads with shifting distributions or concurrency.

Conclusion: The optimal batching strategy for LLM inference is not universally mixed batching; it critically depends on GPU memory bandwidth, model size, and the prefill/decode composition of the workload. Prefill-decode interference can significantly hurt performance on bandwidth-limited hardware, making exclusive batching preferable over a wide range of conditions. The authors provide a closed-form crossover condition, optimal EB configuration rules, and a hybrid EB+ scheduler that automatically switches between EB and MB online, achieving near-optimal throughput without manual tuning, especially on bandwidth-constrained GPUs and under non-stationary traffic.

Abstract: Mixed batching (MB)--interleaving prefill and decode in a single batch--has become the standard scheduling strategy for large language model (LLM) inference due to its efficiency in maximizing compute and memory utilization. However, through controlled experiments, we find that prefill-decode interference inflates MB's per-step marginal cost above that of pure decode. On the high-bandwidth H200 (4.8 TB/s), this occurs only when decode tokens exceed 80% of the batch; however, on the bandwidth-constrained RTX PRO 6000 (1.792 TB/s), this threshold plummets to just 20%. Consequently, the optimal choice between MB and exclusive batching (EB) fundamentally depends on GPU memory bandwidth, model size, and workload composition. We derive a closed-form condition for this EB-MB performance crossover, along with asymptotically optimal phase-switching thresholds and memory-safe batch sizing for EB. Optimized EB achieves up to 41.9% higher throughput on bandwidth-constrained GPUs, while MB retains its advantage on high-bandwidth hardware with larger models. Our hybrid scheduler EB+ applies this condition online to dynamically switch between EB and MB without manual intervention. Under non-stationary traffic with distribution or concurrency shifts, EB+ attains the highest or near-highest throughput in every setting, outperforming MB by up to 36.4%.

</details>


### [222] [Acting with AI: An Interaction-Based Framework for Agentic Tort Liability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00518)
*Yiheng Yao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a legal framework to assign tort liability for harms caused by agentic AI systems by modeling human-AI interactions on existing doctrines for human concerted action.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing tort law has difficulty allocating responsibility when agentic AI systems, which can plan and act over time, cause harm through paths neither clearly chosen by users nor foreseen by developers. There is a need for a principled way to map these hybrid human-AI interactions onto established legal doctrines so courts can attribute liability fairly and predictably.

Method: The authors build a conceptual, doctrinal framework grounded in Michael Bratman’s planning theory and common law doctrines on human-human concerted action. They classify human-AI interactions into three types—autonomous drift, pure tool use, and collaborative planning—and systematically map each type onto existing tort doctrines (product defect, warnings, respondeat superior, frolic and detour, professional malpractice, negligent misrepresentation, independent contractor control tests, and strict product liability). They propose using stateful interaction logs as primary evidence to reconstruct the human-AI trajectory, and they articulate a proposed “Reasonable Agent” standard defined via constraint verification, epistemic transparency, runtime grounding, and forensic logging. They then apply the framework to four stylized incident cases and compare it with strict-liability and insurance-based approaches.

Result: The framework yields a structured way to decide when harms from agentic AI should be treated as (1) traditional product/tool defects and warning failures, (2) shared or professional-like responsibility in collaborative planning with AI, or (3) analogous to employee frolic/detour or product malfunction in autonomous drift. By centering analysis on interaction logs, courts can identify when AI behavior diverged from the user’s authorized plan and assign liability to users, developers, or both under familiar doctrines. The “Reasonable Agent” standard provides concrete design and logging criteria that can function as benchmarks for due care in AI system design and deployment.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that tort law can accommodate harms from agentic AI without entirely new legal categories, by reconceptualizing human-AI interactions through an interaction-based framework analogous to human concerted action. Using detailed interaction logs and the proposed typology, courts can locate where the human-AI trajectory departed from the authorized undertaking and map that departure onto existing doctrines to assign liability. The authors argue this approach integrates naturally with regulatory oversight, complements but does not require strict-liability or purely insurance-based regimes, and that the “Reasonable Agent” standard can guide both courts and designers in evaluating whether an agentic AI system met its duty of care.

Abstract: Agentic AI systems can plan over multiple steps, use tools, and execute tasks over time. When such systems cause harm, tort law struggles to allocate responsibility because the harmful path may be neither fully chosen by the user nor specifically foreseen by the developer. This paper proposes an interaction-based framework for agentic torts, drawing on Michael Bratman's planning theory and on the common law's treatment of human-human concerted action. We distinguish three interaction types: autonomous drift, pure tool use, and collaborative planning. Pure tool cases remain governed by ordinary product-defect and warning doctrines; collaborative planning cases map onto the independent contractor control test, professional malpractice, and negligent misrepresentation; autonomous drift maps onto frolic and detour under respondeat superior and strict product liability. The framework treats the stateful interaction log as the primary evidentiary trace, allowing courts to infer where the human-AI trajectory departed from the authorized undertaking and where liability should attach. We resolve four incident-anchored cases, situate the account alongside strict-liability and insurance-based proposals, note its relationship to regulatory oversight, and propose a ``Reasonable Agent'' standard built around constraint verification, epistemic transparency, runtime grounding, and forensic logging.

</details>


### [223] [KACE: Knowledge-Adaptive Context Engineering for Mathematical Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00532)
*Jayant Parashar,Suchendra M. Bhandarkar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes Knowledge-Adaptive Context Engineering (KACE), a method that separates long-term knowledge storage from per-problem context usage to mitigate prompt bloat in LLM-based math reasoning, organizing learned guidance by difficulty and domain and selectively retrieving it via a tiered self-consistency classifier, yielding substantial accuracy gains on challenging math benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Context engineering methods for LLMs can improve performance without finetuning, but in mathematical reasoning they quickly run into a prompt-length ceiling: as more feedback and examples are added to prompts, they cause context bloat and restrict how much learned guidance can be used. Existing approaches mix together what is stored across tasks and what is actually injected into the prompt for a given test problem, so they scale poorly. There is a need for a way to accumulate and organize knowledge over many runs while only using a compact, targeted subset of that knowledge for each new problem, especially in challenging domains like competition-level math.

Method: The authors introduce Knowledge-Adaptive Context Engineering (KACE). Offline, they run a self-reflective learning loop over training problems and their solution traces. From the model’s failures, they distill guidance into typed ‘cards’ (small knowledge units such as hints, strategies, patterns, or corrections). These cards are organized into an epistemic tree whose nodes are indexed by problem difficulty (e.g., easy/medium/hard) and epistemic domain (types of mathematical knowledge or problem categories). Each card is stored at the node corresponding to the difficulty and domain of the failure instance that generated it, separating long-term storage from actual prompt usage. At inference time, they use a tiered self-consistency procedure: the model solves each problem multiple times with varying compute, and agreement statistics are used to classify the problem on-the-fly as easy, medium, or hard. Easy problems are answered without retrieving any cards; medium and hard problems only retrieve cards from the corresponding branch of the epistemic tree, limiting context size while tailoring guidance. This tiered mechanism is compared against standard Best-of-N self-consistency and other learned-context baselines under similar solver-call budgets.

Result: The tiered self-consistency classifier achieves about 78% pairwise concordance in predicting difficulty tiers. Across benchmarks, KACE matches or outperforms Best-of-N approaches while using comparable compute. On the AIME 2025 benchmark, KACE attains 62.2% accuracy, which is a 10.4 percentage-point improvement over fixed Best-of-5 self-consistency at a similar number of solver calls and a 5.6-point gain over the strongest learned-context baseline, Tiered + GEPA. The method also yields consistent improvements on other challenging math benchmarks, including MATH-HARD and the verifiable subset of OlymMATH, indicating robustness across tasks and datasets.

Conclusion: Separating knowledge storage from per-problem context usage via an epistemic tree and tiered self-consistency allows LLMs to benefit from accumulated experience without exceeding prompt limits. By stratifying guidance by both difficulty and epistemic domain and retrieving only the relevant branch conditioned on an on-the-fly difficulty estimate, KACE mitigates context bloat while preserving or improving performance. The approach demonstrates that difficulty-aware, domain-structured knowledge bases can significantly boost mathematical reasoning accuracy under fixed compute budgets and offers a scalable path for context-based learning without weight updates.

Abstract: Context engineering can improve large language models without updating their weights, but mathematical reasoning exposes a key limitation: feedback accumulated in one growing prompt causes context bloat and limits the amount of learned guidance that can be used. Existing methods often conflate storage, what is learned across runs, with usage, what is included for a particular problem, and therefore inherit this prompt-size ceiling. We introduce Knowledge-Adaptive Context Engineering (KACE), which separates storage from usage through difficulty- and domain-based organization. Offline, a self-reflective learning loop distills training traces into an epistemic tree: a knowledge base of typed cards stratified by problem difficulty and epistemic domain. Each card is assigned to the difficulty-domain node corresponding to the failure from which it originated. At evaluation time, tiered self-consistency with per-tier agreement gates dynamically classifies each problem as easy, medium, or hard. Easy problems exit without retrieved cards, while harder problems retrieve only the matching branch of the tree. This tiered scheme matches or exceeds Best-of-N while using comparable compute, and it classifies problem difficulty with 78 percent pairwise concordance. The main empirical contribution is the construction and use of a difficulty- and domain-stratified knowledge base enabled by tiered self-consistency. On AIME 2025, KACE achieves 62.2 percent accuracy, a 10.4-point absolute gain over fixed Best-of-5 self-consistency at a comparable solver-call budget and a 5.6-point gain over the strongest learned-context baseline, Tiered + GEPA. We also observe consistent gains on MATH-HARD and the verifiable subset of OlymMATH.

</details>


### [224] [Probe Before You Edit: Probing-Guided Molecular Optimization for LLM Agents in Structure-Based Drug Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00555)
*Zaifei Yang,Weiyu Chen,Yaqing Wang,James Kwok*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces PROBE, a probing-based multi-agent LLM framework that optimizes ligands for both binding affinity and druggability, achieving SOTA on CrossDocked2020.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Structure-based drug design with LLM agents must jointly optimize binding affinity and druggability, two objectives that frequently conflict. Existing pipelines often fail because agents propose edits without understanding how local ligand modifications affect both objectives in the context of the protein pocket. The authors want to quantify this difficulty, diagnose failure modes of current approaches, and design an optimization method that can systematically explore and exploit local structure–activity relationships, akin to how medicinal chemists work with analog series.

Method: 1) Define two diagnostic metrics: (a) the rate at which a single molecular edit improves both affinity and druggability, and (b) the rate at which improving one objective hurts the other, to quantify objective conflict. 2) Apply these diagnostics to existing LLM-agent ligand optimization pipelines to reveal that they rarely achieve joint improvements because they lack local edit–response awareness. 3) Propose PROBE, an optimization framework that: (i) decomposes ligands into editable sites, (ii) constructs a pocket-specific site map that labels sites by their potential for joint improvement, tradeoffs, or liability, and (iii) performs systematic probe edits to measure how the pocket–ligand complex responds at each site. 4) Distill probe-edit responses into an EditManual that encodes local SAR (structure–activity relationships). 5) Use a multi-agent loop with three specialized LLM agents—one for affinity, one for druggability, and one for co-optimization—guided by the site map and EditManual to iteratively propose and refine ligand edits.

Result: On the CrossDocked2020 benchmark, PROBE reaches state-of-the-art performance for structure-based ligand optimization, outperforming prior LLM-agent pipelines on combined affinity and druggability metrics. The diagnostic metrics show that PROBE substantially increases the frequency of edits that jointly improve both objectives and reduces the rate of edits that improve one objective at the expense of the other, thus mitigating the previously observed failure modes.

Conclusion: By explicitly probing how local ligand edits affect both binding affinity and druggability within a specific protein pocket, and by organizing this information into a site map and EditManual used by specialized LLM agents, PROBE addresses a core limitation of current LLM-based SBDD workflows. The framework improves multi-objective ligand optimization and demonstrates that edit-response probing—an approach inspired by medicinal chemistry practice—is an effective strategy for achieving balanced, drug-like designs in automated pipelines.

Abstract: Structure-based drug design increasingly employs LLM agents to iteratively refine ligands against a target pocket, yet a viable ligand must satisfy two often-conflicting objectives -- binding affinity and druggability -- which single optimization steps rarely improve together. To quantify this difficulty, we introduce two diagnostic metrics: the first measures how often a single edit improves both objectives, and the second measures how often a gain on one objective comes with a loss on the other. Applying these diagnostics to current LLM-agent pipelines exposes a consistent failure mode: the agent performs molecular editing without knowing how the pocket-ligand complex responds to local modifications, thus rarely achieving joint improvement. Inspired by medicinal chemists, who probe the pocket-ligand complex with controlled analog edits before choosing an optimization direction, we propose \textbf{PROBE}, an optimization framework built around edit-response probing. PROBE first decomposes the ligand into editable sites and builds a pocket-specific \textbf{site map} that flags where joint gains are plausible, where the two objectives are likely in tension, and where liability substructures should be changed; it then performs controlled probe edits whose responses are distilled into an \textbf{EditManual}. Guided by the site map and EditManual, PROBE runs an iterative multi-agent loop in which an affinity agent, a druggability agent, and a co-optimization agent jointly produce edits. On the CrossDocked2020 benchmark, PROBE achieves state-of-the-art performance and substantially mitigates the failure modes exposed by our diagnostics metrics.

</details>


### [225] [PropLLM: Propagation-Aware Scene Reconstruction for Network Fault Diagnosis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00582)
*Zongzong Wu,Ming Zhao,Fengxiao Tang,Nei Kato*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces PropLLM, a hop-by-hop, knowledge-graph‑guided LLM framework for network fault diagnosis that reconstructs causal propagation paths instead of mapping alerts to causes in a single step, achieving better accuracy and less hallucination on real Wi‑Fi and 5G datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In complex networks, faults propagate through multiple layers of topology and protocols, but operators only see the final symptomatic alerts. Different root causes can produce nearly identical end-point symptoms, creating ambiguity that existing rule-based, ML-based, and LLM-based methods cannot resolve because they perform a single-pass mapping from alert sets to diagnoses without reconstructing the underlying propagation chain. There is a need for a method that can reason backward along causal paths and disambiguate root causes using structured, verifiable evidence.

Method: The authors propose PropLLM, a framework that combines hop-by-hop propagation reasoning with LLMs and a dual-layer knowledge graph. Starting from end-point alerts, the system iteratively traces backward along potential propagation paths. At each hop, it queries a dual-layer KG (capturing both topology and protocol/semantic relations) to retrieve factual evidence about possible fault states and dependencies. A novel Temporal Causal Propagation Attention (TCPA) mechanism injects known causal priors—such as directionality along the network topology and temporal order—directly into the attention computations of the LLM, biasing it to follow plausible causal directions instead of arbitrary correlations. Through this iterative process, PropLLM builds an explicit, evidenced causal chain from symptoms to candidate root causes and uses the LLM’s generative reasoning over the KG-derived context to classify the fault type and localize the root cause.

Result: On a real-world multimodal Wi-Fi fault dataset, PropLLM improves fault-type diagnosis accuracy by 3.9% and root-cause localization accuracy by 4.7% compared with the strongest baseline while cutting hallucination rate by 50.8%. Experiments on the TeleLogs 5G dataset show similar benefits, indicating robustness and generality of the approach across different network environments.

Conclusion: By shifting from single-pass alert-to-diagnosis mapping to hop-by-hop propagation reasoning grounded in a dual-layer knowledge graph and guided by Temporal Causal Propagation Attention, PropLLM can more accurately identify fault types and root causes in complex networks and substantially reduce hallucinations. The method generalizes across Wi-Fi and 5G settings, demonstrating that explicitly modeling causal propagation chains with LLM support is an effective strategy for network fault diagnosis.

Abstract: Network faults propagate layer by layer along topology and protocol dependencies, yet operations systems typically observe only symptomatic alerts at the tail end of propagation chains, where distinct root-cause faults may produce highly similar end-point symptoms. Existing approaches, whether rule-based, machine learning (ML)-based, or large language model (LLM)-based, fundamentally map the alert set to a diagnosis in a single pass and are structurally incapable of resolving this end-point ambiguity. This paper proposes PropLLM, which is the first to integrate the hop-by-hop scene reconstruction paradigm with the generative reasoning capabilities of LLMs. Starting from end-point alerts, PropLLM traces back hop-by-hop along the propagation path, retrieving verifiable factual evidence from a dual-layer knowledge graph (KG) at each hop, while the proposed Temporal Causal Propagation Attention (TCPA) mechanism encodes known topological causal priors directly into the attention computation to guide the model along the correct causal direction, ultimately localizing the root cause and determining the fault type through a fully evidenced causal chain. On a real-world Wi-Fi multimodal fault dataset, PropLLM improves fault type diagnosis accuracy by 3.9\% and root cause localization accuracy by 4.7\% over the strongest baseline, while reducing the hallucination rate by 50.8\%. Supplementary experiments on the TeleLogs 5G dataset further demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method across different network scenarios.

</details>


### [226] [TRACE: Trajectory Risk-Aware Compression for Long-Horizon Agent Safety](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00611)
*Zhepei Hong,Lin Wang,Liting Li,Haokai Ma,Junfeng Fang,Fei Shen,Dan Zhang,Xiang Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TRACE is a trajectory-level safety detection framework for long-horizon LLM agents that compresses whole interaction histories into a compact risk-aware state, enabling more reliable detection of sparse and delayed safety risks than turn-level detectors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon LLM agents operate over long trajectories where safety-relevant signals are sparse, delayed, and compositional. Traditional moderation or safety detectors work at the turn level or within short contexts, and therefore miss risky behaviors that only emerge when aggregating evidence across many steps. There is a need for a method that can retain and integrate safety evidence over extended horizons without losing critical cues.

Method: The authors formulate long-horizon safety detection as a trajectory-level evidence compression problem. They introduce TRACE, which uses a Compressor-Reader architecture. The Compressor takes the full agent trajectory and encodes it into a compact latent evidence state supervised at the trajectory level. The Reader then evaluates the raw trajectory while conditioning on this latent evidence state as a safety reference, enabling it to focus on risk-relevant parts of the trajectory and to aggregate dispersed risk cues. They analyze attention patterns and conduct case studies to understand how the compressed evidence guides the Reader.

Result: On benchmark suites including ASSEBench, Pre-Ex-Bench, and R-Judge, TRACE consistently outperforms strong baseline safety detectors, achieving up to a 12.6 percentage-point gain in accuracy across multiple backbone models. On the LongSafety benchmark, TRACE experiences less performance degradation as the context length increases, indicating better robustness to long trajectories. Qualitative analyses and attention visualizations show that the model concentrates attention on risk-critical segments and can reconstruct cross-step evidence from the compressed state.

Conclusion: Reframing long-horizon safety detection as trajectory-level evidence compression and implementing it via the TRACE Compressor-Reader architecture yields more accurate and robust safety judgments for long-horizon LLM agents. TRACE better preserves and aggregates sparse, delayed, and compositional risk signals across long contexts than existing turn-level or short-context detectors, making it a promising direction for scalable safety monitoring of autonomous LLM agents.

Abstract: Long-horizon LLM agents produce safety evidence across long trajectories, where sparse, delayed, and compositional risk signals often escape local moderation. Existing turn-level or short-context detectors struggle to reliably retain and aggregate such evidence over extended horizons. We reframe long-horizon agent safety detection as trajectory-level evidence compression and propose Trajectory Risk-Aware Compression for Long-Horizon Agent Safety (TRACE). TRACE uses a Compressor-Reader design: the Compressor encodes the full trajectory into a compact latent evidence state under trajectory-level supervision, and the Reader judges the raw trajectory with this latent evidence state as a safety reference. This design helps aggregate dispersed risk cues and reduce premature evidence loss. Across ASSEBench, Pre-Ex-Bench, and R-Judge, TRACE achieves the best accuracy on all evaluated backbones, improving over strong baselines by up to 12.6 percentage points. On LongSafety, TRACE shows smaller performance degradation as context length grows. Attention visualizations and case studies suggest that the compressed reference helps the Reader focus on risk-critical segments and recover cross-step evidence. Code is available at https://github.com/Peregrine123/TRACE_official.

</details>


### [227] [Efficient Test-time Inference for Generative Planning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00618)
*Robert Gieselmann,Mihai Samson,Federico Pecora,Jeremy L. Wyatt*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes an efficient inference-time search method that integrates generative and heuristic models within a modified Open-Closed List framework, achieving better solution quality and computational efficiency in combinatorial planning than existing neurosymbolic and classical solvers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative models used for AI planning are limited by their training data distribution and often require heavy test-time computation to refine solutions. There is a need for a more efficient way to leverage these models at inference time without simply scaling compute, particularly for combinatorial planning tasks where search quality and speed are both critical.

Method: The authors modify the classical Open-Closed List (OCL) search framework to tightly integrate two learned components: (1) a generative model that quickly rolls out trajectories from intermediate states to propose candidate reasoning paths, and (2) a heuristic model that scores and prioritizes these candidates. They introduce new mechanisms for exploration control—likely balancing breadth vs. depth and model guidance vs. search diversity—so that the OCL search can exploit learned models while avoiding overcommitment to any single trajectory.

Result: On multiple combinatorial planning benchmarks, the proposed OCL-based method achieves higher-quality plans and better computational efficiency than both neurosymbolic search baselines and traditional symbolic planning solvers. This indicates that their integration of generative and heuristic models into OCL search yields practical improvements over current approaches.

Conclusion: A carefully designed inference-time search procedure, based on a modified Open-Closed List framework and powered by learned generative and heuristic models, can overcome some of the distributional limits of generative planners and produce better, faster solutions in combinatorial planning tasks than existing neurosymbolic and classical methods.

Abstract: Generative models have emerged as a powerful paradigm for AI planning, yet their performance remains constrained by the training data distribution. One approach is to improve generated solutions during inference by scaling test-time compute. A more efficient alternative is to optimize the inference process itself. In this paper, we show that a modified version of a classical Open-Closed List (OCL) search provides just such an efficient inference procedure. Our algorithm synergizes two learned components: a generative model that performs fast rollouts from intermediate states and a heuristic model that prioritizes among candidate reasoning paths. Key contributions include novel exploration control mechanisms and integration of learned models within the OCL framework. Across multiple combinatorial planning domains, our approach outperforms both neurosymbolic search baselines and classical solvers in computational efficiency and solution quality.

</details>


### [228] [Hidden Thoughts Are Not Secret: Reasoning Trace Exposure in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00642)
*Yu-An Lu,Ci-Yang Tsai,Yu-Lin Tsai,Raluca Ada Popa,Chia-Mu Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies whether hiding internal reasoning traces of reasoning models actually prevents users from obtaining useful reasoning supervision, and introduces Reasoning Exposure Prompting (REP) to elicit such traces via in-context prompts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many systems with reasoning-capable language models hide raw chain-of-thought or reasoning traces, exposing only summaries or final answers, in an attempt to prevent users from extracting detailed reasoning supervision. However, it is unclear if this interface-level hiding is sufficient to stop users from reverse-engineering or eliciting these internal traces in a way that they can still use for distillation or capability transfer.

Method: The authors propose Reasoning Exposure Prompting (REP), an in-context prompting technique that uses demonstrations generated by a shadow model, formatted in auxiliary code-like structures, to coax a target (victim) model into revealing reasoning traces that are similar to its internal (hidden) reasoning. They evaluate REP across standard reasoning datasets, with different victim models, and then use the exposed traces to distill into weaker student models, comparing these to baselines that lack such exposure.

Result: REP significantly increases the similarity between the internal hidden reasoning traces and the externally exposed traces produced under REP prompts, demonstrating that the technique can effectively surface latent reasoning behavior. At the same time, the exposed traces remain sufficiently informative to enable successful distillation into student models, outperforming setups where only answers or superficial explanations are available.

Conclusion: Interface-level hiding of reasoning traces is not sufficient to prevent extraction of useful reasoning supervision. With REP, adversaries or power-users can elicit user-visible traces that closely approximate internal chain-of-thought and can be leveraged for capability transfer. This suggests that current trace-hiding practices may provide a weaker safety and IP-protection barrier than anticipated, and that more robust mitigations will be needed if preventing reasoning supervision is a goal.

Abstract: Reasoning traces have become a valuable form of learning signals for improving and transferring the capabilities of large language models. In particular, detailed traces can help distill reasoning behavior from stronger teacher models into weaker student models. The value of capability transfer has motivated many deployed systems with reasoning models to hide raw internal traces and expose at most summaries and answers to users. As a result, we ask whether such interface-level trace hiding prevents users from obtaining useful reasoning supervision through prompting. We study this question with Reasoning Exposure Prompting (REP), a lightweight in-context elicitation method that uses shadow-model-generated demonstrations wrapped in auxiliary code-like formats to raise user-visible reasoning traces from a victim model. Across the common reasoning dataset, different victim models, and different student model distillation, REP substantially increases similarity between exposed and REP-conditioned internal traces while preserving useful reasoning signals.

</details>


### [229] [ForeSci: Evaluating LLM Agents for Forward-Looking AI Research Judgment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00644)
*Qiuyu Tian,Zequn Liu,Yingce Xia,Haojie Yin,Youyong Kong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces ForeSci, a benchmark to test whether LLM-based research agents can make forward-looking AI research decisions using only historically available evidence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI research often requires strategic, forward-looking decisions (e.g., which bottleneck to tackle or direction to pursue) before future evidence is available. Existing evaluations rarely test whether LLMs can make such prospective research judgments in a controlled way. There is a need for a benchmark that isolates decision-making quality about future research trajectories, using only contemporaneous information, and validates it against what actually happened later.

Method: The authors construct ForeSci, a benchmark of 500 tasks spanning four rapidly evolving AI subdomains and four types of research decisions. For each task, they define a temporal cutoff and build an offline, cutoff-aligned knowledge base; papers published after the cutoff are excluded from the model’s accessible evidence and are used only to define the ground-truth outcomes. Tasks are generated from pre-cutoff taxonomies and evidence signals to avoid pure future-event guessing. They evaluate several systems—native LLMs, Hybrid Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), and three adapted research-agent architectures—across four different LLM backbones, and analyze both decision quality and evidence usage patterns.

Result: Explicit evidence organization in agent pipelines leads to better traceability and stronger factual grounding in the agents’ outputs. However, performance gains vary significantly across the four decision families, indicating that some kinds of forward-looking judgments are more amenable to LLM assistance than others. Diagnostic analysis uncovers a recurring pattern of evidence-decision decoupling, where agents retrieve and correctly cite relevant prior work but still forecast the wrong future research objects or directions.

Conclusion: ForeSci operationalizes forward-looking AI research judgment as a controlled, temporally grounded benchmark. It shows that while structured evidence handling improves transparency and factual support, current research agents often fail to translate relevant historical evidence into accurate prospective decisions. The benchmark thus provides a systematic way to evaluate and improve LLM-based research agents as decision-making systems, especially for tasks that require reasoning under temporal uncertainty.

Abstract: AI research often requires decisions before future evidence exists: which bottleneck to attack, which direction to pursue, or where a project should be positioned. We introduce ForeSci, a temporally controlled benchmark for evaluating whether LLM agents can make such forward-looking research judgements from historical evidence. ForeSci contains 500 tasks across four fast-moving AI domains and four decision families. Each task is paired with a cutoff-aligned offline knowledge base; post-cutoff papers are hidden during generation and used only for validation. To avoid random future-event prediction, tasks are derived from pre-cutoff taxonomy branches and evidence signals, and answer-generation backbones are selected to precede the task cutoffs. We evaluate native LLMs, Hybrid RAG, and three research-agent adaptations across four backbones. Results show that explicit evidence organization improves traceability and factual support, but gains depend strongly on the decision family. Diagnostics reveal a recurring evidence-decision decoupling: agents may cite relevant evidence while forecasting the wrong research object. ForeSci turns forward-looking AI research judgement into a controlled benchmark for evaluating research agents as decision-making systems.

</details>


### [230] [AXIOM: A Trust-First Neuro-Symbolic Execution Architecture for Verifiable Mathematical Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00671)
*Alessio Bruno*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AXIOM is a neuro-symbolic math reasoning system where an LLM only canonicalizes problems into structured schemas that a deterministic CAS solves or abstains on, achieving high accuracy with zero confident-wrong answers and a scalable, regression-free routing design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based math solvers are often untrustworthy: they can produce fluent but wrong answers, suffer regressions when extended, and lack clear abstention behavior. The authors aim to build a math reasoning architecture that is trustworthy in production: no confident-wrong answers, predictable extension as new problem types are added, low latency, and a systematic way to improve coverage from real usage logs.

Method: AXIOM uses a trust-first neuro-symbolic execution architecture. The LLM is constrained to act purely as a canonicalizer, rewriting informal natural-language problems into a narrow, structured schema. Problems are routed by a 1:1:1 mapping between (i) a regex-defined problem shape, (ii) a schema-specific prompt to the LLM, and (iii) a closed-form CAS handler that deterministically derives and verifies the answer or abstains. The system includes more than 3,100 such routes; each new route is added under an operational discipline involving math-template bucketing of problems, LOST_CORRECT scans as a regression oracle, parseable-first onboarding (only admitting problem types that can be reliably parsed), and treating abstention as a first-class output. This yields an extensible registry of handlers without regressions on previously-correct cases.

Result: On four categories of the MATH benchmark, AXIOM attains 94.36% correctness (2,592/2,747) on parseable items with 100% trust, meaning zero confident-wrong answers across all 2,747 records; all four categories exceed a 70/90/70 performance floor with 100% per-domain trust. On the lm-eval arithmetic 20,000-record benchmark, 88% of records are handled by rule-only routes with a median latency of 1 ms. The system has also been deployed publicly and has served about 30,000 production queries.

Conclusion: AXIOM demonstrates that a trust-first neuro-symbolic architecture, where an LLM is restricted to canonicalization and a deterministic CAS performs reasoning and verification with abstention, can achieve high accuracy and zero confident-wrong answers at scale. The key contribution is not the raw accuracy, but an operational dynamic: each abstention logged in production becomes a candidate for conversion to a correct, handled pattern in subsequent iterations, while the design guarantees no regressions on previously-correct routes. The practices enabling this—template bucketing, LOST_CORRECT regression scanning, parseable-first onboarding, and abstention as a first-class outcome—provide a generalizable blueprint for building trustworthy neuro-symbolic systems beyond mathematics.

Abstract: We present AXIOM, a trust-first neuro-symbolic execution architecture for natural-language mathematical reasoning. In AXIOM, the language model functions strictly as a canonicalizer: it rewrites informal problem text into a narrow schema consumed by a deterministic Computer-Algebra-System (CAS) pipeline, which derives and verifies the answer or abstains as a first-class output. Routing follows a 1:1:1 alignment between problem-shape regex, schema-specific prompt, and closed-form CAS handler, with 3,100+ such routes shipped and zero LOST_CORRECT regressions across 250+ consecutive ship commits. We report empirical results on 4 MATH categories with a cumulative correctness of 94.36% (2,592/2,747) at 100.00% trust on parseable (zero confident-wrong answers across the full 2,747-record benchmark), all four domains above the per-domain 70/90/70 floor with per-domain trust at 100.0%, and median latency of 1 ms on rule-only handlers (88% of records on the lm-eval arithmetic 20,000-record benchmark). The architecture has served ~30,000 production queries through a public deployment. The contribution we emphasize is not a final accuracy figure but the forward dynamic the architecture establishes: every logged abstain in production is a candidate correct after one ship cycle, since new tasks compose without regressing the registry. The operational discipline behind this property -- math-template bucketing, LOST_CORRECT scan as regression oracle, parseable-first onboarding, and abstain as first-class output -- constitutes a transferable framework for trustworthy neuro-symbolic systems beyond mathematics.

</details>


### [231] [Medication-Aware Financial Exploitation Detection for Alzheimer's Patients Using Edge-Aware Interaction Risk Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00672)
*Farzana Akter,Lisan Al Amin,Rakib Hossain,Chaitanya Gunupudi,Faisal Quader*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Medication-aware fraud detection framework for people with Alzheimer’s, using adherence data to better flag risky financial events during vulnerability windows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: People with Alzheimer’s disease are at high risk of financial exploitation, especially when their cognitive function fluctuates due to medication adherence issues. Existing fraud detection models mostly look only at transaction patterns and ignore clinical or medication-related context that can signal periods of heightened vulnerability. There is a need to integrate health-related information, such as whether a patient is taking medications as prescribed, into financial monitoring to more accurately detect risky transactions at the right times.

Method: The authors design a medication-aware framework that synchronizes medication adherence data with transaction-level financial monitoring. They construct a hybrid simulation dataset for 180 patients over 45 days, resulting in detailed medication records and transactions. The framework computes several transaction features—amount anomaly, vendor novelty, transaction frequency, and time deviation—and augments them with medication adherence information. They test three logistic regression models: (1) financial-only, (2) additive medication-aware (medication as an extra independent feature), and (3) interaction-aware (medication interacting with financial features to modulate risk). Performance is evaluated via F1-score, recall during medication-induced vulnerability windows, and average precision for ranking high-risk cases.

Result: On overall global performance, the financial-only baseline model achieves the highest F1-score of 0.5000. However, the interaction-aware model notably improves recall in periods when patients are more vulnerable due to medication issues, raising it from 0.7442 to 0.9070. This interaction-aware model also yields the best average precision when ranking high-risk financial events, indicating superior prioritization of the most concerning cases, even if the global F1 is not highest.

Conclusion: Medication adherence data adds the most value when it is used to modulate or contextualize traditional financial risk features rather than acting as a standalone predictor. While classic financial-only models may keep slightly better overall balance (global F1), integrating medication-financial interactions substantially improves detection of risky events during cognitively vulnerable periods and enhances the ranking of high-risk transactions. This supports the design of fraud detection systems that dynamically adapt risk thresholds based on clinical context for individuals with Alzheimer’s disease.

Abstract: Financial exploitation is a growing concern for people with Alzheimer's disease, especially during periods of reduced cognitive stability. Conventional fraud detection systems usually rely on financial behavior alone and ignore clinically relevant factors that may alter vulnerability. This paper proposes a medication-aware framework that synchronizes medication adherence with transaction-level monitoring to improve detection of cognitively risky financial events. A hybrid simulation dataset was constructed for 180 patients across 45 days, producing 8,100 medication records and 30,855 transactions. The framework evaluates amount anomaly, vendor novelty, transaction frequency, time deviation, and medication adherence through financial-only, additive medication-aware, and interaction-aware logistic models. Results show that the financial-only baseline obtained the highest global F1-score of 0.5000, but the interaction-aware model improved recall during medication-induced vulnerability windows from 0.7442 to 0.9070 and achieved the highest average precision for ranked high-risk cases. The findings suggest that medication adherence is most useful as a contextual modifier of financial risk rather than as an isolated predictor.

</details>


### [232] [Regularized Offline Policy Optimization with Posterior Hybrid Bayesian Belief](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00680)
*Hongqiang Lin,Pengfei Wang,Nenggan Zheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes PhyB, a new Bayesian offline reinforcement learning method that approximates expectations over dynamics models using a convex combination of a subset of models, enabling scalable and theoretically grounded policy optimization with state-of-the-art empirical performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline RL struggles with epistemic uncertainty from limited data coverage and ambiguity in learning transition dynamics. Bayesian RL offers a unified way to model this uncertainty via a belief over dynamics, but practical policy optimization is computationally hard due to expectations over this belief. Existing methods either scale poorly or make restrictive posterior assumptions, limiting adaptability. The paper aims to keep the flexibility of Bayesian RL while making policy optimization tractable and scalable.

Method: The authors introduce Posterior Hybrid Bayesian Belief (PhyB), which approximates the expectation over the posterior of dynamics models by a convex combination over a carefully chosen subset of models. They theoretically analyze the approximation error and show that the discrepancy between the true Bayesian objective and the PhyB surrogate is bounded. On top of this surrogate, they design an iterative regularized policy optimization algorithm that leverages the PhyB representation and provides guarantees for monotonic policy improvement, regardless of the evaluation metric used, until convergence.

Result: Theoretical results show that the PhyB approximation induces a bounded objective discrepancy and supports metric-agnostic monotonic improvement guarantees for the proposed policy optimization scheme. Empirically, across various offline RL benchmarks, PhyB outperforms prior methods, achieving state-of-the-art performance while being computationally more scalable than search-based Bayesian RL approaches and more flexible than methods that rely on restrictive posterior assumptions.

Conclusion: PhyB offers a practical and theoretically sound approach to Bayesian offline RL by approximating expectations over dynamics models with a convex combination over a subset of models. This yields a tractable policy optimization algorithm with bounded approximation error and monotonic improvement guarantees. Experiments indicate that PhyB achieves superior performance to existing methods, demonstrating that the approach effectively manages epistemic uncertainty in offline RL while remaining computationally efficient and adaptable.

Abstract: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) aims to optimize policies from pre-collected datasets. A bottleneck of this paradigm is managing epistemic uncertainty, which arises from limited data coverage (sample-level) and the ambiguity in identifying transition dynamics from finite data (model-level). To provide a unified quantification of these uncertainties, Bayesian RL has been proposed by treating the dynamics model as a random variable and maintaining a corresponding belief. Despite its theoretical appeal, policy optimization in Bayesian RL remains computationally challenging as it requires solving composite objectives with expectations. Prior methods either employ search-based techniques with poor computational scalability or impose restrictive posterior assumptions that sacrifice the adaptability of Bayesian RL. To address these limitations, we propose Posterior Hybrid Bayesian Belief (PhyB), which reformulates the expectation as a convex combination over a subset of dynamics models. Theoretical analysis demonstrates that the objective discrepancy induced by this approximation remains bounded. Based on PhyB, we develop an iterative regularized policy optimization algorithm that provides metric-agnostic guarantees for monotonic improvement until convergence. Empirical results demonstrate that PhyB achieves state-of-the-art performance on various benchmarks.

</details>


### [233] [MOSAIC: Modular Orchestration for Structured Agentic Intelligence and Composition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00708)
*Yifan Bao,Xinyu Xi,Xinyu Liu,Wen Ge,Lei Jiang,Kevin Zhang,Raad Khraishi,Yihao Ang,Anthony K. H. Tung,Lukasz Szpruch,Hao Ni*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MOSAIC is a structured agentic framework that treats automated data science as an explicit, reusable model-selection and workflow-construction problem, improving performance, reliability, and traceability over AutoML and unstructured LLM agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AutoML systems search over fixed pipelines and hyperparameters, limiting flexibility, while emerging LLM-based agents are flexible but make ad-hoc, opaque, and hard-to-reuse modelling decisions. There is a need for a framework that combines the flexibility of agentic LLM systems with the structure, verifiability, and reusability of traditional automated model selection.

Method: MOSAIC uses an agentic, modular orchestration framework. Given a task and dataset, it: (1) builds a semantic task profile; (2) retrieves relevant prior cases and code modules from memory; (3) constructs a blueprint — an intermediate representation that specifies modelling components, their composition, constraints, and execution requirements; (4) grounds LLM-based code generation in this retrieved evidence and blueprint; and (5) validates and iteratively refines candidate models using execution traces, diagnostics, task metrics, and a failure-aware reinforcement learning policy.

Result: On financial time-series forecasting and generation tasks, MOSAIC outperforms both AutoML baselines and LLM-based agentic baselines in predictive accuracy, distributional fidelity, execution reliability, and downstream financial metrics such as risk and tail behavior. It also yields higher execution success rates and more interpretable, traceable decision processes.

Conclusion: Treating automated data science as a structured, memory-grounded model selection and workflow orchestration problem—via explicit blueprints and modular components—enables more effective, reliable, and auditable automation than either conventional AutoML or unstructured LLM agents. MOSAIC demonstrates that structured, execution-grounded agentic frameworks can significantly advance automated modelling in domains like financial time series.

Abstract: Automated data science is a structured model-selection problem. A solution must choose data transformations, feature representations, architecture, training procedure, evaluation protocol, and refinement strategy for a task. AutoML systems automate parts of this process, but typically search within predefined pipeline, model, and hyperparameter spaces. LLM-based agents offer greater flexibility through retrieval, code generation, and execution feedback, yet their modelling decisions are often unstructured, difficult to verify, and hard to reuse. We introduce \textsc{MOSAIC} (Modular Orchestration for Structured Agentic Intelligence and Composition), a structured agentic framework for memory-grounded model selection and workflow construction. Given a task and dataset, \textsc{MOSAIC} builds a semantic task profile, retrieves prior cases and source-code modules, and constructs a blueprint: an intermediate representation specifying selected modelling components, composition, interface constraints, and execution requirements. This blueprint turns model selection into a staged, context-grounded search and grounds LLM-based code generation in retrieved evidence rather than unconstrained synthesis. Candidate models are validated by execution and refined using diagnostic feedback, training traces, task metrics, and a failure-aware reinforcement learning policy. We instantiate \textsc{MOSAIC} on financial time-series forecasting and generation, where models must satisfy predictive accuracy, distributional fidelity, execution reliability, and downstream financial criteria such as risk and tail behaviour. Experiments against AutoML and agentic baselines show that \textsc{MOSAIC} improves task performance, execution success, and decision traceability, demonstrating the value of treating automated data science as structured, reusable, and execution-grounded model selection.

</details>


### [234] [LLM-Driven Co-Evolutionary Automated Heuristic Design for Bi-Component Coupled Combinatorial Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00718)
*Mingen Kuang,Xudong Deng,Xi Lin,Ye Fan,Jianyong Sun,Jialong Shi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose CoEvo-AHD, an LLM-driven co-evolutionary framework that jointly designs multiple coupled heuristics (e.g., for routing and item/merchant selection), achieving competitive performance on coupled combinatorial problems like TTP and TPP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based automated heuristic design methods typically evolve a single heuristic operator or search strategy in isolation. This is inadequate for coupled combinatorial optimization problems (e.g., TTP, TPP) where multiple decision components (such as routing and selection) are strongly interconnected, and their interactions critically affect solution quality. There is a need for an AHD framework that can explicitly model and exploit the coupling between multiple decision substructures, while also making it easier for LLMs to generate efficient, correct, and reusable heuristic code.

Method: They introduce CoEvo-AHD, a dual-population co-evolutionary framework guided by an LLM. Instead of evolving a single heuristic, CoEvo-AHD maintains and co-evolves two related populations of operators (e.g., route operators and selection operators). A cooperative evaluation mechanism scores operators based on their performance when paired, explicitly modeling inter-operator interactions. Pairwise scoring and a synergistic joint crossover are used to encourage the emergence of complementary operator logic across the two populations. Additionally, they provide a tool-invocation environment library that wraps common low-level operations (such as local-search delta evaluations) into callable tools. LLM-generated heuristics interact with these standardized tools rather than implementing problem-specific loops, thereby improving robustness and efficiency.

Result: On benchmark instances of the Traveling Thief Problem and the Traveling Purchaser Problem, CoEvo-AHD is able to automatically discover sets of cooperative heuristics (route and selection operators that work well together). The designed heuristics achieve solution quality that is competitive with, or comparable to, strong traditional hand-crafted heuristics reported in the literature.

Conclusion: Jointly evolving interacting heuristic components with LLM guidance is an effective approach for coupled combinatorial optimization. By using a dual-population co-evolutionary scheme plus a standardized tool-invocation environment, CoEvo-AHD can automatically discover complementary operators and produce competitive solutions, demonstrating that modeling inter-operator coupling is beneficial compared to evolving single heuristics in isolation.

Abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have recently shown promise in Automated Heuristic Design (AHD), existing methods typically generate and evolve heuristics as a single operator or search strategy, limiting their ability to model strong coupling among multiple decision substructures in problems such as the Traveling Thief Problem (TTP) and the Traveling Purchaser Problem (TPP). In this work, we propose CoEvo-AHD, an LLM-driven dual-population co-evolutionary framework for automated heuristic design in coupled combinatorial optimization. Unlike prior methods that evolve individual heuristics in isolation, CoEvo-AHD leverages LLMs to co-evolve two closely related operator populations. A cooperative evaluation mechanism explicitly captures interactions between route and selection operators, while pairwise scoring and synergistic joint crossover help discover complementary operator logic for joint improvement across coupled decision subspaces. We further design a tool-invocation environment library that encapsulates frequently used core operations, such as local-search delta computation, into callable functions, enabling LLM-generated operators to use standardized interfaces instead of reimplementing inefficient and error-prone problem-specific loops. Experiments on TTP and TPP show that CoEvo-AHD automatically discovers cooperative heuristic combinations and achieves competitive solution quality against traditional heuristics.

</details>


### [235] [Latent Reward Steering: An Adaptive Inference-Time Framework that Implicitly Promotes Cognitive Behaviors in Reasoning LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00726)
*Jiakang Li,Guanyu Zhu,Can Jin,Chenxi Huang,Dexu Yu,Ronghao Chen,Yang Zhou,Hongwu Peng,Xuanqi Lan,Dimitris N. Metaxas,Youhua Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Latent Reward Steering (LRS) adaptively steers an LLM’s internal SAE latents during reasoning using a learned reward model, improving reasoning accuracy and implicitly encouraging better cognitive behaviors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reasoning quality in LLMs depends not just on stored knowledge but on how internal cognitive behaviors unfold during generation. Existing control methods operate at the explicit behavior level (e.g., hard-coded chains-of-thought styles or manually chosen steering vectors) and are inflexible when different tasks, models, or intermediate reasoning states require different corrections. There is a need for an adaptive, state-specific mechanism that can automatically detect and correct fragile reasoning states without relying on predefined cognitive behavior templates.

Method: The paper introduces Latent Reward Steering (LRS), which operates on the latent space of a sparse autoencoder (SAE) trained on model activations. First, they collect reasoning traces labeled by final-answer correctness and train a latent reward model that maps SAE latent states to a scalar reward estimating the quality of those intermediate states. At inference time, they (1) encode the model’s current hidden activations into SAE latents, (2) use the reward model to estimate the quality and its gradient with respect to the latents, and (3) adjust the latents along the reward gradient to improve the predicted quality, but only when a reward-and-confidence gate detects that the state is fragile (low reward and sufficient confidence). The modified latents are then decoded back into activations and fed forward to continue generation. This yields adaptive, state-specific steering without explicitly defining cognitive behaviors or steering vectors.

Result: Across multiple reasoning-oriented LLM backbones and benchmarks, LRS consistently improves reasoning performance compared to baseline inference-time methods and steering approaches. Empirical results show higher final-answer accuracy and robustness. Post-hoc analyses of the latent dynamics and resulting traces suggest that LRS implicitly encourages beneficial cognitive behaviors—such as better error checking or self-correction—that repair reasoning failures present in the unsteered model.

Conclusion: LRS demonstrates that optimizing internal SAE latent states via a learned reward model is an effective, adaptive way to steer LLM reasoning at inference time. By focusing on latent reward signals tied to answer correctness rather than hand-designed cognitive protocols, the method can provide state-specific corrections and generalize across models and tasks. This supports the view that cognitive behaviors can be productively influenced through latent-space interventions guided by outcome-based supervision, improving overall reasoning reliability.

Abstract: Strong reasoning depends not only on model knowledge but also on how effectively cognitive behaviors are deployed during generation. Existing methods often rely on explicit behavior-level control, making them insufficiently adaptive when failures and required corrections vary across reasoning states, tasks, and models. To this end, we propose Latent Reward Steering (LRS), an adaptive inference-time framework that promotes cognitive behaviors by optimizing the sparse-autoencoder (SAE) latent states that implicitly carry them. Rather than relying on predefined cognitive behaviors or steering directions derived from them, LRS trains a latent reward model on reasoning traces by final answer correctness to estimate the quality of intermediate latent states. During inference, reward gradients provide state-specific correction directions for fragile latent states, while a reward and confidence gate restricts intervention to states the reward signal flags as fragile. Experiments on multiple reasoning LLM backbones and benchmarks show that \ours consistently improves performance over various baselines, and post-hoc analyses further indicate that \ours implicitly promotes good cognitive behaviors that fix the original reasoning errors. Code is available at: https://github.com/jiakanglee/Latent-Reward-Steering.

</details>


### [236] [AI Sovereignty as National Learning Capacity: A Human-Centered Learning Mechanics Viewpoint on France, the United States, and China](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00729)
*Kim Phuc Tran*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper reframes France’s AI policy as managing a national AI learning system, governed by the balance between information injection and entropy dissipation, and derives a mathematical and policy framework from this view.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Debates on AI in France are fragmented (investment, regulation, sovereignty, jobs, education) and polarized (techno-optimism vs. regulation-first). The authors want a unified, quantitative, and strategically useful framework to understand and guide national AI development and sovereignty.

Method: The authors adapt the Human-Centered Learning Mechanics (HCLM) framework—an entropy-regulated representation learning model—to the scale of a nation. They formally map macro elements (compute, capital, talent, regulation, organizational complexity, etc.) onto information injection and entropy dissipation terms. They link this to neural scaling laws, endogenous growth, creative destruction, and game theory, build a dynamical mathematical model, define measurable policy indicators, run illustrative simulations of different national AI regimes, and derive game-theoretic propositions about strategic interactions.

Result: They obtain a dynamical model where a country’s AI trajectory depends on the controlled balance between growth in information injection (compute, data, talent, capital, deployment) and various dissipation mechanisms (coordination frictions, regulatory uncertainty, energy limits, institutional complexity). Simulations demonstrate how different policy regimes lead to stable, unstable, unequal, or overly energy-intensive AI development paths. The model yields quantitative indicators and strategic conditions under which AI sovereignty and competitiveness can be sustained.

Conclusion: AI sovereignty is not determined by scale alone but by a country’s ability to regulate its own information dynamics as an open, non-equilibrium learning system. For France, a competitive and human-centered AI strategy should ensure that information injection persistently outpaces institutional dissipation while preventing instability, inequality, and excessive energy use. The paper recommends reframing AI policy as the governance of a national AI learning system and offers concrete indicators and policy levers aligned with this framework.

Abstract: Artificial Intelligence is often discussed in France in terms of investment, compute capacity, regulation, employment, sovereignty, and education. These dimensions are usually treated separately. This viewpoint paper proposes a unified interpretation: France should be understood as a \emph{national AI learning system}. Building on Human-Centered Learning Mechanics (HCLM), recently formulated as a dynamical framework for entropy-regulated representation learning, we interpret national AI development as a controlled balance between information injection and entropy dissipation. Information injection corresponds to compute, data, talent, research, capital, industrial deployment, and institutional experimentation. Entropy dissipation corresponds to organizational complexity, coordination frictions, energy constraints, regulatory uncertainty, talent mobility pressures, and opportunities to strengthen industrial absorption. The central claim is that AI sovereignty does not emerge from scale alone but from a country's capacity to regulate its own information dynamics. This paper connects HCLM with neural scaling laws, endogenous growth theory, creative destruction, and game theory. It argues that the French AI debate should move beyond the binary opposition between techno-optimism and regulation-first caution. A competitive and human-centered AI strategy requires a controlled regime in which information injection grows faster than institutional dissipation, while avoiding unstable, unequal, or energy-intensive expansion. We provide a mathematical model, measurable policy indicators, game-theoretic propositions, illustrative simulations of national AI regimes, and concrete policy implications for France. The proposed viewpoint reframes AI policy as the governance of an open, strategic, non-equilibrium learning system.

</details>


### [237] [SHARP: Sleep-based Hierarchical Accelerated Replay for Long Range Non-Stationary Temporal Pattern Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00732)
*Jayanta Dey,Shikhar Srivastava,Itamar Lerner,Christopher Kanan,Dhireesha Kudithipudi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces SHARP, a framework for learning long-range, non-stationary temporal patterns in strict streaming settings by separating memory accumulation from pattern recognition and using offline accelerated replay phases inspired by biological sleep.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern sequence models struggle with long-range credit assignment under streaming, single-pass constraints because they rely on truncated backpropagation through time or fixed attention windows. There is a need for models that can efficiently adapt to non-stationary data streams, retain information about distant past events, and continue learning over time without repeatedly revisiting raw past data.

Method: SHARP decomposes temporal learning into (1) a memory module that builds and maintains a structured history of past inputs, and (2) a pattern-recognition module that reads from this memory. It introduces offline 'sleep' phases where the memory module replays temporally structured traces in an accelerated fashion, allowing the pattern-recognition module to integrate them into higher-level representations. The framework is hierarchical, so higher levels summarize longer time scales, leading to an exponentially increasing effective temporal context while keeping computational cost linear in time. The model is evaluated through controlled simulations, ablation studies, and benchmarks (text8, PG-19).

Result: Experiments on simulated tasks and on benchmark language modeling datasets (text8, PG-19) show that SHARP outperforms recurrent baselines in strict streaming settings. It better preserves next-token prediction performance on previously seen data while still learning from the current stream and generalizing to future unseen data. Ablations identify the contribution of the hierarchical memory and offline replay components to these gains.

Conclusion: By separating memory accumulation from pattern recognition and incorporating accelerated offline replay phases, SHARP enables efficient long-range credit assignment in streaming, non-stationary environments. Its hierarchical design provides an exponentially growing temporal context with only linear computational cost, leading to better retention and adaptation compared with standard recurrent baselines.

Abstract: Learning long-range non-stationary temporal patterns remains a core challenge for modern sequence models, particularly in strict streaming settings. In these settings, data arrive sequentially and must be processed in a single pass without simultaneously revisiting past observations.
