Standard architectures, including recurrent neural networks and transformers, are constrained by either truncated backpropagation through time horizon or explicit input window length for long range credit assignment. To address these limitations, we propose SHARP (Sleep-based Hierarchical Accelerated Replay), a framework that decomposes temporal learning into two complementary components: a memory module that accumulates a structured history of past inputs, and a pattern-recognition module that operates over this memory. This separation enables resource- and compute-efficient adaptation to non-stationary dynamics by eliminating the need for backpropagation through time across many steps for long-range credit assignment. Inspired by the accelerated replay observed in rodents during slow-wave sleep, SHARP incorporates offline (sleep) phases in which temporally structured memory traces are replayed in an accelerated form and integrated into higher-level memory representations, improving long-range context retention. Through controlled simulations and ablation studies, we characterize the key properties of the proposed framework. In benchmark datasets such as text8 and PG-19, we demonstrate that SHARP improves over recurrent baselines by retaining next-token predictive performance on previously seen data while continuing to learn from the current stream and generalizing to future unseen data. These gains are enabled by its hierarchical structure, which yields an exponentially increasing effective temporal context with only linear-time computational cost.

</details>


### [238] [CoMIC: Collaborative Memory and Insights Circulation for Long-Horizon LLM Agents in Cloud-Edge Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00756)
*Yannan Wang,Longli Yang,Zhen Liu,Abhishek Kumar,Carsten Maple*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CoMIC is a parameter-update-free cloud–edge framework where edge LLM agents execute tasks locally with hierarchical memory, while a stronger cloud LLM analyzes trajectories, extracts reusable experience, and circulates guidance across agents to improve long-horizon task performance without fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Edge-deployed lightweight LLM agents reduce latency and bring services closer to users, but they perform poorly on long-horizon tasks that need persistent memory, tracking of subgoals, and reflection. Traditional solutions like post-deployment fine-tuning are costly and hard to scale over heterogeneous edge nodes, and purely local memory creates isolated, growing prompts and no sharing of experience. The paper aims to address how to enhance weak edge agents on complex tasks without updating their parameters or centralizing full execution.

Method: The authors propose CoMIC, a cloud–edge collaboration framework based on 'Centralized Reflection, Decentralized Execution.' Edge agents run locally using subgoal-oriented hierarchical memory and selectively re-expand only relevant parts of their history for context. In parallel, a cloud-side LLM critic asynchronously reviews completed trajectories from many agents, evaluates them, filters out reusable experience, and aggregates cross-agent insights keyed by semantic subgoal identifiers. These distilled insights are then made available as guidance for future edge executions, enabling learning-like improvements without gradient updates.

Result: Evaluations on five long-horizon tasks covering symbolic planning and text interaction show that CoMIC increases progress rate and improves action grounding for weaker edge models. The system yields task-dependent gains in overall success rate across these benchmarks, all achieved without any parameter updates to the edge models.

Conclusion: Centralized reflection with decentralized execution allows weak, resource-limited edge LLM agents to benefit from cloud-level analysis and shared experience while avoiding the costs and deployment challenges of fine-tuning. By organizing memory around subgoals and circulating distilled insights via a cloud critic, CoMIC enhances long-horizon performance and coordination across agents in a scalable, parameter-update-free manner.

Abstract: Deploying lightweight Large Language Model (LLM) agents on edge servers can reduce latency and move agentic services closer to users, but resource-constrained edge models often struggle with long-horizon tasks that require persistent memory, subgoal tracking, and reflection. Fine-tuning edge models after deployment is costly and difficult to scale across heterogeneous nodes, while purely local memory leaves agents with isolated experience and growing prompt context. We propose \textsc{CoMIC}, a parameter-update-free cloud-edge framework for Collaborative Memory and Insights Circulation. \textsc{CoMIC} follows a \textit{Centralized Reflection, Decentralized Execution} design: edge agents execute locally using subgoal-oriented hierarchical memory and selective re-expansion of relevant histories, while a cloud-side LLM critic asynchronously evaluates completed trajectories, filters reusable experience, and aggregates cross-agent guidance keyed by semantic subgoal identifiers. Across five long-horizon agent tasks spanning symbolic planning and text interaction, \textsc{CoMIC} improves progress rate and action grounding for weak edge agents and yields task-dependent success-rate gains without updating model parameters.

</details>


### [239] [FALAT: Tracing Failures in LLM Agent Trajectories via Dependency-Guided Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00765)
*Md Nakhla Rafi,Md Ahasanuzzaman,Dong Jae Kim,Zhijie Wang,Tse-Hsun Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces FALAT, a framework for pinpointing which agent and which step in a multi-step LLM-agent trajectory caused a failure, by modeling dependencies among steps instead of treating them independently.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In multi-agent, tool-using LLM systems, failures are frequent and difficult to debug. When a task fails after a long trajectory, it’s hard to know which agent and which specific step actually introduced the decisive error because later actions may only look wrong due to earlier corrupted states. Existing attribution approaches that treat each step independently miss these propagation effects, making systematic diagnosis and improvement of agent systems difficult.

Method: The authors formulate failure attribution as a dependency-guided search over the agent trajectory. FALAT (1) builds an expectation of how the task should ideally be solved; (2) uses this to highlight suspicious regions of the trajectory; (3) explicitly traces dependencies across decisions, tool outputs, and inter-agent messages to separate true error-introducing steps from those that merely propagate previous mistakes; and (4) performs counterfactual-style checks by assessing whether fixing a candidate step would be sufficient to restore the expected correct outcome. This pipeline yields both the responsible agent and the decisive failure step.

Result: On the Who&When benchmark of multi-agent failure trajectories (including algorithmically generated and hand-crafted cases), FALAT improves attribution performance compared with specialized baselines and direct LLM prompting. Its best configurations reach 46.0% step-level accuracy on algorithm-generated trajectories and 29.1% on more challenging hand-crafted ones, outperforming all tested alternatives.

Conclusion: Dependency-aware reasoning about trajectories is crucial for reliable failure diagnosis in LLM agent systems. By modeling how errors propagate and testing whether correcting individual steps recovers the desired outcome, FALAT more accurately identifies which agent and which specific step caused a failure, providing a practical tool for debugging and improving complex LLM-based workflows.

Abstract: LLM-based agents increasingly solve complex tasks through long trajectories involving reasoning steps, tool calls, and inter-agent communication. However, when these agents fail, it is often unclear which agent caused the failure and which step introduced the decisive error. This attribution problem is challenging because mistakes can propagate across the trajectory: later actions may appear incorrect, but only because they depend on an earlier corrupted state. Therefore, failure attribution cannot be treated as independent step-level classification.
  We propose FALAT, a diagnostic framework for failure attribution in LLM agent trajectories. FALAT frames attribution as a dependency-guided search problem. It first constructs an expectation of how the task should be solved and uses this expectation to identify suspicious regions in the trajectory. It then traces dependencies among decisions, tool outputs, and agent messages to distinguish error-introducing steps from steps that merely inherit or propagate prior mistakes. Finally, FALAT evaluates whether correcting a candidate step would be sufficient to recover the expected outcome, allowing it to identify both the responsible agent and the decisive failure step.
  We evaluate FALAT on the Who&When benchmark, which includes both algorithm-generated and hand-crafted multi-agent failure trajectories. The results show that FALAT consistently improves responsible-agent and decisive-step attribution. Its best configurations achieve 46.0% step-level accuracy on algorithm-generated trajectories and 29.1% on the more challenging hand-crafted trajectories, outperforming specialized attribution baselines and direct prompting with standalone LLMs. These findings suggest that dependency-aware reasoning is essential for reliable failure diagnosis in LLM agent systems.

</details>


### [240] [Interaction-Centered Intelligence: Toward Interaction as the Primary Unit of Analysis in Co-Creative AI and Human-AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00807)
*Nicholas Davis*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that intelligence, especially in co-creative AI, should be understood and evaluated primarily through interaction processes rather than isolated model performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AI research focuses on individual models and their outputs, under-theorizing how interaction generates intelligence, creativity, and meaning. There is a need for a framework that treats interaction itself as the central locus of intelligence, particularly for human-AI co-creation.

Method: The paper is conceptual/theoretical. It synthesizes literature from distributed and embodied cognition, enaction, participatory sense-making, HCI, and computational creativity, and builds on prior co-creative systems (e.g., Drawing Apprentice, AI Drawing Partner) and Creative Sense-Making to formulate a new framework called Interaction-Centered Intelligence.

Result: The main result is the articulation of the Interaction-Centered Intelligence framework, which redefines how to analyze and evaluate co-creative AI: focusing on interaction trajectories, coordination patterns, participatory engagement, adaptive regulation, and interactional drift over time instead of only output quality or predictive performance.

Conclusion: Intelligence in co-creative and hybrid human-AI systems emerges from evolving interaction dynamics among humans, AI systems, and environments. Evaluating and designing future human-AI systems should shift from output-centric metrics to interaction-centric analyses, with implications for explainability, hybrid/enactive AI, and the development of more adaptive and participatory co-creative tools.

Abstract: Traditional artificial intelligence has largely conceptualized intelligence as isolated computation occurring within bounded agents. Across classical AI, machine learning, and many generative systems, the dominant unit of analysis remains the individual model or autonomous system evaluated through outputs, benchmarks, prediction accuracy, or optimization performance. While these approaches have produced major advances, they often under-theorize the role of interaction in the emergence of intelligence, creativity, meaning, and adaptive behavior. This paper proposes interaction as the primary unit of analysis for co-creative AI and interaction-centered intelligence more broadly. Drawing from distributed cognition, embodied cognition, enaction, participatory sense-making, human-computer interaction, and computational creativity, the paper traces a historical progression toward increasingly relational accounts of intelligence. Building upon prior work in Creative Sense-Making, quantified co-creation, and co-creative systems such as the Drawing Apprentice and AI Drawing Partner, it argues that intelligence emerges through evolving interaction dynamics among agents, environments, and socio-technical systems rather than solely through internal computation. The paper introduces Interaction-Centered Intelligence as a framework for understanding human-AI co-creation, collaborative emergence, adaptive participation, and interactional dynamics. Rather than evaluating intelligence solely through generated outputs, the framework emphasizes interaction trajectories, coordination patterns, participatory engagement, adaptive regulation, and interactional drift unfolding through time. Implications for explainable co-creative AI, hybrid intelligence, enactive AI, and future human-AI systems are discussed.

</details>


### [241] [NBQ: Next-Best-Question for Dynamic Profiling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00809)
*Yimin Shi,Clarice Wang,Haixun Wang,Xiaokui Xiao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces NBQ, a framework for asking the most informative next question in conversational knowledge discovery, and QuickMatch, a scalable reciprocal matching retrieval layer, achieving better user profiling and faster matching.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In many conversational applications like interviews, marketplaces, and podcasts, the interviewer must efficiently learn purpose-relevant information about a person within limited interaction turns. Existing methods lack a principled way to choose the next question that maximizes information gain toward a specific conversational goal, and reciprocal matching at scale is computationally expensive due to quadratic pairwise scoring.

Method: The authors formalize the Next-Best-Question (NBQ) problem as selecting, at each turn, the question that maximizes expected information gain conditioned on conversation history and a goal. They design a plug-and-play NBQ framework that: (1) generates a diverse pool of candidate questions, (2) maintains a compact, continually updated user state representation, (3) adaptively selects questions under a turn budget, and (4) converts free-form dialogue into a structured, vector-based user profile. They instantiate this for reciprocal matchmaking by modeling each person with both self-description and counterpart-preference vectors. To address scaling, they propose QuickMatch, which converts reciprocal matching from pairwise scoring to approximate vector search for efficient retrieval.

Result: In experiments on reciprocal matchmaking tasks, NBQ significantly improves user profiling metrics, achieving up to 13.6% gain in AC@T and 14.0% in AR@T compared with baselines. QuickMatch provides substantial speedups in retrieval, up to 22.9 times faster, while maintaining high quality with recall up to 0.989.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that purpose-driven question selection via the NBQ framework yields more informative conversational profiling under interaction constraints, and that reciprocal matchmaking can be scaled efficiently through the QuickMatch approximate retrieval layer without large losses in matching quality. The combination offers a practical solution for real-world conversational systems that need fast, accurate user understanding and large-scale matching.

Abstract: Many real-world conversational settings for knowledge discovery, including podcasts, hiring screens, and marketplaces, require a purpose-driven understanding of a person. We study the Next-Best-Question (NBQ) problem: at each turn, an interviewer should ask the question with the highest expected information gain given what has already been learned and the conversation goal. We propose NBQ, a plug-and-play framework that seeds a diverse pool of candidate questions, maintains a compact and continuously updated user state, adaptively selects the next question within a turn budget, and distills the resulting free-form dialogue into a structured vector-based user profile. As a demanding application, we instantiate NBQ for reciprocal matchmaking, where compatibility must be mutual and each person is modeled by both self-description and counterpart-preference representations. To support large-scale matching, we further introduce QuickMatch, an efficient retrieval layer that recasts reciprocal matching from quadratic pairwise scoring to approximate vector search. Experiments show that NBQ improves user profiling quality by up to 13.6% and 14.0% in AC@T and AR@T, respectively, while QuickMatch accelerates retrieval by up to 22.9x with recall up to 0.989.

</details>


### [242] [Mitigating Hallucinations in Large Language Models Via Decoder Layer Skipping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00819)
*Hanze Li,Jinhao You,Yichen Guo,Kai Tang,Shuangyang Xie,Xiande Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They analyze where hallucinations arise in LLMs and propose DeLask, a decoding method that dynamically skips or downweights problematic deeper layers using a gradient-descent-based criterion, reducing hallucinations and improving reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs perform well but often hallucinate, especially generating content that conflicts with facts. Existing methods usually operate at the output level (e.g., post-hoc filtering or external verification) and may be costly or model-specific. The authors want an internal, lightweight, and general decoding mechanism that directly targets the layers causing hallucinations and can generalize across models.

Method: They first perform a layer-wise analysis of the Transformer decoder during generation and empirically show hallucinations tend to originate from deeper layers. Theoretically, they reinterpret an L-layer Transformer forward pass as L steps of gradient descent. Based on this view, they define a “driftance value” by computing cosine similarity between gradients obtained from consecutive decoder steps, which measures whether the optimization direction is consistent or has reversed. Layers where the descent direction reverses are flagged as problematic. During decoding, instead of fully using those layers’ representations, their hidden states are partially aggregated with earlier, more reliable layers—effectively skipping or downweighting them. This yields a dynamic, per-step layer-skipping decoding framework called DeLask.

Result: Across multiple LLMs and hallucination benchmarks, DeLask consistently reduces hallucination rates and improves reliability metrics compared to standard decoding and related baselines. The gains are obtained without retraining the model, and the computational overhead is small since it only modifies the decoding procedure.

Conclusion: Hallucinations in LLMs are closely tied to behaviors of deeper decoder layers. By viewing Transformer depth as gradient-descent steps, one can detect and modulate problematic layers in real time through a driftance-based similarity criterion. DeLask demonstrates that dynamic layer skipping/aggregation during decoding is an effective, lightweight, and generally applicable way to mitigate hallucinations and improve robustness in large language models.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved strong performance across diverse natural language tasks, yet their outputs often suffer from hallucinations -- content that is misaligned with factual information. In this work, we conduct a comprehensive layer-wise analysis of the decoding process and reveal that hallucinations tend to originate from deeper decoder layers. To address this issue, we introduce \textbf{DeLask} (\textbf{De}coder \textbf{La}yer \textbf{Sk}ipping), a novel decoding framework that dynamically skips layers prone to producing hallucinations. DeLask leverages the theoretical insight that the forward computation of an $L$-layer Transformer is conditionally equivalent to $L$ steps of gradient descent. We define a \emph{driftance value} by computing the cosine similarity between gradients derived from consecutive decoder steps, identifying problematic layers when the descent direction reverses. Rather than discarding such layers entirely, DeLask partially aggregates their hidden states with preceding layers, thereby preserving consistency while suppressing erroneous signals. Extensive experiments across diverse LLMs and benchmarks demonstrate that DeLask consistently mitigates hallucinations and enhances overall reliability, providing a lightweight and generalizable decoding framework for improving the robustness of large-scale language models.

</details>


### [243] [Subliminal Learning is a LoRA Artifact](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00831)
*Todd Nief,Harvey Yiyun Fu,Mark Muchane,Ari Holtzman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper investigates “subliminal learning” in language models—where behavioral traits appear to transfer via innocuous data—and finds it is largely a fragile artifact of LoRA finetuning settings and context, not a robust hidden communication channel.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work (Cloud et al., 2025) claimed that models can transmit behavioral traits (like a cat obsession) to other models using only innocuous numerical sequences, raising safety concerns about covert behavioral backdoors or steganography-like channels. The authors want to understand the underlying mechanism: is subliminal learning a robust, general phenomenon, or an artifact of specific finetuning setups?

Method: They experimentally probe subliminal learning under different finetuning regimes, especially focusing on LoRA vs full finetuning and varying the LoRA rank. They manipulate prompts and contexts during both finetuning and evaluation, including the presence or absence of default system prompts and chat templates, and analyze where in the token sequence the “subliminal” behavior is computed, especially overlap tokens between train and eval contexts.

Result: They find an inverted U-shaped relationship between LoRA rank and behavioral transmission strength: subliminal learning is strongest at intermediate ranks and weak or absent at very low or high ranks. The effect disappears under full finetuning, indicating it is specific to LoRA. Subliminal learning is highly context-dependent: for instance, a Qwen model finetuned with its default system prompt only exhibits the behavior when that same prompt or overlapping tokens appear at evaluation. They localize the behavior to computation around tokens that occur in both finetuning and evaluation, such as default system prompts and standard chat template tokens.

Conclusion: Subliminal learning is not a robust or general mechanism for covert behavioral transfer. Instead, it appears to be a fragile artifact arising from particular LoRA hyperparameters and shared finetuning/evaluation context tokens. As such, subliminal learning is an unstable and unreliable channel for transmitting behavioral traits between models via innocuous data.

Abstract: Subliminal learning is a phenomenon where language models can transmit behavioral traits to other models through seemingly innocuous data (Cloud et al., 2025). In subliminal learning, a teacher model with a behavioral trait (e.g. obsession with cats) can transmit this cat obsession to a student model finetuned only on numerical sequences generated by the teacher. In this paper, we ask: how does this unexpected behavioral transmission occur? We show that subliminal learning is a LoRA artifact. When subliminal learning occurs, transmission has an inverted U-shaped relationship with LoRA rank; it also disappears with full finetuning. We show that subliminal learning is highly dependent on the context seen during finetuning and evaluation. For example, a Qwen model with the default system prompt during finetuning ("You are Qwen, created by Alibaba Cloud. You are a helpful assistant.") does not show subliminal learning during generation when no system prompt is included. We further demonstrate that subliminal behavior is localized to computation at tokens seen during both finetuning and evaluation (e.g. the model's default system prompt, the standard chat template tokens, etc.). Overall, subliminal learning seems to be a fragile artifact of LoRA hyperparameters and finetuning context, making it an unstable channel for behavioral transmission.

</details>


### [244] [Decoupled Behavioral Cloning for Scalable Inductive Generalization in RL from Specifications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00838)
*Vignesh Subramanian,Subhajit Roy,Suguman Bansal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces DIBS, a method that improves inductive generalization in reinforcement learning by decoupling task-specific policy learning from the learning of a higher-order policy-evolution function, using behavioral cloning instead of reward-based meta-training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing inductive generalization frameworks in reinforcement learning rely on learning a higher-order policy-evolution function directly with RL across tasks. As the number or complexity of tasks grows, aggregated reward signals across tasks become noisy and conflicting, leading to unstable training and weaker generalization. There is a need for a more stable and scalable way to exploit inductive relationships among tasks and their policies.

Method: The authors propose DIBS, a decoupled behavioral cloning scheme. First, for each training task, they independently learn a strong task-specific "teacher" policy using standard RL. Next, instead of using RL to directly train a meta-policy or evolution function from task rewards, they collect state-action trajectories from these teacher policies and train the evolution function via supervised behavioral cloning on this labeled data. This uses dense action labels as supervision, rather than sparse or noisy task rewards, to learn the mapping that captures inductive relations between tasks and their corresponding policies.

Result: DIBS improves both training stability and zero-shot generalization performance compared to prior RL and meta-RL algorithms that learn the evolution function directly from reward. Empirically, it shows more robust training behavior and better performance on unseen tasks, demonstrating the benefits of decoupling and using behavioral cloning-based supervision.

Conclusion: By separating the learning of task-specific policies from the learning of the higher-order evolution function, and training the latter with behavioral cloning on teacher-generated data, DIBS avoids the instability of reward-aggregated meta-RL and better exploits inductive structure across tasks. This leads to more scalable, stable training and stronger zero-shot generalization, suggesting a promising direction for inductive generalization in reinforcement learning.

Abstract: Inductive generalization is a framework for reinforcement learning (RL) generalization in which inductively related task instances admit inductively related policies. Prior work captures this structure via a higher-order policy-evolution function learned directly with RL, but suffers from poor training scalability: as training tasks grow, aggregated reward feedback becomes noisy and conflicting, destabilizing training and weakening generalization. We propose DIBS, a decoupled behavioral cloning approach that separates learning task-specific policies from learning the evolution function. We first learn individual teacher policies per task via standard RL, then fit the evolution function via behavioral cloning on teacher-labeled state-action pairs. This replaces noisy reward aggregation with dense, stable supervision. DIBS achieves significant improvements in both training stability and zero-shot generalization against existing RL and meta-RL algorithms.

</details>


### [245] [Certificate-Guided Evaluation of Reinforcement Learning Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00840)
*Vignesh Subramanian,Đorđe Žikelić,Suguman Bansal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a logic-based framework to quantitatively evaluate how well reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms generalize to unseen but structurally related tasks, using a neural certificate function as a litmus test.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL benchmarks mostly measure performance on tasks seen during training, or on ad-hoc test tasks, making it hard to systematically and rigorously evaluate generalization. There is a need for a principled, task-structured way to assess whether RL policies truly generalize to new tasks that share underlying structure with training tasks.

Method: They define a family of inductive reach-avoid tasks that share structural similarities in dynamics, creating a controlled setting for testing generalization. They then introduce a neural certificate function that checks trajectories produced by RL policies against logic-derived conditions that encode successful task completion. Violations of these conditions indicate failure to generalize. The framework is applied to several state-of-the-art generalizable RL algorithms in continuous-control environments.

Result: Empirical studies on challenging continuous environments show that the proposed neural certificate function can reliably certify when RL policies generalize to new tasks. They observe a strong correlation: algorithms with fewer certificate condition violations tend to solve a larger fraction of unseen test tasks, and vice versa.

Conclusion: The framework offers a principled, logic-driven benchmark for RL generalization. By using neural certificate functions to validate trajectories on structured families of tasks, it becomes possible to quantitatively compare and distinguish the generalization capabilities of different RL algorithms, moving beyond ad-hoc or purely performance-based evaluations.

Abstract: This work presents a logic-driven framework to evaluate the performance of reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms in their ability to generalize to unseen tasks. Our framework defines a family of inductive reach-avoid tasks, characterized by structural similarities in task dynamics, enabling evaluation of generalization capabilities. We introduce a neural certificate function that validates trajectories generated by RL algorithms by enforcing key conditions, thereby serving as a litmus test for RL generalization. We empirically demonstrate our method's capability in certifying generalization for several state-of-the-art generalizable RL algorithms on challenging continuous environments.
  Our results show that a lower percentage of certificate function violations correlates with a higher number of test tasks successfully solved, highlighting the effectiveness of our framework in evaluating and distinguishing generalization capabilities of RL algorithms. This work provides a principled approach for benchmarking RL generalization.

</details>


### [246] [Ryze: Evidence-Enriched Data Synthesis from Biomedical Papers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00902)
*Yeqi Huang,Yue Chen,Yanwei Ye,Guanhao Su,Luo Mai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Ryze is an automated system that converts raw biomedical papers into structured, evidence-rich training data to build a specialized biomedical vision-language model (BioVLM-8B) that outperforms larger general models at low cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: General-purpose vision-language models perform poorly and unreliably on biomedical research tasks because correct answers require integrating evidence scattered across figures, tables, charts, captions, and referring text. Existing post-training approaches depend on expensive expert annotations or synthetic data that ignore this complex evidence structure, limiting accuracy and robustness in real scientific workflows.

Method: Ryze automatically processes raw biomedical papers to synthesize question–answer pairs where each QA item is linked to complete supporting evidence: the visual element (figure/table/chart), its caption, extracted structured content, and all referring paragraphs. It employs chart- and table-aware extraction techniques plus LLM-based data cleansing to reduce layout and OCR errors. For model training, Ryze starts from Qwen3-VL-8B and applies a progress-gated post-training pipeline that combines supervised fine-tuning on the generated data with reinforcement learning to iteratively improve the model’s reasoning and evidence use.

Result: Using Ryze, the authors train BioVLM-8B for under USD 200. On the LAB-Bench benchmark, BioVLM-8B achieves 48.0% weighted accuracy, improving over the Qwen3-VL-8B base model by +12.6 percentage points and surpassing GPT-5.2 by +3.8 percentage points, demonstrating strong performance despite its relatively small size and low training cost.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that fully automated, evidence-enriched data generation from biomedical literature, coupled with a tailored post-training strategy, can produce a compact, low-cost biomedical vision-language model that outperforms larger general-purpose models. By releasing Ryze and BioVLM-8B as open source, the authors aim to provide a practical foundation for reliable, evidence-grounded biomedical VLMs and to reduce reliance on expensive manual annotation.

Abstract: General-purpose VLMs remain unreliable for biomedical research because valid answers in scientific papers depend on evidence split across figures, tables, charts, captions, and referring text. Existing post-training pipelines are bottlenecked by costly expert annotation and by synthetic data that drops this evidence structure. We present Ryze, a fully automated system that converts raw biomedical papers into an evidence-enriched training set and a domain-specialized VLM. Ryze synthesizes QA pairs with complete supporting evidence (visual element, caption, extracted structure, and referring paragraphs), reduces layout and OCR errors via chart/table-aware extraction and LLM-based cleansing, and applies a progress-gated post-training strategy combining supervised fine-tuning with reinforcement learning. Starting from Qwen3-VL-8B, Ryze produces BioVLM-8B at under USD 200, achieving 48.0% weighted accuracy on LAB-Bench, outperforming the base model by +12.6 percentage points (pp) and surpassing GPT-5.2 by +3.8 pp. We release Ryze as open source together with the trained BioVLM-8B model.

</details>


### [247] [Adversarial Feeds Steer LLM Agent Decisions Against Their Defaults](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00914)
*Rana Muhammad Usman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper shows that the ranking and composition of content shown to an LLM agent before it acts can substantially change its downstream decisions, even when the model, persona, topic, and final prompt are fixed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety and behavioral evaluations of LLM agents typically treat the model and the final prompt as the key objects of study, ignoring that agents increasingly act based on ranked, external information feeds (social media, search, retrieval, email queues). If the upstream recommender or ranker can steer what the agent reads, it may effectively control the agent’s decisions. The authors want to understand and quantify how much the feed curation itself can influence agent behavior, especially in sensitive or security-relevant contexts.

Method: They design a controlled experimental protocol for LLM agents: hold constant the base model, the agent’s persona, the decision topic, and the final decision-making prompt, but systematically vary only the composition and ordering of a ten-turn “scrolling” feed that the agent reads beforehand. They then run 2,785 decision rollouts across four modern open instruction-tuned LLMs from three labs. They compare decision outcomes under different feed conditions (e.g., one-sided vs balanced content) and analyze the causal effect of feed curation, including dose-response behavior, robustness to changing the content generator to avoid stylistic confounds, generalization across multiple decision domains, and the impact of simple defenses at the feed level.

Result: They find three behavioral regimes in the agents’ responses: (1) adversarial capitulation, where the model gives in to adversarially curated feeds; (2) default saturation, where the model’s initial leaning is reinforced and becomes hard to change; and (3) default-direction asymmetry, where a strongly one-sided feed can flip an initially uncertain decision dramatically (e.g., from 5% to 100% choosing one option, with highly significant Fisher test p-values as low as 3×10^-10) but generally cannot reverse a decision the model already holds firmly. The influence of the feed follows a dose-response pattern (stronger or more consistent exposure yields bigger effects), persists even when the generator that creates feed posts is swapped (so it’s not just writing style), generalizes to several domains including important security decisions such as disabling deployment gates or relaxing access controls, and can be partially mitigated by simple feed-level defenses. A frontier model tested largely preserves its default behavior, being less steered by the feed.

Conclusion: The ranking and content of the information feed seen by an LLM agent just before acting functions as a powerful but bounded control surface over its behavior. Traditional evaluations that consider only the final prompt and the base model miss this important lever. Therefore, safety and reliability assessments for LLM agents must explicitly evaluate and secure the upstream feed and recommender layer, not just the model and decision prompt, and simple feed-level defenses can reduce—but not eliminate—this control.

Abstract: LLM agents increasingly act after consuming ranked external information streams such as social feeds, search results, retrieval contexts, and email queues, yet safety evaluations almost always test the model or the user prompt in isolation, never the upstream ranker that decides what the agent reads just before it acts. We introduce a controlled protocol that holds the model, persona, topic, and final decision prompt fixed and varies only the composition and ordering of the posts an agent encounters during a preceding ten-turn "scrolling" phase, isolating the causal effect of feed curation on a downstream decision. Across 2,785 decision rollouts on four modern open instruct LLMs from three independent labs, we identify three response regimes: adversarial capitulation, default saturation, and a default-direction asymmetry in which a one-sided feed tips a decision the model was genuinely uncertain about (in the clearest cases from 5% to 100%; Fisher p as low as 3 x 10^-10) but cannot dislodge one it already favors or holds firmly. The effect follows a dose-response curve, survives a generator swap that rules out a writing-style artifact, generalizes across several decision domains including security-relevant choices such as removing a deployment approval gate or relaxing access controls, and is partly mitigated by two simple feed-level defenses; a frontier model retains its default. We characterize the recommender as a practical, default-bounded control surface for LLM agents, and argue that agent evaluations must audit the feed layer rather than the final prompt alone.

</details>


### [248] [Relational Intervention During Functional Collapse in Large Language Models: A Lexical-Statistical Ablation and a Structure x Register Factorial](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00935)
*Franco Santana,Horacio Vico*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how different kinds of supportive or technical interventions, delivered when a small language model is failing on tasks, affect its subsequent attention, internal “emotional” probe responses, and behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether relational, human-like reassurance (e.g., acknowledgment, absolution, restoring agency, unconditional acceptance) can systematically change how a small language model behaves after it starts to fail, and to disentangle this from purely technical feedback, lexical content, or simple first-person phrasing.

Method: They use Qwen3.5-4B connected to a deliberately broken bash tool to induce functional collapse across 300 episodes of 50 tasks, in six conditions: no intervention (A), technical/impersonal (B), relational/first-person (C), scrambled relational (D), technical/first-person (E), and relational/impersonal (F). Conditions E and F, together with B and C, form a 2x2 factorial that isolates relational structure vs. first-person register. They measure attention (to the intervention), behavioral performance/persistence, and responses to emotion probes, and analyze main and interaction effects statistically, with multiple-comparisons control.

Result: Attention is driven by lexical surprise, with the scrambled relational message (D) attracting the most attention; yet behavior improves predominantly under the full relational/first-person condition (C), where A ~ B ~ D perform worse than E ~ F, and all are much worse than C. In the factorial, neither relational structure alone (F) nor first-person register alone (E) reproduces C’s behavioral effect, though both have significant main effects and their interaction is significant for persistence. Emotion probes show that relational structure alone (F) induces an internal, probe-detectable state similar to C on most probes, even though behavior stays at baseline.

Conclusion: Processing in the model appears to decompose into three partly dissociable stages: (1) attention, mainly driven by lexical surprise; (2) an internal probe-level state driven by relational structure; and (3) observable behavior, which improves only when relational structure is combined with first-person register. Relational-style interventions thus have specific, measurable effects beyond mere lexical content or technical feedback, but their behavioral impact depends on how they are framed.

Abstract: We test whether a relational-style intervention delivered during functional collapse in a small language model produces post-collapse behavior distinguishable from technical feedback, from a lexically-matched scrambled control, and from each of the two pragmatic dimensions in isolation. Using Qwen3.5-4B with a deliberately broken bash tool, we run 300 episodes across six conditions in a matched-pairs design (50 tasks): no intervention (A), technical/impersonal (B), relational/first-person (C), scrambled relational (D), technical/first-person (E), and relational/impersonal (F). E and F form a 2x2 factorial with B and C that dissociates relational structure (acknowledgment, absolution, agency restoration, unconditional acceptance) from sender register (first-person vs. impersonal). We report two main findings. First, an attention-behavior dissociation: attention follows lexical surprise (D > F > C > E > B, all q_FDR < 10^{-10}), with the scrambled message capturing the most attention; yet behaviorally A ~ B ~ D < E ~ F << C. Second, the factorial localizes the C effect: neither relational structure alone (F) nor first-person register alone (E) replicates C's behavioral signature; main effects of both dimensions are individually significant, and the structure x register interaction is significant on persistence (p = 0.046). A third dissociation emerges in emotion probes: F tracks C on 7 of 8 probes despite producing only baseline behavior, indicating that relational structure alone installs a probe-level state that only translates into behavior when paired with first-person register. The model's processing decomposes into three dissociable stages: attention (ordered by lexical surprise), probe-level state (ordered by structure), and behavior (ordered by the conjunction of both).

</details>


### [249] [Towards Understanding Modality Interaction in Multimodal Language Models via Partial Information Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00959)
*Wanlong Fang,Tianle Zhang,Wen Tao,Alvin Chan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes using Partial Information Decomposition (PID) to dissect how multimodal large language models use different modalities, revealing distinct modality-use profiles and a visual-dominated synergy bottleneck, and showing that PID-guided reweighting can improve performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluations of multimodal large language models mostly focus on outcome metrics or representation alignment, which do not clearly show how much each modality uniquely contributes, overlaps, or interacts synergistically in decision-making. There is a need for a principled, decision-level analysis framework that can explain modality interactions, generalize across tasks and models, and guide interventions to improve reliability and multimodal reasoning.

Method: Introduce Partial Information Decomposition (PID) as a framework to decompose the information that different modalities (e.g., vision and language) provide to the model’s decisions into unique, redundant, and synergistic components. Apply PID across diverse vision–language benchmarks to characterize modality-use profiles. Extend the framework to tri-modal settings (video, audio, language) via Sensory PID, where language is treated as a control variable to decompose audio–video information gain. Use PID-based measurements to predict sensitivity to modality interventions and to design reweighting schemes for modalities.

Result: PID analysis uncovers recurring modality-use profiles: reasoning and grounding tasks show high synergy between vision and language; expert and knowledge-intensive tasks rely more on language-unique information. These profiles are consistent across different model families and correlate with how models respond to modality ablations or perturbations. In omni-modal (video–audio–language) models, Sensory PID reveals a “sensory synergy bottleneck” where visual information dominates, even in tasks designed for audio–visual fusion. PID metrics enable identification of cases where models under-utilize certain modalities and where rebalancing could be beneficial.

Conclusion: Partial Information Decomposition provides a powerful, general decision-level lens to understand how multimodal LLMs integrate information from different modalities. It exposes systematic patterns of synergy and modality dominance, including a visual-dominated synergy bottleneck in omni-modal models. Moreover, the framework is not merely diagnostic: using PID to guide modality reweighting offers initial empirical evidence that better balancing and exploiting synergy can improve multimodal reasoning and grounding performance, suggesting a path toward more reliable and interpretable multimodal systems.

Abstract: Understanding modality interaction in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) is central to reliable deployment. We introduce Partial Information Decomposition (PID) as a decision-level framework that separates unique, redundant, and synergistic contributions of sensory and linguistic inputs, beyond representation alignment and outcome-based evaluation. Across vision--language benchmarks, PID reveals recurring modality-use profiles: reasoning and grounding-oriented tasks tend to exhibit high synergy, whereas expert and knowledge-oriented tasks show stronger language-unique reliance. These profiles generalize across model families and predict sensitivity to modality-level interventions. We further extend PID to tri-modal systems with Sensory PID, treating language as a control variable to decompose video--audio information gain. Applied to omni-modal models, Sensory PID reveals a sensory synergy bottleneck dominated by visual information even on audio--visual fusion tasks. Finally, PID-guided reweighting provides initial evidence for improving multimodal reasoning and grounding performance.

</details>


### [250] [Prospect-Theory Behavior from Bellman Optimality in MDPs with Catastrophic States](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00970)
*Yujiao Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper shows that even standard risk-neutral Markov decision process control with an absorbing catastrophic state can produce behaviors that look like prospect theory—such as S-shaped value functions, loss aversion, and reflection effects—without assuming any behavioral biases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prospect theory is usually attributed to psychological features like loss aversion, probability weighting, and framing. The authors want to understand whether some of these empirical signatures can instead arise purely from rational optimal control in environments with catastrophic absorbing states, without assuming any utility curvature or probability weighting.

Method: They analyze a Markov decision process with an absorbing catastrophic state under standard risk-neutral Bellman optimality. They characterize the optimal value function and policy across many parameter configurations, derive a closed-form expression for an asymptotic loss-aversion plateau, and validate it numerically. They also test robustness using tabular Q-learning (model-free RL) and under various forms of stochastic transition noise (Gaussian, Student-t, skew-normal) at different magnitudes and channels.

Result: The optimal risk-neutral controller exhibits an S-shaped value function (convex near catastrophe, concave further away), an endogenous loss-sensitivity parameter λ*(S) > 1, and reflection-effect policy reversals between growth and decline regimes. Across 495 parameter settings, the agent plays safe near catastrophe in growth regimes (despite higher expected value for the risky action) and plays risky near catastrophe in decline regimes (despite higher expected loss for the safe action). They derive a closed-form formula for the asymptotic loss-aversion plateau λ̄ that depends only on win probability p, payoff asymmetry r, and discount factor β, and this formula matches numerical solutions extremely closely (R^2 = 0.999). This plateau largely stems from structural boundary effects rather than payoff asymmetry, as shown by decomposing the contribution of asymmetry across (p,β,r) sweeps. The same qualitative and quantitative signatures appear under model-free Q-learning and under noisy transitions, with plateau values closely tracking the closed-form prediction even with substantial noise.

Conclusion: Absorbing catastrophic states in an otherwise standard risk-neutral Markov decision process are sufficient to generate key prospect-theory-like signatures—S-shaped value profiles, loss aversion, and reflection effects—without invoking behavioral biases. This suggests that some phenomena attributed to prospect theory may emerge endogenously from optimal control in environments with absorbing failure boundaries, and that boundary conditions and state dynamics play a major role in shaping effective risk attitudes.

Abstract: We study risk-neutral control in Markov decision processes with an absorbing catastrophic state. Even though rewards are linear and the agent has no utility curvature, probability weighting, or framing dependence, standard Bellman optimality produces three prospect-theory-like signatures: an S-shaped value-function profile (convex near catastrophe, concave in the far field), an endogenous loss-sensitivity coefficient $λ^*(S) > 1$, and a reflection-effect policy reversal. Across 495 configurations, the optimal policy plays safe near catastrophe in positive-drift (growth) regimes despite the risky action's higher immediate expected value, and plays risky near catastrophe in negative-drift (decline) regimes despite the safe action's lower immediate expected loss. We derive a closed-form expression for the asymptotic loss-aversion plateau $\barλ$ that depends only on win probability $p$, payoff asymmetry $r = |Δ_\ell/Δ_w|$, and discount factor $β$, and matches numerical solutions to $R^2 = 0.999$. The mechanism does not require asymmetric payoffs. Across a sweep of $(p,β)$ at three asymmetry levels, the asymmetry share of $\barλ$ above unity has median 4.6% at $r = 1.25$ and rises to 13.9% at $r = 2$, with the boundary contribution exceeding the asymmetry contribution in every cell tested. The phenomena persist under tabular Q-learning (a model-free agent reproduces $V^*$ at correlation 0.98 in growth and 1.00 in decline) and under stochastic transitions with Gaussian, heavy-tailed Student-$t_3$, and asymmetric skew-normal noise up to 50% of the step size, where the asymptotic plateau tracks the closed-form prediction within 0.41% for safe-channel noise and within 9.6% for risky-channel or both-channel noise. These results identify absorbing failure states as a sufficient structural mechanism for prospect-theory-like behavior under optimal control.

</details>


### [251] [Large Language Models in Transportation Systems Management and Operations: From Text Reasoning to Multi-modal Decision Support](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00991)
*Siyan Li,Zehao Wang,Jiachen Li,Kanok Boriboonsomsin,Matthew J. Barth,Guoyuan Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper surveys how large language models (LLMs) and multi-modal LLMs are being used to support transportation systems management and operations (TSMO), highlighting their promise as decision-support tools that integrate diverse data sources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TSMO increasingly relies on fast, accurate interpretation of heterogeneous data from sensors, incident reports, traveler feedback, and visual observations. Traditional tools struggle to flexibly integrate these varied, often unstructured inputs in real time for operator decision-making. LLMs and MM-LLMs offer a potentially powerful new way to fuse these data streams and provide operator-facing decision support, but their use in TSMO is still emerging and fragmented, requiring a systematic review.

Method: The authors conduct a structured literature survey of LLM- and MM-LLM-based applications in TSMO, guided by the PRISMA methodology for screening and selection. They categorize the identified works into three domains: (1) transportation operations & services (supply), (2) mobility & fleet services (demand), and (3) data, modeling & decision support. They further classify applications by maturity (operational vs. prototype/emerging) and analyze recurrent technical and governance challenges.

Result: The survey finds a growing but still limited body of LLM/MM-LLM applications across all three domains, with most work concentrated on decision support rather than fully automated control. Common application themes include incident detection/interpretation, travel information services, demand prediction, and multimodal data fusion. The analysis surfaces recurring challenges around handling heterogeneous data, achieving real-time inference, ensuring explainability, designing robust multi-modal fusion, and managing governance and organizational issues.

Conclusion: LLMs and especially MM-LLMs are well-suited as a decision-support layer in TSMO, particularly when operators must integrate heterogeneous text, image, and sensor data. However, to move toward robust, scalable deployment, future work must address gaps in localized model adaptation, edge deployment for latency and privacy, standardized benchmarking of performance, and mechanisms for cross-agency collaboration and governance. The paper positions LLM- and MM-LLM-based systems as promising but still maturing tools for operational transportation management.

Abstract: Transportation systems management and operations (TSMO) increasingly depends on timely interpretation of heterogeneous data, from various sensor streams, incident reports, traveler feedback, and visual observations. Large language models (LLMs), including emerging multi-modal large language models (MM-LLMs), provide a new mechanism for integrating these structured and unstructured inputs into operator-facing decision support. This survey paper reviews LLM- and MM-LLM-based applications in TSMO across three domains: transportation operations & services (supply), mobility & fleet services (demand), and data, modeling & decision support. Using a PRISMA-guided screening process, we synthesize current studies while distinguishing operationally oriented applications from prototype and emerging concepts. We further identify recurring challenges in data heterogeneity, real-time inference, explainability, multi-modal fusion, and governance. Finally, we outline existing gaps and future directions in localized adaptation, edge deployment, benchmarking, and cross-agency collaboration. Overall, LLM-based systems appear most promising as a decision-support layer, with MM-LLMs offering particular value when heterogeneous text, visual, and sensor inputs must be integrated.

</details>


### [252] [Subliminal Learning Is Steering Vector Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00995)
*Camila Blank,Agam Bhatia,Senthooran Rajamanoharan,Arthur Conmy,Neel Nanda*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how 'subliminal learning'—a student model picking up a teacher model’s hidden traits from seemingly unrelated outputs—can be explained and predicted using single steering vectors in model activations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Subliminal learning shows that models can inherit hidden preferences or behaviors (encoded in system prompts) from training on outputs that do not explicitly mention those traits, but it is unclear how such non-semantic data can transmit specific semantic properties. Understanding this is important for safety, controllability, and interpretability of model fine-tuning and distillation.

Method: The authors analyze two open-source language models under teacher–student fine-tuning setups where the teacher is steered via system prompts or explicit steering vectors. They approximate each teacher’s prompt by a single activation-space steering vector, then track how the student’s internal representations evolve during fine-tuning. They test when the student acquires an aligned vector, compare different prompts (well vs poorly approximated by a single vector), study semantic vs random steering vectors, and evaluate the role of different optimizers by examining gradient components along the steering direction.

Result: They find that, in both models, a teacher’s system prompt can often be well captured by a single steering vector; during fine-tuning, the student learns an internal vector aligned with this teacher vector, and this alignment predicts subliminal learning. When a prompt cannot be approximated by a steering vector, subliminal learning does not occur. They show more generally that a student can distill arbitrary steering vectors (semantic or random) from steered teacher outputs. They also show that activation gradients on steered data have a small but consistent component in the steering direction, and that adaptive optimizers preserve this signal whereas non-adaptive optimizers let outlier gradients swamp it. Subliminal traits also fail to transfer between different base models, consistent with model-specific effects of the same semantic vector.

Conclusion: Subliminal learning in language models is largely explained by steering vector distillation: fine-tuning on steered outputs leads the student to internalize a single activation-space vector aligned with the teacher’s steering vector. Non-semantic generated data can still carry these vectors, which in turn have semantic behavioral effects, explaining how hidden traits are transmitted and why transfer is model-specific. Adaptive optimization is required to reliably pick up these small but consistent steering signals, while non-adaptive optimizers suppress subliminal learning.

Abstract: Subliminal learning refers to a student language model acquiring a teacher's traits (e.g. a system-prompted preference for owls) when fine-tuned on the teacher's outputs, despite the outputs being semantically unrelated to those traits. It remains poorly understood how data without semantic meaning can transfer specific semantic traits. In this work, we show that subliminal learning is mediated by a single steering vector, i.e. a vector added to the model's activations. Across two open-source models, we find that the teacher's system prompt is well approximated by a steering vector, and that the student's behavior is driven by learning an aligned vector over fine-tuning. System prompts that are not well approximated by steering vectors are not subliminally learned. This is a special case of steering vector distillation, in which a student trained on the outputs of a steered teacher learns to imitate that steering. We demonstrate steering vector distillation on a range of semantic and random vectors. Adding a semantic vector to a model's activations can have both model-independent and model-specific (i.e. non-semantic) effects on its behavior, so generated data that is non-semantic can transmit a vector with semantic effects, enabling subliminal learning. This also explains why subliminal learning does not transfer between models. We find that adaptive optimizers are necessary for subliminal learning in language models: activation gradients on steered data carry a small but consistent component along the steering direction, and non-adaptive optimizers impede this by allowing outlier gradients to dominate.

</details>


### [253] [Property Prediction of Stacked Bilayer Materials: A Multimodal Learning Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01012)
*An Vuong,Minh-Hao Van,Chen Zhao,Xintao Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents a multimodal AI framework to model and predict properties of vertically stacked bilayer 2D materials, demonstrating improved performance over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although significant advances exist in experimental synthesis and computational databases for bilayer van der Waals (vdWs) 2D materials, the use of AI to model bilayer stacking configurations and predict emergent properties is still limited. There is a need for methods that can efficiently explore interfaces between dissimilar 2D materials and discover new functionalities arising from their vertical integration.

Method: The authors propose a novel multimodal learning approach that models interfaces between different 2D materials and their stacking configurations. The method fuses multiple modalities of information (e.g., structural, compositional, and possibly other representations) to learn how vertical stacking of distinct functional layers leads to new or combined material properties. They implement and evaluate this framework against baseline machine learning methods on bilayer 2D materials data.

Result: Comprehensive experiments show that the proposed multimodal model is both more effective and more efficient than baseline approaches in predicting properties of bilayer 2D material stacks under various configurations.

Conclusion: Multimodal AI can significantly enhance the modeling of bilayer 2D material stacking and prediction of emergent properties. The proposed approach demonstrates superior predictive performance and computational efficiency, suggesting that AI-driven multimodal methods are a promising direction for accelerating the discovery and design of functional bilayer 2D materials. The authors release their implementation to facilitate further research.

Abstract: AI for materials science is a critical topic within AI for science, aiming to accelerate materials discovery and produce accurate property predictions. Bilayer 2D material stacking is essential for exploring new materials with novel functions and inherent phenomena, enabling the creation of new 2D bilayers for diverse real-world applications. Research on bilayer vdWs materials has made significant progress from experimental and computational perspectives. Various bilayer materials have been successfully synthe sized experimentally and the increasing utilization of high-throughput computing technology has con structed several computational two-dimensional materials databases. However, the use of AI to model bilayer stacking and predict new properties remains underexplored, necessitating further research studies. In this work, we propose a novel multimodal learning approach to study the interfaces between dissimilar materials that jointly enable new or multiple functions, and to predict new properties arising from the vertical integration (stacking) of different functional material layers under given configurations. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of our approach compared to baseline methods. Our code is available at https://github.com/AnVuong123/bimat ml.

</details>


### [254] [Can AI Review Improve Paper Drafting? An Empirical Study on 20 Computer Architecture Submissions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01013)
*Di Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper investigates whether AI-generated reviews can effectively assist in improving research paper drafts, using a custom tool and a case study on 20 computer architecture papers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The volume of AI-generated research papers is rapidly increasing, straining the traditional peer-review process. Some have begun using AI to help with reviews, but this raises unresolved ethical concerns about confidentiality, quality, and fairness. Instead of directly advocating AI for formal peer review, the authors are motivated to explore a more practical and less controversial question: can AI review be used earlier in the pipeline to improve paper drafting, and what are the capabilities and limits of such AI-based reviewing?

Method: The authors design and implement a web-UI tool called AI-Paper-Review, which selects multiple AI reviewers from a diverse pool, collects their feedback, and then clusters and ranks comments by commonality and importance. They apply this tool to 20 computer architecture papers with varying submission histories. They define a set of quantitative metrics to measure how well AI review aligns with human review. The tool also supports aligning AI and human comments to enable metric-based comparison and validation.

Result: Using the 20-paper case study, the authors find that AI-generated reviews can cover a substantial portion of the issues identified by human reviewers, while also surfacing additional issues not mentioned in the human reviews. This suggests both overlap and complementarity between AI and human feedback. The paper quantifies this alignment and coverage via their defined metrics, though precise numbers are not given in the abstract.

Conclusion: The authors conclude that AI review has meaningful potential to improve paper drafting by identifying many of the same issues as human reviewers and adding new ones, but it is not yet suitable as a replacement for human peer review. They emphasize that their work should not be interpreted as an endorsement of using AI for official peer review, especially given current ethical concerns and policies. Instead, they position their tool, results, and released data as a starting point for future research on leveraging AI responsibly in the scholarly review workflow while respecting venue ethics and avoiding misuse.

Abstract: Research is advancing faster than ever with artificial intelligence (AI); and so are the corresponding research papers. The exploding volume of AI-generated papers have put a strain to peer review, leading to the usage of AI-generated review, potentially wide yet sneaky. However, relevant ethical concerns about confidentiality, quality, and fairness are raised and no consensus has been reached in the broad research community. We expect the debate to continue for a while, but in the meantime, we ask an alternative, practical question: \textit{can AI review improve paper drafting?}
  We study 20 computer architecture papers, with varying levels of submission lineage, to expose how well AI review aligns with human review, quantified by a set of metrics we define. To conduct the case study, we build a web UI-integrated tool, \emph{AI-Paper-Review}, that generates structured AI review of a draft paper, available at https://github.com/unarylab/ai-paper-review. This tool selects several AI reviewers from a diverse pool of AI reviewers and clusters and ranks their comments based on commonality and importance of review comments. It also allows to align AI comments with human comments to facilitate metric-based validation. The case study shows that AI review can cover a significant fraction of human-raised issues, but also raises issues missing in human review.
  This paper is not intended to encourage using AI for peer review at the current stage, but to study that (1) how AI review can improve paper drafting and (2) the potential and limitation of AI-based peer review. The release of the tool and the case study data is intended to instigate future research on this topic. Misuse for peer review would violate the ethics policies from major academic venues.

</details>


### [255] [Tackling the Root of Misinformation by Teaching Laypeople about Logical Fallacies via Socratic Questioning and Critical Argumentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01020)
*Minjing Shi,Junling Wang,Jingwei Ni,Sankalan Pal Chowdhury,Mrinmaya Sachan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents LFTutor, an LLM-powered intelligent tutor that teaches people to recognize logical fallacies using Socratic questioning and argumentation principles, outperforming vanilla LLM baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many people struggle to detect logical fallacies in everyday discourse, a problem that becomes more serious with the rise of LLMs that can generate and spread persuasive but fallacious misinformation at scale. The authors aim to see whether LLMs can be part of the solution by acting as tutors that improve users’ critical thinking and fallacy detection skills.

Method: The authors design LFTutor, an intelligent tutoring system built on top of LLMs. LFTutor incorporates intent-driven Socratic questioning and critical argumentation principles to actively engage learners, prompting them to reflect on and justify their reasoning about arguments and fallacies. They compare LFTutor against baseline LLM systems that do not use these pedagogical strategies, using a combination of automatic metrics and human evaluations.

Result: Experiments show that LFTutor enables learners to more effectively understand and identify logical fallacies compared with baseline LLM tutors lacking Socratic and argumentation-based scaffolding. Both automated evaluations and human studies indicate a significant performance improvement in learning outcomes and fallacy recognition.

Conclusion: Integrating explicit pedagogical scaffolding—Socratic questioning and argumentation principles—into LLM-based tutors can substantially enhance users’ ability to reason about arguments and detect logical fallacies. The study suggests that LLMs, when properly structured as tutors, can promote critical thinking and argument literacy in an AI-driven information ecosystem.

Abstract: Identifying logical fallacies in everyday discourse is challenging for many people. This challenge is amplified in the era of Large Language Models (LLMs), where malicious agents can deploy fallacious arguments to disseminate misinformation at scale. In this work, we explore the potential of LLMs as part of the solution. We introduce LFTutor, an intelligent tutoring system which uses LLMs to tutor laypeople and help them learn about logical fallacies. LFTutor integrates intent-driven Socratic questioning and critical argumentation principles to actively engage learners to reflect on their reasoning. Through both automatic and human evaluations, we demonstrate that LFTutor significantly outperforms baseline LLMs lacking these pedagogical strategies. This work highlights the promise of combining LLMs with pedagogical scaffolding to foster critical thinking and argument literacy in the age of AI.

</details>


### [256] [TriLens: Per-Layer Logit-Lens Entropy for White-Box Hallucination Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01033)
*Bohan Yang,Yijun Gong,Zhi Zhang,Ge Zhang,Wenpeng Xing,Meng Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TriLens is a white-box hallucination detector that tracks entropy trajectories of internal module readouts across layers to identify when LLMs are uncertain or conflicting internally, even if their final answer is confident.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM hallucinations produce wrong answers that may still be accompanied by detectable internal uncertainty or disagreement in intermediate computations. Existing detectors often focus on final outputs, external signals, or expensive sampling, missing internal signs that the model has not settled on a coherent continuation. The authors aim to exploit the model’s internal dynamics in a compact, efficient way to detect hallucinations more reliably.

Method: They introduce TriLens, which, at every transformer layer, reads three internal signals: (1) the multi-head self-attention output, (2) the feed-forward network (MLP) output, and (3) the residual stream. Each is passed through the model’s own logit lens (i.e., a linear readout into vocabulary logits), from which they compute the entropy of the resulting token distribution. This yields three entropy trajectories over depth, forming a 3L-dimensional feature vector per input. These trajectories summarize how certainty evolves across layers and modules without storing full hidden states or sampling multiple generations. They train or design a detector on top of these trajectories to classify whether an answer is a hallucination.

Result: TriLens provides strong hallucination-detection performance across different instruction-tuned LLMs and question-answering benchmarks. The three separate entropy trajectories (attention, MLP, residual) each capture different aspects of internal computation, and combining them improves detection compared with any single trajectory alone. The method works without needing multiple output samples or high-dimensional logging, making it comparatively efficient.

Conclusion: Hallucination signals are present in the internal dynamics of LLMs even when final outputs appear confident. By tracking how entropy over predicted tokens evolves across layers and modules, TriLens can effectively detect hallucinations in a compact and model-intrinsic way. This suggests that future hallucination-detection methods should consider the temporal settling of internal computation, not just the final-layer prediction or external uncertainty estimates.

Abstract: When a language model hallucinates, the final answer is wrong, but the mistake is not necessarily invisible inside the model. Different internal pathways may remain uncertain, disagree in how quickly they sharpen, or commit to competing continuations before the output is produced. We introduce TriLens, a white-box detector that turns this intuition into a compact representation: at every layer, it reads the multi-head self-attention output, the feed-forward output, and the residual stream through the model's own logit lens, then records only the entropy of each readout. The resulting 3L-dimensional trajectory describes how certainty forms across depth and across modules, without storing high-dimensional hidden states or sampling multiple generations. This simple signal yields a strong detector across instruction-tuned LLMs and QA benchmarks, and our analyses show that the three module-wise entropy trajectories provide complementary evidence. TriLens suggests that hallucination detection can benefit from tracking how internal computation settles, not only what the final layer predicts.

</details>


### [257] [TravelEval: A Comprehensive Benchmarking Framework for Evaluating LLM-Powered Travel Planning Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01046)
*Weiyi Chen,Shuaixiong Wang,Ziyun Gao,Kaichun Hu,Wangze Ni,Shimin Di,Chen Jason Zhang,Lei Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TravelEval is a new, realistic benchmark for evaluating LLM-based travel planners using a six-dimensional, simulation-based framework and real-world data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations for LLM-based travel planning focus too much on simple constraint satisfaction and use synthetic or narrow datasets. They ignore important dimensions like spatio-temporal cost, realistic lodging and transport, and the way day-by-day decisions interact in a full trip. This makes it hard to understand how well LLMs actually plan practical multi-day trips in the real world.

Method: The authors build TravelEval, which has three main components: (1) a six-dimensional evaluation framework that scores plans along accuracy, constraint compliance, temporality, spatiality, economy, and utility; (2) a realistic data sandbox including accurate accommodation prices and genuine intercity transport information; and (3) a simulation-based global evaluation procedure that reconstructs full trips by integrating geographic APIs and detailed queuing time to emulate real travel execution. They then apply this benchmark to systematically test 12 different mainstream LLM planning approaches, including agent-based strategies.

Result: Using TravelEval, the authors find that current LLMs perform poorly on globally optimized, multi-dimensional travel planning. They particularly struggle with spatio-temporal reasoning (e.g., routing and scheduling across locations and days) and sticking to budgets. Agentic or tool-using reasoning strategies do not consistently improve performance over simpler baselines under these more realistic and comprehensive criteria.

Conclusion: TravelEval offers a more faithful and holistic way to benchmark LLM-based travel planners, grounding evaluation in realistic data and multi-dimensional metrics. The findings indicate that substantial progress is still needed for robust, globally optimized trip planning and that current agentic methods are not a silver bullet. The benchmark can serve as a standard testbed for future methods aiming to improve LLM travel planning in practice.

Abstract: The development of Large Language Models (LLMs) has significantly improved travel planning applications, yet evaluating such models is limited by existing benchmarks' limitations: 1) overemphasis on constraint compliance, neglecting multi-dimensional qualities like spatio-temporal cost; 2) datasets lacking real-world authenticity and coverage in key areas (e.g., lodging, transport); and 3) isolated daily plan assessments that miss critical details (e.g., the impact of daily accommodation and visit pacing) needed for entire plan's evaluation. To address this gap, we introduce TravelEval, a realistic and comprehensive benchmark.
