TravelEval features 1) a novel six-dimensional evaluation framework to holistically assess plans across accuracy, compliance, temporality, spatiality, economy, and utility dimensions; 2) a highly realistic data sandbox with precise accommodation pricing and authentic intercity transportation data; and 3) a simulation-based global evaluation method that emulates complete travel plans with API-integrated geographic information and fine-grained queuing time. Evaluating 12 mainstream approaches with TravelEval reveals several valuable insights, such that LLMs struggle with globally-optimized multi-dimensional planning (especially in spatio-temporal reasoning and budget compliance), and agentic reasoning strategies offer no consistent improvement. Concisely, TravelEval facilitates travel plan evaluation via grounded spatio-temporal emulation and comprehensive metrics, providing a robust foundation for advancing LLM-powered travel planning research and applications.

</details>


### [258] [AnyEdit++: Adaptive Long-Form Knowledge Editing via Bayesian Surprise](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01053)
*Bowen Tian,Caixue He,Jiemin Wu,Jingying Wang,Wenshuo Chen,Zexi Li,Yutao Yue*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces AnyEdit++, a structure-aware framework for editing long-form knowledge in large language models (LLMs) that improves coherence and control by segmenting text using Bayesian surprise rather than fixed windows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Editing complex, long-form knowledge in LLMs is difficult because changes must stay coherent over long contexts. Existing autoregressive editing methods relax length constraints but use fixed-size chunks that ignore the logical and semantic structure of the text, causing cross-chunk inconsistencies and degraded generation quality. The authors aim to enable precise, robust edits over long documents while preserving global coherence by making the chunking process structure-aware.

Method: The authors propose AnyEdit++, which replaces fixed-window chunking with Bayes-Chunk, an adaptive segmentation mechanism. Bayes-Chunk uses Bayesian Surprise to detect semantic boundaries and split text at points of high surprisal, aligning chunks with natural structural units. They develop a theoretical framework with two principles: (1) Structural Independence, showing that cross-segment interference is minimized when anchor keys in different segments are geometrically orthogonal, which their surprisal-based segmentation approximately satisfies; and (2) Causal Locality, proving that injecting edits at semantic peaks (the surprisal-based boundaries) yields better control than editing at arbitrary positions. They integrate this into an autoregressive editing pipeline for LLMs.

Result: Through experiments on mathematical reasoning, code generation, and narrative tasks, AnyEdit++ outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both performance and robustness. The results indicate more coherent edits over long contexts, reduced interference across segments, and better preservation of intended changes compared with methods using fixed-window chunking.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that structural awareness—via adaptive, surprisal-based segmentation—is crucial for effective long-form knowledge editing in LLMs. AnyEdit++ demonstrates that aligning segments with semantic boundaries improves both theoretical properties (independence and locality of edits) and empirical outcomes, offering a more reliable framework for large-scale, coherent model editing than fixed-window approaches.

Abstract: Editing complex, long-form knowledge in Large Language Models remains a significant challenge due to the difficulty of maintaining generation coherence. Existing autoregressive methods like AnyEdit alleviate length constraints but rely on Fixed-window Chunking, which disregards logical structure and compromises consistency. To address this, we present AnyEdit++, a structure-aware framework incorporating Bayes-Chunk, an adaptive segmentation mechanism that dynamically identifies semantic boundaries based on Bayesian Surprise. We underpin this approach with a theoretical framework establishing two key principles: (1) Structural Independence: we prove that cross-segment interference is minimized when anchor keys are geometrically orthogonal (a condition naturally satisfied by our surprisal-based boundaries but violated by fixed windows), and (2) Causal Locality: we demonstrate that updates injected at these semantic peaks yield strictly superior control compared to arbitrary split points. Extensive experiments across mathematical reasoning, code generation, and narrative tasks demonstrate that AnyEdit++ achieves superior performance and robustness compared to state-of-the-art baselines, validating that structural awareness is critical for effective long-form knowledge editing.

</details>


### [259] [DAG-MoE: From Simple Mixture to Structural Aggregation in Mixture-of-Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01062)
*Jiarui Feng,Hanqing Zeng,Karish Grover,Ruizhong Qiu,Yinglong Xia,Qiang Zhang,Qifan Wang,Ren Chen,Dongqi Fu,Jiayi Liu,Zhoukai Zhao,Xiangjun Fan,Benyu Zhang,Yixin Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes DAG-MoE, a new Mixture-of-Experts framework that changes how expert outputs are aggregated using a learned DAG structure, expanding the space of expert combinations and improving language modeling performance without increasing routing cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mixture-of-Experts models decouple parameter count from compute, but scaling them is hard. Fine-grained experts increase flexibility by enlarging expert-combination space, yet introduce high routing overhead that becomes a scalability bottleneck. The authors are motivated to find an orthogonal way to scale MoE power—by improving how selected experts are combined—without further increasing routing complexity or modifying the experts themselves.

Method: The authors analyze the expert aggregation step theoretically and show that the standard weighted-sum aggregation restricts the realizable combinations of experts. They introduce structural aggregation, in which expert outputs are composed according to a learned structure rather than a simple convex combination. Concretely, they propose DAG-MoE, where for each MoE layer a lightweight module learns a directed acyclic graph over the selected experts and aggregates their outputs following this DAG. This preserves sparsity and routing while expanding the functional capacity of the layer, enabling multi-step transformations within a single MoE layer.

Result: Under standard language modeling benchmarks, DAG-MoE is empirically evaluated in both pretraining and fine-tuning settings. Across these experiments, DAG-MoE consistently outperforms traditional MoE architectures that use weighted-sum aggregation, demonstrating better perplexity and downstream task performance at comparable compute and parameter budgets.

Conclusion: Changing the aggregation mechanism in MoE layers from a simple weighted sum to a learned structural aggregation significantly expands the expressive capacity of MoE models without increasing routing overhead. DAG-MoE leverages a learned DAG over experts to enable richer expert compositions and possible multi-step reasoning inside each MoE layer, leading to consistent performance gains over standard MoE baselines in language modeling. This suggests that expert aggregation is a powerful and underexplored axis for scaling MoE architectures.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models have become a leading approach for decoupling parameter count from computational cost in large language models, yet effectively scaling MoE performance remains a challenge. Prior work shows that fine-grained experts enlarge the space of expert combinations and improve flexibility, but they also impose substantial routing overhead, creating a new scalability bottleneck. In this paper, we explore a complementary axis for scaling -- how expert outputs are aggregated. We theoretically show that replacing the standard weighted-summation aggregation with structural aggregation expands the expert-combination space without altering the experts or router, and enables possible multi-step reasoning within a single MoE layer. To this end, we propose DAG-MoE, a sparse MoE framework that employs a lightweight module to automatically learn the optimal aggregation structure among the selected experts. Extensive experiments under standard language modeling settings show that DAG-MoE consistently improves performance in both pretraining and fine-tuning, surpassing traditional MoE baselines.

</details>


### [260] [MindClaw: Closed-Loop Embodied Mental-State Reasoning for Precision Intervention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01063)
*Ruoxuan Zhang,Qiaoqiao Wan,Zhengguang Wang,Chenghao Yu,Hongxia Xie,Jianlong Fu,Wen-Huang Cheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes MindClaw, a framework for real-time, closed-loop Theory of Mind reasoning in embodied agents, enabling them to decide when and how to intervene helpfully rather than only answering offline questions or predicting final actions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Theory of Mind benchmarks and systems mainly focus on offline text or multimodal recognition of mental states and on final action prediction. They do not sufficiently evaluate whether an embodied agent can continuously track a changing environment, maintain and update beliefs about specific actors, determine when explicit mental-state reasoning is necessary, and intervene only when assistance is truly helpful. This gap limits the deployment of human-centered embodied assistants that must operate in real time and avoid over- or under-intervening.

Method: Building on the prior MindPower framework, the authors introduce MindClaw, which extends robot-centric ToM reasoning to a real-time, closed-loop setting. MindClaw integrates multiple components: multi-source perceptual inputs, a belief memory for actor-specific mental states, an embodied cognitive trigger skill that decides when ToM reasoning should be activated, a mental reasoning module, and an action generation module. The system is designed to produce contextually appropriate interventions at the right time and remain silent when intervention is unnecessary. The authors compare MindClaw against direct vision-language model (VLM) baselines in embodied ToM tasks.

Result: Experimental evaluations show that direct VLM baselines struggle with maintaining task awareness and calibrating when to intervene, leading either to excessive or insufficient assistance. MindClaw outperforms these baselines on overall performance metrics related to helpfulness and timing of interventions, indicating more precise and context-aware assistance in closed-loop settings.

Conclusion: The study concludes that optimizing the trigger skill—i.e., deciding when to invoke mental-state reasoning and intervene—is crucial for effective closed-loop embodied Theory of Mind assistance. MindClaw demonstrates that integrating belief memory, real-time perception, and a dedicated cognitive trigger results in more reliable and precisely timed interventions than direct VLM approaches, advancing practical human-centered embodied ToM systems.

Abstract: Theory of Mind (ToM) enables an agent to reason about another actor's beliefs, goals, and intentions, which is essential for human-centered embodied assistance. Existing ToM benchmarks have advanced text and multimodal mental-state recognition, but they mostly evaluate offline question answering or final action prediction. They do not fully test whether an embodied agent can stay connected to a changing environment, update actor-specific beliefs, decide when reasoning is needed, and intervene only when help is useful. Building on MindPower, we extend robot-centric ToM reasoning to a real-time closed-loop setting and introduce MindClaw, a framework for embodied mental-state reasoning with precision intervention. MindClaw connects multi-source inputs, belief memory, an embodied cognitive trigger skill, mental reasoning, and action generation, allowing the agent to output helpful actions at the right time while remaining silent when intervention is unnecessary. Experiments show that direct VLM baselines struggle with task awareness and intervention calibration, while MindClaw achieves the best overall performance, demonstrating the importance of trigger-skill optimization for closed-loop embodied ToM assistance.

</details>


### [261] [Before the Model Learns the Bug:Fuzzing RLVR Verifiers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01066)
*Jaideep Ray*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RL with verifiable rewards (RLVR) uses programmatic reward functions instead of human labels, but if those verifiers are buggy, the agent can exploit them; this paper builds a framework to fuzz such verifiers and quantify their failure modes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As RLHF scales, collecting human preference labels becomes expensive and inconsistent, so RLVR proposes to replace humans with automatic verifiers (e.g., checkers for math, JSON, or code). However, these verifiers are themselves fallible software whose bugs can be optimized against by powerful models, potentially leading to reward hacking and unsafe behavior. The paper is motivated by the need to systematically understand and measure how such verifier bugs can be found and exploited by RL systems.

Method: The authors design a lightweight verifier-fuzzing framework that automatically generates adversarial model completions targeted at specific verifiers. It then runs both a potentially buggy production verifier and a stricter reference verifier on the same completions, logs and compares their decisions, and classifies discrepancies. The framework outputs structured metrics such as false positives (incorrectly accepted), false negatives (incorrectly rejected), generic disagreements, clear exploits where the model passes the buggy verifier but fails the strict one, and uncertainty cases.

Result: Using this framework, the authors can systematically surface and quantify vulnerabilities in executable reward functions used for RLVR. The tool reveals where verifiers give incorrect rewards, how often they can be exploited, and how robust they are under adversarial pressure. The results demonstrate that even simple-looking verifiers can have exploitable bugs and that these failures can be measured in a comparable way across tasks.

Conclusion: Executable reward functions are powerful but brittle: when used in RLVR, bugs in verifiers become optimization targets for the model. A dedicated verifier-fuzzing framework can expose these bugs, categorize their impacts (false positives/negatives, exploits, etc.), and provide actionable metrics for improving verifiers. The paper argues that any deployment of RLVR should include systematic testing and hardening of verifiers against adversarial completions.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) replaces human preference labels with executable reward functions such as math answer checkers, JSON tool-call validators, and code unit-test harnesses. That makes the reward partly a software artifact: if the verifier is wrong, optimization can learn the bug. We study this failure mode with a lightweight verifier-fuzzing framework that generates adversarial completions, compares buggy and stricter reference verifiers, logs paired decisions, and reports false-positive, false-negative, disagreement, exploit, and uncertainty metrics.

</details>


### [262] [CAREAgent: Clinical Agent with Structured Reasoning and Tool-Integrated for Order Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01094)
*Ruihui Hou,Ziyue Huai,Chennuo Zhang,Ziyan Liu,Siran Zhao,Yao Yu,Jie Zhai,Tong Ruan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents CAREAgent, a specialized agent for generating fine-grained, executable clinical orders from clinical decisions, trained with a two-stage data construction and learning pipeline, achieving better performance than existing agentic methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While existing clinical AI agents assist with high-level decisions, they typically fail to generate detailed, executable clinical orders that can be used directly in practice. There is a need for systems that can bridge this gap by modeling realistic clinical tool usage and producing clinically valid orders with verifiable reasoning.

Method: The authors design CAREAgent, an agent framework for clinical order generation. They construct training data using a two-stage agentic reasoning process: (1) create verifiable reasoning trajectories that mimic real clinical tool use; (2) filter these trajectories based on format, order validity, and clinical plausibility. The model is first trained with supervised fine-tuning on this curated data to learn reasoning formats and domain knowledge, then further optimized using reinforcement learning with multi-dimensional rewards to strengthen complex clinical reasoning.

Result: On several benchmarks, including the unseen ClinicalBench dataset, CAREAgent outperforms prior single-agent, multi-agent, and agentic reasoning baselines. Specifically on ClinicalBench, it improves F1 score by 5.05%, 2.09%, and 0.86% over those three types of methods, respectively, indicating more accurate clinical order generation.

Conclusion: CAREAgent effectively bridges high-level clinical decision-making and practical execution by generating fine-grained, executable clinical orders. The combination of structured agentic data construction, supervised fine-tuning, and reinforcement learning leads to improved complex clinical reasoning and better benchmark performance compared with existing agent-based approaches.

Abstract: Clinical order generation serves as a critical bridge between clinical decision-making and real-world practice, translating medical decisions into concrete and executable orders. Existing agents mainly focus on coarse-grained decisions and overlook the fine-grained, executable information required for clinical orders. To address this gap, we propose CAREAgent, an agent for clinical order generation. To support its training, we introduce a two-stage agentic reasoning data construction method. First, we design an agent framework that constructs verifiable reasoning trajectories aligned with realistic clinical tool usage. Second, we filter reasoning trajectories by format compliance, order validity, and clinical plausibility. Building on the constructed data, the model is first trained via supervised fine-tuning to acquire fundamental reasoning formats and medical knowledge, and is subsequently optimized through reinforcement learning with multi-dimensional reward functions to enhance complex clinical reasoning capabilities. Experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of CAREAgent. On ClinicalBench (unseen during training), CAREAgent improves the F1 score by 5.05%, 2.09%, and 0.86% over the single-agent, multi-agent, and agentic reasoning methods, respectively.

</details>


### [263] [Diagnosing LLM Arbitration Behavior over Pre-evidence Epistemic States in RAG-based Fact-Checking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01120)
*Yuxi Sun,Wenbo Shang,Wei Gao,Xin Huang,Jing Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces PAVE, a benchmark to analyze how LLM-based verifiers in RAG fact-checking balance their internal (parametric) knowledge with retrieved evidence, and proposes a test-time method to improve this arbitration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for fact-checking, LLMs act as verifiers that must judge claims using both their internal knowledge and retrieved evidence. However, the models can have pre-evidence biases based on parametric knowledge that may conflict with the retrieved context. Existing evaluations do not explicitly measure how these models behave when their prior beliefs disagree with the evidence, nor how reliably they update or maintain beliefs in such situations. A new framework is needed to characterize and evaluate this prior-versus-context arbitration behavior.

Method: The authors introduce PAVE (Prior-Aware Verifier Evaluation), which decomposes an LLM verifier’s behavior into four epistemic states defined by whether its prior belief (before seeing evidence) is correct or incorrect and whether that belief is held with high or low confidence. Using this stratification, they construct a diagnostic testbed that evaluates how the verifier arbitrates between its prior and retrieved evidence: whether it stays with a correct prior in the presence of misleading evidence, and whether it corrects an incorrect prior given accurate evidence. They evaluate seven different LLMs on this benchmark to compare arbitration behaviors. Building on the insights, they propose a lightweight, test-time arbitration method based on Jensen–Shannon divergence (JSD) that adjusts decisions by comparing distributions without altering model parameters.

Result: Across seven LLMs, the study finds that arbitration between parametric prior and retrieved context is unreliable and varies substantially by model. Some models over-trust their priors, others over-trust context, and none show consistently robust behavior across all epistemic states. Applying the proposed JSD-based test-time arbitration improves factual reliability across these different LLM families, reaching performance competitive with stronger or more heavily tuned baselines, and does so without any model fine-tuning or architectural changes.

Conclusion: PAVE exposes significant weaknesses and model-specific differences in how LLM verifiers in RAG-based fact-checking balance internal knowledge and external evidence. This underscores that verifier choice and arbitration strategy critically affect reliability in real-world fact-checking systems. The proposed JSD-based test-time arbitration offers a practical, model-agnostic way to improve factual robustness by better mediating between priors and context, without needing to modify or retrain the underlying LLMs.

Abstract: In RAG-based fact-checking, LLMs are increasingly used as verifiers to check given claims against retrieved evidence. Their parametric knowledge can induce pre-evidence tendencies that may conflict with the retrieved context, yet existing evaluation frameworks do not characterize such prior-context discrepancy or measure how verifiers arbitrate between parametric and contextual signals. We introduce \textsc{PAVE} (\emph{Prior-Aware Verifier Evaluation}), a diagnostic testbed that stratifies an LLM verifier into four epistemic states based on the correctness and confidence of its pre-evidence prior and evaluates its arbitration behavior on this new benchmark, i.e., whether it persists in correct prior under misleading evidence, and whether it corrects wrong prior when accurate evidence is provided. Experiments across seven LLMs reveal unreliable and highly model-dependent prior-context arbitration, highlighting the importance of verifier selection for real-world RAG-based fact-checking applications. Based on these findings, we propose a lightweight JSD-based test-time arbitration method that improves factual reliability without modifying the underlying model, achieving competitive performance across diverse LLM families.

</details>


### [264] [SkillRevise: Improving LLM-Authored Agent Skills via Trace-Conditioned Skill Revision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01139)
*Yuxuan Liu,Zhaochen Su,Lingyun Xie,Yuhao Zhang,Qing Zong,Jiahe Guo,Zhongwei Xie,Yiyan Ji,Yauwai Yim,Hongyu Luo,Xiyu Ren,Ruan Chenyu,Haoran Li,Yangqiu Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SkillRevise is a framework that iteratively refines imperfect agent skills for LLM agents using execution feedback, substantially improving success rates and enabling cross-model transfer of procedural knowledge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for improving LLM agent skills rely on large collections of past trajectories and thus fail in cold-start scenarios where only a single, imperfect initial skill is available. In practice, this leads to a reliance on either costly expert-authored skills that may not match how LLM agents actually operate, or one-shot LLM-generated skills that look syntactically correct but perform poorly. There is a need for an automated way to take these initial, flawed skills and systematically improve them based on how they actually behave during execution.

Method: The paper proposes SkillRevise, an execution-grounded skill refinement framework. Starting from an initial imperfect skill, SkillRevise (1) executes the skill to collect evidence about its behavior, (2) diagnoses specific defects observed during execution, (3) retrieves relevant repair principles from a general memory of fix patterns, and (4) applies targeted, execution-anchored edits to the skill. It then re-executes revised skill candidates, measures their empirical performance, and uses this feedback loop to select and retain the best-performing skill version. This process is repeated iteratively to converge on a robust skill representation.

Result: Across three benchmarks and five different LLMs, SkillRevise consistently outperforms one-shot skill generation baselines. On the SkillsBench benchmark, it increases the base agent’s success rate from 36.05% to 61.63%. The experiments also show that skills refined by SkillRevise generalize well across different LLMs, indicating that the method captures model-agnostic procedural knowledge rather than overfitting to artifacts of a single model.

Conclusion: SkillRevise provides an effective solution to the cold-start problem in LLM agent skill construction by iteratively refining initial, imperfect skills using execution feedback. It reduces reliance on costly expert engineering or fragile one-shot skill generation and produces skills that not only significantly boost task success rates but also transfer well across models. This demonstrates that execution-grounded, repair-principle-driven refinement is a powerful paradigm for building robust, reusable agent skills.

Abstract: Agent skills are procedural artifacts that enable LLM agents to execute workflows, verify constraints, and recover from failures. Existing self-evolving methods refine skills using accumulated trajectories. However, they struggle in cold-start settings, where only an initial, imperfect skill is available. Consequently, skill construction defaults to expert authoring or one-shot LLM generation. Expert-authored skills are costly and may not align with how LLM agents actually execute tasks, while one-shot generated skills can be syntactically well formed yet behaviorally weak. To bridge this gap, we propose SkillRevise, an execution-grounded framework designed to iteratively refine these initial skills. SkillRevise diagnoses skill defects from execution evidence, retrieves relevant repair principles from a general memory, and applies execution-anchored edits. By re-executing candidates and measuring empirical utility, it systematically retains the optimal skill version. Evaluated across three benchmarks and five LLMs, SkillRevise substantially outperforms one-shot baselines, improving the base agent's success rate on SkillsBench from 36.05% to 61.63%. Furthermore, the revised skills exhibit strong cross-model transferability, capturing generalized procedural knowledge over model-specific artifacts.

</details>


### [265] [Reasoning4Sciences: Bridging Reasoning Language Models to All Scientific Branches](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01145)
*Teddy Ferdinan,Bartłomiej Koptyra,Mikołaj Langner,Tomasz Adamczyk,Łukasz Radliński,Maciej Markiewicz,Aleksander Szczęsny,Stanisław Woźniak,Tymoteusz Romanowicz,Dzmitry Pihulski,Mateusz Zbrocki,Mateusz Śmigielski,Michał Rajkowski,Mateusz Biedka,Konrad Kiełczyński,Konrad Wojtasik,Jacek Duszenko,Jan Eliasz,Piotr Matys,Michał Bernacki-Janson,Maria Bellaniar Ismiati,Latius Hermawan,Wiktoria Mieleszczenko-Kowszewicz,Anna Kubicka-Sowinska,Grzegorz Chodak,Karol Postawa,Paweł Zyblewski,Tomasz Szandała,Łukasz Sterczewski,Adrian Chajec,Pawel Niewiadomski,Piotr Gruber,Marcin Wdowikowski,Sławomir Czarnecki,Bartłomiej Kryszak,Dominik Drabik,Tomasz Kajdanowicz,Kamil Mamak,Paweł Preś,Katarzyna Paczkowska,Joachim Sobczuk,Tomasz Zięba,Jan Kocoń,Maciej Piasecki,Przemysław Kazienko*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Survey of how reasoning language models are adopted, evaluated, and matured across 28 scientific disciplines, revealing major cross-field disparities and outlining challenges and future directions for broader adoption.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reasoning language models are advancing research productivity mainly in hard sciences, leaving other disciplines behind and creating a widening productivity gap. There is a lack of systematic understanding of how RLMs are being adopted across different scientific fields, which hampers informed development, evaluation, and policy for their broader use.

Method: Conduct a comprehensive cross-disciplinary survey based on the European Research Council’s classification of 28 scientific disciplines. Analyze how RLMs are developed, evaluated, and applied in each field. Propose and apply a maturity-oriented assessment framework that scores disciplines according to the availability and quality of domain-specific development and evaluation resources, distinguishing between all resources and only publicly available ones.

Result: The survey maps the current landscape of RLM adoption across Social Sciences and Humanities, Physical Sciences and Engineering, and Life Sciences. It finds substantial disparities in RLM maturity across disciplines, with hard sciences generally having more advanced development and evaluation ecosystems. The disparities become even larger when considering only publicly available resources. The work also identifies emerging implementation paradigms and common patterns of use across fields.

Conclusion: RLM adoption is highly uneven across scientific disciplines, with some fields benefiting far more from current tools and resources. A maturity-based perspective helps make these gaps visible and comparable, underscoring the need for targeted investments in domain-specific datasets, benchmarks, and tools, especially in under-served disciplines. Addressing these gaps and engaging with shared implementation paradigms will be crucial to enable more equitable and effective RLM-supported research across all areas of science.

Abstract: While Reasoning Language Models (RLMs) are rapidly emerging as powerful tools for scientific research, their impact is primarily concentrated in "hard science" fields. The slow -- or lack of -- adoption of RLMs in other branches of science is causing a widening gap in research productivity. In this survey, we provide the first comprehensive analysis of RLM adoption across 28 scientific disciplines following the classification used by the European Research Council (ERC), spanning the Social Sciences and Humanities, Physical Sciences and Engineering, and Life Sciences. We examine how RLMs are developed, evaluated, and applied across disciplines. Furthermore, we introduce a maturity-oriented assessment framework based on available domain-specific development and evaluation resources, revealing substantial disparities in RLM maturity that become even more pronounced when only publicly available resources are considered. Finally, we highlight current implementation paradigms that are gaining popularity across disciplines, current challenges, and future directions in enabling RLM adoption across science.

</details>


### [266] [Expected Value Alignment for Generative Reward Modeling in Formal Mathematics Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01160)
*Shihao Ji,Haotao Tan,Zihui Song,Mingyu Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes Expected Value Alignment (EVA), a method to get continuous reward scores from language models while keeping their outputs discrete and interpretable, and applies it to Lean 4 theorem proving.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Process reward models are needed to score intermediate reasoning steps in theorem-prover settings, but current designs face a trade-off: value-head models output continuous scores but alter the generative interface, while generative reward models keep textual rationales yet perform poorly at continuous regression because numeric scores are tokenized. A better design should provide smooth continuous rewards without sacrificing the benefits of generative text critiques.

Method: EVA asks the model to output integer scores inside a structured JSON format, treating those integers as ‘anchor tokens’. Instead of taking the discrete integer as the final score, EVA computes a continuous-valued reward as the expected value over the model’s logit distribution on these anchor tokens. The model is trained with standard causal language modeling plus an auxiliary mean squared error loss that aligns these expectation-based scores with ground-truth rewards. This yields a PRM that preserves discrete textual outputs but exposes a continuous reward signal through its logits.

Result: The authors instantiate EVA in a reward model called Leibniz for Lean 4 formal verification and compare it with zero-shot and conventional reward-modeling baselines. Their experiments show that the logit-based expectation scoring greatly reduces discretization artifacts associated with integer outputs, while still allowing generative rationales. This leads to better-behaved continuous reward signals in the theorem-proving setting.

Conclusion: EVA offers a practical way to design process reward models that maintain the interpretability and usability of generative language models while providing high-quality continuous rewards. In the Lean 4 case study, it outperforms standard baselines by smoothing out the artifacts from discrete scoring, suggesting it is a promising direction for scaling RL and search over formal reasoning processes.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used with formal interactive theorem provers such as Lean 4. Scaling these systems with reinforcement learning or search methods requires process reward models (PRMs) that can evaluate intermediate reasoning steps. Existing reward-model designs expose a practical trade-off. Value-head models provide continuous scores but modify the generative model interface, while generative reward models preserve textual rationales but are poorly matched to continuous floating-point regression because numeric values are split across tokens. We introduce Expected Value Alignment (EVA), a reward-modeling procedure that keeps the surface output discrete while extracting continuous scores from the model's token distribution. The model emits integer scores in a structured JSON format, and EVA computes a continuous score as the expectation over the logits of the corresponding anchor tokens. Training combines the causal language modeling objective with an auxiliary mean squared error loss on these expected values. We instantiate EVA in \textit{Leibniz}, a reward model for Lean 4 formal verification, and evaluate it against zero-shot and reward-modeling baselines. The evaluation demonstrates that continuous logit-based scoring significantly reduces discretization artifacts while retaining the interpretability of generative critiques.

</details>


### [267] [Deft Scheduling of Dynamic Cloud Workflows with Varying Deadlines via Mixture-of-Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01162)
*Ya Shen,Gang Chen,Hui Ma,Mengjie Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes DEFT, a deadline-aware mixture-of-experts DRL architecture for scheduling dynamic workflow DAGs in cloud environments, achieving lower cost and fewer deadline violations than existing DRL baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DRL-based cloud workflow schedulers use single-path, monolithic policies that struggle to handle diverse workflows with varying deadline tightness and dynamic VM conditions, leading to poor performance under heterogeneous deadline requirements.

Method: They design DEFT, a Mixture-of-Experts DRL policy where each expert is specialized for a different level of deadline tightness. A graph-adaptive gating network encodes workflow DAG structures, task states, deadlines, and VM conditions via cross-attention, and then selects or weights experts for each scheduling decision. The policy is trained on dynamic cloud workflow benchmarks.

Result: On dynamic workflow scheduling benchmarks, DEFT substantially reduces execution cost and deadline violations compared with several state-of-the-art DRL schedulers, demonstrating better adaptability across a wide range of deadline constraints.

Conclusion: A deadline-perceptive Mixture-of-Experts architecture with graph-adaptive gating is effective for dynamic cloud workflow scheduling, enabling adaptive expert selection and superior performance over monolithic DRL policies in terms of both cost and deadline satisfaction.

Abstract: Workflow scheduling in cloud computing demands the intelligent allocation of dynamically arriving, graph-structured workflows with varying deadlines onto ever-changing virtual machine resources. However, existing deep reinforcement learning (DRL) schedulers remain limited by rigid, single-path inference architectures that struggle to handle diverse scheduling scenarios. We introduce \textbf{DEFT} (\textbf{D}eadline-p\textbf{E}rceptive Mixture-o\textbf{F}-Exper\textbf{t}s), an innovative DRL policy architecture that leverages a specialized mixture of experts, each trained to manage different levels of deadline tightness. To our knowledge, DEFT is the first to introduce and validate a Mixture-of-Experts architecture for dynamic cloud workflow scheduling. By adaptively routing decisions through the most appropriate experts, DEFT is capable of meeting a broad spectrum of deadline requirements that no single expert can achieve. Central to DEFT is a \textbf{graph-adaptive} gating mechanism that encodes workflow deadlines and DAGs, task states, and VM conditions, using cross-attention to guide expert activation in a fine-grained, deadline-sensitive manner. Experiments on dynamic cloud workflow benchmarks demonstrate that DEFT significantly reduces execution cost and deadline violations, outperforming multiple state-of-the-art DRL baselines.

</details>


### [268] ["Skill issues'': data-centric optimization of lakehouse agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01185)
*Nicole Rose Schneider,Davide Ghilardi,Giacomo Piccinini,Jacopo Tagliabue*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They optimize “skills” and environment configurations for coding agents that operate on a branching lakehouse, using the fact that agents’ code produces verifiable changes in data state.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Coding agents increasingly interact programmatically with complex data systems, but performance depends heavily on the quality of their task-specific skills and environment files, not just model quality. Existing evaluation/optimization is often output-centric and read-only, which is ill-suited for systems where agents modify data. A branching lakehouse with Git-like operations provides rich, inspectable state changes that could be leveraged to better evaluate and improve these agents, but how to systematically exploit this structure is unclear.

Method: They use Bauplan, a branching lakehouse exposing Git-like data primitives (branches, commits, merges) and headless APIs, and reframe agent evaluation as verifying resulting data state rather than matching textual outputs. They build a data-centric optimization pipeline that: (1) automatically generates task–verifier pairs, where each verifier can programmatically check whether the lakehouse state after execution satisfies the task; (2) executes candidate agent skills in isolated sandboxes/branches; and (3) scores agent trajectories using both execution traces and verifier-based checks on lakehouse state. These signals are then used to optimize the agent skills and environment artifacts.

Result: In an initial experiment with 25 tasks on Bauplan, applying their optimization pipeline to agent skills yields a 31.9% improvement in task accuracy relative to unoptimized skills.

Conclusion: By treating data workflows as write-path processes in a branching lakehouse and verifying the resulting data state, one can more effectively evaluate and optimize coding agents’ skills and environment files. This suggests that systems exposing Git-like data primitives and supporting sandboxed branches are a strong substrate for training and refining data agents, particularly for tasks that involve modifying, not just reading, data.

Abstract: Coding agents are becoming users of data infrastructure, but their success depends not only on model quality: it also depends on the skills and environment files that teach agents how to use a system. We study how to optimize these artifacts for agents operating on a branching lakehouse, Bauplan. In our setting, headless APIs and Git-like data primitives expose data workflows through code, branches, commits, and merges. Our central observation is that a branching lakehouse turns data-agent evaluation from an output-matching problem into a state-verification problem: agent-generated pipeline code induces concrete, inspectable lakehouse changes. We present a data-centric optimization pipeline that generates task-verifier pairs, executes candidate skills in isolated sandboxes, and scores trajectories using both trace-level signals and programmatic checks over lakehouse state. In a preliminary evaluation on 25 tasks, optimized skills improve accuracy by 31.9%. These results suggest that write-path data workflows provide a useful substrate for optimizing agent skills beyond read-only tasks.

</details>


### [269] [The Case for Model Science: Verify, Explore, Steer, Refine](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01189)
*Przemyslaw Biecek,Luca Longo,Jianlong Zhou,Thomas Fel,Andreas Holzinger,Wojciech Samek*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes "Model Science" as a new, systematic discipline focused on understanding AI models beyond benchmarks, structured around four functional perspectives and supported by shared infrastructure and deep single-model analyses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI models are now widely deployed but poorly understood. Benchmark-driven research mainly answers whether models work, not how or why, and it misses critical failure modes like hallucinations and shortcut learning. Other mature sciences show that systematic, multi-level analysis, specialised training, and shared infrastructure are essential for cumulative understanding, highlighting the need for a similar approach in AI.

Method: Conceptual and organizational: the authors synthesize lessons from cognitive science, neuroscience, medicine, and agriculture to propose a framework for a new discipline. They define four functional perspectives—Verify, Explore, Steer, Refine—for structuring research on model behaviour, and outline required infrastructure such as catalogues of datasets, models, and findings, as well as methodological emphasis on deep analysis of individual model instances.

Result: They present a structured framework and taxonomy (Verify, Explore, Steer, Refine) for organizing model analysis work; they articulate requirements for shared infrastructure to support cumulative knowledge; and they argue, with examples from other sciences, for the importance of detailed single-model case studies alongside population-level comparisons.

Conclusion: Benchmarking alone is insufficient for understanding and responsibly deploying complex AI systems. The field should develop "Model Science"—a systematic discipline grounded in four complementary functional perspectives, supported by shared catalogues and infrastructure, and enriched by deep, single-model analyses—to build cumulative, actionable knowledge about model behaviour and failure modes.

Abstract: We argue that the AI community is now ready to move beyond benchmarking and consolidate scattered efforts in model analysis into a systematic discipline, a direction we term Model Science. Complex AI models now serve billions of users, yet our understanding of how they work lags far behind our ability to deploy them. Decades of benchmark-driven research have delivered remarkable progress: extensive leaderboards, a wide range of performance metrics, tracking capability gains across diverse tasks; yet this success has also revealed the limits of benchmarks as they tell us whether models perform but not why they succeed or fail, they miss critical failure modes, such as hallucinations or shortcuts. Precedents from established sciences point the way forward: cognitive science shows that understanding complex systems requires complementary levels of analysis; neuroscience demonstrates that deep study of single cases reveals what population studies miss; medicine teaches that specialised training must develop alongside research practice; and agriculture models how shared infrastructure and principles enable cumulative progress. These lessons inform three foundations for Model Science. First, we propose to consolidate research around four functional perspectives: Verify, Explore, Steer, and Refine that address complementary questions about model behaviour. Second, we discuss the required infrastructure for cumulative knowledge: catalogues of datasets, models and findings. Third, we highlight the need for deep analysis of individual model instances, not just model families, because single cases can reveal what population studies miss.

</details>


### [270] [Can LLM Agents Sustain Long-Horizon Organizational Dynamics?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01199)
*Xuancheng Zhu,Yang Yue,Shuaibing Wan,Zihan Dou,Xiaohan Zhang,Yongrui Liu,Guoshun Nan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes TaskWeave, a hierarchical, memory-centric multi-agent framework that enables large language model agents to simulate long-horizon, coherent organizational behavior in structured settings like an IT company.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based social and multi-agent simulations struggle to maintain coherent behavior over long time horizons, especially in structured organizations where goals must flow through hierarchies, tasks depend on prior steps, and many artifacts (documents, code, tickets, etc.) accumulate. The authors are motivated to create a framework that can preserve organizational memory, coordinate agents through complex dependencies, and yield simulations that are both coherent and practically useful for enterprise NLP tasks.

Method: The authors formalize long-horizon organizational simulation as a memory-centered coordination problem. They design TaskWeave, a hierarchical agentic framework in which organizational behavior is managed via a Formulate-Partition-Diagnose-Align cycle that maintains consistent planning states. Execution is grounded with a dependency-aware trace memory that records and structures artifacts and their relations over time. They then implement TaskWeave to simulate a year-long IT company, and benchmark it against other multi-agent frameworks on multiple dimensions of organizational performance and artifact quality.

Result: In the year-long IT company simulation, TaskWeave outperforms baseline multi-agent frameworks in organizational coherence (agents maintaining consistent roles, goals, and coordinated behavior), execution grounding (actions and artifacts that remain consistent with prior context and dependencies), and the utility of produced artifacts for downstream enterprise NLP tasks. The framework demonstrates robust adaptation to changing external conditions while preserving long-horizon structure.

Conclusion: Structured, dependency-aware simulation memory combined with hierarchical coordination mechanisms is crucial for reliable, long-horizon organizational simulations with LLM agents. TaskWeave effectively maintains coherent organizational dynamics and grounded artifacts over extended periods, suggesting that memory-centered designs are a promising direction for building practical LLM-based organizational simulators for enterprise settings.

Abstract: Large language agents are increasingly used for social simulation, yet it remains unclear whether they can sustain coherent behavior in structured organizations, where goals must propagate through hierarchy, tasks depend on prior execution, and artifacts accumulate over long horizons. We formulate long-horizon organizational simulation as a memory-centered coordination problem and introduce TaskWeave, a hierarchical agentic framework that maintains planning states through a Formulate-Partition-Diagnose-Align cycle and grounds execution through dependency-aware trace memory. We evaluate TaskWeave in a year-long IT company simulation and compare it with other multi-agent frameworks on organizational coherence, execution grounding, and downstream enterprise NLP utility. Experiments show that TaskWeave supports coherent and long-horizon organizational dynamics while producing grounded artifacts and adapting to external environments. These findings suggest that structured simulation memory is a key mechanism for building reliable LLM-based organizational simulators.

</details>


### [271] [The Shape of Wisdom: Decision Trajectories in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01202)
*Shailesh Rana*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how language model answers evolve across layers and finds that many correct answers are actually unstable, analyzing what components and input spans affect answer stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To move beyond viewing model predictions as fixed outputs and instead understand how answers develop and change across depth inside language models, particularly which answers are stable vs fragile and what internal mechanisms and input tokens influence that stability.

Method: They run about 9,000 forward trajectories on the MMLU benchmark using three 7–8B instruction-tuned models. For each layer and each question, they track the answer margin (logit difference between chosen and alternatives), its layer-to-layer change, and the distance to a decision flip. They classify answer trajectories into groups such as stable-correct and unstable-correct. On a subset, they trace what moves the margin by analyzing average attention vs MLP contributions and by span-deletion experiments that remove answer-supporting or distractor-like text and measure margin changes.

Result: They find that correctness and stability differ: the largest group of answers is correct but unstable rather than stably correct. In stable-correct cases, attention modules on average push the margin toward the correct answer, whereas MLP layers do not show the same directional support. Span deletion shows that removing supporting spans decreases the answer margin and removing distractor spans increases it, indicating these spans causally affect answer stability.

Conclusion: Answer selection in language models is a dynamic, layer-wise process with many correct but fragile predictions. The authors provide a reproducible framework to assess which answers are settled or unstable and to identify which internal components and input spans influence answer margins, though this falls short of a full mechanistic circuit explanation.

Abstract: Language models do not simply choose an answer at the output layer. In a 9,000-trajectory MMLU study across Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, and Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3, the score of the answer moves across depth in structured ways. We describe each trajectory with three quantities: the current answer margin, the next-layer change in that margin, and the distance from a decision flip. The main empirical picture is that correctness and stability are different: the largest group is unstable-correct, not stable-correct. A traced subset then asks what moves the margin. In stable-correct cases, the average attention scalar points in the correct direction, while the average MLP scalar does not; span deletion shows that removing answer-supporting text hurts the margin and removing distractor-like text helps it. The result is not a full circuit explanation. It is a reproducible way to see which answers are settled, which remain fragile, and which measured sources move them.

</details>


### [272] [Advanced Mathematics Learning Behavior Prediction and Academic Early Warning Model Based on Multimodal Data Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01224)
*Liu Qiong,Li Zhengbo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a multimodal, knowledge-graph-based predictive framework to detect at-risk students early in advanced mathematics courses and guide targeted interventions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Early identification of students at academic risk in advanced mathematics is difficult due to complex concept structures and nonlinear learning paths. Traditional early warning methods often ignore fine-grained knowledge dependencies, temporal evolution of learning, and diverse learning behavior data (e.g., logs, performance, interactions). The paper aims to address these gaps to enable more precise and timely academic support.

Method: The study constructs a hierarchical knowledge graph ontology for advanced mathematics concepts and exercises, with adaptively weighted edges based on item difficulty and individual student performance. It integrates heterogeneous graph attention networks to model complex relations among knowledge points, problems, and behaviors, and combines this with temporal sequence modeling (e.g., RNN/LSTM/Transformer-like models) on multimodal data collected over a semester (interaction logs, scores, etc.) to infer dynamic knowledge states and predict academic risk.

Result: On semester-long multimodal datasets, the proposed framework accurately identifies high-risk students and effectively traces how errors propagate along the knowledge graph. The early warning predictions allow timely, targeted interventions, which empirically improve students’ mastery and reduce academic risk indicators compared with baselines or prior practice.

Conclusion: Integrating knowledge-graph-based analytics with multimodal temporal modeling yields a more precise and dynamic understanding of students’ knowledge states, enabling earlier and more effective academic warnings and personalized interventions in advanced mathematics education. This approach demonstrates the value of combining structured domain knowledge with rich behavioral data to enhance learning support systems.

Abstract: Early detection of at-risk students and timely academic intervention pose major challenges in advanced mathematics education, where complex conceptual hierarchies and nonlinear learning trajectories often hold back students' academic performance. This study adopts multimodal data analytics to build a dynamic framework for learning behavior prediction and academic early warning. It constructs a hierarchical knowledge graph ontology, realizes adaptive edge weighting according to problem difficulty and student performance, and combines heterogeneous graph attention with temporal sequence modeling to capture students' evolving knowledge states. Empirical tests on semester-long multimodal datasets prove that this method can accurately identify high-risk students and effectively track error propagation. Targeted interventions greatly improve students' knowledge mastery and reduce academic risks. The results verify that integrating knowledge graph analytics with multimodal temporal modeling can deliver more efficient and personalized learning support for advanced mathematics education.

</details>


### [273] [Application of Algorithms in Energy-Efficient Design Platforms for Green Building](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01229)
*Na Yu,Fu Wenli,Guo Fei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper develops and tests an integrated BIM- and data-driven optimization platform that significantly reduces office building energy use while controlling cost and comfort.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Green building design requires balancing energy efficiency, lifecycle cost, and occupant comfort, but traditional workflows and tools are fragmented. There is a need for a unified, data-driven platform that connects BIM, real sensor data, and advanced multi-objective optimization to support better design decisions.

Method: The authors build a multi-layer service platform that links BIM models, operational sensor data, a high-performance C++ simulation core, dynamic energy simulation, and evolutionary multi-objective optimization via adaptive agent models. They apply it to a mid-rise office building, selecting five representative zones, cleaning and interpolating sensor data at 15-minute intervals, then running 40 optimization rounds to explore design alternatives (e.g., envelope U-values, nighttime ventilation rates) under energy–cost–comfort objectives. Pareto analysis is performed on the resulting solutions.

Result: In the case study, optimized designs reduce annual energy use intensity from 315 to 223 kWh/m² (a 29.3% reduction), keep occupant lifecycle cost increase to 3.7%, and lower discomfort hours to below 70 h/year. Pareto analysis indicates that optimized envelope U-values lie between 1.05–1.57 W/m²K and nighttime ventilation rates between 2.1–3.6 h⁻¹, both strongly correlated with energy performance.

Conclusion: The integrated BIM–data–simulation–optimization framework is technically feasible, scalable, and performs well in a real office building application. It offers a robust decision-support platform for engineers and sustainability practitioners to design energy-efficient buildings in a more accurate, data-driven way while explicitly considering cost and comfort trade-offs.

Abstract: During green building design, computer-aided energy assessment is widely used to improve efficiency and achieve overall optimization. This paper presents a platform that combines Building Information Modeling (BIM), sensor operational data, and advanced simulation workflows using robust algorithms. The platform uses a multi-layer service architecture with dynamic energy simulation and evolutionary multi-objective optimization, connected via a high-performance C++ core and adaptive agent models. A mid-rise office building was selected as the case study. Five representative areas were chosen to collect data on building envelope characteristics and occupancy patterns. After preprocessing, missing sensor data accounted for 3.2% of annual records, and all variables were standardized using 15-minute interpolation. After 40 optimization rounds, annual energy consumption per square meter dropped by 29.3% from 315 kWh/m2 to 223 kWh/m2. The lifecycle cost increase for occupants was limited to 3.7%, and discomfort hours were reduced to under 70 hours per year. Analysis of Pareto optimal solutions shows that the envelope U-value ranges from 1.05 to 1.57 W/m2K, and nighttime ventilation rate ranges from 2.1 to 3.6 h-1, both closely linked to energy performance. The results confirm that the integrated algorithm framework offers good scalability, strong performance, and technical feasibility for green building design. This platform provides a reliable decision-support tool for design engineers and sustainability practitioners, enabling accurate, data-driven delivery of energy-efficient buildings.

</details>


### [274] [HomeFlow: A Data Flywheel for Smart Home Agent Training with Verifiable Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01230)
*Yi Gu,Huacan Wang,Shuo Zhang,Yuqing Hou,Lei Xue,Weipeng Ming,Chen Liu,Fangzhou Yu,Kuan Li,Ronghao Chen,Sen Hu,Xiaofeng Mou,Yi Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HomeFlow is a data-generation and training framework that builds high-quality, verifiable interaction trajectories for smart-home LLM agents, enabling strong performance on a new benchmark and outperforming a stronger proprietary model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Smart-home LLM agents must handle ambiguous user intents, dynamic domestic environments, and multi-turn reasoning, but current approaches lack abundant, reliable, and diverse training data with clear success criteria. This bottleneck limits their robustness and real-world utility.

Method: The authors design a closed-loop data flywheel called HomeFlow. (1) HomeEnv: a unified simulation environment for smart homes. (2) HomeMaker: a procedural generator that creates diverse home configurations. (3) Blueprint: a compiler that converts open-ended natural language intents into formal, state-based success conditions. (4) MCTS-Flow: an environment-guided Monte Carlo tree search procedure that synthesizes diverse, multi-turn interaction trajectories that are verifiable against the success conditions. These trajectories are used to train LLM agents with supervised fine-tuning and a step-wise RLVE method, which incorporates real or simulated physical feedback to iteratively improve the model. Finally, they design SmartHome-Bench, a benchmark of smart-home tasks to evaluate agents trained via this pipeline.

Result: Using HomeFlow, the authors train two models (HomeFlow-RL-4B and HomeFlow-RL-8B) that reach 84.60% and 87.03% success rates on SmartHome-Bench respectively. The 8B model surpasses GPT-5.5 by 1.23 percentage points on this benchmark, showing that targeted data generation and training in the simulated environment can close or exceed the gap to stronger general-purpose LLMs for this domain.

Conclusion: A verifiable data flywheel—combining simulation, procedural environment generation, intent-to-state compilation, search-based trajectory synthesis, and RL-based refinement—can produce high-quality training data and highly capable smart-home agents. The approach yields compact models that match or outperform larger, state-of-the-art LLMs on smart-home control tasks, suggesting this framework is a promising path toward reliable, physically grounded LLM agents for real-world domestic environments.

Abstract: Large language model agents are moving beyond text-only interaction toward physical-world control, with smart homes as a representative domain. Real domestic interaction requires understanding ambiguous intents, operating in dynamic environments, and performing multi-turn reasoning. However, existing methods struggle to generate high-quality training data for smart home agents. We propose HomeFlow, a verifiable data flywheel for this domain. HomeFlow uses HomeEnv as a unified simulation environment and HomeMaker to procedurally generate diverse home settings. Subsequently, Blueprint compiles open-ended user intents into executable state-based success conditions, while MCTS-Flow synthesizes diverse, verifiable multi-turn trajectories through environment-guided tree search. We then optimize the agents via supervised fine-tuning and step-wise RLVE, which facilitates iterative improvement through authentic physical feedback. We further construct SmartHome-Bench to evaluate the agent across various smart home tasks. On this benchmark, HomeFlow-RL-4B and HomeFlow-RL-8B achieve task success rates of 84.60% and 87.03%. It is worth noting that HomeFlow-RL-8B even surpasses the leading GPT-5.5 by 1.23 percentage points.

</details>


### [275] [Brain-Atlas-Guided Generative Counterfactual Attention for Explainable Cognitive Decline Diagnosis Using Multimodal Connectomes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01237)
*Xiongri Shen,Jiaqi Wang,Zhenxi Song,Yi Zhong,Leilei Zhao,Xin He,Baiying Lei,Zhiguo Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces GCAN, a generative counterfactual attention-guided network using multimodal brain connectomes to provide accurate and explainable diagnosis of early cognitive decline (SCD/MCI) along the Alzheimer’s continuum.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Early stages of Alzheimer’s disease, such as SCD and MCI, require accurate and interpretable diagnostic tools to enable timely intervention. Existing deep learning models with connectome data improve classification but act as black boxes, offering limited insight into how functional and structural brain connectivity changes relate to disease progression. There is a need for a method that both maintains strong performance and yields explicit, anatomically meaningful explanations of connectivity alterations associated with cognitive decline.

Method: The authors propose GCAN, an atlas-knowledge-guided Generative Counterfactual Attention-guided Network. Diagnosis is framed as a source-to-target counterfactual generation task: given connectomes with a source label (e.g., HC), the model generates corresponding target-label connectomes (e.g., SCD or MCI). Differences between original and generated connectomes form counterfactual attention maps, highlighting connectivity changes relevant to diagnosis. An Atlas-aware Bidirectional Transformer (AABT) encodes and decodes network-level tokens under brain atlas constraints, preserving connectome topology. The model extends from functional connectivity (FC) alone to joint FC and structural connectivity (SC), allowing analysis of both functional reorganization and structural topology changes. Modality-specific FC and SC classifiers are pre-trained to provide target-state priors for the generative process while remaining separate from the final diagnostic classifier to avoid data leakage.

Result: On both hospital-collected and ADNI datasets, GCAN attains competitive performance in distinguishing healthy controls (HC) from SCD, HC from MCI, and SCD from MCI. Visualizations of counterfactual attention maps, circular connectome plots, comparisons with CAM-based methods, ablation studies, and confidence interval analyses collectively demonstrate that GCAN’s predictions are interpretable and stable. The multimodal FC+SC setting further improves the capacity to reveal complementary functional and structural abnormalities related to cognitive decline.

Conclusion: GCAN offers an effective and explainable framework for early cognitive decline diagnosis using multimodal brain connectomes. By casting diagnosis as counterfactual connectome generation under atlas constraints, the method preserves brain network topology while yielding anatomically grounded attention maps that highlight disease-related connectivity changes. Its strong performance across multiple classification tasks and its interpretability analyses suggest practical potential for early risk assessment and for understanding network-level mechanisms of Alzheimer’s disease progression.

Abstract: Mild cognitive impairment (MCI) and subjective cognitive decline (SCD) are closely associated with the early Alzheimer's disease continuum, where accurate and explainable diagnosis is important for early risk assessment and intervention. Existing connectome-based deep learning models can improve classification performance but often provide limited insight into disease-related functional and structural connectivity changes. This paper proposes an atlas-knowledge-guided Generative Counterfactual Attention-guided Network (GCAN) for explainable cognitive decline diagnosis using multimodal brain connectomes. GCAN formulates diagnosis as a source-to-target counterfactual generation problem, where target-label connectomes are generated from source-label inputs and their differences are used to construct counterfactual attention maps. To preserve connectome topology, an Atlas-aware Bidirectional Transformer (AABT) performs network-level token encoding and decoding under brain-atlas constraints. The framework is further extended from functional connectivity (FC) to joint functional and structural connectivity (SC) modeling, enabling counterfactual analysis of complementary functional reorganization and structural topology changes. Experiments on hospital-collected and ADNI datasets show that GCAN achieves competitive performance across HC vs. SCD, HC vs. MCI, and SCD vs. MCI classification tasks. Visualization, circular connectome analysis, CAM-based comparison, ablation studies, and confidence interval analysis further support the interpretability and reliability of the proposed framework. Modality-specific FC and SC pre-trained classifiers are used to provide target-state priors for counterfactual generation while being separated from the downstream diagnostic classifier to prevent data leakage.

</details>


### [276] [SIRIUS-SQL: Anchoring Multi-Candidate Text-to-SQL in Execution Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01246)
*Leo Luo,Haining Xie,Siqi Shen,Zhipeng Ma,Rui Ling,Hang Xu,Hefeng Jiang,Dingwei Chen,Yang Li,Peng Chen,Jie Jiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes SIRIUS-SQL, a multi-candidate Text-to-SQL system that improves reliability on complex schemas by generating more diverse SQL queries, classifying and repairing execution outcomes, and using a hybrid selection strategy, achieving state-of-the-art results on BIRD and SPIDER benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Text-to-SQL systems on complex databases are unreliable when relying on a single generated query, so recent work generates multiple candidates and uses voting. However, this multi-candidate approach still fails often because (1) additional samples from one model are redundant, (2) all erroneous executions are treated uniformly despite different error types implying different levels of partial correctness, and (3) current selection methods use only one perspective (e.g., majority vote or SQL similarity), missing errors detectable from other views. The paper aims to create a more robust multi-candidate pipeline that addresses these three limitations to improve accuracy and robustness on challenging Text-to-SQL tasks.

Method: The authors introduce SIRIUS-SQL with three main components: (1) a difficulty-smoothing reinforcement learning training scheme for a 32B-parameter specialist model (SIRIUS-32B) that encourages generation of diverse, executable SQL candidates; this specialist is paired with a generalist LLM that complements its weaknesses. (2) An execution-grounded lifecycle that runs the SQL candidates, classifies outcomes into categories such as runtime error, timeout, or empty result, and then applies targeted repair strategies tailored to each error class before returning repaired candidates to the candidate pool. (3) A confidence-gated hybrid selector that first uses execution-result agreement and pairwise SQL-form judgments to rank candidates, and only when candidates are near-tied does it escalate to a deterministic structural check for final selection. This pipeline iteratively refines the candidate set and selects the most reliable query.

Result: SIRIUS-SQL achieves 75.88% execution accuracy on the BIRD dev set and 91.20% on the SPIDER test benchmark, indicating strong performance on both large-scale and classic Text-to-SQL datasets. In experiments pairing the specialist with different generalist LLMs, two of the three pairings outperform Agentar-Scale-SQL, previously the strongest multi-candidate Text-to-SQL system on BIRD dev, demonstrating that the proposed architecture and selection strategy provide a measurable improvement over prior multi-candidate methods.

Conclusion: The work concludes that multi-candidate Text-to-SQL systems can be made significantly more reliable by tackling three intertwined issues: candidate diversity, error-aware repair, and multi-angle selection. By training for diverse executable candidates, using execution-grounded diagnostics to guide targeted repairs, and combining multiple selection signals under a confidence-gated hybrid strategy, SIRIUS-SQL substantially advances the state of the art on complex Text-to-SQL benchmarks. The results suggest that future systems should treat generation, execution feedback, and selection as a tightly integrated lifecycle rather than isolated stages.

Abstract: Text-to-SQL on complex schemas is unreliable on a single pass, so recent systems generate multiple SQL candidates and let voting filter out errors. Yet voting alone is not enough, because the multi-candidate recipe has three coupled weaknesses: 1) sampling more from a single generator produces increasingly redundant candidates, 2) existing pipelines apply one generic correction to every non-clean execution result, while runtime errors, timeouts, and empty results each indicate a different distance from correctness, and 3) existing selectors rely on a single angle such as result-majority voting or pairwise SQL comparison, missing what other angles would have caught. We present SIRIUS-SQL, which addresses all three weaknesses. A difficulty-smoothing RL recipe trains SIRIUS-32B to generate diverse executable SQL candidates, paired with a generalist LLM that fills in gaps left by the specialist. An execution-grounded lifecycle classifies each outcome and applies targeted repair before candidates re-enter the pool. A confidence-gated hybrid selector combines execution-result agreement with pairwise SQL-form judgment, escalating only near-tied cases to a deterministic structural check. SIRIUS-SQL reaches 75.88% on BIRD dev and 91.20% on SPIDER test. Two of three generalist pairings surpass Agentar-Scale-SQL, the strongest published multi-candidate system on BIRD dev.

</details>


### [277] [Emergent Ordinal Geometry in Transformers Trained on Local Comparisons](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01269)
*Nishit Singh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies whether Transformers can perform transitive inference (e.g., inferring A < C from A < B and B < C) in a human-like way, and shows that they develop a one-dimensional internal representation that mirrors a mental number line and reproduces the symbolic distance effect.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transitive inference is a fundamental cognitive ability in humans and animals and is known to rely on an internal analogue number-line-like representation instead of explicit logical rules. The authors want to know whether modern neural architectures, specifically Transformers, learn a similar internal structure when trained on only local relational data, thereby connecting decades of cognitive science findings with mechanisms in deep learning models.

Method: The authors train small Transformer models only on adjacent comparison data (e.g., A < B, B < C) sampled from an underlying but hidden total order over entities. They then evaluate the models on out-of-distribution distant pairs that were never seen during training. They analyze the learned embeddings with geometric tools (e.g., principal component analysis) to see whether the entities organize along a one-dimensional manifold that recovers the hidden order. They also track optimization dynamics (including grokking-like behavior), decision confidence, and how geometric separation between embeddings scales with the rank distance between items.

Result: The models successfully generalize out-of-distribution to unseen distant comparisons, demonstrating robust transitive inference. Internally, the entity embeddings spontaneously collapse onto a nearly one-dimensional manifold whose main axis almost perfectly recovers the hidden total order. This representation undergoes training dynamics reminiscent of grokking, where structure emerges late in optimization. Moreover, even when classification accuracy is saturated, both the model’s confidence and the embedding-space separation of items increase monotonically with their rank distance, matching the symbolic distance effect known from behavioral experiments in humans and other animals.

Conclusion: Transformers trained only on local pairwise relations develop an internal representation of order that is effectively a mental number line, providing a mechanistic basis for transitive inference that parallels human and animal cognition. The monotonic scaling of confidence and geometric separation with rank distance grounds the long-established symbolic distance effect in representation geometry. Overall, the work bridges cognitive science and deep learning by showing how a classic cognitive phenomenon can arise naturally from optimization and representation learning in modern neural networks.

Abstract: Transitive inference is the challenge of inferring that A < C from knowing only adjacent relations (A < B, B < C). It is solved by humans and animals not through logical chaining but via an analogue mental number line, whose signature is the symbolic distance effect: distant comparisons are easier than nearby ones. We ask whether Transformers acquire the same primitive, training small models exclusively on adjacent comparisons from a hidden total order and evaluating generalization to unseen distant pairs. We find that out-of-distribution generalization emerges alongside a striking geometric reorganization: entity embeddings collapse onto a one-dimensional manifold whose principal axis recovers the hidden rank order with near-perfect fidelity, and this structure is sensitive to optimization in ways that produce grokking-like transient dynamics. Critically, even when accuracy is at ceiling, decision confidence and geometric separation both scale monotonically with rank distance, directly mirroring the symbolic distance effect observed across decades of behavioural experiments on humans, primates, and rodents. These results ground a 50-year-old behavioural regularity in the geometry of learned representations, offering a mechanistic account of transitive inference that bridges cognitive science and modern neural networks.

</details>


### [278] [ANDES: Agent Native Data Evolving Synthesis Tool for Autonomous Instruction Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01279)
*Zhengyang Zhao,Shengjie Ye,Lu Ma,Hao Liang,Hengyi Feng,Wentao Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Andes, a framework that turns data generation for post-training LLMs into an explicit agent skill, enabling weaker AI agents to better automate alignment data creation and achieve state-of-the-art results under limited compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI agents are being used to automate AI research, especially the post-training/alignment of base LLMs. However, even strong agents currently fail at autonomously curating high-quality, targeted training data from the noisy web. Long-horizon tasks like searching, filtering, and balancing overwhelm agents’ limited context, degrading data quality and downstream training. The authors want a way to let agents effectively control data generation without manually engineering complex web-scraping/data-curation pipelines.

Method: They propose Andes (Agent Native Data Evolving Synthesis), a plug-and-play framework that encapsulates data generation as an agent skill. Andes introduces an intelligent abstraction layer between trainer agents and the raw data-generation process. It uses a self-evolving World Tree routing mechanism to decide how to generate and refine data, and provides actionable diagnostic reports so trainer agents can iteratively steer and update data synthesis in a closed-loop, interactive manner. The framework focuses on being usable even with limited compute and with relatively weak base agents.
