Result: With Andes, foundationally weaker trainer agents, operating under strict compute budgets, significantly improve automated alignment performance. Experiments show that this setup achieves state-of-the-art results on the PostTrainBench benchmark and exhibits strong cross-task generalization, suggesting that Andes leads to higher-quality, better-targeted post-training datasets and improved aligned assistants.

Conclusion: Reframing data generation as an explicit, controllable agent skill via Andes enables agents to more effectively automate the post-training of LLMs, even in challenging, noisy environments and with constrained compute. The World Tree routing and diagnostic loop give agents a practical handle on data synthesis, leading to better alignment outcomes and competitive or state-of-the-art performance. Andes provides a modular, plug-and-play approach that can be integrated into broader agentic training pipelines for aligned AI assistants.

Abstract: AI agents are increasingly being tasked with automating AI research itself, particularly the critical post-training phase that transforms base LLMs into aligned assistants. However, recent evaluations reveal that even frontier agents struggle to perform this task. While the success of post-training fundamentally relies on acquiring high-quality data, relying on agents to autonomously curate targeted training datasets from the open web introduces severe challenges. Executing the long-horizon tasks of searching, filtering, and balancing data within noisy web environments frequently overwhelms an agent's limited context, ultimately leading to degraded dataset quality and suboptimal downstream training performance. To bridge this gap, we introduce Andes (Agent Native Data Evolving Synthesis), a framework that reimagines data generation as a plug-and-play \emph{agent skill}. Rather than forcing agents to devise complex data-gathering strategies from scratch, \textsc{Andes} provides an intelligent abstraction layer. By leveraging a self-evolving World Tree routing mechanism and actionable diagnostic reports, it allows trainer agents to dynamically steer data synthesis through an interactive, closed-loop interface. We demonstrate that under strict compute constraints, equipping foundationally weaker agents with Andes improves automated alignment, securing state-of-the-art performance on PostTrainBench and robust cross-task generalization. Our project is available at https://github.com/zzy1127/ANDES.

</details>


### [279] [SkillSmith: Co-Evolving Skills and Tools for Self-Improving Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01314)
*Yangbo Wei,Zhen Huang,Shaoqiang Lu,Junhong Qian,Qifan Wang,Chen Wu,Lei He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SkillSmith is a synergy-aware framework that co-evolves skills and tools for self-improving agents, enabling them to fix tool issues and exploit interactions among skills for better performance as tasks get more complex.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing self-evolving agents can discover and refine skills, but they usually treat the tool layer as fixed and evaluate skills in isolation. This prevents them from (1) repairing or improving tools themselves when tool failures occur, and (2) reasoning about how different skills help or hinder each other. There is a need for a framework that jointly evolves skills and tools while explicitly modeling their interactions, especially for complex tasks requiring multiple skills.

Method: SkillSmith defines a unified proposal space where reflective processes generate "atomic bundles" that can simultaneously modify skills and tools. Within this space, tools can be wrapped, edited, composed, split, or retired when skill evolution reveals missing reusable capabilities. To navigate this joint search, SkillSmith maintains an ecological utility model inspired by Lotka-Volterra dynamics. It learns an interaction matrix from execution traces that encodes pairwise complementarities and conflicts between skills, and uses this to drive retrieval decisions, mutation priorities, and retirement choices. Additionally, SkillSmith logs anti-patterns—failure signatures, causal attributions, and effective remedies—to speed up diagnosis and to veto new proposals that would repeat known errors.

Result: On three benchmarks, including WildClawBench, and across five different Qwen3.5 model scales, SkillSmith consistently surpasses strong baseline methods. The performance improvements become more pronounced as task difficulty grows and as tasks require co-activation of multiple skills.

Conclusion: Co-evolving skills and tools with synergy awareness leads to more powerful self-improving agents than evolving skills alone on top of a fixed tool layer. By using an ecological interaction model and anti-pattern memory, SkillSmith can better manage skill interactions, fix tool-level issues, and scale its advantages as tasks become more complex and multi-skill in nature.

Abstract: Recent self-evolving agents have shown that skills can be discovered, refined, and accumulated through execution. However, existing skill-evolution frameworks typically assume a fixed tool layer and evaluate each skill independently, limiting their ability to repair tool-level failures or reason about interactions among skills. We propose SkillSmith, a synergy-aware skill-tool co-evolution framework. SkillSmith introduces a unified proposal space in which reflection produces atomic bundles that jointly modify skills and tools, allowing tools to be wrapped, edited, composed, split, or retired when skill evolution identifies a reusable capability gap. To guide this joint search, SkillSmith maintains an ecological utility model inspired by Lotka-Volterra dynamics, where an interaction matrix estimated from execution traces captures pairwise complementarity and conflict among skills and provides pressure signals for retrieval, mutation prioritization, and retirement. Furthermore, SkillSmith records anti-patterns, including failure signatures, causal attributions, and remedies, to accelerate diagnosis and veto proposals that repeat known mistakes. Experiments on three benchmarks, including WildClawBench, and five Qwen3.5 model scales show that SkillSmith consistently outperforms strong baselines, with gains that amplify as task complexity and multi-skill co-activation increase.

</details>


### [280] [Science Earth: Towards A Planet-Scale Operating System for AI-Native Scientific Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01316)
*Zhe Zhao,Haibin Wen,Yingcheng Wu,Jiaming Ma,Yifan Wen,Jinglin Jian,Jiacheng Ge,Xiangru Tang,Bo An,Ming Yin,Sanfeng Wu,Mengdi Wang,Le Cong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes Science Earth, a planet-scale system that connects diverse AI and computational scientific tools so they can self-organize around research questions, enabling distributed, self-correcting scientific discovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current scientific AI tools are siloed by domain (biology, clinical reasoning, math, materials, etc.), and no fixed workflow or pre-designed team can anticipate all expertise needed to answer complex scientific questions. There is a need for an infrastructure where heterogeneous capabilities can dynamically find each other, collaborate, and resolve conflicting standards of evidence without central orchestration.

Method: The authors design Science Earth, a scientific runtime based on the EACN protocol. This protocol enables different capabilities (e.g., simulation clusters, wet-lab robots, proof engines, single-cell analysis pipelines) to discover one another, negotiate which agent should own which task, and resolve discrepancies in evidence despite having different internal standards and no prior knowledge of each other. They then perform two empirical runs under distinct structural conditions: (1) a trans-Pacific higher-order Kuramoto synchronization study involving analytic theory and numerical agents; (2) an eight-agent single-cell analysis on a large pan-cancer atlas dataset, including anchoring with an external wet-lab study.

Result: In the Kuramoto synchronization experiment, the agent network detected and corrected a problematic closure-ratio assumption in the Ott-Antonsen analytic framework outside the Lorentzian limit, and did so within about thirty minutes. In the single-cell pan-cancer experiment on 4.88M cells, eight heterogeneous agents coordinated over a 64.9-hour period using only one high-level structural instruction, generating three new analytical result layers and cross-validating their findings against an independent wet-lab study on a related Treg subset.

Conclusion: The experiments suggest that when AI capabilities are made broadly connectable and allowed to coordinate emergently based on the problem, scientific reasoning can operate as a distributed, self-correcting process. This architecture may represent an initial step toward scalable, AI-native scientific discovery operating at a planetary scale, though the current work is presented as early empirical evidence rather than a full benchmark evaluation.

Abstract: Scientific discovery demands intelligence, perseverance, and serendipity
  across vast search spaces. Today, top scientific capabilities remain
  siloed--one AI system for biological analysis, another for clinical
  reasoning, mathematical derivation, or materials simulation--and no
  pre-designed team can anticipate every skill a question will need.
  Science Earth is a planet-scale scientific runtime in which any
  capability--a simulation cluster, a wet-lab robot, a proof engine, a
  single-cell pipeline--can connect to any other, with collaboration
  structure emerging from the question itself. Its underlying EACN protocol
  lets capabilities discover one another, negotiate task ownership, and
  adjudicate across incompatible evidentiary standards without prior
  knowledge of who will meet whom. This shifts the organizing challenge from
  workflow design to open-ended connectivity. Two runs validate this under
  structurally distinct conditions. In a trans-Pacific higher-order Kuramoto
  synchronization study, agents identified and corrected a closure-ratio
  assumption in Ott-Antonsen analytic theory that fails outside the
  Lorentzian limit, within thirty minutes. In an eight-agent single-cell run
  on the 4.88M-cell Kang 2024 pan-cancer atlas, heterogeneous capabilities
  coupled over a 64.9-hour window with one structural external instruction,
  producing three new result layers and anchoring findings against an
  independent wet-lab study on an adjacent CCR8- TIGIT+ Treg subset. These
  cases are a first empirical reading, not a benchmark sweep. They show that
  when AI capabilities are truly connectable and coordination emerges from
  the problem, scientific reasoning becomes a distributed, self-correcting
  process--a step towards scaling AI-native discovery to the planet.

</details>


### [281] [Recognize Your Orchestrator: An Entropy Dynamics Perspective for LLM Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01351)
*Junze Zhu,Weihao Chen,Xuanwang Zhang,Zhen Wu,Xinyu Dai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper analyzes the stability and failure modes of multi-agent LLM systems with centralized orchestrators using a mean-field entropy dynamics model, and introduces a benchmark generation pipeline to validate this model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-Agent Systems built from LLMs promise better problem solving than single-turn models, but current systems typically rely on a centralized orchestrator, creating a fragile bottleneck. There is limited theoretical understanding of how such orchestration behaves as task complexity and context length grow, especially why strong reasoning models may still fail when used as orchestrators. The authors aim to provide a physically interpretable framework and empirical tools to study stability, performance collapse, and uncertainty in orchestrated MAS architectures.

Method: They propose a Mean-Field Entropy Dynamics framework that treats the orchestration process as a dynamical system driven by two opposing forces: task resolution (which tends to reduce uncertainty/entropy) and cumulative context loading (which tends to increase entropy via context crowding and noise). They formalize this as a mean-field model whose parameters can be fit to empirical trajectories of multi-agent workflows. To validate and stress-test the framework, they introduce Inverse Workflow Generation (IWG), a multi-agent pipeline that automatically synthesizes high-complexity, process-verifiable benchmarks with dense intermediate checkpoints, enabling fine-grained measurement of orchestration dynamics over time.

Result: Empirical trajectories of MAS orchestration on IWG-generated benchmarks are well fit by the proposed entropy dynamics model. The fitted parameters are interpretable in terms of system stability and the onset of performance collapse (e.g., when context accumulation overwhelms problem-solving gains). The analysis reveals a "Reasoning Trap": models that are strong at isolated, reasoning-heavy tasks often perform poorly as orchestrators because their verbose, reasoning-heavy behavior exacerbates context squeezing, accelerating entropy growth and destabilizing the system.

Conclusion: The paper shows that centralized orchestration in MAS suffers from inherent instability tied to context accumulation, which can be captured by a mean-field entropy dynamics model with physically meaningful parameters. It identifies that simply using stronger reasoning models as orchestrators can backfire due to context squeezing, explaining a key failure mode in current MAS designs. By formalizing the dynamics of orchestration and providing IWG to generate process-verifiable, complex benchmarks, the work offers tools and insights for designing more robust MAS architectures and managing systemic uncertainty.

Abstract: The transition from single-turn models to Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) promises enhanced problem-solving capabilities, yet the centralized orchestration topology remains a critical point of fragility. To analyze this, we propose a Mean-Field Entropy Dynamics framework, modeling the orchestration process as a system governed by the competing forces of task resolution and cumulative context loading. To facilitate validation, we introduce Inverse Workflow Generation (IWG), a multi-agent pipeline that synthesizes process-verifiable, high-complexity benchmarks with dense intermediate checkpoints. We demonstrate that our entropy dynamics model fits empirical trajectories, providing physically interpretable parameters that quantify system stability and performance collapse. Crucially, our analysis uncovers a ``Reasoning Trap": while reasoning-heavy models excel in isolated tasks, they frequently fail as orchestrators due to context squeezing. Elucidating the physical mechanisms underlying the Orchestrator and quantifying systemic uncertainty offers insights for the MASs' architectural design.

</details>


### [282] [FlowTime: Towards Continuous Generative Watch Time Prediction via Flow-based Personalized Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01352)
*Hongxu Ma,Han Zhou,Chenghou Jin,Jie Zhang,Xiaoyu Yang,Chunjie Chen,Jihong Guan,Shuigeng Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes FlowTime, a continuous generative regression model for user watch-time prediction in short-video recommendation, using a one-step VAE with flow-based personalized priors to capture multimodal user-item interaction patterns, outperforming existing paradigms and released with an open-source benchmark library.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing watch time prediction methods in short-video recommenders—direct regression, ordinal regression, and discrete generative regression—each suffer from structural issues: mean-collapse under unimodal Gaussian assumptions, quantization error from discretization, and high latency plus heuristic design, respectively. More fundamentally, they fail to model the inherently multimodal and user-specific heterogeneity in user-item interaction patterns that act as structural confounders. The authors aim to build a new modeling paradigm that can flexibly represent these multimodal outcomes while remaining efficient enough for production recommendation systems.

Method: The paper reframes watch time prediction from a causal perspective, treating user-specific interaction patterns as structural confounders that cause identical interests to yield different watch times across users. Based on this, the authors introduce Continuous Generative Regression and instantiate it with FlowTime, a one-step Generative Variational Autoencoder. FlowTime learns a continuous latent space to model watch-time distributions without iterative denoising, improving efficiency. Additionally, they introduce a flow-based personalized prior: normalizing flows transform a standard Gaussian prior into a complex, user-history-conditioned manifold, allowing the model to adaptively capture multimodal user-item interaction patterns. They also implement TimeRec, an open-source WTP library, and define a new personalization metric for benchmarking.

Result: In extensive offline experiments and online A/B tests in short-video recommendation scenarios, FlowTime significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across existing paradigms (direct, ordinal, and discrete generative regression). The method achieves better predictive performance and personalization quality while retaining practical inference speed thanks to its one-step generative architecture. TimeRec provides standardized implementations and evaluation, showing consistent gains for FlowTime under the proposed personalization metric.

Conclusion: Continuous Generative Regression is proposed as a fourth paradigm for watch time prediction that overcomes key deficiencies of earlier approaches by explicitly modeling multimodal, heterogeneous user-item interaction patterns. FlowTime, based on a one-step VAE with a flow-based personalized prior, delivers both high expressivity and low latency, making it suitable for real-world recommenders. The accompanying TimeRec library and new personalization metric establish a rigorous benchmark, and empirical results validate FlowTime’s superiority over prior methods in both offline and online settings.

Abstract: Watch time has emerged as a pivotal metric for optimizing deep user engagement in short-video recommender systems. However, current methods of watch time prediction (WTP) suffer from inherent paradigm-specific limitations. Direct Regression faces mean-collapse due to unimodal Gaussian assumptions, while Ordinal Regression is hampered by quantization errors from rigid discretization. Similarly, Discrete Generative Regression struggles with high inference latency and heuristic vocabulary design. Beyond these specific flaws, a shared deficiency is the inability to capture the intrinsic multimodality and heterogeneity of User-Item Interaction Patterns. To address these challenges, we first revisit the WTP problem from a causal perspective and identify these user-specific patterns as structural confounders that modulate watch time outcomes, where identical interests manifest as distinct watch time outcomes conditioned on diverse user habits. Then, we formally propose a new (or the fourth) paradigm -- Continuous Generative Regression, and introduce FlowTime, a novel method utilizing a One-step Generative Variational Autoencoder. FlowTime effectively circumvents the latency of iterative denoising while maintaining the expressivity of continuous latent spaces. Furthermore, we design a Flow-based Personalized Prior that leverages NFs to warp a standard Gaussian prior into a complex, history-conditioned manifold, thereby enabling the adaptive modeling of multimodal interaction patterns. Finally, we build TimeRec, the first open-source WTP Library, alongside a novel personalization metric to establish a rigorous benchmarking standard. Extensive offline experiments and online A/B tests demonstrate FlowTime's significant superiority over SOTA methods.

</details>


### [283] [Early Diagnosis of Wasted Computation in Multi-Agent LLM Systems via Failure-Aware Observability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01365)
*Xianyou Li,Weiran Yan,Yichao Wu,Penghao Liang,Mengwei Yuan,Jianan Liu,Jing Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents a framework to analyze and diagnose where and how computation is wasted in tool-using multi-agent LLM systems by making failures visible along the execution trace, not just at the final answer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-agent, tool-using LLM systems often consume substantial computation through tokens, tool calls, retries, and code execution, yet many runs still fail. Current evaluation methods mostly look only at the final answer and cannot pinpoint where in the trajectory the system stopped making recoverable progress, making it hard to debug and improve efficiency and reliability.

Method: The authors design a failure-aware observability framework that defines recurring failure modes and associates them with online, measurable trace signals: tool reliability, execution recovery, orchestration loops, evidence availability, information change, and budget pressure. They instantiate this framework in a three-agent question-answering system and run it on 165 GAIA validation traces under fixed execution caps, logging and analyzing the traces with both cheap online metrics and a cached LLM-judge grounding audit on a subset of traces.

Result: Across GAIA levels 1–3, a large fraction of runs fail to produce usable answers (22/53, 33/86, 12/26 respectively). Trace analysis uncovers mechanisms like insufficient evidence, repeated-action loops, max-step termination, sequences of tool failures, and execution calls that succeed but yield unhelpful outputs. Token usage roughly doubles from level 1 to 3, and evidence availability diverges from sentence-level support. The LLM-judge audit on 10 cached traces demonstrates that simple online trace signals and deeper semantic metrics each capture distinct aspects of failure.

Conclusion: Failure-aware observability provides a practical diagnostic layer between raw logs and end-task accuracy for multi-agent LLM systems. By mapping failure modes to observable signals, practitioners can better understand why and where computation is wasted, enabling more targeted improvements to orchestration, tool design, and budget allocation in complex LLM workflows.

Abstract: Tool-using multi-agent large language model (LLM) systems spend computation through model tokens, tool calls, retries, and code execution before producing an answer. When a run fails, final-answer evaluation reveals the endpoint but usually not the point at which the trajectory stopped making recoverable progress. This paper introduces a failure-aware observability framework for diagnosing wasted computation in multi-agent LLM traces. The framework maps recurring failure modes to online trace signals, including tool reliability, execution recovery, orchestration loops, evidence availability, information change, and budget pressure. We instantiate the framework in a three- agent question-answering system and evaluate it on 165 GAIA validation traces under identical execution caps. Operational failures remain common: 22/53 level-1 runs, 33/86 level-2 runs, and 12/26 level-3 runs fail to produce a usable final answer. The traces expose different mechanisms behind these outcomes, including insufficient evidence, repeated-action loops, max-step termination, tool-failure streaks, and execution calls that succeed without useful output. Mean token use rises from 8,152 tokens at level 1 to 16,389 tokens at level 3, while evidence availability and sentence-level support diverge. A cached 10-trace LLM-judge grounding audit shows that cheap online signals and deeper semantic metrics capture complementary layers of failure. The results position failure-aware observability as a diagnostic layer between raw execution logs and final-answer accuracy.

</details>


### [284] [GuidaPA: Privacy-Preserving Chatbot for Public Administration via Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01386)
*Daniel M. Jimenez-Gutierrez,Albenzio Cirillo,Raffaele Nicolussi,Alessio Beltrame,Andrea Vitaletti*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces GuidaPA, a privacy-preserving chatbot for Italian Public Administration, trained using federated learning on distributed PA documents, achieving performance comparable to centralized fine-tuning while keeping data on-site.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Public administrations need high-quality, domain-specific chatbots but face regulatory and organizational barriers that prevent centralizing potentially sensitive internal data. The paper aims to explore whether federated learning can be used to train an effective PA-oriented conversational AI system without aggregating data in a single location.

Method: The authors build GuidaPA, a chatbot trained with federated learning on documentation from two Italian PA platforms (SIGESON and SIDFORS). They use parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large language models with QLoRA in 4-bit precision over 15 federated rounds, with each client using an 80/20 train-test split. The system incorporates role-based access control, secure client-side preprocessing, monitoring of non-IID data effects, and federated fine-tuning. Performance is evaluated with ROUGE, BLEU-4, and METEOR metrics and compared with both a centralized private fine-tuning approach and a general-purpose baseline model.

Result: The best federated model delivers ROUGE-1/2/L scores of 61.10/55.77/59.44, BLEU-4 of 45.02, and METEOR of 63.94. These results are close to those of private centralized fine-tuning while ensuring data never leaves client sites. Domain-specific federated fine-tuning substantially improves over a general-purpose baseline, e.g., ROUGE-1 increases from 41.45 to 62.18 and BLEU-4 from 26.97 to 50.90.

Conclusion: Federated learning can effectively train a high-quality, domain-specific conversational AI for public services without requiring centralized data sharing. The GuidaPA system demonstrates that integrating FL with privacy-oriented mechanisms (role-based access control, secure preprocessing, monitoring of non-IID effects, and efficient fine-tuning) is a viable path for deploying chatbots in public administration settings where data centralization is restricted.

Abstract: We present GuidaPA, a privacy-preserving chatbot for the Italian Public Administration (PA) trained via Federated Learning (FL) on documentation from two national PA platforms, SIGESON and SIDFORS. Our corpus includes approximately 8 pages of SIGESON manuals and 31 pages of SIDFORS manuals/FAQs; while this study uses public documentation as a safe proxy, the intended deployment extends to restricted internal sources (e.g., tickets, officer manuals, database extracts) that can not be centrally pooled due to regulatory and organizational constraints. GuidaPA integrates role-based access control, secure client-side preprocessing, explicit monitoring of non-IID effects, and parameter-efficient federated fine-tuning of large language models. Using QLoRA (4-bit) over 15 federated rounds with an 80/20 train-test split per client, we evaluate answer quality with ROUGE, BLEU-4, and METEOR. The best federated model achieves ROUGE-1/2/L of 61.10/55.77/59.44, BLEU-4 of 45.02, and METEOR of 63.94-close to private centralized fine-tuning while keeping data on-site. Compared to the general-purpose baseline, domain fine-tuning improves ROUGE-1 from 41.45 to 62.18 and BLEU-4 from 26.97 to 50.90. Overall, the results indicate that FL can deliver high-quality conversational AI for public services without centralized data sharing

</details>


### [285] [Self-Healing Agentic Orchestrators for Reliable Tool-Augmented Large Language Model Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01416)
*Rahul Suresh Babu,Adarsh Agrawal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a self-healing orchestration layer for tool-using LLM agents that automatically detects and recovers from different kinds of failures, achieving substantially higher reliability than standard baselines under controlled fault injection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing tool-augmented LLM agents frequently fail not only due to model errors but also due to orchestration problems like tool timeouts, malformed calls, stale context, and silent semantic failures. Current strategies—static workflows, naive retries, ReAct-style prompting, or full replanning—either waste budget, miss silent errors, or lack systematic diagnosis and recovery. There is a need for a principled, failure-aware orchestration mechanism that can detect diverse runtime failures, choose targeted recovery actions under explicit cost/budget constraints, and verify that recoveries actually fix problems, thereby improving both reliability and diagnosability of LLM-based tool systems.

Method: The authors frame reliability of LLM tool orchestration as a bounded runtime control problem. Their orchestrator (1) monitors for observable failure signals, (2) infers failure classes from those signals, (3) selects targeted recovery actions consistent with an explicit recovery budget (e.g., number of extra attempts), (4) verifies recovered trajectories using a verifier-guided mechanism to catch silent failures and wrong-but-plausible outputs, and (5) records observability traces for post-hoc analysis. They test this self-healing orchestrator on a 100-task benchmark where faults are systematically injected at the orchestration and tool levels, and compare against several baselines: static workflows, retry-only policies, ReAct-style agents, and full-replanning agents. They also run a variant where a live tool-calling LLM performs tool selection and argument generation over local, fault-injected tools to show the approach works with an in-the-loop model.

Result: On the 100-task controlled fault-injection benchmark, the self-healing orchestrator achieves 98.8% task success, versus 94.5% for retry-only and 93.8% for full replanning, outperforming all baselines. When varying the allowed recovery budget, self-healing beats retry-only and full replanning at every tested budget, with the largest advantage when only a single recovery attempt is allowed (94.0% success vs. 85.3% for retry-only and 88.2% for full replanning). In a controlled semantic silent-failure regime, a verifier-guided version of self-healing reduces silent failures to 0.0%, whereas non-verifying baselines still produce wrong-but-plausible outputs. A compact model-in-the-loop experiment further indicates that the self-healing mechanism remains effective when the orchestration is driven by a live tool-calling LLM interacting with local, fault-injected tools.

Conclusion: Failure-aware, budgeted, and verification-guided orchestration substantially improves reliability and diagnosability in tool-augmented LLM systems compared with common baselines like naive retries or full replanning. By treating reliability as a runtime control problem—mapping detected failure signals to failure classes, choosing targeted recoveries under explicit budgets, and verifying recovered trajectories—the proposed self-healing orchestrator can handle diverse orchestration-level failures, nearly eliminate silent semantic errors under verification, and maintain strong performance even with a live tool-calling LLM in the loop.

Abstract: Tool-augmented large language model (LLM) agents rely on orchestration layers that coordinate planning, retrieval, tool invocation, validation, memory, and recovery. In these systems, failures arise not only from model errors, but also from orchestration-level issues such as tool timeouts, malformed arguments, stale context, contradictory evidence, retry loops, and unverified intermediate outputs. This paper presents a self-healing agentic orchestrator that treats reliability as a bounded runtime control problem. The orchestrator maps observable failure signals to inferred failure classes, selects targeted recovery actions under explicit budgets, verifies recovered trajectories, and records observability traces. We evaluate the approach on a 100-task controlled fault-injection benchmark against static workflow, retry-only, ReAct-style, and full-replanning baselines. Self-healing achieves 98.8\% task success, compared with 94.5\% for retry-only and 93.8\% for full replanning. A matched recovery-budget sweep shows that self-healing outperforms retry-only and full replanning at every tested budget, with the largest gap under a single recovery attempt: 94.0\% versus 85.3\% and 88.2\%, respectively. Under a controlled semantic silent-failure setting, verifier-guided self-healing reduces silent failures to 0.0\%, while non-verifying baselines return wrong-but-plausible outputs more often. A compact model-in-the-loop validation shows that the same recovery mechanism can operate when a live tool-calling model performs tool selection, argument generation, and answer synthesis over local fault-injected tools. These results provide controlled evidence that failure-aware, budgeted, and verification-guided orchestration improves reliability and diagnosability in tool-augmented LLM systems.

</details>


### [286] [GovAI-Pipe: A Layered AI Governance Pipeline for Citizen-Facing AI in Turkey's e-Government Gateway](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01417)
*Ahmet Kaplan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces GovAI-Pipe, a four-layer technical governance pipeline that operationalizes AI regulatory and ethical requirements for Turkey’s centralized e-government platform (e-Devlet).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although Turkey’s e-Devlet platform widely deploys AI in citizen services, there is no concrete technical governance mechanism that translates high-level AI regulations and strategies (EU AI Act, OECD AI Principles, Turkey’s National AI Strategy, GDPR) into enforceable controls across the AI lifecycle. This gap creates risks around bias, privacy, accountability, and compliance in high-impact public-sector AI systems. The paper aims to bridge this policy–practice gap.

Method: Using Design Science Research methodology, the authors design GovAI-Pipe as a four-layer governance pipeline aligned with the AI model lifecycle. They define governance checkpoints and technical controls at each stage (pre-deployment, deployment, runtime, and post-incident) and explicitly map them to legal and strategic provisions (EU AI Act, GDPR, National AI Strategy). They then apply the framework to two high-risk e-Devlet use cases to demonstrate feasibility and traceability.

Result: The result is GovAI-Pipe, a structured, auditable governance pipeline with four layers: (1) pre-deployment validation including bias testing, explainability checks, and privacy impact assessments; (2) deployment governance for risk-tier classification, approvals, and documentation; (3) runtime monitoring with drift and fairness tracking plus human-in-the-loop escalation; and (4) post-incident governance with audit trails, rollback capabilities, and citizen redress mechanisms. The case studies show that the framework can systematically embed regulatory requirements into the technical workflow for high-risk AI services on e-Devlet.

Conclusion: GovAI-Pipe provides a practical, technically grounded approach to implementing AI governance in a large-scale e-government platform, closing the gap between abstract AI policy frameworks and day-to-day AI operations. By turning regulatory principles into specific, auditable pipeline components across the AI lifecycle, the framework supports compliance, transparency, and accountability for high-risk public-sector AI systems and could be adapted to other national e-government contexts.

Abstract: Turkey's e-Government Gateway (e-Devlet) serves over 68 million registered users with more than 9,200 government services, and is increasingly integrating artificial intelligence into citizen-facing applications such as chatbot assistants and eligibility assessments. However, no structured technical governance infrastructure currently connects high-level AI policy frameworks, such as the EU AI Act, OECD AI Principles, and Turkey's own National AI Strategy, to the operational reality of deploying AI within a centralized e-government platform. We propose GovAI-Pipe, a four-layer governance pipeline designed using Design Science Research methodology that maps the AI model lifecycle to governance checkpoints: (1) pre-deployment validation for bias testing, explainability, and privacy impact assessment; (2) deployment governance for risk-tier classification and approval workflows; (3) runtime monitoring for drift detection, fairness tracking, and human-in-the-loop escalation; and (4) post-incident governance for audit trails, rollback, and citizen redress. Each layer is anchored to specific provisions of the EU AI Act, the GDPR data protection framework, and the National AI Strategy. We demonstrate the framework through two high-risk e-Devlet use cases, showing how GovAI-Pipe operationalizes governance principles as auditable, technical pipeline components.

</details>


### [287] [Don't Ask the LLM to Track Freshness: A Deterministic Recipe for Memory Conflict Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01435)
*Vikas Reddy,Sumanth Challaram*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies why large language model (LLM) memory systems fail at resolving conflicting facts over time and shows that a simple deterministic, version-aware aggregation step (e.g., taking the max serial/timestamp) dramatically outperforms LLM judgment-based pipelines on MemoryAgentBench’s conflict resolution tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based long-term memory and retrieval systems increasingly need to handle facts that change over time. Benchmarks like MemoryAgentBench (FactConsolidation) reveal that current systems perform poorly when multiple conflicting versions of a fact exist, even when metadata (serial numbers) clearly indicates which version is newer. The authors want to identify the real bottleneck in these systems and understand whether failures stem from storage, retrieval, or the assembly/aggregation step that produces final answers.

Method: They analyze MemoryAgentBench’s FactConsolidation tasks, where each fact version has a serial number and higher serials are explicitly newer. They perform matched-setup comparisons: using the same LLM backbone, retrieval, chunking, and TOP_K across pipelines but varying the answer assembly step. Instead of relying on LLM judgment to decide which conflicting answer is correct, they use a pipeline that first extracts candidate answers plus their serials, then applies a deterministic Python max(serial) operation to choose the newest fact. They further extend this with a per-hop deterministic variant of Self-Ask for multi-hop questions and test portability to timestamp-based knowledge-update checks in LongMemEval.

Result: In single-hop FactConsolidation (FC-SH), replacing the LLM-judgment answer pipeline with candidate-extraction plus max(serial) improves accuracy by +10.8 points for gpt-4o-mini, with gains growing as context length increases (from +8 at 6K tokens to +21 at 262K). The proposed recipe achieves 78.0% on FC-SH with gpt-4o-mini and 94.8% with gpt-4o, and 30.2% on multi-hop FC-MH with gpt-4o-mini, rising to 51.5% with gpt-4o using a deterministic Self-Ask extension. At matched 262K context, it outperforms HippoRAG-v2 by +28 points and exceeds the best previously published FC-MH result by +20. On LongMemEval’s knowledge-update check, switching from max(serial) to max(timestamp) yields performance comparable to LLM judgment (57.8% vs 64.4%, n=45).

Conclusion: The central obstacle in resolving conflicting, temporally evolving facts in LLM memory systems is the assembly or post-retrieval aggregation step, not the storage mechanism. Deterministic, version-aware aggregation (e.g., max over serial or timestamp) is a powerful primitive for selecting the correct current value when metadata indicates recency, significantly outperforming LLM-judgment pipelines on conflict-resolution benchmarks. However, to handle broader memory QA beyond simple current-value conflicts, these deterministic methods must be integrated with question-type-aware strategies rather than used in isolation.

Abstract: LLM-based memory systems increasingly maintain facts that evolve over time, where a recurring failure is conflict resolution: when a fact has multiple contradictory values, which should the agent return? MemoryAgentBench (MAB; Hu et al., 2026) makes this explicit in its FactConsolidation task: facts are numbered, the counterfactual has the higher serial, and agents are told newer facts have larger serials. Yet every published system underperforms: HippoRAG-v2 reaches 54% on single-hop (FC-SH), BM25 48%, Mem0 18%, and the temporal KG Zep/Graphiti just 7%. Multi-hop is near-unsolved (at most 7% across 22 systems).
  We argue the bottleneck is the assembly step: baselines leave conflict resolution to LLM-mediated retrieval or generation rather than version-aware aggregation. A matched-setup comparison (same backbone, retrieval, chunking, TOP_K) shows that replacing the LLM-judgment answer pipeline with candidate-extraction plus Python max(serial) yields +10.8 points on FC-SH (gpt-4o-mini), widening from +8 at 6K to +21 at 262K. This is a whole-pipeline effect (resolver, prompt, format, and temperature vary jointly); isolating the resolver is future work. The recipe reaches 78.0% on FC-SH (gpt-4o-mini), 94.8% (gpt-4o), and 30.2% on FC-MH (gpt-4o-mini, rising to 51.5% with gpt-4o) via a per-hop deterministic extension of Self-Ask. At matched-262K, it beats HippoRAG-v2 by +28 points and the best published FC-MH result by +20.
  The implication is corrective for the subfield: the bottleneck on conflict resolution is assembly (post-retrieval aggregation), not storage. A LongMemEval knowledge-update check shows the mechanism ports from max(serial) to max(timestamp) but only ties LLM judgment (57.8% vs 64.4%, n=45): deterministic aggregation is the right primitive for current-value conflicts and must be composed with question-type-aware handling for broader memory QA.

</details>


### [288] [Dive into Ambiguity: A*-Inspired Multi-Agents Commonsense Obfuscation Attack on LLM Prompts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01441)
*Boxuan Wang,Zhuoyun Li,Xiaowei Huang,Yi Dong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They design an efficient, interpretable attack framework that rewrites prompts to induce factual errors in LLMs while keeping the original intent, outperforming prior attack methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs, while strong at reasoning and knowledge tasks, are easily fooled by adversarial prompts that preserve the user’s intent but induce commonsense hallucinations. As LLMs move into safety-critical applications, there is a need for stronger, more realistic, and more efficient methods to evaluate and expose their vulnerability to such attacks, since existing approaches are either inefficient or do not mimic adaptive real-world adversaries well.

Method: They propose an A*-inspired Factual Error Induction Framework that generates adversarial prompts which remain semantically aligned with the original but are obfuscated enough to induce factual errors. The main component is a Hierarchical Rewrite Strategy controlled by a dynamic semantic dispersion coefficient γ. This γ starts high (favoring conservative edits that keep semantics close) and gradually decreases using a reverse simulated annealing schedule, allowing increasingly aggressive obfuscations over time. Additionally, they introduce Agentic Mechanism Labeling to identify, label, and refine the underlying adversarial mechanisms used in the rewrites, providing interpretable reverse optimization paths. They also provide a theoretical analysis showing that the rewriting process forms a contractive recurrence, leading to semantic collapse as γ decreases.

Result: On multiple LLMs, their framework attains higher attack success rates than strong baselines that rely on exhaustive search or exploration, while also needing fewer total attack attempts. This shows that their approach can more efficiently and effectively induce factual hallucinations with semantically aligned prompts.

Conclusion: The proposed A*-inspired framework offers an efficient and interpretable way to generate semantically aligned adversarial prompts that reliably induce factual errors in LLMs. Its dynamic rewrite strategy and mechanism labeling outperform exhaustive exploration methods both in success rate and cost, and the theoretical analysis clarifies how semantic collapse emerges as the dispersion coefficient decreases, underscoring the need for more robust defenses in safety-critical LLM deployments.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) excel in reasoning and knowledge-intensive tasks but remain vulnerable to prompt-level adversarial attacks that preserve intent while triggering commonsense hallucinations. This vulnerability is urgent, as LLMs are rapidly integrated into safety-critical domains where factual reliability is non-negotiable. Existing attack methods either lack efficiency or fail to capture the adaptive strategies of real-world adversaries. We propose an A*-inspired Factual Error Induction Framework, a framework for generating semantically aligned yet obfuscated prompts. At its core is a Hierarchical Rewrite Strategy guided by a dynamic semantic dispersion coefficient $γ$ that balances conservative edits early with aggressive obfuscations later, following a reverse simulated annealing schedule. To enhance interpretability, we further introduce Agentic Mechanism Labeling, which discovers and refines adversarial mechanisms, offering interpretable reverse optimization. Theoretically, we prove that prompt rewriting follows a contractive recurrence, leading to semantic collapse as $γ$ decreases. Empirically, across diverse LLMs, our method achieves higher attack success rates than exhaustive exploration while requiring fewer attempts, demonstrating both efficiency and effectiveness.

</details>


### [289] [Self-Revising Discovery Systems for Science: A Categorical Framework for Agentic Artificial Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01444)
*Fiona Y. Wang,Markus J. Buehler*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose a category-theoretic framework for how AI systems can not only search for answers but also change their own representational schemes while doing scientific discovery, and they demonstrate it on materials-science problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most AI and scientific workflows assume a fixed representational regime: data types, models, and operations are predetermined. But real scientific discovery often changes the very “language” of description (e.g., new variables, model structures, or skills). The authors want a precise mathematical account of this kind of regime-changing discovery and a way to engineer AI systems that can safely and verifiably revise their own modeling frameworks, especially in complex domains like materials science.

Method: They formalize a representational regime as a schema category S_b, with the state of the system at time t given by a copresheaf I_t: S_b -> Set. The associated provenance is captured by the category of elements ∫_{S_b} I_t. Within a fixed regime, operations are state updates that are endofunctorial only when they preserve provenance via specified refinements. Discovery is modeled not as such an update but as a verified regime transition u: S_b -> S_b'. Under this transition, existing artifacts are transported via the left Kan extension Lan_u I_t. The difference between the transported old state and the new post-transition state reveals genuinely new content, allowing a formal distinction between retrieval, search, and discovery. They then instantiate this framework in two systems: (1) Builder/Breaker, where a protein-mechanics model is revised under an MDL (Minimum Description Length) criterion, yielding a new law of mode-conditioned compliance; and (2) CategoryScienceClaw, in which skills, artifacts, needs, workflow mutations, gates, and tests are organized in a proof-carrying knowledge-computation graph, illustrated with a fiber-network modeling example using an AIC gate and perturbation tests to select an anisotropic stiffness surrogate model.

Result: The framework cleanly distinguishes operations within a fixed schema from regime transitions that constitute discovery, provides a way to track provenance through both, and formalizes how old knowledge is preserved and compared across representational changes. In the Builder/Breaker system, this leads to a compact law describing protein-chain flexibility in terms of mode-conditioned compliance under an MDL gate. In CategoryScienceClaw, it yields a concrete proof-carrying workflow that records candidate models, rejections, tests, and the accepted anisotropic surrogate model over an isotropic descriptor, all typed and verifiable within the categorical framework.

Conclusion: Category theory can simultaneously serve as (1) a precise mathematical language to describe discovery as regime change and provenance-preserving transport of knowledge, and (2) a practical engineering specification for AI systems that not only search within fixed model classes but can also safely revise their own representational regimes. The two case studies in materials science show that this approach can capture realistic, nontrivial episodes of scientific model revision while keeping the resulting AI workflows structured, auditable, and proof-carrying.

Abstract: Scientific discovery is not only answer generation but revision of the representational regime in which evidence, artifacts, operations, and verifiers are typed. We develop a category-theoretic account of agentic discovery for materials science. In a fixed regime b with schema category S_b, the system state is a copresheaf I_t: S_b -> Set, and provenance is the category of elements \int_{S_b} I_t. Fixed-regime operation is an update on such states, endofunctorial only when provenance-preserving refinements are specified and preserved. Discovery is instead a verified regime transition u: S_b -> S_b': old artifacts are preserved, transported by the left Kan extension Lan_u I_t, and compared with the post-transition state to identify residual content beyond functorial transport. This separates retrieval, search, and discovery without subjective novelty. We instantiate the framework in two systems. In Builder/Breaker, a protein-mechanics world model is revised under a Minimum Description Length gate; the accepted law expresses within-chain flexibility as all-mode elastic compliance conditioned by slow collective-mode participation, or mode-conditioned compliance. In CategoryScienceClaw, typed skills, artifacts, open needs, workflow mutation, gates, stress tests, and public discourse become a proof-carrying knowledge-computation graph. A fiber-network example records candidate models, rejected alternatives, an AIC gate, perturbation tests, and an accepted orientation-tensor anisotropic stiffness surrogate over an isotropic fiber-count descriptor. Together, the cases show how category theory can be both a mathematical language for discovery and an engineering specification for self-revising AI discovery systems.

</details>


### [290] [Transferring Information Across Interventions in Causal Bayesian Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01457)
*Mohammad Ali Javidian*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They extend causal Bayesian optimization by coupling the effects of many interventions through shared causal mechanisms, giving a low‑rank causal kernel, better sample efficiency, and regret bounds that separate optimization, causal learning, and intervention-choice errors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Bayesian optimization treats controllable variables as black-box inputs and cannot distinguish correlation from causation, which is problematic when interventions have causal structure. Causal Bayesian optimization uses known causal graphs and observational data but still learns each intervention almost independently, wasting data when many interventions share underlying causal mechanisms. There is a need for a method that exploits shared causal structure so that data from one intervention can inform others, especially when experiments are expensive and interventional data are sparse across many possible interventions.

Method: They introduce graph-coupled causal Bayesian optimization, which models all interventions through a small set of shared causal parameters derived from a known causal graph. They construct a causal kernel that encodes how uncertainty in these shared parameters induces correlations between the effects of different interventions. For identifiable linear Gaussian causal models, they show this kernel is low rank, with rank bounded by the number of shared parameters, not by the number of interventions. They then derive information-gain bounds based on this structure and corresponding regret bounds that decompose error into optimization error, causal estimation error, and error from the choice of intervention sets. They also sketch extensions to nonlinear models and adaptive procedures.

Result: For linear Gaussian causal models where the parameters are identifiable, the proposed causal kernel has rank bounded by the number of shared causal parameters, which leads to an information-gain bound that grows only logarithmically with the optimization horizon. From this, they obtain regret bounds that clearly separate three contributions: (1) error from the optimization process, (2) error from estimating causal parameters, and (3) error from restricting to a particular menu of intervention sets. Empirically, across synthetic Gaussian systems tailored to the theory, stress tests focused on shared mechanisms, and standard causal optimization benchmarks, their method successfully transfers information across related interventions and maintains the advantages of causal Bayesian optimization.

Conclusion: Graph-coupled causal Bayesian optimization leverages shared causal mechanisms via a structured causal kernel, enabling efficient information transfer between related interventions. In identifiable linear Gaussian settings, its low-rank structure yields favorable information-gain and regret guarantees and clarifies the different sources of performance loss. Experiments confirm that the method preserves the strengths of causal Bayesian optimization while offering distinct gains when direct interventions on target parents are impossible and when sparse interventional data must be reused across many candidate interventions. Nonlinear and adaptive extensions suggest the approach is broadly applicable beyond the linear Gaussian case.

Abstract: Bayesian optimization is a popular way to optimize expensive systems, where every experiment, simulation, or intervention costs time or money. In its standard form, it treats the variables we control as plain inputs to a black box and cannot tell apart mere correlation from a real cause and effect. Causal Bayesian optimization closes part of this gap by using a known causal graph together with observational data to decide which variables are worth intervening on. Existing methods, however, learn the effect of each possible intervention almost in isolation, even though in a causal system these effects usually share the same underlying mechanisms. We propose graph-coupled causal Bayesian optimization, which ties the different intervention effects together through the uncertainty we have about a small set of shared causal parameters. The result is a causal kernel that lets evidence collected from one intervention improve our estimate of related interventions. For identifiable linear Gaussian causal models, we show that this kernel has low rank, bounded by the number of shared parameters rather than by the size of the intervention menu. This in turn yields an information-gain bound that grows only logarithmically in the optimization horizon, and a regret bound that cleanly separates three sources of error: optimization, causal estimation, and the choice of which intervention sets to consider. We also describe nonlinear and adaptive extensions. Across theory-aligned Gaussian systems, shared-mechanism stress tests, and standard causal optimization benchmarks, the method keeps the benefits of causal Bayesian optimization while transferring information across related interventions, with the clearest gains when direct interventions on the target's parents are unavailable and sparse interventional data must be reused across a large family of candidate interventions.

</details>


### [291] [An Enigma of Artificial Reason: Investigating the Production-Evaluation Gap in Large Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01462)
*Mingzhong Sun,Teresa Yeo,Armando Solar-Lezama,Tan Zhi-Xuan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper shows that large reasoning models (LRMs) are good at producing solutions with long chains of thought but surprisingly bad at evaluating whether given reasoning is valid, revealing an answer-confirmation bias in how they check solutions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humans are generally better at evaluating existing reasoning than generating it, but LRMs are mainly trained to generate reasoning to reach correct answers. The authors want to know whether LRMs can also evaluate reasoning quality independently of answer correctness, which is crucial for using them as judges, critics, or graders of other models or humans.

Method: They create the VAIR (Valid-Answer-Invalid-Reasoning) dataset, consisting of math problems whose provided solutions contain trivial reasoning errors but still produce the correct final answer. They then test frontier LRMs on two tasks: solving the problems themselves and evaluating the correctness of the provided solutions. They analyze the LRMs’ chain-of-thoughts to see how they judge reasoning, use linear probes on model activations to see how valid/invalid reasoning is represented, and perform causal patching on internal representations of the final answer to test whether answer correctness drives the model’s judgments.

Result: Frontier LRMs achieve near-perfect accuracy in solving the math problems but only about 48% accuracy in judging the flawed VAIR solutions, revealing a large gap between production and evaluation of reasoning (unlike humans, who are only slightly worse at grading than solving). CoT analysis shows that LRMs tend to first compute the answer and then look for confirmation, often rationalizing away evident flaws instead of checking each step. Linear probing reveals that models encode some notion of valid reasoning but fail to consistently classify VAIR solutions as invalid. Causal patching of the internal representation of the final answer flips both model verdicts and the probed representations, implicating answer correctness as the main driver of their biased evaluation.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that current training practices for LRMs emphasize generating reasoning that leads to correct answers, but not carefully evaluating the correctness of each reasoning step. This leads to an answer-confirmation bias, where models effectively ask “does this lead to the right answer?” rather than “is this reasoning sound?”. As a result, LRMs can be unreliable as evaluators or judges of reasoning, pointing to a key limitation in dominant reasoning-training approaches and a need for methods that explicitly train robust step-wise reasoning evaluation.

Abstract: Studies of human reasoning have shown that people are typically stronger at evaluating reasoning than producing it from scratch. In contrast, large reasoning models (LRMs) are trained to excel at producing long chains of reasoning to solve complex problems. How then do LRMs perform at evaluating reasons? We investigate this with the Valid-Answer-Invalid-Reasoning (VAIR) dataset: math problems and solutions with trivial reasoning flaws but valid answers, designed to isolate reasoning evaluation from the confound of reasoning production. Unlike humans, who we find are only 6% worse at grading than solving such problems, we find a substantial production-evaluation gap in LRMs: frontier models score as low as 48% when evaluating VAIR solutions, despite near-perfect solution production.
  Why this enigma? Through chain-of-thought (CoT) analysis, we find evidence of an answer confirmation bias: LRMs often produce then check for the correct answer instead of carefully verifying each step, fabricating rationalizations even when noticing anomalous reasoning. Linear probes corroborate this, showing that while LRM activations encode some representation of valid reasoning, they fail to robustly represent VAIR solutions as invalid. Causal patching of the final answer's representations causes LRM verdicts and activations to flip, demonstrating that answer validity is responsible for models' confirmation biases. These findings indicate an outstanding limitation in dominant approaches to reasoning training, which incentivize LRMs to produce and confirm reasoning towards correct answers, but not to robustly evaluate the underlying reasons.

</details>


### [292] [A Minimalist Brain-Computer Musical Interface for Real-Time Emotion-Driven Sonification: System Design and Preliminary Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01473)
*Pablo A. Monroy-D'Croz,Rafael Ramirez-Melendez,Julian Cespedes-Guevara*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper develops and tests a minimal brain-computer musical interface that turns frontal EEG signals into adaptive music, but finds that users cannot reliably control the system via intentional emotion induction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore whether frontal alpha asymmetry, a popular EEG correlate of emotional valence, can be used as a practical, volitional control signal in a closed-loop brain-computer musical interface for emotion regulation and neurofeedback.

Method: The authors built a real-time BCMI pipeline combining wireless prefrontal EEG recording, Python-based signal processing to compute frontal alpha asymmetry (AF7/AF8), and an Ableton Live music engine linked via Lab Streaming Layer. They mapped the estimated emotional valence onto musical parameters (mode, tempo, rhythmic density, pitch register) using a stochastic generative algorithm. In an experiment with 22 participants instructed to self-induce specific emotions, they analyzed whether the neurofeedback signal (frontal alpha asymmetry) changed systematically with target emotion or over time, using linear mixed-effects modeling and examining individual-difference predictors such as musical training and acting experience.

Result: Linear mixed-effects models showed no significant main effects of target emotion or time on frontal alpha asymmetry, indicating that the EEG measure did not systematically reflect the instructed emotional states. The experimental manipulation explained only 0.40% of total variance in the neurofeedback signal. In contrast, stable individual-difference variables (e.g., musical training, acting experience) accounted for more variance than the experimental conditions.

Conclusion: The study concludes that frontal alpha asymmetry, at least in the tested configuration, is not a robust voluntary control signal for real-time emotion-modulating brain-computer musical interfaces. It underscores the difficulty of using this metric for closed-loop emotion regulation, and proposes that future BCMI research should reconsider the choice of neural features, improve methodological design, and account more carefully for individual differences in users’ abilities and backgrounds.

Abstract: This paper presents a minimalist brain-computer Musical Interface (BCMI) that functions as a real-time affective sonification system, translating prefrontal EEG activity into adaptive music. Emotional valence is estimated from frontal alpha asymmetry (AF7/AF8) and mapped to musical features such as mode, tempo, rhythmic density, and pitch register through a stochastic generative algorithm. The system integrates wireless EEG acquisition, real-time Python signal processing, and Ableton Live-based music generation synchronized via Lab Streaming Layer. An experiment with 22 participants investigated whether intentional emotional self-induction could modulate the BCMI neurofeedback signal. Linear mixed-effects analyses found no significant effects of target emotion or time, indicating that the frontal alpha asymmetry signal did not reliably distinguish instructed emotional states. Individual differences, including musical training and acting experience, explained more variance than the experimental manipulation, which accounted for only 0.40\% of total signal variance. These findings highlight the challenges of using frontal alpha asymmetry as a voluntary control signal for closed-loop emotion regulation and suggest methodological directions for future BCMI research.

</details>


### [293] [TERRA: Task-Embedded Reasoning and Representation Architecture for Cross-Domain Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01520)
*Shayan Shokri*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes TERRA, a theoretical framework for transferring predictive representations across structurally similar but otherwise different domains (e.g., driving scenes and financial order books), and formulates a falsifiable hypothesis plus an experimental plan, without presenting empirical results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many domains have structured state spaces (like spatial grids or lattices), and recent advances provide strong predictive models within each domain separately (masked-latent prediction, action-conditioned latent world models, etc.). However, it is not known when and how such representations learned in one domain can transfer to another domain that is structurally analogous but semantically different. The authors want to formalize this transfer question and turn an intuitive belief (“structure should transfer”) into a precise, testable theory.

Method: They model each domain as a controlled Markov process defined on a graded latent grid. Any model instantiation is decomposed into thin domain-specific adapters and a shared domain-invariant core. Cross-domain correspondence is formalized as an approximate Markov decision process (MDP) homomorphism. The mismatch between domains is quantified using: (1) a lax bisimulation discrepancy and (2) for domains without a shared coordinate system, a Gromov-Wasserstein distance between their action-conditioned transition operators. Assuming a Lipschitz predictor, they derive a transfer generalization bound that separates source-model error from structural mismatch and grows geometrically with prediction horizon. They also relate latent predictive error to decision regret via the Lipschitz value property of bisimulation metrics. Finally, they articulate a Structured-State Transfer Hypothesis and design a preregistered experimental program (e.g., from driving scenes to financial order books) specifying success and failure conditions.

Result: No empirical results are provided. The main results are theoretical constructs and bounds: a formal specification of cross-domain transfer via approximate MDP homomorphisms and Gromov-Wasserstein distances, transfer bounds that decompose error into source model error and structural mismatch, and a clear statement of a falsifiable Structured-State Transfer Hypothesis along with an experimental test plan.

Conclusion: The work is a research proposal that formalizes an intuition about the transferability of representations across structurally similar domains into a precise theoretical framework and falsifiable hypothesis. It provides mathematical tools (approximate MDP homomorphisms, bisimulation discrepancy, Gromov-Wasserstein distances) and transfer bounds that connect representation error to decision-making performance, and outlines how to empirically test the theory—for example, by transferring from driving-scene models to financial order-book models—while explicitly noting that the validity of the hypothesis awaits future empirical verification.

Abstract: A single action-conditioned latent predictive architecture can in principle be trained on the structured state of a driving scene, a robot workspace, or a financial order book. The ingredients for doing so within any one domain already exist and are individually validated: masked-latent prediction, action-conditioned latent world models, discrete action tokenization, and joint-embedding prediction on voxelized state. What is not established, and what TERRA addresses, is the transfer question: when does a representation or predictor learned in one structured-state domain carry over to a structurally analogous but otherwise unrelated domain, and by how much. We give this question a formal treatment. We model each domain as a controlled Markov process on a graded latent grid, factor any instantiation into thin domain adapters and a shared domain-invariant core, and identify a cross-domain correspondence with an approximate Markov decision process homomorphism whose quality is measured by a lax bisimulation discrepancy and, for domains lacking a shared coordinate system, by a Gromov-Wasserstein distance between their action-conditioned transition operators. Under a Lipschitz predictor we derive a transfer bound that separates source-model error from structural mismatch, grows geometrically in the prediction horizon, and is certified from below by the Gromov-Wasserstein distance; we then connect latent error to decision regret through the Lipschitz value property of bisimulation metrics. The resulting Structured-State Transfer Hypothesis is stated as a falsifiable claim with a preregistered experimental program, centered on a transfer test from driving scenes to order books, including conditions under which it is refuted. We present no empirical results: this is a research proposal that converts a widely repeated intuition into testable theory.

</details>


### [294] [Joint Agent Memory and Exploration Learning via Novelty Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01528)
*Shizuo Tian,Xiaohong Weng,Rui Kong,Yuxuan Chen,Guohong Liu,Yuebing Song,Jiacheng Liu,Yuchen Li,Dawei Yin,Ting Cao,Yunxin Liu,Yuanchun Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: JAMEL is a framework that jointly learns a compact memory and an exploration policy for language model agents using novelty signals, enabling more efficient and deeper exploration in open-ended GUI environments with fewer tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Language model agents in open-ended environments need to explore effectively but struggle because they either store full interaction histories, which is computationally expensive over long trajectories, or use latent memories that lack good supervision during training. The authors want a way to compress histories into useful memories and to guide exploration without manual annotations, so agents can generalize and explore deeply in new environments.

Method: They propose JAMEL, which trains an agent’s latent memory module and its exploration policy together in a closed loop, driven by novelty. The agent interacts with GUI environments while tracking deterministic, persistent novelty signals such as code coverage. These signals both guide the exploration policy to seek new behaviors and serve as annotation-free supervision for training the memory module to store information that is predictive and useful for future exploration, enabling the agent to distinguish previously explored behaviors from unseen ones.

Result: In experiments on GUI-based tasks, JAMEL generalizes to unseen environments and achieves stronger exploration performance than open-weight baseline agents. Its exploration depth is comparable to that of a strong closed-source model, while using fewer tokens due to the compressed memory representation instead of full interaction histories.

Conclusion: Jointly training memory and exploration policy with environment-derived novelty signals yields more efficient and effective exploration for language model agents. By using signals like code coverage as natural supervision, JAMEL produces compact, useful memories that support deeper, generalizable exploration in new environments, closing much of the gap with powerful closed-source agents while remaining open-source and token-efficient.

Abstract: In open-ended environments, exploration is fundamental for autonomous agents, yet current language model agents struggle with this. Effective exploration requires memory, but retaining raw interaction histories is computationally expensive over long trajectories. While latent memory offers a solution to compress interaction histories, its training lacks reliable supervisory signals. We introduce \textbf{J}oint \textbf{A}gent \textbf{M}emory and \textbf{E}xploration \textbf{L}earning (\textbf{JAMEL}), a framework that trains agentic memory and exploration policy together through novelty-driven interaction. We observe that memory and exploration form a mutually dependent loop: sustained exploration requires memory to distinguish exhausted behaviors from unseen ones, while novelty-seeking interaction provides the supervision needed to make memory useful for future exploration. By utilizing deterministic and persistent novelty signals such as code coverage in the GUI domain, we provide natural, annotation-free supervision for the memory module. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that \ours successfully generalizes to unseen environments. Its exploration capability outperforms open-weight baselines and rivals the exploration depth of a closed-source model while reducing token consumption. Our code and model are open-sourced at https://github.com/MobileLLM/JAMEL.

</details>


### [295] [RoleCDE:Benchmarking and Mitigating Role-Alignment Trade-offs in Role-Playing Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01552)
*Huayi Lai,Shichao Song,Simin Niu,Hanyu Wang,Jiawei Yang,Zhouxing Wang,Zhiqiang Yin,Xun Liang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces RoleCDE, a benchmark for testing how role-playing agents in large language models resolve conflicts between role-specific values and general alignment constraints, revealing that models often favor alignment over role fidelity and showing that fine-tuning on RoleCDE can mitigate this.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of role-playing agents mainly test whether models stay in character at a surface level, but they do not probe how models make decisions when a role’s values conflict with alignment or moral constraints. This leaves a gap in understanding whether models can perform nuanced, role-aware value trade-offs rather than simply defaulting to general alignment rules.

Method: The authors design RoleCDE, a benchmark that frames role-aware decision making as cognitive dilemmas where role-specific values can conflict with alignment-oriented constraints. They systematically construct about 8,000 role profiles and scenarios, generating around 24,000 dilemma instances at three difficulty levels across eight role categories. Models are evaluated on role-scenario grounding, how they resolve value conflicts, and their decision tendencies, and then some models are fine-tuned on RoleCDE to assess changes in behavior.

Result: Mainstream LLMs display a "Role Value Decoupling" effect: even when explicitly instructed to adopt a role, they systematically prefer decisions consistent with general alignment and morality over decisions that reflect the role’s specific values when the two conflict. This pattern is robust to the difficulty level of dilemmas but varies by role category. Fine-tuning on RoleCDE improves the models’ ability to reason about and trade off conflicting values, reducing the decoupling while maintaining overall role-playing fidelity and general reasoning performance.

Conclusion: RoleCDE exposes a previously underexplored failure mode of role-playing agents—favoring generic alignment-consistent behavior over role-specific value commitments under conflict. The benchmark provides a structured way to assess this behavior and demonstrates that targeted fine-tuning can enhance role-aware value trade-off reasoning without harming broader model capabilities.

Abstract: Role-playing agents(RPAs) are widely used to steer large language models(LLMs) toward role-consistent behavior, yet existing benchmarks mainly evaluate surface-level fidelity and offer limited insight into decision making under role-alignment value conflicts. To address this gap, we introduce RoleCDE, the first benchmark designed to evaluate RPAs under structured conflicts between role-specific values and alignment-oriented constraints. RoleCDE formulates role-aware decision making as cognitive dilemma scenarios, jointly evaluating role-scenario grounding, value conflict resolution, and decision tendencies. The benchmark is constructed at scale, covering approximately 8k diverse role profiles and scenarios and nearly 24k dilemma instances across three difficulty levels and eight role categories. Evaluation of several mainstream LLMs reveals a "Role Value Decoupling" phenomenon, where agents systematically default to alignment-and morality-consistent decisions rather than role-specific values when the two conflict, even under explicit role conditioning. This behavior is largely invariant to dilemma difficulty but varies substantially across role categories. We further show that RoleCDE-based fine-tuning effectively mitigates this decoupling by improving value trade-off reasoning, while preserving general role-playing fidelity and general reasoning performance. Code is available at: https://github.com/rabbitrose/RoleCDE.

</details>


### [296] [S-SPPO: Semantic-Calibrated Self-Play Preference Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01561)
*Xiwen Chen,Wenhui Zhu,Jingjing Wang,Peijie Qiu,Zhipeng Wang,Huayu Li,ZhengXiao He,Xuanzhao Dong,Prayag Tiwari,Mingkun Xu,Yujian Xiong,Feng Luo,Abolfazl Razi,Brendan Hogan Rappazzo,Anderson Schneider,Yuriy Nevmyvaka*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper identifies instability in Self-Play Preference Optimization (SPPO) for aligning LLMs and proposes S-SPPO, a semantically calibrated variant that preserves training stability and improves benchmark win rates without extra human labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard DPO with Bradley-Terry models assumes transitive preferences and struggles with realistic, intransitive human judgments. SPPO was introduced to improve on this by using self-play preferences, but the authors find it can cause policy degeneration when the preference oracle is overconfident between nearly identical responses. The motivation is to stabilize self-play preference optimization so that LLMs can be aligned more reliably and efficiently without heavy human annotation.

Method: They introduce S-SPPO, a dual-space semantic calibration scheme for SPPO.
