In supervision space, they use semantic gating: when two responses are semantically very similar, they reduce the target win rate toward a maximum-entropy (50-50) baseline, preventing overconfident training signals. In representation space, they introduce latent repulsion between chosen and rejected samples to enforce geometric diversity, reducing the risk of manifold collapse. They also analyze the learning dynamics and show that these calibrations preserve a constant-sum game structure, enabling convergence to a Nash equilibrium.

Result: Experiments on AlpacaEval 2.0 with Llama-3-8B show that S-SPPO avoids the performance degradation observed in vanilla SPPO and related methods. It reaches a 52.19% win rate and a 47.46% length-controlled win rate, outperforming baselines while using no additional human preference labels beyond those used to train the base model or preference oracle.

Conclusion: S-SPPO provides a safer and more stable way to use self-play preference optimization for LLM alignment. By calibrating both supervision signals and latent representations based on semantic similarity, it mitigates policy degeneration, maintains diversity, and preserves desirable game-theoretic properties, leading to stronger and more robust performance without extra human annotations.

Abstract: Aligning Large Language Models (LLMs) with human preferences is often formulated via Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). However, the standard Bradley-Terry instantiation of DPO is limited in modeling common departures from transitivity in human preferences. To address this, recent work has introduced Self-Play Preference Optimization (SPPO), which iteratively refines the policy by training on self-generated win-lose pairs. Our investigation, however, reveals a critical instability in SPPO: the optimization is prone to policy degeneration when the preference oracle assigns overly confident wins to semantically indistinguishable responses. To mitigate this, we propose S-SPPO, a dual-space semantic calibration framework comprising: i) Supervision Calibration via semantic gating, which anneals win rate targets toward the maximum-entropy baseline as semantic overlap increases; and ii) Representation Calibration via latent repulsion to enforce geometric diversity to prevent manifold collapse and maintain latent diversity between chosen and rejected samples. Theoretically, we show that the calibration preserves the constant-sum game structure, facilitating convergence to a Nash Equilibrium. Empirically, S-SPPO avoids the performance degradation seen in prior methods, achieving 52.19% win rate and 47.46% length-controlled win rate on AlpacaEval 2.0 with Llama-3-8B, without using additional human-annotated preferences during training. The code will be available at https://github.com/xiwenc1/s-sppo.

</details>


### [297] [TRON: Targeted Rule-Verifiable Online Environments for Visual Reasoning RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01599)
*Tianze Yang,Yucheng Shi,Ruitong Sun,Jingyuan Huang,Ninghao Liu,Jin Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TRON is an online, programmatic environment suite that generates infinite, verifiable visual reasoning tasks on demand for RL post-training of vision-language models, yielding consistent gains on external benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visual reasoning with reinforcement learning currently depends on static, curated datasets of image-question-answer triplets, which are expensive to collect, finite in size, and limited in controllability (e.g., difficulty, coverage of skills). This restricts scalability, curriculum design, and robust evaluation. The authors aim to build a more scalable, controllable, and verifiable training signal for visual RL that can continuously generate diverse, correctly labeled tasks tailored to a model’s current ability.

Method: The authors propose TRON (Targeted, Rule-verifiable Online eNvironments), a programmatic substrate that acts as an on-demand generator of visual reasoning tasks. For each training rollout, a generator-verifier program samples a latent visual state, renders a corresponding image, generates a question, and uses built-in rules to exactly verify the answer. TRON currently includes 520 distinct environments grouped into five skill buckets: spatial reasoning, mathematical reasoning, diagram understanding, pattern/logic, and counting. The same environment suite can be used for training one unified model across all buckets or separate specialist models per bucket, without further data collection. They further perform a structured analysis of the substrate, including checks on generation reliability, diversity of instances and difficulty levels, cross-environment near-duplicates, and baseline model pass rates as a function of difficulty. They then apply a specific RL post-training method (referred to as METHOD) using TRON-generated rollouts to train several vision-language models (Qwen3-VL-4B, Qwen2.5-VL-7B, MiMo-VL-7B-SFT).

Result: TRON delivers an effectively unbounded supply of automatically labeled, difficulty-controllable visual reasoning tasks across 520 environments and five reasoning categories. The analysis shows the generator-verifier pipeline is reliable, produces diverse instances and difficulty levels, and has manageable cross-environment duplication. Using TRON as the training substrate, RL post-training with the proposed METHOD consistently improves performance across ten external multimodal reasoning benchmarks for multiple base models (Qwen3-VL-4B, Qwen2.5-VL-7B, MiMo-VL-7B-SFT), demonstrating good transfer from synthetic, programmatically generated tasks to real-world benchmarks.

Conclusion: Programmatic, rule-verifiable online environments such as TRON can replace static curated datasets as the primary training signal for visual reasoning RL. By generating an infinite stream of fresh, difficulty-controlled, and exactly verifiable visual QA instances, TRON enables scalable curriculum learning and reduces reliance on expensive data collection. The environment suite supports both broad and specialist training regimes and yields consistent gains on diverse external multimodal benchmarks, suggesting that carefully designed synthetic environments are an effective way to improve general visual reasoning capabilities in vision-language models.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) for visual reasoning needs scalable, verifiable, and controllable training signals. Existing visual RL post-training trains on static curated datasets, with fixed image-question-answer samples bounded by their collection budget. In this work, we introduce TRON (Targeted, Rule-verifiable Online eNvironments), an online environment substrate: a training rollout is generated on demand by a controllable generator-verifier program that samples a fresh latent visual state, renders an image, asks a question, and exactly verifies the answer. A single run can therefore draw an unbounded stream of fresh instances at the difficulty level required by the current curriculum. The current TRON suite contains 520 environments organized into five ability buckets (spatial, mathematical, diagram, pattern/logic, and counting); the same substrate supports both a single full model trained on all buckets and per-bucket ability-specialist models, with no additional data collection. We also introduce a substrate analysis covering generation reliability, instance and level diversity, cross-environment near-duplicates, and base-model pass rate by difficulty level. RL post-training with METHOD consistently improves performance on ten external multimodal reasoning benchmarks across Qwen3-VL-4B, Qwen2.5-VL-7B, and MiMo-VL-7B-SFT.

</details>


### [298] [Revisiting Ripple Effects in Knowledge Editing through Pressure-Aware Joint Neighborhood Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01610)
*Haoben Huang,Shuxin Liu,Ou Wu,Di Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They study how single knowledge edits in large language models cause coupled ripple effects in nearby facts, and propose a joint optimization framework (JNO) that plans edits to improve desirable propagation while limiting unintended changes, achieving better metrics on RippleEdits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: When updating a specific fact in a large language model, the change can propagate to related facts (sometimes desired) and also accidentally corrupt other supposedly preserved knowledge. Existing edit methods treat these as separate problems and do not explicitly model how they interact, leading to suboptimal control over the overall behavior of the model after an edit.

Method: They empirically analyze ripple responses of several baseline knowledge-editing methods, and identify two coupled design pressures: (1) editable-side coordination (how to coherently update a cluster of related knowledge around the edit) and (2) preserved-side leakage (how much the edit bleeds into facts that should remain unchanged). Based on this, they propose Joint Neighborhood Optimization (JNO), a framework that plans edits in the representation space. JNO uses Pressure-Aware Coordination (PAC) to jointly optimize target representations for a local neighborhood under coupled constraints reflecting both pressures, and incorporates a semantic pre-execution gate that inspects and rejects high-risk target plans before actually updating model parameters.

Result: On the RippleEdits benchmark, JNO achieves at least a 7.0% improvement on both propagation and preservation metrics compared with prior methods, while maintaining editing stability across different backbone model architectures.

Conclusion: Jointly modeling propagation and preservation as coupled design pressures during the target-planning stage leads to better-behaved knowledge edits in LLMs. The proposed JNO framework, with PAC and a semantic pre-execution gate, yields more desirable ripple effects—stronger intended propagation and reduced unintended leakage—without sacrificing stability across backbones.

Abstract: Single-edit updates in large language models can trigger ripple effects across local knowledge neighborhoods: desirable propagation to related facts and unintended perturbation of preserved ones. Existing methods address these two effects separately, without explicitly modeling their coupling. We challenge this separation through an analysis of ripple responses across typical baselines, identifying two coupled design pressures: editable-side coordination and preserved-side leakage. We propose Joint Neighborhood Optimization (JNO), a new knowledge-editing framework to formalize and jointly address both pressures at the target-planning stage. JNO instantiates this principle through Pressure-Aware Coordination (PAC), which jointly optimizes neighborhood target representations under coupled constraints, and a semantic pre-execution gate that rejects high-risk target plans before parameter execution. Experiments on RippleEdits show JNO improves propagation and preservation metrics by at least 7.0% while preserving cross-backbone editing stability.

</details>


### [299] [ReSkill: Reconciling Skill Creation with Policy Optimization in Agentic RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01619)
*Zelin He,Haotian Lin,Boran Han,Wei Zhu,Haoyang Fang,Bernie Wang,Xuan Zhu,Runze Li,Matthew Reimherr*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ReSkill is an RL-in-the-loop framework that lets LLM agents automatically create, test, refine, and prune modular skills so they generalize better across tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing agentic RL for LLMs learns policies that improve on given tasks, but they fail to organize what they learn into reusable, modular strategies that transfer to new tasks. Prior skill-augmented RL methods create skills separately from policy optimization, which can introduce skills that conflict with the current policy and hinder learning. The paper aims to tightly couple skill evolution with policy learning so skills remain useful, aligned, and transferable as the agent improves.

Method: The authors propose ReSkill, built on the group-wise structure of GRPO. It adds three key mechanisms with little overhead: (1) an assertion-driven skill creator that inspects past failures and generates conditional, trigger-based skill revisions; (2) within-group rollout sampling that evaluates different versions of a skill in matched conditions to see which one best aids the current policy; and (3) a Thompson Sampling scheme with adaptive discounting to choose among competing skill versions over time, balancing exploration of new skills and exploitation of proven ones as the policy changes.

Result: In multiple experimental domains, ReSkill systematically outperforms memory-based and existing skill-based RL baselines, with especially strong improvements on previously unseen tasks, indicating better generalization. Empirical analyses of training trajectories illustrate how skills are automatically created, compared, refined, and removed as needed.

Conclusion: ReSkill successfully couples skill learning with policy optimization in agentic RL. By embedding skill creation, evaluation, and selection directly in the RL loop, it supports ongoing co-evolution of skills and policy, leading to more reusable strategies and stronger performance, particularly on new tasks.

Abstract: Agentic reinforcement learning (RL) enables LLM agents to improve continuously from environment rewards, yet the resulting policies do not systematically accumulate reusable strategies that generalize across tasks. Modular skills can provide such reusable strategies, yet existing skill-augmented RL methods decouple skill creation from policy optimization, risking adopting skills that conflict with the evolving policy. Inspired by Anthropic's Skill Creator, we introduce ReSkill, an RL-in-the-loop skill creation framework that reconciles skill evolution with policy learning. ReSkill exploits the group-wise structure of GRPO to naturally embed three mechanisms with only marginal additional overhead: (1) an assertion-driven skill creator that diagnoses failures from past experience and proposes conditional, trigger-based skill revisions; (2) within-group rollout sampling that enables controlled comparison of skill versions, capturing which version best supports the policy's ongoing learning; and (3) Thompson Sampling with adaptive discounting to balance exploration and exploitation in skill version selection as the policy evolves. Across several domains, ReSkill consistently outperforms existing memory and skill-based RL methods, with the largest gains on unseen tasks. Analysis of the skill lifecycle shows skills being automatically created, tested, refined, and pruned as the policy improves, demonstrating reconciled skill-policy co-evolution.

</details>


### [300] [MobEvolve: An Agentic Self-Evolving Heuristic System for Interpretable Human Mobility Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01640)
*Junlin He,Yihong Tang,Tong Nie,Ao Qu,Yuebing Liang,Hamzeh Alizadeh,Bang Liu,Wei Ma,Lijun Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MobEvolve is an agentic, self-evolving heuristic framework that uses an LLM to iteratively refine a behavior-inspired rule system for generating realistic human mobility trajectories, achieving better fidelity, distributional alignment, and efficiency than prior deep or LLM-based models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing human mobility generation methods (deep generative models, LLM-based approaches, and fixed heuristics) cannot simultaneously deliver interpretability, realistic individual behaviors, population-level statistical alignment, and fast inference. There is a need for a method that combines the transparency and efficiency of heuristics with the adaptivity and performance of learning-based approaches.

Method: The authors design MobEvolve, which starts from a behavior-inspired heuristic system that encodes rules for generating trip chains for individuals. An LLM-based agent is used to inspect validation data, diagnose where the current heuristic system fails (empirical misalignments and failure cases), and then propose concrete updates to the heuristic rules. These updates are accumulated as an evolution memory, enabling iterative self-improvement of the heuristic logic over multiple evolution rounds. The framework is evaluated on real-world benchmarks from Singapore and Montreal.

Result: On both the Singapore and Montreal datasets, MobEvolve outperforms state-of-the-art deep generative models and LLM-based baselines on metrics of individual trajectory fidelity, alignment with population-level distributions, and behavioral plausibility. At the same time, it maintains interpretability of its rule system and offers high inference efficiency, indicating that the evolved heuristics are both transparent and computationally lightweight compared with large learned models.

Conclusion: An agentic self-evolving heuristic framework can effectively bridge the gap between rigid, interpretable heuristics and opaque, high-performing generative models in human mobility generation. By using an LLM agent to iteratively refine a behavior-inspired rule system, MobEvolve achieves more realistic, behaviorally plausible, and statistically aligned mobility trajectories while remaining interpretable and efficient, suggesting a promising paradigm for other domains requiring both performance and transparency.

Abstract: Human mobility generation aims to synthesize realistic trip chains for target populations based on individual features. Existing paradigms, including deep generative models, LLM-based methods, and traditional heuristics, struggle to satisfy the complex demands of this task while simultaneously maintaining interpretability, behavioral plausibility, population-level distributional alignment, and inference efficiency. To bridge this gap, we introduce MobEvolve, the first agentic self-evolving heuristic framework for human mobility generation. MobEvolve initializes a behavior-inspired heuristic system and employs an LLM agent to iteratively evolve its internal logic. By diagnosing empirical misalignments and failure cases on a validation set, the agent proposes targeted updates and accumulates evolution memory for cumulative self-improvement. Extensive evaluations on the Singapore and Montreal benchmarks demonstrate that MobEvolve significantly outperforms state-of-the-art deep generative and LLM-based methods in individual trajectory fidelity, population-level distribution alignment, and behavioral plausibility, while preserving interpretability and high inference efficiency.

</details>


### [301] [Characterization of Multi-Model Agentic AI Systems on General Tasks via Trace-Driven Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01725)
*Donghwan Kim,Prakhar Singh,Younghoon Min,Jongryool Kim,Jongse Park,Kiwan Maeng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces GAIATrace, a token-level trace dataset and a replay simulator (Vidur-Agent) to study system-level behavior of agentic AI on general tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agentic AI systems that iteratively plan, use tools, and reason over outcomes are widely used, but their overall system-level behavior is poorly understood, especially on complex benchmarks and for sophisticated agent architectures. This gap is due to non-deterministic executions, high costs of large-scale evaluations, and lack of visibility into proprietary models’ inner workings. The authors aim to enable rigorous, reproducible, and fine-grained analysis of such systems.

Method: The authors construct GAIATrace, a detailed dataset recording token-level traces of two state-of-the-art agentic AI systems (MiroThinker and OWL) as they solve tasks from GAIA, a heterogeneous benchmark of general-purpose tasks. GAIATrace logs full reasoning tokens, task-level structures, and the activities of all major participating LLM components. They also build Vidur-Agent, a trace-driven simulator that replays these traces to emulate agent behavior under varied simulated environments, enabling low-cost, reproducible experiments without repeatedly querying live models.

Result: They obtain comprehensive traces for two cutting-edge agentic systems on the GAIA benchmark and demonstrate that these traces can be used with Vidur-Agent to systematically evaluate and probe agent behavior. Through analyses based on this dataset and simulator, they uncover several distinctive patterns in how modern agentic systems approach general tasks and how different architectural and design decisions influence their operation and performance.

Conclusion: GAIATrace and Vidur-Agent together provide a new experimental foundation for studying agentic AI systems at fine granularity and low cost. By exposing full reasoning tokens and system structure, they make it possible to perform reproducible, system-level evaluations and to better understand the impact of design choices on agent behavior across a wide range of general-purpose tasks.

Abstract: Agentic AI completes tasks through iterative planning, tool use, and reasoning based on observed outcomes. Despite its popularity, its system-level behavior remains poorly understood, particularly for complex datasets and agent architectures-owing to highly non-deterministic execution, prohibitive evaluation costs, and limited visibility into proprietary models. This paper presents GAIATrace, the first token-level trace dataset of two state-of-the-art agentic systems (MiroThinker and OWL) running GAIA, a benchmark composed of a heterogeneous mix of general-purpose tasks. Unlike prior trace datasets, GAIATrace captures full reasoning tokens, task-level structures, and activities of every major participating LLMs, enabling in-depth systems research. Complementing the dataset, we present Vidur-Agent, a trace-driven simulator that can replay GAIATrace to perform reproducible, low-cost system evaluation across diverse simulated environments. Using both artifacts, we characterize how modern agentic systems handle general tasks and how various system design choices shape their behavior, yielding several unique findings.

</details>


### [302] [Evidence-Gated LLM Priors for Multi-Objective Bayesian Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01730)
*Jiangyu Chen,Banyi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces a reputation-based mechanism to safely use LLM-generated priors in discrete multi-objective Bayesian optimization, dynamically calibrating and gating LLM influence per objective to improve robustness over naive use of LLM advice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are increasingly used as heuristic advisors for black-box optimization, but their suggestions and confidence scores are often miscalibrated relative to actual objective values, especially in multi-objective settings where different objectives require different expertise. Blindly trusting LLM priors can thus harm optimization performance, and there is a need for methods that can leverage LLM knowledge when it is helpful while down-weighting or ignoring it when it is misleading.

Method: The authors design an objective-wise reputation-market mechanism that models each expert-objective pair (including LLMs) as a falsifiable prior source. Expert weights are updated online using observed objective feedback, with time discounting and a global market-level trust gate. They then propose a decoupled counterfactual gating scheme with three modes: use the LLM prior without confidence, use it with confidence, or abstain from it entirely. They evaluate these mechanisms on synthetic stress tests and molecular optimization benchmarks using qwenflash-generated priors, and also explore a margin-portfolio strategy for gate selection based on prior error margins.

Result: Dynamic objective-wise calibration of LLM priors leads to more robust optimization performance compared to fixed LLM priors in both synthetic tests and molecule optimization tasks. However, LLM self-reported confidence is inconsistently useful: on ESOL, higher confidence correlates with larger prediction errors; on FreeSolv, incorporating confidence helps; on Lipophilicity, ignoring confidence yields the best results. The proposed three-arm counterfactual gate outperforms an earlier counterfactual variant on ESOL and FreeSolv, while a margin-based portfolio approach fails to provide consistent gains, revealing that naive margin selection is inadequate.

Conclusion: Reputation-based, objective-wise calibration and gated use of LLM-generated priors can safely and effectively integrate LLM expertise into discrete multi-objective Bayesian optimization, improving robustness over blindly trusting LLMs. LLM confidence should not be relied on as a generic quality signal, as its utility is dataset-dependent. Effective gating strategies must be acquisition-aware rather than based solely on one-step prior error margins, suggesting future work on tighter integration between acquisition design and expert-prior calibration.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as heuristic advisors for black-box optimization, yet their suggestions and self-reported confidence are not necessarily calibrated to downstream objective values. This issue becomes more pronounced in multi-objective Bayesian optimization, where different objectives may require different expert knowledge and where an LLM expert can be useful for one objective but misleading for another.
  We study how to use LLM-generated expert priors in discrete multi-objective Bayesian optimization without blindly trusting them. We propose an objective-wise reputation-market mechanism that treats each expert-objective pair as a falsifiable prior source. Expert weights are updated online from observed objective feedback, discounted over time, and gated by market-level trust. We then introduce a decoupled counterfactual gate that can use the LLM prior without confidence, use it with confidence, or abstain from the LLM prior entirely.
  Across controlled synthetic stress tests and three molecule optimization benchmarks with \qwenflash{}-generated expert priors, we find that dynamic objective-wise calibration improves robustness over fixed LLM priors. However, raw LLM confidence is not reliably beneficial: on ESOL, confidence is positively correlated with prediction error; on FreeSolv, confidence can help; and on Lipophilicity, ignoring confidence remains strongest. Our fixed three-arm counterfactual gate improves over the first counterfactual variant on ESOL and FreeSolv, while an attempted margin portfolio exposes a useful negative result: margin selection should be acquisition-aware rather than based only on one-step prior error.

</details>


### [303] [TrafficRAG: A Multimodal RAG Framework for Traffic Accident Liability Determination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01737)
*Xu Li,Zedong Fu,Xinyi Li,Xun Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TrafficRAG is a multimodal retrieval-augmented framework that converts accident videos into text, retrieves relevant laws and similar cases, and uses an LLM to generate standardized, legally grounded traffic liability reports, significantly improving accuracy and reliability over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traffic accident liability analysis is important for transportation safety and legal assistance but current approaches are inefficient, subjective, and inconsistent. Large language models alone struggle due to noisy video input and a lack of specialized legal knowledge, motivating a system that can ground LLM reasoning in structured multimodal evidence and explicit legal regulations.

Method: The framework first uses a vision-language model to turn traffic accident videos into structured textual descriptions of the scene. These descriptions are used as queries for a hybrid retrieval module that combines BM25-based sparse retrieval and dense embedding retrieval to fetch relevant traffic regulations and similar past cases. A large language model then takes the retrieved legal documents and the multimodal accident evidence as input, performs comprehensive reasoning, and outputs standardized liability analysis reports.

Result: Experiments show that TrafficRAG outperforms baseline methods, reaching 77.32% Legal Norm Adaptation Accuracy, 81.71% Factual Faithfulness, and a Liability Ratio Mean Absolute Error of 5.48%, indicating more accurate application of legal norms, more faithful grounding in facts, and more precise liability apportionment.

Conclusion: By integrating structured descriptions from accident videos with retrieved legal clauses and historical cases through a retrieval-augmented generation pipeline, TrafficRAG improves the reliability, objectivity, and accuracy of automated traffic accident liability determinations and report generation.

Abstract: Traffic accident liability analysis is a critical yet challenging task in intelligent transportation and legal assistance. Existing methods often suffer from low efficiency, subjective judgment, and inconsistent analysis results. Meanwhile, large language models are constrained by noisy video inputs and insufficient legal domain knowledge. To address these issues, this work presents TrafficRAG, a multimodal retrieval-augmented framework for automated traffic accident analysis and report generation. Specifically, the proposed framework first adopts a vision-language model to produce structured textual descriptions of accident scenarios, which serve as accurate retrieval queries. Based on these textual queries, a hybrid retrieval strategy integrating BM25 sparse retrieval and dense embedding retrieval is employed to fetch relevant traffic regulations and similar historical cases. Finally, the large language model incorporates retrieved legal knowledge and multimodal accident evidence for comprehensive reasoning, and generates standardized, legally grounded liability analysis reports. Extensive experiments show that TrafficRAG consistently outperforms baseline methods, achieving 77.32% Legal Norm Adaptation Accuracy, 81.71% Factual Faithfulness, and a Liability Ratio MAE of 5.48%. The results validate that integrating multimodal factual evidence with legal clauses via retrieval augmentation can effectively improve the reliability and accuracy of traffic accident liability determination.

</details>


### [304] [TriAlign: Towards Universal Truth Consistency in Personalized LLM Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01755)
*Thi-Nhung Nguyen,Linhao Luo,Rollin Omari,Junae Kim,Thuy-Trang Vu,Dinh Phung*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Truth-Invariant Alignment (TIA) for personalized LLMs and proposes TriAlign, an offline multi-agent RL framework that improves objective accuracy and personalization while keeping universal truths consistent and fair across social groups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Personalized LLMs can adapt to users’ preferences and social attributes, but this personalization can cause systematic accuracy gaps and inconsistent answers to universal, objective questions across different social groups. Existing alignment methods focus mainly on subjective preference alignment and do not explicitly address fairness or truth consistency between groups, leading to potential harms and bias. The paper is motivated by the need for a principled way to preserve both personalization and fairness while ensuring that objective truths do not vary by social group.

Method: The authors formalize Truth-Invariant Alignment (TIA) as an alignment objective for personalized LLMs that requires universal truths to be consistent across social groups while still preserving personalization. They propose TriAlign, an offline multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) framework where each social group is modeled as an agent. TriAlign optimizes a fairness-aware objective that jointly accounts for (1) universal truth accuracy, (2) cross-group truth consistency, and (3) personalization quality. The method includes an explicit penalty for cross-group inconsistencies in responses to objective questions, and uses offline MARL training on logged interaction data to update policies for each group under shared constraints.

Result: Across multiple benchmarks, TriAlign outperforms strong baseline alignment methods in jointly optimizing universal truth accuracy, cross-group consistency, and personalization. It reduces disparities in the accuracy of universal truths across social groups, improves overall performance on objective tasks, and maintains or enhances personalization quality compared to baselines that either ignore fairness or focus only on preference alignment.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that personalizing LLMs without explicit truth-invariant and fairness-aware alignment can lead to harmful disparities in objective accuracy across social groups. TriAlign, as a concrete instantiation of Truth-Invariant Alignment via offline MARL, offers an effective way to mitigate these issues, achieving a better balance between universal truth consistency, fairness, and user personalization. The authors position TIA and TriAlign as foundational steps toward more equitable and reliable personalized LLMs, and suggest future work on broader social attributes, more complex interaction settings, and real-world deployment considerations.

Abstract: Personalized large language models adapt responses to users' preferences and social attributes, but can introduce substantial universal truth inconsistencies across social groups, where some groups systematically receive less accurate responses on objective tasks. Existing alignment methods either ignore personalization or mainly focus on subjective preference alignment, largely overlooking fairness and consistency in universal truths. To address this gap, we study Truth-Invariant Alignment (TIA), an alignment problem for personalized LLMs that aims to ensure universal truths remain consistent across social groups while preserving personalization. We propose TriAlign, the first offline multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) framework for TIA, where each social group is modeled as an agent interacting. TriAlign jointly optimizes universal truth accuracy, cross-group truth consistency, and personalization through a fairness-aware objective and an explicit inconsistency penalty. Experiments across diverse benchmarks demonstrate that TriAlign achieves a stronger balance among these three objectives than strong baselines, reducing universal truth disparities across social groups while improving both objective task performance and personalization quality.

</details>


### [305] [EvoBrain: Continual Learning of EEG Foundation Models Across Heterogeneous BCI Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01767)
*Yangxuan Zhou,Sha Zhao,Jiquan Wang,Shijian Li,Gang Pan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EvoBrain is a continual-learning framework that turns adaptation of EEG foundation models across many BCI tasks into a single, unified process, improving performance and reducing forgetting across tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing EEG-based BCIs use task-specific or statically fine-tuned models, which do not scale across many heterogeneous tasks and require separate models or heads. Even EEG foundation models, though pre-trained on large datasets, are still adapted via isolated fine-tuning per task, which blocks cross-task knowledge transfer, causes catastrophic forgetting, and leads to linear growth of computation and storage with the number of tasks. There is a need for a dynamic, scalable way to adapt EEG foundation models across multiple tasks while retaining old knowledge.

Method: The authors cast downstream EEG task adaptation as a cross-task continual learning problem and propose EvoBrain, a dynamic task-aware framework. EvoBrain combines two key components: (1) Neuro-Spectral Task Normalization (NSN), which aligns new tasks to historical feature statistics and adaptively recalibrates spectral responses to cope with distributional and neuro-spectral shifts; and (2) Response-Affinity Distillation (RAD) with time-dependent replay, which distills and preserves the response geometry of previous tasks and uses task affinity (spectral compatibility) to guide selective knowledge transfer. Together these mechanisms manage the stability–plasticity trade-off during continual adaptation of EEG foundation models.

Result: On six different BCI tasks and across multiple EEG foundation model backbones, EvoBrain consistently outperforms state-of-the-art continual learning and adaptation methods in accuracy and overall decoding performance, while better maintaining performance on previous tasks (less catastrophic forgetting) and effectively exploiting beneficial transfer between spectrally related tasks.

Conclusion: EvoBrain demonstrates that formulating EEG foundation model adaptation as cross-task continual learning, and explicitly addressing spectral shifts and task relationships, yields a more scalable and unified approach to EEG decoding. The framework optimally balances plasticity (learning new tasks) and stability (retaining old tasks), outperforming prior methods and taking a significant step toward a universal, one-for-all EEG brain decoding system.

Abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) is the cornerstone of non-invasive brain-computer interfaces (BCIs), yet conventional decoding relies on fragmented, task-specific architectures that severely limit cross-task scalability. While EEG foundation models pre-trained on massive corpora promise universal brain decoding, current post-training depends on task-isolated fine-tuning. This static paradigm restricts knowledge transfer across heterogeneous tasks, hinders model scalability, and incurs computational and storage overheads that scale linearly with task count. To overcome these bottlenecks, we formulate downstream adaptation as a cross-task continual learning problem and propose EvoBrain, a dynamic, task-aware continual learning framework for unified EEG decoding. EvoBrain addresses the plasticity-stability trade-off via two complementary components: (1) Neuro-Spectral Task Normalization (NSN) aligns incoming tasks with historical statistics while recalibrating spectral responses to handle distributional and neuro-spectral shifts; and (2) Response-Affinity Distillation (RAD), combined with time-dependent replay, preserves old-task response geometry and promotes selective knowledge transfer between spectrally compatible tasks, effectively mitigating forgetting. Extensive evaluations across six distinct BCI tasks demonstrate that EvoBrain consistently surpasses state-of-the-art methods across diverse foundation backbones, optimally balancing plasticity and stability. To our knowledge, this work pioneers cross-task continual learning in the EEG domain, advancing the realization of a unified, one-for-all brain decoding system.

</details>


### [306] [Structure-Guided Adaptive Propagation for Protein-Protein Interaction Site Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01781)
*Enqiang Zhu,Yizi Liu,Yilong Luo,Yao Chen,Yu Zhang,Baoshan Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces SGAP-PPIS, a protein-protein interaction site predictor that uses structure-guided adaptive information propagation over protein graphs to better distinguish true interface residues from similar non-interacting residues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing graph-based deep learning methods for predicting protein-protein interaction sites use fixed message-passing/propagation schemes that treat all residues similarly. This ignores the fact that residues at interfaces exist in diverse local geometric and functional contexts. As a result, models struggle to adapt how information diffuses depending on the local 3D structure, hindering accurate discrimination between real interaction sites and nearby but non-interacting residues.

Method: The authors propose SGAP-PPIS, a structure-guided adaptive propagation model. They first use an equivariant graph neural network to compute multi-scale geometric states for residues that respect the 3D structure. From these geometric states, the model learns residue-wise propagation coefficients that control how much each residue preserves its own features vs. diffuses information to and from neighbors. The propagation is therefore adaptive, conditioned on each residue’s geometric microenvironment, and is further enhanced by scale-aligned geometric guidance and multi-step propagation-state representations.

Result: On the Test_60 benchmark, SGAP-PPIS reaches performance that is competitive with current state-of-the-art PPIS predictors. Ablation experiments demonstrate that removing geometry-conditioned adaptive propagation, scale-aligned geometric guidance, or multi-step propagation-state representation degrades performance, indicating that each of these components contributes meaningfully to the gains.

Conclusion: Incorporating structure-guided, residue-wise adaptive propagation into graph-based models improves protein-protein interaction site prediction. By leveraging multi-scale geometric information from an equivariant GNN to control local information diffusion, SGAP-PPIS better captures heterogeneous interface environments and outperforms or rivals existing methods, with ablations confirming the importance of its key architectural innovations.

Abstract: Accurate prediction of protein-protein interaction sites (PPIS) is essential for understanding cellular processes, disease mechanisms, and therapeutic target discovery. Graph-based deep learning has advanced PPIS prediction by incorporating residue-level structural context. However, most graph-based models still rely on fixed propagation schemes that treat all residues similarly, despite the structural and functional heterogeneity of protein interfaces. Such propagation may limit the ability to adapt information diffusion to local geometric environments, making it difficult to distinguish true interaction sites from structurally similar non-interacting neighbors. We present SGAP-PPIS, a structure-guided adaptive propagation model for PPIS prediction. Rather than using a fixed propagation mechanism, SGAP-PPIS leverages multi-scale geometric states from an equivariant graph neural network to generate residue-wise propagation coefficients. This design allows each residue to adaptively balance local feature preservation and neighborhood diffusion according to its geometric microenvironment. Experimental results show that SGAP-PPIS achieves competitive performance among the state-of-the-art methods on Test\_60. Ablation studies show that geometry-conditioned adaptive propagation, scale-aligned geometric guidance, and multi-step propagation-state representation jointly drive these improvements.

</details>


### [307] [Stochastic convergence of parallel asynchronous adaptive first-order methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01787)
*Serge Gratton,Philippe L. Toint*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces and analyzes a new class of asynchronous adaptive first-order optimizers for large-scale, heterogeneous ML systems, proving O(1/sqrt{t}) convergence (up to logs) on non-convex problems and validating with experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing adaptive first-order optimization methods (like Adam, RMSProp, etc.) are mostly designed and analyzed for synchronous settings. Modern large-scale and heterogeneous machine learning systems, however, heavily rely on asynchronous, distributed computation where workers may have stale gradients and different speeds. There is a need for principled, theoretically grounded optimization algorithms that are both adaptive and asynchronous, with guarantees for non-convex objectives prevalent in deep learning.

Method: Define a broad class of asynchronous adaptive first-order methods that extend popular optimizers to settings with delayed/stale gradient information. Consider variants that incorporate momentum and use either exact or inexact gradient normalization. Formulate the optimization process in a fully stochastic framework and impose reasonable assumptions on delays, stochastic gradients, and step sizes. Derive non-asymptotic convergence bounds for these methods on non-convex functions, carefully handling asynchrony and adaptivity in the analysis.

Result: Proves that the proposed class of asynchronous adaptive methods achieves convergence rates of order O(1/sqrt{t}) on non-convex objectives, up to logarithmic factors, under the stated assumptions. Shows that the momentum and inexact normalization variants also enjoy similar guarantees. Provides numerical experiments demonstrating that these algorithms perform well and are practically effective in heterogeneous, large-scale machine learning environments.

Conclusion: Asynchronous adaptive first-order methods can be designed to retain the practical advantages of popular adaptive optimizers while accommodating the realities of large-scale, heterogeneous, and asynchronous computing environments. The theoretical analysis establishes near-optimal O(1/sqrt{t}) convergence (modulo log factors) for non-convex problems, and empirical results indicate that these methods are well-suited for large-scale machine learning systems in practice.

Abstract: A new class of asynchronous adaptive first-order optimization methods is introduced, comprising asynchronous variants of several popular algorithms. Versions of these methods using momentum and/or inexact normalization are also considered. The convergence of methods in the class on non-convex functions is analyzed in a fully stochastic setting, and is shown to be (up to logarithmic factors) of order O(1/sqrt{t}) under reasonable assumptions. Numerical experiments suggest that such asynchronous adaptive algorithms are very relevant in heterogeneous large-scale machine learning systems.

</details>


### [308] [Consistency evaluation of benchmarks used for causal discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01789)
*Yuzhe Zhang,Chihui Chen,Lina Yao,Chen Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper evaluates the reliability of existing benchmark causal graphs using an automated, LLM-based pipeline that cross-checks them against up-to-date domain literature, revealing large inconsistencies across popular benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Causal discovery methods are usually assessed on benchmark causal graphs assumed to encode correct domain knowledge. However, as scientific domains evolve, these benchmarks may become outdated or partially wrong, leading to misleading evaluations—especially for LLM-based methods that can reflect the latest literature. There is currently no systematic way to assess and update the quality of these benchmark graphs, which undermines fair comparison and progress in causal discovery research.

Method: The authors build an automated pipeline that: (1) retrieves large collections of relevant domain papers from scientific databases for each benchmark graph, and (2) uses large language models prompted with the benchmark graph structure and textual evidence from papers to assess whether the reported causal relations are consistent with the scientific literature. They apply this procedure to 11 widely used real-world causal discovery benchmarks, processing a total of 38,081 domain-specific research articles, and then quantify the agreement between each benchmark’s edges and the literature-based LLM judgments.

Result: The analysis shows substantial variability in how well different benchmark causal graphs align with the current domain literature. Some benchmarks have many edges that are unsupported or contradicted by the papers, while others are more consistent. These discrepancies are systematically measured across all 11 benchmarks, evidencing that many widely used datasets contain mis-aligned or outdated causal relationships.

Conclusion: Benchmark causal graphs, which underpin evaluation in causal discovery, are far from uniformly reliable and often lag behind current scientific knowledge. The proposed LLM-based pipeline offers a scalable way to audit and update these benchmarks by comparing them against the latest domain research. The findings suggest that researchers should be cautious when interpreting results based on existing benchmarks and should consider benchmark quality and consistency as a central factor in evaluating causal discovery methods, particularly those leveraging LLMs.

Abstract: In graphical causal model, causal discovery aims to construct a causal graph based on numerical data and domain knowledge in plain text. However, the evaluation of causal discovery methods remains a challenge in the area as the progress of domain researches often makes benchmark causal graphs contain mis-aligned knowledge. This problem especially affects the evaluation of large language model (LLM) based causal discovery methods as they are sensitive to the new discoveries in the literature. This work is the first to systematically study the quality of benchmark causal graphs. Specifically, we design a pipeline that automatically retrieves relevant research papers from scientific databases, and prompts LLMs to check the consistency between the benchmark causal graphs and domain research papers. We evaluate 11 popular real-world benchmarks, for which our pipeline in total proceeds 38,081 domain papers. Our results show that popular benchmarks vary significantly in their consistency with domain research, with clear implications for causal discovery research.

</details>


### [309] [OctoT2I: A Self-Evolving Agentic Text-to-Image Router](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01803)
*Xu Jiang,Bin Chen,Gehui Li,Yule Duan,Ronggang Wang,Jian Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes OctoT2I, an agentic framework that coordinates multiple text-to-image models through a self-evolving knowledge base to jointly optimize image quality and inference efficiency, achieving strong performance and substantial speed/energy gains without human supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single text-to-image models, even when scaled up, show diminishing returns and cannot easily balance generation quality with inference efficiency. Existing multi-model (agentic) T2I approaches depend on costly human-designed priors or annotations, follow rigid decision paths, and often ignore speed and energy considerations. The authors are motivated to build an autonomous, flexible, and efficient multi-model system that can intelligently route prompts among tools while learning the tools’ capabilities without human supervision.

Method: They introduce OctoT2I, an agentic T2I framework that treats image generation as a joint optimization problem over quality and efficiency. OctoT2I uses a stateful, multi-round routing policy that chooses among multiple generation tools based on a learned knowledge base and memory of past interactions. The knowledge base is constructed via a Self-Evolving Mechanism: the system first autonomously defines foundational Conceptual Dimensions (such as style, color, and count), then iteratively runs a Propose–Solve–Evaluate–Learn (PSEL) loop. In each loop, it proposes combinations of conceptual dimensions, queries tools, evaluates outcomes, and updates its understanding of each tool’s capability frontier, gradually refining routing decisions without human supervision.

Result: On the GenEval benchmark, OctoT2I attains a competitive performance score of 0.96 while achieving a 90.3% reduction in inference latency and a 56.6% improvement in energy efficiency compared with the strong baseline Flow-GRPO. These results show that OctoT2I can maintain high generation quality while substantially improving inference speed and energy usage.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that OctoT2I effectively addresses the limitations of existing agentic T2I systems by autonomously building a rich, concept-based knowledge base and using it for adaptive multi-round routing across tools. This yields a favorable trade-off between image quality and computational efficiency, demonstrating that self-evolving, multi-model frameworks can surpass simple model scaling in both performance and practicality. The authors plan to release code and models to support further research and application.

Abstract: The explosive growth of Text-to-Image (T2I) models, from large-scale versions to lightweight, real-time ones, now faces diminishing marginal returns from single-model scaling. Agentic T2I methods emerged to alleviate this bottleneck by using multiple models. However, existing agentic T2I methods suffer from three key challenges: reliance on expensive handcrafted priors or human annotations, rigid single-path decision mechanisms, and a neglect of inference efficiency. To address these challenges, we introduce OctoT2I, a novel agentic framework that reformulates the T2I task as a joint optimization of generation quality and inference efficiency. OctoT2I implements a stateful, multi-round routing strategy that adaptively selects the most suitable tool based on its knowledge and memory. This strategy is enabled by a knowledge base built from scratch by our novel Self-Evolving Mechanism. This mechanism, which requires no human supervision, first autonomously defines foundational Conceptual Dimensions (eg, style, color, count) and then intelligently explores their combinations via an iterative" Propose--Solve--Evaluate--Learn"(PSEL) loop. The PSEL loop efficiently discovers each tool's capability frontier, driving continuous improvement without external guidance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OctoT2I achieves competitive performance (0.96) on GenEval while delivering a 90.3% inference speedup and a 56.6% energy-efficiency gain over the leading baseline (Flow-GRPO), striking an exceptional balance between performance and efficiency. Code and models will be made available.

</details>


### [310] [Token Predictors Are Not Planners: Building Physically Grounded Causal Reasoners](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01810)
*Zheng Lu,Mingqi Gao,Qinlei Xie,Wanqi Zhong,Hanwen Cui,Heng Cao,Zirui Song,Yifan Yang,Chong Luo,Bei Liu,Yiming Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues current embodied vision-language planning benchmarks reward shallow language modeling instead of true physical reasoning, and introduces a new benchmark and large causal-reasoning corpus that show better physical planning emerges when models are trained on explicit causal traces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing embodied vision-language benchmarks largely reduce planning to predicting the next text token, so models can perform well by exploiting language statistics instead of understanding physical dynamics and causal dependencies in the environment. This mismatch hinders progress toward reliable physical autonomy, where agents must reason about future world states rather than just produce plausible verbal descriptions.

Method: The authors (1) design Causal-Plan-Bench, a high-fidelity diagnostic benchmark for embodied planning that decomposes evaluation into four causal dimensions, and validate it via a multi-stage verification pipeline; (2) build Causal-Plan-1M, a dataset of one million explicit causal reasoning traces over egocentric videos, constructed through a four-stage annotation pipeline; and (3) propose a training recipe that uses this causal data to train an embodied planning model, Causal Planner, based on the Qwen3-VL-8B architecture, focusing on predicting next physical states from visual input and reasoning traces.

Result: State-of-the-art models, including Gemini 3 Pro, perform poorly on the new benchmark (e.g., Gemini 3 Pro scores 38.18), indicating limited physical agency and causal understanding under this stricter evaluation. In contrast, the proposed Causal Planner model, trained with the new causal corpus and recipe, achieves substantially higher scores, demonstrates better next-state estimation, and generalizes across benchmarks. The authors also empirically identify a Causal Scaling Law: increasing causal training data to one million instances boosts performance by 36.3% relative, from 33.22 to 45.28 on their metric.

Conclusion: Benchmarks and training regimes that prioritize linguistic token prediction are insufficient for developing truly physically grounded autonomous agents. By introducing Causal-Plan-Bench and the Causal-Plan-1M dataset, and by training a model explicitly on causal traces, the paper shows that models can acquire more genuine physical reasoning abilities. The observed Causal Scaling Law suggests that scaling structured causal supervision is a key pathway to stronger embodied causal planners, helping shift the field from superficial language mimicry toward robust causal reasoning about physical environments.

Abstract: Current benchmarks for embodied vision-language planning often favor linguistic next-token prediction over physically grounded next-state reasoning. This rewards models that mimic statistical language priors rather than track causal dependencies, reducing physical planning to shallow sequence modeling. We argue that reliable physical autonomy requires a shift from linguistically grounded token prediction toward physically grounded causal reasoning. To this end, we introduce Causal-Plan-Bench, a high-fidelity diagnostic suite curated through multi-stage verification to evaluate embodied planning across four causal dimensions. We also construct Causal-Plan-1M, a million-scale corpus of explicit reasoning traces produced by a four-stage annotation pipeline over egocentric videos. Extensive evaluation shows that leading models still struggle to demonstrate genuine physical agency, with Gemini 3 Pro reaching only 38.18 on our benchmark. In contrast, our training recipe enables Causal Planner, built on Qwen3-VL-8B, to internalize physical logic for more accurate next-state estimation. The model achieves strong in-domain performance and cross-benchmark generalization, and reveals a Causal Scaling Law: scaling causal training data to one million instances yields a 36.3% relative gain, from 33.22 to 45.28. Overall, our work provides a concrete step toward turning agents from superficial token predictors into physically grounded causal reasoners.

</details>


### [311] [CAPF: Guiding Search-Agent Rollouts with Credit-Attenuated Privileged Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01830)
*Bin Chen,Xinye Liao,Yiming Liu,Xin Liao,Chonghan Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Credit-Attenuated Privileged Feedback (CAPF), a training-time mechanism that leverages verifier-side information in RL with verifiable rewards to turn failed rollouts into successful repair trajectories, improving search-augmented reasoning performance of LLM agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Outcome-only RL with verifiable rewards (RLVR) on hard search-augmented reasoning tasks suffers because successful end-to-end trajectories are rare, leaving the learner with very few positive signals. However, the verifier often has rich information about why an answer is wrong or incomplete that is ignored in standard RLVR. The authors want to exploit this existing verifier-side information to guide training more effectively without changing the deployment-time interface (no privileged signals at test time).

Method: They propose Credit-Attenuated Privileged Feedback (CAPF), a training-time mechanism that allows the policy to query a verifier for structured privileged feedback after submitting an answer. This feedback identifies specific errors or omissions and guides a revision step within the same rollout. CAPF treats these revision-based trajectories as repair trajectories that can yield positive reward even when the initial attempt failed. To avoid over-crediting the privileged signal, they attenuate the learning credit assigned to the feedback call and earlier actions, aligning training with a deployment setting where the privileged feedback is not available.

Result: Using CAPF with Qwen3-4B as the base model on seven open-domain QA benchmarks, the average exact-match score improves from 44.7% with standard outcome-only RLVR to 48.5%, indicating more effective learning from sparse outcome rewards on hard reasoning problems.

Conclusion: Verifier-side information, when exposed as privileged feedback during training and carefully credit-assigned via CAPF, can significantly improve RLVR-based search agents on challenging QA tasks, by converting zero-reward attempts into useful repair trajectories without requiring extra signals at deployment time.

Abstract: Recent LLM search agents use reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) to learn search-augmented reasoning from outcome rewards. On hard problems, these agents rarely sample end-to-end successful rollouts, leaving outcome-only RLVR with few positive-reward trajectories. We argue that improving learning on such problems requires additional guidance during training, and RLVR already contains verifier-side information that can provide it. This information can identify errors or omissions in the agent's submitted answer and guide revision within the rollout. We propose a training-time mechanism called \textbf{Credit-Attenuated Privileged Feedback} (CAPF), which makes this verifier-side information available through a Privileged Feedback call during training. CAPF lets the policy revise zero-reward attempts into positive-reward repair trajectories and attenuates credit for the feedback call and earlier actions to accommodate deployment without this call. Empirical research demonstrates that CAPF improves Qwen3-4B's average exact-match score from 44.7% under outcome-only RLVR to 48.5% on seven open-domain QA benchmarks.

</details>


### [312] [Evaluation of Baseline Methods for IDD-based SSD External Memory Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01840)
*Yuki Suzuki,Alex Fukunaga*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how simple immediate duplicate detection strategies for A* search perform when using external memory, and how OS mechanisms like page caches affect that performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many hard search problems exceed the capacity of RAM, making standard A* inapplicable. Existing external-memory A* variants either rely on delayed duplicate detection or on complex immediate duplicate detection schemes. Simple, systematic baselines for immediate duplicate detection, and the influence of OS-level caching mechanisms, have not been thoroughly analyzed.

Method: The authors implement and evaluate simple baseline immediate duplicate detection (IDD) strategies for A* that access external memory devices such as SSDs and HDDs. They experimentally compare these IDD-based A* variants, paying particular attention to performance interactions with operating system mechanisms such as the page cache, measuring how these factors influence search efficiency and I/O behavior.

Result: The empirical results characterize the performance of simple IDD-based A* variants on external memory, showing how effective such basic strategies can be and under what conditions. The study also reveals how OS-level page caching and related mechanisms impact throughput, latency, and overall search performance when using external memory.

Conclusion: Simple baseline approaches to immediate duplicate detection for A* on external memory can be competitive and provide valuable performance insights, and OS-level caching mechanisms play a significant role in determining their efficiency. The paper fills a gap in the literature by systematically analyzing these baselines and highlighting the importance of accounting for OS behavior in the design and evaluation of external-memory search algorithms.

Abstract: Many difficult search problems cannot be solved by algorithms such as A* using only RAM. Search algorithms which use external memory such as SSDs and HDDs with much higher capacity than RAM have been proposed in previous work, but previous work has focused on delayed duplicate detection approaches, as well as complex immediate duplicate detection (IDD) methods, and relatively simple methods for IDD have not been systematically studied. In addition, the effect of OS-level mechanisms for managing and speeding up accesses to external memory, such as page caches, has not been studied. This paper addresses these gaps in the literature by evaluating and analyzing the performance of simple baseline approaches for IDD-based A*.

</details>


### [313] [Does Compression Preserve Uncertainty? A Unified Benchmark for Quantized and Sparse LLMs via Conformal Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01850)
*Yujia Tong,Yuxi Wang,Yunyang Wan,Tian Zhang,Junhao Dong,Jingling Yuan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies whether common model compression methods for LLMs (quantization and pruning) preserve not just accuracy but also reliable uncertainty estimation, finding that compression can break the link between accuracy and uncertainty and that larger models handle compression better.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While compression methods effectively reduce the computational and memory cost of deploying LLMs, current evaluations focus almost entirely on maintaining accuracy. In real-world, safety-critical scenarios, models also need to know when they are uncertain. The authors are motivated by the gap in understanding whether compression harms a model’s ability to quantify and communicate uncertainty, which is essential for trustworthy deployment.

Method: The authors systematically benchmark 12 large language models under different compression settings, including various levels of quantization and pruning. They evaluate these models on five NLP tasks and employ conformal prediction as a distribution-free framework to assess and compare uncertainty estimates across compressed and uncompressed models. This setup allows them to examine how compression affects the calibration and reliability of model uncertainty, beyond raw accuracy.

Result: The study finds three main effects: (I) compression often disrupts the usual relationship between accuracy and uncertainty, so that a model may remain accurate but poorly calibrated or over/under-confident; (II) larger models are more robust to compression, better preserving reliable uncertainty estimates than smaller models under similar compression levels; and (III) the degradation of uncertainty is not gradual but often occurs in threshold-like jumps, where small additional compression can suddenly cause a large increase in uncertainty or miscalibration.

Conclusion: Evaluating compressed LLMs solely by accuracy is inadequate, particularly for safety-critical applications. Model compression can significantly and nonlinearly distort uncertainty estimates, with robustness depending on model size. Therefore, uncertainty-aware evaluation—such as via conformal prediction—should become a standard part of compression and deployment pipelines to ensure models remain both efficient and trustworthy.

Abstract: Model compression techniques such as quantization and pruning are widely used to reduce the deployment cost of large language models (LLMs), with existing evaluations focusing almost exclusively on accuracy preservation. However, in safety-critical applications, a model's ability to reliably quantify its own uncertainty is equally important. We ask: does compression preserve this ability? To answer this question, we benchmark 12 LLMs under various compression configurations across five NLP tasks, using conformal prediction to provide a rigorous, distribution-free measure of uncertainty. Our experiments reveal that: (I) compression frequently decouples accuracy from uncertainty; (II) larger models absorb compression-induced uncertainty far more effectively than smaller ones; and (III) uncertainty inflation is often threshold-like rather than gradual. These results suggest that accuracy-only evaluation is insufficient for assessing the deployment readiness of compressed LLMs, and that uncertainty-aware benchmarking should be a standard component of model compression pipelines.

</details>


### [314] [WorldCoder-Bench: Benchmarking Physically Grounded 3D World Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01869)
*Shuo Lu,Yinuo Xu,Kecheng Yu,Siru Jiang,Yongcan Yu,Yubin Wang,Haitao Yang,Yuxiang Zhang,Bin Wang,Ran He,Jian Liang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They introduce WorldCoder-Bench, a benchmark and execution-based evaluation protocol for testing how well LLMs can generate correct, interactive Three.js 3D worlds from natural language.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are now being used to generate not just static web pages but complex, interactive 3D experiences in the browser. Existing benchmarks focus on what appears in the DOM or pixels, but Three.js applications encapsulate their core logic and state inside a canvas, making current evaluation methods blind to the true behavior and correctness of these worlds. There is a need for a benchmark and evaluation approach that can inspect and verify the internal runtime state and dynamics of generated 3D applications, including physics, interaction, and synchronization between UI and hidden state.

Method: They construct WorldCoder-Bench, a set of 2,026 expert-designed tasks spanning Simulation, Rendering, and Application categories, many involving .glb assets and hidden behavioral contracts that specify desired runtime behavior. They also develop StateProbe, an execution-based evaluation protocol that runs the generated Three.js programs in a sandboxed browser, programmatically probes their internal state and transitions, and checks them against hardened contracts designed to resist trivial or superficial solutions. They additionally define new utility metrics—Return on Automation and Time Efficiency Multiplier—to quantify correctness-adjusted cost and time savings when using different LLMs.

Result: Testing nine advanced LLMs on WorldCoder-Bench shows relatively low verification coverage: the best model attains 27.8% on the core benchmark (WorldCoder-Core) and 19.9% on a robustness-focused variant (WorldCoder-Robust). Detailed analysis attributes most failures not to missing or incorrect scene elements, but to issues in maintaining correct internal state representations and interaction chains over time. Utility metrics indicate that even cheaper or faster models can still be beneficial, especially on simpler tasks, despite lower overall coverage.

Conclusion: WorldCoder-Bench and the StateProbe protocol expose significant limitations in current LLMs’ ability to synthesize correct, robust, and physically grounded Three.js 3D worlds from natural language. The benchmark highlights that the main challenge lies in managing internal state and interaction logic rather than merely rendering the right visual content. The work provides a rigorous, behavior-focused evaluation framework and metrics for future research, and suggests that while current models struggle on complex scenarios, they can still offer practical value for easier domains.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly asked not only to write static interfaces, but to construct executable interactive worlds from natural language. Browser-native 3D, commonly built with Three.js, is a natural next frontier: generated programs must integrate assets, obey spatial and physical constraints, and keep user-facing controls synchronized with hidden runtime state. Existing web-generation benchmarks and evaluators, however, largely observe only pixels or DOM nodes, while the mechanics of a Three.js world unfold inside an opaque <canvas>. We introduce WorldCoder-Bench, a benchmark for autonomous, physically grounded 3D world synthesis. WorldCoder-Bench contains 2,026 expert-curated tasks across Simulation, Rendering, and Application scenarios, with optional .glb assets and hidden behavioral contracts. We further propose StateProbe, an execution-based protocol that probes generated programs in a sandboxed browser and verifies hidden, mutation-hardened contracts over runtime states and transitions. Beyond verification coverage, we report Return on Automation and Time Efficiency Multiplier to measure correctness-adjusted cost and time savings. Across nine frontier models, the best system reaches only 27.8% verification coverage on WorldCoder-Core and 19.9% on WorldCoder-Robust, with failures dominated by state-schema drift and broken interaction chains rather than missing scene elements. Utility metrics further show that cheap or fast models can still provide substantial value on easier domains. WorldCoder-Bench is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/WorldCoder-Bench/.

</details>


### [315] [EVA-Net: Subject-Independent EEG Motor Decoding with Video-Derived Motor Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01884)
*Ziyuan Li,Yueyu Sun,Yimeng Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose EVA-Net, a two-stage framework that uses action videos as semantic priors to improve subject-independent EEG motor decoding, outperforming text-based semantic anchoring and boosting cross-subject accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-invasive BCI systems need EEG decoders that generalize well across subjects with minimal per-user calibration, but inter-subject variability and EEG non-stationarity cause subject-specific noise to be entangled with motor semantics, limiting subject-independent decoding. Existing multimodal methods use text as a semantic anchor, which is sparse and static for inherently dynamic motor processes, motivating a richer, temporally aligned semantic modality.

Method: EVA-Net is a two-stage framework. Stage 1 aligns EEG and video features in a shared latent space using cross-modal contrastive learning and supervised contrastive objectives to suppress subject-specific variation and bind EEG to video-based motor semantics. Stage 2 removes video at inference time by constructing video category prototypes and using knowledge distillation so that an EEG-only classifier can inherit the video-derived priors without adding runtime overhead.

Result: On two public datasets, EVA-Net delivers strong subject-independent (cross-subject) EEG motor decoding performance, including an 8.66% leave-one-subject-out (LOSO) accuracy improvement on the EEGMMI dataset compared to baselines. Ablation studies show that the video-based semantic anchoring provides clear benefits and outperforms the text-based semantic anchor used as a baseline.

Conclusion: Using action videos as semantic priors is an effective strategy for improving subject-independent EEG motor decoding. By aligning EEG and video features and then distilling video-derived knowledge into an EEG-only classifier, EVA-Net reduces subject-specific noise and enhances cross-subject generalization without increasing inference cost. Video proves to be a more suitable semantic anchor than text for dynamic motor processes.

Abstract: Practical non-invasive Brain-Computer Interface (BCI) systems require EEG decoders with strong cross-subject generalization and minimal calibration. However, inter-subject variability and signal non-stationarity often entangle motor semantics with subject-specific noise, limiting subject-independent decoding. Recent multimodal approaches use text as a semantic anchor, yet text provides sparse and static supervision for inherently dynamic motor processes. To address this issue, we propose EVA-Net, a two-stage framework that uses action videos as semantic priors for subject-independent EEG motor decoding. In the first stage, EEG and video features are aligned in a shared space using cross-modal and supervised contrastive objectives to reduce subject-specific variation. In the second stage, video category prototypes and knowledge distillation transfer video-derived priors to an EEG-only classifier without adding inference overhead. Experiments on two public datasets show that EVA-Net achieves strong subject-independent decoding performance, including an 8.66% LOSO accuracy gain on EEGMMI. Ablation results further suggest that video provides a more effective semantic anchor than the text baseline considered in this work.

</details>


### [316] [Absorbing Complexity: An Interaction-Native Knowledge Harness for Financial LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01886)
*Ailiya Borjigin,Igor Stadnyk,Ben Bilski,Maksym Chikita,Dmytro Kyrylenko,Sofiia Pidturkina,Julia Stadnyk*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces InKH, an architecture that offloads financial task complexity from users to the AI agent by turning interaction history and events into structured, efficiently retrievable knowledge, significantly improving quality, latency, and auditability versus existing memory systems in a synthetic benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing financial AI agents perform poorly because they rely on users to continually restate goals, preferences, and contextual information. This leads to forgotten constraints, stale assumptions, repetitive work, latency, and unsafe or non-auditable decisions in workflows like market analysis and trade preparation. The authors are motivated to design an architecture where the system, not the user, bears the burden of managing complex, evolving context and knowledge, while remaining fast, cost-efficient, and governable.

Method: They propose the interaction-native knowledge harness (InKH), which: (1) converts user, market, portfolio, and tool events into structured operational knowledge; (2) uses passive knowledge injection to build a bounded working context buffer before each LLM step; (3) employs temporal graph memory for fast retrieval of relevant past information; (4) exposes a wiki-like audit surface for human-readable governance and traceability; and (5) runs background knowledge extraction with maturity, decay, and write-time invalidation to control staleness. They evaluate InKH on a controlled synthetic financial benchmark with 24 random seeds, 4 rounds, 80 episodes per round, and 6 baseline systems, yielding 46,080 baseline-conditioned evaluations, and compare performance, latency, token cost, and stale-knowledge usage against baselines such as agent-driven wiki-walk memory and a temporal-graph system without invalidation.

Result: InKH reaches a mean task quality of 0.815 at 900 ms latency in the benchmark. Against an agent-driven wiki-walk memory baseline, it reduces latency by 82.95%, token cost by 82.29%, and stale-knowledge usage by 96.58%, while increasing task quality by 0.108 and traceability by 0.461. Compared to a temporal-graph system without invalidation, InKH improves task quality by 0.050 and cuts stale-memory usage by 96.58% with roughly similar serving costs. These results indicate that InKH outperforms existing memory and context-management strategies across both effectiveness and efficiency metrics.

Conclusion: The study concludes that financial AI agents are more likely to be adopted when the architecture absorbs interaction complexity rather than offloading it onto users. InKH exemplifies this design principle by turning interaction events into structured, retrievable knowledge and managing staleness and context composition automatically, leading to better quality, lower latency and cost, and improved auditability in a synthetic benchmark.
