The authors emphasize that the benchmark validates architecture-level behavior and memory design, not real-world live trading performance, so further work is required before deployment in production trading environments.

Abstract: Financial AI agents often fail for a simple reason: they make users carry the complexity. A user must repeatedly restate goals, risk preferences, portfolio context, past judgments, and shifting market assumptions, while the agent answers, retrieves, acts, and forgets. In finance, this is not just inconvenient. In tasks such as market analysis, copy-trading review, and trade preparation, forgotten context and stale memory can create latency, repeated errors, weak auditability, and unsafe decisions.
  We propose the interaction-native knowledge harness (InKH), an architecture for financial LLM agents that absorbs complexity into the system. InKH converts user, market, portfolio, and tool events into structured operational knowledge. It uses passive knowledge injection to assemble a bounded working context buffer before the main model step, temporal graph memory for low-latency retrieval, a wiki audit surface for human-readable governance, and background extraction with maturity, decay, and write-time invalidation.
  We evaluate InKH on a reproducible controlled synthetic benchmark with 24 random seeds, 4 rounds, 80 episodes per round, and 6 baselines, producing 46,080 baseline-conditioned evaluations. InKH achieves mean task quality of 0.815 at 900 ms latency. Compared with agent-driven wiki-walk memory, it reduces latency by 82.95 percent, token cost by 82.29 percent, and stale-knowledge usage by 96.58 percent, while improving quality by 0.108 and traceability by 0.461. Compared with a temporal-graph system without invalidation, it improves quality by 0.050 and reduces stale-memory usage by 96.58 percent with comparable serving cost.
  The results support a design thesis for financial AI: adoption happens when complexity is absorbed by the system rather than transferred to the user. The benchmark validates architecture-level behavior, not live trading performance.

</details>


### [317] [Physically-Constrained Mamba-SDE for Remaining Useful Life Prediction under Irregular Observations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01894)
*Deyu Zhuang,Peiliang Gong,Yang Shao,Liyuan Shu,Qi Zhu,Xiaoli Li,Daoqiang Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes PC-MambaSDE, a physics-guided continuous-time latent SDE framework that improves Remaining Useful Life prediction under irregular sensor observations by combining mask-aware sequence encoding, monotonic degradation dynamics, and terminal failure constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Remaining Useful Life prediction is vital for predictive maintenance, but in practice sensor data are highly irregular (asynchronous sampling, missing bursts, jitter), and purely data-driven models can yield non-physical, non-monotonic degradation paths that conflict with the irreversible nature of damage accumulation. The authors aim to build a model that can robustly handle irregular observations while embedding physical degradation priors to produce realistic and reliable trajectories.

Method: The authors design PC-MambaSDE, which has (1) a Mask-Aware Continuous Mamba Encoder that takes both sensor readings and their observation masks to learn continuous-time control signals; (2) a Physics-Guided Latent SDE with a hybrid drift term that includes a global physical bias, enforcing monotonic degradation and robustness to large observation gaps; and (3) a Terminal Degradation Penalty that formulates RUL prediction as a boundary value problem by decoupling a Health Index dimension and penalizing trajectories that do not reach the failure state appropriately. They provide theoretical analysis, showing that the variational training objective is equivalent to minimizing KL divergence via Girsanov’s theorem and proving global asymptotic stability via Lyapunov methods. They also propose a Hybrid Irregularity Generation Scheme to synthetically create realistic irregular sampling patterns for evaluation.

Result: On public RUL benchmarks subjected to synthetic irregularity via the proposed generation scheme, PC-MambaSDE achieves significantly better prediction accuracy and robustness than existing state-of-the-art methods, particularly when observations are extremely sparse or irregular, and produces more physically plausible degradation trajectories.

Conclusion: Embedding physics-based constraints into continuous-time latent dynamics, combined with mask-aware sequence modeling, leads to more accurate and stable RUL prediction under irregular observations. PC-MambaSDE offers a unified framework that is theoretically grounded, robust to real-world data imperfections, and empirically superior to prior approaches, supporting the value of physics-guided machine learning for predictive maintenance.

Abstract: Accurate Remaining Useful Life prediction is critical for industrial predictive maintenance. However, real-world deployment is challenging due to the irregular nature of sensor observations, characterized by asynchronous sampling, burst missingness, and temporal jitter. Compounding this issue, purely data-driven models often generate physically implausible degradation trajectories that violate the irreversible nature of damage accumulation. To address this, we propose PC-MambaSDE, a unified continuous-time framework for robust RUL prediction under irregular observations. Specifically, we design a Mask-Aware Continuous Mamba Encoder that explicitly leverages observation masks to extract context-rich control signals. Furthermore, we introduce a Physics-Guided Latent SDE with parametrically rectified hybrid drift, superimposing a global physical bias to enforce monotonic degradation even amid severe observation gaps. Additionally, we formulate RUL prediction as a boundary value problem via a Terminal Degradation Penalty, which decouples a Health Index dimension and applies a penalty loss to guide trajectories toward the failure state. Theoretically, we prove that our variational objective is mathematically equivalent to minimizing the KL divergence via Girsanov's theorem, and we guarantee the global asymptotic stability of the learned dynamics through Lyapunov analysis. To enable rigorous evaluation, we develop a Hybrid Irregularity Generation Scheme that simulates realistic industrial imperfections. Extensive experiments on public benchmarks demonstrate that PC-MambaSDE significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, particularly under extreme observation scarcity, validating the efficacy of embedding physical priors into continuous-time latent dynamics.

</details>


### [318] [Community-Aware Assessment of Social Textual Engagement and Resonance: A Human-Centric Perspective on User-Generated Content Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01897)
*Tianjiao Li,Kai Zhao,Xiang Li,Yang Liu,Huyang Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new, human-centric way to evaluate user-generated videos by predicting their positive community resonance instead of just visual quality, introducing a new task (CASTER), model (MEDEA with Social-CoT), and benchmark (CASTER-Bench).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Video Quality Assessment (VQA) methods focus mainly on aesthetic or signal-level fidelity and ignore the social and engagement aspects that determine whether user-generated content is perceived as high quality by communities. There is a need to model how diverse viewers collectively react—cognitively and emotionally—to UGC, and to evaluate videos on their ability to generate positive community resonance rather than just technical quality.

Method: The authors define a new task, CASTER (Community-Aware Assessment of Social Textual Engagement and Resonance), which predicts whether a UGC item achieves positive community resonance based on multimodal features. They propose MEDEA, a multimodal evaluation architecture with a Social Chain-of-Thought (Social-CoT) mechanism. Social-CoT instantiates multiple viewer personas and simulates their perspectives to approximate a community mind, then aggregates these simulated reactions into a final quality judgment. Training is done in two stages: supervised fine-tuning to learn basic reasoning patterns, followed by process-supervised reinforcement learning using a Social Alignment Reward to align the reasoning paths with authentic human social cognition. The authors also build CASTER-Bench, a human-annotated multimodal benchmark of UGC across varied categories, to train and evaluate the model.

Result: On the newly introduced CASTER-Bench benchmark, MEDEA significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines for assessing UGC resonance. It not only yields better quantitative performance but also generates interpretable, empathetic Social-CoT reasoning traces whose structure and content align well with real community feedback patterns observed in the annotations.

Conclusion: Shifting from traditional signal-centric VQA to human-centric, socially grounded resonance assessment provides a more realistic measure of quality for UGC. By modeling a simulated community mind via Social-CoT and aligning reasoning with human social cognition through process-supervised RL, MEDEA achieves superior performance and interpretability on the new CASTER-Bench benchmark. The framework demonstrates that incorporating social perspective-taking into multimodal evaluation is both feasible and beneficial for assessing user-generated content.

Abstract: Traditional Video Quality Assessment (VQA) focuses narrowly on aesthetic fidelity, overlooking the complex social dynamics that define quality in User-Generated Content (UGC). In this work, we propose a paradigm shift from signal-centric metrics to human-centric resonance assessment. We introduce CASTER (Community-Aware Assessment of Social Textual Engagement and Resonance), a new task that evaluates whether a UGC item achieves positive community resonance based on its multimodal attributes rather than visual quality alone. To address this, we present MEDEA (Multimodal Engagement-Driven Evaluation Architecture), which introduces a novel Social Chain-of-Thought (Social-CoT) mechanism. Unlike traditional logical CoT, Social-CoT performs multimodal perspective-taking, instantiating diverse viewer personas to simulate collective cognitive and emotional reactions (i.e., the "community mind") before deriving a quality judgment. MEDEA is trained via a two-stage approach involving supervised fine-tuning and process-supervised reinforcement learning with Social Alignment Reward to ensure reasoning paths are grounded in authentic human social cognition. To support this task, we release CASTER-Bench, a comprehensive human-annotated benchmark covering diverse UGC categories. Experiments demonstrate that MEDEA significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on CASTER-Bench while providing interpretable and empathetic reasoning paths that align with real community feedback.

</details>


### [319] [Bayesian Spectral Emotion Transition Discovery from Multi-Annotator Disagreement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01906)
*Keito Inoshita,Takato Ueno*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose a Bayesian, spectral method to discover how emotions change turn‑by‑turn in dialogues, using full distributions of annotator labels instead of single majority labels, and show it aligns with known psychological emotion spaces and generalizes across corpora and languages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing conversational emotion research usually collapses multiple annotators’ judgments into a single hard emotion label per utterance. This removes information about uncertainty and disagreement, which is precisely what matters when studying how emotions transition from one turn to the next. The authors want a statistically principled way to model these transitions directly from soft multi-rater labels, quantify which transitions are truly over- or under-represented, and connect the discovered structure to established psychological models of emotion.

Method: They introduce BSETD, a two-stage framework. Stage 1: construct a hierarchical Dirichlet–Multinomial model over emotion transitions using the outer product of soft labels for consecutive utterances, producing a K×K transition matrix whose cells have posterior credible intervals and FDR-controlled significance via Benjamini–Hochberg. Stage 2: treat the significant transition matrix as a graph, compute the symmetrized graph Laplacian, and perform spectral decomposition to separate low-frequency (inertia-like, within-valence) and high-frequency (contagion-like, cross-valence) components of the transition structure. They then analyze these spectral components on multiple corpora, including LLM-generated soft labels, and compute correlations across datasets and languages.

Result: On the EmotionLines dataset, BSETD uncovers specific over-represented transitions, such as disgust→anger and anger→disgust (consistent with Plutchik’s wheel of emotions), and under-represented transitions like joy→anger and anger→joy (consistent with a valence-based space where opposite-valence transitions are rare). In cross-corpus tests over five sources, the discovered transition structures exhibit high pairwise Pearson correlations: 0.91–0.98 among English datasets, 0.79–0.85 when comparing English to Chinese (M3ED), and 0.979 when comparing transitions inferred from human hard labels versus LLM-derived virtual soft labels on the same utterances.

Conclusion: Modeling emotion dynamics with full soft-label information via BSETD yields statistically reliable transition structures that both match psychological theories of affect and transfer well across datasets and languages. Preserving annotator uncertainty, rather than collapsing to hard labels, provides a better bridge between computational emotion modeling and established affective science, and LLM-generated soft labels can approximate human-derived transition structure very closely.

Abstract: Emotions evolve through the dynamics of conversation, and understanding their transition structure is foundational to applications ranging from mental-health screening to dialogue systems. However, existing studies typically compress multi-rater judgments into a single hard label by majority voting, discarding the uncertainty signal needed to understand turn-to-turn transitions. In this article, we propose Bayesian Spectral Emotion Transition Discovery (BSETD), a two-stage framework that discovers emotion-transition structure from multi-rater soft labels. In the first stage, a hierarchical Dirichlet-Multinomial posterior is constructed through the outer product of soft labels, equipping each cell of the K x K transition matrix with a credible interval and Benjamini-Hochberg (BH) false discovery rate (FDR)-controlled significance. In the second stage, the symmetrized graph Laplacian is spectrally decomposed to separate a low-frequency (inertia) component from a high-frequency (contagion) component. On EmotionLines, BSETD simultaneously recovers the signatures of two distinct affective spaces: the Plutchik-adjacent transitions disgust to anger (log2 lift +0.94) and anger to disgust (+0.86) are over-represented, while the Russell-valence-reversed transitions joy to anger (-0.90) and anger to joy (-0.89) are under-represented. A five-source cross-corpus validation yields pairwise Pearson correlations in 0.91-0.98 within English, 0.79-0.85 against Chinese M3ED, and 0.979 between the human hard labels and the LLM virtual soft labels on the same utterance set, demonstrating that a pipeline preserving annotator uncertainty bridges the computational study of emotion dynamics with established psychological theory.

</details>


### [320] [SMH-Bench: Benchmarking LLM Agents for Environment-Grounded Reasoning and Action in Smart Homes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01912)
*Kuan Li,Shuo Zhang,Huacan Wang,Fangzhou Yu,Zecheng Sheng,Yi Gu,Weipeng Ming,Lei Xue,Chen Liu,Sen Hu,Ronghao Chen,Siyue Lin,Yuqing Hou,Xiaofeng Mou,Yi Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents SMH-Bench, a realistic benchmark to evaluate how well LLMs can reason and act in complex smart-home environments, revealing current models’ weaknesses in automation, ambiguity resolution, and personalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing smart-home benchmarks mainly test direct instruction-to-API mapping or limited simulations, which do not capture the complexity of real homes where LLMs must reason over user intent, preferences, and interactions between many devices. There is a need for a realistic, executable, and verifiable environment to assess whether LLMs can operate reliably as smart-home agents.

Method: The authors build SMH-Bench on top of HomeEnv, an executable smart-home simulator. They design 1,100 tasks covering 7 categories and 22 subcategories, and distribute them across three levels of home complexity (simple, medium, complex) that range from small apartments to dense multi-room homes with up to 135 devices. They then evaluate frontier LLMs on these tasks to measure performance across different smart-home capabilities.

Result: Frontier LLMs perform well on explicit control and straightforward query tasks in SMH-Bench but perform poorly on more demanding capabilities such as automation scheduling, handling ambiguous instructions, and performing personalized reasoning. Performance degrades as the simulated home environment becomes more complex and device-dense.

Conclusion: SMH-Bench exposes important shortcomings of current LLMs when deployed as smart-home agents, particularly in complex, realistic environments. The benchmark is proposed as a tool to drive research toward more reliable, context-aware, and practically deployable LLM-based smart-home systems.

Abstract: Smart homes are evolving toward complex state-dependent living environments, requiring Large Language Models (LLMs) to reason over user intent, preferences, and multi-device interactions. However, existing smart-home benchmarks often focus on static instruction-to-API mapping or limited simulations, failing to evaluate whether LLMs can reason, interact, and act reliably in realistic household scenarios. To address these limitations, we introduce SMH-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating LLMs in smart-home environments. Built upon HomeEnv, an executable and verifiable smart-home simulator, SMH-Bench contains 1,100 high-quality tasks spanning 7 categories and 22 fine-grained subcategories. It further stratifies tasks across simple, medium and complex homes, ranging from small apartments to dense multi-room environments with 135 devices. Experiments show that although frontier LLMs achieve strong performance on explicit control and query tasks, they still exhibit significant weaknesses in automation task scheduling, ambiguity handling and personalized reasoning, especially as home complexity increases. We hope SMH-Bench will facilitate the development of more reliable, context-aware, and practically deployable smart-home agents.

</details>


### [321] [VET: A Framework for Analyzing AI Discourse](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01929)
*Meredith Ringel Morris*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces the VET Framework to categorize and critique polarized AI discourse and use it as an AI literacy tool.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Public discourse on AI is highly polarized, with exaggerated hype and doom narratives in media that hinder the development of nuanced AI literacy among the general public. There is a need for a systematic way to classify and critically evaluate these narratives so people can better understand AI’s real capabilities and limitations.

Method: Proposes the VET Framework, which categorizes AI discourse along three dimensions: valence (positive–negative attitude toward AI), effectiveness (perceived capabilities and impact of AI), and trajectory (expected direction of AI’s development). The paper then applies this framework to four common stances in AI discourse: AI Hype, AI Doom, AI Denial, and AI Normalcy, comparing how each exaggerates or distorts aspects of AI’s current and future state.

Result: Using the VET framework, the paper demonstrates that common public stances (AI Hype, Doom, Denial, Normalcy) can be systematically mapped and compared, revealing the specific ways each position overstates or understates AI’s risks, benefits, and developmental paths. The framework highlights patterns of exaggeration and imbalance across narratives.

Conclusion: The VET Framework offers a practical tool for “vetting” polarized AI narratives, helping educators, communicators, and the public to critically assess claims about AI. By encouraging more balanced and structured analysis of AI discourse, it can support stronger AI literacy and reduce the influence of oversimplified or extreme positions.

Abstract: Public discourse on AI has become polarized; exaggerated positions on AI in traditional and social media threaten the development of AI Literacy among the general public. In this article, I introduce the VET Framework, a method for categorizing AI discourse along the dimensions of valence, effectiveness, and trajectory. I show how this framework can be used to identify, compare, and critique prevalent narratives of AI Hype, AI Doom, AI Denial, and AI Normalcy. Using VET, I analyze how each of these four stances exaggerates some aspects of the current state and/or likely evolution of AI, and illustrate how the VET framework can serve as an AI Literacy tool by supporting the ``vetting'' of polarized AI discourse.

</details>


### [322] [AutoMedBench: Towards Medical AutoResearch with Agentic AI Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01961)
*Junqi Liu,Salena Song,Yuhan Wang,Jiawei Mao,Hardy Chen,Xiaoke Huang,Tianhao Qi,Pengfei Guo,Yucheng Tang,Yufan He,Can Zhao,Andriy Myronenko,Dong Yang,Daguang Xu,Yuyin Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AutoMedBench is a workflow-aware benchmark for evaluating autonomous agents on end-to-end medical-AI research tasks, with fine-grained, stage-level analysis of agent behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing medical agent benchmarks mainly judge final outputs and offer little insight into how agents behave across the full research workflow, especially in complex, long-horizon medical-AI tasks. There is a need to understand and evaluate agents not just on end performance but on their intermediate decision-making and reliability at different workflow stages.

Method: The authors design AutoMedBench, a benchmark that structures autonomous medical-AI research into a unified five-stage workflow (Plan, Setup, Validate, Inference, Submit). They construct long-horizon tasks (average 33 agent turns) across five tracks—segmentation, image enhancement, VQA, report generation, and lesion detection. Each task has two difficulty tiers (Lite, Standard) differing in scaffolding. They score each run by both final task performance and per-stage (S1-S5) scores, and perform large-scale error and stage-level analyses over thousands of recorded runs.

Result: Stage-level scoring shows that agents perform best in the Setup stage and worst in the Validate stage, indicating relative strength in building executable pipelines and weakness in reliability checking. Error analysis reveals that verification and submission failures are the most common error types (37.7% and 38.1% of fired error codes), while task-understanding errors are rare (0.9%). Additionally, runs with at least one fired error code have, on average, a 48% lower overall score than runs with no errors.

Conclusion: AutoMedBench exposes critical weaknesses of current autonomous medical-AI agents in validation and submission phases, despite generally good task understanding and pipeline setup. Its workflow-aware, stage-level evaluation enables more granular diagnosis of agent behavior and can guide the development of more robust, end-to-end medical research agents that better verify and reliably submit their results.

Abstract: Autonomous agents are increasingly expected to support end-to-end medical-AI research workflows, moving beyond isolated prediction tasks or short-form clinical question answering. However, existing medical agent benchmarks primarily evaluate final outputs, providing limited visibility into agent behavior within the research process. To address this gap, we present AutoMedBench, a workflow-aware benchmark for autonomous medical-AI research across diverse medical imaging and multimodal inference tasks, organizing agent execution into a unified five-stage workflow (S1-S5): Plan, Setup, Validate, Inference, and Submit. It comprises long-horizon tasks with each run averaging 33 agent turns, spanning five research tracks: segmentation, image enhancement, visual question answering (VQA), report generation, and lesion detection. Each task is evaluated under two difficulty tiers, Lite and Standard, which use the same data and metrics but differ in the amount of task-brief scaffolding, and each run is scored using both final task performance and S1-S5 stage scores, enabling stage-level analysis from the initial task brief to the final submitted artifact. Across thousands of recorded runs, stage-level scoring reveals that Validate is the weakest workflow stage on average, whereas Setup is the strongest, suggesting that current agents are better at making pipelines executable than at verifying their reliability. Post-run error analysis further shows that verification and submission failures dominate tagged errors, accounting for 37.7% and 38.1% of fired codes respectively, whereas task-understanding errors are rare at 0.9%, and runs with one fired error code have a 48% lower overall score than runs with no error code on average.

</details>


### [323] [Algorithmic algorithm development with LLMs: A Case Study on LLM-Usage for Contraction Order Optimization in Tensor Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01975)
*Fabian Hoppe,Melven Röhrig-Zöllner,Philipp Knechtges*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how to use LLM-based, verifier-guided evolutionary coding agents (via OpenEvolve) to develop and improve algorithms, using tensor network contraction-order optimization as a case study, and emphasizes both their promise and the need for careful human-led evaluation and interpretation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Algorithm design for complex problems like contraction-order optimization in tensor networks is difficult and typically requires substantial human expertise. With the rise of large language models and autonomous coding agents, there is interest in whether these systems can automatically generate, refine, and improve algorithms. The authors are motivated to understand how to effectively use such LLM-based agents, what choices (model, metrics, test sets) matter, and what limitations remain that still require human oversight.

Method: They use OpenEvolve, a verifier-guided evolutionary coding framework powered by LLMs, to tackle the specific algorithmic problem of contraction-order optimization for tensor networks. They systematically vary design choices such as: (1) which LLM is used as the code generator, (2) which evaluation metric guides the evolutionary search, and (3) which test instances (problem inputs) are used for assessment. They then analyze the behavior, performance, and robustness of the resulting algorithms, focusing on how these design decisions impact outcomes and how human scientists must evaluate and interpret the generated solutions.

Result: The study finds that LLM-based, verifier-guided evolutionary agents can indeed discover or improve algorithms for contraction-order optimization, demonstrating strong potential for automated algorithm development. However, the results also show that the outcomes are sensitive to choices of LLM, evaluation metrics, and test instances, and that without thoughtful design and human scrutiny, the generated algorithms may be misleading, brittle, or hard to interpret.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that LLM-based evolutionary coding agents like OpenEvolve are promising tools for algorithm discovery and optimization but are not a turnkey replacement for human algorithm designers. Effective use of these systems requires careful selection of models, metrics, and benchmarks, along with sustained human involvement in evaluation, validation, and interpretation to ensure that the resulting algorithms are reliable, meaningful, and scientifically sound.

Abstract: We consider LLM-based algorithm development through a case study on contractionorder optimisation for tensor networks with OpenEvolve. We pay particular attention to the choice of the LLM as well as design choices such as evaluation metric and test instances. Our results highlight both the promise of verifier-guided evolutionary coding agents for algorithm development/improvement and the continuing importance of evaluation, validation, and interpretation -- and corresponding challenges -- by the human scientist.

</details>


### [324] [An NLP-Driven Framework for Curriculum-Labor Market Alignment: Schema-Constrained LLM Extraction, ESCO-Anchored Semantic Matching, and Multi-Dimensional Gap Quantification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01982)
*Sherzod Turaev,Mary John,Mamoun Awad,Nazar Zaki,Khaled Shuaib*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents a four-stage NLP framework using LLMs, SBERT, and verification metrics to extract and align educational competencies with labor-market demands, demonstrating high reliability and revealing specific curriculum-job market skill gaps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing information-extraction pipelines in education and labor-market analysis rely heavily on surface-level lexical methods. These methods struggle to detect implicit competencies, are not grounded in standardized taxonomies, and lack formal measures of extraction reliability and document completeness. This limits their usefulness for high-stakes applications such as curriculum–labor market alignment and quality assurance in higher education.

Method: The authors design a four-stage framework: (1) schema-constrained prompting of a two-model frontier LLM ensemble to extract competencies into a JSON Schema-enforced seven-slot structure; (2) aligning extracted competencies with the ESCO v1.2.1 controlled vocabulary across eleven domains using Sentence-BERT (SBERT) cosine similarity; (3) a two-tier adjudication protocol to resolve disagreements between the two LLMs; and (4) a verification mechanism combining per-slot Cohen’s kappa, schema-conformance checks, and document-level completeness audits. They instantiate this on the BSc Computer Science curriculum at UAE University and 30 job postings, tuning the SBERT similarity threshold to 0.50.

Result: The pipeline extracts 400 competency records from an 85-course curriculum and aligns them, under five different analytical scopes, with 483 requirement clauses from 30 job postings using an SBERT threshold of 0.50. The skill-slot extractor attains a Cohen’s kappa of 0.79, with 100% schema conformance and 100% document-level completeness, indicating high reliability and robustness of the extraction process.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively performs schema-constrained, taxonomically grounded, and reliability-audited information extraction and alignment between curriculum competencies and labor-market requirements. Applied to a CS program, it reveals interpretable supply–demand gaps, including significant shortfalls in general/transversal skills, algorithms and theory, and software engineering/project management, while showing near-alignment in AI and data science. This demonstrates the framework’s potential for data-driven curriculum quality assurance and program improvement.

Abstract: Schema-constrained information extraction from diverse educational and labor-market corpora remains an open challenge in natural language processing because existing pipelines rely primarily on lexical-surface methods that cannot recover implicit competencies, lack grounding in shared taxonomies, and provide no formal measures of extraction reliability or document-level completeness. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a four-stage NLP framework that combines (i) schema-constrained prompting of a two-model frontier-LLM ensemble against a JSON Schema-enforced seven-slot competency formalism, (ii) Sentence-BERT (SBERT) alignment of the extracted records against an eleven-domain ESCO v1.2.1 controlled vocabulary, (iii) a two-tier adjudication protocol that resolves inter-model disagreements, and (iv) a verification mechanism that combines per-slot Cohen's kappa, schema conformance, and document-level completeness audits. The framework is instantiated for a critical application in higher-education quality assurance, namely curriculum-labor market alignment for the ABET-accredited BSc Computer Science program at the United Arab Emirates University. The pipeline extracts 400 competency records from the 85-course 2025-2026 study plan and aligns them, under a five-scope analysis ranging from the computing core to a probability-weighted student trajectory, with 30 job postings (483 requirement clauses) at an SBERT cosine threshold of 0.50. The extractor achieves Cohen's kappa of 0.79 on the skill slot, with 100% schema conformance and 100% document-level completeness. The alignment surfaces interpretable supply-demand gaps of 25.0% in general and transversal skills, 13.8% in algorithms and computational theory, and 12.2% in software engineering and project management, with a near-zero 1.8% gap in artificial intelligence and data science despite 38.6% supply coverage.

</details>


### [325] [SafeMCP: Proactive Power Regulation for LLM Agent Defense via Environment-Grounded Look-Ahead Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01991)
*Lichao Wang,Zhaoxing Ren,Tianzhuo Yang,Jiaming Ji,Chi Harold Liu,Yaodong Yang,Juntao Dai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes SafeMCP, a server-side safety plugin for LLM agents using MCP that limits unsafe tool acquisition through predictive, look-ahead reasoning about future risks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLM agents use MCP to access many tools and act in complex environments, their enlarged action spaces can lead to unsafe capabilities, power-seeking behavior, and catastrophic failures from small errors or hallucinations. There is a need for a mechanism that preserves usefulness while constraining dangerous power expansion.

Method: Introduce SafeMCP, a server-side defense that uses an internal world model for look-ahead reasoning and implements a two-tier defense: (1) proactive filtering of potentially hazardous tools before the agent can use them, and (2) immediate intervention as a fail-safe during execution. Train SafeMCP with a three-stage pipeline: environmental dynamic grounding, safe policy initialization, and reinforcement learning with dual verifiable rewards focused on both safety and utility.

Result: On benchmarks PowerSeeking Bench, ToolEmu, and AgentHarm, SafeMCP attains a safe equilibrium, substantially reducing power-seeking and harmful behaviors while maintaining task performance and agent utility.

Conclusion: Server-side, look-ahead safety control over tool acquisition in MCP-based LLM agents can effectively mitigate power-seeking risks and catastrophic failures without significantly sacrificing utility, as demonstrated by SafeMCP’s performance on multiple safety benchmarks.

Abstract: As Large Language Model (LLM) agents increasingly leverage the Model Context Protocol (MCP) to operate in complex environments, the expansion of their action spaces offers agents unsafe capabilities and underscores the risk of power-seeking. While broad action space and greater environment influence are essential for task fulfillment, they create a fragile risk surface where minor errors or hallucinations are magnified into catastrophic failures. In response, we propose SafeMCP, a {server-side} defense plugin that constrains tool acquisition via predictive reasoning regarding future safety risks. SafeMCP utilizes an internal world model for look-ahead reasoning to implement a two-tier defense: proactive tool filtering to constrain hazardous power expansion and immediate intervention as a fail-safe. To train SafeMCP, we introduce a three-stage pipeline comprising environmental dynamic grounding, safe policy initialization, and reinforcement learning (RL) with dual verifiable rewards. Experiments on PowerSeeking Bench, ToolEmu, and AgentHarm show that SafeMCP achieves a safe equilibrium, effectively mitigating risks while preserving agent utility.

</details>


### [326] [Extreme Low-Bit Inference in Reasoning Models: Failure Modes and Targeted Recovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02011)
*Ekaterina Alimaskina,Darya Rudas,Denis Shveykin,Gleb Molodtsov,Pavel Vasiliev,Aleksandr Beznosikov*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies why 2‑bit quantization of large reasoning models often fails to speed up end-to-end inference and proposes simple control mechanisms that make extreme low-bit reasoning both accurate and fast.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large Reasoning Models use long chain-of-thought traces, so inference is costly. Low-bit quantization should reduce per-token cost, but in practice 2-bit quantization often breaks the generation process: it creates unstable, excessively long, looping traces that erase any speed gains and hurt accuracy. The authors want to understand these failure modes at the process level and find ways to fix them so that aggressive quantization remains practically useful.

Method: 1) Empirically analyze full reasoning traces of Qwen3 reasoning models under 2-bit quantization across math and commonsense benchmarks, characterizing behaviors such as loops, repetitive segments, delayed commitment, and unclosed reasoning. 2) Measure how these process pathologies relate to accuracy degradation and inflated token counts. 3) Propose two lightweight controls: (a) FP16 planning, where a short, high-precision outline of the reasoning is generated in FP16 and then followed in 2-bit mode; and (b) loop rescue, which monitors the trace for repetitive patterns and, upon detection, either commits to an earlier plausible answer or falls back to FP16 decoding. 4) Evaluate these controls on benchmarks like MATH-500 using Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-32B, comparing accuracy and end-to-end speed under different inference regimes.

Result: Under naive 2-bit quantization, models' outputs exhibit severe generation pathologies: excessively long traces, loops, failure to close reasoning segments, and delayed final answers, which cause both accuracy drops and increased total token usage that negate per-token speed benefits. Introducing loop rescue alone substantially boosts accuracy (e.g., Qwen3-8B on MATH-500 from 17.2% to 74.2%) by detecting loops and intervening. Combining FP16 planning with loop rescue further improves performance (e.g., Qwen3-32B from 65.0% to 87.2%), while still preserving most of the efficiency gains from 2-bit inference.

Conclusion: The main issue with extreme low-bit reasoning is not just lower precision in token probabilities but process-level failures in generation. By treating these as controllable pathologies and adding simple runtime controls—FP16 planning and loop rescue—2-bit quantized reasoning models can recover much of their original accuracy while still realizing real end-to-end speedups. Therefore, aggressive quantization becomes practical for reasoning models when coupled with lightweight detection and selective high-precision support.

Abstract: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) rely on long reasoning traces, making inference expensive. While low-bit quantization reduces per-token decoding cost, we show that aggressive 2-bit inference can fail to deliver end-to-end speedup because instability in the generation process inflates total token count. Instead of merely lowering answer accuracy, 2-bit quantization often produces much longer traces with repetitive loops, budget exhaustion, delayed commitment, and unclosed reasoning segments. We analyze full reasoning traces of Qwen3 reasoning models across mathematical and commonsense benchmarks and show that accuracy degradation is tightly linked to these process-level failures. To address them, we introduce two lightweight controls: FP16 planning, which gives the 2-bit model a short high-precision outline, and loop rescue, which detects repetitive traces and either commits to an earlier answer or falls back to FP16. On MATH-500, loop rescue improves Qwen3-8B accuracy from 17.2% to 74.2%, while planning plus loop rescue improves Qwen3-32B from 65.0% to 87.2%. Overall, our results show that extreme low-bit reasoning becomes practical when its failures are treated as controllable generation pathologies: with lightweight detection and selective FP16 support, 2-bit inference can recover accuracy while preserving real end-to-end speed. Our code is available at: https://github.com/brain-lab-research/quantized-reasoning.

</details>


### [327] [RL-ACRGNet: Reinforcement Learning-Based Chest Radiology Report Generation Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02035)
*Yogesh Kumar Meena,Saurabh Agarwal,K. V. Arya*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces RL-ACRGNet, a reinforcement-learning-enhanced encoder–decoder model for automatic radiology report generation, achieving better accuracy and clinical relevance than previous methods on IU-Xray and MIMIC-CXR datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual radiology report writing is slow and subject to inconsistencies, while existing deep-learning-based report generators struggle with fine-grained disease features and clinically coherent descriptions. The paper aims to improve both disease detection from images and the quality/faithfulness of generated reports.

Method: The authors design RL-ACRGNet, which combines a pre-trained DenseNet as the image encoder with a multilevel LSTM as the text decoder, trained within an off-policy reinforcement learning framework. A dual-network mechanism refines visual–semantic embeddings, and a metric-based reward encourages better alignment between images and generated text. The approach is evaluated on IU-Xray and MIMIC-CXR datasets using standard captioning/report metrics (BLEU-4, METEOR, ROUGE-L).

Result: On the IU-Xray dataset, RL-ACRGNet consistently surpasses state-of-the-art baselines, with reported gains of 0.47% in BLEU-4, 0.17% in METEOR, and 0.518 in ROUGE-L. Additional experiments on the larger MIMIC-CXR dataset show that the method generalises well and produces clinically meaningful reports, though exact numbers are not provided in the abstract.

Conclusion: Integrating a DenseNet encoder and multilevel LSTM decoder within an off-policy RL framework, plus a dual-network metric-based reward on visual–semantic embeddings, yields more accurate and clinically coherent radiology reports than prior methods, and the approach scales robustly from IU-Xray to the larger MIMIC-CXR dataset.

Abstract: Medical imaging interpretation is a foundational pillar of modern clinical diagnostics, yet the manual generation of radiology reports remains a time-consuming process prone to interpretation inconsistencies. Within the field of medical AI, automating these descriptions through deep learning promises to streamline clinical workflows and standardise diagnostic output. However, accurate disease detection and precise report generation remain significant challenges due to limitations in capturing fine-grained visual features and ensuring clinical coherence. To address these issues, we propose RL-ACRGNet, an improved encoder-decoder model that integrates a pre-trained DenseNet encoder with a multilevel LSTM decoder within an off-policy reinforcement learning framework. Using a dual-network approach to refine visual-semantic embeddings through a metric-based reward mechanism, we demonstrate that RL-ACRGNet consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on the IU-Xray dataset, achieving quantitative improvements in BLEU-4 (0.47%), METEOR (0.17%) and ROUGE-L (0.518). Furthermore, comprehensive evaluations on the large-scale MIMIC-CXR data set confirm the robust generalisation of the model and its ability to generate high-quality, clinically relevant reports

</details>


### [328] [Topological texture analysis of microscopy images of dynamic casein gelation and its relation to rheological properties](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02048)
*Zahra Tabatabaei,Diana Soto Aguilar,Jose C. Bonilla,Mathias P. Clausen,Jon Sporring*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A computational toolbox combining topological and texture/multifractal analyses is proposed to quantify microstructural dynamics during sodium caseinate gelation from super-resolution microscopy images, revealing subtle structural transitions aligned with rheological sol-gel behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional bulk rheology only provides averaged mechanical responses and cannot resolve subtle, spatially heterogeneous microstructural changes during gelation or network formation in soft materials and foods. There is a need for robust, quantitative image-based tools that can capture evolving microstructure, validate them on known patterns, and link them to physical transitions like sol-gel percolation.

Method: The authors develop an integrated image-analysis toolbox that combines: (1) Topological Data Analysis (TDA) to extract persistent homology features, focusing on max-Betti-1 curves that track the evolution of loops and ring-like structures in the protein network; (2) Differential Box Counting (DBC) to estimate fractal-like properties and complexity; (3) Multifractal Partition (MFP) to quantify spatial heterogeneity across scales; and (4) Local Binary Patterns (LBP) to capture local textural features. The pipeline is first validated on simulated fractal images, then applied to time-lapse STED microscopy of sodium caseinate gelation at different temperatures (30 °C, 40 °C) and GDL concentrations (1.8%, 3.5% w/v). Temporal trajectories of the descriptors are analyzed and compared with rheological sol-gel transition data.

Result: TDA via max-Betti-1 curves identifies distinct phases in gelation: an initial lag phase with dispersed aggregates and many loops, a sharp decay in loops that coincides with network percolation and the rheometrically measured sol-gel transition, and a later post-gelation increase in loops reflecting network rearrangements. DBC and MFP confirm corresponding changes in structural complexity and spatial heterogeneity, demonstrating concordant transitions across methods. The toolbox shows high sensitivity to microstructural changes that are not directly resolvable from bulk rheological curves alone. Validation on simulated fractal images supports the correctness and robustness of the descriptors.

Conclusion: The integrated TDA–DBC–MFP–LBP toolbox provides a robust, quantitative framework for characterizing evolving microstructures from microscopy images, linking them to macroscopic transitions such as sol-gel behavior. By capturing topology, multifractality, and texture, it reveals subtle microstructural dynamics that bulk rheology averages out. This makes it a promising general tool for studying complex microstructured systems in food and materials science, and the open-source code facilitates adoption and further development.

Abstract: We propose a novel computational toolbox that integrates Topological Data Analysis (TDA), Differential Box Counting (DBC), Multifractal Partition (MFP), and Local Binary Patterns (LBP), applied to time-lapse super-resolution STED microscopy images of sodium caseinate gelation induced by glucono-delta-lactone (GDL) at 30 °C and 40 °C and two GDL concentrations (1.8% and 3.5% w/v).
  TDA tracked topological loops, closed ring-like structures reflecting protein network interconnectivity, via max-Betti-1 curves, which revealed a lag phase of dispersed aggregates, a sharp decay coinciding with network percolation and the rheologically observed sol-gel transition, and a post-gelation increase corresponding to network rearrangements. These topological transitions were corroborated by DBC and MFP as these methods were able to resolve changes in structural complexity and spatial heterogeneity. The toolbox was validated on simulated fractal images prior to experimental application. Together, these descriptors provided sensitivity to subtle microstructural transitions that bulk rheology captured as averaged bulk mechanical responses. This integrated approach provides a robust quantitative tool for characterizing complex microstructure in food and material science with evolving microstructural dynamics. Code is available at https://github.com/Zahratabatabaei/Delifood_CV_paper.git

</details>


### [329] [Explainable Data-driven Deep Reinforcement Learning Methods for Optimal Energy Management in Buildings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02049)
*Hallah Shahid Butt,Qiong Huang,Gökhan Demirel,Kevin Förderer,Erfan Tajalli-Ardekani,Simnon Waczowicz,Luigi Spatafora,Veit Hagenmeyer,Benjamin Schäfer*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes an explainable deep reinforcement learning framework to optimize energy management in residential buildings with PV and storage, showing that on-policy algorithms (A2C, PPO) both reduce electricity costs and yield interpretable control policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Growing penetration of distributed renewables, PV, batteries, and flexible loads (like heat pumps) makes building energy systems complex to operate due to volatility, dynamic prices, and many interacting assets. DRL can in principle handle this complexity, but its black-box nature makes it hard to trust and deploy in real buildings. The authors are motivated to create a framework that keeps the optimization benefits of DRL while providing transparency and explanations of decisions for operators and stakeholders.

Method: The authors design a DRL-based building energy management framework with an expanded state space including real-time measurements (demand, PV output, battery power and SoC), external signals (dynamic electricity prices, weather), calendrical/holiday features, and forecasts (demand and price). They train and compare on-policy (A2C, PPO) and off-policy DRL agents on both synthetic and real-world data from KIT’s Living Lab Energy Campus. They then apply post-hoc explainability tools (XRL) to analyze and visualize the learned policies and feature importance, making the agent’s behavior interpretable.

Result: Experiments show that on-policy methods, especially A2C and PPO, achieve higher cumulative rewards and more stable policies than off-policy agents when managing residential building energy. The DRL agents effectively learn optimal battery management strategies that lower electricity costs under dynamic tariffs, while the XRL analysis reveals how different inputs (e.g., prices, SoC, forecasts) drive decisions. The framework works on both synthetic and real-world data from the LLEC site.

Conclusion: The proposed XRL framework enables DRL-based energy management that is both effective and interpretable. By combining on-policy algorithms (A2C, PPO) with post-hoc explanation methods, the system reduces electricity costs via improved battery control and provides transparent, actionable insights into the control logic. This increases the trustworthiness and practical deployability of DRL for building energy management in complex, renewable-rich environments.

Abstract: The increasing integration of renewable energy sources into power systems, particularly in buildings equipped with photovoltaic (PV) panels and energy storage systems, introduces significant complexity in energy systems. Volatile power generation, varying electricity tariffs, and increased entities, e.g., PV systems, and heat pumps, have increased the complexity and made the system harder to operate. This leads to the demand for additional control and optimization routes including data-based controls, such as reinforcement learning. While deep reinforcement learning (DRL) has emerged as a promising solution to optimize building operations in dynamic and ever more complex environments, its black-box nature impedes user trust and practical adoption. This paper presents a framework for explainable deep reinforcement learning (XRL) applied to energy management in residential buildings. We demonstrate its usage on both synthetic data but also on real-world data from the Living Lab Energy Campus (LLEC) at KIT. We train and compare both on-policy and off-policy DRL agents on an expanded state space that incorporates real-time measurements (demand, PV generation, battery power, state of charge), external signals (dynamic electricity price, local weather data), calendrical and holiday indicators, and forecasts for demand and price. Our experimental results indicate that on-policy algorithms, particularly Advantage Actor Critic (A2C) and Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), outperform off-policy methods in terms of cumulative rewards and policy stability. To explain these models, we employ post-hoc interpretation techniques to elaborate the learned control policies. Our findings demonstrate that the XRL framework not only reduces electricity costs through optimal battery management, but also provides transparent, actionable insights into the agent's decision-making process.

</details>


### [330] [eMoT: evolving Memory-of-Thought via Symbolic Anchoring and Memory Corrosion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02054)
*Xiang Li,Jiwei Wei,Ke Liu,Yitong Qin,Jinyu Guo,Malu Zhang,Peng Wang,Yang Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes eMoT, a framework that treats reasoning traces as evolving memory to make LLM multi-step reasoning more accurate and consistent, using symbolic computation and consistency checks to outperform standard CoT and structured baselines, even with a smaller base model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs do well on multi-step reasoning but suffer from hallucinations and weak numerical skills because they handle reasoning as a single-shot generation without reusing and refining good reasoning procedures. The authors want a way to stabilize and control reasoning so that logic can be retained, improved, and grounded in reliable computation, rather than just scaling model size.

Method: They introduce eMoT (evolving Memory-of-Thought), consisting of three modules: (1) a memory corrosion mechanism that maintains a dynamic memory of reasoning trajectories, reinforcing frequently successful structures while letting less useful ones decay; (2) a symbolic anchoring engine that delegates exact computations to Python, similar to humans using calculators, to avoid arithmetic and logical errors; and (3) a consistency-driven refinement process that reconciles and adjusts neural reasoning steps based on the symbolic results, reducing logical inconsistencies over multiple steps.

Result: On multiple reasoning benchmarks, eMoT improves both answer accuracy and solution consistency relative to Chain-of-Thought and other structured reasoning methods. In the Game of 24, it reaches 100% accuracy, up to 17.6% better than baselines. On math benchmarks GSM8K, ASDiv, SVAMP, and MGSM, it yields consistent gains in multi-step mathematical reasoning, even though it uses a relatively small backbone model with limited base capabilities.

Conclusion: Reasoning performance gains can be achieved by better reasoning control and memory-of-thought mechanisms rather than simply scaling model size. By treating reasoning as evolving memory, grounding computations symbolically, and enforcing consistency between neural and symbolic reasoning, eMoT provides a more stable and reliable framework for multi-step reasoning in LLMs.

Abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve impressive performance on multi-step reasoning tasks, their reliability is persistently hindered by critical limitations such as unconstrained hallucinations and poor numerical computation. Fundamentally, these issues arise because standard models treat reasoning as a transient, one-off generation process rather than retaining and refining successful procedural logic. To address these challenges, we propose eMoT (evolving Memory-of-Thought), a unified framework that stabilizes multi-step reasoning by treating reasoning trajectories as dynamic, evolving memories rather than static templates. The framework primarily consists of three interconnected modules: (i) a memory corrosion mechanism that reinforces high-utility reasoning structures while gradually decaying less frequent ones; (ii) a symbolic anchoring engine that utilizes Python for deterministic computation, much like a human uses a calculator; and (iii) a consistency-driven refinement process that aligns neural inference with symbolic outcomes, reducing the accumulation of logical discrepancies. Across multiple reasoning benchmarks, eMoT improves accuracy and solution consistency over standard Chain-of-Thought and structured reasoning baselines.On the traditional task Game of 24, eMoT achieves 100% accuracy, surpassing the baseline by up to 17.6%. Evaluations on mathematical task GSM8K, ASDiv, SVAMP, and MGSM further show consistent gains in multi-step mathematical reasoning. In our evaluation, we achieve superior performance despite utilizing a lightweight backbone model with constrained baseline capabilities. Compared to alternative methods that rely on massively scaled models, our results demonstrate that the performance gains are fundamentally driven by the eMoT framework's reasoning control rather than sheer model size.

</details>


### [331] [Where Do Deep-Research Agents Go Wrong? Span-Level Error Localization in Agent Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02060)
*Jiaming Wang,Ziteng Feng,Jiangtao Wu,Ruihao Li,Qianqian Xie,Yuxiang Ren,He Zhu,Xueming Han,Fanyu Meng,Junlan Feng,Jiaheng Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces a benchmark (TELBench) and a claim-centric auditing framework (DRIFT) to localize span-level errors in deep-research AI agents’ trajectories, improving fine-grained reliability assessment beyond final-answer accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation of deep-research agents focuses on whether the final answer is correct, which ignores where and how errors arise within long trajectories of tool calls, searches, and intermediate reasoning. This makes it hard to trust or debug such agents, because one cannot see which specific parts of the process introduce harmful errors that impact the final answer. The authors aim to provide a process-level view of reliability and a way to automatically pinpoint problematic spans in an agent’s reasoning and evidence-use trajectory.

Method: The authors collect 2,790 real trajectories from multiple agent frameworks, backbone models, and benchmarks, then convert low-level logs into higher-level semantic spans (e.g., exploration, search, hypothesis, noise). Using LLM-assisted expert review, they annotate spans that contain harmful errors, producing TELBench, a 1,000-instance dataset for span-level error localization. Building on this, they propose DRIFT, a claim-centric auditing framework that extracts claims made by the agent, checks whether these claims are supported by the evidence present in the trajectory, and flags spans where unsupported or conflicting claims influence the answer path. DRIFT is then evaluated against various baselines and model families for its ability to detect the first and overall error spans.

Result: TELBench offers a curated benchmark that distinguishes harmful error spans from benign exploration, failed searches, and tentative hypotheses. Empirically, when applied across different model families and auditing frameworks, DRIFT achieves up to a 30 percentage point improvement in span-level error localization and first-error detection accuracy compared to baselines, demonstrating its superior ability to pinpoint where trajectories become unreliable.

Conclusion: The work shows that it is feasible and valuable to move from outcome-only evaluation of deep-research agents to process-level assessment. By providing TELBench and the DRIFT auditing framework, the authors enable more precise localization of harmful errors within complex trajectories, improving our ability to understand, trust, and debug deep-research agents. Their results suggest that claim-centric, evidence-grounded auditing is an effective approach for enhancing reliability and transparency in such systems.

Abstract: Deep-research agents solve tasks through long trajectories of search, tool use, evidence inspection, and answer synthesis. Evaluation based on final answers shows whether an agent succeeds, but not which parts of the trajectory make the answer unreliable. We study span-level error localization for deep-research agents. We collect 2,790 real trajectories from two agent frameworks, three backbone models, and three benchmarks, convert raw logs into semantic spans, and annotate harmful error spans through LLM-assisted expert review. From these annotations, we build TELBench, a 1,000-instance benchmark for identifying error spans among normal exploration, failed searches, tentative hypotheses, and harmless noise. We further propose DRIFT, a claim-centric auditing framework that tracks agent claims, checks their support in trajectory evidence, and marks spans where unsupported or conflicting claims affect the answer path. Experiments across model families and auditing frameworks show that DRIFT improves span-level error localization and first-error accuracy by up to 30 percentage points. Our work provides a process-level view of reliability in deep-research agents.

</details>


### [332] [BADGER: Bridging Agentic and Deterministic Evaluation for Generative Enterprise Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02109)
*Shannon Serrao,Soumitra Chatterjee,Dorina Strori,Abhishek Sharma,Nathan Miller*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BADGER is a unified, production-grade evaluation framework for enterprise text-to-SQL and agentic AI systems, combining improved SQL execution accuracy metrics with an integrated agent evaluation pipeline that aligns closely with human expert judgments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluation methods for natural-language-to-SQL and multi-step agentic AI are fragmented: benchmarks like Spider/BIRD focus on execution accuracy, newer efforts like Spider 2.0 and BIRD-Interact touch enterprise and agentic aspects, and LLM-based judges like G-Eval and RAGAS handle different pieces. However, no single, production-ready framework jointly evaluates text-to-SQL quality and agentic behavior in a way that scales to enterprise SQL (CTEs, dialects) and is calibrated to human experts within governed data environments.

Method: The authors design BADGER, a unified evaluation framework. (1) They extend Spider-style SQL component extraction using LLM assistance so it can robustly parse complex, CTE-heavy and dialect-specific SQL. (2) They introduce Hybrid-EX, a hybrid execution-accuracy metric that first uses an LLM to resolve structural differences—such as column aliasing and numeric tolerance issues—then applies deterministic, cell-level scoring on aligned outputs. (3) They assemble an enterprise agentic evaluation suite that integrates existing LLM-based metrics (RAGAS, G-Eval) and agent benchmarks into a single pipeline, adding a new metric called Excess Tool Usage to capture inefficient agent behavior. They validate Hybrid-EX on 150 human-annotated industry queries against six competing frameworks.

Result: Hybrid-EX shows strong alignment with human expert judgments: Cohen’s kappa of 0.717 with a 95% confidence interval of 0.600–0.822 (classified as “Substantial agreement”) and a balanced accuracy of 87.3%. It outperforms six other frameworks by a kappa margin of 0.322–0.502, with all improvements statistically significant at p≤0.001. BADGER is implemented to run fully within a client’s governed data environment and supports configurable LLM judge backends and custom metrics.

Conclusion: BADGER provides a unified, enterprise-ready evaluation backbone for text-to-SQL and agentic AI systems. By combining LLM-assisted SQL analysis, a robust hybrid execution metric, and an integrated suite of agentic evaluation metrics, it offers closer alignment with human expert assessments than existing approaches. Its design for governed environments and configurable judges enables continuous, customizable evaluation rather than one-off benchmarking, making it suitable as an ongoing quality and monitoring layer for production AI systems.

Abstract: Enterprise AI systems that translate natural language into SQL queries and orchestrate multi-step agentic reasoning pipelines require evaluation approaches fundamentally different from academic benchmarks. Spider and BIRD established execution-accuracy protocols; G-Eval and RAGAS advanced LLM-based assessment; and recent work such as Spider 2.0, BEAVER, and BIRD-Interact has begun to address enterprise and agentic dimensions. No single framework unifies text-to-SQL assessment with agentic behavior evaluation into a production-grade pipeline calibrated against human expert judgment.
  We present BADGER, developed at Merkle, a unified evaluation framework integrating text-to-SQL assessment with agentic behavior evaluation. BADGER offers three contributions. First, LLM-assisted SQL component extraction extending Spider methodology to handle CTE-heavy, dialect-specific SQL. Second, a hybrid execution accuracy metric (Hybrid-EX) resolving column-aliasing and numeric-tolerance brittleness by using an LLM to infer structural alignments before deterministic cell-level scoring. Validated on 150 human-annotated industry queries, Hybrid-EX achieves Cohen's kappa=0.717 [95% CI: 0.600-0.822] (Substantial agreement) and 87.3% balanced accuracy, outperforming all six competing frameworks (Delta-kappa: 0.322-0.502, all p<=0.001). Third, an enterprise agentic evaluation suite assembling RAGAS, G-Eval, and agent benchmark metrics into a unified pipeline; Excess Tool Usage is the sole novel element.
  BADGER runs entirely within the client's governed data environment, supports configurable LLM judge backends, and enables rapid prototyping of client-specific judges and metrics, serving as a continuous evaluation backbone rather than a one-time quality gate.

</details>


### [333] [Learning When Not to Act: Mitigating Tool Abuse in Agentic Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02132)
*Liuji Chen,Dianxing Tang,Xing Shi,Dingshuo Chen,Qiang Liu,Shu Wu,Liang Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces EAPO, a reinforcement learning framework that teaches language agents to use external tools selectively, improving both performance and efficiency compared to existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agentic LMs often overuse external tools (e.g., search, calculators, code runners), even when problems can be solved via internal reasoning. Existing fixes like uniform penalties or hard caps reduce tool usage but also hinder beneficial tool-based exploration and overall performance. There is a need for a training framework that encourages judicious tool use—preserving tool benefits while preventing unnecessary calls.

Method: The authors propose Efficient Agentic Policy Optimization (EAPO). During RL training, (1) they inject tool-free trajectories into each rollout group so the policy sees examples of solving tasks without tools; (2) they apply difficulty-aware reward shaping, penalizing redundant tool calls more heavily on easy queries while being lenient on hard ones; and (3) they use confidence-aware token reweighting to emphasize higher-confidence parts of trajectories during policy optimization. EAPO is implemented on several base models (Qwen2.5-3B/7B and Llama3.1-8B) and evaluated on nine math and knowledge-intensive reasoning benchmarks.

Result: On nine benchmarks, EAPO improves the accuracy–efficiency trade-off versus GRPO-style baselines. For Qwen2.5-3B, Qwen2.5-7B, and Llama3.1-8B, EAPO boosts average task performance by 10.45%, 7.27%, and 9.69%, respectively, while simultaneously cutting average tool calls by 18.33%, 18.33%, and 24.59%. This demonstrates reduced tool abuse without sacrificing, and in fact improving, end-task accuracy.

Conclusion: EAPO enables agentic language models to learn selective tool use: they call tools when beneficial and avoid them when unnecessary. By combining tool-free trajectories, difficulty-aware penalties, and confidence-aware optimization, the approach reduces redundant tool usage while enhancing reasoning performance. This suggests that properly shaped RL can teach agents nuanced tool-use policies instead of relying on blunt global penalties or rigid tool limits.

Abstract: Agentic reinforcement learning can induce tool abuse, where models overuse external tools even for queries solvable by internal reasoning. Existing approaches mitigate this issue with uniform tool-use penalties or hard limits, which reduce tool frequency but may also suppress useful tool-assisted exploration. We propose EAPO, an Efficient Agentic Policy Optimization framework that learns selective tool use. EAPO introduces tool-free trajectories into each rollout group, applies difficulty-aware reward shaping to penalize redundant tool calls mainly on easier queries, and uses confidence-aware token reweighting to improve policy learning. Across nine mathematical and knowledge-intensive reasoning benchmarks, EAPO consistently improves the accuracy efficiency trade-off on Qwen2.5-3B, Qwen2.5-7B, and Llama3.1-8B. Compared with GRPO, EAPO improves average performance by 10.45%, 7.27%, and 9.69%, while reducing average tool calls by 18.33%, 18.33%, and 24.59%, respectively. These results show that agents can learn when not to use tools without compromising tool-integrated reasoning.

</details>


### [334] [S3TS: Stochastic Scenario-Structured Tree Search for Advanced Planning Under Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02151)
*Fabio Pavirani,Bert Claessens,Pierre Pinson,Chris Develder*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces a new planning algorithm (S3TS) that combines scenario-based uncertainty modeling with tree search to schedule energy resources more effectively under both non-linearity and uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Energy scheduling must balance reliability and cost while handling complex, often non-linear system models and significant uncertainty from renewables and demand. Existing tools typically handle either non-linearity (e.g., MCTS) or uncertainty (e.g., stochastic optimization), but not both together, limiting performance in realistic grid settings. The authors want a planning method that can explicitly model uncertainty and still leverage rich, non-linear models.

Method: They propose Stochastic Scenario-Structured Tree Search (S3TS), which embeds stochastic scenario trees (to represent uncertainty in future events) inside a tree-search-based planning framework that can work with advanced non-linear models. The algorithm explores decision trees structured according to scenarios of uncertain outcomes, enabling look-ahead planning that accounts for both system non-linearities and probabilistic futures. They test it on a simulated demand response signal publication problem modeled after the Belgian imbalance settlement mechanism.

Result: In linear, analytically solvable cases, S3TS comes close to the mathematically optimal solution obtained from stochastic optimization, with costs within 14% of the optimum conditioned on the scenario trees. In complex, highly non-linear settings, S3TS substantially improves over alternatives: up to 51% cost reduction compared to a simple myopic (greedy) algorithm and 5.4% reduction compared to a deterministic Monte Carlo Tree Search that ignores uncertainty.

Conclusion: S3TS can effectively integrate explicit uncertainty modeling with non-linear system representations for energy scheduling problems. It achieves near-optimality when the problem is tractable and yields significant cost savings in more realistic, non-linear scenarios compared to common baselines, suggesting it is a promising approach for decision-making in modern power systems with high uncertainty.

Abstract: Effective scheduling in the energy sector is essential to ensure the reliable operation of electrical grids and their connected assets by, for instance, optimizing the dispatch of generation units and storage systems. An effective planning strategy must (a) accommodate advanced and potentially non-linear system models -- exploiting the increasing data availability of modern grids, and (b) explicitly handle uncertainties arising, for instance, from the integration of renewable energy sources. While existing approaches can address either non-linearity (e.g., Monte Carlo Tree Search) or uncertainty (e.g., stochastic mathematical optimization), there is a lack of planning techniques capable of addressing both challenges simultaneously. To bridge this gap, we propose a Stochastic Scenario-Structured Tree Search (S3TS) algorithm that explicitly represents uncertainty through scenario trees while enabling the integration of advanced non-linear models. We evaluate S3TS on a simulated demand response signal publication problem, largely mimicking the imbalance settlement mechanism in Belgium. The results demonstrate near-optimal performance in linear, analytically tractable settings, with costs within 14% of the mathematically optimal solution conditioned to the scenario trees. In highly non-linear scenarios, S3TS significantly outperforms baseline methods, achieving cost reductions of up to 51% and 5.4% compared to a myopic algorithm and deterministic MCTS, respectively.

</details>


### [335] [An Abstract Worlds Semantic Framework for Belief Change Operators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02163)
*Daniel Grimaldi,M. Vanina Martinez,Ricardo O. Rodriguez*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A set-theoretic, syntax-free semantic framework (Abstract Worlds Semantics) is proposed to uniformly represent and analyze different belief change models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing belief change theories (e.g., AGM, KM, multiple change, non-prioritized updates) are often presented in different, sometimes incompatible, formalisms and are tightly coupled to specific logical languages. There is a need for a more abstract, syntax-independent framework that can capture these models uniformly, clarify their relationships, and generalize belief change theory beyond particular logics.

Method: The paper introduces Abstract Worlds Semantics (AWS), a set-theoretic semantics where worlds are primitive elements and no specific logical syntax is assumed. Over sets of these worlds, the authors define world contraction and world revision operators, inspired by Grove’s sphere-based approach. They then define general belief change operators in terms of these world-level operations and show how, when instantiated with classical propositional logic, AWS recovers and unifies classical models such as AGM contraction/revision, KM update, and multiple belief change constructions, as well as non-prioritized models.

Result: AWS is shown to be expressive enough to represent both classical and non-prioritized belief change operators within a single, uniform semantic framework. By specifying appropriate constraints on world contraction/revision, the framework reproduces AGM, KM, and multiple change models and clarifies their structural similarities and differences. This yields a homogeneous characterization of disparate belief change theories using the same underlying set-theoretic machinery.

Conclusion: Abstract Worlds Semantics provides a general, syntax-free, set-theoretic basis for belief change that systematizes and unifies diverse belief change frameworks. It simplifies the conceptual landscape by treating different operators as instances of a common abstract schema, thereby generalizing and organizing belief change theory over belief sets and facilitating comparison, extension, and potential application to logics beyond classical propositional logic.

Abstract: This article proposes a set-theoretic framework for belief change, called Abstract Worlds Semantics, in which no logical syntax is assumed. Inspired by Grove's (1988) results, our approach treats worlds as primitive elements, over which world contraction and world revision operators are defined. This semantic framework enables a unified analysis of belief change models. Within this framework, we unify classical and non-prioritized belief change constructions by defining versatile operators. When classical propositional logic is considered, our framework provides a homogeneous account of AGM, KM, and Multiple Change models. In summary, AWS systematizes belief change frameworks and operators, simplifying and generalizing belief change theory over belief sets.

</details>


### [336] [From Capability Models to Automated Planning: An AAS-Native Approach for Automatic PDDL Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02167)
*Hamied Nabizada,Thomas Wirt,Luis Miguel Vieira da Silva,Felix Gehlhoff,Alexander Fay*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents a method to automatically generate PDDL planning problems from standardized Asset Administration Shell (AAS) capability models, enabling production engineers to verify whether system layouts support required production sequences without learning PDDL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Production engineers must ensure that a production system layout can realize all required production sequences. Automated planning can support this verification, but specifying planning problems in PDDL is complex and requires AI planning expertise that most engineers lack. Meanwhile, Industry 4.0 standards around Asset Administration Shells define rich, machine-interpretable descriptions of assets and their capabilities. The paper is motivated by the opportunity to leverage these existing standardized models to automatically construct planning problems, bridging the gap between domain experts and AI planning tools without requiring them to understand PDDL or planning formalisms.

Method: The authors use AAS capability models structured according to four Industry 4.0 standards—VDI 3682 for process descriptions, IEC 61360-1 for semantic property qualification, IDTA 02011 for type hierarchies, and IDTA 02016 for instance descriptions. They design an extraction algorithm that interprets resource capabilities, their preconditions, and effects from these domain-level descriptions and systematically maps them to PDDL actions, predicates, objects, and initial/goal states. The algorithm takes distributed Multi-AAS architectures as input and outputs complete PDDL planning problems. Unlike prior approaches, no PDDL-specific submodels are introduced; instead, all planning constructs are inferred from existing capability descriptions.
