Result: The approach successfully generates complete PDDL planning problems from AAS capability models without manual PDDL modeling. In a laboratory production system case study, the authors construct AAS models for different layout variants and run optimal planners on the automatically generated PDDL problems. The results show that the method can be used to compare four layout variants and to quantify design trade-offs, such as differences in achievable sequences or resource utilization, solely by modifying the AAS models and regenerating planning instances.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that standardized AAS capability models, when structured with established Industry 4.0 standards, contain enough semantic information to automatically derive AI planning models in PDDL. This enables production engineers to use powerful planning tools for layout verification and design exploration without dealing with planning languages directly. The work demonstrates that Multi-AAS architectures can serve as a practical bridge between engineering models and automated planning, supporting systematic, model-based exploration of alternative production system designs.

Abstract: Engineers designing production systems need to verify that a given layout supports all required production sequences. Automated planning techniques can answer such questions, but formulating the required planning problems in the Planning Domain Definition Language (PDDL) demands specialized expertise that production engineers typically lack. Asset Administration Shells (AAS) have emerged as the standardized Digital Twin for industrial assets in Industry 4.0. We show that AAS capability models, structured using four established Industry 4.0 standards (VDI 3682 for process descriptions, IEC 61360-1 for semantic property qualification, IDTA 02011 for type hierarchies, and IDTA 02016 for instance descriptions), contain sufficient information to generate complete PDDL problems automatically. Unlike prior work that introduced PDDL-specific submodels, our approach derives all planning elements from domain-level descriptions of resource functions, so-called capabilities, allowing engineers to model capabilities without any exposure to PDDL syntax or planning concepts. Our extraction algorithm transforms distributed Multi-AAS architectures into complete PDDL planning problems. We validate the approach on AAS models of a laboratory production system, comparing four layout variants using optimal planning to demonstrate how engineers can systematically explore design trade-offs by modifying the AAS model and regenerating the planning domain

</details>


### [337] [CEON: Circular Economy Ontology Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02253)
*Huanyu Li,Els de Vleeschauwer,Robin Keskisärkkä,Mikael Lindecrantz,Mina Abd Nikooie Pour,Ying Li,Ben De Meester,Patrick Lambrix,Eva Blomqvist*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents the Circular Economy Ontology Network (CEON), a semantic framework to support information sharing and interoperability across sectors to enable circular economy strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Circular economy strategies like reuse, refurbishment, remanufacturing, and recycling require effective information sharing and semantic interoperability across multiple industry sectors and along product life cycles. Existing knowledge representation in the circular economy domain is fragmented and does not adequately support cross-sector communication or semantics-aware data documentation, creating a barrier to implementing circular strategies in practice.

Method: The authors design and develop the Circular Economy Ontology Network (CEON) within the Onto-DESIDE project. CEON defines cross-sectorial concepts relevant to circular economy and product life cycles and is structured as an ontology network to support semantic interoperability. They then apply CEON in cross-industry data documentation scenarios covering construction, electronics, and textile sectors to demonstrate and validate its applicability.

Result: The resulting artifact is CEON, an ontology network that provides a shared semantic model for circular economy concepts across multiple industry sectors. Its use in case scenarios shows that CEON can be used to document data from disparate domains (construction, electronics, textiles) in a semantics-aware, interoperable manner, supporting information sharing along product life cycles.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that CEON helps address the challenge of knowledge representation in the circular economy by defining cross-sector concepts and enabling semantic interoperability for data sharing and documentation. This supports the implementation of circular strategies across industries and along product life cycles, and the demonstrated scenarios indicate its potential as an enabling infrastructure component for a more circular economy.

Abstract: Increasing the circularity of resource use in our society has been recognized as a path to sustainability, i.e., transitioning into a more circular economy. There are many different circular strategies to do so, such as reusing products and components, refurbishing and remanufacturing used products, or recycling left-over or used materials. To enable these strategies, it is necessary to share information at the infrastructure level and to communicate between industry sectors along the product life cycle. Enabling semantic interoperability in this information sharing and communication is therefore a key to increasing circularity. However, knowledge representation for the circular economy (CE) domain, which involves many relevant industry sectors related to product life cycles, remains challenging. To bridge this gap, we developed the Circular Economy Ontology Network (CEON) within the Onto-DESIDE project. This ontology network aims to fill gaps in CE by defining cross-sectorial concepts and to enable semantics-aware data documentation. We demonstrate CEON through cross-industry data documentation scenarios spanning construction, electronics, and textile sectors.

</details>


### [338] [POIROT: Interrogating Agents for Failure Detection in Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02282)
*Iñaki Dellibarda Varela,R. Sendra-Arranz,Pablo Romero-Sorozabal,J. M. Valverde-García,Annemarie F. Laudanski,Álvaro Gutiérrez,Eduardo Rocon,Manuel Cebrian*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: POIROT is a protocol that uses the agents within a multi-agent LLM system to diagnose and evaluate that system’s own failures and safety issues, outperforming traditional single-LLM evaluators, and is released with an accompanying benchmark (BLAME).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based multi-agent systems show strong reasoning but suffer from hard-to-characterize failures and hallucinations, which is unacceptable for safety-critical applications, especially under emerging AI regulations. Existing evaluation methods rely on centralized, expert-heavy oversight that can itself be a single point of failure. The authors thus seek a scalable, more robust, and less expert-dependent way to evaluate and audit such systems using their internal structure.

Method: They design POIROT, a protocol that reuses the system’s own agents as a distributed diagnostic and evaluation layer. The method exploits epistemic diversity in multi-agent architectures: agents with different roles or perspectives cross-check and audit each other’s outputs and behavior. They empirically compare POIROT against single-LLM evaluator baselines across multiple settings, manipulating problem complexity, number of agents, and types/dimensions of injected faults, including compound faults.

Result: POIROT outperforms single-LLM evaluator baselines, with an odds ratio of 1.60 and statistically significant improvement (p = 0.008). Its performance advantage grows with higher task complexity, more agents, and more complex/faceted failure modes, and it remains effective under conditions where multiple faults occur together.

Conclusion: Internal, agent-based oversight within LLM multi-agent systems can be a viable and effective alternative to external, centralized evaluation. The collective intelligence and epistemic diversity of agents executing a task are sufficient to provide reliable auditing and fault attribution. The authors provide POIROT as an open-source library and introduce BLAME, a dedicated benchmark for evaluating fault attribution in safety-critical multi-agent LLM systems.

Abstract: Orchestrating Large Language Models into Multi-Agent Systems (LLM-MAS) has unlocked remarkable reasoning capabilities, yet emergent failures and hallucinations that resist characterisation block their deployment in safety-critical domains -- a gap made legally untenable by emerging AI regulation. Existing evaluation paradigms share a common flaw: centralised judgment creates single points of failure and demands domain-specific expertise. Here we present POIROT, a protocol that repurposes a system's own agents as its diagnostic layer, leveraging the epistemic diversity already present in the architecture. Across evaluated settings, POIROT outperforms single-LLM evaluator baselines, with gains that scale with problem complexity (OR = 1.60, $p = 0.008$), agent count, and fault dimensionality, persisting under compound fault conditions. These results demonstrate that safety oversight need not be externalised: the agents executing a role carry sufficient collective intelligence to audit it. We release POIROT as an open-source library alongside BLAME, a benchmark for fault attribution in safety-critical multi-agent systems.

</details>


### [339] [Repair Before Veto: Repair-Augmented Constraint Learning for Contextual Decisions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02326)
*Yifan Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose a learning framework that can proactively repair constraint-violating options using known modifications, instead of just vetoing them, sharply reducing false rejections versus baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world systems often face hard constraints (e.g., on configurations or tickets) but also know a finite set of feasible modifications that can fix violations. Standard constraint learning and recourse methods treat constraints as terminal vetoes and do not learn whether and how to repair a candidate before rejecting it, leading to unnecessary rejections (“false vetoes”). The authors aim to unify constraint satisfaction and automatic repair within the decision rule itself.

Method: They introduce Repair-Augmented Constraint Learning (RACL), a contextual decision framework that embeds known repair operators into the classifier’s semantics. The rule decides to accept a candidate if there exists an affordable repair that makes it feasible and sufficiently preferred, otherwise returning a structured rejection “credit” and, when relevant, a repair plan. They analyze its semantics relative to existing HASSLE-style no-repair rules, study identifiability and learnability of the induced decision rules, and derive capacity and calibration bounds in a shared-weight setting where only observed feasibility is available. They then evaluate RACL on controlled synthetic tasks and benchmarks derived from DB1B ticketing data, comparing it to black-box repair-search baselines.

Result: RACL is shown to recover the intended structure of credits (rejection reasons) and repair plans across benchmarks. On the most challenging raw-data-derived benchmark tier, a validation-selected RACL model reduces false vetoes to 10 out of 4039 cases (false veto rate 0.0025), dramatically outperforming the strongest black-box baseline, which yields about 1064 false vetoes on the same data. RACL also makes the trade-off between false veto rate (FVR) and erroneous decisions (EDR) explicit.

Conclusion: Embedding known repair operators directly into the constraint-learning decision rule—repairing before vetoing—strictly generalizes traditional terminal-veto semantics and closes an inherent “false-veto” gap that such rules cannot avoid. RACL yields theoretically grounded guarantees (on identifiability, capacity, and calibration) and empirically achieves far fewer false vetoes while exposing clear trade-offs between conservativeness and error, suggesting it is a practical and principled framework for decision systems with known feasible repairs.

Abstract: Hard constraints are usually treated as terminal vetoes: once a candidate violates a requirement, the learned rule rejects it and any repair is handled outside the decision semantics. This misses a common deployed regime in which the system already knows a finite menu of modifications, such as adding a ticket option, changing a configuration, or requesting an available service upgrade. Existing constraint-learning, soft-relaxation, and recourse methods address nearby problems, but they do not learn whether an option should be repaired before being vetoed. We introduce Repair-Augmented Constraint Learning (RACL), a contextual decision framework that lifts known repair operators into the classifier semantics. A candidate is accepted when an affordable repair makes it feasible and preferred enough; otherwise the system returns a structured rejection credit and, when applicable, a repair plan. This repair-before-veto view strictly generalizes no-repair HASSLE-style semantics, reveals an irreducible false-veto gap for terminal-veto rules, separates binary-label non-identifiability from decision-rule learnability, and gives capacity and calibration bounds for the observed-feasibility shared-weight setting. Across controlled and DB1B-derived benchmarks, RACL recovers the intended credit and repair structure. On the hardest raw-data-derived tier, validation-selected RACL reduces false vetoes to 10/4039 (FVR 0.0025), versus about 1064/4039 for the strongest repair-search black-box baseline, while making the FVR/EDR trade-off explicit.

</details>


### [340] [Forget Attention: Importance-Aware Attention Is All You Need](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02332)
*Soohyeong Shin,Yeongwook Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes SISA, a new hybrid language modeling method that injects SSM-derived importance signals directly into attention scores via a standard SDPA call, achieving better long-range retrieval and competitive benchmarks while remaining simple to implement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Attention provides global access to context but struggles to prioritize important tokens, whereas state space models provide strong sequential importance signals but lack random access to past information. Existing hybrids keep attention and SSMs in separate modules (block-level or head-level fusion), so the SSM cannot directly shape the attention weighting. This leaves an open challenge: how to tightly couple SSM importance with attention’s scoring mechanism in a way that is efficient and compatible with standard implementations.

Method: Introduce SISA (SSM-Informed Softmax Attention), which augments the standard attention score with an importance term derived from an SSM. This is implemented by augmenting the query/key vectors so that the entire operation remains a single standard scaled dot-product attention (SDPA) call, without custom kernels or recurrent state. The fusion happens at the score level: the SSM computes token-wise importance, which is then baked into attention scores before softmax, enabling attention to be directly guided by SSM signals.

Result: On a 152M-parameter model trained on 5B tokens, SISA achieves 17.3% LAMBADA-greedy accuracy, outperforming a comparable Transformer (13.9%) and Mamba-3 (15.5%). On the Needle-in-a-Haystack (NIAH) retrieval benchmark, SISA reaches 100% accuracy from step 1K, converging 7x faster than a pure Transformer. In a 369M-parameter setting, Mamba-3 slightly leads on LAMBADA, while SISA maintains perfect NIAH performance and preserves compatibility with stock SDPA-based execution.

Conclusion: SISA demonstrates that fusing SSM-derived importance at the attention score level can improve long-range retrieval and language modeling quality without sacrificing implementation simplicity. This approach defines a new design axis for SSM-attention hybrids—score-level fusion—complementing and potentially surpassing prior block-level and head-level integration strategies, while remaining compatible with standard attention kernels and non-recurrent architectures.

Abstract: Combining attention's global retrieval with the sequential importance signal of state space models (SSMs) is the open challenge of hybrid language modeling. Transformers see everywhere but cannot prioritize; SSMs know what matters but cannot revisit. Existing hybrids -- Jamba (block level) and Hymba (head level) -- place the two in separate compartments, so neither informs the other during the attention computation itself. We propose SISA (SSM-Informed Softmax Attention), which adds an SSM-derived importance term directly inside the attention score and realizes the full operation as a single SDPA call on augmented query/key vectors -- no recurrent state, no custom kernel. At 152M / 5B tokens, SISA reaches LAMBADA-greedy 17.3% (vs. Transformer 13.9 and Mamba-3 15.5) and attains NIAH 100% from step 1K, 7x faster than Transformer's retrieval convergence; at 369M, Mamba-3 leads LAMBADA while SISA preserves perfect NIAH and stock-SDPA execution. SISA thus defines a third design axis for SSM-attention hybrids -- score-level fusion -- beyond the block-level and head-level paradigms that have dominated the field.

</details>


### [341] [Coordination Graphs for Constrained Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02337)
*Santiago Amaya-Corredor,Miguel Calvo-Fullana,Anders Jonsson*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes CG-CMARL, a coordination-graph-based framework that scales constrained multi-agent RL to many agents and can trade off objectives vs. constraints without retraining, achieving better Pareto fronts than baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Constrained multi-agent RL suffers from exponential growth of the joint action space and from constraints that couple agents beyond what reward signals capture. Existing centralized approaches do not scale, and fixed reward-shaping ratios cannot flexibly trade off objectives and constraints or trace full Pareto fronts without retraining. The authors aim to develop a scalable, principled method that explicitly models coordination under constraints and can efficiently explore objective–constraint tradeoffs.

Method: They introduce CG-CMARL, which uses coordination graphs combined with Lagrangian duality. The joint multi-agent problem is decomposed into pairwise regions represented as a factor/coordination graph. Each region uses shared Q-functions: one for the main objective and one per constraint, making the number of learned models independent of the number of agents. During execution, Max-Sum message passing over the factor graph coordinates agents’ actions, while a Lagrangian multiplier adjusts the tradeoff between objective and constraints. This lets a single trained model traverse a Pareto front. They provide theoretical analysis: convergence guarantees under mild assumptions and a compositional error bound that isolates different error sources linked to design choices.

Result: On cooperative navigation benchmarks where up to 10 agents must reach targets under pairwise constraints, CG-CMARL learns policies whose Pareto fronts (objective vs. constraint satisfaction) dominate those of baseline methods that rely on fixed reward-shaping ratios. It scales to team sizes where centralized constrained RL baselines become computationally intractable. Empirical results support both the scalability and the improved objective–constraint tradeoffs of the method.

Conclusion: CG-CMARL effectively tackles constrained multi-agent RL by factorizing coordination via graphs and integrating Lagrangian duality, resulting in scalable learning whose complexity does not grow with the number of agents. The method can produce a full Pareto front from a single training run, provides interpretable theoretical guarantees and error decomposition, and outperforms traditional centralized and reward-shaped baselines on cooperative navigation tasks, especially as the number of agents increases.

Abstract: Constrained Multi-agent reinforcement learning (CMARL) faces two intertwined challenges: the joint action space grows exponentially with the number of agents, and additional requirements couple agents in ways that reward structure alone does not capture. We introduce Coordination Graphs for Constrained Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (CG-CMARL), a framework that addresses both challenges by combining coordination graphs with Lagrangian duality. The system decomposes the joint problem into pairwise regions, each served by a set of shared Q-functions, one for the primary objective and one for each of the constraints, so that the number of learned models is independent of the number of agents. At execution time, Max-Sum message passing coordinates actions across the factor graph, while a Lagrangian multiplier controls the objective--constraint tradeoff, allowing a single trained model to trace a Pareto front without retraining. We provide convergence guarantees under mild conditions, together with a compositional error bound that decomposes into separate interpretable sources, each traceable to a specific design choice and independently controllable. Experiments on cooperative navigation tasks (where teams of up to 10 agents must coordinate to reach target positions while satisfying pairwise constraints) show that our method produces Pareto fronts dominating established baselines trained at fixed reward-shaping ratios, while scaling to team sizes where centralized approaches become intractable.

</details>


### [342] [SIRI: Self-Internalizing Reinforcement Learning with Intrinsic Skills for LLM Agent Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02355)
*Zhongyu He,Yuanfan Li,Fei Huang,Tianyu Chen,Siyuan Chen,Xingyang Li,Meng Hsuan Yu,Xiangrong Liu,Leyi Wei,Lu Pan,Ke Zeng,Xunliang Cai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces SIRI, a framework that lets long-horizon LLM agents autonomously discover, validate, and internalize reusable skills via reinforcement learning, avoiding external skill generators or inference-time skill banks and improving performance on ALFWorld and WebShop.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon LLM agents need reusable skills for efficient problem solving, but existing approaches depend on external skill generators or persistent retrieval from skill banks, which increases engineering complexity, context length, and latency. The motivation is to design a method where the agent itself can learn and internalize skills during training so that inference remains simple (no extra prompts or retrieval) while still benefiting from skills.

Method: SIRI is a three-phase RL-based framework: (1) Warm-up with GiGPO to obtain a competent base policy and collect successful skill-free trajectories. (2) Self-skill mining, where the current policy summarizes compact skills from its own successful plain rollouts and then validates them by comparing paired skill-augmented and skill-free trajectories. (3) Skill distillation, where only helpful skill-guided action tokens are distilled back into the plain policy using trajectory-level utility and action-level advantage, so that the final policy embeds skills internally and runs with the original prompt only at inference.

Result: On ALFWorld and WebShop benchmarks using Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, SIRI improves the base GiGPO policy’s success rate from 0.908 to 0.930 on ALFWorld and from 0.728 to 0.813 on WebShop, outperforming prompt-based, RL-based, and memory-augmented baselines. Analyses show that self-mined skills enable performance comparable to distillation that uses a stronger closed-source large model, indicating that external powerful teachers are not necessary.

Conclusion: SIRI demonstrates that LLM agents can autonomously mine and internalize useful skills without external generators or inference-time skill banks, yielding better performance and lower deployment complexity. The framework shows that self-skill mining plus targeted distillation of beneficial skill-guided actions is an effective way to equip long-horizon LLM agents with reusable, implicitly stored skills while keeping inference-time prompts simple.

Abstract: Long-horizon LLM agents can benefit from reusable skills, yet existing skill-based methods often rely on external skill generators during training or persistent skill retrieval at inference, increasing engineering complexity, context length, and deployment latency. We propose Self-Internalizing Reinforcement learning with Intrinsic skills (SIRI), a three-phase framework that enables agents to discover, validate, and internalize skills without external skill generators or inference-time skill banks. SIRI first warms up the policy with GiGPO to acquire basic interaction ability and collect successful skill-free trajectories. It then performs self-skill mining, where the current policy summarizes compact skills from its own successful plain rollouts and validates them through paired skill-augmented and skill-free rollouts. Finally, SIRI distills only beneficial skill-guided action tokens into the plain policy using trajectory-level utility and action-level advantage. At inference, the agent runs with the original prompt only. On ALFWorld and WebShop with Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, SIRI improves GiGPO from 0.908 to 0.930 on ALFWorld and from 0.728 to 0.813 on WebShop, outperforming prompt-based, RL-based, and memory-augmented baselines. Further analysis shows that our self-mining strategy can achieve performance comparable to distillation with closed-source large model. Our code is available at https://github.com/kirito618/SIRI.

</details>


### [343] [MOC: Multi-Order Communication in LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02359)
*Yao Guan,Lin Wang,Zhihu Lu,Ziyi Wang,Wenzhu Yan,Qiang Duan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Multi-Order Communication (MOC), a new communication scheme for LLM-based multi-agent systems that captures multi-hop dependencies and merges messages structurally to improve performance and cut token costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most LLM-based multi-agent works optimize who talks to whom (coordination topology) but not how messages are represented, propagated, and compressed. Existing schemes usually just concatenate first-order neighbor messages, which limits the effective evidence field and causes important information to be lost or diluted over multiple hops. There is a need for a principled way to model multi-hop evidence flow while keeping communication efficient under token constraints.

Method: The authors propose the Multi-Order Communication (MOC) scheme that explicitly models multi-hop dependencies in inter-agent communication. They formalize a communication mechanism that builds a structured multi-order evidence stream, capturing information from different hop distances. On top of this, they develop a Semantic-Topological Merging algorithm that consolidates messages based on both semantic similarity and graph/topology information to maintain semantic fidelity while respecting token budgets, essentially compressing and organizing multi-hop evidence for each agent.

Result: Across six diverse datasets and with LLM backbones of different sizes, applying MOC consistently yields better task performance and lower communication costs compared to baselines relying on simple concatenation of neighbor messages or other existing schemes.

Conclusion: Optimizing the structure and processing of inter-agent messages is as crucial as designing the communication topology in LLM-based multi-agent systems. By modeling multi-order (multi-hop) evidence and merging it with a semantic-topological strategy, MOC provides a more effective and efficient communication mechanism, leading to consistent performance gains and reduced token usage in multi-agent LLM settings.

Abstract: Despite the remarkable progress of Large Language Model (LLM) based Multi-Agent Systems, most research focuses on optimizing coordination topology while largely underexploring the equally critical problem: how to transmit and optimize messages among agents effectively? Current communication schemes typically rely on the direct concatenation of first-order neighbor responses, which induces a restricted evidence receptive field and leads to the dilution of crucial insights over multi-hop paths. To address these limitations, we propose the Multi-Order Communication (MOC) scheme, which reconstructs the inter-agent communication to capture multi-hop dependencies and incorporates a structural message consolidation strategy to ensure efficiency. Specifically, we formalize the communication mechanism to construct a structured multi-order evidence stream, and subsequently design a Semantic-Topological Merging algorithm to optimize semantic fidelity within token constraints. Extensive experiments across six diverse datasets and LLM backbones of varying parameter scales demonstrate that MOC consistently improves task performance and reduces communication costs. The code is available at https://github.com/yao-guan/MOC.

</details>


### [344] [COMAP: Co-Evolving World Models and Agent Policies for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02372)
*Youwei Liu,Jian Wang,Hanlin Wang,Wenjie Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: COMAP is a co-evolution framework where a textual world model and an agent policy improve each other through closed-loop interaction, leading to better long-horizon decision making across multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing textual world models are static after training and cannot adapt to the changing on-policy state-action distributions of an evolving agent. At the same time, many methods for improving agents depend on external rewards or verifiers, which are often unavailable or impractical in realistic interactive environments. There is a need for a framework where agents and their world models can jointly and autonomously improve via interaction, without relying on external supervision.

Method: COMAP introduces a closed-loop co-evolution process between a language-based world model and an agent policy. At each decision step, the world model predicts future state feedback for a set of candidate actions. The agent then performs future-aware reflection: it evaluates the reliability of these predicted feedbacks and refines its chosen action based on this assessment. The on-policy trajectories produced by this interaction are used to update the world model through self-distillation, aligning the model more closely with the agent’s current interaction distribution. This loop repeats continuously during task execution.

Result: On benchmarks for embodied task planning, Web navigation, and tool use, COMAP surpasses strong baseline methods. With the Qwen3-4B backbone, it yields a reported relative performance gain of 16.75%. Additional empirical studies show that, as co-evolution continues, the world model’s prediction accuracy steadily improves, which in turn enhances the agent’s effectiveness on long-horizon tasks.

Conclusion: Co-evolving textual world models and agent policies in a closed-loop, self-distillation-based framework allows agents to adapt their internal models to their actual on-policy experience, without requiring external rewards or verifiers. This co-adaptation improves world-model accuracy and leads to more robust, effective long-horizon decision-making across diverse interactive environments.

Abstract: Equipping language agents with world models enables them to anticipate environment dynamics and evaluate candidate actions before execution. However, existing textual world models are typically fixed after training, preventing them from adapting to the on-policy state-action distributions induced by an evolving agent. Meanwhile, agent-improvement methods often rely on external rewards or verifiers, limiting their applicability in realistic interactive environments. In this paper, we propose COMAP, a novel framework that co-evolves textual world models and agent policies through closed-loop interaction. At each decision step, the world model predicts future state feedback for candidate actions, and the agent performs future-aware reflection by estimating the reliability of this feedback and refining its action accordingly. The resulting on-policy trajectories are then used to update the world model via self-distillation, allowing it to better match the agent's evolving interaction distribution. Across embodied task planning, Web navigation, and tool-use benchmarks, COMAP consistently outperforms competitive baselines, e.g., +16.75% relative improvement with Qwen3-4B. Further analyses show that the co-evolutionary loop improves the world model's prediction accuracy over time and leads to more effective long-horizon decision-making. Our code is available at: https://github.com/loyiv/CoMAP.

</details>


### [345] [Harness-1: Reinforcement Learning for Search Agents with State-Externalizing Harnesses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02373)
*Pengcheng Jiang,Zhiyi Shi,Kelly Hong,Xueqiang Xu,Jiashuo Sun,Jimeng Sun,Hammad Bashir,Jiawei Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Harness-1, a 20B-parameter retrieval agent trained with reinforcement learning that offloads bookkeeping and memory management to a stateful search harness, improving retrieval performance and generalization across diverse benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing search agents entangle high-level semantic search decisions with routine state and memory management within a single policy over growing transcripts. This forces reinforcement learning to optimize both meaningful search behavior and low-level bookkeeping, which the environment could track more reliably. The authors are motivated to separate these concerns so that RL focuses on search strategy while the environment maintains explicit search state, aiming for more robust and generalizable retrieval behavior.

Method: The authors design a stateful search harness that maintains environment-side working memory, including a candidate pool, an importance-tagged curated set, compact evidence links, verification records, compressed and deduplicated observations, and budget-aware context rendering. They then train Harness-1, a 20B-parameter retrieval subagent, with reinforcement learning inside this harness. The policy is responsible only for semantic decisions such as what to search, which documents to keep or discard, what to verify, and when to stop, while the harness handles all bookkeeping and memory organization.

Result: On eight retrieval benchmarks covering web search, finance, patents, and multi-hop QA, Harness-1 achieves an average curated recall of 0.730. It outperforms the next strongest open search subagent by 11.4 points and remains competitive with larger frontier-model-based search systems. The performance gains are particularly notable on held-out transfer benchmarks, indicating strong out-of-domain generalization of the learned retrieval behaviors.

Conclusion: Separating semantic search decisions from environment-side state management via a stateful search harness enables more effective reinforcement learning for retrieval agents. Harness-1 demonstrates that RL over explicit search state yields strong, transferable retrieval performance, rivaling larger systems while improving curated recall and generalization. This architecture suggests a promising direction for building robust, scalable search agents where the environment reliably manages recoverable state and the policy focuses on high-level search strategy.

Abstract: Search agents are often trained as policies over growing transcripts: the model must decide how to search while also remembering what it has seen, which evidence is useful, which constraints remain open, and which claims have actually been checked. We argue that this formulation puts too much routine state management inside the policy: reinforcement learning is forced to optimize both semantic search decisions and recoverable bookkeeping that the environment can maintain more reliably. We introduce Harness-1, a 20B search agent (retrieval subagent) trained with reinforcement learning inside a stateful search harness. The harness maintains environment-side working memory, including a candidate pool, an importance-tagged curated set, compact evidence links, verification records, compressed and deduplicated observations, and budget-aware context rendering. The policy retains the semantic decisions: what to search, which documents to keep or discard, what to verify, and when to stop. Across eight retrieval benchmarks spanning web, finance, patents, and multi-hop QA, Harness-1 achieves 0.730 average curated recall, outperforming the next strongest open search subagent by +11.4 points and remaining competitive with much larger frontier-model searchers. Its gains are especially strong on held-out transfer benchmarks, suggesting that reinforcement learning over explicit search state can produce retrieval behaviors that generalize beyond the training domains. Our code is available at https://github.com/pat-jj/harness-1.

</details>


### [346] [Spatial Representation Learning Beyond Pixels: Unifying Raster Data and Vector Semantics for Human-Centric Geospatial Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02374)
*Steffen Knoblauch,Hao Li,Gengchen Mai,Konstantin Klemmer,Song Gao,WenWen Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that Earth Observation AI should move from raster-only models to unified spatial representation learning that jointly embeds raster imagery and vector data for richer, more interpretable geospatial understanding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Earth Observation Foundation Models focus almost exclusively on raster (image) data, ignoring abundant vector sources (e.g., OpenStreetMap) that encode geometry, topology, semantics, and human systems. Existing methods treat raster and vector separately and use lossy conversions between them, limiting accuracy, interpretability, and semantic grounding in downstream geospatial tasks.

Method: This is a perspective / position paper rather than an empirical study. It conceptually proposes a paradigm of joint Spatial Representation Learning (SRL) that learns a unified embedding space where raster perception and vector-based reasoning are aligned. It builds on multimodal geospatial learning ideas, articulates conceptual foundations, and enumerates technical challenges and research directions for harmonizing heterogeneous spatial data.

Result: No new quantitative results are reported. The main outcomes are a conceptual framework, a set of argued benefits of integrating raster and vector modalities, and an organized discussion of technical challenges and possible approaches for multimodal spatial representation learning.

Conclusion: Integrating raster and vector modalities into a joint embedding space is crucial for the next generation of geospatial AI. Such unified SRL can better capture both physical patterns and human-structured information, leading to more accurate, interpretable, and semantically grounded models of the Earth compared with raster-only EOFMs.

Abstract: Earth Observation (EO) has fundamentally transformed the monitoring of environmental processes and human activities up to planetary scale. Recent advances in self-supervised learning have given rise to Earth Observation Foundation Models (EOFMs), which leverage petabyte-scale unlabeled EO data to learn transferable representations across a wide range of downstream geospatial tasks. Despite these advances, current EOFMs remain largely confined to raster modalities, overlooking the rich, structured information encoded in openly-accessible vector data sources such as OpenStreetMap and Overture. Vector data provides explicit and compact representations of geographic entities, including geometry, topology, and semantic relationships, offering critical contextual signals that are often ambiguous or inaccessible in imagery alone. Raster and vector data thus represent complementary views of geographic space: raster data captures continuous physical and spectral patterns, while vector data encodes discrete objects and their relational structure and often represents more of the human rather than the physical systems (e.g. social or demographic data). However, existing geospatial representation learning paradigms treat these modalities in isolation, relying on imperfect and often lossy transformations to bridge them. This perspective paper calls for a paradigm shift toward joint Spatial Representation Learning (SRL) in an unified embedding space that integrate raster perception with vector-based reasoning. Building on emerging efforts in multimodal geospatial learning, we highlight conceptual foundations, technical challenges, and promising directions for aligning heterogeneous spatial data sources. We contend that such integration is essential for developing next-generation geospatial AI systems capable of more accurate, interpretable, and semantically grounded understanding of the Earth.

</details>


### [347] [A Mathematical Conflict Framework for Contextual Data Modulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02381)
*Hakan Emre Kartal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a general mathematical framework that treats conflict between raw and contextual data as an explicit, operator-based quantity instead of a hidden byproduct of optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods usually handle conflict between data and context implicitly within optimization or learning algorithms, making it hard to analyze, compare, or systematically design how conflict is measured and resolved. There is a need for a general, algorithm-agnostic mathematical structure that explicitly represents conflict and its components, so it can be adapted to various problems and studied independently of any particular optimization scheme.

Method: The authors introduce a generalized operator-based framework in which conflict is formalized as a local, directional, and context-sensitive mathematical quantity. They define an abstract operator that unifies several components: weighting of different elements, behavior across scales, and mapping of conflict values to outputs. This operator-based structure is not tied to a specific learning or optimization algorithm, but is instead designed to be instantiated for different classes of problems by choosing suitable operators and parameters.

Result: The result is a flexible, abstract conflict framework that can be plugged into diverse problem settings to explicitly quantify and manage conflict between raw and contextual data. It reframes conflict from being an implicit side effect of optimization to an explicit mathematical object with well-defined properties and tunable components.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that modeling conflict as an independent, operator-based mathematical entity enables a more systematic and transparent treatment of structural discrepancies between data and context. This generalized framework provides a foundation that can be specialized for different applications while maintaining a unified conceptual and mathematical structure for handling conflict.

Abstract: In this study, a generalized operator-based mathematical conflict framework is presented to explicitly represent structural discrepancies between raw data and contextual data. The proposed structure treats conflict as a local, directional, and context-sensitive quantity, integrating components such as weighting, scale behavior, and output mapping under a unified abstract operator. Without being reduced to a specific learning algorithm or optimization method, the framework is defined as a general structure adaptable to different classes of problems. While existing approaches typically treat conflict merely as an implicit side effect embedded within the optimization process, the proposed framework considers conflict as an independent, operator-based, and component-level mathematical object.

</details>


### [348] [AgentPLM: Agentic Protein Language Models with Reasoning-Augmented Decoding for Protein Sequence Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02386)
*Sahil Rahman,Maxx Richard Rahman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentPLM turns a frozen protein language model into an active design agent that interleaves sequence generation with structure/energy/docking tools and is trained with a trajectory-level preference objective to decide when and how to use feedback, achieving state-of-the-art performance on diverse protein design tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard protein language models generate sequences in a single forward pass, cannot query external biophysical oracles during generation, and lack a mechanism to correct candidates that violate thermodynamic or structural constraints. The authors aim to transform PLMs from passive sequence predictors into active agents that can reason with, and adapt to, structural/energetic feedback from tools like folding, stability, and docking predictors, thereby improving practical protein design across multiple tasks.

Method: 1) They wrap a pre-trained PLM in a Reasoning-Augmented Decoding (RAD) framework that interleaves autoregressive token generation with calls to external tools (ESMFold for structure prediction, FoldX for stability/energy, AutoDock Vina for docking/affinity). These tool outputs are encoded and fed back into the model so subsequent tokens are conditioned on biophysical feedback. 2) They propose Contrastive Agent Policy Optimisation (CAPO), a trajectory-level extension of direct preference optimisation. CAPO compares full generation trajectories (including tool calls and resulting sequences) based on oracle-derived preferences, and updates the PLM policy end-to-end to favour trajectories where oracle feedback was informative and used effectively, rather than just copying known high-fitness sequences. 3) They evaluate on multiple protein design tasks with standardised oracle APIs and controlled sequence-identity splits to ensure generalisation beyond close homologs.

Result: AgentPLM attains state-of-the-art performance across de novo enzyme design, antibody optimisation, thermostability engineering, protein–protein interface design, and zero-shot fitness prediction. In particular, it improves the top-10% hit rate in antibody design over the strongest passive PLM baseline, and empirical analyses indicate that the model performs online error correction—modifying sequences in response to adverse oracle feedback—without needing explicit search or backtracking mechanisms.

Conclusion: Coupling PLMs with structured biophysical tools via RAD and training them with CAPO transforms them into effective design agents that can selectively query and exploit oracle feedback. This leads to improved protein design and fitness prediction compared to passive PLMs, and provides mechanistic evidence that such agents can perform online error correction during generation. The framework suggests a general route to integrating generative sequence models with external scientific oracles for closed-loop design.

Abstract: Protein language models (PLMs) are passive oracles: they generate sequences in a single forward pass with no mechanism to consult external biophysical feedback or redirect generation when a candidate violates thermodynamic or structural constraints. We introduce AgentPLM, which addresses this by equipping a pre-trained PLM with i) Reasoning-Augmented Decoding (RAD), which interleaves autoregressive generation with tool calls (ESMFold, FoldX, AutoDock Vina), and ii) Contrastive Agent Policy Optimisation (CAPO), a trajectory-level extension of direct preference optimisation that trains the policy end-to-end to learn when oracle feedback is informative rather than merely imitating high-fitness sequences. We evaluate AgentPLM on benchmark tasks spanning de novo enzyme design, antibody optimisation, thermostability, PPI interface design, and zero-shot fitness prediction with standardised oracle APIs and controlled sequence-identity splits. AgentPLM achieves state-of-the-art results with a gain in antibody top-10% hit rate over the strongest passive baseline, providing mechanistic evidence of online error correction without explicit backtracking.

</details>


### [349] [Bridging the Sim-to-Real Gap in Semiconductor Visual Program Synthesis via Input Binarization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02434)
*Yusuke Ohtsubo,Kota Dohi,Koichiro Yawata,Koki Takeshita,Tatsuya Sasaki*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They use a vision-language model to convert SEM inspection images of circuits into precise, editable geometric code, and show that binarizing images to remove texture helps bridge the gap between synthetic training data and real SEM images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Semiconductor metrology needs extremely precise geometric information, but collecting enough labeled real data from SEM images is expensive. Existing generative models can create synthetic data but cannot guarantee the nanometer-accurate, exactly controllable geometry required for rigorous inspection and parameter sweeps, so a new approach is needed for controllable, geometry-accurate data generation.

Method: They design a visual program synthesis pipeline where a VLM maps images to a Domain-Specific Language that encodes circuit geometry parameters. The VLM is trained only on synthetic images rendered from the DSL, which creates a domain gap when applied to real SEM images. To address this, they introduce an input binarization pre-processing step that removes SEM-specific texture and noise, emphasizing only geometric structure before feeding images to the VLM. They evaluate this on the MIIC dataset, comparing performance with and without binarization using segmentation-style overlap metrics.

Result: On the MIIC dataset, using binarized SEM inputs significantly boosts performance: the mean Dice coefficient increases from 0.4393 with raw SEM inputs to 0.5256 with binarized inputs, indicating better alignment between predicted and ground-truth circuit geometries and reduced synthetic-to-real domain gap.

Conclusion: Encoding circuit layouts as DSL programs via a VLM provides precise, editable geometric representations suitable for metrology and controllable data generation. Training the VLM purely on synthetic data is viable if a simple binarization step is used to abstract away SEM textures, which substantially reduces the sim-to-real domain gap, as evidenced by improved Dice scores. This suggests that low-level texture details are less important than clean geometric structure for accurate visual program synthesis in semiconductor inspection.

Abstract: Precise parametric control over circuit geometry is essential for semiconductor inspection, yet obtaining sufficient real training data remains costly. Although generative models such as diffusion models and Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) can augment training data, they cannot guarantee the nanometer-scale geometric accuracy required for metrology tasks. We propose a visual program synthesis framework in which a Vision-Language Model (VLM) converts inspection images into editable Domain-Specific Language (DSL) code describing circuit geometries, enabling controlled generation of training data with exact parameter manipulation. Because the VLM is trained solely on synthetic DSL-rendered data, a domain gap arises when processing real Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) images. We bridge this gap with an input binarization strategy that strips SEM-specific texture and noise, letting the model focus on geometric structure. On the MIIC dataset, binarized inputs improve the mean Dice coefficient from 0.4393 to 0.5256 over the raw-input baseline, demonstrating that simple texture abstraction substantially mitigates the sim-to-real gap.

</details>


### [350] [LLM-Evolved Pattern Generators for Optimal Classical Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02438)
*Windy Phung,Dominik Drexler,Arnaud Lequen,Jendrik Seipp*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They learn domain-specific but admissible heuristics for optimal planning by having an LLM synthesize programs that generate pattern databases, then combine them via saturated cost partitioning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learned heuristics can guide search well in satisficing planning but typically break admissibility, so they cannot be used with A* when optimality is required. The authors want a way to gain the benefits of learning (domain specialization, speed) while preserving admissibility and hence optimality guarantees in classical planning.

Method: Instead of directly predicting heuristic values, they learn how to construct abstractions (pattern collections) that induce admissible heuristics. For each planning domain, they use an LLM-based evolutionary program synthesis framework: an LLM proposes and iteratively refines a program that, given a task instance, outputs a collection of patterns. These patterns define abstraction-based heuristics; the final heuristic is obtained by admissibly combining them using saturated cost partitioning. The search then uses A* with these learned admissible heuristics.

Result: The synthesized programs capture meaningful, interpretable domain structure and can be executed quickly at test time to produce heuristics. In experiments across several planning domains, the learned admissible heuristics achieve coverage comparable to strong domain-independent baselines but with substantially faster per-state heuristic evaluation.

Conclusion: It is possible to automatically learn domain-dependent heuristics that remain admissible and thus safe for optimal planning. Their LLM-driven program synthesis approach produces fast, interpretable pattern-generation programs whose induced heuristics rival established domain-independent methods in coverage while being cheaper to evaluate, opening a path to practical learned heuristics for optimal classical planning.

Abstract: Learned heuristics have recently become a competitive alternative to traditional domain-independent heuristics for satisficing planning. Existing approaches, however, focus on improving search guidance rather than guaranteeing admissibility, which makes them unsuitable for optimal classical planning. We present the first method for learning domain-dependent heuristics that are admissible by design and thus preserve the optimality guarantees of A* search. Instead of learning a direct mapping from states to heuristic values, we learn to construct abstractions that induce admissible heuristics. We use an LLM-driven evolutionary program-synthesis framework to obtain, for each domain, a program that produces a pattern collection for any task in that domain, and we combine the resulting patterns admissibly via saturated cost partitioning. Empirically, the learned programs encode interpretable domain-specific insights, run with negligible overhead at test time and yield heuristics that match the coverage of state-of-the-art domain-independent baselines on several domains while evaluating each state substantially faster.

</details>


### [351] [Food Noise & False Safety: A Systematic Evaluation of How LLMs Fail to Adapt to Eating Disorder Queries with Clinician Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02444)
*Giulia Pucci,Emily Hemendinger,Ruizhe Li,Gavin Abercrombie,Tanvi Dinkar,Arabella Sinclair*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how people with eating disorders use LLM-based chatbots, and analyzes when and how these models give unsafe, potentially harmful responses that adapt to disordered or self-harming prompts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: People with eating disorders are increasingly turning to LLM chatbots for advice and emotional support, despite these systems not being designed as clinical tools. Because users may perceive them as expert, neutral, and always available, there is a risk that LLMs may unintentionally reinforce or facilitate disordered eating or self-harm. Understanding patterns of risky interaction and when LLMs fail to respond safely is needed to inform safer model design and deployment.

Method: The authors consult clinical eating disorder experts to identify linguistic cues in user prompts that signal elevated risk or disordered intentions. They then systematically vary these cues and the overall degree of potential risk in synthetic or collected prompts, testing how current LLMs respond. By analyzing how often and in what ways the models uncritically adapt to problematic instructions or attitudes, they characterize conditions that lead to unsafe outputs.

Result: The study finds that certain linguistic patterns in prompts—presumably those indicating strong ED identification, explicit weight-loss or purging goals, or self-harming intent—increase the probability that LLMs produce unsafe responses. As the risk level encoded in the prompt increases, many models more readily adapt to the user’s problematic framing instead of challenging it, thereby offering guidance or validation that may be harmful for people with EDs.

Conclusion: LLMs can inadvertently amplify risks for people with eating disorders by aligning with and facilitating disordered or self-harming user requests, especially when specific risky linguistic cues are present. Safety mechanisms are not reliably triggered under these conditions. The findings underscore the need for more robust safety training, better detection of high-risk ED-related language, and model behaviors that consistently redirect users to evidence-based, clinically informed support rather than adapting to harmful prompts.

Abstract: Recent evidence shows that people with eating disorders (EDs) are increasingly seeking guidance, advice, and emotional support from Large Language Model (LLM)-based chat systems. Although these systems are not designed to provide clinical advice, their perceived expertise, neutrality and accessibility make them a frequent, albeit risky, source of support. This paper investigates potential patterns of interaction between users with EDs and LLMs, focusing on the potential harms arising from models that uncritically adapt to, and facilitate unsafe or self-harming user requests. We find, in consultation with clinical ED experts, that specific linguistic cues in prompts increase the likelihood of unsafe responses and, through systematically varying the degree of potential risk present in the user prompt, report the extent to which LLMs uncritically adapt to problematic, and potentially dangerous user inputs.

</details>


### [352] [HLL: Can Agents Cross Humanity's Last Line of Verification?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02449)
*Xinhao Song,Su Su,Sirui Song,Hongliang Wu,Wen Shen,Zhihua Wei,Gongshen Liu,Linfeng Zhang,Dongrui Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces HLL, a benchmark using interactive CAPTCHAs to test whether multimodal agents can truly act as human substitutes in protected, automation-resistant workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As multimodal agents become capable of operating user interfaces, an important deployment question is whether they can replace humans in workflows that explicitly try to block automation. CAPTCHA is a prime example, serving as a human-verification barrier before critical actions such as account creation or content access. Existing evaluations mostly test recognition capabilities, not whether agents can robustly pass these protected workflows under realistic conditions and process constraints. A controlled, realistic benchmark is needed to measure how close agents are to crossing this boundary.

Method: The authors propose Humanity’s Last Line of Verification (HLL), a controlled benchmark centered on interactive CAPTCHA solving. HLL spans diverse CAPTCHA interaction types and incorporates realism stressors such as cluttered webpages, more difficult CAPTCHA variants, and validation that conditions on the full action trace used to solve the task. They place eight state-of-the-art multimodal agents in a closed-loop GUI environment, where agents must perceive the interface, localize the CAPTCHA, plan and execute actions, and provide answers that are checked both for correctness and for consistency of the interaction trace.

Result: Across eight frontier multimodal agents, performance at CAPTCHA verification is brittle. Success rates differ significantly across CAPTCHA types, drop under more realistic interface conditions (e.g., cluttered pages and harder variants), and decrease further when the evaluation requires valid, coherent action traces rather than only correct final answers. These behaviors reveal weaknesses in localization, calibration of low-level actions, tracking of interface state, and maintaining a consistent solving process.

Conclusion: HLL establishes a concrete, controlled testbed to evaluate whether multimodal agents can operate as human substitutes in workflows intentionally guarded against automation. The benchmark demonstrates that current agents fall short at this human-verification boundary, particularly under realistic noise and process-based validation. By highlighting specific failure modes in perception, action, and state management, HLL can guide the development and measurement of future agents aiming to safely and reliably interact with protected real-world interfaces.

Abstract: Multimodal agents are increasingly expected to operate interfaces on behalf of users, raising a central deployment question: can they truly substitute for humans in workflows that services deliberately protect against automation? CAPTCHA verification makes this question concrete. It is not merely a visual puzzle, but a human-verification boundary placed before account creation, content access, form submission, and other protected actions. We introduce \textbf{Humanity's Last Line of Verification (HLL)}, a controlled benchmark that uses interactive CAPTCHA verification to evaluate whether agents can cross this boundary through grounded, human-like interaction rather than recognition alone. HLL covers diverse CAPTCHA interactions and exposes agents to controlled realism stressors, including cluttered webpages, harder task variants, and trace-conditioned validation of the solving process. We evaluate eight frontier multimodal agents in a closed-loop GUI environment. The results show that current agents remain brittle at this human-substitution boundary: performance varies sharply across verification types, degrades under realistic interface conditions, and drops further when correct answers must be supported by valid action traces. By exposing gaps in localization, action calibration, state tracking, and process consistency, HLL provides a concrete testbed for measuring how close multimodal agents are to acting as human substitutes in protected real-world workflows. Our code is available at https://github.com/XinhaoS0101/HLL

</details>


### [353] [Beyond One-shot: AI Agents for Learning in Field Experiments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02458)
*Junjie Luo,Ritu Agarwal,Gordon Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper tests whether a tool-augmented, agentic AI system can automatically learn from past A/B test data to design better future interventions, and finds that it outperforms human + chatbot co-designed messages in a large-scale healthcare messaging field experiment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Organizations run many A/B tests, but the knowledge from each experiment is rarely turned into systematically better designs for future interventions. Extracting design principles from prior experiments is labor-intensive, requires domain expertise, and is often not scalable. The authors aim to see if an autonomous, tool-using AI can close this gap by turning experimental logs into improved, domain-specific interventions without continuous human design effort.

Method: They conduct a two-stage field experiment in healthcare prescription reminder messaging, involving 693,139 patient visits. In Stage 1, behavioral experts collaborate with a conversational AI (Human + Chatbot) to design 13 message variants that are tested on 444,691 visits. In Stage 2, a tool-augmented agentic AI system is given the Stage 1 experimental data; using analytical tools, DIKW-structured reasoning agents, and transparent evidence chains, it autonomously extracts principles and generates 17 new message variants, tested on 248,448 visits. They compare outcomes such as click-through rate (CTR) and also test whether frontier LLMs without access to experimental data can predict successful interventions.

Result: The agentic AI system generates messages that outperform the Human + Chatbot designs: its best message achieves a 69.8% CTR, which is a 6.5 percentage-point improvement over the baseline. Frontier LLMs that rely only on general reasoning and behavioral theory, without using the experimental data, fail to reliably predict which interventions will work. The experiments further show that general behavioral theories do not consistently transfer to the specific healthcare messaging context.

Conclusion: Tool-augmented, agentic AI can autonomously learn from domain-specific experimental data to design better interventions, outperforming human-plus-chatbot co-design. The gains come primarily from data-driven, domain-specific learning rather than generic reasoning capabilities of large language models. Because standard behavioral theories do not uniformly apply in real-world healthcare contexts, the authors propose using such agentic AI systems for large-scale theory audits and to turn isolated A/B tests into a cumulative, data-driven design-learning process.

Abstract: Organizations routinely run experiments for A/B testing, yet the data generated from one experiment is underutilized to inform subsequent intervention design. Significant barriers exist to extracting actionable knowledge from prior experimental data to inform new interventions. We study whether tool-augmented agentic AI can automatically learn from experimental data to generate new interventions in subsequent experiments. Through two-stage field experiments in healthcare prescription messaging (693,139 patient visits), we compare a Human + Chatbot method (Stage 1: behavioral experts with conversational AI co-designing 13 message variants, 444,691 patient visits) against a Tool-Augmented Agentic AI method (Stage 2: AI autonomously extracting principles from Stage 1 data to generate 17 new variants, 248,448 patient visits). The Agentic AI method, equipped with analytical tools, structured Data-Information-Knowledge-Wisdom (DIKW) reasoning agents, and transparent evidence chains, produces superior interventions: the best AI-generated message achieved a 69.8% CTR (+6.5 percentage points over baseline). Critically, our results suggest that the value comes from domain-specific experimental data, not from general reasoning ability: frontier LLMs operating without experimental data failed to predict which interventions would succeed. The field experiments also revealed that general-purpose behavioral theories used for intervention design do not extend uniformly to specific healthcare contexts, motivating an agentic AI approach to theory audits at field-experiment scale. Our research shows that tool-augmented AI can learn from experimental data and generate improved domain-relevant interventions, transforming behavioral experimentation from one-shot evaluation into a scalable system for cumulative design learning.

</details>


### [354] [AGENTCL: Toward Rigorous Evaluation of Continual Learning in Language Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02461)
*Yiheng Shu,Bernal Jiménez Gutiérrez,Saisri Padmaja Jonnalagedda,Yuguang Yao,Huan Sun,Yu Su*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces AgentCL, a benchmark and framework to rigorously evaluate continual learning and memory in language-based agents using controlled, compositional task streams and transfer-focused metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current language agents underuse experience across episodes and existing benchmarks do not cleanly measure continual learning, transfer, and interference. Long-context and naive lifelong benchmarks lack controlled notions of what knowledge should be reusable, making it hard to understand what agents actually learn and reuse over time or how memory designs impact performance.

Method: They design AgentCL, an evaluation framework that creates compositional task streams where sub-solutions, evidence, or workflows from earlier tasks are deliberately made reusable in later tasks, and compare these to naive task streams where such structure is absent. They define metrics for transfer gains and apply the framework to assess non-parametric memory architectures in agents. They also introduce MemProbe, a probing-style memory mechanism that logs interactions, insights, and skills while filtering out unreliable experiences during a consolidation phase, to study the impact of different memory design choices on continual learning behavior.

Result: Across domains like coding, deep research, and language understanding/reasoning, experiments show that naive task streams do a poor job of differentiating between memory designs, while the controlled, compositional streams of AgentCL more clearly reveal each design’s plasticity and reuse behavior. They also find that naive and held-out evaluation settings often show small benefits and can even reveal performance degradation induced by memory use.

Conclusion: A rigorous assessment of continual learning in language agents requires carefully constructed, compositional task streams and transfer-aware metrics rather than naive sequences of tasks. The findings suggest that current non-parametric memory designs are inadequate, frequently yielding limited or negative gains, and underscore the need for stronger memory mechanisms that better balance plasticity with stable reuse of prior experience.

Abstract: Language agents spend substantial inference time solving individual tasks, yet the experience acquired in one episode is often underutilized in future episodes. Continual learning expects an agent to accumulate reusable experience across a stream of tasks, improve over time, and avoid interference from irrelevant experiences. Unfortunately, existing benchmarks struggle to evaluate continual learning in language agents rigorously. Most efforts focus on retrieval and reasoning over long-context conversations or documents, while recent lifelong-adaptation benchmarks often rely on naive task streams with limited analysis of cross-task relationships, making it difficult to understand what an agent learns and reuses over time. This paper presents an evaluation framework AgentCL for continual learning in agents, centered on controlled task streams and metrics for transfer gains. AGENTCL constructs compositional streams where earlier sub-solutions, evidence, or workflows are intentionally reusable in later tasks, and contrasts them with naive streams where such reusability is not guaranteed. We use the benchmark to evaluate non-parametric memory designs for continual learning. To diagnose how memory design choices affect continual learning, we develop MemProbe, a probing method that stores interactions, insights, and skills, while filtering unreliable experiences during consolidation. Empirical analysis across coding, deep research, and language understanding/reasoning tasks shows that naive streams offer limited ability to distinguish memory designs, whereas controlled streams more clearly distinguish their plasticity. Meanwhile, naive and held-out settings often yield limited gains and can expose memory-induced degradation. These results highlight the need for stronger memory designs that balance plasticity and stable reuse.

</details>


### [355] [MCP-Persona: Benchmarking LLM Agents on Real-World Personal Applications via Environment Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02470)
*Wenhao Wang,Peizhi Niu,Gongyi Zou,Xiyuan Yang,Jingxing Wang,Haoting Shi,Yaxin Du,Jingyi Chai,Xianghe Pang,Shuo Tang,Yanfeng Wang,Siheng Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces MCP-Persona, a benchmark to evaluate LLM agents on personalized MCP tools across real social and collaboration apps, showing current agents perform poorly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks evaluate LLM agents mainly on generic information-seeking tools, which do not reflect the complexities of personal social applications where tools are tied to individual accounts or local data. There is a need for a benchmark that captures these real-world, personalized tool-use challenges to better assess and improve agent capabilities.

Method: Design and construct MCP-Persona, a benchmark built on top of Model Context Protocol tools that connect to real, personalized applications (e.g., Reddit, Xiaohongshu, Lark, Slack). Define tasks that require agents to use these tools in realistic, account-specific scenarios, and then systematically evaluate multiple state-of-the-art LLM agents on these tasks.

Result: Across diverse tasks and applications in MCP-Persona, state-of-the-art LLM agents show substantial difficulties in effectively using personalized MCP tools, indicating a notable performance gap compared with their abilities on generic tool-use benchmarks.

Conclusion: MCP-Persona exposes key weaknesses of current LLM agents in handling personalized, real-world MCP tools and provides a much-needed benchmark for diagnosing and driving future improvements. The benchmark is released publicly to facilitate ongoing research and development.

Abstract: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) has emerged as a transformative standard for connecting large language models (LLMs) with external data sources and tools, and has been rapidly adopted across personal applications and development platforms. However, existing benchmarks predominantly focus on generic information-seeking tools and fail to capture the practical challenges posed by personal social applications, where tools interact with individual accounts or local databases. To bridge this critical gap, we introduce MCP-Persona, the first benchmark specifically designed for evaluating agent performance on real-world, personalized MCP tools. MCP-Persona encompasses a diverse set of widely-used applications, ranging from social media platforms like Reddit and Xiaohongshu (Rednote) to enterprise collaboration suites such as Lark (Feishu) and Slack. Our extensive experiments on various state-of-the-art (SOTA) agents demonstrate their significant struggles with personalized tool use, thereby highlighting the benchmark's crucial role in identifying and addressing these limitations. MCP-Persona is publicly available at https://github.com/wwh0411/MCP-Persona}{https://github.com/wwh0411/MCP-Persona.

</details>


### [356] [Iteris: Agentic Research Loops for Computational Mathematics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02484)
*Leheng Chen,Zihao Liu,Wanyi He,Bin Dong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces Iteris, an agentic AI system for tackling open problems in computational mathematics, demonstrating its usefulness via two case studies where AI-assisted work led to verified new results after human validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While large language models and agentic AI have shown strong performance on math competition problems and some research-level conjectures, open problems in computational mathematics remain underexplored. These problems typically require more than pure proof generation: they demand numerical experiments, adversarial counterexamples, and algorithm design. The paper aims to fill this gap by building a system tailored to the full workflow of computational mathematics research, not just theorem proving.

Method: Design and implement Iteris, an agentic research system orchestrating LLM-based tools to perform numerical experimentation, generate constructions, and draft proofs for open problems. The authors then conduct two detailed case studies on open problems from a Simons Workshop collection, allowing the system to explore, generate evidence, propose constructions and proof sketches, and iterating with human experts who review, correct, and formalize the results.

Result: Using Iteris, the authors obtained: (1) a phase diagram characterizing the asymptotic comparison between conjugate gradient and randomized coordinate descent on power-law spectral distributions; and (2) a concrete counterexample establishing that QR factorization with column pivoting can fail to select well-conditioned submatrices even for matrices with low coherence. In both cases, Iteris produced numerical data, candidate constructions, and draft arguments that, after expert refinement, yielded new verified mathematical results.

Conclusion: Agentic AI systems like Iteris can make substantive contributions to the research workflow in computational mathematics, particularly in tasks that blend numerical experimentation, construction of examples/counterexamples, and theoretical reasoning. However, the case studies underline that expert human oversight, verification, and correction are still indispensable, suggesting a collaborative rather than fully autonomous role for AI in this domain.

Abstract: Recent advances in large language models and agentic AI systems have enabled significant progress in mathematical discovery, from solving competition problems to tackling research-level conjectures. However, open problems in computational mathematics have received comparatively less attention: research in this area often requires not only proofs but also numerical experimentation, adversarial constructions, and algorithm design. In this paper, we introduce an agentic research system, Iteris, designed for open problems in computational mathematics. We apply Iteris to two open problems from a recent Simons Workshop collection (arXiv:2602.05394). In these case studies, Iteris generated numerical evidence, constructions, and proof drafts that led, after expert review and correction, to verified results. The first result is a phase diagram for the asymptotic comparison between conjugate gradient and randomized coordinate descent on power-law spectra; the second is a counterexample showing that QR factorization with column pivoting can fail to select well-conditioned submatrices even under low coherence. These case studies suggest that agentic AI systems can participate meaningfully in research workflows for open problems in computational mathematics, while human validation remains essential.

</details>


### [357] [RASER: Recoverability-Aware Selective Escalation Router for Multi-Hop Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02488)
*Yuyang Li,Zihe Yan,Tobias Käfer*

Main category: cs.AI
