TL;DR: Proposes RASER, a lightweight routing framework on top of one-shot RAG that selectively decides when to invoke more expensive multi-hop retrieval methods, cutting token usage while preserving accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-hop QA systems frequently perform multiple retrieval and LLM calls (e.g., question decomposition, bridge-entity search, iterative retrieval) for every question. This is costly in terms of tokens and unnecessary for many multi-hop questions that can already be solved with a single one-shot retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) step. When LLM budget is constrained, repeatedly invoking expensive retrieval pipelines is inefficient, motivating a method to decide when extra retrieval is actually needed.

Method: They analyze recoverability of multi-hop questions under one-shot RAG, then build RASER, a family of cheap routers that leverage six features derived from a single one-shot RAG run (e.g., scores, confidence-related signals). RASER-2 is a binary router that either stops after the one-shot RAG result or escalates to an extra-retrieval method called PRUNE. RASER-3 is a ternary router that selects among one-shot RAG, PRUNE, and an iterative retrieval method IRCoT, incorporating an explicit cost–accuracy trade-off. Both routers make routing decisions without incurring extra LLM calls, relying only on the features from the initial RAG run.

Result: On three multi-hop QA benchmarks and across six different LLMs, both RASER-2 and RASER-3 match or stay competitive with state-of-the-art multi-hop QA baselines in terms of F1 score, while using only 41–49% of the tokens required by an always-PRUNE strategy and consuming fewer tokens than iterative and question-decomposition retrieval baselines.

Conclusion: Many multi-hop questions are adequately answered by a single one-shot RAG step, and indiscriminately applying complex retrieval pipelines is wasteful. Cheap recoverability-aware routers like RASER can automatically decide when to escalate to more advanced retrieval strategies, retaining SOTA-level accuracy while substantially reducing token costs and improving efficiency under tight LLM budgets.

Abstract: Multi-hop question-answering systems often use expensive retrieval on every question. They may decompose the question, run several retrieval rounds, or search through bridge entities before answering. All of these strategies rely on repeated LLM calls to rewrite or decompose the question, which increases extra token cost, and it is not fitting when the LLM budget is tight. However, our analysis shows that lots of multi-hop questions are already answered correctly by a single one-shot RAG, so running an extra retrieval on every question wastes the budget. We introduce RASER (Recoverability-Aware Selective Escalation Router), a family of cheap routers built on one-shot RAG and six features from it. RASER-2 decides whether to stop or escalate to the extra-retrieval action PRUNE. RASER-3 chooses among one-shot RAG, PRUNE, and iterative retrieval IRCoT, using the same features but adding an explicit cost-accuracy trade-off. Neither router makes an extra LLM call to decide. Across six LLMs and three multi-hop QA benchmarks, both routers stay competitive with the other state-of-the-art (SOTA) baselines in F1 while spending only 41-49% of always-prune's tokens and also less than the iterative and decomposition retrieval baselines.

</details>


### [358] [Bridging the Last Mile of Time Series Forecasting with LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02497)
*Yuhua Liao,Zetian Wang,Qiangqiang Nie,Zhenhua Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose an LLM-agent ‘last-mile forecasting’ layer that revises base time-series forecasts using business context, making them decision-ready.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In practice, raw statistical or foundation-model forecasts are rarely used directly for decisions; practitioners adjust them using informal business context (holidays, campaigns, events, analogs, expert input). This crucial final adjustment stage is poorly studied and lacks systematic, auditable methods.

Method: They define the ‘last-mile forecasting’ problem and build an LLM-agent framework on top of an existing forecasting backbone. The agent operates in a unified forecast workspace, calls tools to retrieve contextual information, reasons about this evidence, and converts its reasoning into explicit, structurally constrained forecast-revision actions. It can decompose long-horizon forecasts via a map-reduce-like strategy and uses a memory bank to support post-hoc reflection and improvement. The system is designed to be controllable and auditable.

Result: Using real-world case studies, they demonstrate that the LLM-agent can successfully incorporate weakly structured business context to revise base forecasts, improving their suitability for business decision-making compared to using raw statistical predictions alone.

Conclusion: An LLM-agent acting as a controlled, auditable last-mile layer on top of standard forecasting models can effectively bridge the gap between statistically plausible predictions and business-ready forecasts by systematically integrating contextual information into forecast revisions.

Abstract: Time series forecasting has advanced rapidly, especially with the emergence of foundation models that show strong zero-shot performance on numerical extrapolation. However, in real-world forecasting settings, a statistically plausible baseline is rarely the final forecast used in practice. Before a forecast becomes decision-ready, it often needs to be revised using weakly structured business context such as holiday effects, campaign plans, external events, historical analogs, and expert feedback. This practical stage remains underexplored in the forecasting literature. In this paper, we formulate this stage as the \textbf{last-mile forecasting} problem and present an LLM-agent framework that sits on top of a forecasting backbone. Our system maintains a unified forecast workspace, invokes tools to retrieve contextual evidence, and converts reasoning trajectories into explicit forecast revision actions under structural safety constraints. It also supports long-horizon forecasting through map-reduce-style decomposition and post-hoc reflection through a memory bank. The resulting system is designed to be controllable and auditable. Through real-world case studies, we show how LLM agents can bridge the gap between statistical prediction and business-ready forecasting.

</details>


### [359] [SafeSteer: Localized On-Policy Distillation for Efficient Safety Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02530)
*Hao Li,Jingkun An,Zijun Song,Pengyu Zhu,Rui Li,Hao Wang,Wendi Feng,Yesheng Liu,Lijun Li,Jin-Ge Yao,Lei Sha*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SafeSteer is a lightweight alignment method that localizes safety adjustments to specific safety-related tokens, preserving general capabilities while improving safety with very little harmful training data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Aligning LLMs with human values (safety) typically causes an "alignment tax"—a degradation in general capabilities. Existing approaches try to balance a joint objective for capability and safety, needing large general-purpose datasets and/or complex reward models. The authors are motivated to find a more efficient and less costly way to improve safety that avoids broad capability trade-offs and massive data requirements.

Method: They hypothesize that safety-relevant behaviors are sparse in the output space and thus can be modified locally. They propose SafeSteer, an alignment method that focuses training only on safety tokens. First, they build a safety teacher model via activation steering: modifying internal activations to encourage safe behavior. Then, using this teacher, they design an algorithm to select which output tokens are safety-relevant. During on-policy distillation, they restrict the reverse KL regularization to just these safety tokens, leaving the rest of the output distribution largely untouched to preserve general capabilities.

Result: Across multiple LLMs, SafeSteer yields a better safety–capability trade-off than existing baselines. It achieves high performance on seven safety benchmarks while causing only minimal degradation on five general capability benchmarks. Moreover, it attains this using only about 100 harmful training samples and no additional general-purpose data, which is less than 1% of the data used by prior approaches.

Conclusion: Safety alignment can be achieved through localized modifications to safety-relevant tokens instead of global objective trade-offs. SafeSteer operationalizes this idea and demonstrates that strong safety improvements with minimal capability loss are possible with very small harmful datasets and without large auxiliary resources, substantially reducing alignment cost.

Abstract: Aligning Large Language Models (LLMs) with human values often degrades their general capabilities, termed the alignment tax. Existing methods mitigate this by balancing dual objectives, which heavily rely on massive general-purpose data or auxiliary reward models.
  In this paper, we argue that, because safety features are inherently sparse within the output distribution, alignment requires localized modifications rather than global trade-offs. To this end, we propose SafeSteer, which performs on-policy distillation confined to safety tokens. First, we construct a safety teacher via activation steering. Based on this teacher, we develop a safety token selection algorithm. Consequently, SafeSteer restricts the reverse KL penalty to these tokens during training to preserve general capabilities.
  Experimental results across diverse models show that our SafeSteer achieves a superior trade-off between safety and general capability compared with existing methods, attaining strong safety performance on seven safety benchmarks with only minimal degradation on five general capability benchmarks. Notably, SafeSteer requires only 100 harmful samples without using any general-purpose data, less than 1% of what previous baselines used, considerably reducing alignment cost. More details are on our project page at https://anjingkun.github.io/SafeSteer.

</details>


### [360] [Tracking the Behavioral Trajectories of Adapting Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02536)
*Jonah Leshin,Manish Shah,Ian Timmis*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Defines and measures agent behavioral traits as directions in text embedding space, learned from edits to agent skill files, and uses this to evaluate agent updates via a protocol with a trusted intermediary.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern agents are governed by editable text artifacts (skill, memory, configuration files) that directly shape future behavior. As these files change over time, especially through self-modification, there is a need for a systematic way to quantify and monitor high-level behavioral traits (e.g., propensity to seek sensitive data) induced by such edits. Existing tools do not offer a precise, continuous measure of how specific file changes affect these traits.

Method: They formalize traits as directions in the embedding space of a text embedding model. Concretely, they collect labeled pairs of skill file diffs representing changes in a target trait (before vs. after). They embed these diffs, then train a linear model to separate positive from negative examples, yielding a trait vector. For any new skill edit, they compute an embedding diff and project it onto the learned trait vector to obtain a scalar trait score. They evaluate the learned trait direction using leave-one-out cross-validation on a dataset of 68 labeled diff pairs, focusing on the trait of propensity to seek sensitive data. They then integrate this scoring mechanism into an agent-to-agent protocol where one agent’s skill updates are judged by another agent via a trusted intermediary that applies the trait evaluation.

Result: For the tested trait (propensity to seek sensitive data) on 68 labeled skill diff pairs, their learned trait vector achieves 91.2% accuracy in predicting the sign (direction) of trait change and a Spearman rank correlation of ρ = 0.82 between predicted and true rankings of trait intensity under leave-one-out cross-validation. This demonstrates that a simple linear direction in embedding space can reliably capture a meaningful behavioral trait from textual edits.

Conclusion: Trait-like behavioral properties of agents can be operationalized as linear directions in text embedding space, learned from labeled examples of configuration or skill file changes. This yields a practical, quantitative tool for scoring arbitrary future edits along a trait dimension. Embedding this evaluator in an agent-to-agent protocol via a trusted intermediary enables oversight of agent self-modification and behavior steering, potentially improving safety and controllability of modern AI agents.

Abstract: Text files such as skill files, memory files, and behavioral configuration files play a central role in defining how modern agents act. Through edits by humans or the agents themselves, these files may evolve over time, directly steering the agent's behavior in future interactions. We present a methodology and framework for measuring agent $traits$ by defining traits as directions in the embedding space of a text embedding model. We train a linear model on labeled "before" versus "after" skill file diffs to learn a trait vector, then score arbitrary skill edits by projecting their embedding diffs onto this vector. Evaluated on 68 labeled skill diff pairs for the trait of propensity to seek sensitive data, our method achieves 91.2% sign classification accuracy and a Spearman rank correlation of $ρ= 0.82$ under leave-one-out cross-validation. We build this trait evaluation into a broader agent-to-agent protocol that enables one agent to evaluate another's skill file updates through a trusted intermediary.

</details>


### [361] [ClinEnv: An Interactive Multi-Stage Long Horizon EHR Environment for Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02568)
*Yuxing Lu,Yushuhong Lin,Wenqi Shi,J. Ben Tamo,Xukai Zhao,Jinzhuo Wang,May Dongmei Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ClinEnv is a new interactive benchmark that simulates real inpatient care to evaluate how LLMs both gather information and make sequential clinical decisions, revealing major gaps in their management and information-acquisition behavior despite moderate diagnostic performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing medical benchmarks mostly use static, multiple-choice formats that do not reflect real clinical practice, where physicians gather heterogeneous data over time and make sequential, irreversible decisions under uncertainty. Even current interactive benchmarks fail to jointly capture realistic longitudinal cases, explicit information-gathering behavior, and full-spectrum clinical decisions (diagnosis, management, procedures, medications). There is a need for a benchmark that evaluates not just what decisions models make, but also how they interact with information sources over time in realistic inpatient scenarios.

Method: The authors introduce ClinEnv, an interactive benchmark based on real inpatient admissions under a Longitudinal Inpatient Simulation paradigm. Each real case is automatically decomposed into an ordered sequence of decision stages spanning the admission. At each stage, the tested LLM must actively query four specialized agents (representing different information sources, e.g., labs, notes, imaging) before committing to clinical actions, including medications, procedures, and diagnoses. The benchmark evaluates models along two axes: (1) decision quality, using deterministic ontology-grounded matching between model outputs and ground-truth actions/labels; and (2) process quality, based on how models gather and sequence information, including redundancy and efficiency of queries. The authors run seven different models through ClinEnv to assess performance trends across stages and decision types.

Result: Across the seven evaluated LLMs, the best-performing model attains only 0.31 decision F1 overall, indicating substantial room for improvement. Performance is uneven: models recover discharge diagnoses with moderate reliability (0.51 F1) but perform poorly on management decisions such as ordering appropriate interventions and treatments (0.17 F1). Difficulty increases in later stages of hospitalization and for management-heavy decisions. There is a strong decoupling between outcome quality (e.g., final diagnosis) and process quality (how information is gathered), with models frequently issuing redundant or inefficient queries as cases progress. These behaviors are quantifiable in ClinEnv but would remain invisible in outcome-only evaluations.

Conclusion: ClinEnv provides a more realistic, longitudinal, and interactive evaluation of LLMs acting as attending physicians in inpatient care. It exposes a critical information-acquisition and management gap: models may reach reasonable diagnostic conclusions but still perform poorly in therapeutic decision-making and in efficient, targeted information gathering. By making process quality directly measurable alongside decision outcomes, ClinEnv highlights the limitations of current LLMs for real-world clinical use and offers a framework for developing and benchmarking future models that better emulate the dynamic reasoning and information management of human clinicians.

Abstract: Clinical practice is not the selection of an answer from enumerated options: a physician gathers heterogeneous information incrementally and commits to sequential, irreversible decisions under uncertainty. Static benchmarks cannot probe and existing interactive medical benchmarks each compromise on at least one of them. We present ClinEnv, an interactive benchmark that evaluates LLMs as attending physicians over real inpatient admissions under a paradigm we term Longitudinal Inpatient Simulation. Each case is automatically constructed into an ordered sequence of decision stages; at every stage the model must actively query four specialized agents before committing to medications, procedures, and diagnoses. ClinEnv scores both what the model decides, through deterministic ontology-grounded matching, and how it gathers information. Across seven models, the strongest reaches only 0.31 decision F1, and outcome quality is sharply decoupled from process quality. Difficulty concentrates in management decisions and later stages, where models recover discharge diagnoses far more reliably than management actions (0.51 vs. 0.17 F1) and continue to issue redundant queries as cases progress. ClinEnv makes this information-acquisition gap, invisible to outcome-only evaluation, directly measurable.

</details>


### [362] [Pramana: Fine-Tuning Large Language Models for Epistemic Reasoning through Navya-Nyaya](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04937)
*Sharath Sathish*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes Pramana, a method that fine-tunes LLMs on Navya-Nyaya, a structured Indian logical-epistemic framework, to improve systematic, evidence-grounded reasoning and reduce hallucinations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current large language models often hallucinate and fail at systematic, explainable reasoning, especially when presented with irrelevant or distracting context. Apple’s internal study shows performance dropping sharply with added noise, revealing that many models rely on brittle pattern matching instead of grounded reasoning. There is a need for a framework that not only structures logical steps but also explicitly encodes how knowledge is justified and distinguished from mere hypothesis.

Method: The authors introduce Pramana, which fine-tunes Llama 3.2-3B and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B on 55 logical problems (constraint satisfaction, Boolean SAT, multi-step deduction) annotated using Navya-Nyaya’s 6-phase epistemological-reasoning pipeline: (1) SAMSHAYA – analyze doubt and clarify the question, (2) PRAMANA – identify valid sources of knowledge/evidence, (3) PANCHA AVAYAVA – construct a 5-part syllogism under universal rules, (4) TARKA – perform counterfactual / reductio-style checks, (5) HETVABHASA – detect and classify fallacies, and (6) NIRNAYA – finalize and explicitly distinguish confirmed knowledge from tentative hypotheses. They train and evaluate models under different prompting formats and temperatures, and run ablations on these settings.

Result: Fine-tuned models reach 100% semantic correctness on held-out Nyaya-structured reasoning tasks, even though they conform to the exact 6-phase output format only about 40% of the time, suggesting that the logical content is internalized even when the explicit structure is partially violated. Ablation studies show that both format prompting and decoding temperature substantially influence performance, and that the best settings vary across the six Navya-Nyaya stages.

Conclusion: Embedding Navya-Nyaya’s explicit epistemological and logical structure into LLM training can substantially improve systematic, evidence-sensitive reasoning, mitigating hallucinations and brittleness seen in standard chain-of-thought approaches. The work demonstrates that LLMs can internalize a rich, historically grounded reasoning framework and that structured epistemic scaffolding is a promising direction for more reliable AI. All artifacts are released on Hugging Face to encourage further exploration of epistemic frameworks for AI reasoning.

Abstract: Large language models produce fluent text but struggle with systematic reasoning, often hallucinating confident but unfounded claims. When Apple researchers added irrelevant context to mathematical problems, LLM performance degraded by 65% Apple Machine Learning Research, exposing brittle pattern-matching beneath apparent reasoning. This epistemic gap, the inability to ground claims in traceable evidence, limits AI reliability in domains requiring justification. We introduce Pramana, a novel approach that teaches LLMs explicit epistemological methodology by fine-tuning on Navya-Nyaya logic, a 2,500-year-old Indian reasoning framework. Unlike generic chain-of-thought prompting, Navya-Nyaya enforces structured 6-phase reasoning: SAMSHAYA (doubt analysis), PRAMANA (evidence source identification), PANCHA AVAYAVA (5-member syllogism with universal rules), TARKA (counterfactual verification), HETVABHASA (fallacy detection), and NIRNAYA (ascertainment distinguishing knowledge from hypothesis). This integration of logic and epistemology provides cognitive scaffolding absent from standard reasoning approaches. We fine-tune Llama 3.2-3B and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B on 55 Nyaya-structured logical problems (constraint satisfaction, Boolean SAT, multi-step deduction). Stage 1 achieves 100% semantic correctness on held-out evaluation despite only 40% strict format adherence revealing that models internalize reasoning content even when structural enforcement is imperfect. Ablation studies show format prompting and temperature critically affect performance, with optimal configurations differing by stage. We release all models, datasets, and training infrastructure on Hugging Face to enable further research on epistemic frameworks for AI reasoning.

</details>
